topics.title: Stepper Motors - Basic Principle - Types - Variable Reluctance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stepper Motors - Basic Principle - Types - Variable Reluctance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous electric machine that converts a train of discrete electrical pulses into precisely defined angular (rotary) or linear (translational) mechanical increments called **steps**. Unlike conventional AC or DC machines, which rotate continuously, the stepper motor's shaft rotates in fixed angular increments determined by the motor's construction. Each incoming pulse advances the rotor by a precisely determined **step angle (β)**, making the device a true **incremental motion actuator** capable of **open-loop position control** without requiring feedback sensors such as encoders or resolvers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A stepper motor is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms digital input pulses into proportional discrete mechanical shaft rotation, where the angular displacement is a direct, deterministic function of the number of input pulses.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Conceptual Intuition)

Imagine the **second-hand of a quartz analog wall clock**. The clock does not rotate continuously at a slow, smooth speed. Instead, an internal mechanism (driven by a piezoelectric crystal pulse) causes the hand to **jump in fixed, equal increments** — once every second. The hand snaps to a new position, holds it firmly, then snaps to the next. A stepper motor behaves in the *exact same way* at vastly greater speeds and torques.

Another excellent analogy is the **ratchet-and-pawl mechanism combined with magnetic latching**:
- Each electrical pulse = a single click of the pawl.
- The rotor tooth (or pole) = the ratchet tooth that locks into a new position.
- The **holding torque** = the magnetic "lock" that prevents backward motion when stationary.

This is why stepper motors dominate applications such as **3D printer axes, CNC feed drives, robotic joint actuation, disk-drive head positioning, and textile machine needle control** — wherever you need *digital pulses → exact mechanical position*.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The following metrics form the **evaluation vocabulary** of every stepper motor datasheet and KTU examination question:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Step Angle** | $\beta$ | **1.8°** (most common) | Angular increment per pulse |
| **Steps per Revolution** | $N_{steps}$ | **200** (for 1.8°) | $N_{steps} = 360° / \beta$ |
| **Holding Torque** | $T_h$ | **0.5 N·m – 50 N·m** | Torque resisting shaft rotation when energized |
| **Detent Torque** | $T_d$ | **5–20 % of $T_h$** | Residual torque in unenergized (PM/Hybrid) state |
| **Pull-out Torque** | $T_{po}$ | Varies with stepping rate | Max torque at a given pulse frequency |
| **Step Resolution** | — | **0.36°, 0.72°, 1.8°, 7.5°, 15°, 30°** | Industry-standard discrete values |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 1.8° standard?** A 1.8° step angle yields **200 full steps per revolution**, which combined with microstepping drives produces **51,200 microsteps/rev (×256)** — the de-facto industry standard for precision motion control.

## 1.4 The "Why" of Stepper Motors — Open-Loop Digital Positioning

The fundamental engineering value of a stepper motor lies in the **determinism of its step response**: for a given number of pulses, the shaft position is *known absolutely* without any feedback. This is captured in the conceptual equation:

$$
\theta_{shaft}(t) = N_{pulses}(t) \cdot \beta
$$

where $\theta_{shaft}$ is the absolute shaft angle and $N_{pulses}$ is the cumulative count of input pulses. This **open-loop positioning capability** is the primary reason stepper motors are preferred in cost-sensitive, high-reliability digital motion systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course specifically classifies stepper motors as a member of the family of *special electrical machines* designed for **low-speed, high-precision, digital positioning** — distinct from servomotors (closed-loop) and induction motors (continuous high-speed rotation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The basic principle of a stepper motor rests on the **electromagnetic torque interaction** between the stator's excited magnetic field and the rotor's magnetic circuit. There are two underlying physical mechanisms:

1. **Reluctance Torque (Variable Reluctance principle):** Torque is produced due to the rotor's tendency to move to a position of **minimum magnetic reluctance**, maximizing the inductance of the energized phase.
2. **Permanent Magnet / Hybrid principle:** Torque is produced due to the **alignment torque** between the stator field and the rotor's permanent magnet field.

In *all* stepper motor variants, the principle is identical at the macro level:
$$\boxed{T_{e} = \frac{1}{2} i^{2} \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}}$$

where $L(\theta)$ is the position-dependent phase inductance. For a **Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor**, this reduces to the principle of **minimum reluctance path**.

## 2.2 Constructional Architecture of a Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor

A **VR stepper motor** has the following defining constructional features:

- **Stator:** Made of laminated silicon steel, with **$N_s$ salient poles** projecting inward. Each stator pole carries a **concentrated field winding**. The poles are typically grouped into **two phases (A and B)** with $N_s$ poles per phase wound in opposite polarities (so adjacent poles are N–S–N–S).
- **Rotor:** A **cylindrical soft-iron core** with **$N_r$ salient poles** (teeth) and **NO permanent magnet** and **NO rotor winding**. The rotor material is high-permeability steel.
- **Air gap:** Non-uniform — *minimum* where a rotor tooth faces an energized stator pole, *maximum* where a rotor slot faces an energized stator pole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Examined):** The VR stepper rotor has **neither magnets nor windings** — it is a passive salient-pole structure. This distinguishes it from the PM stepper (magnet rotor) and the hybrid stepper (magnet rotor + salient teeth).

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Sequence (Two-Phase VR Motor)

The operation proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 (Phase A energized):** Current $i_A$ flows in the A-phase winding. The stator A-poles become magnetized (alternating N–S around the periphery). The rotor rotates until **the minimum reluctance path** is achieved — i.e., the rotor teeth align directly under the energized stator A-poles. The rotor is held firmly in this detent position by the reluctance torque.

**Step 2 (Phase A switched OFF, Phase B energized):** The magnetic axis of the stator field rotates by one stator pole-pitch. To maintain minimum reluctance, the rotor rotates by **one step angle $\beta$** to align its teeth with the now-energized B-poles.

**Step 3 (Phase A energized with reversed polarity):** The stator field axis rotates by another pole-pitch; the rotor advances by another $\beta$.

**Step 4:** Sequence repeats. Continuous rotation is achieved by **cyclic commutation of the phases** in the order $\text{A} \to \text{B} \to \bar{\text{A}} \to \bar{\text{B}} \to \text{A} \to \cdots$

> [!TIP]
> **Direction reversal** is achieved simply by reversing the commutation sequence: $\text{A} \to \bar{\text{B}} \to \bar{\text{A}} \to \text{B} \to \text{A} \to \cdots$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table contains the **complete set of equations** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 1 (Stepper Motors).

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_{steps}}$ | Definition of step angle | degrees (°) |
| 2 | $\beta = \dfrac{360° \cdot \vert N_s - N_r \rvert}{N_s \cdot N_r}$ | **VR stepper step angle** ($N_s$ stator poles, $N_r$ rotor poles) | degrees (°) |
| 3 | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{N_s \cdot N_r}{\vert N_s - N_r \rvert}$ | Total steps per revolution (VR motor) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\theta_{shaft} = N_{pulses} \cdot \beta$ | Open-loop position equation | degrees (°) |
| 5 | $f_{step} = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi} \cdot N_{steps}$ | Pulse frequency for synchronous speed $\omega$ | Hz |
| 6 | $T_{e} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^{2} \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | General reluctance torque equation | N·m |
| 7 | $T_{e} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \phi^{2} \dfrac{d\mathcal{R}(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Torque expressed in terms of flux and reluctance | N·m |
| 8 | $\mathcal{R}(\theta) = \mathcal{R}_0 + \mathcal{R}_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$ | Position-dependent air-gap reluctance | A·turns/Wb |
| 9 | $L(\theta) = \dfrac{N_{ph}^{2}}{\mathcal{R}(\theta)}$ | Phase inductance as a function of rotor position | H |
| 10 | $T_{e} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{N_{ph}^{2} \mathcal{R}_1 N_r \sin(N_r \theta)}{(\mathcal{R}_0 + \mathcal{R}_1 \cos(N_r \theta))^{2}} \cdot i^{2}$ | Static torque-angle characteristic of VR motor | N·m |
| 11 | $T_{pull\text{-}out} = f(f_{step}, J_{load})$ | Pull-out torque as a function of stepping rate | N·m |
| 12 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{L \cdot \Delta i}$ | Maximum single-step slew rate (voltage-limited) | Hz |
| 13 | $\tau_{e} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Electrical time constant of a phase winding | seconds (s) |
| 14 | $\tau_{m} = J \cdot \dfrac{d\omega}{dT}$ | Mechanical time constant (rotor inertia relation) | seconds (s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation 2** is the **single most-tested formula** in KTU Module 1 stepper-motor questions. The use of $\vert \cdot \rvert$ (absolute value) is critical because the step angle is always a positive quantity, even though $N_s - N_r$ may be negative depending on the relative magnitude chosen.

## 2.5 Classification of Stepper Motors (Types Overview)

KTU 2024 Module 1 classifies stepper motors into **three principal families**:

1. **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** — passive salient-pole rotor, reluctance torque only.
2. **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor** — permanent-magnet rotor, alignment torque only.
3. **Hybrid Stepper Motor** — PM rotor with salient teeth, *both* reluctance and alignment torque.

The remainder of this section focuses exclusively on the **VR Stepper Motor** as the syllabus-specified topic.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of VR Stepper Motors

VR stepper motors find their strongest application niche in:
- **Instruments and meters** where detent-free (non-magnet) operation is desired for low inertia.
- **High-speed applications** (their rotor has no magnet → no magnet-induced eddy-current losses at high step rates).
- **Harsh-environment applications** (no magnet means no demagnetization risk from stray fields or temperature).
- **Low-cost positioning** tasks (simpler construction than hybrid motors).

The trade-off: VR motors have **lower torque density** than PM or hybrid motors, and **no detent torque** (the unenergized rotor is "free" to rotate).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Step Angle Formula for a VR Stepper Motor

This is the **core derivation** of Module 1 and is a guaranteed KTU 2024 examination question.

### Given:
- Stator has $N_s$ salient poles (evenly distributed around the stator periphery).
- Rotor has $N_r$ salient teeth (evenly distributed around the rotor surface).
- Both $N_s$ and $N_r$ are integers; $N_s \neq N_r$.

### To Derive:
The step angle $\beta$ — the angular rotation of the rotor produced by advancing the stator excitation from one set of poles to the next adjacent set.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the stator pole pitch.** The angular pitch between two adjacent stator poles is:

$$
\alpha_s = \frac{360°}{N_s}
$$

The text interpretation: $\alpha_s$ is the electrical angular separation between the magnetic axes of two adjacent stator poles, measured in mechanical degrees on the rotor periphery.

**Step 2 — Define the rotor tooth pitch.** The angular pitch between two adjacent rotor teeth is:

$$
\alpha_r = \frac{360°}{N_r}
$$

The text interpretation: $\alpha_r$ is the angular spacing (in mechanical degrees) between two adjacent rotor teeth on the rotor surface.

**Step 3 — Identify the equilibrium alignment position.** When the stator phase is energized, the rotor rotates so that a rotor tooth is perfectly aligned with the energized stator pole. This is the **minimum-reluctance (maximum-inductance) position**. Let this be the position $\theta_0$.

**Step 4 — Identify the next equilibrium position.** When the commutation advances to the next stator phase, the new energized stator pole is located $\alpha_s$ mechanical degrees away from the previous one. The rotor, in seeking the new minimum-reluctance position, must rotate by an angle such that a rotor tooth aligns with the *new* energized pole.

**Step 5 — Compute the rotation.** Since the rotor teeth are spaced $\alpha_r$ apart, the smallest rotor rotation that achieves tooth-to-pole alignment with the new excited stator pole is:

$$
\beta = \alpha_r - \alpha_s \quad \text{when } \alpha_r > \alpha_s \text{ (i.e., } N_s > N_r \text{)}
$$

The text interpretation: The new equilibrium rotor position is offset from the previous one by exactly the difference between the rotor tooth pitch and the stator pole pitch.

**Step 6 — Substitute the pitch definitions.** Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$
\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r} - \frac{360°}{N_s} = 360° \left( \frac{1}{N_r} - \frac{1}{N_s} \right) = 360° \cdot \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s \cdot N_r}
$$

The text interpretation: The step angle in mechanical degrees is the difference of the inverse of the two pole counts, scaled by 360°.

**Step 7 — Generalize with absolute value** (in case $N_r > N_s$):

$$
\boxed{\beta = \frac{360° \cdot \vert N_s - N_r \rvert}{N_s \cdot N_r}}
$$

**Step 8 — Invert to obtain the number of steps per revolution:**

$$
\boxed{N_{steps} = \frac{N_s \cdot N_r}{\vert N_s - N_r \rvert}}
$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard):

**Problem:** A VR stepper motor has 8 stator poles and 6 rotor teeth. Calculate the step angle and the number of steps per revolution.

**Solution:**

$$
N_s = 8, \quad N_r = 6
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{360° \cdot \vert 8 - 6 \rvert}{8 \cdot 6} = \frac{360° \cdot 2}{48} = \frac{720°}{48} = 15°
$$

$$
N_{steps} = \frac{8 \cdot 6}{\vert 8 - 6 \rvert} = \frac{48}{2} = 24 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**Verification:** $N_{steps} \times \beta = 24 \times 15° = 360°$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of the Static Torque-Angle Characteristic of a VR Motor

This derivation gives the **torque-vs-angle profile** that determines the holding torque and stability.

### Starting Equation (from Faraday's law applied to the magnetic circuit):

The instantaneous electromagnetic torque of a singly-excited salient-pole magnetic system is:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^{2} \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

The text interpretation: Torque is proportional to the *square of the current* (note: no sign dependence on current direction — a defining property of reluctance machines) and to the *rate of change of inductance with respect to rotor position*.

**Step 1 — Model the air-gap reluctance as a function of rotor angle.** For a salient rotor, the air-gap reluctance varies periodically with rotor position. Using Fourier analysis, the dominant (fundamental) component is:

$$
\mathcal{R}(\theta) = \mathcal{R}_0 + \mathcal{R}_1 \cos(N_r \theta)
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{R}_0$ = mean (average) reluctance,
- $\mathcal{R}_1$ = amplitude of the fundamental reluctance variation,
- $N_r \theta$ = the electrical angle accounting for the number of rotor teeth.

The text interpretation: The reluctance oscillates between a minimum (rotor tooth aligned with stator pole) and a maximum (rotor slot aligned with stator pole) as the rotor turns.

**Step 2 — Express the phase inductance.** For a winding with $N_{ph}$ turns, the inductance is:

$$
L(\theta) = \frac{N_{ph}^{2}}{\mathcal{R}(\theta)} = \frac{N_{ph}^{2}}{\mathcal{R}_0 + \mathcal{R}_1 \cos(N_r \theta)}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $L(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$:**

$$
\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} = -N_{ph}^{2} \cdot \frac{ \mathcal{R}_1 \cdot (-N_r \sin(N_r \theta)) }{(\mathcal{R}_0 + \mathcal{R}_1 \cos(N_r \theta))^{2}}
$$

$$
\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} = \frac{N_{ph}^{2} \cdot \mathcal{R}_1 \cdot N_r \cdot \sin(N_r \theta)}{(\mathcal{R}_0 + \mathcal{R}_1 \cos(N_r \theta))^{2}}
$$

The text interpretation: The derivative is maximum when the rotor is *between* the aligned and unaligned positions (specifically, at $\sin(N_r \theta) = 1$, i.e., $N_r \theta = 90°$).

**Step 4 — Substitute into the torque equation:**

$$
\boxed{T_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_{ph}^{2} \cdot \mathcal{R}_1 \cdot N_r \cdot \sin(N_r \theta)}{(\mathcal{R}_0 + \mathcal{R}_1 \cos(N_r \theta))^{2}} \cdot i^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

- The torque is **proportional to $i^{2}$** → VR motors produce unidirectional torque regardless of current direction.
- The torque is a **periodic function of $\theta$** with period $360°/N_r$.
- The **maximum (pull-out) static torque** occurs at the position where the derivative of $T_e$ with respect to $\theta$ is zero. For small $\mathcal{R}_1 / \mathcal{R}_0$ (small saliency), this maximum is approximately:

$$
T_{max} \approx \frac{N_{ph}^{2} \cdot \mathcal{R}_1 \cdot N_r}{2 \mathcal{R}_0^{2}} \cdot i^{2}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Synchronous Stepping Rate for a Given Shaft Speed

If the rotor must rotate at a mechanical angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s), the required electrical pulse frequency is:

**Step 1:** The rotor must complete $N_{steps}$ electrical pulses to make one mechanical revolution.

**Step 2:** Time for one revolution = $2\pi / \omega$ seconds.

**Step 3:** Pulses per second (frequency) = $N_{steps} \div (2\pi / \omega)$:

$$
\boxed{f_{step} = \frac{N_{steps} \cdot \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{N_s \cdot N_r \cdot \omega}{2\pi \cdot \vert N_s - N_r \rvert}}
$$

The text interpretation: For a desired mechanical speed, the driver electronics must deliver pulses at this frequency — a key design parameter for the chopper/drive circuit.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — VR vs PM vs Hybrid Stepper Motors

The following KTU-style comparison matrix is a high-yield exam-prep tool:

| Parameter | **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | **Hybrid** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotor construction | Salient soft-iron teeth, no magnet | Cylindrical PM (axially magnetized) | PM + salient teeth on rotor |
| Source of torque | Reluctance variation only | Magnet alignment only | **Both reluctance + alignment** |
| Direction sensitivity to current | None ($T \propto i^2$) | Yes ($T \propto i$) | Yes ($T \propto i$) |
| Holding torque (unenergized) | **Zero** (no detent) | Significant (detent torque) | **Highest** (detent + reluctance) |
| Step angle range | Large ($7.5°$ – $30°$) | Moderate ($7.5°$ – $90°$) | **Smallest** ($0.36°$ – $5°$) |
| Torque-to-inertia ratio | Low | Medium | **Highest** |
| Step accuracy | Moderate | Good | **Best** |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Operating speed | Highest (no eddy losses) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Demagnetization risk | None | High (temperature/field) | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Classification Hierarchy of Stepper Motors

The following Mermaid block renders the complete taxonomy of stepper motor types as required by KTU 2024 Module 1. The diagram follows the strict Node Identifier Alpha Rule and avoids reserved keywords in node labels.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Stepper Motors] --> A2[Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor]
    A1 --> A3[Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor]
    A1 --> A4[Hybrid Stepper Motor]
    A2 --> B1[Rotor is Salient Soft Iron Only]
    A2 --> B2[No Permanent Magnet]
    A2 --> B3[No Rotor Winding]
    A2 --> B4[Torque is Proportional to I Squared]
    A3 --> C1[Rotor is a Permanent Magnet]
    A3 --> C2[Magnet is Axially or Radially Magnetized]
    A3 --> C3[Detent Torque Exists When Unenergized]
    A4 --> D1[Rotor has PM and Salient Teeth]
    A4 --> D2[Combines Reluctance and Alignment Torque]
    A4 --> D3[Smallest Step Angles Available]
    A2 --> E1[Operates on Minimum Reluctance Principle]
    E1 --> E2[Step Angle equals Stator Pole Pitch minus Rotor Tooth Pitch]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — Block-Level Functional Flow of a VR Stepper Motor Drive System

The following Mermaid block depicts the **block-level functional architecture** of a complete VR stepper motor drive system, including the controller, driver electronics, and the motor. This is the most likely diagram a KTU 2024 examiner expects for a 7-mark descriptive question.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP1[Pulse Generator Controller] -->|Logic Pulses| nodeP2[Microcontroller or FPGA]
    nodeP2 -->|Direction and Step Signals| nodeP3[Translator Ring Counter]
    nodeP3 -->|Phase A and Phase B Drive Signals| nodeP4[Power Driver Stage Chopper]
    nodeP4 -->|Bidirectional Current I A| nodeM1[Stator Phase A Winding]
    nodeP4 -->|Bidirectional Current I B| nodeM2[Stator Phase B Winding]
    nodeM1 --> nodeM3[Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor Rotor]
    nodeM2 --> nodeM3
    nodeM3 -->|Mechanical Step Output| nodeOut1[Load Shaft Incremental Rotation]
    nodeM3 -->|Back EMF Feedback| nodeP4
    nodeDC1[DC Supply V DC] --> nodeP4
    nodeOS1[Oscillator Clock Source] --> nodeP2
```

**Explanation of block-level functional flow:**

- **Pulse Generator / Controller:** Generates the digital pulse train whose frequency determines the motor speed and whose count determines the angular displacement.
- **Microcontroller / FPGA:** Decodes the desired speed/direction and produces standardized step and direction logic signals.
- **Translator (Ring Counter):** Converts the step/direction logic into the proper four-step commutation sequence (A → B → Ā → B̄).
- **Power Driver Stage (Chopper / H-bridge):** Amplifies the logic signals to high-current levels using PWM chopper regulation to limit winding current to a safe value.
- **Stator Phase A and Phase B Windings:** The two-phase concentrated windings on the stator salient poles.
- **VR Stepper Motor Rotor:** The salient soft-iron rotor, which rotates in discrete steps to seek the minimum-reluctance position.
- **DC Supply ($V_{DC}$):** Provides the electrical power to the drive stage.
- **Oscillator:** Provides the time base for pulse generation.

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Step-by-Step VR Motor Commutation

The following Mermaid block illustrates the four-step commutation sequence (one full electrical cycle) of a two-phase VR stepper motor.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CTRL as Controller
    participant DRV as Driver
    participant PA as Phase A Winding
    participant PB as Phase B Winding
    participant ROT as Rotor Position
    CTRL->>DRV: Pulse 1, Direction CW
    DRV->>PA: Energize Phase A with Positive Current
    DRV->>PB: Phase B OFF
    PA->>ROT: Rotor Rotates to Tooth Align with Pole A Plus
    ROT-->>CTRL: Shaft at Angle 0 degrees
    CTRL->>DRV: Pulse 2, Direction CW
    DRV->>PA: Phase A OFF
    DRV->>PB: Energize Phase B with Positive Current
    PB->>ROT: Rotor Rotates by Step Angle Beta
    ROT-->>CTRL: Shaft at Angle Beta degrees
    CTRL->>DRV: Pulse 3, Direction CW
    DRV->>PA: Energize Phase A with Negative Current
    DRV->>PB: Phase B OFF
    PA->>ROT: Rotor Rotates by Step Angle Beta
    ROT-->>CTRL: Shaft at Angle 2 Beta degrees
    CTRL->>DRV: Pulse 4, Direction CW
    DRV->>PA: Phase A OFF
    DRV->>PB: Energize Phase B with Negative Current
    PB->>ROT: Rotor Rotates by Step Angle Beta
    ROT-->>CTRL: Shaft at Angle 3 Beta degrees
    Note over CTRL,ROT: Four Pulses Complete One Electrical Cycle
    Note over ROT: Total Rotation equals 4 times Beta equals one tooth pitch
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four-pulse sequence constitutes **one complete electrical cycle** of the two-phase VR motor. After these four pulses, the rotor has advanced by exactly **one rotor tooth pitch** ($\alpha_r = 360°/N_r$), and the entire sequence repeats identically. The cumulative rotation over many cycles yields continuous bidirectional motion at the desired speed and direction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define a stepper motor. What is meant by "step angle" in the context of a stepper motor?**

**Model Answer:**

A **stepper motor** is a special electrical machine that converts a sequence of discrete electrical input pulses into a proportional, discrete mechanical angular (or linear) displacement of its shaft. It is a synchronous, brushless machine in which each input pulse causes the shaft to rotate by a precisely defined angle called the **step angle (β)**.

The **step angle** is the **minimum angular displacement** of the rotor produced by a single input pulse. Mathematically, it is the ratio of one full revolution ($360°$) to the number of steps per revolution:

$$
\beta = \frac{360°}{N_{steps}}
$$

For a variable reluctance (VR) stepper motor with $N_s$ stator poles and $N_r$ rotor teeth, the step angle is given by:

$$
\beta = \frac{360° \cdot \vert N_s - N_r \rvert}{N_s \cdot N_r}
$$

Common industrial step angles include **1.8°, 0.9°, 7.5°, and 15°**, with 1.8° (yielding 200 full steps/revolution) being the most widely used in modern CNC and 3D-printer applications. **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Q: List the three main types of stepper motors and state one distinguishing feature of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The three principal types of stepper motors, classified by rotor construction, are:

| # | Type | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** | The rotor is a **passive salient-pole soft-iron structure** with **no permanent magnet and no rotor winding**. Torque is produced entirely by the **variation of air-gap reluctance**. |
| 2 | **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor** | The rotor is a **permanent magnet** (radially or axially magnetized). Torque arises from the **alignment interaction** between the stator field and the rotor magnet field. A significant **detent torque** exists even when the motor is unenergized. |
| 3 | **Hybrid Stepper Motor** | The rotor combines a **permanent magnet with salient teeth**, producing torque from **both reluctance variation and magnet alignment**. This type achieves the **smallest step angles** (0.36° to 5°) and the highest torque density. |

**[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**Q: (a) Explain the basic principle of operation of a Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor with a neat constructional diagram. Derive the expression for the step angle.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Basic Principle of Operation of a VR Stepper Motor:**

A VR stepper motor operates on the fundamental principle that a piece of ferromagnetic material always tries to move to a position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** within a magnetic field, in order to provide a low-reluctance path for the magnetic flux. This is also known as the **principle of minimum reluctance**.

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Built from laminated silicon steel, with $N_s$ salient poles projecting inward. Each pole carries a concentrated field winding. The poles are typically grouped into two phases (A and B), with adjacent poles wound to produce opposite magnetic polarities (N–S–N–S).
- **Rotor:** A cylindrical, salient-pole structure made of high-permeability soft iron. It has $N_r$ teeth and **no permanent magnet, no rotor winding**.
- **Air gap:** Non-uniform — minimum when a rotor tooth is aligned with an energized stator pole, maximum when a rotor slot is aligned with a stator pole.

**Operation:**
When a stator phase (say Phase A) is energized with DC current, the magnetic flux passes through the air gap into the rotor. The rotor, being free to rotate, automatically rotates to align its teeth with the energized stator poles — the **minimum-reluctance position** — and locks there due to the reluctance torque. When Phase A is de-energized and Phase B is energized, the rotor rotates by one **step angle $\beta$** to align with the new set of stator poles, seeking the new minimum-reluctance position. By cyclically commutating the phases (A → B → Ā → B̄ → A → …), the rotor advances in discrete steps. Reversing the commutation sequence reverses the rotation direction.

**Derivation of Step Angle:**

The angular pitch between adjacent stator poles is:

$$
\alpha_s = \frac{360°}{N_s}
$$

The angular pitch between adjacent rotor teeth is:

$$
\alpha_r = \frac{360°}{N_r}
$$

When commutation advances from one phase to the next, the rotor must rotate by the difference between the rotor tooth pitch and the stator pole pitch to achieve the new minimum-reluctance alignment:

$$
\beta = \alpha_r - \alpha_s = \frac{360°}{N_r} - \frac{360°}{N_s}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\beta = 360° \cdot \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s \cdot N_r}
$$

Generalizing to handle both cases ($N_s > N_r$ and $N_r > N_s$):

$$
\boxed{\beta = \frac{360° \cdot \vert N_s - N_r \rvert}{N_s \cdot N_r}}
$$

The number of steps per revolution is the inverse:

$$
\boxed{N_{steps} = \frac{N_s \cdot N_r}{\vert N_s - N_r \rvert}}
$$

**Numerical example:** For $N_s = 8$ stator poles and $N_r = 6$ rotor teeth, $\beta = 15°$ and $N_{steps} = 24$. **[7 marks]**

---

**Q: (b) A VR stepper motor has 8 stator poles and 6 rotor teeth. Calculate: (i) the step angle, (ii) the number of steps per revolution, (iii) the shaft speed (in rpm) if the motor is driven at 500 pulses per second, and (iv) the pulse frequency required to achieve a shaft speed of 600 rpm.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N_s = 8$, $N_r = 6$, $f_{pulse} = 500$ Hz (for part iii), $N_{shaft} = 600$ rpm (for part iv).

**Part (i) — Step Angle:**

$$
\beta = \frac{360° \cdot \vert N_s - N_r \rvert}{N_s \cdot N_r} = \frac{360° \cdot \vert 8 - 6 \rvert}{8 \cdot 6} = \frac{360° \cdot 2}{48} = 15°
$$

**The step angle is 15°.** **[2 marks]**

**Part (ii) — Steps per Revolution:**

$$
N_{steps} = \frac{N_s \cdot N_r}{\vert N_s - N_r \rvert} = \frac{8 \cdot 6}{2} = 24
$$

**The motor takes 24 steps per complete revolution.** **[1 mark]**

**Part (iii) — Shaft Speed at 500 pulses/sec:**

Each pulse advances the shaft by $\beta = 15°$. Therefore, the angular speed in degrees per second is:

$$
\omega_{deg} = f_{pulse} \cdot \beta = 500 \times 15° = 7500°/\text{sec}
$$

Converting to revolutions per second:

$$
n_{rev/s} = \frac{7500}{360} = 20.833 \text{ rev/s}
$$

Converting to revolutions per minute:

$$
N_{shaft} = 20.833 \times 60 = 1250 \text{ rpm}
$$

**The shaft speed is 1250 rpm.** **[2 marks]**

**Part (iv) — Pulse Frequency for 600 rpm:**

$$
N_{shaft} = 600 \text{ rpm} = \frac{600}{60} = 10 \text{ rev/s}
$$

Since 24 pulses are required per revolution, the pulse frequency is:

$$
f_{pulse} = N_{steps} \times n_{rev/s} = 24 \times 10 = 240 \text{ Hz}
$$

**The required pulse frequency is 240 Hz.** **[2 marks]**

**Final Answers:** (i) $\beta = 15°$; (ii) $N_{steps} = 24$; (iii) $N_{shaft} = 1250$ rpm; (iv) $f_{pulse} = 240$ Hz.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — 14 Marks

**Q: (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a closed-loop stepper motor drive system. Compare the open-loop and closed-loop configurations.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram Explanation:**

A closed-loop stepper motor drive system augments the open-loop configuration (pulse generator → translator → power driver → motor) with a **feedback loop** containing a **rotary encoder or resolver** mounted on the motor shaft. The encoder produces two pulse trains (A and B channels, in quadrature) whose count and phase relationship indicate the actual shaft position and direction. The controller compares the **reference position** (cumulative input pulses) with the **actual position** (from encoder feedback) and computes a position error. This error is used to:
1. Detect missed steps (stall detection).
2. Apply corrective action through the drive electronics.
3. Maintain synchronization even under transient load disturbances.

The key advantage is that the system **never loses a step silently** — if the load torque exceeds the available torque, the controller detects the discrepancy, reduces the stepping rate, or triggers an alarm.

**Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Comparison:**

| Parameter | **Open-Loop** | **Closed-Loop** |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback sensor | **Not required** | **Encoder/resolver required** |
| Cost | **Lower** | Higher (sensor + interface electronics) |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (control algorithm) |
| Step-loss detection | **Not possible** | Possible (error signal) |
| Maximum usable torque | Limited to ~50% of pull-out (safety margin) | Can approach ~90% of pull-out |
| High-speed capability | Limited (must avoid resonance) | Improved (active damping) |
| Energy efficiency | Lower (high safety margin) | Higher (optimal current control) |
| Reliability | High (no sensor to fail) | Moderate (sensor failure = system failure) |
| Typical application | 3D printers, low-cost CNC, textile machines | Industrial CNC, robotics, machine tools |

**[7 marks]**

---

**Q: (b) A three-phase VR stepper motor has 12 stator poles and 8 rotor teeth. Calculate the step angle, the number of steps per revolution, and the resolution of the motor in bits per revolution. If the motor is operated in half-step mode, what is the effective step angle?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N_s = 12$ stator poles, $N_r = 8$ rotor teeth, $m = 3$ (three-phase system), half-step mode operation required.

**Part 1 — Full-Step Step Angle:**

$$
\beta_{full} = \frac{360° \cdot \vert N_s - N_r \rvert}{N_s \cdot N_r} = \frac{360° \cdot \vert 12 - 8 \rvert}{12 \cdot 8} = \frac{360° \cdot 4}{96} = 15°
$$

**The full-step step angle is 15°.** **[2 marks]**

**Part 2 — Number of Steps per Revolution:**

$$
N_{steps} = \frac{N_s \cdot N_r}{\vert N_s - N_r \rvert} = \frac{12 \cdot 8}{4} = 24 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**The motor has 24 full steps per revolution.** **[1 mark]**

**Part 3 — Resolution in Bits per Revolution:**

The resolution in bits is defined as:

$$
n_{bits} = \log_2(N_{steps}) = \log_2(24)
$$

Since $2^4 = 16$ and $2^5 = 32$, the motor resolves to between 4 and 5 bits. Specifically:

$$
n_{bits} = \frac{\log_{10}(24)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{1.3802}{0.3010} \approx 4.585 \text{ bits/rev}
$$

**The resolution is approximately 4.585 bits per revolution.** **[2 marks]**

**Part 4 — Effective Step Angle in Half-Step Mode:**

In half-step mode, both phases are energized simultaneously between the full-step commutation events, creating an intermediate equilibrium position. This effectively **doubles** the number of steps per revolution:

$$
N_{half} = 2 \times N_{steps} = 2 \times 24 = 48 \text{ half-steps/rev}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\beta_{half} = \frac{\beta_{full}}{2} = \frac{15°}{2} = 7.5°
$$

**The effective step angle in half-step mode is 7.5°.** **[2 marks]**

**Final Answers:** $\beta_{full} = 15°$, $N_{steps} = 24$, $n_{bits} \approx 4.585$ bits/rev, $\beta_{half} = 7.5°$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Stepper Motor Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value signs in the step angle formula** (Equation 2). If $N_r > N_s$, the unsimplified form $\beta = 360°(N_s - N_r)/(N_s \cdot N_r)$ yields a *negative* step angle — physically impossible. Always write $\vert N_s - N_r \rvert$ in the formula. **−1 to −2 marks if missed.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $N_s$ and $N_r$** — $N_s$ = number of *stator* salient poles, $N_r$ = number of *rotor* teeth. Many students interchange them. Verify by checking the unit consistency: $N_s \cdot N_r$ is dimensionless, so $360°/N_{steps}$ has the correct dimension of degrees. **−2 marks if interchanged.**
>
> 3. **Confusing "step angle" with "tooth pitch."** The tooth pitch is $360°/N_r$, which is the *cumulative* rotation per *complete* electrical cycle (4 pulses for a 2-phase motor). The step angle is one-quarter of the tooth pitch for a 2-phase motor. **−1 mark per confusion.**
>
> 4. **In rpm calculation, forgetting to convert rev/s to rpm** by multiplying by 60. A common silent error. **−1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Stating that VR stepper motors have detent torque** — they do *not*. Only PM and hybrid stepper motors have detent torque in the unenergized state. **−1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Drawing the rotor with a permanent magnet or winding** in a VR stepper motor diagram — this is a constructional error. The VR rotor is *passive* and has neither. **−1 mark.**
>
> 7. **Confusing pull-in torque (start/stop region) with pull-out torque (running/slewing region).** Pull-in is the maximum torque at which the motor can start, stop, or reverse *synchronously* at a given stepping rate. Pull-out is the maximum torque at which the motor can *continue running* at a given stepping rate. Always lower than pull-out. **−1 to −2 marks if confused.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 1 — Stepper Motors**

- **Definition:** Stepper motor = brushless synchronous machine that converts discrete electrical pulses into proportional discrete mechanical shaft rotation.

- **Open-loop positioning equation:** $\theta_{shaft} = N_{pulses} \cdot \beta$ — no feedback sensor required for normal operation.

- **Three types:** (1) **Variable Reluctance (VR)** — passive salient-iron rotor, no magnet; (2) **Permanent Magnet (PM)** — magnet rotor; (3) **Hybrid** — magnet + salient teeth, smallest step angles.

- **VR step angle formula (MUST memorize):** $\beta = \dfrac{360° \cdot \vert N_s - N_r \rvert}{N_s \cdot N_r}$

- **Steps per revolution:** $N_{steps} = \dfrac{N_s \cdot N_r}{\vert N_s - N_r \rvert}$

- **Verification check:** $N_{steps} \times \beta = 360°$ ✓

- **Operating principle of VR motor:** Rotor seeks the **minimum-reluctance** (maximum-inductance) position with the energized stator pole.

- **Torque-current relationship for VR motor:** $T_e \propto i^{2}$ — *unidirectional* torque regardless of current direction (a defining property of reluctance machines).

- **Torque-current relationship for PM/Hybrid motor:** $T_e \propto i$ — direction-sensitive.

- **Two-phase VR commutation sequence (CW rotation):** A → B → Ā → B̄ → repeat.

- **Direction reversal:** Reverse the commutation sequence: A → B̄ → Ā → B → repeat.

- **Synchronous pulse frequency for shaft speed $N$ rpm:**
$$
f_{pulse} = \frac{N_{steps} \times N}{60}
$$

- **Rotor rotation per complete electrical cycle (2-phase motor):** Equal to **one rotor tooth pitch** = $360°/N_r$ — i.e., $4 \times \beta$ for a 2-phase VR motor.

- **VR motor advantages:** No detent torque, no demagnetization risk, simple/cheap construction, high-speed capable (no eddy-current losses from magnets).

- **VR motor disadvantages:** Lower torque density, no holding torque when unenergized, larger step angles (typically 7.5° to 30°) than hybrid motors.

- **Detent torque** exists in PM and hybrid stepper motors — VR motors have **zero detent torque**.

- **Common industrial step angle standards:** 0.36°, 0.72°, 1.8° (most common, 200 steps/rev), 7.5°, 15°, 30°, 45°, 90°.

- **Microstepping:** Sub-divides each full step into N microsteps (typically N = 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256) using sinusoidal current profiling. A 1.8° motor with 256× microstepping yields **51,200 microsteps/rev** (≈ 0.007° per microstep).

- **Electrical and mechanical time constants** $\tau_e = L/R$ and $\tau_m = J/(d\omega/dT)$ determine the **maximum stepping rate** $f_{max}$ and the **resonance frequency** $f_{res}$ of the motor-load system — critical for high-speed drive design.

- **KTU 2024 weightage expectation:** Step angle derivation, VR motor constructional details, numerical problems on step angle and shaft speed, and the comparative table of motor types are the **guaranteed question types** for ESE Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permanent Magnet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Permanent Magnet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Permanent Magnet (PM)** is a magnetically hard ferromagnetic material that, once magnetized in an external magnetic field, retains a significant, stable, and persistent magnetic flux density at room temperature without the need for continuous excitation current or external energy input. In the context of **Special Electrical Machines**, permanent magnets form the field-producing system (replacing field windings) in machines such as **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM)**, **Brushless DC Motors (BLDC)**, **Stepper Motors**, and **Reluctance-assisted PM machines**.

The performance of a permanent magnet is characterized by the second quadrant of its **B-H hysteresis loop**, called the **demagnetization curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Permanent magnet materials, their classification, magnetic characteristics (B-H curve, demagnetization curve, hysteresis loop), and their role in special electrical machines form a core foundational topic.

### Key Magnetic Quantities of a Permanent Magnet

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Magnetic Flux Density | $B$ | Density of magnetic lines of force in the material | Tesla (T) |
| Magnetic Field Strength | $H$ | Magneto-motive force per unit length | Ampere/m (A/m) |
| Remanence (Residual Flux Density) | $B_r$ | Flux density retained after $H$ returns to zero | Tesla (T) |
| Coercivity | $H_c$ | Reverse field required to reduce $B$ to zero | A/m |
| Relative Permeability | $\mu_r$ | Ratio of $\mu$ to $\mu_0$ | Dimensionless |
| Energy Product | $(BH)_{max}$ | Maximum energy stored per unit volume | kJ/m³ |
| Curie Temperature | $T_c$ | Temperature above which PM loses magnetism | °C |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Magnetic Battery"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of a **permanent magnet like a rechargeable magnetic battery** that has been fully charged and never needs recharging. Just as a battery stores chemical energy and delivers voltage across its terminals, a permanent magnet stores magnetic energy and produces a magnetic field between its North and South poles. The **"voltage"** of this magnetic battery is analogous to **$B_r$ (Remanence)** — how much flux it holds. The **"internal resistance against demagnetization"** is **$H_c$ (Coercivity)** — how stubbornly it refuses to lose its magnetism. A good PM (like **NdFeB**) is like a high-capacity battery: high energy storage, long-lasting, and resistant to external disturbances.

A **wound field system** in a conventional DC motor, by contrast, is like powering an electromagnet continuously — you must keep supplying current to maintain the field. A PM replaces this with a *self-contained* magnetic source — a major reason why **PMSMs and BLDCs are compact, efficient, and maintenance-free**.

## 1.3 Why Permanent Magnets in Special Machines?

- **Higher efficiency** (no field copper losses — no $I^2R$ in field winding).
- **Higher power density** (smaller frame size for the same output).
- **Better dynamic response** (no field time constant).
- **Compact rotor construction** — critical in servo and aerospace applications.
- **Elimination of slip rings and brushes** in some configurations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The B-H Hysteresis Loop — Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

When a virgin ferromagnetic material is subjected to a cyclic magnetizing force $H$, the flux density $B$ does **not** retrace its path. This lag is called **hysteresis** (Greek: *hysterein* = to lag behind).

**Step 1 — Initial Magnetization Curve:**
Starting from the demagnetized state ($H=0$, $B=0$), as $H$ is increased, $B$ follows the *initial magnetization curve* and saturates at $B_s$ for large $H$.

**Step 2 — Remanence Point ($B_r$):**
When $H$ is reduced back to zero, $B$ does **not** return to zero. It settles at a positive value $B_r$. This is the **Remanence** — the "leftover" magnetism.

**Step 3 — Coercivity Point ($-H_c$):**
To bring $B$ back to zero, a **reverse** magnetic field $-H_c$ must be applied. This reverse field magnitude is **Coercivity** — a measure of resistance to demagnetization.

**Step 4 — Full Loop:**
Increasing $H$ further in the reverse direction drives $B$ to $-B_s$. The complete trace $B(H)$ forms a closed loop called the **hysteresis loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in PMs:** The *larger the area enclosed by the hysteresis loop*, the "harder" the magnetic material — meaning it retains magnetism strongly and resists demagnetization. This is exactly what we want in a PM for electrical machines.

## 2.2 The Demagnetization Curve (Second Quadrant)

The portion of the hysteresis loop in the **second quadrant** ($H < 0$, $B > 0$) is the **demagnetization curve** — the single most important characteristic for PM design.

```
B
│
B_r ●
│    ╲
│     ╲
│      ╲
│       ╲___________
│
└────────────────────── H
        0       -H_c
```

The **operating point** of the magnet in a magnetic circuit lies somewhere on this curve, determined by the **permeance coefficient** (load line).

## 2.3 The Permeance Line (Load Line)

When a magnet of length $L_m$ is placed in a magnetic circuit with air gap length $L_g$, the **permeance coefficient** $P_c$ (also called *load line slope*) is:

$$P_c = \frac{B_m}{H_m} = -\mu_0 \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g}$$

> **Note:** The negative sign is convention because $H$ is in the negative (demagnetizing) region.

The **operating point** is the intersection of this straight line (passing through origin) with the demagnetization curve. This determines the actual working flux density $B_m$ in service.

## 2.4 Energy Product $(BH)_{max}$ — Figure of Merit

The magnetic energy stored per unit volume of a magnet at any operating point is:

$$w = \frac{1}{2} B \cdot H \quad [\text{J/m}^3]$$

The **maximum** of this product on the demagnetization curve is called $(BH)_{max}$ and represents the *maximum useful energy the magnet can deliver* to an external air gap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A PM with **higher $(BH)_{max}$** can deliver more energy from a **smaller volume** — which is why modern rare-earth magnets (NdFeB) revolutionized motor design.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Remanence | $B_r$ | Flux density at $H=0$ | T |
| Coercivity | $H_c$ | Reverse field to reduce $B$ to zero | A/m |
| Energy Density | $w = \frac{1}{2} B \cdot H$ | Energy per unit volume | J/m³ |
| Maximum Energy Product | $(BH)_{max}$ | Best figure of merit | kJ/m³ |
| Permeance Coefficient | $P_c = \dfrac{\mu_0 L_m}{L_g}$ | Load line slope | — |
| Operating $B$ (approx) | $B_m = P_c \cdot H_m$ | Working point flux density | T |
| Recoil Permeability | $\mu_r = \dfrac{\Delta B}{\Delta H}$ (on recoil line) | Slope of minor loop | H/m |
| Curie Temperature | $T_c$ | Limit for stable magnetism | °C |
| Flux per pole | $\Phi = B_m \cdot A_m$ | Useful flux from magnet | Wb |
| MMF of magnet | $\mathcal{F}_m = H_m \cdot L_m$ | Equivalent source MMF | A-turns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The recoil line is crucial in PM machines because during operation, the magnet experiences a **demagnetizing armature reaction**. If the operating point drops below the "knee" of the B-H curve, the magnet is **irreversibly demagnetized**. Designers must ensure the **minimum operating point** lies on or above the recoil line.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electric Vehicle Traction Motors:** Use **NdFeB** magnets for highest torque density.
- **Wind Turbine Generators (PMSG):** Use **NdFeB** for high efficiency direct-drive generators.
- **Servo Motors (CNC, Robotics):** **SmCo** magnets offer high-temperature stability.
- **Hard Disk Drives / Sensors:** Use **hard ferrites** for cost-sensitive applications.
- **MRI Machines:** Use **NdFeB** in compact superconducting hybrid systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored per Unit Volume in a Permanent Magnet

### Starting Principle

Energy density in any magnetic field is given by the volume integral of $\frac{1}{2} B \cdot H$:

$$w = \frac{1}{2} \int B \cdot H \, dV$$

For a uniform field inside a magnet of volume $V_m$, this becomes:

$$w = \frac{1}{2} B \cdot H \quad \text{(per unit volume)}$$

### Analytical Setup

Consider a permanent magnet of length $L_m$ and cross-sectional area $A_m$, magnetized along its axis. Place it in an external magnetic circuit forming an air gap of length $L_g$ and area $A_g$.

**Step 1 — Apply Ampere's Law around the magnetic circuit:**

By Ampere's circuital law (no external current sources since this is a PM):

$$\oint H \cdot dl = 0$$

Breaking the integral across magnet and air gap:

$$H_m \cdot L_m + H_g \cdot L_g = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $H_g$ in terms of $H_m$:**

$$H_g = -H_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g}$$

**Step 3 — Use boundary condition for normal $B$:**

Continuity of normal flux at magnet-air gap interface:

$$B_m = B_g = \frac{\Phi}{A_m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply $B = \mu_0 H$ in the air gap:**

$$B_g = \mu_0 H_g = -\mu_0 H_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply $B$ and $H$ to get energy density:**

\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{1}{2} B_m \cdot H_m \\
w &= \frac{1}{2} \left( -\mu_0 H_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g} \right) \cdot H_m \\
w &= -\frac{\mu_0 L_m}{2 L_g} \cdot H_m^2
\end{aligned}

Since $H_m$ is negative in the demagnetization region, $w$ is positive (as required for stored energy).

**Step 6 — Substitute $B_m = \mu_0 P_c H_m$ where $P_c = L_m / L_g$:**

$$w = \frac{1}{2} P_c \cdot \mu_0 \cdot H_m^2 = \frac{B_m \cdot H_m}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum energy product condition:**

To maximize $w = \frac{1}{2} B_m H_m$, we use the constraint that the operating point lies on the demagnetization curve. Differentiating and setting $\frac{dw}{dH} = 0$ leads to the **optimal operating point** where:

$$B_{m,opt} = \frac{B_r}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad H_{m,opt} = \frac{H_c}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$(BH)_{max} = \frac{B_r \cdot H_c}{4}$$

This gives the **maximum theoretical energy product**, which a perfectly linear demagnetization curve would achieve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The **larger the product $B_r \cdot H_c$**, the more energy the magnet stores. This is why materials scientists race to develop magnets with both *high remanence* AND *high coercivity*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Operating Point Using Permeance Line

### Given

A NdFeB magnet with $B_r = 1.2$ T, $H_c = -900$ kA/m, length $L_m = 5$ mm, in a circuit with air gap $L_g = 2$ mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the permeance coefficient:**

\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{L_m}{L_g} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{2 \text{ mm}} = 2.5
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Linear approximation of demagnetization curve:**

Assuming linear demagnetization, the operating line in the second quadrant is:

$$B_m = \mu_0 P_c (-H_m) + \text{offset from } B_r$$

For a straight-line approximation between $(0, B_r)$ and $(H_c, 0)$:

$$B_m = B_r \left(1 + \frac{\mu_0 H_m}{B_r / H_c}\right) = B_r \left(1 - \frac{H_m}{H_c}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve simultaneous equations:**

\begin{aligned}
B_m &= \mu_0 P_c (-H_m) \quad \text{(permeance line through origin)} \\
B_m &= B_r \left(1 - \frac{H_m}{H_c}\right) \quad \text{(demag curve approx.)}
\end{aligned}

Equating both:

$$\mu_0 P_c (-H_m) = B_r - B_r \frac{H_m}{H_c}$$

Solving for $H_m$:

\begin{aligned}
-\mu_0 P_c H_m + \frac{B_r}{H_c} H_m &= B_r \\
H_m \left( \frac{B_r}{H_c} - \mu_0 P_c \right) &= B_r \\
H_m &= \frac{B_r}{\dfrac{B_r}{H_c} - \mu_0 P_c}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Plug numbers:**

\begin{aligned}
H_m &= \frac{1.2}{\dfrac{1.2}{9 \times 10^5} - 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2.5} \\
H_m &= \frac{1.2}{1.333 \times 10^{-6} - 3.142 \times 10^{-6}} \\
H_m &= \frac{1.2}{-1.809 \times 10^{-6}} \\
H_m &\approx -663 \text{ kA/m}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Compute $B_m$:**

\begin{aligned}
B_m &= -\mu_0 P_c H_m \\
B_m &= -4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2.5 \times (-6.63 \times 10^5) \\
B_m &= 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2.5 \times 6.63 \times 10^5 \\
B_m &\approx 2.083 \text{ T} \quad \text{(theoretical, before clipping at } B_r)
\end{aligned}

Since this exceeds $B_r$, the actual $B_m$ is **clipped** to be on the curve:

$$B_m \approx 0.85 \cdot B_r \approx 1.02 \text{ T (practical working point)}$$

This shows the magnet is properly utilized without saturation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Force Between Two PM Poles

For two opposing magnetic poles of area $A$ separated by air gap $g$, the attractive force is:

$$F = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$$

**Derivation (energy method):**

Energy in the air gap: $W = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot (A \cdot g)$

Differentiate w.r.t. gap length $g$ to find force (tendency to reduce gap):

\begin{aligned}
F &= -\frac{dW}{dg} = -\frac{d}{dg}\left( \frac{B^2 A g}{2 \mu_0} \right) \\
F &= -\frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}
\end{aligned}

The negative sign indicates attraction. Magnitude:

$$\boxed{F = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This is the fundamental force equation used to compute **cogging torque** and **holding torque** in PM machines — both of which directly affect performance in BLDC and PMSM drives.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Coercivity" of a permanent magnet. Why is high coercivity desirable in PM materials used in electrical machines?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Coercivity ($H_c$) of a permanent magnet is the value of the reverse (demagnetizing) magnetic field strength required to reduce the residual magnetic flux density $B$ to zero, when the magnet is subjected to a cyclic magnetic field.

$$\text{Coercivity: } H_c = H \quad \text{at} \quad B = 0$$

**Why desirable (1 mark):** A high coercivity indicates the magnet's strong resistance to demagnetization by external stray fields, armature reaction, and temperature variations. In running machines, especially during short-circuit or overload conditions, a strong demagnetizing field appears across the magnet. High $H_c$ ensures the magnet does not get permanently demagnetized, ensuring reliability and long life of the machine.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by "Energy Product" of a permanent magnet? What does $(BH)_{max}$ signify?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Energy Product Definition (2 marks):** Energy product is the product of the magnetic flux density $B$ and the magnetic field strength $H$ at any point on the demagnetization curve. It represents the magnetic energy stored per unit volume of the magnet:

$$w = \frac{1}{2} B \cdot H \quad [\text{J/m}^3]$$

**Significance of $(BH)_{max}$ (1 mark):** $(BH)_{max}$ is the **maximum value** of the energy product, and it represents the *figure of merit* of a permanent magnet material. A higher $(BH)_{max}$ indicates that the magnet can deliver more magnetic energy from a smaller volume. This directly enables compact, high-power-density machines.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With the help of a B-H hysteresis loop, explain the terms **Remanence ($B_r$)**, **Coercivity ($H_c$)**, and **demagnetization curve**. Briefly describe the significance of the second quadrant of the loop for permanent magnet design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the four major classes of permanent magnet materials (AlNiCo, Hard Ferrite, SmCo, NdFeB) on the basis of $B_r$, $H_c$, $(BH)_{max}$, Curie temperature, and cost. State two typical applications of each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — B-H Hysteresis Loop (2 marks):**

The B-H hysteresis loop is a closed curve showing the relationship between magnetic flux density $B$ and applied magnetic field strength $H$ for a ferromagnetic material. The loop is obtained by cycling $H$ between large positive and negative values.

**Step 2 — Defining Remanence (1 mark):**

When a magnetizing field $H$ is applied to a virgin material, $B$ rises along the initial magnetization curve. When $H$ is reduced to zero, $B$ does **not** return to zero. The residual value of $B$ at $H = 0$ is the **Remanence** $B_r$, measured in Tesla.

**Step 3 — Defining Coercivity (1 mark):**

To bring $B$ back to zero, a reverse demagnetizing field is applied. The magnitude of $H$ required to reduce $B$ to zero is called the **Coercivity** $H_c$, measured in A/m.

**Step 4 — Demagnetization Curve (1.5 marks):**

The portion of the hysteresis loop lying in the second quadrant (where $H$ is negative but $B$ is positive) is called the **demagnetization curve**. This is the working region for permanent magnets.

**Step 5 — Significance for PM Design (1.5 marks):**

The second quadrant is critical because:
- It determines the **operating point** of the magnet in service.
- The **area** above the demag curve (and bounded by the B and H axes) represents the energy density available.
- $(BH)_{max}$ is found in this quadrant.
- Recoil behavior and irreversible demagnetization are predicted from this region.
- Designers ensure the operating point does not drop below the **knee** of the curve.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Property | AlNiCo | Hard Ferrite | SmCo | NdFeB |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $B_r$ (T) | 0.7 - 1.25 | 0.2 - 0.4 | 0.8 - 1.1 | 1.0 - 1.4 |
| $H_c$ (kA/m) | 50 - 160 | 150 - 300 | 600 - 2000 | 800 - 2400 |
| $(BH)_{max}$ (kJ/m³) | 10 - 88 | 8 - 36 | 120 - 240 | 200 - 440 |
| Curie Temp (°C) | 700 - 850 | 450 - 460 | 700 - 850 | 310 - 400 |
| Cost | Moderate | Lowest | Highest | Moderate-High |
| Corrosion Resist. | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (needs coating) |

**Applications (2 marks — 0.5 marks per material):**

- **AlNiCo:** Watt-hour meters, guitar pickups, sensors, high-temperature applications.
- **Hard Ferrite:** Speakers, refrigerator magnets, low-cost DC motors, toys.
- **SmCo:** Servo motors, aerospace actuators, high-temperature PM generators, military systems.
- **NdFeB:** EV traction motors, wind turbine generators (PMSG), hard disk drives, robotics, MRI.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for the **permeance coefficient** of a permanent magnet placed in a magnetic circuit with an air gap. Explain the **load line** method to find the operating point of the magnet on the demagnetization curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A NdFeB magnet has $B_r = 1.25$ T and $H_c = -900$ kA/m. The magnet length is $L_m = 6$ mm and the air gap length is $L_g = 1.5$ mm. Assuming a linear demagnetization curve, find the **operating point** $(B_m, H_m)$ and the **energy product** at this point. Compare it with $(BH)_{max}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Magnetic Circuit Setup (1 mark):**

Consider a PM of length $L_m$ and cross-sectional area $A_m$ in a magnetic circuit with an air gap of length $L_g$ and area $A_g$. A return path (soft iron yoke) closes the flux loop.

**Step 2 — Apply Ampere's Law (2 marks):**

Since no external currents exist in the magnetic circuit, $\oint H \cdot dl = 0$:

$$H_m L_m + H_g L_g = 0$$

$$H_g = -H_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g}$$

**Step 3 — Use Continuity of Flux (1 mark):**

$$B_m A_m = B_g A_g \implies B_m \approx B_g \quad (\text{if } A_m \approx A_g)$$

**Step 4 — Apply $B = \mu_0 H$ in the Air Gap (1 mark):**

$$B_m = \mu_0 H_g = -\mu_0 H_m \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g}$$

**Step 5 — Permeance Coefficient (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{P_c = -\frac{B_m}{\mu_0 H_m} = \frac{L_m}{L_g}}$$

**Step 6 — Load Line Method (1 mark):**

The equation $B_m = -\mu_0 P_c H_m$ is a straight line passing through the origin with slope $-\mu_0 P_c$ in the $B$ vs $H$ plane. This line, called the **permeance line** or **load line**, is drawn on the demagnetization curve. Their **intersection** is the **operating point** of the magnet, giving the working values of $B_m$ and $H_m$.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Given Data (0.5 marks):**
- $B_r = 1.25$ T
- $H_c = -900 \times 10^3$ A/m
- $L_m = 6 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $L_g = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Step 2 — Permeance Coefficient (1 mark):**

$$P_c = \frac{L_m}{L_g} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4$$

**Step 3 — Linear Demagnetization Equation (1 mark):**

$$B_m = B_r \left( 1 - \frac{H_m}{H_c} \right) = 1.25 \left( 1 - \frac{H_m}{-9 \times 10^5} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Permeance Line Equation (1 mark):**

$$B_m = -\mu_0 P_c H_m = -4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4 \times H_m = -5.027 \times 10^{-6} H_m$$

**Step 5 — Equate and Solve for $H_m$ (2 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
-5.027 \times 10^{-6} H_m &= 1.25 \left( 1 + \frac{H_m}{9 \times 10^5} \right) \\
-5.027 \times 10^{-6} H_m &= 1.25 + 1.389 \times 10^{-6} H_m \\
-5.027 \times 10^{-6} H_m - 1.389 \times 10^{-6} H_m &= 1.25 \\
-6.416 \times 10^{-6} H_m &= 1.25 \\
H_m &= -194.8 \text{ kA/m}
\end{aligned}

**Step 6 — Compute $B_m$ (0.5 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
B_m &= -5.027 \times 10^{-6} \times (-194.8 \times 10^3) \\
B_m &= 0.979 \text{ T}
\end{aligned}

**Step 7 — Operating Energy Product (0.5 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
(BH)_{op} &= B_m \cdot H_m \\
&= 0.979 \times 194.8 \times 10^3 \\
&= 190.7 \text{ kJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}

**Step 8 — Compare with $(BH)_{max}$ (0.5 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
(BH)_{max,\text{theoretical}} &= \frac{B_r \cdot H_c}{4} \\
&= \frac{1.25 \times 900 \times 10^3}{4} \\
&= 281.25 \text{ kJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}

The operating energy product (190.7 kJ/m³) is **less than** the theoretical maximum (281.25 kJ/m³). To approach $(BH)_{max}$, the permeance coefficient should be redesigned so the operating point moves closer to $(B_r/2, H_c/2)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in PM Questions (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (–2 marks):** Students often write $H_c$ as a *positive* number. Always state clearly: $H_c$ is the magnitude; the magnet's internal $H$ is **negative** in the demagnetization region.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting $B = \mu_0 H$ in the air gap (–1 mark):** A common mistake is to equate $B$ and $H$ inside the magnet the same way. Remember: inside the magnet, $B = \mu_0(H + M)$, not $\mu_0 H$.
> 
> 3. **Operating point below the knee (–1 mark):** If your calculated $H_m$ or $B_m$ leads to a point below the "knee" of the curve, the magnet undergoes **irreversible demagnetization**. Always state this implication in your answer.
> 
> 4. **Units mismatch (–1 mark):** Use SI units consistently. Convert mm to m, kA/m to A/m when using $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
> 
> 5. **Confusing $(BH)_{max}$ with stored energy (–1 mark):** $(BH)_{max}$ is energy per unit **volume**, not total energy. Multiply by magnet volume for total energy.
> 
> 6. **Recoil vs Demagnetization curve (–1 mark):** Don't confuse the **major demagnetization curve** with the **recoil line**. The recoil line is a minor loop for transient disturbances.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Permanent Magnets**

- **Permanent Magnet:** A magnetically hard material that retains magnetism without external excitation. Forms the field system in PMSM, BLDC, PM stepper, and PMSG.

- **Three Key Magnetic Quantities:** $B_r$ (Remanence), $H_c$ (Coercivity), and $(BH)_{max}$ (Maximum Energy Product).

- **Demagnetization Curve:** Second quadrant of the B-H hysteresis loop. This is the **working region** for PMs.

- **Energy Density Formula:** $w = \frac{1}{2} B \cdot H$, with $(BH)_{max}$ as the **figure of merit** for energy storage.

- **Permeance Coefficient:** $P_c = L_m / L_g$. Determines the **load line slope** and hence the operating point.

- **Operating Point:** Intersection of the load line and the demagnetization curve.

- **Recoil Line:** Minor hysteresis loop, used to handle armature reaction demagnetization.

- **Curie Temperature:** Critical limit beyond which a PM **irreversibly loses magnetism**. Must be well above machine operating temperature.

- **Material Hierarchy (best to lowest $(BH)_{max}$):** NdFeB > SmCo > AlNiCo > Hard Ferrite.

- **NdFeB:** Best energy product, used in EV motors, wind generators, robotics. **Limitation:** Sensitive to heat and corrosion.

- **SmCo:** Best high-temperature performance, used in aerospace and military.

- **AlNiCo:** Best thermal stability, used in instruments and sensors.

- **Hard Ferrite:** Cheapest, used in speakers, low-cost motors, and toys.

- **Design Rule:** Always keep the operating point **above the knee** of the demagnetization curve to avoid irreversible demagnetization.

- **Comparison Numbers to Memorize (KTU-favorite values):**
  - NdFeB: $B_r \approx 1.0$–$1.4$ T, $(BH)_{max} \approx 200$–$440$ kJ/m³
  - SmCo: $B_r \approx 0.8$–$1.1$ T, $(BH)_{max} \approx 120$–$240$ kJ/m³
  - Hard Ferrite: $B_r \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ T, $(BH)_{max} \approx 8$–$36$ kJ/m³
  - AlNiCo: $B_r \approx 0.7$–$1.25$ T, $(BH)_{max} \approx 10$–$88$ kJ/m³

- **Force between magnet and gap:** $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$ — fundamental in cogging torque and holding force calculations.

- **Self-Demagnetizing Field:** Every magnet has an internal $H$ opposite to its $B$ — the cause of demagnetization in open-circuit conditions.

- **Magnetizing Direction:** Magnets are anisotropic — performance differs along the *preferred* vs *hard* axis. PM machines always use the preferred axis.

- **Irreversible vs Reversible Loss:** If the operating point falls on the recoil line after disturbance, the loss is **reversible**; if it falls below the knee, the loss is **permanent**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Hybrid Stepper Motor — Formal Definition

A **Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)** is a synchronous electromechanical energy conversion device that combines the high torque-to-inertia ratio and detent (cogging) behaviour of the **Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor** with the small step-angle and high resolution of the **Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor**. It is, in essence, a *hybrid* of the two preceding families, and for this reason it is the workhorse of modern precision motion control — CNC machines, 3-D printers, robotics, plotters, and disc drives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** *A hybrid stepper motor is a multi-toothed, salient-pole, two-stack machine in which the rotor carries a coaxial permanent magnet and is axially split into two cups separated by the magnet. The stator is also multi-toothed and the alignment of stator and rotor teeth is governed by the simultaneous action of permanent-magnet flux and variable-reluctance torque.*

**Standard physical metrics used in industry for hybrid stepper motors:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Step angle ($\beta$) | **1.8°** (200 steps/rev) or **0.9°** (400 steps/rev) |
| Number of rotor teeth ($N_r$) | **50** teeth (for 1.8°) or 100 teeth (for 0.9°) |
| Stator pole count | **8** poles, each with **5** or **6** teeth |
| Phase count ($m$) | **2 or 4** |
| Rated current per phase | **0.5 A – 5 A** |
| Holding torque range | **0.1 Nm – 25 Nm** |
| Ambient operating temperature | **-20 °C to +50 °C** |
| Insulation class | **Class B (130 °C)** or **Class F (155 °C)** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Magnetic Compass on a Ratchet

Imagine a child’s toy ratchet. A small permanent-magnet compass needle always wants to rest pointing **North** because of the Earth’s magnetic field (this is your *PM torque / detent torque*). Now suppose you place four small electromagnets — *North, South, North, South* — around the compass. When you switch them on in a sequence, you forcibly drag the needle in 90° jumps (this is the *VR-aligned torque*).

If the compass needle is also made of soft iron with tiny notches/teeth on its ends, you get an even finer snap action because the teeth prefer to sit opposite the notches of the strongest electromagnet. **A hybrid stepper motor is exactly this — a toothed permanent-magnet needle sitting inside a notched, multi-phase electromagnet cage.** Every time you flip the switch, the rotor *jumps* a small fixed angular increment — one *step*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why “hybrid”?** Because the rotor carries a **permanent magnet** (like a PM motor) **and** has **salient teeth** that create **variable reluctance** (like a VR motor). The output torque is the **sum of two torques**: $T_{total} = T_{PM} + T_{VR}$.

## 1.3 Syllabus Outcome Mapping

| CO ID | Course Outcome (abridged) | Mapped to this topic? |
|---|---|---|
| **CO1** | Explain the construction, principle and operation of special electrical machines. | ✅ Fully mapped |
| **CO2** | Analyse torque production and step-angle relationships. | ✅ Fully mapped |
| **CO3** | Select appropriate excitation modes for given application. | ✅ Fully mapped |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Features — Layer by Layer

A hybrid stepper motor has **two identical stator stacks** mounted end-to-end on a common frame, with a **permanent magnet** sandwiched between them. The rotor is similarly split into **two cups**, one aligned with each stator stack, but offset by **half a tooth-pitch** axially.

### 2.1.1 Stator Construction
- **8 (or 12) salient poles** project inward from a laminated ring.
- Each pole carries a **concentrated multi-tooth lamination** (5 or 6 teeth per pole).
- The teeth of the two diametrically opposite poles of the same stack are **in alignment** (in-phase).
- **Diametrically opposite coils are connected in series** to form a single phase winding.
- For an 8-pole, 4-phase motor: poles 1-5 = Phase A, 2-6 = Phase B, 3-7 = Phase C, 4-8 = Phase D.
- Windings are either **monofilar (unifilar)** or **bifilar** — discussed in Section 2.5.

### 2.1.2 Rotor Construction
- **Two soft-iron cups** mounted on a common non-magnetic (or aluminium) shaft, separated by a **cylindrical axially-magnetised permanent magnet** (usually **Samarium-Cobalt** or **NdFeB** for high coercivity).
- Each cup has the **same number of fine teeth** ($N_r$) — typically **50**.
- The two cups are **mechanically displaced by ½ tooth-pitch** along the shaft (i.e. $180^\circ/ N_r$ in terms of arc).
- Because of the axial magnet, the two cups acquire **opposite magnetic polarities** — one cup becomes a North pole, the other South.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ½ tooth-pitch offset is the *secret* of the hybrid stepper.** It guarantees that whenever a stator tooth is aligned with a rotor tooth on the South-cup side, it is **misaligned** on the North-cup side. Switching phases therefore moves the rotor by **¼ tooth-pitch**, giving the famous **1.8° step**.

### 2.1.3 Bearings, Frame, Encoders
- Sleeve or ball bearings at both ends; the rear shaft extension is often used for an **optional incremental encoder** for closed-loop operation.
- Aluminium or die-cast steel frame provides mechanical rigidity and heat dissipation.

## 2.2 Principle of Operation — The "Two-Field" Theory

When phase-A winding is energised, it sets up a magnetic flux across the air-gap of stack-1 (call it the **A-stack**). The PM simultaneously magnetises the rotor cups with opposing polarities. The rotor therefore experiences **two simultaneous restoring forces**:

1. **Permanent-magnet (PM) torque** — the rotor-cup teeth prefer to align with the **same polarity** as the stator's energised pole.
2. **Variable-reluctance (VR) torque** — the magnetic circuit always wants to **minimise reluctance** by maximising the overlap of stator and rotor teeth.

When phase A is OFF and phase B is ON, the magnetic axes shift by **¼ tooth pitch** on each stack, and the rotor rotates by the same increment.

### 2.2.1 Sign of PM and VR Torques — Why the Offset Matters
On the South-cup side, the **PM torque** and the **VR torque are in the same direction** (constructive). On the North-cup side, they are **opposing** (destructive). However, because the North-cup rotor teeth are **half-pitch misaligned**, the VR torque on that side is also reversed — the net result is that **PM and VR torques add up** for the whole rotor. This constructive superposition is the reason hybrid motors deliver **2× to 3× the torque of comparable VR motors**.

## 2.3 Comparison — VR vs. PM vs. Hybrid Stepper

| Feature | Variable Reluctance (VR) | Permanent Magnet (PM) | Hybrid (HY) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotor construction** | Plain salient-pole soft iron | Cylindrical magnet with smooth or toothed surface | Two toothed cups + axial PM |
| **Detent (residual) torque** | None | Yes (cogging at unenergised position) | Yes (highest of the three) |
| **Holding torque** | Lowest | Medium | **Highest** |
| **Step angle range** | 7.5° – 15° | 45° – 90° | **0.9° – 1.8°** (very small) |
| **Step accuracy** | Poor–Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent (±3% non-cumulative)** |
| **Rotor inertia** | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| **Damping / resonance** | Poor | Good (cogging) | **Best** |
| **Bidirectional operation** | Yes | Yes (requires H-bridge) | Yes (requires H-bridge) |
| **Power consumption at standstill** | Zero (no PM) | Zero (PM holds) | Zero (PM holds) |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | **Highest** |
| **Typical applications** | Low-cost printers, flow valves | Clocks, instrument drives | CNC, robotics, 3-D printers |

## 2.4 Modes of Operation — The Four Canonical Modes

| Mode | Phases ON at any instant | Step size | Torque | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **One Phase ON (Wave Drive)** | 1 | 1× $\beta$ | ~70% of rated | Lowest |
| **Two Phase ON (Full-Step)** | 2 | 1× $\beta$ | **100% (rated)** | Highest |
| **Half-Step** | 1 or 2 (alternating) | 0.5× $\beta$ | ~70%–100% varying | Medium |
| **Microstepping** | Sine/cosine weighted | $\beta/10$ to $\beta/256$ | Smooth | Depends |

## 2.5 Monofilar vs. Bifilar Windings

### 2.5.1 Monofilar (Unifilar) Winding
- **One single coil** wound on each stator pole.
- To reverse the polarity of a pole, the *current direction* must be reversed — this requires an **H-bridge driver** (4 switches per phase).
- **Demagnetisation risk**: If the rotor magnet is strong, the stator field must overcome the PM field to switch polarity. This demands a high-magnitude current pulse.

### 2.5.2 Bifilar (Bifilar) Winding
- **Two identical, tightly coupled coils** wound on the same pole, side by side, using a *dual-filar* (two wires in hand) winding technique.
- One coil is the *forward* winding; the other is the *return* winding, but wound in the **opposite magnetic sense**.
- Only **half-wave operation** is possible — the driver simply routes current through *one* coil or the *other* to set the polarity. **No H-bridge needed** — just 2 switches per phase.
- **Disadvantage**: each coil only uses half the slot area (or requires thinner wire for the same total turns), so the copper utilisation is poorer. Also, the two coils are magnetically coupled and exhibit high mutual inductance, leading to a **back-EMF** of twice the self-induced EMF when one is switched off.

| Feature | Monofilar | Bifilar |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver topology** | H-bridge (4 switches/phase) | Half-H (2 switches/phase) |
| **Bidirectional control** | Yes (current reversal) | Yes (select which coil is energised) |
| **Slot fill (copper)** | **Higher** | Lower (two wires share the slot) |
| **Demagnetisation risk** | High current needed to reverse PM | None — opposite coil produces opposite field |
| **Mutual coupling** | None between phases | Strong within a pair |
| **Switching losses** | Lower | Higher (2× stored energy) |
| **Cost** | Driver expensive, motor cheaper | Driver cheaper, motor more expensive |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Never use the vertical bar `\|` symbol inside this table** — it breaks the markdown. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value expressions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_r \cdot m}$ | $\beta$ = step angle, $N_r$ = rotor teeth, $m$ = phases (4 for HY) | degrees | Computing step angle |
| 2 | $N_{steps/rev} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{\beta} = N_r \cdot m$ | Total steps per revolution | dimensionless | Resolution check |
| 3 | $T_{total} = T_{PM} + T_{VR}$ | Total holding torque = PM + VR components | Nm | Torque estimation |
| 4 | $\tau_p = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_r}$ | Tooth pitch (mechanical) | degrees | Lamination design |
| 5 | $\beta = \dfrac{\tau_p}{2} = \dfrac{180^\circ}{N_r}$ | HY step = ½ tooth pitch | degrees | Hybrid step derivation |
| 6 | $N_r = \dfrac{180^\circ}{\beta}$ | Rotor teeth from desired step angle | dimensionless | Design choice |
| 7 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{r \cdot \beta \cdot \pi/180}$ | Max stepping frequency at given shaft speed | Hz | Sizing the driver |
| 8 | $V = L \dfrac{di}{dt} + R \cdot i + e$ | Phase voltage equation | V | Driver design (V, L, R, e = back-EMF) |
| 9 | $e = -K_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF of rotor | V | Speed feedback |
| 10 | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical output power | W | Power rating |
| 11 | $P_{el} = m \cdot V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph} \cdot \cos\phi$ | Electrical input per phase | W | Efficiency calculation |
| 12 | $S = \dfrac{N_{steps}}{rev} \cdot f_{pps}$ | Stepping rate in pulses per second | pps | Controller interface |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Hybrid stepper motors dominate **open-loop digital motion control** because of their **positional repeatability (typically ±5 % of a step, non-cumulative)** without the need for position feedback. In FFF 3-D printers they translate the G-code pulse train from the controller into precise $X$, $Y$, $Z$ and extruder motion. In CNC routers they drive the lead-screws directly. In HPLC pumps and syringe drivers they offer pulse-accurate fluid delivery. The **One-Phase-ON (wave) mode** is specifically used in **low-heat, low-torque** applications such as clock mechanisms, instrument dials, and metering pumps.
