# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Step Angle of a Hybrid Stepper Motor

We will derive the fundamental relation used in **every KTU exam** for step-angle calculation.

### Step A — Define tooth pitch
Let the rotor carry $N_r$ uniformly spaced teeth around its circumference. The angular **tooth pitch** is the angle between two adjacent rotor teeth:

$$
\tau_p = \frac{360^\circ}{N_r}
$$

### Step B — Effect of the two rotor cups
The two rotor cups are mechanically offset by **half a tooth pitch** along the shaft. The axially-magnetised permanent magnet makes the upper cup a *North* pole and the lower cup a *South* pole. The PM magnetises the rotor teeth such that:
- The teeth of cup-1 acquire one polarity (say N at tooth tip),
- The teeth of cup-2 acquire the opposite polarity (S at tooth tip).

### Step C — Energise Phase A
When Phase A is energised, stator teeth of stack-1 (N polarity) attract rotor-cup-1 teeth (S polarity at tip) and repel cup-2 teeth (N polarity at tip). The rotor settles at a position where **stack-1 stator teeth are aligned with cup-1 rotor teeth** and **misaligned with cup-2 rotor teeth**.

### Step D — De-energise A, energise B
The energised pole moves by **¼ tooth pitch** on each stack. The rotor must therefore rotate by the *same* ¼ tooth-pitch to re-establish alignment on stack-1 and a *new alignment* on stack-2:

$$
\beta = \frac{\tau_p}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{360^\circ}{N_r} = \frac{180^\circ}{N_r}
$$

### Step E — Number of phases
For a 4-phase (A, B, C, D) sequential excitation, each electrical cycle produces $m = 4$ steps. The **total steps per revolution** are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{steps/rev} &= m \cdot N_r \\
N_{steps/rev} &= m \cdot \frac{180^\circ}{\beta} \\
\beta &= \frac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step F — Numerical example (KTU board standard)
A 4-phase hybrid stepper has $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth. Calculate the step angle and the resolution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{180^\circ}{N_r} \\
     &= \frac{180^\circ}{50} \\
     &= 3.6^\circ \quad \text{(per stack advance)} \\
\\
\beta_{electrical} &= \frac{\beta}{m} = \frac{3.6^\circ}{2} = 1.8^\circ \\
&\text{or equivalently,} \quad \beta = \frac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r} = \frac{360^\circ}{4 \cdot 50} = 1.8^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
N_{steps/rev} = \frac{360^\circ}{1.8^\circ} = 200 \text{ steps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A standard NEMA 17 hybrid stepper has **50 rotor teeth, 4 phases, 1.8° step, 200 steps/rev**. Memorise this triplet — it is the KTU exam default.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Equation of a Hybrid Stepper

The instantaneous electromagnetic torque in a doubly-salient, PM-excited machine is given by the **co-energy derivative**:

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial W_{co}'(i,\theta)}{\partial \theta} \bigg\vert_{i = const}
$$

For a hybrid stepper with rotor angle $\theta$ (mechanical) measured from the aligned position of phase A, and with two stator stacks offset by half a tooth-pitch, the air-gap permeance per phase is:

$$
\mathcal{P}(\theta) = \mathcal{P}_0 + \mathcal{P}_1 \cos\bigl(N_r \theta\bigr)
$$

where $\mathcal{P}_0$ is the average permeance and $\mathcal{P}_1$ is the permeance variation amplitude. The co-energy stored in the air-gap has two contributions: from the **stator MMF** and from the **PM**:

$$
W_{co}'(\theta, i) = \frac{1}{2} L(i,\theta) \cdot i^2 + \Psi_{PM} \cdot i \cdot f(\theta)
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\theta$ and grouping the two terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e(\theta,i) &= -\frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \;-\; \Psi_{PM} \cdot i \cdot \frac{\partial f(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \\
\\
&\text{Defining:} \quad T_{VR} = -\frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \quad\text{and}\quad T_{PM} = -\Psi_{PM} \cdot i \cdot \frac{\partial f(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \\
\\
&\therefore \quad \boxed{T_{total} = T_{VR} + T_{PM}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **algebraically** confirms the superposition claim in Section 2.2.1.

### Numerical torque check
Given: $N_r = 50$, $i = 2$ A, $\Psi_{PM} = 0.05$ Wb-turns, $\mathcal{P}_1/\mathcal{P}_0 = 0.4$, $L_0 = 10$ mH.

$$
T_{VR,peak} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2}(2)^2 (50)(0.4)(10 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 0.4 \text{ Nm}
$$

$$
T_{PM,peak} = \Psi_{PM} \cdot i \cdot N_r = 0.05 \cdot 2 \cdot 50 = 5.0 \text{ Nm (in same units scale)}
$$

The PM term **dominates** in a well-designed hybrid motor — typically contributing **70 – 80 % of holding torque**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resolution of Half-Step Mode

Half-step mode alternates between *One-Phase-ON* and *Two-Phase-ON* excitations. For each electrical cycle of 4 phase transitions, the half-step sequence gives **8 transitions** instead of 4:

$$
\beta_{half} = \frac{\beta_{full}}{2} = \frac{1.8^\circ}{2} = 0.9^\circ
$$

$$
N_{steps/rev}^{half} = 2 \cdot m \cdot N_r = 400 \text{ steps (for 1.8° motor)}
$$

Effective resolution doubles, but the torque ripple is higher (varies between $T_{1\phi} \approx 0.707 \cdot T_{rated}$ and $T_{2\phi} = T_{rated}$).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Operating Mode

| Application | Recommended Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Clock / instrument dial** | One-Phase-ON (Wave) | Lowest heat dissipation, detent torque holds position |
| **CNC axis drive** | Two-Phase-ON (Full step) | Maximum torque, accurate positioning |
| **3-D printer** | Microstepping (1/16 or 1/32) | Smooth motion, low resonance, low acoustic noise |
| **Valve actuator** | One-Phase-ON (Wave) | Holding torque is enough, low continuous current |
| **Textile / plotter** | Half-step | Resolution needed but low-speed |
| **Camera focus ring** | Microstepping | Ultra-fine angular steps required |

## 4.2 Magnetic Flux Path During One-Phase-ON Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["STATOR POLE-A - Energised - North polarity"] -- "FLUX leaves N-pole" --> G1["AIR GAP 1 - Aligned rotor teeth - Low reluctance"]
    G1 --> RC1["ROTOR CUP 1 - 50 teeth - South polarity at tip"]
    RC1 -- "Flux returns through shaft side" --> SH["SOFT-IRON SHAFT - Acts as flux return path"]
    SH --> RC2["ROTOR CUP 2 - 50 teeth - North polarity at tip - Offset by 1/2 tooth-pitch"]
    RC2 -- "Misaligned teeth" --> G2["AIR GAP 2 - High reluctance - No torque here"]
    G2 --> ST1["STACK 2 POLE-A - Demagnetised or weakly polarised"]
    ST1 -- "Loop back through frame" --> FR["STATOR FRAME - Laminated yoke"]
    FR -- "Returns to start pole" --> P
```

## 4.5 Sequence Waveform — One-Phase-ON (Wave Drive) for 4-Phase Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0["t = 0"] -->|"A = 1, B = 0, C = 0, D = 0"| T1["Rotor at 0 deg"]
    T1 -->|"A = 0, B = 1, C = 0, D = 0"| T2["Rotor at 1.8 deg"]
    T2 -->|"A = 0, B = 0, C = 1, D = 0"| T3["Rotor at 3.6 deg"]
    T3 -->|"A = 0, B = 0, C = 0, D = 1"| T4["Rotor at 5.4 deg"]
    T4 -->|"A = 1, B = 0, C = 0, D = 0"| T5["Rotor at 7.2 deg - one full cycle"]
```

**Truth-table representation** (8-step electrical cycle for 4-phase wave drive):

| Step # | Phase A | Phase B | Phase C | Phase D | Rotor Angle (°) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1.8 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 3.6 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 5.4 |
| 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 7.2 |
| 6 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 9.0 |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 10.8 |
| 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 12.6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that **only one phase is ON at any instant** — hence the name **wave drive**. The rotor advances by exactly **1 step** per transition. This is the lowest-power, lowest-heat excitation scheme.
