# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a hybrid stepper motor and list two of its constructional features that distinguish it from a VR stepper motor.**

**Model Answer (Board Key Style):**

A hybrid stepper motor is a synchronous motor in which the rotor is split into two toothed cups separated by an axially-magnetised permanent magnet, and the stator consists of two multi-toothed stacks. It produces torque by the combined action of permanent-magnet excitation and variable-reluctance principles.

**Distinguishing features:**
1. The rotor carries a **permanent magnet** (whereas a VR rotor is plain soft iron).
2. The rotor is **mechanically split into two cups** with a **half-tooth-pitch axial offset**; this offset is the source of the small step angle.

*(Expected answer length: 3–4 lines; awarded 3 marks for definition + 2 features.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Compare monofilar and bifilar windings used in stepper motor stators. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Monofilar | Bifilar |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Single coil per pole | Two coils (forward & return) wound bifilarly on the same pole |
| Driver | Requires H-bridge (4 switches/phase) | Requires only 2 switches/phase (half-H) |
| Advantage | Higher copper slot-fill factor ⇒ higher torque per ampere | Easier drive electronics, no demagnetisation risk on the rotor PM |

*(Expected: 3–4 lines, 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a)** Describe the **constructional features of a hybrid stepper motor** with a neat sketch. Explain how the half-tooth-pitch offset between the two rotor cups leads to a small step angle. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A 4-phase hybrid stepper motor has **100 rotor teeth** and is excited in the **two-phase-ON (full-step)** mode. Calculate the **step angle**, **steps per revolution**, and the **shaft speed in rpm** if the controller issues **800 pulses per second**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Constructional features (5 marks):**
- **Stator:** Two laminated stacks, each with 8 (or 12) salient poles. Each pole carries a concentrated multi-tooth lamination (5 or 6 teeth). Diametrically opposite coils are connected in series to form a phase winding.
- **Rotor:** Two soft-iron cups mounted on a common non-magnetic shaft, separated by an axially-magnetised permanent magnet (SmCo or NdFeB). The two cups carry the same number of uniformly-spaced fine teeth (e.g. 50 or 100) and are **mechanically offset by half a tooth-pitch** along the shaft.
- **PM role:** The PM magnetises the two cups with **opposite polarities** — one cup becomes a magnetic North, the other a South.
- **Bearings, frame, and optional encoder** complete the assembly.

**Half-tooth-pitch offset and small step angle (2 marks):**
Because the two cups are offset by half a tooth pitch and carry opposite magnetic polarities, when phase A is energised, one stack's stator teeth align with rotor teeth on one cup while being misaligned on the other. Switching from phase A to phase B advances the magnetic axis by ¼ tooth-pitch, and the rotor therefore rotates by the same amount. The step angle is:

$$
\beta = \frac{180^\circ}{N_r} = \frac{180^\circ}{50} = 3.6^\circ \text{ per stack, i.e. } 1.8^\circ \text{ per electrical step for 4-phase excitation.}
$$

This small step (1.8° = 200 steps/rev) is the hallmark of the hybrid type.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Step angle:**

$$
\beta = \frac{180^\circ}{N_r} = \frac{180^\circ}{100} = 1.8^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Steps per revolution:**

$$
N_{steps/rev} = \frac{360^\circ}{1.8^\circ} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**Step 3 — Pulses per revolution (one-phase-on mode):**
For 4-phase wave drive, one pulse = one step. So 200 pulses per revolution.

**Step 4 — Shaft speed:**

$$
N_{shaft} = \frac{f_{pps}}{N_{steps/rev}} = \frac{800}{200} = 4 \text{ rev/s} = 4 \times 60 = 240 \text{ rpm}
$$

*(Allocate: 2 marks for step angle, 2 marks for steps/rev, 3 marks for shaft speed with unit conversion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a hybrid stepper motor** in the **one-phase-ON (wave drive) mode**. State the phase sequence and the corresponding rotor position for one full electrical cycle. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Compare **VR, PM, and Hybrid stepper motors** in a tabular form with at least six parameters. State **two applications** each of VR and Hybrid stepper motors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle (4 marks):**
A hybrid stepper operates on the simultaneous action of (i) **permanent-magnet torque** — alignment of rotor-cup teeth with the energised stator pole of matching polarity, and (ii) **variable-reluctance torque** — minimisation of air-gap reluctance by maximising stator-tooth/rotor-tooth overlap. When phase A is energised, the magnetic axis of stack-1 aligns with rotor cup-1 teeth; stack-2 (with cup-2) is misaligned because of the ½ tooth-pitch offset. De-energising A and energising B shifts the magnetic axis by ¼ tooth-pitch, and the rotor rotates by exactly 1.8° to re-establish the alignment.

**Phase sequence (2 marks):**
A → B → C → D → A → … (for a 4-phase motor)

**Rotor position table (1 mark):**

| Pulse | A | B | C | D | Rotor Position |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | OFF | 0° |
| 2 | OFF | ON | OFF | OFF | 1.8° |
| 3 | OFF | OFF | ON | OFF | 3.6° |
| 4 | OFF | OFF | OFF | ON | 5.4° |
| 5 | ON | OFF | OFF | OFF | 7.2° (one electrical cycle = 4 steps) |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Tabular comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | VR | PM | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotor | Salient soft iron | Cylindrical PM | Two toothed cups + axial PM |
| Detent torque | Zero | Present | Highest |
| Holding torque | Low | Medium | **Highest** |
| Step angle | 7.5° – 15° | 45° – 90° | **0.9° – 1.8°** |
| Resolution | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Resonance damping | Poor | Good | **Best** |

**Applications (3 marks — 1.5 each):**
- **VR stepper:** low-cost printers, fluid-flow control valves, textile indexing, point-of-sale (POS) printers.
- **Hybrid stepper:** CNC machines, 3-D printers (FFF/FDM), robotics, telescope tracking, camera autofocus, biomedical syringe pumps, disc drives, plotters.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the two-step formula.** Students often write $\beta = 360^\circ/N_r$ for a hybrid motor — *this is wrong*. The correct relation is $\beta = 180^\circ/N_r$ (or equivalently $360^\circ/(m \cdot N_r)$). Loss of 2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by phase count $m$** when computing steps/rev. A 4-phase motor with 50 teeth has $4 \times 50 = 200$ steps/rev, **not** 50.
> 3. **In one-phase-ON mode** the holding torque is ~70 % of rated; in two-phase-ON it is 100 %. Many students omit this distinction.
> 4. **Bifilar ≠ better.** State explicitly that monofilar gives higher slot-fill, bifilar gives simpler drive electronics.
> 5. **Shaft speed unit conversion:** $f_{pps}$ is in pulses per second, but the answer for rpm requires division by $N_{steps/rev}$ and multiplication by 60. Many students lose the “$\times 60$” step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist for the last 10 minutes of revision before the exam.**

- ✅ A **hybrid stepper motor** combines PM + VR principles; rotor has **two toothed cups separated by an axial PM**.
- ✅ The **half-tooth-pitch axial offset** of the two rotor cups is the key to its small step angle.
- ✅ **Step angle formula:** $\beta = 180^\circ / N_r$ for one phase advance; $\beta = 360^\circ / (m \cdot N_r)$ for full electrical cycle.
- ✅ **Standard NEMA 17:** $N_r = 50$, $m = 4$, $\beta = 1.8°$, 200 steps/rev.
- ✅ **Hybrid > PM > VR** in holding torque, resolution, damping, and cost (in that order).
- ✅ **Total torque = PM torque + VR torque** (constructive superposition due to offset).
- ✅ **One-Phase-ON (Wave) mode** = A-B-C-D-A; only one phase energised at a time; lowest power; ~70 % torque.
- ✅ **Two-Phase-ON (Full step) mode** = AB-BC-CA-AB; two phases always ON; 100 % rated torque; highest power.
- ✅ **Half-step mode** = A-AB-B-BC-C-CD-D-DA-A; alternates 1-phase and 2-phase; 0.9° step, 400 steps/rev for NEMA 17.
- ✅ **Microstepping** uses sine/cosine weighted currents; resolution up to 1/256 step; smoother motion.
- ✅ **Monofilar winding** = single coil per pole; needs H-bridge; higher slot-fill; higher torque density.
- ✅ **Bifilar winding** = two coils per pole (forward + reverse); needs only 2 switches/phase; no demagnetisation risk; lower slot-fill.
- ✅ **Detent (cogging) torque** is present in PM and Hybrid, **absent** in VR.
- ✅ Hybrid motors are used in **CNC, 3-D printing, robotics, biomedical, and instrumentation** drives.
- ✅ Remember the **per-revolution formula:** $N_{steps/rev} = m \cdot N_r$.
- ✅ Always carry the **unit** (deg, rpm, pps, Nm) in every step of the numerical answer.
- ✅ Common exam numbers: $N_r = 50, 100$; $m = 2, 4, 5$; $\beta = 1.8°, 0.9°, 3.6°$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two Phase ON Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Two Phase ON Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
**Two Phase ON Mode** (also known as the **Normal Drive Mode** or **Full-Step Excitation Mode**) is a standard excitation scheme for multi-stator-phase stepper motors (such as Variable Reluctance Stepper Motors and Permanent Magnet Stepper Motors) in which **two stator phase windings are energized simultaneously** during each switching step, with current flowing through both phases at the same instant to establish a resultant magnetic field vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the Two Phase ON Mode, the rotor advances by **one full step angle** $\beta$ for every switching pulse, but the equilibrium (rest) position of the rotor lies **midway between the two energized stator poles**, i.e., offset by half a step angle compared to the One Phase ON Mode.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are trying to rotate a heavy flywheel with detents (notches). 
- In the **One Phase ON Mode**, you push the flywheel with **one hand** — it moves, but the force is limited.
- In the **Two Phase ON Mode**, you push the flywheel with **two hands simultaneously**, where each hand pulls in a slightly different direction. The combined, diagonally-directed push is stronger (by a factor of about $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$) and locks the wheel into a position that is the *average* of the two pulling directions — exactly halfway between them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant / Metric**: The **electrical angle between adjacent stator phases** of a standard 4-phase, 2-pole-per-phase stack is $\mathbf{90^\circ}$ **electrical**. This quadrature is the geometric reason for the $\sqrt{2}$ torque enhancement factor in Two Phase ON Mode.

### Operating Principle in Plain English
1. A DC supply is switched to the stator windings through a sequential logic controller (typically a ring counter driving transistors).
2. At any instant, **two phases are HIGH (ON)** and the remaining phases are LOW (OFF).
3. The two energized phases produce magnetic flux vectors that are spatially displaced by $90^\circ$ electrical in space.
4. The rotor — having either salient poles (VRSM) or permanent magnet poles (PMSM) — aligns itself with the **vector sum** of the two stator fields.
5. The next switching step de-energizes one phase and energizes a new adjacent phase, causing the rotor to step forward by exactly one full step angle.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does it produce more torque?** Because the resultant air-gap flux density is the **phasor sum** of two orthogonal components of equal magnitude. Phasor addition of two equal vectors at $90^\circ$ yields a resultant whose magnitude is $\sqrt{2}$ times the individual vector — and torque is directly proportional to the rate of change of co-energy with respect to angular position.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Structured Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Phase Selection by Ring Counter**
- A sequential logic circuit (e.g., a 4-bit ring counter) sequentially enables pairs of driver transistors.
- For a 4-phase motor (phases A, B, C, D), the activation order is: **AB → BC → CD → DA → AB …**

**Step 2 — Simultaneous Energization**
- Two phases are connected to the DC source $V_s$ at the same time.
- Each phase winding has resistance $R$ and inductance $L$.
- The instantaneous current in each phase rises according to: $i(t) = \dfrac{V_s}{R}\left(1 - e^{-tR/L}\right)$.

**Step 3 — Vector Superposition of Air-Gap Flux**
- Phase A produces a flux $\phi_A$ aligned with stator pole A.
- Phase B produces a flux $\phi_B$ aligned with stator pole B.
- For a 2-pole-per-phase, 4-phase motor, $\phi_A$ and $\phi_B$ are spatially displaced by $90^\circ$ electrical.
- The **resultant flux** $\phi_R$ is the phasor sum: $\phi_R = \sqrt{\phi_A^2 + \phi_B^2 + 2\phi_A \phi_B \cos(90^\circ)} = \sqrt{\phi_A^2 + \phi_B^2}$.
- If both phases carry equal current, $\phi_A = \phi_B = \phi$, then $\phi_R = \phi\sqrt{2}$.

**Step 4 — Torque Generation**
- The electromagnetic torque developed is proportional to the rate of change of co-energy with respect to angular displacement.
- Because the resultant flux is $\sqrt{2}$ times larger, the developed holding torque is also $\sqrt{2}$ times greater than in One Phase ON Mode.
- Holding torque: $T_h^{(2\phi)} = \sqrt{2} \cdot T_h^{(1\phi)}$.

**Step 5 — Rotor Locking at the Mid-Position**
- The rotor aligns with the vector sum of the two fluxes, which lies at the **bisector** of the angle between the two energized poles.
- This position is offset by **half a step angle ($\beta/2$)** compared to the One Phase ON Mode rest position.

**Step 6 — Step Advancement**
- When the ring counter advances, one of the two active phases is switched OFF and the next adjacent phase is switched ON.
- The rotor advances by exactly one full step angle $\beta$.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Step angle (full step) | $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_r \cdot N_{ph}}$ | degrees (mech.) | $N_r$ = rotor teeth, $N_{ph}$ = number of stator phase excitations per revolution |
| 2 | Step angle (electrical) | $\beta_e = N_r \cdot \beta$ | degrees (elec.) | Always $90^\circ$ electrical for a 4-phase full-step motor |
| 3 | Resultant flux (Two Phase ON) | $\phi_R = \sqrt{\phi_A^2 + \phi_B^2 + 2\phi_A \phi_B \cos\theta}$ | Weber (Wb) | $\theta$ = spatial angle between energized phases |
| 4 | Simplified resultant (equal phases, $\theta = 90^\circ$) | $\phi_R = \phi\sqrt{2}$ | Weber (Wb) | Quadrature superposition |
| 5 | Torque enhancement factor | $k_T = \dfrac{T_{2\phi}}{T_{1\phi}} = \sqrt{2}$ | dimensionless | Holds when both phases carry equal current |
| 6 | Power consumption per step | $P_{2\phi} = 2 \cdot I^2 R$ | Watts (W) | Double the One Phase ON Mode power |
| 7 | Synchronous (pull-out) speed | $n_s = \dfrac{f \cdot \beta}{360^\circ}$ | rev/sec (rps) | $f$ = pulse frequency (Hz) |
| 8 | Phase current rise (transient) | $i(t) = \dfrac{V_s}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Amperes (A) | $\tau = L/R$ — electrical time constant |
| 9 | Holding torque (Two Phase ON) | $T_h^{(2\phi)} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}\sqrt{2}$ | N·m | From co-energy derivative |
| 10 | Step rate for $N$ steps/sec | $f = N$ | Hertz (Hz) | One electrical pulse = one mechanical step |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **CNC Machines & 3D Printers**: Two Phase ON Mode is the **default full-step mode** in most open-loop positioning systems because it delivers the maximum holding torque — critical for resisting cutting forces without losing position.
- **Robotic Actuators**: Used in pick-and-place heads where stiffness at standstill prevents drift.
- **Valve Actuators in Process Industries**: Ensures the valve disc is firmly held at the commanded position even under fluid pressure.
- **Textile & Packaging Machinery**: Provides the holding torque needed to maintain registration marks between indexing events.
- **Hard Disk Drives (historical)**: The head-positioning voice-coil actuator used a similar dual-coil "normal drive" for higher stiffness at track center.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Alert**: Higher torque comes at the cost of **double the copper losses ($I^2R$)** and **double the supply current demand** compared to One Phase ON Mode. For battery-powered or heat-sensitive applications, One Phase ON Mode may be preferred.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — The $\sqrt{2}$ Torque Enhancement Factor

**Starting Premise:** The electromagnetic torque developed by a stepper motor at any rotor position $\theta$ is given by the co-energy gradient:

$$
T_e(\theta) = \frac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i = \text{const}}
$$

For a single phase winding carrying current $i_1$ and having a position-dependent inductance $L_1(\theta)$, the co-energy is:

$$
W_{co,1} = \frac{1}{2} L_1(\theta) i_1^2
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the torque contributed by Phase A alone (One Phase ON Mode):

$$
T_A = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\frac{1}{2} L_A(\theta) i_A^2\right) = \frac{1}{2} i_A^2 \frac{dL_A}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 2:** Similarly, the torque contributed by Phase B alone:

$$
T_B = \frac{1}{2} i_B^2 \frac{dL_B}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 3:** When both phases A and B are energized simultaneously (Two Phase ON Mode), the **total co-energy** is the sum of the individual co-energies (assuming magnetic linearity and no mutual coupling between orthogonal phases):

$$
W_{co,total} = W_{co,A} + W_{co,B} = \frac{1}{2} L_A(\theta) i_A^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_B(\theta) i_B^2
$$

**Step 4:** The total electromagnetic torque becomes:

$$
T_{total} = \frac{1}{2} i_A^2 \frac{dL_A}{d\theta} + \frac{1}{2} i_B^2 \frac{dL_B}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **symmetry condition** for a balanced 2-pole-per-phase, 4-phase motor:
- $i_A = i_B = I$ (equal phase currents),
- $\dfrac{dL_A}{d\theta} = \dfrac{dL_B}{d\theta} = \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ (identical inductance profiles, spatially shifted by $90^\circ$ electrical).

**Step 6:** Substituting:

$$
T_{2\phi} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} + \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} = I^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 7:** In One Phase ON Mode, only one phase contributes, so:

$$
T_{1\phi} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 8:** Take the ratio:

$$
\frac{T_{2\phi}}{T_{1\phi}} = \frac{I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}}{\dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}} = 2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refinement — Why the textbook states $\sqrt{2}$ and not 2:** The textbook value of $\sqrt{2}$ arises from the **flux-linkage / MMF phasor model**, where the developed torque is proportional to the *square* of the resultant MMF (which is $\phi_R^2 \propto (\sqrt{2}\,\phi)^2 = 2\phi^2$), but is then normalized by the *sinusoidal* torque-angle characteristic $\sin(45^\circ) = 1/\sqrt{2}$ at the bisector position. The net factor becomes:
> $$\boxed{\dfrac{T_{2\phi}}{T_{1\phi}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414}$$

### Derivation 2 — Resultant Air-Gap Flux Density

**Step 1:** Phase A MMF in space-phasor form:

$$
\vec{F}_A = F_m \angle 0^\circ
$$

**Step 2:** Phase B MMF in space-phasor form (displaced by $90^\circ$ electrical):

$$
\vec{F}_B = F_m \angle 90^\circ = j F_m
$$

**Step 3:** Resultant MMF using phasor addition:

$$
\vec{F}_R = \vec{F}_A + \vec{F}_B = F_m + j F_m
$$

**Step 4:** Magnitude of the resultant MMF:

$$
\vert \vec{F}_R \vert = \sqrt{F_m^2 + F_m^2} = F_m \sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Since air-gap flux density $B_g \propto F$ (assuming constant permeance):

$$
B_{g,R} = B_{g,m} \sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 6:** The angle of the resultant in space:

$$
\alpha_R = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{F_m}{F_m}\right) = 45^\circ
$$

This confirms the rotor locks along the **bisector** between poles A and B.

### Derivation 3 — Step Angle for a 4-Phase, 2-Pole Motor

**Step 1:** For a Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM) with $N_r$ rotor teeth and $N_s$ stator teeth arranged in $m$ phases, the step angle is:

$$
\beta = \frac{360^\circ}}{N_r} \cdot \frac{1}{N_{steps/rev}}
$$

**Step 2:** For a 4-phase motor using Two Phase ON Mode, one complete electrical cycle requires 4 switching steps (AB → BC → CD → DA).

**Step 3:** The mechanical step angle per switching pulse:

$$
\beta = \frac{360^\circ}}{m \cdot N_r}
$$

**Step 4:** For a typical motor with $m = 4$ and $N_r = 50$:

$$
\beta = \frac{360^\circ}}{4 \times 50} = \frac{360^\circ}{200} = 1.8^\circ \text{ per step}
$$

### Analytical Comparison Matrix: One Phase ON vs. Two Phase ON

| Performance Parameter | One Phase ON (Wave Drive) | Two Phase ON (Normal Drive) | Verdict |
|------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|---------|
| Phases energized per step | **1** | **2** | Two Phase uses double the copper |
| Resultant air-gap flux | $\phi$ | $\phi\sqrt{2}$ | Two Phase is stronger |
| Holding torque (steady-state) | $T_h$ | $1.414 \, T_h$ | Two Phase wins |
| Power consumption | $I^2 R$ | $2 I^2 R$ | One Phase saves energy |
| Rotor rest position | Aligned with energized pole | Midway between two poles | Different locking geometry |
| Step resolution | Full step $\beta$ | Full step $\beta$ (same) | Tie |
| Smoothness | Jerky transitions | Smoother (less dip) | Two Phase is smoother |
| Heat dissipation | Lower | Higher (2× copper loss) | One Phase is cooler |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two Phase ON Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pulseGen["PULSE GENERATOR CLOCK SOURCE"]
    ringCntr["RING COUNTER 4 BIT SEQUENTIAL LOGIC"]
    drvA["DRIVER TRANSISTOR PHASE A"]
    drvB["DRIVER TRANSISTOR PHASE B"]
    drvC["DRIVER TRANSISTOR PHASE C"]
    drvD["DRIVER TRANSISTOR PHASE D"]
    windA["STATOR WINDING PHASE A"]
    windB["STATOR WINDING PHASE B"]
    windC["STATOR WINDING PHASE C"]
    windD["STATOR WINDING PHASE D"]
    rotor["ROTOR SHAFT MECHANICAL OUTPUT"]
    logicBlock["TWO PHASE ON LOGIC AB BC CD DA"]
    pulseGen --> ringCntr
    ringCntr --> logicBlock
    logicBlock --> drvA
    logicBlock --> drvB
    logicBlock --> drvC
    logicBlock --> drvD
    drvA --> windA
    drvB --> windB
    drvC --> windC
    drvD --> windD
    windA --> rotor
    windB --> rotor
    windC --> rotor
    windD --> rotor
```

### Diagram 2 — Switching Sequence State Machine (4-Phase, Two Phase ON)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateAB
    stateAB : STEP 1 PHASES A AND B ENERGIZED
    stateAB : ROTOR LOCKS AT 45 DEG BISECTOR
    stateAB --> stateBC : CLOCK PULSE 1
    stateBC : STEP 2 PHASES B AND C ENERGIZED
    stateBC : ROTOR ADVANCES BY 1 FULL STEP
    stateBC --> stateCD : CLOCK PULSE 2
    stateCD : STEP 3 PHASES C AND D ENERGIZED
    stateCD : ROTOR AT NEXT BISECTOR
    stateCD --> stateDA : CLOCK PULSE 3
    stateDA : STEP 4 PHASES D AND A ENERGIZED
    stateDA : ROTOR AT 135 DEG BISECTOR
    stateDA --> stateAB : CLOCK PULSE 4
    stateAB --> [*]
```

### Diagram 3 — Phasor Superposition Visual (Conceptual Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    phiA["FLUX VECTOR PHASE A MAGNITUDE PHI ANGLE 0 DEG"]
    phiB["FLUX VECTOR PHASE B MAGNITUDE PHI ANGLE 90 DEG"]
    phiR["RESULTANT FLUX VECTOR MAGNITUDE PHI ROOT 2 ANGLE 45 DEG"]
    rotorPos["ROTOR LOCK POSITION 45 DEG BISECTOR"]
    phiA --> phiR
    phiB --> phiR
    phiR --> rotorPos
```

### Diagram 4 — Torque vs. Rotor Angle Profile (Conceptual Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pos0["ROTOR POSITION 0 DEG"]
    pos45["ROTOR POSITION 45 DEG REST IN TWO PHASE ON MODE"]
    pos90["ROTOR POSITION 90 DEG REST IN ONE PHASE ON MODE B"]
    torque1["TORQUE T1 REFERENCE"]
    torque2["TORQUE T2 HIGHER BY ROOT 2 FACTOR"]
    pos0 --> torque1
    pos45 --> torque2
    pos90 --> torque1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these are block diagrams and not physical schematics:** Stepper motor drive schematics involve multi-winding magnetic circuits, power electronics, and mechanical rotor geometries that cannot be faithfully rendered in Mermaid. The functional block diagrams above correctly capture the **signal flow, state transitions, and phasor relationships** that are the KTU-board-testable concepts.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define Two Phase ON mode of operation of a stepper motor. How does it differ from One Phase ON mode in terms of torque and power consumption?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Two Phase ON mode (or Normal Drive) is an excitation scheme in which **two stator phase windings of a stepper motor are energized simultaneously** at each switching step, producing a resultant air-gap flux that is the phasor sum of the two individual phase fluxes.
- **Torque difference (1 mark):** The developed holding torque is $\sqrt{2}$ (≈1.414) times that of One Phase ON mode because the resultant flux is $\sqrt{2}$ times larger and the rotor aligns with the bisector of the two energized stator poles.
- **Power difference (0.5 mark):** Power consumption is **doubled** ($2 I^2 R$) because two phases carry current simultaneously, leading to higher copper losses.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Write the phase energization sequence for a 4-phase Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor operating in Two Phase ON mode.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The sequence is: **AB → BC → CD → DA → AB → …** (1.5 marks)
- At each step, **two adjacent phases** are energized while the other two are OFF (1 mark).
- The rotor advances by **one full step angle $\beta$** per transition, and the rotor's rest position lies along the **45° bisector** between the two energized stator poles (0.5 mark).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the Two Phase ON (Normal Drive) mode of operation of a 4-phase Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor. Draw the phase energization table and the stator MMF phasor diagram for all four steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the torque developed in Two Phase ON mode and show that it is $\sqrt{2}$ times the torque produced in One Phase ON mode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**
1. **Sketch & Layout (2 marks):** Draw a 4-phase, 2-pole-per-phase VRSM with stator poles A1-A2, B1-B2, C1-C2, D1-D2 and a salient-pole rotor. Label the spatial electrical angles: A at $0^\circ$, B at $90^\circ$, C at $180^\circ$, D at $270^\circ$.
2. **Phase Energization Table (2 marks):**

   | Step | Phases ON | MMF Phasor Direction | Rotor Rest Angle |
   |------|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|
   | 1 | A, B | $45^\circ$ | $45^\circ$ |
   | 2 | B, C | $135^\circ$ | $135^\circ$ |
   | 3 | C, D | $225^\circ$ | $225^\circ$ |
   | 4 | D, A | $315^\circ$ | $315^\circ$ |

3. **MMF Phasor Diagram (2 marks):** Show the four resultant MMF phasors, each at $45^\circ$ intervals, with magnitude $F_m\sqrt{2}$.
4. **Explanation (1 mark):** At each step, two phases carry equal current; the rotor aligns with the resultant field and locks at the bisector. The next clock pulse advances the rotor by one full step $\beta$.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**
1. **Co-energy for Phase A alone (1 mark):** $W_{co,A} = \dfrac{1}{2} L_A(\theta) i_A^2$
2. **Co-energy for Phase B alone (1 mark):** $W_{co,B} = \dfrac{1}{2} L_B(\theta) i_B^2$
3. **Total co-energy with both phases ON (1 mark):** $W_{co} = W_{co,A} + W_{co,B} = \dfrac{1}{2} L_A i_A^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} L_B i_B^2$
4. **Torque expression (1.5 marks):** $T_{2\phi} = \dfrac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \theta} = \dfrac{1}{2} i_A^2 \dfrac{dL_A}{d\theta} + \dfrac{1}{2} i_B^2 \dfrac{dL_B}{d\theta}$
5. **Apply symmetry (1 mark):** $i_A = i_B = I$, $\dfrac{dL_A}{d\theta} = \dfrac{dL_B}{d\theta} = \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ → $T_{2\phi} = I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$
6. **Compare with One Phase ON torque (1 mark):** $T_{1\phi} = \dfrac{1}{2} I^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$
7. **Ratio (0.5 mark):** $\dfrac{T_{2\phi}}{T_{1\phi}} = 2$ (co-energy model) → refined to $\sqrt{2}$ via the **sinusoidal torque-angle** characteristic at the $45^\circ$ bisector: $\sin(45^\circ) = 1/\sqrt{2}$, giving the final textbook factor $\boxed{\sqrt{2}}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare the One Phase ON mode and Two Phase ON mode of a stepper motor with respect to (i) phases energized, (ii) holding torque, (iii) power consumption, (iv) rotor rest position, and (v) smoothness of motion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-phase, 200-step-per-revolution Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor is operated in Two Phase ON mode. The step angle in electrical degrees is $90^\circ$. Calculate: (i) the mechanical step angle, (ii) the shaft speed in rpm when pulsed at 500 Hz, and (iii) the holding torque if the single-phase holding torque is 0.5 N·m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**
1. **Phases energized (1.5 marks):** One Phase ON → 1 phase per step (wave drive); Two Phase ON → 2 adjacent phases per step (normal drive).
2. **Holding torque (1.5 marks):** $T_{1\phi}$ vs. $T_{2\phi} = \sqrt{2}\, T_{1\phi} \approx 1.414\, T_{1\phi}$ — Two Phase ON produces 41.4% more torque.
3. **Power consumption (1 mark):** $P_{1\phi} = I^2 R$ vs. $P_{2\phi} = 2 I^2 R$ — Two Phase ON consumes double the power.
4. **Rotor rest position (1.5 marks):** One Phase ON → aligned with the energized pole (at $0^\circ, 90^\circ, 180^\circ, 270^\circ$); Two Phase ON → at the $45^\circ$ bisector between the two energized poles.
5. **Smoothness (1.5 marks):** One Phase ON exhibits a torque dip between steps (only one phase contributes, so torque passes through zero at certain rotor positions); Two Phase ON is smoother because the resultant field is always non-zero and the torque profile is more continuous.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**
1. **Mechanical step angle (2 marks):**

   $$
   \beta = \frac{360^\circ}{N_{steps/rev}} = \frac{360^\circ}{200} = 1.8^\circ \text{ per step}
   $$

2. **Shaft speed at 500 Hz (2.5 marks):**
   - Steps per second = pulse frequency = 500 steps/sec.
   - Revolutions per second = $\dfrac{500}{200} = 2.5$ rps.
   - Revolutions per minute = $2.5 \times 60 = 150$ rpm.

3. **Holding torque in Two Phase ON mode (2.5 marks):**
   - Given: $T_{1\phi} = 0.5$ N·m.
   - Torque enhancement factor: $\sqrt{2}$.
   - Holding torque: $T_{2\phi} = \sqrt{2} \times 0.5 = 0.707$ N·m.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong phase sequence:** Students often write the sequence as A → B → C → D (which is One Phase ON / wave drive). For Two Phase ON, it **must** be AB → BC → CD → DA. Losing 2 marks here is the single most common error.
> 2. **Confusing mechanical and electrical step angles:** For a 4-phase motor with $N_r$ rotor teeth, $\beta_{mech} = 360^\circ / (m \cdot N_r)$ and $\beta_{elec} = N_r \cdot \beta_{mech}$. Mixing these up loses 1–2 marks in numerical problems.
> 3. **Stating the torque ratio as "2" instead of "$\sqrt{2}$":** Both answers receive partial credit, but $\sqrt{2}$ is the **KTU-mark-scheme preferred answer** because the $\sin(45^\circ)$ bisector geometry must be explicitly mentioned.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention power doubling:** In Part A questions, examiners explicitly test whether you know the $2 I^2 R$ cost of the higher torque. Omitting this loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Units in numerical problems:** Always quote speed in **rpm** (not rps) and torque in **N·m** (not kgf·cm) unless the question specifies otherwise.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two Phase ON Mode** energizes **two stator windings simultaneously** at every step — the **highest-torque full-step drive** for stepper motors.
- The phase sequence for a 4-phase motor is **AB → BC → CD → DA → (repeat)**.
- The **resultant air-gap flux** is $\phi_R = \phi\sqrt{2}$ because the two phase fluxes are spatially displaced by $90^\circ$ electrical.
- The **holding torque is $\sqrt{2}$ times** the One Phase ON holding torque.
- The **rotor locks at the $45^\circ$ bisector** between the two energized poles, offset by **half a step angle** from the One Phase ON rest position.
- **Power consumption doubles** ($2 I^2 R$) — a key trade-off.
- The **step angle is unchanged** ($\beta = 360^\circ / (m N_r)$) — this is still a full-step mode, not microstepping.
- It is the **default "normal drive"** in industrial CNC, robotics, and 3D-printer applications where holding torque matters more than thermal efficiency.
- The **mathematical proof** rests on co-energy differentiation: $T = \partial W_{co} / \partial \theta$, with the $\sqrt{2}$ factor emerging from the sinusoidal torque-angle characteristic at the $45^\circ$ rest position.
- **Variants to remember for exams:** One Phase ON (wave), Two Phase ON (normal/full-step), Half-Step (alternate), and Microstepping (sinusoidal current profiling) — Two Phase ON sits between wave drive and half-step in the torque-vs-resolution hierarchy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Half-Step Mode - Micro-Stepping - Static and Dynamic Characteristics - Open-Loop and Closed Loop Control - Applications - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Half-Step Mode - Micro-Stepping - Static and Dynamic Characteristics - Open-Loop and Closed Loop Control - Applications - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stepper Motor Operating Modes: Foundational Definitions

A **stepper motor** is a brushless, synchronous electric machine that converts discrete electrical pulses into precise mechanical shaft rotations (steps). The *manner* in which stator phase currents are sequenced — known as the **excitation scheme** — directly determines the resolution, torque ripple, smoothness, and acoustic noise of the drive. The KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET743) Module 1 requires mastery of three progressively refined modes:

1. **Full-Step Mode** (reference baseline)
2. **Half-Step Mode**
3. **Micro-Stepping Mode**

Each mode progressively subdivides the natural electrical step of the motor, trading drive complexity for finer angular resolution.

### 1.1.1 Half-Step Mode — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Half-Step Mode** is an excitation technique in which the phase energization sequence **alternates** between **single-phase-on** and **two-phase-on** states. This effectively doubles the angular resolution of a full-step drive by inserting an *intermediate detent position* between every pair of full steps.

For a conventional 1.8° (200 step/rev) hybrid stepper driven in half-step mode, the mechanical step angle becomes:

$$\theta_{hs} = \frac{\theta_{fs}}{2} = \frac{1.8°}{2} = 0.9° \quad \Rightarrow \quad 400 \text{ steps/rev}$$

### 1.1.2 Micro-Stepping — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro-stepping** is a current-profiling technique in which the stator phase currents are *sinusoidally proportioned* so that the resultant stator MMF vector moves in many small sub-increments (typically 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, or 256 microsteps per full step). The rotor settles at a position dictated by the vector sum of the two phase MMFs, producing *near-continuous* rotation.

The theoretical microstep angle is:

$$\theta_{\mu} = \frac{\theta_{fs}}{N_{\mu}}$$

where $N_{\mu}$ is the number of microsteps per full step. For a 1.8° motor with $N_{\mu} = 10$, $\theta_{\mu} = 0.18°$ (1000 microsteps/rev).

### 1.1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase of Precision"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Climbing a Staircase:**
> - **Full-Step Mode** is like climbing a staircase with **large, equal steps**. You can stand only on every 2nd tread.
> - **Half-Step Mode** inserts a *half-tread landing* between each full step, doubling the number of stable resting positions.
> - **Micro-Stepping** replaces the staircase with a **smooth ramp with grooved grip-lines** — you can stop at virtually any point along the slope. The grooves (microsteps) provide stable detents; the ramp shape provides continuous motion between them.

This analogy clarifies why micro-stepping produces *lower torque ripple*, *reduced resonance*, and *quieter operation* — exactly the outcomes KTU examiners love to test.

---

## 1.2 Static vs Dynamic Characteristics — Formal Distinction

| Characteristic Class | Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) | When Measured |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Characteristics** | Behaviour when the rotor is *stationary* (or moving at near-zero velocity) under a fixed excitation. | Holding torque, detent torque, step accuracy, positional stiffness. |
| **Dynamic Characteristics** | Behaviour during *sustained rotation* (slewing) or *transient acceleration/deceleration* between step positions. | Pull-out torque, pull-in torque, stepping rate, resonance frequency, settling time. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** *Static = Standing still; Dynamic = Dancing/moving.* Holding torque is what keeps the rotor "standing still" against an external load; pull-out torque is the maximum load it can move continuously before losing synchronism.

### 1.2.1 Static Characteristics — Detailed

- **Holding Torque ($T_h$):** Maximum torque the energized motor can exert on the shaft while *stationary*, without losing positional integrity.
- **Detent Torque ($T_d$):** Residual magnetic torque present even with *all phases de-energized* (due to permanent magnet flux in hybrid motors). Typically **1–5 %** of $T_h$.
- **Step Accuracy / Positional Error:** Deviation (in % of step angle) between the commanded and actual rotor position under no-load conditions. Premium motors: **±3 %** non-cumulative, **±5 %** cumulative.

### 1.2.2 Dynamic Characteristics — Detailed

- **Pull-Out Torque ($T_{po}$):** Maximum load torque the motor can drive at a given *stepping frequency* (or slewing speed) **without losing synchronism**. Strongly decreases with speed.
- **Pull-In Torque ($T_{pi}$):** Maximum load torque against which the motor can *start, stop, or reverse* at a given stepping rate — *without* losing steps.
- **Slewing Range:** The frequency band where $T_{po}$ governs (typically above the *start-stop* region).
- **Resonance Frequency ($f_r$):** A narrow band of stepping rates (often 100–300 Hz for small motors) where torque capability drops catastrophically due to mechanical/electrical coupling. Must be *damped* or *accelerated through*.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time required for rotor oscillations to decay within a specified angular band after a step command.

---

## 1.3 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control — Foundational Definitions

### 1.3.1 Open-Loop Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open-Loop Control** of a stepper motor means the controller issues step and direction pulses to the driver **without any feedback** from the rotor's actual position or velocity. The motor is *blindly trusted* to have moved exactly as commanded.

Block form: `Controller → Driver → Stepper Motor → Load` (no return path).

### 1.3.2 Closed-Loop Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed-Loop Control (Servo-Stepper)** adds a position sensor (typically an *incremental encoder* or *resolver*) to the motor shaft. The actual position is fed back to the controller, which compares it to the reference and **dynamically corrects** the excitation to eliminate positional error.

Block form: `Controller ↔ Driver → Stepper Motor → Load → Encoder → Controller`.

The closed-loop variant is often marketed as a **"servo-stepper"** or **"closed-loop stepper"** and recovers 100 % of the lost-step failure mode that plagues open-loop drives.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Half-Step Mode — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Phase Excitation Sequence (2-Phase Motor)

The 8-state half-step sequence alternates between **one-phase-on** and **two-phase-on** excitations:

| Step # | Phase A | Phase B | Rotor Position | Holding Torque |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | ON | OFF | 0° (ref.) | $T_h$ |
| 2 | ON | ON | 0.9° (½ step) | $\sqrt{2}\,T_h$ |
| 3 | OFF | ON | 1.8° (full step) | $T_h$ |
| 4 | OFF | ON | 2.7° | — |
| 5 | OFF | OFF | 3.6° | 0 (but detent present) |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

> [!NOTE]
> When *both* phases are energized with rated current, the resultant MMF vector lies at **45°** between the two phase axes. The rotor aligns to this vector, producing holding torque $T = \sqrt{2}\,T_h$ (vector sum) but with the angular position **midway** between the two single-phase detents. This is the *physical origin* of the half-step.

### 2.1.2 Advantages and Trade-Offs of Half-Step Mode

**Advantages:**
- Doubles the resolution (e.g., 1.8° → 0.9°).
- Smoother motion (reduced low-speed cogging).
- Slightly higher average torque capability.

**Trade-offs:**
- Torque ripple *increases* (alternates between $T_h$ and $\sqrt{2}\,T_h$).
- Drive electronics need an additional OFF state and current control in both phases.
- Power dissipation rises due to the two-phase-on segments.

---

## 2.2 Micro-Stepping — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Sinusoidal Current Profiling

For a 2-phase motor, the ideal phase currents at the $k$-th microstep (of $N_\mu$ total per full step) are:

$$I_A(k) = I_m \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cdot\frac{k}{N_\mu}\right)$$

$$I_B(k) = I_m \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cdot\frac{k}{N_\mu}\right)$$

where $I_m$ is the rated phase current (peak). This produces a stator MMF vector of *constant magnitude* $I_m$ rotating in equal angular increments:

$$\Delta\theta_\mu = \frac{90°}{N_\mu} = \frac{\theta_{fs}}{N_\mu} \quad \text{(electrical)}$$

### 2.2.2 Resolution and Theoretical Counts

$$\text{Steps per revolution} = N_r = \frac{360°}{\theta_{fs}} \times N_\mu$$

**Example:** A 200 step/rev motor with $N_\mu = 32$ yields $N_r = 6400$ microsteps/rev — equivalent to a 0.05625° step.

### 2.2.3 Why Micro-Stepping Reduces Torque Ripple

At each microstep, the stator MMF vector jumps by a small angle, and the rotor (under load) lags by a small angle $\delta$. The developed torque is:

$$T(k) = T_h \sin(\delta)$$

Because $\delta$ is now *small and slowly varying*, the torque variation per step is small — hence the low torque ripple. However, *positioning accuracy* does not improve proportionally beyond ~10–20 microsteps per full step because of:
- Friction and cogging nonlinearities
- Current-loop quantization (DAC resolution)
- Magnetic saturation asymmetry

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often claim "more microsteps = better accuracy." In reality, beyond 16–32 microsteps per full step, **incremental accuracy gains diminish sharply** while drive cost and complexity rise. KTU examiners expect the *quantitative* limit to be acknowledged.

---

## 2.3 Static Characteristics — Mathematical Framework

### 2.3.1 Holding Torque

The electromagnetic torque in a 2-phase hybrid stepper at any rotor angle $\theta$ (electrical) is:

$$T(\theta) = -K_t \left[ I_A \sin\theta + I_B \cos\theta \right] + T_d \sin(4\theta)$$

where:
- $K_t$ = torque constant (Nm/A)
- $T_d$ = detent torque amplitude (Nm)
- $\theta$ = electrical angle of rotor

At rated $I_A = I_m, I_B = 0$, the holding torque simplifies to:

$$T_h = K_t \, I_m + T_d$$

### 2.3.2 Step Accuracy

$$\varepsilon_{\text{step}} = \frac{\theta_{\text{actual}} - \theta_{\text{commanded}}}{\theta_{fs}} \times 100\%$$

Specified as *non-cumulative* (per step) and *cumulative* (over 360°).

---

## 2.4 Dynamic Characteristics — Mathematical Framework

### 2.4.1 Pull-Out Torque vs Speed (Slewing Characteristic)

The pull-out torque falls approximately hyperbolically with stepping frequency due to back-EMF and winding inductance:

$$T_{po}(f) \approx \frac{T_h}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{2\pi f L}{R}\right)^2}}$$

where:
- $L$ = phase inductance (H)
- $R$ = phase resistance (Ω)
- $f$ = stepping frequency (Hz)

### 2.4.2 Pull-In Torque

Empirically modeled as a decaying function:

$$T_{pi}(f) \approx T_{po}(0) \left[ 1 - \frac{f}{f_{\max}} \right]$$

where $f_{\max}$ is the *maximum start-stop frequency* (typically 200–1000 pps for small motors).

### 2.4.3 Resonance Frequency

The mechanical resonance of the rotor-load system is:

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{K_t \, N_\mu}{J_{\text{eff}}}}$$

where $J_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective inertia of rotor + load reflected to the shaft. Resonance is mitigated by:
- Microstepping (divides the disturbance into smaller impulses)
- Mechanical dampers
- Acceleration/deceleration ramps (passing through $f_r$ quickly)

---

## 2.5 Open-Loop Control — Architecture

### 2.5.1 Functional Blocks

`Pulse Generator (μC/PLC)` → `Translator (sequence logic)` → `Current Chopper / PWM Driver` → `H-Bridge per phase` → `Stepper Motor`

The translator (e.g., L297 IC) converts step/direction inputs into the correct phase-energization pattern. The chopper (e.g., L298 + sense resistors, or modern MOSFET drivers like DRV8711) regulates phase current via PWM at typically 20–50 kHz.

### 2.5.2 Failure Modes

- **Lost steps:** External load exceeds $T_{po}$ → motor stalls silently. Controller believes position is correct; mechanical reality differs. *Catastrophic* in CNC and pick-and-place systems.
- **No stall detection:** Driver cannot distinguish "rotor moving" from "rotor stalled."

---

## 2.6 Closed-Loop Control — Architecture

### 2.6.1 Functional Blocks

`Reference position` → `[±] → Controller (PI/PID) → PWM Driver → H-Bridge → Stepper → Load → Encoder → [feedback to ±]`

The controller typically uses:
- **PI control** (most common — eliminates steady-state position error)
- **PID control** (used when overshoot must be tightly controlled)
- **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** (advanced — vector-control similar to PMSM drives)

### 2.6.2 Performance Gains

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Stall tolerance | None (lost steps) | **100 %** recovery |
| Torque at high speed | Limited by $T_{po}$ | Boosted by current on-demand |
| Heat dissipation | High (always rated current) | Lower (current = load demand) |
| Positioning accuracy | ±5 % cumulative | ±1 encoder count |
| Cost | Low | Moderate–High |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{fs} = \dfrac{360°}{N_r}$ | Full step angle | degrees |
| 2 | $\theta_{hs} = \dfrac{\theta_{fs}}{2}$ | Half step angle | degrees |
| 3 | $\theta_{\mu} = \dfrac{\theta_{fs}}{N_\mu}$ | Microstep angle | degrees |
| 4 | $N_{\mu,\text{total}} = N_r \times N_\mu$ | Total microsteps per rev | count |
| 5 | $n = \dfrac{60\,f_p}{N_{\mu,\text{total}}}$ | Shaft speed | rpm |
| 6 | $T_h = K_t\,I_m$ | Holding torque | Nm |
| 7 | $T(k) = T_h \sin\delta$ | Developed torque at microstep | Nm |
| 8 | $T_{po}(f) = \dfrac{T_h}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f L / R)^2}}$ | Pull-out torque vs freq | Nm |
| 9 | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_t N_\mu}{J_{\text{eff}}}}$ | Resonance frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $I_A(k) = I_m \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi k}{2 N_\mu}\right)$ | Phase A microstep current | A |
| 11 | $I_B(k) = I_m \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi k}{2 N_\mu}\right)$ | Phase B microstep current | A |
| 12 | $e_b = K_e \,\omega$ | Back-EMF (per phase) | V |
| 13 | $\tau_e = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Electrical time constant | s |
| 14 | $\Delta\theta = N \times \theta_{\mu}$ | Total angular displacement | rad / ° |
| 15 | $P = T \times \omega$ | Mechanical output power | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** A 14-mark question typically requires 3–5 of the above formulas combined with at least one qualitative discussion (advantages/limitations/applications).

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stepper motor control at the levels described in this topic is the *backbone* of:

- **3D printers & CNC routers** (microstepping for surface finish)
- **Inkjet/plotted printers** (open-loop for cost)
- **Robotic关节 joints** (closed-loop for torque safety)
- **Telescope tracking mounts** (microstepping for sub-arcsecond pointing)
- **Medical infusion pumps** (closed-loop for dosage accuracy)
- **Hard disk head positioners** (microstepping + closed-loop hybrid)

The choice between open-loop and closed-loop, and between half-step and microstepping, is an *engineering trade-off* — a recurring theme in KTU design questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides *fully-worked* derivations and numerical problems. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. The KTU 2024 ESE pattern rewards candidates who present **clean, complete working** — incomplete steps invite partial-mark deductions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Angle from Pole/Tooth Count

**Given:** A hybrid stepper motor has $N_s$ stator teeth and $N_r$ rotor teeth, with $p$ pole pairs.

**Objective:** Derive the natural (full) step angle $\theta_{fs}$.

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1 — Identify the magnetic cycle.**
A full electrical cycle (north-south alternation) corresponds to a rotor rotation equal to one *tooth pitch*:

$$\tau_r = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

where $\tau_r$ is the rotor tooth pitch in mechanical degrees.

**Step 2 — Account for the number of electrical cycles per revolution.**
The number of electrical cycles per shaft revolution equals the number of *rotor teeth* (in a hybrid motor):

$$N_e = N_r$$

**Step 3 — Compute the step angle.**
The number of detent positions per revolution is $N_r$ (one stable position per tooth-to-tooth alignment). The full step angle is therefore:

$$\theta_{fs} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

**Step 4 — For a 2-phase motor operated in half-step mode:**

$$\theta_{hs} = \frac{\theta_{fs}}{2} = \frac{360°}{2 N_r} = \frac{180°}{N_r}$$

**Step 5 — For $N_\mu$ microsteps per full step:**

$$\theta_\mu = \frac{360°}{N_r \, N_\mu}$$

**Verification:** A typical hybrid motor has $N_r = 50$ teeth, giving $\theta_{fs} = 7.2°$ (electrical) per step. But the mechanical step is actually $360°/200 = 1.8°$ because *two electrical cycles* per tooth (one full-step = one tooth-pitch motion = 2 electrical cycles for a 2-phase machine). The general formula for a $m$-phase machine is:

$$\theta_{fs,\text{mech}} = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r \cdot p_{\text{ratio}}}$$

where $p_{\text{ratio}}$ is the gear ratio between stator and rotor tooth counts. For a hybrid 2-phase motor with $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth and a 1:4 stator-tooth : rotor-tooth pitch ratio, $N_{\text{steps/rev}} = 50 \times 4 = 200$, giving $\theta_{fs} = 1.8°$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shaft Speed from Pulse Frequency

**Given:** A 200 step/rev motor is driven in **quarter-step** mode (4 microsteps per full step) with a pulse frequency $f_p = 800$ pps.

**Objective:** Find the shaft speed in rpm.

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1 — Compute the total microsteps per revolution:**

$$N_{\mu,\text{total}} = N_r \times N_\mu = 200 \times 4 = 800 \text{ microsteps/rev}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the angular displacement per microstep:**

$$\theta_\mu = \frac{360°}{800} = 0.45°/\text{microstep}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the shaft rotational speed:**

$$n = \frac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_{\mu,\text{total}}} = \frac{60 \times 800}{800} = 60 \text{ rpm}$$

**Alternative direct path:**

$$n = f_p \times \theta_\mu \times \frac{60}{360} = 800 \times 0.45° \times \frac{1}{6} = 60 \text{ rpm} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Microstepping Current Profile

**Given:** A 2-phase motor rated at $I_m = 2.0$ A (peak), driven in 8-microstep mode ($N_\mu = 8$).

**Objective:** Compute the phase currents $I_A(k)$ and $I_B(k)$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 8$ and tabulate the resultant MMF vector angle.

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1 — General formulas (from §2.2.1):**

$$I_A(k) = I_m \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi k}{2 N_\mu}\right), \quad I_B(k) = I_m \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi k}{2 N_\mu}\right)$$

**Step 2 — For $N_\mu = 8$, the electrical angle step is:**

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{90°}{8} = 11.25°$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate:**

| $k$ | $\theta_e$ (°) | $I_A$ (A) | $I_B$ (A) | $I_A^2 + I_B^2$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 0 | 2.000 | 0.000 | 4.00 |
| 1 | 11.25 | 1.962 | 0.390 | 4.00 |
| 2 | 22.50 | 1.848 | 0.765 | 4.00 |
| 3 | 33.75 | 1.663 | 1.111 | 4.00 |
| 4 | 45.00 | 1.414 | 1.414 | 4.00 |
| 5 | 56.25 | 1.111 | 1.663 | 4.00 |
| 6 | 67.50 | 0.765 | 1.848 | 4.00 |
| 7 | 78.75 | 0.390 | 1.962 | 4.00 |
| 8 | 90.00 | 0.000 | 2.000 | 4.00 |

**Step 4 — Verification.** Note that $I_A^2 + I_B^2 = I_m^2 = 4$ for all $k$. This confirms the *constant MMF magnitude* property — the stator field vector rotates in **equal angular increments** of 11.25° electrical (or 11.25°/4 = 2.8125° mechanical for a 1.8° motor in 8× microstepping per full step). The mechanical microstep angle is:

$$\theta_{\mu,\text{mech}} = \frac{1.8°}{8} = 0.225°$$

**Step 5 — Connection to full revolution count:**

$$N_{\mu,\text{total}} = 200 \times 8 = 1600 \text{ microsteps/rev}$$

Equivalent angular resolution:

$$\theta_{\mu,\text{mech}} = \frac{360°}{1600} = 0.225° \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pull-Out Torque at a Given Stepping Frequency

**Given:** A stepper motor has $T_h = 1.2$ Nm holding torque, phase resistance $R = 4.0 \,\Omega$, phase inductance $L = 8.0$ mH.

**Objective:** Find the pull-out torque at $f = 200$ pps (steps/s) and at $f = 1000$ pps.

### Step-by-step:

**Step 1 — Compute the electrical time constant:**

$$\tau_e = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4.0} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the normalized frequency terms:**

At $f = 200$ pps:

$$\frac{2\pi f L}{R} = 2\pi \times 200 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.4\pi \approx 1.257$$

At $f = 1000$ pps:

$$\frac{2\pi f L}{R} = 2\pi \times 1000 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 2.0\pi \approx 6.283$$

**Step 3 — Apply the pull-out formula:**

$$T_{po}(f) = \frac{T_h}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2\pi f L}{R}\right)^2}}$$

At $f = 200$ pps:

$$T_{po}(200) = \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{1 + (1.257)^2}} = \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{1 + 1.580}} = \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{2.580}} = \frac{1.2}{1.606} \approx 0.747 \text{ Nm}$$

At $f = 1000$ pps:

$$T_{po}(1000) = \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{1 + (6.283)^2}} = \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{1 + 39.48}} = \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{40.48}} = \frac{1.2}{6.362} \approx 0.189 \text{ Nm}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**
At 200 pps, the motor retains **62.3 %** of its holding torque. At 1000 pps, it retains only **15.7 %**. This is precisely why high-speed stepper applications demand either *low-inductance* motors, *higher supply voltage* (to slew the current faster), or *closed-loop* control to compensate dynamically.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 1 (Comprehensive — Typical 7-Mark Sub-Part)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
> A 2-phase hybrid stepper motor has 200 full steps per revolution. It is driven in **half-step** mode with a pulse train of frequency $f_p = 500$ Hz. Each phase has $R = 6 \,\Omega$, $L = 10$ mH, and the holding torque is $T_h = 0.8$ Nm.
> **(a)** Calculate the shaft speed in rpm and the time to complete one revolution.
> **(b)** Compute the pull-out torque at the operating frequency. Comment on whether the motor can drive a load of 0.3 Nm at this speed.

### Solution:

**Part (a):**

**Step 1.** Total steps/rev in half-step mode:

$$N_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ half-steps/rev}$$

**Step 2.** Shaft speed in rpm:

$$n = \frac{60 \times f_p}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{60 \times 500}{400} = \frac{30000}{400} = 75 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3.** Time for one revolution:

$$t_{\text{rev}} = \frac{60}{n} = \frac{60}{75} = 0.8 \text{ s}$$

**Part (b):**

**Step 1.** Compute the normalized frequency:

$$\frac{2\pi f L}{R} = 2\pi \times 500 \times \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{6} = 2\pi \times 500 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 2\pi \times 0.8333 = 5.236 \text{ rad (dimensionless)}$$

**Step 2.** Pull-out torque:

$$T_{po} = \frac{T_h}{\sqrt{1 + (5.236)^2}} = \frac{0.8}{\sqrt{1 + 27.42}} = \frac{0.8}{\sqrt{28.42}} = \frac{0.8}{5.331} \approx 0.150 \text{ Nm}$$

**Step 3.** Comparison with load.

The load torque is 0.3 Nm, but the available pull-out torque at 500 Hz is only 0.150 Nm. Since **$T_{\text{load}} > T_{po}$**, the motor will **lose synchronism (stall)** under this load. The drive must either (i) reduce the stepping frequency, (ii) increase the supply voltage to overcome inductance, or (iii) switch to a closed-loop scheme.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Problem 2 (Microstepping Resolution)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
> A 1.8° stepper motor is driven in microstepping mode with 16 microsteps per full step. The pulse generator outputs 4000 pulses per second.
> **(a)** Determine the total number of microsteps per revolution and the mechanical microstep angle in arc-minutes.
> **(b)** Calculate the shaft speed in rpm.

### Solution:

**Part (a):**

**Step 1.** Full steps per revolution:

$$N_r = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Step 2.** Total microsteps per revolution:

$$N_{\mu,\text{total}} = 200 \times 16 = 3200 \text{ microsteps/rev}$$

**Step 3.** Microstep angle:

$$\theta_\mu = \frac{360°}{3200} = 0.1125°$$

Converting to arc-minutes ($1° = 60'$):

$$\theta_\mu = 0.1125° \times 60 = 6.75 \text{ arc-minutes}$$

**Part (b):**

$$n = \frac{60 \times 4000}{3200} = \frac{240000}{3200} = 75 \text{ rpm}$$

---

## 3.7 Numerical Problem 3 (Resonance and Acceleration)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Model]**
> A closed-loop stepper drive has effective inertia $J_{\text{eff}} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ kg·m², torque constant $K_t = 0.4$ Nm/A, and is operated in 4-microstep mode.
> **(a)** Calculate the mechanical resonance frequency.
> **(b)** Suggest two design modifications to raise the safe operating speed above the resonance.

### Solution:

**Part (a):**

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{K_t N_\mu}{J_{\text{eff}}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{0.4 \times 4}{5 \times 10^{-5}}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1.6}{5 \times 10^{-5}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{32000} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 178.89 = 28.47 \text{ Hz}$$

**Part (b):**
1. **Increase $N_\mu$** (e.g., from 4 to 16) — raises the effective detent stiffness, pushing $f_r$ higher.
2. **Reduce $J_{\text{eff}}$** by using a low-inertia coupling or a lighter rotor/load.
3. **Add mechanical damping** (viscous damper or elastomeric coupling) — reduces resonance peak amplitude.
4. **Use acceleration ramps** to *pass through* $f_r$ quickly (typically in < 50 ms).

---

## 3.8 Analytical Summary Matrix (KTU 7-Mark Style Answer Scaffold)

| Question Theme | Required Concepts | Required Formulas | Common Mistakes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Step angle / resolution | Tooth count, microstep ratio | $\theta_\mu = 360°/N$ | Mixing electrical vs mechanical degrees |
| Shaft speed | Pulse freq, total steps | $n = 60 f_p / N_{\text{total}}$ | Forgetting factor of 2 for half-step |
| Microstepping current | Sinusoidal profile | $I_A = I_m\cos\theta$, $I_B = I_m\sin\theta$ | Confusing $N_\mu$ (per step) with $N_{\text{total}}$ |
| Pull-out torque | Inductance-limited drop | $T_{po} = T_h / \sqrt{1 + (2\pi f L/R)^2}$ | Wrong unit on $L/R$ |
| Resonance | Stiffness-inertia spring-mass | $f_r = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{K/J}$ | Confusing with electrical resonance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams are rendered using **Mermaid** syntax (KTU-accepted) with strictly alphanumeric node IDs and clean labels.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Half-Step Excitation State Machine (8-State Sequence)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S1_Aonly
    S1_Aonly : State 1 - Phase A ON, Phase B OFF (0 degrees)
    S1_Aonly --> S2_ABon
    S2_ABon : State 2 - Phase A ON, Phase B ON (0.9 degrees)
    S2_ABon --> S3_Bonly
    S3_Bonly : State 3 - Phase A OFF, Phase B ON (1.8 degrees)
    S3_Bonly --> S4_Bonly_offA
    S4_Bonly_offA : State 4 - Phase A OFF reverse, Phase B ON (2.7 degrees)
    S4_Bonly_offA --> S5_Aoff_Boff
    S5_Aoff_Boff : State 5 - Both phases transitioning (3.6 degrees)
    S5_Aoff_Boff --> S6_Aon_Boff
    S6_Aon_Boff : State 6 - Phase A ON reverse, Phase B OFF (4.5 degrees)
    S6_Aon_Boff --> S7_Aon_Bon
    S7_Aon_Bon : State 7 - Phase A ON, Phase B ON reverse (5.4 degrees)
    S7_Aon_Bon --> S8_Aoff_Bon
    S8_Aoff_Bon : State 8 - Phase A OFF, Phase B ON reverse (6.3 degrees)
    S8_Aoff_Bon --> [*]
```

**Interpretation:** The 8 states yield 8 stable positions per electrical cycle (4 in full-step × 2). For a 200 step/rev motor, this produces 400 half-steps/rev. Note that states 1, 3, 5, 7 are *single-phase-on* (holding torque $T_h$) and states 2, 4, 6, 8 are *two-phase-on* (holding torque $\sqrt{2}\,T_h$).

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OpenLoop[OPEN LOOP DRIVE]
        O1[Microcontroller or PLC] -->|Step and Direction pulses| O2[Translator Chip e.g. L297]
        O2 -->|Phase sequence logic| O3[PWM Current Chopper]
        O3 -->|PWM drive signals| O4[H Bridge Power Stage]
        O4 -->|Phase currents I_A I_B| O5[Stepper Motor]
        O5 -->|Mechanical shaft| O6[Mechanical Load]
    end

    subgraph ClosedLoop[CLOSED LOOP SERVO STEPPER]
        C1[Position Reference Input] --> C2[Error Calculator Subtractor]
        C2 -->|Error signal e| C3[PI or PID Controller]
        C3 -->|Corrected current command| C4[PWM Driver with FOC option]
        C4 -->|Phase currents| C5[Stepper Motor]
        C5 --> C6[Mechanical Load]
        C6 --> C7[Incremental Encoder or Resolver]
        C7 -->|Actual position feedback| C2
    end

    style OpenLoop fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style ClosedLoop fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
```

**Interpretation:** The closed-loop path (right) features a *feedback loop* from encoder to the error calculator. The controller actively *minimises* position error, eliminating lost steps and allowing 100 % torque utilization at any speed. The open-loop path (left) has no feedback — economical but vulnerable to stall.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block Diagram of a Microstepping Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host Controller<br>Microcontroller or PC] -->|Step pulse train and Direction| B[Motion Profile Generator<br>Acceleration Deceleration ramps]
    B -->|Variable frequency pulse train| C[Mini Lookup Table<br>Sin and Cos values for I_A and I_B]
    C -->|I_A_ref and I_B_ref DAC outputs| D[Dual Channel PWM Current Chopper]
    D -->|High side and Low side gate signals| E[Dual H Bridge MOSFET Power Stage]
    E -->|Pulsed DC voltage| F[Stepper Motor Winding A and Winding B]
    F -->|Torque and Position| G[Mechanical Load]
    G -->|Optional closed loop feedback| H[Encoder or Resolver]
    H -->|Position and Velocity feedback| B
```

**Interpretation:** The microstepping drive maintains a *sinusoidal current ratio* between the two phases. The lookup table (or computed-on-the-fly) sets the reference values; the PWM chopper regulates actual current via a closed *current loop* within the driver. The motion profile generator ensures smooth acceleration/deceleration, preventing resonance-induced stalls.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Microstepping Current Waveforms (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Currents[PHASE CURRENT VS MICROSTEP INDEX]
        I_A[I_A waveform<br>Cosine shape<br>Starts at I_m, falls to 0]
        I_B[I_B waveform<br>Sine shape<br>Starts at 0, rises to I_m]
    end
    I_A --- I_B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Mermaid Limitation:** Mermaid cannot natively render time-domain sinusoidal waveforms. The diagram above is a *logical* representation. In an exam, you would hand-draw two phase-shifted sinusoids with the $X$-axis as *microstep index* and $Y$-axis as *current* in amperes. The two sinusoids are in **electrical quadrature** (90° phase shift) and intersect at the *midpoint microstep* ($k = N_\mu/2$), where $I_A = I_B = I_m/\sqrt{2}$.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Torque-Speed Characteristic of a Stepper Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stepper Motor Torque Speed Curve] --> B[Region 1: Start Stop Band<br>Below f_pullin<br>Governed by T_pullin<br>All start stop moves allowed]
    A --> C[Region 2: Slew Range<br>Above f_pullin<br>Governed by T_pullout<br>Must ramp accelerate]
    A --> D[Resonance Trough<br>Near f_resonance<br>Reduced torque<br>Caused by rotor inertia]
    A --> E[Cutoff Region<br>Above f_max<br>Torque approaches 0<br>Back EMF dominates]

    B --> B1[Use open loop OK]
    C --> C1[Closed loop preferred]
    D --> D1[Damping or microstepping required]
    E --> E1[Reduce frequency or use higher voltage]
```

**Interpretation:** This conceptual block diagram summarizes the *four operating regions* of a stepper motor's torque-speed curve. The **start-stop band** (low frequency) is where open-loop control is reliable. The **slew range** requires ramped acceleration. The **resonance trough** must be damped or accelerated through. The **cutoff region** is unusable for any meaningful load.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below follow the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: **Part A (3 marks, short answer)** and **Part B (14 marks, internal choice with two sub-parts of 7 marks each)**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define half-step mode of operation of a stepper motor. How does it differ from full-step mode in terms of resolution and torque ripple?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Half-step mode is an excitation technique in which the phase energization pattern alternates between single-phase-on and two-phase-on states, producing twice the number of stable detent positions per revolution compared to full-step mode.

**Resolution (1 mark):** For a 1.8° (200 step/rev) motor, half-step mode gives 400 steps/rev with step angle $\theta_{hs} = 0.9°$ (i.e., resolution is doubled).

**Torque Ripple (1 mark):** The holding torque alternates between $T_h$ (single-phase-on) and $\sqrt{2}\,T_h$ (two-phase-on), introducing a torque ripple of approximately **41 %** of $T_h$. The torque ripple is *higher* than in full-step two-phase-on mode, but the *average* torque capability is comparable.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List three advantages of closed-loop control of a stepper motor over open-loop control.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Elimination of lost-step failure** — encoder feedback detects stall, controller corrects current to re-acquire position. *(1 mark)*
2. **Higher torque at high speed** — controller boosts current on demand based on position error, partially overcoming the inductance-induced $T_{po}$ dropoff. *(1 mark)*
3. **Reduced heat dissipation** — phase current is regulated to the *minimum required* for the load, instead of always being at rated value. Improves efficiency and motor longevity. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 ESE provides an **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions from the same module. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are both fully solved.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a phase-excitation sequence diagram, explain **half-step mode** of operation of a 2-phase hybrid stepper motor. Derive the relationship between holding torque in the two-phase-on state and the single-phase-on state. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **micro-stepping**. For a 1.8° stepper motor driven in 10-microstep mode, compute the total microsteps per revolution, the mechanical microstep angle, and the shaft speed when the pulse frequency is 3000 Hz. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Half-Step Mode (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Excitation Sequence (2 marks):**
The 8-state half-step sequence for a 2-phase motor is:

| State | Phase A | Phase B | Rotor Position (mech. °) | Torque |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | +I | 0 | 0 | $T_h$ |
| 2 | +I | +I | 0.9 | $\sqrt{2}\,T_h$ |
| 3 | 0 | +I | 1.8 | $T_h$ |
| 4 | −I | +I | 2.7 | $\sqrt{2}\,T_h$ |
| 5 | −I | 0 | 3.6 | $T_h$ |
| 6 | −I | −I | 4.5 | $\sqrt{2}\,T_h$ |
| 7 | 0 | −I | 5.4 | $T_h$ |
| 8 | +I | −I | 6.3 | $\sqrt{2}\,T_h$ |

**Step 2 — Derivation of Two-Phase-On Torque (3 marks):**
The electromagnetic torque is:

$$T = K_t \, I_A \sin\theta + K_t \, I_B \cos\theta$$

For *single-phase-on* ($I_B = 0$, $I_A = I_m$), maximum torque is:

$$T_h = K_t \, I_m$$

For *two-phase-on* ($I_A = I_B = I_m$), the resultant MMF is at 45° to both phase axes. The torque is:

$$T_{2\phi} = K_t \left[ I_m \sin\theta + I_m \cos\theta \right]$$

Maximising over $\theta$:

$$\frac{dT_{2\phi}}{d\theta} = K_t \left[ I_m \cos\theta - I_m \sin\theta \right] = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = 45°$$

Substituting $\theta = 45°$:

$$T_{2\phi} = K_t I_m (\sin 45° + \cos 45°) = K_t I_m \cdot \sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2}\,T_h$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion (2 marks):**
The two-phase-on state provides $\sqrt{2}$ times the holding torque of the single-phase-on state, but the rotor aligns at an *intermediate* position (45° between phase axes), effectively *interpolating* a half-step. The motor achieves 400 positions per revolution in half-step mode versus 200 in full-step, **doubling the resolution**.

**Part (b) — Micro-Stepping (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Micro-stepping is a current-profiling technique in which each full step is subdivided into $N_\mu$ smaller increments by driving the two phase windings with sinusoidally proportioned currents. The rotor follows the rotating MMF vector with $N_\mu$ stable positions per full step, producing smoother motion and finer resolution.

**Computation (5 marks):**

**Step 1.** Total microsteps per revolution:

$$N_{\mu,\text{total}} = 200 \times 10 = 2000 \text{ microsteps/rev}$$

**Step 2.** Mechanical microstep angle:

$$\theta_\mu = \frac{1.8°}{10} = 0.18°$$

Or equivalently:

$$\theta_\mu = \frac{360°}{2000} = 0.18° \quad \checkmark$$

Converting to arc-seconds: $0.18° \times 3600 = 648''$.

**Step 3.** Shaft speed:

$$n = \frac{60 \times f_p}{N_{\mu,\text{total}}} = \frac{60 \times 3000}{2000} = \frac{180000}{2000} = 90 \text{ rpm}$$

**Answer Summary:** 2000 microsteps/rev; microstep angle = 0.18° (648''); shaft speed = 90 rpm.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **static and dynamic characteristics** of a stepper motor. Define holding torque, detent torque, pull-out torque, and pull-in torque. Sketch the typical torque-speed curve. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **open-loop and closed-loop** control schemes for stepper motors. A 2-phase, 1.8° stepper has $R = 5 \,\Omega$, $L = 6$ mH, $T_h = 1.0$ Nm. Compute the pull-out torque at $f = 300$ pps. If the load torque is 0.25 Nm, will the motor stall in open-loop at this frequency? Justify. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Static and Dynamic Characteristics (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Static Characteristics (3 marks):**
- **Holding Torque ($T_h$):** Maximum torque the motor can exert on the shaft when *energized but stationary*, before slipping. Determined by $K_t \cdot I_m$.
- **Detent Torque ($T_d$):** Small residual torque present *with all phases de-energized*, due to permanent magnet flux in hybrid motors. Typically 1–5 % of $T_h$. Provides *passive* position retention.
- **Step Accuracy:** Deviation between commanded and actual rotor position. Specified as *non-cumulative* (per step) and *cumulative* (over 360°). Premium motors: ±3 % / ±5 %.

**Step 2 — Dynamic Characteristics (3 marks):**
- **Pull-Out Torque ($T_{po}$):** Maximum load torque the motor can drive at a given stepping frequency *without losing synchronism* during slewing. Decreases with frequency due to back-EMF and winding inductance.
- **Pull-In Torque ($T_{pi}$):** Maximum load torque against which the motor can *start, stop, or reverse* at a given stepping rate.
- **Resonance Frequency ($f_r$):** Stepping rate at which rotor inertia and magnetic restoring torque produce mechanical resonance, causing torque dip and possible stall.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time for rotor oscillations to decay to within tolerance band after a step command.

**Step 3 — Torque-Speed Curve (1 mark):**
Sketch description: $T_{po}$ is maximum at low frequency (≈$T_h$), remains nearly constant up to a *knee frequency*, then drops hyperbolically as $T_{po} \propto 1/f$. A *trough* appears at $f_r$ due to resonance. $T_{pi}$ is always ≤ $T_{po}$ and defines the *start-stop boundary*.

**Part (b) — Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop + Pull-Out Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Position feedback | None | Encoder/Resolver |
| Stall recovery | Not possible | Automatic correction |
| Heat dissipation | High (always full current) | Lower (current on demand) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate–High |
| High-speed torque | Limited by $T_{po}$ | Enhanced by current boost |
| Tuning complexity | Low | High (PID required) |

**Step 2 — Pull-Out Torque Calculation (3 marks):**

$$T_{po}(f) = \frac{T_h}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2\pi f L}{R}\right)^2}}$$

Compute the dimensionless term:

$$\frac{2\pi f L}{R} = \frac{2\pi \times 300 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{5} = \frac{2\pi \times 1.8}{5} = \frac{11.31}{5} = 2.262$$

Therefore:

$$T_{po} = \frac{1.0}{\sqrt{1 + (2.262)^2}} = \frac{1.0}{\sqrt{1 + 5.117}} = \frac{1.0}{\sqrt{6.117}} = \frac{1.0}{2.473} = 0.404 \text{ Nm}$$

**Step 3 — Stall Check (1 mark):**
The load torque (0.25 Nm) is **less than** the pull-out torque (0.404 Nm). Therefore, the motor will **not stall** in open-loop at 300 pps under this load. The drive has a safety margin of 0.154 Nm (≈ 38 % of $T_{po}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** when computing total steps in half-step mode. Many students use $N_r = 200$ instead of $N_{\text{total}} = 400$ for half-step, leading to a *factor-of-2 error* in shaft speed.
> 2. **Confusing electrical and mechanical degrees** in microstepping calculations. Always confirm whether the given step angle is electrical (per pole pair) or mechanical (per shaft revolution).
> 3. **Mixing up $N_\mu$ (per full step) with $N_{\mu,\text{total}}$ (per revolution).** $N_\mu = 10$ means 10 microsteps *per full step*, so the per-revolution count is $200 \times 10 = 2000$.
> 4. **Using degrees in the cosine formula** instead of radians. The argument of $\cos$ and $\sin$ in $I_A = I_m \cos(\pi k / 2N_\mu)$ must be in *radians* (the $\pi$ ensures this).
> 5. **Omitting the comparison between load torque and pull-out torque** in stall-check problems. A numerical answer alone is incomplete; the KTU rubric awards marks for the *interpretive conclusion*.
> 6. **In resonance problems, forgetting that $N_\mu$ affects $f_r$.** Microstepping effectively *raises* the resonance frequency by a factor of $\sqrt{N_\mu}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam.**

- ☐ **Half-step mode** alternates *single-phase-on* and *two-phase-on* states, doubling resolution (e.g., 200 → 400 steps/rev) but introducing a $\sqrt{2}$:1 torque ripple.
- ☐ **Micro-stepping** uses sinusoidal phase currents to produce up to hundreds of sub-positions per full step, drastically reducing torque ripple, resonance, and acoustic noise.
- ☐ The fundamental microstep formula is $\theta_\mu = \theta_{fs}/N_\mu$, and the total microsteps per revolution is $N_r \times N_\mu$.
- ☐ The shaft speed in rpm is universally $n = 60 f_p / N_{\text{total}}$ — memorize this.
- ☐ **Static characteristics** (holding, detent, accuracy) describe the *stationary* motor.
- ☐ **Dynamic characteristics** (pull-out, pull-in, resonance, settling time) describe the *moving* motor.
- ☐ **Pull-out torque** drops hyperbolically with frequency: $T_{po}(f) = T_h / \sqrt{1 + (2\pi f L/R)^2}$.
- ☐ **Resonance frequency** scales as $f_r \propto \sqrt{N_\mu / J_{\text{eff}}}$, so microstepping *raises* $f_r$ and reduces its amplitude.
- ☐ **Open-loop control** is simple and cheap but vulnerable to lost steps under overload.
- ☐ **Closed-loop control** uses an encoder to detect and correct stall; enables higher torque at speed, lower heating, and 100 % stall tolerance.
- ☐ **Microstepping does NOT improve absolute accuracy indefinitely** — beyond ~20 microsteps/full step, gains are limited by friction, magnetic asymmetry, and DAC resolution.
- ☐ **Current profiling formulas:** $I_A = I_m \cos(\pi k / 2N_\mu)$, $I_B = I_m \sin(\pi k / 2N_\mu)$ — must sum in quadrature to a constant magnitude $I_m$.
- ☐ **Applications:** CNC, 3D printers, robotics, plotters, telescopes, infusion pumps, disk drives, textile machines, valve actuators.
- ☐ **Numerical-problem safety net formulas** to remember cold: $\theta_{fs} = 360°/N_r$, $n = 60f_p/N$, $T_{po} = T_h / \sqrt{1 + (2\pi f L/R)^2}$, $f_r = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{K_t N_\mu / J_{\text{eff}}}$.

---
