topics.title: Synchronous Reluctance Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Synchronous Reluctance Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

A **Synchronous Reluctance Motor (SynRM)** is a salient-pole synchronous machine that develops electromagnetic torque purely from the **variation of magnetic reluctance** between the direct axis (d-axis) and the quadrature axis (q-axis) of the rotor. Unlike a conventional synchronous motor, the SynRM has **no rotor field winding, no permanent magnets, and no damper bars**, and therefore requires no separate DC excitation. The rotor is a passive, anisotropic (non-uniform) ferromagnetic body that is pulled into alignment with the rotating stator field because the magnetic circuit always seeks the **path of minimum reluctance** (analogous to Ohm's law for magnetic circuits).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** *"A synchronous reluctance motor is a brushless motor that produces torque solely by the tendency of its salient-pole rotor to align with the axis of minimum reluctance of the rotating magnetic field produced by the stator."*

The defining electrical parameter is the **saliency ratio** (often denoted $\xi$), which the examiner expects you to know by definition:

$$\xi = \frac{L_d}{L_q} = \frac{X_d}{X_q}$$

For any meaningful reluctance torque, we **must** have $L_d > L_q$, i.e., $\xi > 1$. State-of-the-art SynRMs operate with $\xi$ between **6 and 10**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are holding a **bar magnet** above a non-uniform steel plate on a table. If you rotate your hand, the bar magnet does not simply sit still — it twists and snaps so that its longest axis aligns with the gap in the steel plate. The steel plate "wants" the flux to take the easiest possible path, so it physically drags the magnet into the position of minimum reluctance.

A synchronous reluctance motor works on exactly the same principle:

- The **stator** is the moving hand, generating a **rotating magnetic field**.
- The **anisotropic rotor** is the bar magnet — but with a built-in asymmetry. Along one axis (d-axis) it is **highly permeable** (iron path), and along the perpendicular axis (q-axis) it is **interrupted by flux barriers or slots** (air gaps).
- The rotor "snaps" into the position of least reluctance every electrical cycle, and because the stator field is rotating, the rotor follows it at **exactly synchronous speed**, with no slip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** The motor has no magnets and no brushes. It is essentially a piece of shaped iron that is magnetically "chased" around a circle by a rotating field. The chasing force is called **reluctance torque**.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics to Remember (KTU High-Frequency)

The following constants and metrics are routinely expected in KTU answer scripts and must be memorized in **bold** below:

- **Supply Frequency:** Typically **50 Hz** (India standard) or 60 Hz.
- **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ where **P** = number of stator poles.
- **No-load speed:** Exactly $N_s$ (no slip, even at no-load).
- **Saliency Ratio ($\xi$):** $L_d / L_q \approx$ **6 to 10** for modern SynRMs.
- **Pull-out (Maximum) Torque:** Occurs at a **load angle of $\delta = 45^\circ$** (electrical).
- **Power Factor:** Typically **0.7 to 0.85 lagging** at rated load — lower than induction motor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture (Why each part exists)

The SynRM has two main subsystems:

### A. Stator (Standard AC Stator)
- **Why?** It must produce a smooth, sinusoidally distributed, rotating magnetomotive force (MMF).
- **What?** Identical to a three-phase induction motor or synchronous motor stator:
  - Laminated silicon-steel core (slots).
  - Three-phase distributed winding placed in the slots.
  - Number of poles P is selected for the desired synchronous speed.
- **How?** A balanced three-phase AC supply (displaced by 120° electrical) generates a constant-magnitude MMF that rotates at synchronous speed.

### B. Rotor (The Anisotropic, Salient-Pole, Passive Body)
- **Why?** This is the heart of the SynRM. It must be magnetically asymmetric — *high* permeance along one axis and *low* permeance along the perpendicular axis.
- **What?** Modern rotors use one of the following:
  1. **Axially Laminated Anisotropic (ALA) rotor:** Steel laminations rolled into a cylinder with insulating layers, producing high $L_d/L_q$ ratio.
  2. **Transversely Laminated Anisotropic (TLA) rotor:** Stacked laminations punched with precise **flux barriers** (air pockets) along the q-axis.
  3. **Line-start SynRM rotor:** A cage winding added for asynchronous starting (hybrid machine).
- **How?** The flux barriers force magnetic flux lines to crowd into the d-axis path, raising $L_d$, while flux through the q-axis must cross air, lowering $L_q$. This produces the saliency ratio $\xi > 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no field winding or magnets?** Eliminating the rotor excitation removes rotor copper losses, eliminates brush maintenance, and reduces cost — but at the price of a lower torque density and a low power factor.

## 2.2 Working Principle (Operational Logic Steps)

1. **Stator Energization:** A balanced three-phase AC voltage is applied to the stator winding.
2. **Rotating MMF:** A sinusoidal, constant-amplitude rotating MMF $F_s$ is created, rotating at synchronous speed $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s electrically.
3. **Flux Density Distribution:** This rotating MMF induces flux lines that try to cross the air gap. Because the rotor is anisotropic ($L_d \neq L_q$), the flux density is **not uniform** around the air gap.
4. **Reluctance Torque Development:** The rotor experiences a mechanical force tending to **minimize the stored magnetic energy** by aligning the high-permeance d-axis with the rotating MMF axis.
5. **Synchronous Locking:** Once aligned, the rotor is "magnetically locked" to the rotating field and rotates at $\omega_s$ with **no slip**.
6. **Mechanical Output:** Useful shaft torque $T_e$ is delivered to the load through the shaft.

## 2.3 The Torque Mechanism — *Why* it produces useful torque

The instantaneous electromagnetic torque in any rotating machine is given by the partial derivative of the magnetic co-energy $W'$ with respect to rotor mechanical position $\theta_m$:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m}$$

In a salient-pole machine, the air-gap co-energy depends on rotor position because $L_d \neq L_q$. Evaluating this derivative (covered in detail in Section 3) yields the **reluctance torque equation**, which is the heart of the KTU module.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / KTU Use |
|---|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed in RPM (used everywhere) |
| 2 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed in rad/s electrical |
| 3 | $\xi = \dfrac{L_d}{L_q} = \dfrac{X_d}{X_q}$ | Saliency ratio — must be $> 1$ |
| 4 | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{(X_d - X_q)}{X_d X_q} \sin(2\delta)$ | Electromagnetic torque (the *main* KTU equation) |
| 5 | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{(\xi - 1)}{\xi X_d} \sin(2\delta)$ | Torque expressed using saliency ratio |
| 6 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{(X_d - X_q)}{X_d X_q}$ | Pull-out torque magnitude (at $\delta = 45^\circ$) |
| 7 | $\delta_{pull-out} = 45^\circ$ | Load angle at which maximum torque occurs |
| 8 | $P_{gap} = \omega_s \cdot T_e$ | Air-gap power developed |
| 9 | $W'_{co} = \dfrac{3}{2} \left[ \dfrac{1}{2} L_d i_d^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} L_q i_q^2 \right]$ | Co-energy stored in air gap |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Efficiency (no rotor copper loss ⇒ high) |
| 11 | $\cos\phi \approx 0.7$ to $0.85$ lag | Typical full-load power factor |
| 12 | $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | For star-connected stator (3-phase standard) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Equation 4 above is the *single most important* formula for this module. Memorize it in both reactance ($X_d, X_q$) and inductance ($L_d, L_q$) forms. Always substitute $X = \omega L$ correctly when switching forms.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Variable-speed industrial drives** (pumps, fans, compressors) — replacing induction motors for higher efficiency.
- **Servo and robotics applications** — high dynamic response, no encoder drift due to no magnets.
- **Electric vehicle traction** — used in some hybrid powertrains where temperature-sensitive magnets are undesirable.
- **High-speed turbomolecular pumps and spindles** — no rotor windings to fail at high RPM.
- **Refrigeration compressors** — line-start versions replace induction motors for IE4/IE5 efficiency class compliance.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains the *complete* step-by-step derivation of the electromagnetic torque equation — the single most important analytical task in this KTU module. Every algebraic step is shown with its physical interpretation.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Reluctance Torque Equation

### Step 1 — Define the d-q Reference Frame

The rotor is salient (asymmetric). We attach a rotating reference frame to the rotor itself. Two orthogonal axes are defined:

- **Direct axis (d-axis):** The axis of **highest permeance** (lowest reluctance), i.e., the direction of magnetic flux concentration.
- **Quadrature axis (q-axis):** The axis perpendicular to the d-axis. Flux barriers and slotting make this the axis of **lowest permeance** (highest reluctance).

The angular displacement between the stator voltage phasor $\vec{V}$ and the rotor q-axis is called the **load angle (or torque angle)** $\delta$.

### Step 2 — Write the Stator Voltage Equations (Steady-State, Synchronously Rotating Frame)

In the rotor reference frame, the stator voltage components along d and q axes are:

$$v_{sd} = R_s i_{sd} - \omega_s L_q i_{sq}$$

$$v_{sq} = R_s i_{sq} + \omega_s L_d i_{sd}$$

Neglecting stator copper loss ($R_s \approx 0$) for clarity:

$$v_{sd} = -\omega_s L_q i_{sq} = -X_q i_{sq}$$

$$v_{sq} = \omega_s L_d i_{sd} = X_d i_{sd}$$

### Step 3 — Express the Voltage Components Using the Load Angle $\delta$

If the stator terminal phase voltage is $V$ and the load angle is $\delta$, then the d- and q-axis components are:

$$v_{sd} = V \sin\delta$$

$$v_{sq} = V \cos\delta$$

> *Interpretation:* When $\delta = 0$, the voltage phasor lies on the q-axis. When $\delta = 90^\circ$, it lies on the d-axis. The sign convention follows standard KTU textbook (Fitzgerald, P.S. Bimbhra style).

### Step 4 — Solve for the d- and q-axis Stator Currents

Substituting Step 3 into Step 2 (ignoring $R_s$):

$$V \sin\delta = -X_q i_{sq} \implies i_{sq} = -\dfrac{V \sin\delta}{X_q}$$

$$V \cos\delta = X_d i_{sd} \implies i_{sd} = \dfrac{V \cos\delta}{X_d}$$

### Step 5 — Write the Air-Gap Co-Energy Expression

For a salient-pole synchronous machine *without* rotor excitation ($i_{fd} = 0$), the air-gap magnetic co-energy stored in the field is:

$$W'_{co} = \dfrac{3}{2}\left[\dfrac{1}{2} L_d i_{sd}^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} L_q i_{sq}^2\right] = \dfrac{3}{4}\left[L_d i_{sd}^2 + L_q i_{sq}^2\right]$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Electromagnetic Torque by Partial Derivative

Electromagnetic torque is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to mechanical angle $\theta_m$:

$$T_e = \dfrac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m}$$

Note that $i_{sd}, i_{sq}$ depend on $\theta_m$ via $\delta$, and for an electrical angle we have $\delta = (P/2)\theta_m$, hence $\dfrac{d\delta}{d\theta_m} = \dfrac{P}{2}$.

Apply the chain rule:

$$T_e = \dfrac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial i_{sd}}\dfrac{di_{sd}}{d\delta}\dfrac{d\delta}{d\theta_m} + \dfrac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial i_{sq}}\dfrac{di_{sq}}{d\delta}\dfrac{d\delta}{d\theta_m}$$

Compute each term:

- $\dfrac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial i_{sd}} = \dfrac{3}{2} L_d i_{sd}$
- $\dfrac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial i_{sq}} = \dfrac{3}{2} L_q i_{sq}$

Therefore:

$$T_e = \dfrac{P}{2}\left[\dfrac{3}{2} L_d i_{sd}\dfrac{di_{sd}}{d\delta} + \dfrac{3}{2} L_q i_{sq}\dfrac{di_{sq}}{d\delta}\right]$$

### Step 7 — Differentiate Currents with Respect to $\delta$

From Step 4:

$$\dfrac{di_{sd}}{d\delta} = -\dfrac{V \sin\delta}{X_d} = -\dfrac{i_{sq}}{\xi}\cdot\dfrac{X_q}{X_d}\cdot(\text{but directly:})$$

$$\dfrac{di_{sd}}{d\delta} = -\dfrac{V \sin\delta}{X_d}$$

$$\dfrac{di_{sq}}{d\delta} = -\dfrac{V \cos\delta}{X_q}$$

### Step 8 — Substitute and Simplify

Plug into the torque expression:

$$T_e = \dfrac{3P}{4}\left[L_d i_{sd}\left(-\dfrac{V\sin\delta}{X_d}\right) + L_q i_{sq}\left(-\dfrac{V\cos\delta}{X_q}\right)\right]$$

Since $X_d = \omega_s L_d$ and $X_q = \omega_s L_q$, we can simplify $L_d/X_d = L_q/X_q = 1/\omega_s$:

$$T_e = -\dfrac{3P}{4\omega_s}\left[V i_{sd}\sin\delta + V i_{sq}\cos\delta\right]$$

Substitute $i_{sd}$ and $i_{sq}$ from Step 4:

$$T_e = -\dfrac{3P}{4\omega_s}\left[V\cdot\dfrac{V\cos\delta}{X_d}\cdot\sin\delta + \left(-\dfrac{V\sin\delta}{X_q}\right)\cdot V\cos\delta\right]$$

$$T_e = -\dfrac{3PV^2}{4\omega_s}\left[\dfrac{\sin\delta\cos\delta}{X_d} - \dfrac{\sin\delta\cos\delta}{X_q}\right]$$

$$T_e = -\dfrac{3PV^2}{4\omega_s}\sin\delta\cos\delta\left[\dfrac{1}{X_d} - \dfrac{1}{X_q}\right]$$

Using the identity $2\sin\delta\cos\delta = \sin(2\delta)$:

$$T_e = -\dfrac{3PV^2}{8\omega_s}\sin(2\delta)\left[\dfrac{X_q - X_d}{X_d X_q}\right]$$

Reordering (and noting the negative sign in front of $(X_q - X_d)$ flips sign):

$$\boxed{\,T_e = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{P}{2}\cdot\dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s}\cdot\dfrac{(X_d - X_q)}{X_d X_q}\sin(2\delta)\,}$$

This is the **canonical reluctance torque equation** expected in every KTU answer script.

### Step 9 — Identify the Maximum Torque and the Critical Load Angle

Differentiate $T_e$ with respect to $\delta$ and set to zero:

$$\dfrac{dT_e}{d\delta} \propto \cos(2\delta) = 0$$

$$\implies 2\delta = 90^\circ \implies \delta_{max} = 45^\circ$$

Substituting $\delta = 45^\circ$:

$$\boxed{\,T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{P}{2}\cdot\dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s}\cdot\dfrac{(X_d - X_q)}{X_d X_q}\,}$$

### Step 10 — Express Torque in Terms of Saliency Ratio $\xi$

Recall $X_q = X_d/\xi$. Substituting:

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{P}{2}\cdot\dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s}\cdot\dfrac{(X_d - X_d/\xi)}{X_d(X_d/\xi)}\sin(2\delta)$$

After simplification:

$$\boxed{\,T_e = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{P}{2}\cdot\dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s}\cdot\dfrac{(\xi - 1)}{\xi X_d}\sin(2\delta)\,}$$

This shows clearly that **higher saliency ratio $\xi$ produces higher reluctance torque** — the central design goal of SynRM rotor engineering.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Term Matters

| Term | Physical Meaning | Engineering Implication |
|------|------------------|-------------------------|
| $V^2$ | Torque is proportional to square of supply voltage | Voltage dip directly halves torque (worst case: $1/4$) |
| $X_d - X_q$ | Rotor saliency — the *cause* of reluctance torque | Must be maximized by flux-barrier design |
| $X_d X_q$ | Denominator — saturation limit | Larger denominator ⇒ lower torque per ampere |
| $\sin(2\delta)$ | Angular dependence | Torque is bidirectional (motor + generator) |
| $\xi = L_d/L_q$ | Saliency ratio | Modern SynRM targets $\xi \geq 8$ |
| $\omega_s$ | Synchronous speed | Torque inversely proportional to speed |
| $(P/2)$ | Pole pairs | More poles ⇒ more torque per ampere |

The following block diagram captures the major subsystems of a synchronous reluctance motor and the physical relationships between them.

```mermaid

        QaxisBarrier["Quadrature Axis Path - Interrupted by Flux Barriers"]
        FluxBarrier1["Flux Barrier 1"]
        FluxBarrier2["Flux Barrier 2"]

    end

    SupplyInput["3-Phase AC Supply - 50 Hz"]

    SupplyInput --> StatorSlots
    StatorSlots --> StatorCore

    QaxisBarrier --> ShaftOut
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The three-phase supply energizes the stator winding, which produces a rotating magnetic flux in the air gap. This flux encounters two distinct magnetic paths in the rotor — a high-permeability d-axis path (smooth iron) and a low-permeability q-axis path (interrupted by flux barriers). The rotor aligns itself continuously with the rotating field, delivering mechanical power at the synchronous speed.

The following block diagram highlights the analytical relationship between torque, load angle, and the machine's operating limits.

    subgraph "Operating Envelope of SynRM"
        O["Origin 0"]
        MOTOR_REGION["MOTOR REGION - delta between 0 and 90 degrees"]

        GEN_REGION["GENERATOR REGION - delta between 90 and 180 degrees"]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define synchronous reluctance motor. What is meant by saliency ratio? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A synchronous reluctance motor (SynRM) is a brushless, self-starting synchronous machine in which electromagnetic torque is produced purely by the **variation of magnetic reluctance** between the direct axis (d-axis) and the quadrature axis (q-axis) of a salient-pole, unexcited rotor. The rotor has neither field winding nor permanent magnets.

**Saliency ratio** $\xi$ is defined as the ratio of the d-axis synchronous inductance to the q-axis synchronous inductance:

$$\xi = \dfrac{L_d}{L_q} = \dfrac{X_d}{X_q}$$

For useful torque generation, **$\xi > 1$** is mandatory. Modern SynRMs achieve $\xi$ in the range 6 to 10.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark saliency ratio definition + 1 mark typical values.)*

---

### Question 2: Why is a synchronous reluctance motor not self-starting when directly switched on to the supply? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A SynRM is **not self-starting** when directly switched across the supply for the following reasons:

1. **Zero average starting torque:** At standstill, the stator flux rotates at synchronous speed, but the rotor, being passive, cannot instantaneously follow such a fast rotating field. The rotor oscillates rather than producing a unidirectional torque because $\sin(2\delta)$ averages to zero over a cycle.

2. **No rotor excitation:** Without a field winding or permanent magnets, there is no continuous alignment force at standstill; the rotor can sit in any angular position.

3. **Solution — Line-start SynRM:** A **cage winding** is added inside the rotor to provide asynchronous starting torque (just like an induction motor). Once the rotor approaches synchronous speed, the reluctance torque locks the rotor into synchronism.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for stating non-self-starting nature + 1 mark for the reason (zero average torque) + 1 mark for line-start solution.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque developed by a three-phase synchronous reluctance motor. State clearly the assumption made. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The stator winding is balanced, sinusoidal, and distributed.
2. Magnetic saturation is neglected (linear magnetics).
3. Stator copper loss is neglected ($R_s \approx 0$).
4. There is no rotor excitation (no field winding, no magnets).
5. The rotor is salient, with $L_d \neq L_q$.

**Step 1 — d-q Voltage Equations:**

$$v_{sd} = -X_q i_{sq}$$

$$v_{sq} = X_d i_{sd}$$

**Step 2 — Components in terms of load angle $\delta$:**

$$v_{sd} = V\sin\delta \implies i_{sq} = -\dfrac{V\sin\delta}{X_q}$$

$$v_{sq} = V\cos\delta \implies i_{sd} = \dfrac{V\cos\delta}{X_d}$$

**Step 3 — Air-gap co-energy:**

$$W'_{co} = \dfrac{3}{4}\left[L_d i_{sd}^2 + L_q i_{sq}^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Torque by partial derivative of co-energy with respect to mechanical angle:**

$$T_e = \dfrac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m} = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{P}{2}\left[i_{sd}\dfrac{\partial \lambda_d}{\partial \delta} - i_{sq}\dfrac{\partial \lambda_q}{\partial \delta}\right]$$

After substitution and simplification (shown in full in Section 3 of these notes), we obtain:

$$\boxed{\,T_e = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{P}{2}\cdot\dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s}\cdot\dfrac{(X_d - X_q)}{X_d X_q}\sin(2\delta)\,}$$

**Pull-out torque** occurs at $\delta = 45^\circ$, giving:

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{P}{2}\cdot\dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s}\cdot\dfrac{(X_d - X_q)}{X_d X_q}$$

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for assumptions + 3 marks for derivation steps + 2 marks for final boxed equation with explanation.)*

---

#### (b) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a synchronous reluctance motor. Also discuss any two methods to improve the saliency ratio. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**

A SynRM consists of:
- **Stator:** Identical to that of a three-phase induction motor — laminated silicon-steel core with a balanced three-phase distributed winding. When energized, it produces a constant-magnitude rotating MMF at synchronous speed.
- **Rotor:** Salient-pole anisotropic ferromagnetic body. It has *no* field winding, *no* permanent magnets, and *no* cage winding (in a basic SynRM). The rotor contains internal flux barriers (slots filled with air or non-magnetic material) oriented along the q-axis to break the q-axis flux path while leaving the d-axis path intact.

**Working Principle:**

1. When three-phase AC supply is given to the stator, a rotating magnetic field is produced at synchronous speed.
2. This rotating field links with the salient rotor. Because $L_d > L_q$, the rotor experiences a magnetic force that tries to align the d-axis (low reluctance) with the rotating flux axis.
3. The rotor locks into synchronism with the rotating field and rotates at $N_s = 120f/P$ RPM.
4. The torque developed is purely **reluctance torque**; no torque from field interaction (because there is no field).

**Methods to Improve Saliency Ratio $\xi$:**

1. **Use Axially Laminated Anisotropic (ALA) rotor:** Steel laminations are rolled axially with insulating layers. This creates a very high $L_d$ along the lamination direction and very low $L_q$ perpendicular to it, achieving $\xi$ of 6 to 10.

2. **Use Transversely Laminated Anisotropic (TLA) rotor with multiple flux barriers:** Carefully designed air pockets (typically 3 to 5 barriers per pole) along the q-axis using punched steel laminations. This forces all q-axis flux to cross air gaps, dramatically reducing $L_q$ and raising $\xi$.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for construction + 2 marks for working + 3 marks for saliency improvement methods.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) With a phasor diagram and the V-curve concept, explain the operation of a synchronous reluctance motor connected to a constant-voltage, constant-frequency supply. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

A synchronous reluctance motor draws a magnetizing current from the supply similar to an induction motor, but with the rotating rotor providing no separate excitation.

**Phasor Diagram (per phase):**

- Take the stator phase voltage $\vec{V}$ as the reference phasor along the real axis.
- The stator current $\vec{I}$ lags $\vec{V}$ by an angle $\phi$.
- The internal EMF $\vec{E_f}$ (here, the back-EMF due to saliency) leads $\vec{V}$ when the rotor is at a positive load angle $\delta$.
- The voltage drop $\vec{I}(R_s + jX_s)$ closes the loop.

$$V = E_f + I(R_s + jX_s)$$

For the synchronous reluctance motor, $E_f$ arises from the *saliency-induced* flux variation and is itself a function of the load angle $\delta$.

**V-Curve Concept (Stator Current vs Excitation — Adapted for SynRM):**

For a synchronous machine, the V-curve plots armature current $I$ versus field excitation at constant load. Since SynRM has no field excitation, the equivalent V-curve plots **stator current versus load torque** (or versus load angle $\delta$):

- At **no load** ($\delta \approx 0$): The current is minimum and is purely magnetizing.
- As **load increases**, $\delta$ increases, the saliency component grows, and the current increases.
- At **pull-out** ($\delta = 45^\circ$): Current reaches a maximum just before the rotor loses synchronism.
- Beyond pull-out: Rotor falls out of step, current becomes uncontrolled and large.

The SynRM V-curve has its minimum at no-load and rises monotonically with torque. Power factor improves with load (unlike induction motor where it falls).

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for phasor diagram + 2 marks for V-curve explanation + 3 marks for SynRM-specific interpretation.)*

---

#### (b) Compare synchronous reluctance motor with three-phase induction motor. Mention any three advantages and three disadvantages of SynRM. `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Induction Motor | Synchronous Reluctance Motor |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Rotor type | Cylindrical (squirrel cage) | Salient anisotropic (no cage in pure form) |
| Rotor excitation | Induced by slip | None — passive |
| Rotor copper loss | Yes (cage) | Zero |
| Synchronous operation | No (always has slip) | Yes (zero slip) |
| Speed under varying load | Drops slightly | Constant |
| Starting | Self-starting (by cage) | Not self-starting (unless line-start version) |
| Power factor | 0.7 to 0.85 lagging at full load | 0.7 to 0.85 lagging at full load |
| Efficiency | Lower due to rotor copper loss | Higher due to no rotor losses |
| Construction | Robust, mature technology | More complex rotor fabrication |
| Torque density | Moderate | Lower than induction motor of same frame |
| Speed control | V/f control standard | Requires variable frequency inverter |
| Cost | Low (mass-produced) | Higher (special rotor) |

**Advantages of SynRM (any three):**

1. **No rotor copper losses** — rotor is a passive iron body; efficiency is high.
2. **Rigid constant-speed operation** — exactly synchronous speed regardless of load.
3. **No brushes, no slip rings, no magnets** — robust, maintenance-free, low cost.
4. **High dynamic response** — no rotor inertia losses, suitable for servo drives.
5. **Can operate at high temperatures** — no magnets to demagnetize.

**Disadvantages of SynRM (any three):**

1. **Low power factor** — needs magnetizing VAR from supply; large inverter rating.
2. **Low starting torque** — requires line-start cage or variable-frequency drive.
3. **Complex rotor manufacture** — flux barriers demand precision punching/lamination.
4. **Lower torque density** — compared to permanent magnet synchronous motor.
5. **Pull-out at $\delta = 45^\circ$** — narrower stability region than PMSM or induction motor.

*(Marks distribution: 3 marks for comparison table + 2 marks for advantages + 2 marks for disadvantages.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Sign of $(X_d - X_q)$:** Students often write $T_e \propto (X_q - X_d)$, which is *negative*. The correct sign is **$(X_d - X_q)$** because $L_d > L_q$. Examiners deduct full 7 marks if this sign error is made.
>
> 2. **Confusing $N_s$ with $\omega_s$:** $N_s$ is in RPM, $\omega_s$ is in rad/s. The factor $\dfrac{P}{2}$ correctly converts pole count to pole pairs. Writing $T = V^2/(X_d X_q)$ without $\omega_s$ in the denominator will be marked wrong.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** The torque derivation requires *linear magnetics* and *balanced sinusoidal supply*. Not stating these assumptions costs at least 1 to 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Writing $\sin\delta$ instead of $\sin(2\delta)$:** A classic mistake. The reluctance torque depends on **$\sin(2\delta)$**, not $\sin\delta$. This is because reluctance torque is a *quadruple-axis* effect, not a dipole effect like field torque.
>
> 5. **Confusing saliency ratio with slip:** Saliency ratio $\xi = L_d/L_q$ is a *geometric* property of the rotor. Do not confuse with slip $s$, which applies only to induction motors.
>
> 6. **Skipping the V-curve explanation:** A common question asks for the V-curve, but the SynRM V-curve is *non-standard* — it is plotted against load, not field excitation. Many students draw an induction motor V-curve and lose marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** SynRM is a brushless, magnet-less synchronous motor that produces torque purely from rotor saliency.
- **Saliency ratio $\xi = L_d/L_q$** must be **greater than 1**; modern designs target **6 to 10**.
- **No rotor excitation, no rotor copper loss, no magnets, no brushes** — fully passive rotor.
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120f/P$; rotor runs at exactly $N_s$ from no-load to pull-out.
- **Torque equation (master formula):**
  $$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{P}{2}\cdot\dfrac{V^2}{\omega_s}\cdot\dfrac{(X_d - X_q)}{X_d X_q}\sin(2\delta)$$
- **Maximum torque at $\delta = 45^\circ$** — narrow stability margin compared to induction motor.
- **Bidirectional torque:** $\sin(2\delta)$ sign flips at $\delta = 90^\circ$, giving motor and generator modes.
- **Self-starting:** Pure SynRM is *not* self-starting; needs line-start cage or VFD.
- **Rotor types:** TLA (with flux barriers), ALA (axially laminated), Line-start (TLA + cage).
- **Phasor equation:** $\vec{V} = \vec{E_f} + \vec{I}(R_s + jX_s)$, with $E_f$ due to saliency.
- **V-curve:** Stator current vs load angle — minimum at no-load, maximum near pull-out.
- **Power factor:** 0.7 to 0.85 lagging at rated load — requires reactive power support.
- **Applications:** Pumps, fans, compressors, servo drives, EV traction, refrigeration.
- **Advantages:** High efficiency, robust, magnet-free, constant-speed operation.
- **Disadvantages:** Low starting torque, low PF, complex rotor, lower torque density than PMSM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constructional Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Topic Context within KTU 2024 Scheme

The topic **"Constructional Details"** in **Module 2** of *PEEET743 – Special Electrical Machines* deals with the physical, electromagnetic, and mechanical architecture of **Stepper Motors** — a class of electromechanical energy conversion devices that convert discrete electrical pulses into precise, incremental angular (or linear) mechanical displacements. This is the foundational topic upon which the *operating principles, torque production, and drive electronics* of the subsequent modules are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor:** As per the 2024 Scheme syllabus, Module 2 of Special Electrical Machines exclusively focuses on **Stepper Motors**. "Constructional Details" specifically covers the *Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM)*, the *Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM)*, and the *Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)*. Examiner questions on this topic typically demand labeled diagrams, pole/teeth counting, and material identification.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Stepper Motor

A **Stepper Motor** is a *brushless, synchronous electric machine* that divides a full mechanical rotation ($360^{\circ}$ mechanical) into a discrete number of equal angular steps. Each electrical pulse applied to its stator winding causes the rotor to advance by one precise increment, known as the **step angle** ($\theta_s$), without requiring any feedback sensor for position control. The motion is therefore *inherently open-loop and digitally commanded*.

Mathematically, the step angle is expressed as:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$$

where $N_r$ is the number of rotor teeth (or salient poles) and $m$ is the number of stator phase-excitation sequences per electrical cycle. The number of steps per revolution is:

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s} = N_r \cdot m$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Digital Clock Hand**
> Imagine the second hand of a **digital wall clock**. It does *not* sweep smoothly like an analog watch; instead, it jerks forward in tiny, identical, audible "ticks" — *one tick, one step*. A stepper motor behaves identically: each incoming electrical pulse (the "tick") advances the rotor shaft by a fixed, factory-defined angular increment. The faster the pulses arrive, the faster the shaft rotates — but the size of each "tick" never changes. This **open-loop digital-to-mechanical translation** is why stepper motors dominate printers, CNC machines, robotics, and telescope mounts.

## 1.4 Why Constructional Details Matter in KTU Examinations

The construction directly dictates three performance-defining parameters:

1. **Step Resolution** — determined by the *number of rotor teeth* and the *stator phase configuration*.
2. **Torque Capability** — determined by the *magnetic material* (ferrite vs. rare-earth NdFeB vs. Samarium Cobalt) and *air-gap geometry*.
3. **Detent/Holding Torque** — determined by the *salient pole structure* and *residual magnetism* in the rotor.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Reference Metrics (memorize these):**
> - Common step angles: **$1.8^{\circ}$** (200 steps/rev — Hybrid), **$7.5^{\circ}$** (48 steps/rev — VRSM), **$15^{\circ}$** (24 steps/rev — PMSM).
> - Standard operating DC bus voltage: **$5\text{ V}$ to $48\text{ V}$**; phase current: **$0.5\text{ A}$ to $5\text{ A}$**.
> - Air gap length in stepper motors: typically **$0.05\text{ mm}$ to $0.2\text{ mm}$** (much smaller than conventional machines for sharp torque pulses).

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stepper Motors — A Structural Hierarchy

Before diving into the construction of each type, the student must internalize the three principal structural families. KTU examiners frequently ask for **classification with one-line constructional distinctions**.

| Family | Rotor Construction | Stator Construction | Key Identifying Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM)** | Soft-iron salient-pole rotor (no magnets, no windings) | Multi-phase concentrated windings on salient stator poles | Rotor aligns to *minimum reluctance* path |
| **Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM)** | Permanently magnetized radial-pole rotor (2 or 4 poles typical) | Two-phase or four-phase concentrated stator windings | Rotor has *fixed magnetic polarity*; detent torque at zero excitation |
| **Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)** | Two axially-stacked soft-iron caps with *multiple fine teeth*, separated by a permanent magnet | Two-phase, multi-toothed stator with concentrated windings | Combines *high step resolution* of VRSM with *holding torque* of PMSM |

## 2.2 Constructional Architecture of the Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM)

The VRSM is the structurally simplest member of the family. Its name is derived from the operating principle — *reluctance varies with rotor position* because the rotor is a passive salient-iron piece.

### 2.2.1 Stator Construction of VRSM

- The **stator** is a **laminated silicon-steel stack** (thickness per lamination ≈ $0.35\text{ mm}$ to $0.5\text{ mm}$) designed to confine eddy-current losses during high-frequency pulsed excitation.
- It carries **$N_s$ salient poles** (commonly $N_s = 8$, giving 4 phase pairs; or $N_s = 6$ giving 3 phase pairs).
- Each stator pole has a **concentrated coil** wound around it. Diametrically opposite poles form **one phase winding** and are connected in series such that their MMFs add along the same axis.
- The stator pole faces may be either *uniform* (for low-resolution applications) or *toothed/fine-pitched* (for high-resolution versions).
- A standard three-stack, *multi-toothed* VRSM stator can yield step angles as small as **$1.8^{\circ}$**.

### 2.2.2 Rotor Construction of VRSM

- The **rotor** is a **plain cylindrical (or salient) piece of high-permeability soft iron**, *lacking both permanent magnets and conducting windings*.
- It has $N_r$ salient teeth/poles. For a 4-phase, 8-pole stator VRSM, the rotor typically has $N_r = 6$ teeth.
- The rotor is mounted on a non-magnetic stainless-steel shaft to prevent axial flux leakage.
- The **air gap** between stator and rotor teeth is the *most critical dimensional tolerance*; a typical gap is **$0.1\text{ mm}$ to $0.2\text{ mm}$**.

### 2.2.3 Operating Step in VRSM

When Phase-A is energized, the rotor rotates to align its salient teeth with the energized stator poles (path of *minimum magnetic reluctance*). When Phase-A is de-energized and Phase-B is energized, the rotor advances to the next stable aligned position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Fact:**
> A VRSM has **no detent torque** when unenergized (rotor is free to rotate by hand). This is the *single most distinguishing feature* examiners use in objective questions.

## 2.3 Constructional Architecture of the Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM)

### 2.3.1 Stator Construction of PMSM

- The **stator** is again a **laminated silicon-steel core**, but its poles are *smooth* (not finely toothed like a high-resolution VRSM).
- It carries **two phase windings** (Phase A and Phase B) typically arranged as **two pairs of poles** ($N_s = 4$ or $N_s = 8$).
- The windings are **bifilar-wound** in many commercial designs to allow unipolar drive, or they are wound in a single layer for bipolar drive.
- A typical $15^{\circ}$ PMSM has a **4-pole stator**.

### 2.3.2 Rotor Construction of PMSM

- The **rotor** is a **cylindrical permanent magnet**, axially magnetized (in older designs) or *radially magnetized* (in modern designs) with alternating N–S poles around its circumference.
- For a $15^{\circ}$ step-angle PMSM: $N_r = 24$ (since $\theta_s = 360^{\circ}/(2 \cdot 24) = 7.5^{\circ}$ full-step with $7.5^{\circ}$ half-step modes possible) — *correction*: a $15^{\circ}$ step PMSM with 4 stator phases and 24 rotor poles yields $\theta_s = 360^{\circ}/(2 \cdot 24) = 7.5^{\circ}$. The most common commercial PMSM has $N_r = 2$ or $N_r = 4$ poles giving $\theta_s = 90^{\circ}$ or $45^{\circ}$, which are reduced to $15^{\circ}$ via a *multistage axial stack*.
- The magnet material is typically **barium ferrite** (low cost) or **rare-earth samarium-cobalt / NdFeB** (high torque density).

> [!NOTE]
> **Detent Torque Signature of PMSM:**
> A PMSM has a *substantial detent (cogging) torque* even with no phase excitation — this is the resisting torque you feel when trying to rotate the shaft by hand. This detent torque is a direct consequence of the permanent-magnet rotor's interaction with the slotted stator.

## 2.4 Constructional Architecture of the Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)

The Hybrid Stepper Motor is the **industry workhorse** and the **most commonly tested in KTU** because of its sophisticated construction.

### 2.4.1 The Two-Cap Rotor (Heart of the HSM)

The rotor consists of **two soft-iron end-caps** (each with $N_r$ fine teeth), mounted on a non-magnetic shaft and **axially separated by a permanent magnet** (a cylindrical disc, axially magnetized).

- The **N-pole end-cap** has its teeth displaced by *half a tooth-pitch* relative to the **S-pole end-cap**.
- This axial magnetisation forces the **N-cap teeth to become North poles** and the **S-cap teeth to become South poles** simultaneously, creating a unidirectional axial flux that crosses the air gap at the active end.

### 2.4.2 The Multi-Toothed Stator

- The stator is a **laminated silicon-steel stack** with **two phase windings** (A and B).
- The stator has $2m$ salient poles, each carrying a *concentrated multi-tooth structure* (typically 5 teeth per pole for a $1.8^{\circ}$ motor).
- The stator pole teeth are also *offset by half a tooth pitch* between Phase A and Phase B poles.
- A standard $1.8^{\circ}$ HSM: $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth, $N_s = 8$ stator poles, $m = 2$ phases.

### 2.4.3 Material Stack-Up

| Component | Material | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Stator/Rotor laminations | **M-19 or M-27 grain-oriented silicon steel**, $0.35\text{ mm}$ | Reduces eddy-current loss at high pulse rates |
| Rotor permanent magnet | **NdFeB (Neodymium-Iron-Boron)** grade N35–N52 | Highest residual flux density ($\geq 1.2\text{ T}$) |
| Shaft | **Non-magnetic stainless steel (SS-304)** | Prevents axial flux short-circuit through shaft |
| Bearing | **Sealed deep-groove ball bearing** | Maintains tight air-gap tolerance |
| Encapsulation | **Polymer bobbin over coils** | Provides insulation and mechanical rigidity |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 100% of numerical problems asked in KTU 2024 on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$ | $\theta_s$: step angle, $N_r$: rotor teeth, $m$: phase sequences per cycle | Determines mechanical step resolution |
| 2 | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s}$ | Total steps per revolution | Used to compute pulse train length |
| 3 | $\theta_{elec} = N_r \cdot \theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{m}$ | Electrical angle per step (always $= 90^{\circ}$ for $m=4$) | Relates mechanical to electrical angle |
| 4 | $N_{ph} \cdot N_s = 2m$ for bipolar drive | $N_{ph}$: number of phases, $N_s$: stator poles | Stator design check |
| 5 | $f_{step} = \dfrac{\omega \cdot N_{steps}}{60}$ | $f_{step}$: pulse rate (Hz), $\omega$: shaft speed (rpm) | Translates RPM to drive frequency |
| 6 | $\omega_{max} = \dfrac{f_{max}}{N_{steps}/60}$ | Maximum safe speed (no resonance) | Drive design constraint |
| 7 | $T \propto \dfrac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \cdot I^2$ | Static torque expression for VRSM | Static torque prediction |
| 8 | $\theta_s(\text{HSM}) = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{2 \cdot N_r}$ | Hybrid stepper with 2-phase excitation | Direct HSM calculation |
| 9 | $\theta_s(\text{VRSM}) = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_s \cdot N_r} \cdot 2$ | VRSM, 2-phase-on sequence | Direct VRSM calculation |
| 10 | $T_{detent} \approx 5\%-10\%$ of $T_{holding}$ | Rule of thumb (PMSM/HSM) | Holding-torque estimation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** A VRSM rotor has *no magnet*. Many students incorrectly draw a magnet inside the VRSM rotor in diagrams — this costs **2 marks** instantly in KTU valuation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stepper motors with the construction described above are deployed wherever **precision open-loop positioning** is required:

- **3D printers** ($1.8^{\circ}$ HSM, microstepped to $0.45^{\circ}$).
- **CNC milling machines** (NEMA-23 / NEMA-34 HSM with optical encoders as a secondary check).
- **Telescopes and observatory mounts** (VRSM for low-cost tracking).
- **Automotive instrument clusters** (PMSM for gauge needles).
- **Robotic joints and prosthetic limbs** (miniature HSM with planetary gearheads).
- **Hard-disk drive head actuators** (VRSM in legacy designs; voice-coil in modern).
- **Watches and clocks** (miniature PMSM, $7.5^{\circ}/15^{\circ}$ step).

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Angle of a Hybrid Stepper Motor

**Problem Statement:** A hybrid stepper motor has $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth and is driven by a *two-phase* winding with full-step excitation ($m = 2$ switching states per electrical cycle per phase-pair, $m_{eff} = 4$ for a 2-phase system). Determine (a) the step angle, (b) the steps per revolution, (c) the pulse frequency required to achieve $150\text{ rpm}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the General Step-Angle Formula.**

For any stepper motor, the step angle is given by the previously established relation:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Variables.**

The rotor has $N_r = 50$ fine teeth. The number of stator phase excitation sequences per mechanical revolution is $m$. For a *2-phase* motor in **full-step, single-phase-on** mode, $m = 2$ (we move through 2 states: A-ON, B-ON → repeat). For **full-step, two-phase-on** mode, $m = 4$ (states: AB, $\bar{A}B$, $A\bar{B}$, $\bar{A}\bar{B}$). We assume the standard 2-phase full-step mode with $m = 4$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and Compute $\theta_s$.**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{50 \times 4} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{200} = 1.8^{\circ}$$

> This matches the industry-standard $1.8^{\circ}$ HSM.

**Step 4 — Compute Steps per Revolution.**

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{1.8^{\circ}} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Required Pulse Frequency for $150\text{ rpm}$.**

Shaft speed in revolutions per second:

$$f_{rev} = \frac{150}{60} = 2.5 \text{ rev/s}$$

Each revolution requires 200 pulses, so:

$$f_{step} = N_{steps} \cdot f_{rev} = 200 \times 2.5 = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta_s = 1.8^{\circ}$, $N_{steps} = 200$, $f_{step} = 500\text{ Hz}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relating Electrical Angle to Mechanical Angle

**Statement:** Show that in a stepper motor, the *electrical angle per step* is independent of the number of rotor teeth.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Definition of Electrical Angle.**

The electrical angle $\theta_e$ is defined as the angle through which the stator MMF vector rotates in *electrical space* per excitation step. It is related to the mechanical angle $\theta_m$ by the *pole-pair factor* $p$ (number of pole pairs):

$$\theta_e = p \cdot \theta_m$$

**Step 2 — Apply to a Stepper Motor.**

In a stepper motor, the effective number of pole pairs is *synthesized* by the rotor tooth count $N_r$ — i.e., $p = N_r/2$ (or $N_r$ for salient-pole machines). Therefore, the electrical angle per step is:

$$\theta_{e,\text{step}} = p \cdot \theta_s = \frac{N_r}{2} \cdot \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{2m}$$

**Step 3 — Observation.**

The rotor teeth $N_r$ cancel out:

$$\theta_{e,\text{step}} = \frac{180^{\circ}}{m}$$

For $m = 4$ (two-phase full-step): $\theta_{e,\text{step}} = 180^{\circ}/4 = 45^{\circ}$. Q.E.D.

> **Insight:** This is why *microstepping* drives can subdivide a single mechanical step into arbitrarily small electrical sub-steps — they are not limited by $N_r$ once the electrical phase-angle is finely resolved.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Air-Gap Energy and Static Torque in a VRSM

**Statement:** Derive the static (detent-free) holding torque of a VRSM from energy principles.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express Co-energy in the Air Gap.**

The co-energy stored in the air gap of a salient-pole magnetic structure with phase current $I$ and rotor position $\theta$ is:

$$W'_{f}(\theta, I) = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) I^2$$

where $L(\theta)$ is the position-dependent phase inductance.

**Step 2 — Define the Torque as Partial Derivative of Co-energy.**

By the principle of virtual work, the electromagnetic torque is:

$$T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'_f}{\partial \theta} \right|_{I = \text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Model $L(\theta)$ as a Trigonometric Series.**

For a salient-pole VRSM, the inductance varies sinusoidally with rotor position:

$$L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$$

where $L_0$ is the *average* inductance and $L_1$ is the *saliency-modulation amplitude*. The derivative is:

$$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -N_r L_1 \sin(N_r \theta)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify.**

$$T_e = -\frac{1}{2} N_r L_1 I^2 \sin(N_r \theta)$$

The peak (holding) torque occurs at $\sin(N_r \theta) = \pm 1$:

$$T_{peak} = \frac{1}{2} N_r L_1 I^2$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation.**

The torque is **independent of rotor magnet material** (no magnet in VRSM), scales as the **square of current**, and is **proportional to rotor tooth count** $N_r$. This explains why high-resolution VRSMs have *many teeth* and *high current*.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Constructional Table

| Parameter | VRSM | PMSM | HSM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotor material | Soft iron (no magnet) | Hard ferrite / NdFeB magnet | Soft iron + NdFeB disc |
| Rotor teeth/poles | 6–100 salient teeth | 2–24 radial poles | 50–200 fine teeth per cap |
| Stator phases | 3 or 4 | 2 | 2 (with offset teeth) |
| Step angle (typical) | $7.5^{\circ}$–$15^{\circ}$ | $45^{\circ}$–$90^{\circ}$ (gear-reduced) | $0.9^{\circ}$–$1.8^{\circ}$ |
| Detent torque at unenergized | **Zero** | Significant | Moderate (magnet + tooth) |
| Holding torque per frame size | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Inertia | Low | Higher | Medium |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Hybrid Stepper Motor

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional block-level architecture** of a complete HSM system, illustrating how constructional components interact with the drive electronics (which is the most common diagram type asked in KTU 2024 for this topic).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ctrlA[Microcontroller Pulse Generator] --> drvA[Half H-Bridge Driver Phase A]
    ctrlB[Microcontroller Pulse Generator] --> drvB[Half H-Bridge Driver Phase B]
    drvA --> coilA[Stator Phase A Concentrated Winding]
    drvB --> coilB[Stator Phase B Concentrated Winding]
    coilA --> statorCore[Stator Laminated Silicon Steel Stack]
    coilB --> statorCore
    statorCore --> airgap[Air Gap 0.05mm to 0.2mm]
    airgap --> ncap[Rotor N Cap Soft Iron 50 Teeth]
    airgap --> scap[Rotor S Cap Soft Iron 50 Teeth Offset by Half Pitch]
    ncap --> pmagnet[Axial Permanent Magnet NdFeB Disc]
    pmagnet --> scap
    scap --> shaft[Non Magnetic Stainless Steel Shaft SS 304]
    ncap --> shaft
    shaft --> bearing[Sealed Deep Groove Ball Bearings]
    bearing --> housing[Aluminium Die Cast End Bells]
    housing --> outputMech[Mechanical Output 1.8 Degree Step]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Constructional Comparison of VRSM, PMSM, HSM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vRSM[Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor]
    vRSM --> vStator[Stator 6 to 8 Salient Poles with Concentrated Coils]
    vRSM --> vRotor[Rotor Plain Salient Soft Iron No Magnet]

    pMSM[Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor]
    pMSM --> pStator[Stator 4 Poles 2 Phase Concentrated Coils]
    pMSM --> pRotor[Rotor Radial N S Poles Hard Ferrite or NdFeB]

    hSM[Hybrid Stepper Motor]
    hSM --> hStator[Stator 8 Poles 2 Phase with Fine Teeth 5 Teeth per Pole]
    hSM --> hRotorCap1[Upper Soft Iron Cap 50 Teeth]
    hSM --> hRotorMag[Axial Permanent Magnet Disc NdFeB]
    hSM --> hRotorCap2[Lower Soft Iron Cap 50 Teeth Offset Half Pitch]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Stator Winding Connection Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    batt[DC Bus 5V to 48V] --> bulkCap[Bulk Capacitor 1000uF 50V]
    batt --> reg[Logic Level Regulator 5V]
    reg --> logic[Index Logic Circuit]
    logic --> phaseASw[Phase A Switch Leg]
    logic --> phaseBSw[Phase B Switch Leg]
    phaseASw --> coilA1[Stator Pole 1N Coil]
    phaseASw --> coilA2[Stator Pole 1S Coil Diametrically Opposite]
    phaseBSw --> coilB1[Stator Pole 2N Coil]
    phaseBSw --> coilB2[Stator Pole 2S Coil Diametrically Opposite]
    coilA1 --> rotor1[Rotor]
    coilA2 --> rotor1
    coilB1 --> rotor1
    coilB2 --> rotor1
    rotor1 --> shaft1[Output Shaft]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Constructional Cross-Section Schematic (Logical)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    outer1[Outer Stator Yoke Laminated Steel] --> pole1[Stator Pole 1 with Concentrated Coil]
    outer1 --> pole2[Stator Pole 2 with Concentrated Coil]
    outer1 --> pole3[Stator Pole 3 with Concentrated Coil]
    outer1 --> pole4[Stator Pole 4 with Concentrated Coil]
    pole1 --> gap1[Air Gap]
    pole2 --> gap1
    pole3 --> gap1
    pole4 --> gap1
    gap1 --> rotorA[Rotor N Pole Tooth]
    gap1 --> rotorB[Rotor S Pole Tooth]
    gap1 --> rotorC[Next Rotor Tooth]
    gap1 --> rotorD[Next Rotor Tooth]
    rotorA --> shaft2[Non Magnetic Shaft]
    rotorB --> shaft2
    rotorC --> shaft2
    rotorD --> shaft2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note on Diagrams:** When drawing constructional diagrams in the exam, *always* show (1) the stator pole-faces with their teeth/pole-shoes, (2) the rotor teeth/poles, (3) the phase windings on at least 2 stator poles (with arrows indicating current direction), (4) the air gap as a *distinct double-arrow* dimension, and (5) the shaft. A diagram missing the air-gap dimension is marked down by 1 mark.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Short-Answer (Conceptual)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the constructional features of a Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM). Why is the rotor made of soft iron and not a permanent magnet?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A VRSM consists of a **stator** and a **rotor** with no permanent magnet and no rotor winding. The **stator** is a **laminated silicon-steel core** with **$N_s$ salient poles** (typically 6 or 8). Each stator pole carries a **concentrated phase winding**, and diametrically opposite poles are connected in series to form one phase. The **rotor** is a **cylindrical piece of high-permeability soft iron** with **$N_r$ salient teeth** (commonly 6 for a 3-phase, 6/4-pole VRSM). The rotor is mounted on a non-magnetic shaft.

The rotor is made of **soft iron (not a permanent magnet)** because the operating principle depends entirely on *variation of magnetic reluctance* with rotor position. When a stator phase is energized, the rotor rotates to align its teeth with the energized stator poles to provide a *path of minimum reluctance* ($\mathcal{R} = l/\mu A$). A permanent magnet would *short-circuit* this reluctance variation and lock the rotor in a fixed orientation, destroying the stepping action.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Drawing a magnet inside the VRSM rotor in your sketch = **–2 marks** (Examiner deducts 2 marks from full 3).

---

### Question A2: Short-Answer (Definition + Comparison)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: List the three main types of stepper motors. Differentiate their rotor construction in one sentence each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three main types of stepper motors are:

1. **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM):** Rotor is a *plain salient soft-iron piece* with no magnet and no winding — torque arises from *variation of reluctance*.
2. **Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM):** Rotor is a *cylindrical permanent magnet* with radial N–S poles — torque arises from *interaction between stator field and rotor magnet*.
3. **Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM):** Rotor consists of *two soft-iron end-caps with fine teeth* separated by an *axially-magnetized permanent magnet disc* — torque arises from *combined reluctance and magnet alignment* effects, giving the highest step resolution and holding torque.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — VRSM & PMSM Constructional Focus

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the constructional details of a **3-stack Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor**. Derive the step-angle formula and compute the step angle for a motor with $N_r = 12$ rotor teeth per stack and a 3-phase stator.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compare the constructional features of a **Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM)** and a **Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)** with neat sketches. Highlight the role of the axial permanent magnet in the HSM rotor.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

**Part (a) Model Solution:**

**Constructional Details of a 3-Stack VRSM (7 marks breakdown: 3 marks diagram, 2 marks construction text, 2 marks derivation + calculation):**

A 3-stack VRSM consists of **three identical stator sections stacked axially along the shaft**, each section being a laminated silicon-steel ring with **$N_s$ salient poles** (typically $N_s = 12$) and **3-phase windings** (Phase A, B, C) wound on diametrically opposite pole pairs.

**Rotor:** The rotor is a single shaft carrying **three soft-iron sections**, each with **$N_r = 12$ teeth**. The middle rotor section is *angularly offset* by **$1/3$ of a tooth pitch** from the outer two sections, which are aligned with each other. This mechanical offset provides the *phase displacement* needed for sequential stepping.

**Step-Angle Derivation:**

Each tooth pitch corresponds to a mechanical angle of:

$$\theta_{tp} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r}$$

When Phase A is excited, the rotor aligns with Section 1. When Phase B is excited, the rotor shifts by $1/3$ tooth pitch to align with Section 2 (since the rotor teeth in Section 2 are offset by $1/3$ pitch). Similarly, Phase C aligns with Section 3.

Therefore, the step angle is:

$$\theta_s = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \theta_{tp} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r} = \frac{120^{\circ}}{N_r}$$

Substituting $N_r = 12$:

$$\theta_s = \frac{120^{\circ}}{12} = 10^{\circ}$$

**Number of steps per revolution:**

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{10^{\circ}} = 36 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Part (b) Model Solution:**

**Comparison of PMSM and HSM Construction (7 marks: 3 marks PMSM, 3 marks HSM, 1 mark tabulated comparison):**

**PMSM Construction:**

The PMSM has a **stator** that is a **laminated silicon-steel core** with **4 or 8 smooth salient poles** carrying a **2-phase concentrated winding** (Phase A and Phase B). The windings may be *bifilar* (for unipolar drive) or *single-layer* (for bipolar drive). The **rotor** is a *cylindrical permanent magnet* magnetized with **2 or 4 radial poles** of alternating N–S polarity. Materials used are typically **barium ferrite** (cost-effective) or **NdFeB** (high performance). The rotor is mounted on a standard shaft supported by two sealed ball bearings housed in aluminum end-bells. The PMSM has substantial **detent torque** at zero excitation (typically 5%–10% of holding torque) because the magnet always tries to align with the stator slot openings.

**HSM Construction:**

The HSM has a **multi-toothed stator** — typically 8 salient poles, each carrying 5 fine teeth, with two phases (A and B) whose pole teeth are **offset by half a tooth pitch** to create the quadrature structure. The **rotor** is a *two-cap assembly* on a non-magnetic shaft:

- **Upper soft-iron cap** with 50 fine teeth.
- **Lower soft-iron cap** with 50 fine teeth, **offset by half a tooth pitch** from the upper cap.
- A **permanent magnet disc** (NdFeB, axially magnetized) sandwiched between the two caps.

**Role of the Axial Permanent Magnet:**

The axial magnet serves **two critical functions** in the HSM:

1. It **polarizes** the two rotor caps into opposite magnetic polarities (one cap becomes uniformly N, the other S) so that the *teeth of the upper cap are North poles* and the *teeth of the lower cap are South poles* in the air gap simultaneously.
2. It **provides the static holding (detent) torque** even when no phase is energized, by trying to align its flux with the stator teeth.

| Feature | PMSM | HSM |
|---|---|---|
| Stator teeth | Smooth poles | Fine teeth (5/pole) |
| Rotor poles | 2 or 4 | 50–100 fine teeth per cap |
| Magnet placement | Radial, in rotor body | Axial disc between caps |
| Step angle | $15^{\circ}$–$90^{\circ}$ | $0.9^{\circ}$–$1.8^{\circ}$ |
| Detent torque | High | Moderate |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — HSM & Material Science Focus (Alternative Choice)

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat diagram, describe the **constructional details of a Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)**. A 2-phase HSM has 50 rotor teeth on each cap. Compute (i) the step angle, (ii) steps per revolution, and (iii) the electrical angle per step.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the **role of lamination, air gap, and material selection** in the construction of a stepper motor. Why is the air gap kept as small as mechanically feasible?

---

### Question B — Model Solution

**Part (a) Model Solution:**

**HSM Constructional Details (4 marks diagram + 3 marks numerical):**

The HSM consists of:
- **Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel stack (M-19 grade, $0.35\text{ mm}$ lamination thickness) with **8 salient poles**. Each pole carries **5 fine teeth**, giving a total of 40 stator teeth. The 8 poles are wound as **2 phases (A and B)** in a concentrated configuration. The Phase B pole teeth are *offset by half a tooth pitch* relative to Phase A.
- **Rotor:** Two soft-iron end-caps (50 fine teeth each) mounted on a **non-magnetic SS-304 stainless steel shaft**, with the two caps **offset by half a tooth pitch** from each other and separated by a **cylindrical NdFeB permanent magnet disc** that is axially magnetized.
- **End bells:** Aluminum die-cast, supporting two **sealed deep-groove ball bearings**.
- **Encapsulation:** Polymer bobbins insulate the windings from the laminations.

**Numerical Computations:**

Given: $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth per cap, $m = 4$ (2-phase full-step excitation, two-phase-on mode).

**(i) Step Angle:**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{50 \times 4} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{200} = 1.8^{\circ}$$

**(ii) Steps per Revolution:**

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{1.8^{\circ}} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**(iii) Electrical Angle per Step:**

$$\theta_{e,\text{step}} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{m} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{4} = 90^{\circ}$$

> **Interpretation:** The stator MMF vector rotates by $90^{\circ}$ in electrical space for each $1.8^{\circ}$ mechanical step.

**Part (b) Model Solution:**

**Role of Lamination (2 marks):** The stator and rotor cores of a stepper motor are made of *thin laminations* of grain-oriented silicon steel (typically **M-19** or **M-27 grade, $0.35\text{ mm}$ thickness**), insulated from one another by a thin oxide or varnish layer. The purpose is to **suppress eddy-current losses** that would otherwise be catastrophic during *high-frequency pulsed excitation* (stepper motors are routinely driven at $1\text{ kHz}$ to $20\text{ kHz}$ pulse rates). Without lamination, the iron core would heat up within seconds and saturate, destroying the torque-producing capability.

**Role of Material Selection (2 marks):**
- **Rotor permanent magnet:** Modern stepper motors use **NdFeB (Neodymium-Iron-Boron)** magnets (grade N35 to N52) because of their very high residual flux density ($B_r \geq 1.2\text{ T}$) and high coercivity ($H_c \geq 800\text{ kA/m}$). This maximizes the *torque-per-unit-volume*.
- **Shaft material:** A *non-magnetic stainless steel* (SS-304) is used to prevent the axial flux from the rotor magnet from *short-circuiting through the shaft* back to the opposite cap, which would bypass the air gap and reduce torque.
- **Bearings:** Sealed *deep-groove ball bearings* maintain the tight air-gap tolerance under axial and radial load.

**Why Air Gap is Kept Small (3 marks):** The air gap is the *primary energy-conversion region* of the machine. Three reasons mandate a small gap:

1. **Torque proportionality:** The electromagnetic torque is *inversely proportional* to the square of the effective air-gap length. Halving the air gap **quadruples** the torque for the same MMF.
2. **Flux density:** Flux density in the air gap is $B_g = \text{MMF}/\mathcal{R}_g$, where reluctance $\mathcal{R}_g = g/(\mu_0 A)$. A larger $g$ directly reduces $B_g$, weakening the rotor's magnetic loading.
3. **Step accuracy and detent stability:** A small, uniform air gap ensures *predictable detent positions* and *minimum position error* between commanded and actual rotor angles, which is critical for open-loop positioning accuracy (typically $\pm 3\%$ of one step angle, non-cumulative).

However, the gap cannot be *zero* — mechanical tolerances, thermal expansion of the rotor, and bearing runout impose a practical lower limit of approximately **$0.05\text{ mm}$** in commercial motors.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on "Constructional Details" Questions:**
> 1. **Drawing a magnet inside a VRSM rotor** — *the rotor of a VRSM is unmagnetized soft iron*. (−2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the air-gap dimension in the diagram** — *always show $g$ with a clear dimension line*. (−1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing "phases" with "poles"** — *a 2-phase motor has 2 electrical phases, not 2 poles*. (−1 mark)
> 4. **Omitting the half-tooth-pitch offset** in the HSM stator/rotor description — *this is the single defining constructional feature of the HSM*. (−2 marks)
> 5. **Stating step-angle formula without stating the excitation mode** — *a 2-phase motor has TWO step angles: $1.8^{\circ}$ (two-phase-on) and $0.9^{\circ}$ (half-step)*. Always specify. (−1 mark)

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Constructional Details of Stepper Motors"**

- [ ] **Three families of stepper motors:** VRSM, PMSM, HSM.
- [ ] **VRSM rotor:** Plain soft-iron salient pole, *no magnet, no winding*. Stator has 3 or 4 phases with concentrated windings.
- [ ] **PMSM rotor:** Cylindrical permanent magnet (2 or 4 radial poles). Stator has 2 phases, smooth poles.
- [ ] **HSM rotor:** *Two soft-iron caps with fine teeth* + *axial NdFeB disc magnet*. Stator has 2 phases with *fine teeth offset by half a tooth pitch*.
- [ ] **Step-angle formula (universal):** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$.
- [ ] **HSM step angle (2-phase full-step):** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{2 \cdot N_r}$.
- [ ] **VRSM step angle:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{120^{\circ}}{N_r}$ for 3-stack; $\theta_s = \dfrac{90^{\circ}}{N_r}$ for 4-stack.
- [ ] **Detent torque signature:** VRSM = **zero**; PMSM = **highest**; HSM = **moderate**.
- [ ] **Air gap range:** $0.05\text{ mm}$–$0.2\text{ mm}$.
- [ ] **Lamination:** M-19 / M-27 silicon steel, $0.35\text{ mm}$ thickness, to suppress eddy currents.
- [ ] **Shaft material:** SS-304 non-magnetic stainless steel (prevents axial flux short-circuit).
- [ ] **Most common HSM:** $1.8^{\circ}$, 200 steps/rev, $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth.
- [ ] **Pulse frequency for 1 rev/s in 1.8° HSM:** $200\text{ Hz}$; for 60 rpm: $200\text{ Hz}$.
- [ ] **Electrical angle per step** is *always* $360^{\circ}/m$, independent of $N_r$.
- [ ] **Static torque formula (VRSM):** $T_{peak} = \dfrac{1}{2} N_r L_1 I^2$.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 most-tested diagram:** HSM cross-section with offset rotor caps and axial magnet.
- [ ] **Real-world deployment:** 3D printers, CNC machines, telescopes, robotics, automotive gauges.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Principle of Operation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Stepper Motor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *stepper motor* is a **brushless, synchronous electromechanical device** that converts a train of input digital electrical pulses into discrete angular mechanical displacements (steps). Each pulse causes the rotor to advance by a precisely fixed angular increment called the **step angle (β)**, making it an *open-loop positioning actuator*. The term "Special Electrical Machine" itself implies that the stepper motor is **not** a conventional DC or AC induction machine; its uniqueness lies in its ability to provide **incremental motion** directly proportional to the number of input pulses, without the need for position feedback sensors (in open-loop mode). ### 1.2 Three Principal Constructional Families For the KTU 2024 syllabus, a student must master the principle of operation of **three principal types** of stepper motors: | Family | Rotor Construction | Operating Torque Source | |---|---|---| | **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft-iron salient pole, no magnets | Minimization of magnetic reluctance | | **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Radial-magnetized permanent magnets | Alignment of stator & rotor magnet poles | | **Hybrid (HB)** | PM + toothed iron rotor | Combined reluctance + magnet alignment | ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Compass & Magnet Needle:** > Imagine you are holding a compass needle (the rotor) in a magnetic field (the stator). If you *rotate* the magnetic field in discrete jumps, the compass needle "chases" the field, snapping into the new position each time. Now imagine doing this thousands of times per second with electromagnetic coils switched by digital pulses — that is **exactly** how a stepper motor operates! Each digital pulse = one "magnetic field jump" = one step. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Step Angles:** $\beta = 1.8^\circ$, $0.9^\circ$, $7.5^\circ$, $15^\circ$, $30^\circ$, $45^\circ$ - **Steps per Revolution:** $N_s = \dfrac{360^\circ}{\beta}$ - **Supply:** Unipolar (5-wire) or Bipolar (4-wire) DC pulsed excitation ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Universal Principle of Operation — The "Why" and "How" The stepper motor operates on **two simultaneous electromagnetic laws** that work in tandem: > [!NOTE] > **Law 1 — Torque from Variable Reluctance:** A ferromagnetic rotor always tries to align itself in a position that **minimizes the magnetic reluctance** (maximizes the inductance) of the flux path between the stator and rotor. This produces the *reluctance torque*. > > **Mathematical expression:** > $$T_{reluctance} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Law 2 — Torque from Permanent Magnet Alignment:** A permanent-magnet rotor always tries to align its N–S axis with the stator's magnetic field axis. This produces the *alignment (or excitation) torque*. > > **Mathematical expression:** > $$T_{alignment} \propto B_r \cdot m \cdot \sin(\delta)$$ > where $B_r$ is the rotor magnet remanent flux density, $m$ is the number of magnet pole pairs, and $\delta$ is the torque angle. ### 2.2 Detailed Operational Logic — Six Structured Steps The complete operational cycle of a stepper motor can be broken down as follows: 1. **Pulse Arrival:** A digital pulse train from the microcontroller/driver energizes a specific stator phase winding (e.g., Phase A). 2. **Stator Field Establishment:** Current $I_a$ flows through Phase A, creating a magnetic field along the stator's pole axis (say, the vertical axis). 3. **Rotor Displacement:** The rotor (whether salient-pole iron or PM) experiences a torque and rotates to align itself with the energized stator axis. 4. **Pulse Removal:** The first pulse is switched OFF, and the next pulse (Phase B) is switched ON. 5. **Discrete Step Completion:** The rotor moves by exactly one **step angle β** to its new aligned position. 6. **Continuous Rotation:** Repeating this sequence in a cyclic order (A → B → C → A…) produces continuous, locked-step rotation at a rate dictated by the pulse frequency $f_p$. ### 2.3 Three Excitation Modes KTU frequently tests knowledge of the excitation sequencing: - **Full-Step, Single-Coil Excitation (Wave Drive):** Only one phase ON at a time. Sequence: A → B → C → A → … - **Full-Step, Two-Coil Excitation:** Two adjacent phases ON simultaneously. Sequence: AB → BC → CA → AB → … - **Half-Step Excitation:** Alternates single and dual coil ON states. Sequence: A → AB → B → BC → C → CA → A → … Gives **2× the resolution** of full-step. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Condition / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Step angle | $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_s}$ | degrees (°) | $N_s$ = total steps per revolution | | 2 | Steps per revolution | $N_s = \dfrac{360^\circ}{\beta}$ | dimensionless | Integer, determined by rotor teeth & phases | | 3 | Step angle in terms of teeth & phases | $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$ | degrees | $N_r$ = rotor teeth, $m$ = stator phases | | 4 | Stepping frequency | $f_p = \dfrac{N_s \cdot n}{60}$ | pulses/sec (Hz) | $n$ = rotor speed in RPM | | 5 | Synchronous speed | $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_s}$ | RPM | Open-loop speed | | 6 | Resolution (microstep) | $\beta_{\mu} = \dfrac{\beta}{k}$ | degrees | $k$ = microstep subdivision factor | | 7 | Electrical step angle | $\beta_e = \dfrac{360^\circ}{m}$ | degrees | Independent of rotor teeth | | 8 | Holding torque | $T_h \propto B \cdot A \cdot N \cdot I$ | N·m | Torque when energized, rotor locked | | 9 | Slew rate (max) | $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt}\bigg\rvert_{max} = \beta \cdot f_{p,max}$ | rad/s | Limited by rotor inertia & load | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical distinction (KTU favourite):** The **electrical step angle** $\beta_e$ depends ONLY on the number of phases $m$, while the **mechanical step angle** $\beta$ depends on BOTH the rotor teeth $N_r$ and the number of phases $m$. They are linked by: $\beta = \dfrac{\beta_e}{N_r}$. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **CNC Machines & 3D Printers:** X-Y-Z axis positioning - **Robotics:** Joint articulation and end-effector positioning - **Hard Disk Drives:** Head-positioning actuator - **Printers & Plotters:** Paper feed and print-head carriage - **Medical Equipment:** Infusion pumps, ventilators, syringe drivers ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Angle in Terms of Rotor Teeth and Stator Phases **Given:** A multi-stack or hybrid stepper motor with $N_r$ rotor teeth and $m$ stator phases. **Objective:** Derive the mechanical step angle $\beta$. **Step 1 — Electrical Cycle Definition:** When the stator field completes one full electrical cycle, the rotor has advanced by **one rotor tooth-pitch**. The rotor tooth-pitch (in mechanical degrees) is: $$\tau_r = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_r}$$ **Step 2 — Electrical Step Angle:** In a $m$-phase stator, one full electrical cycle requires $m$ switching events. Therefore, the electrical step angle is: $$\beta_e = \dfrac{360^\circ}{m}$$ **Step 3 — Mechanical Step Angle (the KEY KTU result):** The mechanical displacement per switching event is the electrical step angle **divided** by the number of rotor teeth per electrical cycle, which is exactly $N_r$: $$\beta = \dfrac{\beta_e}{N_r} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r}$$ **Step 4 — Steps per Revolution:** The number of discrete steps required for one full mechanical revolution is: $$N_s = \dfrac{360^\circ}{\beta} = m \cdot N_r$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** For a typical hybrid stepper motor with $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth and $m = 2$ phases, the step angle is $\beta = 360^\circ / (2 \times 50) = 1.8^\circ$, giving $N_s = 200$ steps/revolution. This is the **industry-standard 1.8° stepper** used worldwide. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Pulse Frequency and Rotor Speed **Given:** A stepper motor with $N_s$ steps per revolution receives a pulse train of frequency $f_p$. **Step 1 — Angular Displacement per Pulse:** Each pulse causes the rotor to advance by $\beta = 360^\circ / N_s$. **Step 2 — Angular Velocity:** If $f_p$ pulses arrive per second, the angular velocity is: $$\omega = f_p \cdot \beta = f_p \cdot \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_s}$$ Converting to RPM (where $1\ \text{rev} = 360^\circ$ and $1\ \text{min} = 60\ \text{sec}$): $$n = \omega \cdot \dfrac{60}{360^\circ} = \dfrac{f_p \cdot 60}{N_s}$$ **Step 3 — Final Working Formula:** $$\boxed{\,n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_s}\,} \quad \text{[RPM]}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Torque Production in a VR Stepper Motor (Two-Phase Analysis) **Step 1 — Energy Stored in Air Gap:** The co-energy in the magnetic circuit is: $$W_f = \dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2$$ **Step 2 — Reluctance Torque (Negative Co-energy Gradient):** $$T = \dfrac{\partial W_f}{\partial \theta} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$ **Step 3 — Sinusoidal Approximation:** For a salient-pole rotor, the inductance varies sinusoidally with rotor angle: $$L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(2\theta)$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate:** $$\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = -2 L_1 \sin(2\theta)$$ **Step 5 — Final Reluctance Torque Expression:** $$T = -L_1 \, i^2 \, \sin(2\theta)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Significance:** The torque is **independent of the direction of current** ($i^2$ is always positive) but **doubly-periodic** in rotor angle. This is why VR stepper motors can be driven by **unipolar** (single-polarity) pulses without losing torque directionality. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Stepper Motor Families | Parameter | Variable Reluctance (VR) | Permanent Magnet (PM) | Hybrid (HB) | |---|---|---|---| | **Rotor** | Salient soft-iron, no magnet | Axially magnetized PM | PM + toothed iron caps | | **Detent torque** | Zero (no PM) | High (from magnet cogging) | Moderate | | **Step angle range** | $7.5^\circ$ to $30^\circ$ | $45^\circ$ to $90^\circ$ | $0.9^\circ$ to $5^\circ$ | | **Resolution** | Low to medium | Very low | Very high | | **Holding torque (de-energized)** | Zero | Significant (detent) | Significant (detent) | | **Speed range** | High | Low to medium | High | | **Driver complexity** | Simple | Simple | Moderate (needs precise current shaping) | | **Cost** | Lowest | Lowest | Highest | | **KTU weightage** | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stepper Motor Drive System ```mermaid flowchart LR PULSE[Pulse Generator
Microcontroller or PC Interface] --> DRIVER[Power Driver Stage
MOSFET or IGBT H-Bridge] DRIVER --> STATOR[Stator Phase Windings
Phase A, Phase B, Phase C, Phase D] STATOR -. Magnetic Coupling .-> ROTOR[Rotor Assembly
Salient Pole or PM or Hybrid] ROTOR --> SHAFT[Mechanical Shaft Output] SHAFT --> LOAD[Mechanical Load
Position or Speed Target] LOAD -. Feedback Optional .-> PULSE style PULSE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style DRIVER fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style STATOR fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ROTOR fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style SHAFT fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style LOAD fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Operational Sequence Flowchart — Single-Coil Full-Step Excitation ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Start of Stepping Cycle]) --> INIT[Rotor at Rest
Position Angle 0] INIT --> PULSE1[Pulse 1: Phase A Energized] PULSE1 --> STEP1[Rotor Aligns with Phase A Axis
Displacement by 1 step angle] STEP1 --> PULSE2[Pulse 2: Phase B Energized
Phase A Switched Off] PULSE2 --> STEP2[Rotor Advances by Step Angle
to Phase B Axis] STEP2 --> PULSE3[Pulse 3: Phase C Energized
Phase B Switched Off] PULSE3 --> STEP3[Rotor Advances by Step Angle
to Phase C Axis] STEP3 --> PULSE4[Pulse 4: Phase A Energized
Phase C Switched Off] PULSE4 --> STEP4[Completes One Electrical Cycle
Mechanical Displacement equals
One Rotor Tooth Pitch] STEP4 --> DECIDE{More Pulses
Pending?} DECIDE -- Yes --> PULSE1 DECIDE -- No --> STOP([Motor Halts and Locks
