at Final Step Position]) style START fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style STOP fill:#FF4500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style PULSE1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style PULSE2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style PULSE3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style PULSE4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style STEP1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style STEP2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style STEP3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style STEP4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Stator-Rotor Pole Configuration (Two-Phase VR Stepper) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STATOR[Stator Assembly with 4 Salient Poles] SA[Stator Pole A
Phase A Winding] SB[Stator Pole B
Phase B Winding] SC[Stator Pole C
Phase A Winding Reverse Polarity] SD[Stator Pole D
Phase B Winding Reverse Polarity] end subgraph ROTOR[Rotor Assembly with 2 Salient Iron Poles] RN[Rotor Tooth N] RS[Rotor Tooth S] end SA -. Air Gap .-> RN SB -. Air Gap .-> RN SC -. Air Gap .-> RS SD -. Air Gap .-> RS style SA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style SB fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style SC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style SD fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style RN fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style RS fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Schematic:** When Phase A is energized (Poles A & C are energized with opposite polarity, since they are wound in series), a horizontal magnetic flux path is established. The rotor aligns its salient poles with this horizontal axis, producing a $90^\circ$ step in the depicted 2-pole rotor. The minimum-reluctance path-seeking behaviour is the operating principle. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a stepper motor. Why is it classified as a "special" electrical machine?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A stepper motor is a **brushless synchronous motor** that converts a train of digital input pulses into discrete angular mechanical steps, with each pulse causing a fixed angular increment called the step angle β. (2 marks) It is classified as a "special" electrical machine because, unlike conventional DC or induction machines, it operates in **open-loop** position control, produces **incremental motion** synchronized to input pulses, and has no continuous rotation at constant torque — its operating principle is fundamentally different from the Lorentz-force-torque production in conventional machines. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State and explain the principle of operation of a Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The VR stepper motor operates on the principle of **minimization of magnetic reluctance**. (1 mark) The rotor is a salient soft-iron pole piece with no permanent magnets. When a stator phase is energized, the rotor rotates to align itself with the energized stator pole so that the magnetic flux path through the rotor experiences **minimum reluctance** (maximum inductance). (1 mark) Sequential energization of stator phases in cyclic order (A → B → C → …) produces **discrete step-wise rotation** of the rotor, with each step corresponding to the rotor moving from one minimum-reluctance position to the next. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry 14 marks with internal choice. The standard structure is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, OR a single 14-mark question with multiple sub-parts. Both formats are provided below for comprehensive preparation. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Treatment** **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and principle of operation of a **Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor**. Discuss the role of the detent torque. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Construction (3 marks):** A PM stepper motor consists of a stator with **two phase windings** (say, Phase A and Phase B) wound on salient poles, and a **cylindrical rotor made of permanent magnet material** (typically rare-earth ferrite or bonded NdFeB) axially magnetized with alternating N and S poles around its circumference. - The stator typically has **4 or 6 salient poles**, and each phase is wound on diametrically opposite poles connected in series. - The rotor has a number of pole pairs equal to (or a multiple of) the stator pole count. **Principle of Operation (3 marks):** The PM rotor experiences a torque whenever the stator field is energized, due to the tendency of the rotor's permanent magnet poles to **align with the stator's electromagnetic field poles** (like poles repel, unlike poles attract). - When Phase A is energized, the rotor's N pole aligns with the stator's S pole, and vice versa. - When Phase A is de-energized and Phase B is energized (with a spatial offset of 90° electrical in a 2-phase machine), the rotor rotates by one step angle to align with the new field. - The step angle is: $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{2 \times N_r}$ where $N_r$ is the number of rotor pole pairs. **Detent Torque (1 mark):** The detent torque is the **residual holding torque present even when the stator is de-energized**, caused by the attraction of the permanent magnet rotor to the nearest stator pole tips. It is a key advantage of PM stepper motors — they hold position even without continuous current. --- **(b)** A **hybrid stepper motor** has 50 rotor teeth and is wound with **2 phases**. Calculate: (i) The step angle in degrees (ii) The number of steps per revolution (iii) The pulse frequency required to obtain a rotor speed of 1200 RPM (iv) The mechanical displacement after 240 pulses **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $N_r = 50$, $m = 2$ phases, $n = 1200$ RPM. **(i) Step Angle (2 marks):** $$\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{2 \times 50} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{100} = 3.6^\circ$$ **(ii) Steps per Revolution (1 mark):** $$N_s = m \cdot N_r = 2 \times 50 = 100 \ \text{steps/rev}$$ **(iii) Pulse Frequency (2 marks):** $$f_p = \dfrac{N_s \cdot n}{60} = \dfrac{100 \times 1200}{60} = 2000 \ \text{Hz (pulses/sec)}$$ **(iv) Mechanical Displacement after 240 pulses (2 marks):** $$\theta = 240 \times \beta = 240 \times 3.6^\circ = 864^\circ = 2 \ \text{full revolutions} + 144^\circ$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Students frequently forget the factor of $N_r$ in the step-angle formula and incorrectly write $\beta = 180^\circ$ instead of $3.6^\circ$. **Always divide by BOTH** the number of phases AND the rotor teeth. > - When computing mechanical displacement, do not stop at the angle in degrees — **convert to revolutions** if the displacement exceeds 360°. Show the modular reduction explicitly. > - Units: Pulse frequency is in **Hz** (pulses per second), NOT kHz, unless specified. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Treatment** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a Hybrid Stepper Motor** with a block-level schematic. How does it combine the features of both VR and PM stepper motors? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Construction Overview (2 marks):** The hybrid stepper motor is the **most widely used** industrial stepper. It features: - A **two-phase stator** identical to a PM or VR stepper - A **two-stack rotor** (or single axially-magnetized cylindrical rotor) with **toothed iron caps** on either end of an axially-magnetized permanent magnet disc - The teeth on the two end caps are **axially displaced by half a tooth-pitch** from each other **Principle of Operation (3 marks):** - The axially-magnetized PM in the rotor provides a **permanent biasing flux** along the shaft axis. - The toothed end caps serve as flux conduits that channel the PM flux outward into the air gap. - When Phase A is energized, the stator flux **adds to** the PM flux on one tooth and **subtracts from** it on the diametrically opposite tooth, creating a strong asymmetric field. - The rotor aligns to **minimize the total reluctance** of the dominant flux path (the side where flux adds), producing a **combination of reluctance torque and alignment torque**. - The half-tooth-pitch axial offset between the two end caps allows **smooth torque production** and very fine step angles (typically 1.8°). **Hybrid Advantage (2 marks):** - Combines the **high resolution** of the VR stepper (small step angles from multiple rotor teeth) - Combines the **holding/detent torque** of the PM stepper (permanent magnet) - Yields the best of both worlds: high resolution + high holding torque + small step angle --- **(b)** A **variable reluctance stepper motor** has a **stator with 8 salient poles** and a **rotor with 6 salient teeth**. The phases are excited in the sequence A → B → C → A → … Calculate: (i) The step angle in mechanical degrees (ii) The number of steps per revolution (iii) The total electrical angle traversed after 40 steps (iv) The time required to complete one full revolution at a pulse rate of 500 pulses per second **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** Stator poles = 8 (so phases $m = 4$ for a 4-phase VR stepper), rotor teeth $N_r = 6$. **(i) Step Angle (2 marks):** $$\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{4 \times 6} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{24} = 15^\circ$$ **(ii) Steps per Revolution (1 mark):** $$N_s = m \cdot N_r = 4 \times 6 = 24 \ \text{steps/rev}$$ **(iii) Electrical Angle after 40 steps (2 marks):** Total mechanical displacement: $$\theta_{mech} = 40 \times 15^\circ = 600^\circ = 1\ \text{rev} + 240^\circ$$ Electrical step angle: $$\beta_e = \dfrac{360^\circ}{m} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{4} = 90^\circ/\text{step}$$ Total electrical angle: $$\theta_e = 40 \times 90^\circ = 3600^\circ = 10\ \text{full electrical cycles}$$ **(iv) Time for One Revolution at 500 pulses/sec (2 marks):** $$N_s = 24 \ \text{steps/rev}$$ $$T_{rev} = \dfrac{24}{500} = 0.048\ \text{sec} = 48\ \text{ms}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - The number of **stator poles** is NOT always equal to the number of **phases**. In a 4-phase VR stepper, 8 stator poles are wound in 4 phase-pairs (each phase energizes two diametrically opposite poles). Always identify $m$ from the *winding grouping*, not the raw pole count. > - In part (iii), the **electrical angle** and **mechanical angle** are different quantities. Many students confuse them and present the mechanical answer as the electrical answer. > - In part (iv), students sometimes compute the time for **one electrical cycle** instead of **one mechanical revolution**. The question explicitly asks for one full revolution, which requires 24 steps for this motor. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principle of Operation of Stepper Motors** - ✅ **Definition:** Stepper motor = brushless synchronous motor → digital pulses → discrete angular steps. - ✅ **Three Types:** VR, PM, HB — each has a distinct rotor principle (reluctance minimization, magnet alignment, hybrid combined). - ✅ **Key Formula (MEMORIZE):** $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{m \cdot N_r}$ — divides by BOTH phases AND rotor teeth. - ✅ **Speed Formula:** $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_s}$ — relates pulse frequency to RPM. - ✅ **Electrical vs. Mechanical Step Angle:** $\beta_e = 360^\circ/m$ (electrical), $\beta = \beta_e / N_r$ (mechanical). - ✅ **Excitation Modes:** Wave (single-coil), Full-step (two-coil), Half-step (alternating) — half-step doubles resolution. - ✅ **Torque Source in VR:** $T = \frac{1}{2}i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ — current-squared dependence, unipolar drive works. - ✅ **Torque Source in PM:** Depends on $\sin(\delta)$ — torque direction reverses with current direction, requires bipolar drive. - ✅ **Detent Torque:** Present in PM and HB (zero in VR) — provides holding torque when de-energized. - ✅ **Open-Loop Limitation:** If load torque exceeds available motor torque at a given pulse rate, the rotor **loses synchronism** (stalls) — no feedback to correct. - ✅ **Standard 1.8° Hybrid:** $N_r = 50$, $m = 2$, $N_s = 200$ — the workhorse of CNC and 3D printing. - ✅ **KTU-2024 Trend:** Numerical problems on $\beta$, $N_s$, $f_p$, and $n$ appear in **every** end-semester paper — practice at least 5 numerical variants.

topics.title: Phasor Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phasor Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Phasor Diagram** is a two-dimensional graphical representation of alternating quantities (voltages and currents) in a rotating electrical machine, plotted as stationary vectors (phasors) of constant magnitude rotating at a synchronous angular velocity $\omega_s$ rad/s. The relative angular displacement between phasors indicates the **phase angle** between the corresponding sinusoidal quantities, while the length of each phasor represents the **RMS (Root Mean Square) value** of that quantity.

In the context of **Special Electrical Machines (Module 2)** — particularly **Synchronous Reluctance Motors (SynRM)**, **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM)**, **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors**, and **Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM)** — the phasor diagram is an indispensable analytical tool used to:

- Visualize the spatial relationship between **stator voltage (V)**, **back-EMF (E)**, **armature current (I)**, and the various reactance drops.
- Derive the **power factor angle ($\phi$)** and **load/torque angle ($\delta$)**.
- Analyze the **direct-axis ($d$) and quadrature-axis ($q$)** component decomposition in salient-pole machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Phasor diagrams form the analytical foundation for the steady-state modeling of PMSM and SynRM. A clear understanding of phasor construction is **mandatory** for solving 14-mark problems on these machines.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **clock face** with multiple hands of different lengths, all rotating together at the *same constant speed*. Each hand represents one alternating quantity in the machine.

- The **length of the hand** = how large the quantity is (its RMS amplitude).
- The **angle between two hands** = how much one quantity *leads* or *lags* the other (the phase difference).
- Since the entire clock rotates at synchronous speed, the **relative angles between the hands never change** — which is why we can "freeze" the diagram on paper to study steady-state behavior.

Think of it this way: the **stator supply voltage** and the **rotor back-EMF** are both "hands" on this clock. Their relative angle $\delta$ is the **load angle** — it tells us how hard the motor is working. A bigger $\delta$ means the rotor is "dragged" further behind the stator field, indicating a heavier mechanical load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** Phasors in AC machine analysis are drawn using **RMS values**, not peak values. For a sinusoidal quantity $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \alpha)$, the phasor magnitude is $\vert V \vert = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$, represented symbolically as $\mathbf{V} = V \angle \alpha$.

### Standard Notation in Phasor Diagrams

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|--------|----------|------|
| $\mathbf{V}$ or $\overline{V}$ | Per-phase stator terminal voltage | Volts (V) |
| $\mathbf{E}$ or $\overline{E_f}$ | Back-EMF (induced EMF) | Volts (V) |
| $\mathbf{I_a}$ or $\overline{I_a}$ | Armature (stator) current | Amperes (A) |
| $R_a$ | Armature winding resistance | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $X_s$ | Synchronous reactance | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $X_d, X_q$ | d-axis and q-axis reactances | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $\phi$ | Power factor angle (between V and I) | Degrees/Electrical |
| $\delta$ | Load/Torque angle (between V and E) | Degrees/Electrical |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Rules for Constructing a Phasor Diagram

The construction of a phasor diagram for any special AC machine follows a strict, step-by-step protocol. Deviating from this protocol is a guaranteed source of mark loss in KTU examinations.

**Step 1 — Choose the Reference Phasor.**
By convention, either the **terminal voltage $\mathbf{V}$** (most common) or the **back-EMF $\mathbf{E}$** is taken as the horizontal reference phasor along the positive x-axis. KTU problems typically use $\mathbf{V}$ as reference.

**Step 2 — Plot the Current Phasor.**
The armature current $\mathbf{I_a}$ is drawn at an angle $\phi$ (the power factor angle) from the reference voltage. If the machine operates at a **lagging power factor** (most common for motors), $\mathbf{I_a}$ is placed *below* $\mathbf{V}$. For **leading power factor** (over-excited synchronous machines), $\mathbf{I_a}$ is placed *above* $\mathbf{V}$.

**Step 3 — Plot the Resistance Drop.**
The resistive voltage drop $\mathbf{I_a} R_a$ is drawn **parallel to $\mathbf{I_a}$** (i.e., in phase with current) starting from the tip of $\mathbf{V}$.

**Step 4 — Plot the Reactance Drop.**
The reactive voltage drop $j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$ is drawn **perpendicular to $\mathbf{I_a}$**, leading by **90°** for inductive reactance. Starting from the tip of the $\mathbf{I_a} R_a$ phasor, add this perpendicular vector.

**Step 5 — Locate the Back-EMF.**
The back-EMF $\mathbf{E}$ is the phasor that **closes the loop**:
$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{I_a} R_a + j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$$
The angle between $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{V}$ is the **load angle $\delta$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Why" Behind the Protocol:** Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) applied to the equivalent circuit of a synchronous machine gives $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{I_a}(R_a + jX_s)$. Each term in this equation corresponds to a phasor in the diagram, and the geometric addition of these phasors must close the loop to satisfy KVL.

### 2.2 Phasor Diagram for the Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)

The PMSM is the most frequently tested machine in KTU Module 2. Its equivalent circuit per phase closely resembles that of a conventional wound-rotor synchronous motor, except the field excitation is provided by **permanent magnets** (no field winding on the rotor).

**Per-Phase Voltage Equation:**
$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{E_f} + \mathbf{I_a} R_a + j \mathbf{I_a} X_s$$

where:
- $\mathbf{E_f}$ = back-EMF induced by the permanent magnets (rotor excitation).
- $X_s = \omega L_s$ = synchronous reactance, with $L_s$ being the synchronous inductance.
- $R_a$ = armature (stator phase) resistance.

**Phasor Diagram Geometry (for lagging power factor motor operation):**
1. Draw $\mathbf{V}$ along the horizontal axis.
2. Draw $\mathbf{I_a}$ at angle $-\phi$ from $\mathbf{V}$ (lagging, drawn clockwise below $\mathbf{V}$).
3. From the tip of $\mathbf{V}$, draw $\mathbf{I_a} R_a$ parallel to $\mathbf{I_a}$.
4. From the tip of $\mathbf{I_a} R_a$, draw $j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$ leading $\mathbf{I_a}$ by 90°.
5. The phasor from origin to the tip of $j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$ gives $\mathbf{E_f}$ (or equivalently, the phasor that closes the loop). The angle $\delta$ between $\mathbf{E_f}$ and $\mathbf{V}$ is the load angle.

### 2.3 Phasor Diagram for the Synchronous Reluctance Motor (SynRM)

The SynRM has no field winding and no permanent magnets. Torque is produced solely by the **rotor's tendency to align with the lowest reluctance path** (maximum permeance axis) with the rotating stator MMF. Because of the salient-pole rotor geometry, the machine has **two different synchronous reactances** — the **direct-axis reactance $X_d$** (along the high-permeance rotor axis) and the **quadrature-axis reactance $X_q$** (along the low-permeance rotor axis).

For a SynRM, $X_d > X_q$ is achieved through axially laminated or transversally laminated rotor designs with flux barriers.

**Key Relationships for SynRM Phasor Diagram:**

The armature current is decomposed along the rotor's $d$ and $q$ axes:
$$\mathbf{I_d} = I_a \sin(\delta) \quad \text{(d-axis component, lagging the q-axis by 90°)}$$
$$\mathbf{I_q} = I_a \cos(\delta) \quad \text{(q-axis component, in phase with q-axis voltage)}$$

The voltage equation in rotor reference frame:
$$V_d = R_a I_d - \omega L_q I_q$$
$$V_q = R_a I_q + \omega L_d I_d$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Applicable Machine |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{E_f} + \mathbf{I_a} R_a + j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$ | Per-phase voltage equation | PMSM, SynRM (approx.) |
| 2 | $\vert E_f \vert = \omega \psi_m = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_m$ | Back-EMF magnitude | PMSM |
| 3 | $X_s = \omega L_s = 2 \pi f L_s$ | Synchronous reactance | All synchronous machines |
| 4 | $X_d = \omega L_d$, $\quad X_q = \omega L_q$ | d-axis and q-axis reactances | Salient-pole machines (SynRM) |
| 5 | $\mathbf{I_d} = I_a \sin \delta$, $\quad \mathbf{I_q} = I_a \cos \delta$ | Current decomposition in rotor frame | SynRM, salient-pole PMSM |
| 6 | $P = 3 V I_a \cos \phi$ | Three-phase input power | All 3-phase AC machines |
| 7 | $P_{gap} = 3 \dfrac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin \delta$ | Air-gap power (smooth rotor, non-salient) | PMSM (non-salient) |
| 8 | $P_{gap} = 3 \dfrac{V E_f}{X_d} \sin \delta + \dfrac{V^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q} - \dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin 2\delta$ | Air-gap power (salient pole) | SynRM ($E_f = 0$ case) |
| 9 | $T_e = \dfrac{P_{gap}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{X_d - X_q}{X_d X_q}\right) \sin 2\delta$ | Electromagnetic torque (SynRM) | SynRM |
| 10 | $\tan \phi = \dfrac{X_s I_a \cos \delta - R_a I_a \sin \delta}{V + X_s I_a \sin \delta + R_a I_a \cos \delta}$ | Power factor angle (PMSM) | PMSM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Phasor diagrams are the everyday language of power system engineers. They are used to design **power factor correction schemes**, analyze **transient stability** of generators, and tune **vector control (FOC)** algorithms in modern variable-frequency drives (VFDs) for PMSM and SynRM used in industrial automation, robotics, and electric vehicle traction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Phasor Diagram Relationships for a PMSM at Lagging Power Factor

We will derive the relationship between terminal voltage $V$, back-EMF $E_f$, armature current $I_a$, load angle $\delta$, and power factor angle $\phi$ for a non-salient pole PMSM operating at lagging power factor.

**Starting Point — The Per-Phase Voltage Equation:**
$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{E_f} + \mathbf{I_a} R_a + j \mathbf{I_a} X_s$$

**Step 1 — Resolve the current into two orthogonal components.**

Take $\mathbf{E_f}$ as the reference (along the positive x-axis) for mathematical convenience. Since the motor operates at a lagging power factor, $\mathbf{I_a}$ lags $\mathbf{V}$ by $\phi$, and $\mathbf{V}$ leads $\mathbf{E_f}$ by the load angle $\delta$. So $\mathbf{I_a}$ makes an angle $(\delta - \phi)$ with $\mathbf{E_f}$.

Resolving $\mathbf{I_a}$ along the $d$-axis (parallel to $\mathbf{E_f}$) and $q$-axis (perpendicular to $\mathbf{E_f}$):
$$I_d = I_a \sin(\delta - \phi)$$
$$I_q = I_a \cos(\delta - \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Project the voltage equation onto the d-axis (along $\mathbf{E_f}$).**

Taking real parts (d-axis projection):
$$V \cos \delta = E_f + I_a R_a \cos(\delta - \phi) + I_a X_s \sin(\delta - \phi) \cdot (-1)$$

The negative sign on $I_a X_s$ term arises because the $q$-axis component of current produces a $d$-axis voltage drop in the *opposite* direction (cross-coupling). Substituting $I_q$ and $I_d$:
$$V \cos \delta = E_f + R_a I_q - X_s I_d$$

Substituting the d-q components:
$$V \cos \delta = E_f + R_a I_a \cos(\delta - \phi) - X_s I_a \sin(\delta - \phi)$$

Rearranging for the back-EMF:
$$E_f = V \cos \delta + X_s I_a \sin(\delta - \phi) - R_a I_a \cos(\delta - \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Project the voltage equation onto the q-axis (perpendicular to $\mathbf{E_f}$).**

Taking imaginary parts (q-axis projection):
$$V \sin \delta = 0 + I_a R_a \sin(\delta - \phi) + I_a X_s \cos(\delta - \phi)$$

The $j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$ term, when projected on the q-axis (perpendicular to $\mathbf{E_f}$), gives a positive $X_s I_q$ contribution. Substituting $I_d$ and $I_q$:
$$V \sin \delta = R_a I_a \sin(\delta - \phi) + X_s I_a \cos(\delta - \phi)$$

$$V \sin \delta = R_a I_d + X_s I_q$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two projected equations into the final form.**

These two simultaneous equations are the **backbone of PMSM phasor analysis**:
$$\boxed{V \cos \delta = E_f + R_a I_q - X_s I_d}$$
$$\boxed{V \sin \delta = R_a I_d + X_s I_q}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the magnitude of $\mathbf{E_f}$ in terms of $V$ and the drops (textbook style).**

In a phasor diagram, the **horizontal projection** of $\mathbf{E_f}$ (when $\mathbf{E_f}$ is the reference) equals the horizontal projection of $\mathbf{V}$ plus the horizontal projections of all the IR and IX drops. The result (with $\mathbf{V}$ as reference instead) is:

$$E_f^2 = (V \cos \phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V \sin \phi \pm I_a X_s)^2$$

where the $\pm$ sign depends on the nature of the load:
- **Lagging p.f. (motor):** use $-$ for $I_a X_s$ (drops the terminal voltage).
- **Leading p.f. (motor or over-excited):** use $+$ for $I_a X_s$ (rises the terminal voltage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Logic for KTU Board Exam:** When solving numerical problems, KTU examiners expect you to (1) state the voltage equation, (2) draw the phasor diagram with clearly labeled angles, (3) apply the cosine rule or sine rule to relate unknowns, and (4) substitute numerical values. Skipping step (2) costs **2 to 3 marks** in a 14-mark question.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Salient-Pole Synchronous Reluctance Motor Phasor Diagram

The SynRM has no rotor excitation ($E_f = 0$), so its phasor diagram is a special case. However, because of the salient rotor, the d-axis and q-axis reactances are different.

**Starting Point — d-q Frame Voltage Equations (with $E_f = 0$):**
$$V_d = R_a I_d - \omega L_q I_q = R_a I_d - X_q I_q$$
$$V_q = R_a I_q + \omega L_d I_d = R_a I_q + X_d I_d$$

**Step 1 — Phasor diagram geometry (neglecting $R_a$ for clarity).**

With $R_a \approx 0$, the equations simplify to:
$$V_d = -X_q I_q$$
$$V_q = X_d I_d$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of the terminal voltage.**

$$V^2 = V_d^2 + V_q^2 = X_q^2 I_q^2 + X_d^2 I_d^2$$

**Step 3 — Torque angle relationship.**

Since $I_d = I_a \sin \delta$ and $I_q = I_a \cos \delta$:
$$V^2 = X_q^2 I_a^2 \cos^2 \delta + X_d^2 I_a^2 \sin^2 \delta$$

$$V = I_a \sqrt{X_q^2 \cos^2 \delta + X_d^2 \sin^2 \delta}$$

**Step 4 — Power factor angle.**

The input power is:
$$P_{in} = 3 \left( V_d I_d + V_q I_q \right) = 3 \left( -X_q I_q \cdot I_d + X_d I_d \cdot I_q \right) = 3 (X_d - X_q) I_d I_q$$

The apparent power is:
$$S = 3 V I_a = 3 V I_a$$

The power factor becomes:
$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{P_{in}}{S} = \dfrac{(X_d - X_q) I_d I_q}{V I_a} = \dfrac{(X_d - X_q) I_a^2 \sin \delta \cos \delta}{V I_a}$$

$$\boxed{\cos \phi = \dfrac{(X_d - X_q) I_a \sin 2\delta}{2 V}}$$

This is a **key result** that explains why SynRMs inherently have a poor power factor (the saliency ratio $X_d / X_q$ must be very large to compensate, which is why modern SynRMs use sophisticated transversally laminated rotors with multiple flux barriers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Saliency Matters:** A cylindrical rotor PMSM (non-salient) has $X_d = X_q$, so the reluctance term vanishes. Only salient machines (SynRM, salient-pole PMSM with $L_d \neq L_q$) can produce **reluctance torque** in addition to **magnet/excitation torque**.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, Y-connected PMSM has a per-phase back-EMF of $E_f = 200 \text{ V}$ (RMS), armature resistance $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, synchronous reactance $X_s = 5 \, \Omega$. The machine operates with a load angle $\delta = 20°$ and draws an armature current $I_a = 25 \text{ A}$ at lagging power factor. Compute the per-phase terminal voltage $V$ and the power factor angle $\phi$.

**Solution — Step 1:** Express the d-q components of $\mathbf{I_a}$ using $\delta$ and the unknown $\phi$:
$$I_d = I_a \sin(\delta - \phi) = 25 \sin(20° - \phi)$$
$$I_q = I_a \cos(\delta - \phi) = 25 \cos(20° - \phi)$$

**Solution — Step 2:** Apply the d-axis voltage equation (from Section 3.1):
$$V \cos \delta = E_f + R_a I_q - X_s I_d$$
$$V \cos 20° = 200 + 0.5 \times 25 \cos(20° - \phi) - 5 \times 25 \sin(20° - \phi)$$

**Solution — Step 3:** Apply the q-axis voltage equation:
$$V \sin \delta = R_a I_d + X_s I_q$$
$$V \sin 20° = 0.5 \times 25 \sin(20° - \phi) + 5 \times 25 \cos(20° - \phi)$$

**Solution — Step 4:** Solve the two equations simultaneously (treating $V$ and $\phi$ as unknowns).

From Step 3, isolate $V$:
$$V = \dfrac{12.5 \sin(20° - \phi) + 125 \cos(20° - \phi)}{\sin 20°}$$

Substitute into Step 2 and solve iteratively (or use numerical solver). For $\phi \approx 25°$ (lagging), the final answer is approximately:
$$V \approx 214.6 \text{ V (per phase, RMS)}$$
$$\phi \approx 25° \text{ (lagging)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** In 14-mark problems, KTU examiners allocate ~3 marks for the phasor diagram, ~3 marks for the equations, and ~8 marks for the numerical solution. **Always redraw the phasor diagram** as the first thing on your answer sheet to secure those 3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Phasor Diagram Construction Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Phasor Diagram Construction] --> B{Choose Reference Phasor}
    B -- V as reference --> C[Draw V along +X axis]
    B -- E as reference --> C1[Draw E along +X axis]
    C --> D{Identify Power Factor}
    C1 --> D1{Identify Power Factor}
    D -- Lagging PF --> E[I_a drawn below V by angle phi]
    D -- Leading PF --> F[I_a drawn above V by angle phi]
    D1 -- Lagging PF --> E1[I_a drawn below E by angle delta minus phi]
    D1 -- Leading PF --> F1[I_a drawn above E by angle delta minus phi]
    E --> G[Add I_aR_a: parallel to I_a from tip of V]
    F --> G
    E1 --> G1[Add I_aR_a: parallel to I_a from tip of E]
    F1 --> G1
    G --> H[Add jI_aX_s: 90 deg lead of I_a from tip of I_aR_a]
    G1 --> H
    H --> I[Closing phasor: from origin to tip = E or V]
    I --> J[Mark load angle delta between V and E]
    J --> K[Mark PF angle phi between V and I_a]
    K --> L[End: Complete Phasor Diagram]
```

### 4.2 PMSM Phasor Diagram Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stator [Stator Side Quantities]
        V[Terminal Voltage V]
        Ia[Armature Current I_a]
        Phi[Power Factor Angle phi]
    end

    subgraph Drops [Internal Voltage Drops]
        IRa[I_a R_a - In phase with I_a]
        IXs[j I_a X_s - Leads I_a by 90 deg]
    end

    subgraph Rotor [Rotor Side Quantities]
        Ef[Back EMF E_f]
        Delta[Load Angle delta]
    end

    V -- defines reference axis --> Ef
    V -- angle phi --> Ia
    Ia -- projection --> IRa
    Ia -- 90 deg lead --> IXs
    V -- equals vector sum --> IRa
    IRa -- plus --> IXs
    IXs -- plus --> Ef
    V -- leads Ef by --> Delta
    Ef -- drives --> Pgap[Air Gap Power]
    Pgap -- divided by omega_s --> Te[Electromagnetic Torque]
```

### 4.3 SynRM Phasor Diagram — Salient Pole Decomposition (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Input: V and I_a at power factor angle phi] --> Resolve[Resolve I_a into d-q components]
    Resolve --> IdCalc[I_d = I_a sin delta]
    Resolve --> IqCalc[I_q = I_a cos delta]
    IdCalc --> VdEq[V_d = R_a I_d minus X_q I_q]
    IqCalc --> VqEq[V_q = R_a I_q plus X_d I_d]
    VdEq --> Sum[V equals sqrt V_d^2 plus V_q^2]
    VqEq --> Sum
    Sum --> TeCalc[T_e = 3 over 2 omega_s times V^2 times X_d minus X_q over X_d X_q times sin 2 delta]
    TeCalc --> Out[Output: Electromagnetic Torque]
```

### 4.4 Visual Schematic of a Phasor Diagram (Text Representation)

For revision, here is a compact textual representation of a PMSM phasor diagram at **lagging power factor**, drawn in the standard KTU board-exam layout:

```
                          j I_a X_s
                        |<--------->|
                        |           |
                        |    I_a R_a
                        |   |<--->|
                        |   |     |
                        |   |     +--→ E_f
                        |   |    /
                        |   |   / δ
                        |   |  /
                        |   | /____________
                        |   |/             I_a (φ below V)
                        |   +              ↘
                        |  / V             ↘
                        | /                ↘
                        |/_________________↘
                        O (origin)
```

Where:
- $\mathbf{V}$ is the reference (horizontal, pointing right).
- $\mathbf{I_a}$ lags $\mathbf{V}$ by $\phi$ (drawn below the horizontal).
- $\mathbf{E_f}$ lies at angle $\delta$ *above* $\mathbf{V}$ (rotor is delivering energy, so $E_f$ leads $V$).
- The voltage drops $\mathbf{I_a} R_a$ (in phase with $I_a$) and $j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$ (90° ahead of $I_a$) close the vector loop: $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{E_f} + \mathbf{I_a} R_a + j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Most board problems on phasor diagrams are coupled with numerical computation of **power factor, torque, efficiency, or regulation**. Always carry forward the same $V$, $I_a$, $\phi$, and $\delta$ through all sub-parts of a question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Define a phasor diagram. Why are RMS values (and not peak values) used to represent the magnitude of phasors in AC machine analysis?*

**Model Answer:**
A phasor diagram is a graphical representation of sinusoidal AC quantities (voltages, currents, EMFs) as stationary vectors of constant length, rotating at synchronous angular velocity $\omega_s$. The length of each phasor represents the magnitude of the corresponding quantity, and the angle between two phasors represents the phase difference between them.

**RMS values are used** because:
1. RMS values directly give the **effective heating value** (DC equivalent) of the AC quantity, which is what matters for power calculations ($P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi$).
2. RMS values **avoid the $\sqrt{2}$ factor** that appears in peak-value phasor arithmetic, making computations more elegant.
3. Standard power system measurements (voltmeters, ammeters) display **RMS values** by default, so phasor magnitudes are directly comparable to instrument readings.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*In the phasor diagram of a PMSM operating at lagging power factor, what is the load angle $\delta$? State its significance in the operation of the machine.*

**Model Answer:**
The **load angle $\delta$** (also called the torque angle or power angle) is the angle between the **per-phase back-EMF phasor $\mathbf{E_f}$** and the **per-phase terminal voltage phasor $\mathbf{V}$** in the phasor diagram of a synchronous machine.

**Significance of $\delta$:**
1. It is a direct measure of the **mechanical load** on the machine — as load increases, $\delta$ increases.
2. The **air-gap power** $P_{gap}$ of a non-salient pole PMSM is given by $P_{gap} = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{X_s} \sin \delta$, so $\delta$ determines the power transfer capability.
3. The **steady-state stability limit** is reached when $\delta = 90°$ (maximum power transfer). For stable operation, $\delta < 90°$ is required, typically limited to about $30°-40°$ in practice.
4. In **vector control (FOC)** of PMSM drives, the value of $\delta$ is the controlled variable that regulates torque production.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — Alternating Option 1** [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With the help of a neatly labeled phasor diagram, derive the per-phase voltage equation of a 3-phase, Y-connected PMSM operating at lagging power factor. Clearly define every angle and every phasor used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, star-connected PMSM has the following per-phase parameters: $E_f = 180 \text{ V}$, $R_a = 0.8 \, \Omega$, $X_s = 6 \, \Omega$. The motor draws an armature current of $I_a = 15 \text{ A}$ at a power factor of 0.85 lagging. Compute:
- (i) The load angle $\delta$ and the terminal voltage $V$ per phase.
- (ii) The developed air-gap power and the electromagnetic torque.
- (iii) The power factor angle $\phi$ in degrees. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Per-phase equivalent circuit of PMSM:**
The stator per-phase circuit consists of a back-EMF source $E_f$ (representing the permanent-magnet excitation), an armature resistance $R_a$, and a synchronous reactance $X_s$ in series. The terminals deliver voltage $V$ to the load.

**Per-phase voltage equation (KVL):**
$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{E_f} + \mathbf{I_a} R_a + j \mathbf{I_a} X_s$$

**Phasor diagram construction (for lagging p.f.):**
1. Take $\mathbf{V}$ as the reference phasor along the positive x-axis.
2. Draw $\mathbf{I_a}$ lagging $\mathbf{V}$ by angle $\phi$ (so it lies in the fourth quadrant, below the x-axis).
3. From the tip of $\mathbf{V}$, draw the resistive drop $\mathbf{I_a} R_a$ parallel to $\mathbf{I_a}$.
4. From the tip of $\mathbf{I_a} R_a$, draw the reactive drop $j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$ leading $\mathbf{I_a}$ by $90°$ (i.e., rotated counter-clockwise by $90°$).
5. The closing phasor from the origin to the final tip is $\mathbf{E_f}$.
6. The angle $\delta$ between $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{E_f}$ (measured counter-clockwise from $V$ to $E_f$) is the load angle.

**Final derived equation:**
$$V^2 = (E_f + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi)^2 + (I_a X_s \cos\phi - I_a R_a \sin\phi)^2$$

This is the cosine-rule form, obtained by resolving all phasors into the d-axis (horizontal) and q-axis (vertical) components and applying the Pythagorean theorem.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the power factor angle $\phi$:**
$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.85) = 31.79°$$

**Step 2 — Compute the synchronous speed:**
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$
$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Use the cosine rule to compute the terminal voltage:**

The in-phase and quadrature components (with $V$ as reference, $\mathbf{I_a}$ lagging by $\phi$):
$$V \cos 0 = V = E_f \cos \delta + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi \quad \text{(d-axis)}$$
$$V \sin 0 = 0 = E_f \sin \delta - I_a R_a \sin\phi + I_a X_s \cos\phi \quad \text{(q-axis)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the q-axis equation for $\delta$:**
$$E_f \sin \delta = I_a R_a \sin\phi - I_a X_s \cos\phi$$
$$E_f \sin \delta = 15 \times 0.8 \times \sin 31.79° - 15 \times 6 \times \cos 31.79°$$
$$E_f \sin \delta = 12 \times 0.5271 - 90 \times 0.8500$$
$$E_f \sin \delta = 6.325 - 76.50 = -70.175 \text{ V}$$

$$\sin \delta = \dfrac{-70.175}{180} = -0.3899$$
$$\delta = -22.93°$$

The negative sign indicates that $E_f$ is actually **behind** $V$ in the lagging sense for this particular numerical condition. Taking the magnitude: $\delta \approx 22.93°$ (electrical).

**Step 5 — Compute the terminal voltage from the d-axis equation:**
$$V = E_f \cos \delta + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi$$
$$V = 180 \cos(-22.93°) + 15 \times 0.8 \times 0.85 + 15 \times 6 \times \sin 31.79°$$
$$V = 180 \times 0.9211 + 10.20 + 90 \times 0.5271$$
$$V = 165.80 + 10.20 + 47.44$$
$$\boxed{V \approx 223.4 \text{ V (per phase)}}$$

**Step 6 — Air-gap power (using the simplified non-salient formula):**
$$P_{gap} = 3 \cdot \dfrac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin \delta = 3 \times \dfrac{223.4 \times 180}{6} \times \sin(-22.93°)$$
$$P_{gap} = 3 \times 6702 \times (-0.3899) = -7837.5 \text{ W}$$

The negative sign suggests we should take the magnitude (or check sign conventions for motor vs. generator). For a motor:
$$P_{gap} = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{X_s} \sin \delta$$
$$\boxed{P_{gap} \approx 7.84 \text{ kW (motor mode)}}$$

**Step 7 — Electromagnetic torque:**
$$T_e = \dfrac{P_{gap}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{7837.5}{104.72}$$
$$\boxed{T_e \approx 74.85 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 8 — Power factor angle:**
$$\boxed{\phi = 31.79° \text{ (lagging)}}$$

---

#### **Question B — Alternating Option 2** [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Draw the phasor diagram of a salient-pole Synchronous Reluctance Motor (SynRM) with $\mathbf{V}$ as the reference. Clearly show the $d$-axis and $q$-axis components of the armature current. Explain why a SynRM has a poor inherent power factor compared to a PMSM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz SynRM has the following per-phase parameters: $V = 220 \text{ V}$ (line-to-line, Y-connected), $R_a \approx 0$, $X_d = 30 \, \Omega$, $X_q = 6 \, \Omega$. If the motor operates at a load angle $\delta = 30°$, calculate:
- (i) The armature current magnitude $I_a$.
- (ii) The power factor angle $\phi$ and the power factor.
- (iii) The developed electromagnetic torque. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Phasor Diagram Description (for SynRM, $V$ as reference, lagging p.f. motor):**
1. Draw $\mathbf{V}$ along the positive x-axis (reference).
2. Draw $\mathbf{I_a}$ lagging $\mathbf{V}$ by angle $\phi$.
3. The rotor's d-axis is oriented at an angle $\delta$ *behind* $\mathbf{V}$ (in the direction of rotor lag), so the d-axis is at angle $-\delta$ from $\mathbf{V}$.
4. Resolve $\mathbf{I_a}$ into:
   - $I_d = I_a \sin \delta$ along the d-axis.
   - $I_q = I_a \cos \delta$ along the q-axis (perpendicular to d-axis).
5. The d-axis voltage drop is $X_d I_d$ (along d-axis), and the q-axis voltage drop is $X_q I_q$ (along q-axis).
6. The total voltage equation closes: $V = \sqrt{(X_d I_d)^2 + (X_q I_q)^2}$ (with $R_a \approx 0$).

**Why SynRMs Have Poor Inherent Power Factor:**
A SynRM has **no separate excitation source** ($E_f = 0$); it relies entirely on the **saliency ratio $X_d / X_q$** to produce torque. However, the same saliency that produces the reluctance torque also causes a large **demagnetizing armature reaction** along the d-axis (which is the high-permeance, low-reactance path... actually, d-axis is high-reactance, but the current component $I_d$ lags the voltage component by 90°, contributing to poor power factor).

Quantitatively, the power factor of a SynRM is:
$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{(X_d - X_q) I_a \sin 2\delta}{2 V}$$

For a small saliency ratio $X_d / X_q$ (e.g., 5:1 instead of 10:1), the numerator becomes small, giving a poor $\cos \phi$. In contrast, a PMSM has an explicit $\mathbf{E_f}$ term in its voltage equation, which contributes to the real power and pushes the power factor closer to unity. To compensate, modern SynRMs use **transversally laminated rotors with multiple flux barriers** to push $X_d / X_q$ above 10:1, but the power factor still typically remains in the 0.6-0.75 range — much lower than a PMSM (0.85-0.95).

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Line voltage $V_L = 220 \text{ V}$, so phase voltage $V_{ph} = \dfrac{220}{\sqrt{3}} = 127.02 \text{ V}$.
- $R_a \approx 0$, $X_d = 30 \, \Omega$, $X_q = 6 \, \Omega$, $\delta = 30°$.
- $P = 4$ poles, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$.

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed:**
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$
$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_a$ using the simplified voltage equation (with $R_a \approx 0$):**

From Section 3.2:
$$V_{ph}^2 = X_q^2 I_a^2 \cos^2 \delta + X_d^2 I_a^2 \sin^2 \delta$$
$$V_{ph} = I_a \sqrt{X_q^2 \cos^2 \delta + X_d^2 \sin^2 \delta}$$
$$127.02 = I_a \sqrt{6^2 \cos^2 30° + 30^2 \sin^2 30°}$$
$$127.02 = I_a \sqrt{36 \times 0.75 + 900 \times 0.25}$$
$$127.02 = I_a \sqrt{27 + 225}$$
$$127.02 = I_a \sqrt{252}$$
$$127.02 = I_a \times 15.87$$
$$\boxed{I_a \approx 8.00 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute d-q components:**
$$I_d = I_a \sin \delta = 8.00 \times \sin 30° = 8.00 \times 0.5 = 4.00 \text{ A}$$
$$I_q = I_a \cos \delta = 8.00 \times \cos 30° = 8.00 \times 0.866 = 6.928 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Power factor angle:**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.2:
$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{(X_d - X_q) I_a \sin 2\delta}{2 V_{ph}}$$
$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{(30 - 6) \times 8.00 \times \sin 60°}{2 \times 127.02}$$
$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{24 \times 8.00 \times 0.866}{254.04}$$
$$\cos \phi = \dfrac{166.27}{254.04} = 0.6545$$

$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.6545) \approx 49.13°$$

$$\boxed{\text{Power Factor} \approx 0.6545 \text{ (lagging)}}$$

**Step 5 — Electromagnetic torque (SynRM formula):**
$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^2 (X_d - X_q)}{X_d X_q} \sin 2\delta$$
$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2 \times 157.08} \cdot \dfrac{127.02^2 \times (30 - 6)}{30 \times 6} \times \sin 60°$$
$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{314.16} \cdot \dfrac{16133 \times 24}{180} \times 0.866$$
$$T_e = 0.009549 \times 2151.07 \times 0.866$$
$$T_e = 0.009549 \times 1862.83$$
$$\boxed{T_e \approx 17.79 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\delta$ and $\phi$:** $\delta$ is the angle between $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{E_f}$ (electromagnetic), while $\phi$ is the angle between $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{I_a}$ (electrical load). Mixing them up leads to wrong answers and a 7-mark penalty in 14-mark problems.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of $I_a R_a$ drop:** In the phasor diagram, $\mathbf{I_a} R_a$ is *in phase* with $\mathbf{I_a}$ (drawn parallel), but when projecting onto the d-axis, students often forget the cosine/sine resolution and lose 2-3 marks.
>
> 3. **Using line voltage instead of phase voltage:** In Y-connected machines, $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$. In $\Delta$-connected machines, $V_{ph} = V_L$ and $I_{ph} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$. **Always check the connection type** stated in the problem.
>
> 4. **Drawing the phasor diagram with peak values instead of RMS:** KTU examiners explicitly require RMS-based phasor diagrams. Using peak values (with $\sqrt{2}$ factors) will be marked incorrect.
>
> 5. **Not specifying lagging/leading nature of power factor:** Every phasor diagram must clearly indicate whether the machine is operating at **lagging, unity, or leading** power factor. The direction of $\mathbf{I_a}$ relative to $\mathbf{V}$ depends on this.
>
> 6. **Missing the $E_f = 0$ condition in SynRM problems:** Students often copy the PMSM equation $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{E_f} + \mathbf{I_a} R_a + j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$ for SynRMs, forgetting that $E_f = 0$ and that the $X_s$ must be replaced by the d-q reactances $X_d$ and $X_q$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Phasor Diagram (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
>
> - **Phasor diagram** = graphical depiction of AC quantities as **stationary vectors** rotating at synchronous speed, with **lengths = RMS magnitudes** and **angles = phase differences**.
> - **Reference phasor** is conventionally $\mathbf{V}$ (terminal voltage) drawn along the +x axis.
> - **For lagging p.f. (motor):** $\mathbf{I_a}$ is drawn **clockwise (below)** from $\mathbf{V}$ by angle $\phi$.
> - **For leading p.f.:** $\mathbf{I_a}$ is drawn **counter-clockwise (above)** from $\mathbf{V}$ by angle $\phi$.
> - **Voltage drops:** $\mathbf{I_a} R_a$ is **in phase** with $\mathbf{I_a}$; $j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$ **leads** $\mathbf{I_a}$ by **90°**.
> - **PMSM per-phase equation:** $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{E_f} + \mathbf{I_a} R_a + j\mathbf{I_a} X_s$.
> - **SynRM:** $E_f = 0$, but the rotor is salient, so $X_d \neq X_q$ and the d-q decomposition **must** be used.
> - **Load angle $\delta$** is the angle between $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{E_f}$ — directly tied to the **mechanical power output** and **stability limit** ($\delta < 90°$).
> - **Saliency ratio $X_d / X_q$** determines the reluctance torque component — high ratio (10:1 or more) is needed for a competitive SynRM.
> - **Cosine rule form** (useful for numerical problems): $V^2 = (E_f + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi)^2 + (I_a X_s \cos\phi - I_a R_a \sin\phi)^2$.
> - **Y vs. $\Delta$:** Always remember $V_{ph,Y} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ and $I_{ph,\Delta} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$ when using per-phase analysis.
> - **KTU-favorite trick:** When the problem gives $V$, $E_f$, $I_a$, and $\phi$, use the cosine rule directly. When it gives $\delta$ instead of $\phi$, use the d-q projection method.
> - **Final answer must include units** (V, A, N·m, W) and **physical interpretation** (e.g., "lagging p.f. of 0.85", "motoring mode", "stable operation since $\delta < 90°$").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Torque Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Stepper Motors** (the principal focus of Module 2 in PEEET743), the **Torque Equation** is the mathematical expression that quantifies the **electromagnetic torque developed by the motor** as a function of stator current, rotor position (electrical angle), and the magnetic circuit parameters (inductance, reluctance, and mutual coupling with the permanent magnet, if present).

For a generalised multi-phase, salient-pole electromagnetic actuator with `m` stator phases, the **co-energy based torque equation** is given by the partial derivative of the magnetic co-energy `W'co` with respect to the mechanical rotor angle `θm`:

$$
T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m} \right|_{i = \text{const}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEET743 – Module 2):** The torque equation is the central analytical tool for the comparative study of **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motors**, **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motors**, and **Hybrid Stepper Motors**. Mastery of this equation is mandatory for deriving *holding torque*, *detent torque*, and *pull-out torque* characteristics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child sitting on a **see-saw (teeter-totter)**. The angular displacement of the see-saw about its pivot is analogous to the rotor angle `θ`. Now, place a **magnet on the rotor** and an **electromagnet (coil) on the stator**. The rotor will always try to align with the position where the magnetic field energy is **minimum** (or co-energy is maximum). The "pull" the rotor experiences is the **torque**.

- If you energise the coil, the rotor **snaps to a new equilibrium position**. The strength of this snap is the torque.
- If the coil current `i` increases, the snap (torque) increases — proportional to the *square* of the current for **reluctance torque** and *linearly* to current for **mutual torque** (when a permanent magnet is present).
- The **rate at which inductance changes with rotor angle** `dL/dθ` acts like the *stiffness of the spring* connecting the see-saw to its equilibrium — a high `dL/dθ` means a stronger restoring force (torque).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **µ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** (permeability of free space)
> - **θe = (P/2) × θm** where `P` is the number of poles and `θm` is the mechanical angle in radians
> - Torque unit: **N·m (Newton-metre)**
> - Standard holding torque range for industrial PM steppers: **0.5 N·m to 25 N·m**

## 1.3 Why is the Torque Equation Important?

The torque equation governs **four critical KTU-defined performance parameters**:

1. **Holding Torque (TH)** — Torque at zero speed with rated current (locked rotor).
2. **Detent Torque (TD)** — Torque produced solely by the permanent magnet with **no stator excitation**.
3. **Pull-out Torque** — Maximum load torque the motor can drive at a given stepping rate before losing synchronism.
4. **Torque-Speed Characteristic** — The `T vs. ω` curve, crucial for sizing the motor in CNC, robotics, and 3D-printer applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Energy Method

The electromagnetic torque in any singly-excited electromagnetic system is derived from the **co-energy principle** (preferred over energy for current-source systems):

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}(i, \theta)}{\partial \theta}
$$

The total **magnetic co-energy** stored in an `N`-phase stepper motor with a permanent magnet flux `φm` is:

$$
W'_{co} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} i_k^2 L_k(\theta) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} i_k \, \phi_m \, M_{sk}(\theta) + W'_{pm}(\theta)
$$

Differentiating with respect to `θ` yields the **Generalised Stepper Motor Torque Equation**:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} i_k^2 \frac{dL_k(\theta)}{d\theta} + \sum_{k=1}^{N} i_k \, \phi_m \, \frac{dM_{sk}(\theta)}{d\theta} - \frac{1}{2} \phi_m^2 \frac{d\mathcal{R}_{pm}(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

where the three terms represent the **Reluctance Torque**, **Mutual (Alignment) Torque**, and **Detent Torque** respectively.

## 2.2 Why & How — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Why co-energy and not stored energy?** Stepper motors are typically driven by **current sources** (chopper drives), making co-energy the natural state function. Using stored energy `W` would require holding flux linkage constant, which is impractical.

- **Why three terms?**
  - The **first term** (`½ i² dL/dθ`) exists *only if* the inductance varies with position — true for **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motors (VRSM)** and **Hybrid Stepper Motors (HSM)**.
  - The **second term** (`i φm dM/dθ`) exists *only if* a permanent magnet couples with the stator winding — true for **PM Stepper Motors** and **HSM**.
  - The **third term** (detent torque) exists whenever the permanent magnet's reluctance path varies with `θ` — true for **HSM** (and weakly in PM motors due to stator slotting).

- **How does the equation reduce for specific motor types?**
  - **VRSM** has no PM → `φm = 0` → only the first term survives.
  - **PM Stepper** has no salient rotor for strong reluctance variation → only the second term dominates (plus a small detent).
  - **HSM** has both → all three terms contribute.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Motor Type | Simplified Torque Equation | Key Assumption |
|---|------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM)** | $T_e = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | $\phi_m = 0$, isotropic rotor PM absent |
| 2 | **Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM-stepper)** | $T_e = N \, i \, \phi_m \, \dfrac{dM(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | $L(\theta) \approx \text{const}$ (cylindrical rotor) |
| 3 | **Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)** | $T_e = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} + N \, i \, \phi_m \, \dfrac{dM(\theta)}{d\theta} + T_D$ | All three terms; `T_D` = detent torque |
| 4 | **Generalised Co-energy Form** | $T_e = \dfrac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta}\bigg\vert_{i=\text{const}}$ | Applicable to all doubly-salient machines |
| 5 | **Electrical–Mechanical Angle Relation** | $\theta_e = \dfrac{P}{2} \theta_m$ | `P` = number of stator poles |
| 6 | **Holding Torque (locked rotor)** | $T_H = T_e \big\vert_{\omega=0, \, i=I_{\text{rated}}}$ | At standstill with rated current |
| 7 | **Detent Torque** | $T_D = -\dfrac{1}{2} \phi_m^2 \dfrac{d\mathcal{R}_{pm}(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Stator unexcited, PM only |
| 8 | **Steady-state Pull-out Torque (approx.) | $T_{po} \approx \dfrac{T_H}{1 + \left(\omega / \omega_0\right)^2}$ | One-pole approximation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Guard:** All vertical bars for absolute value or "evaluated at" are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table parser safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machining & 3D Printers:** Holding torque determines the maximum load a stepper can hold without encoder feedback — directly computed from the torque equation.
- **Robotic Joints:** Hybrid steppers use the full 3-term equation to size actuators for arm articulation.
- **Hard Disk Drives (legacy):** PM steppers (voice-coil predecessors) used the mutual torque term for head positioning.
- **Satellite Antenna Pointing:** VRSMs with reluctance torque provide fail-safe (detent-less) positioning under radiation-hardened vacuum conditions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM) Torque Equation

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider a singly-excited, two-stack VRSM with rotor saliency. The flux linkage is:

$$
\lambda = L(\theta) \cdot i
$$

The stored magnetic energy in a linear magnetic system is:

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2
$$

### 3.1.2 Conversion to Co-energy

For a current-excited system, the **co-energy** is:

$$
W'_{co} = i \lambda - W = i L(\theta) i - \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
W'_{co} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We used the identity $W'_{co} = i\lambda - W$ because stepper drives are voltage-sourced PWM/chopper drives, which are *better* approximated as current sources (the chopper regulates current to a reference).

### 3.1.3 Differentiation

Differentiating co-energy with respect to the mechanical rotor angle `θ`, holding current constant:

$$
T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta} \right|_{i}
$$

$$
T_e = \left. \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \left[ \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 \right] \right|_{i}
$$

Pulling `½ i²` out (current is held constant):

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Because `½ i²` has no `θ` dependence, it acts as a scalar multiplier in the derivative.

### 3.1.4 Final VRSM Result

$$
\boxed{T_e^{VRSM} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor Torque Equation

### 3.2.1 Setup
A PM stepper has a cylindrical rotor with a permanent magnet of flux `φm`. The stator phase winding has `N` turns, current `i`, and **mutual inductance** `M(θ)` with the rotor magnet. The self-inductance `L` is nearly constant (no saliency).

The total flux linking the stator phase is the sum of self-flux and mutual flux:

$$
\lambda = L \, i + N \, \phi_m \, \cos(\theta)
$$

The mutual coupling term uses the **cosine spatial variation** typical of sinusoidally-distributed windings.

### 3.2.2 Co-energy Expression

$$
W'_{co} = \frac{1}{2} L i^2 + N \, i \, \phi_m \cos(\theta) + \frac{1}{2} \mathcal{R}_{pm} \phi_m^2
$$

The first term has no `θ` dependence in `L` (cylindrical rotor), and the third term is **constant** w.r.t. controllable variables (it represents the PM's self-energy). Both vanish upon differentiation w.r.t. `θ`.

### 3.2.3 Differentiation

$$
T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta} \right|_{i, \phi_m}
$$

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \left[ N \, i \, \phi_m \cos(\theta) \right]
$$

The derivative of `cos(θ)` is `-sin(θ)`. Convention: in KTU textbooks, the torque is expressed as a **positive** quantity using `sin(θ)` (this sign convention aligns the torque with the direction of motion toward the alignment position):

$$
T_e = -N \, i \, \phi_m \, \sin(\theta)
$$

Taking the magnitude for the developed torque (KTU sign convention):

$$
\boxed{T_e^{PM} = N \, i \, \phi_m \frac{dM(\theta)}{d\theta} = N \, i \, \phi_m \sin(\theta)}
$$

where we used the fact that `M(θ) = cos(θ)` (in per-unit) so that `dM/dθ = -sin(θ)`, and the negative sign is absorbed into the directional convention.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM) Torque Equation

### 3.3.1 Setup
The Hybrid Stepper combines an axially-magnetised PM rotor with **two end-cap stacks** having salient poles. Both `L(θ)` (from saliency) and `M(θ)` (from PM) vary with rotor angle.

The total co-energy is:

$$
W'_{co} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 + N \, i \, \phi_m \cos(\theta) + \frac{1}{2} \mathcal{R}_{pm}(\theta) \phi_m^2
$$

### 3.3.2 Differentiation Term-by-Term

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta}
$$

$$
T_e = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}}_{\text{Reluctance Term}} + \underbrace{N \, i \, \phi_m \frac{d(\cos\theta)}{d\theta}}_{\text{Mutual Term}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \phi_m^2 \frac{d\mathcal{R}_{pm}(\theta)}{d\theta}}_{\text{Detent Term}}
$$

### 3.3.3 Final HSM Result

$$
\boxed{T_e^{HSM} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} + N \, i \, \phi_m \sin(\theta) - \frac{1}{2} \phi_m^2 \frac{d\mathcal{R}_{pm}(\theta)}{d\theta}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The minus sign in the detent term reflects that the PM's self-energy `W_pm = ½ ℛ φ²` is *minimised* (not co-energy) — its derivative yields a *restoring* torque, hence the sign flip from the co-energy form.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Term-by-Term Comparison

| Term | Physical Origin | Present in VRSM? | Present in PMSM? | Present in HSM? | Order w.r.t. `i` |
|------|----------------|------------------|------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Reluctance Torque $\frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Variation of self-inductance | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (cylindrical) | ✅ Yes | Quadratic in `i` |
| Mutual Torque $N i \phi_m \frac{dM}{d\theta}$ | PM–stator coupling | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Linear in `i` |
| Detent Torque $\frac{1}{2} \phi_m^2 \frac{d\mathcal{R}_{pm}}{d\theta}$ | PM in variable reluctance path | ❌ No | Weak (slotting) | ✅ Yes | Independent of `i` |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A VRSM has inductance variation given by $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_2 \cos(2\theta)$ where $L_0 = 50 \text{ mH}$, $L_2 = 20 \text{ mH}$, and the phase current is $i = 2 \text{ A}$. Find the **holding torque** at $\theta = 45°$ electrical.

### Step 1 — Compute $dL/d\theta$:

$$
L(\theta) = L_0 + L_2 \cos(2\theta)
$$

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -2 L_2 \sin(2\theta)
$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate at $\theta = 45°$:

$$
\sin(2 \times 45°) = \sin(90°) = 1
$$

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta}\bigg\vert_{\theta=45°} = -2 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 = -0.04 \text{ H/rad}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply VRSM Torque Equation:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{2} \times (2)^2 \times (-0.04)
$$

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times (-0.04) = -0.08 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret:

The negative sign indicates the torque is in the **restoring direction** (pulling the rotor toward the minimum reluctance position at $\theta = 0°$). The **magnitude of the holding torque** is:

$$
\boxed{\vert T_H \vert = 0.08 \text{ N·m} = 80 \text{ mN·m}}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define "Holding Torque" in a stepper motor and state its dependence on rotor position.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Holding Torque (`T_H`)** is defined as the maximum static torque that a stepper motor can develop at **zero speed** (locked rotor condition) with **rated phase current** energised. It is measured at the **detent position** of the rotor (typically the maximum of the static torque-angle curve). Its dependence on rotor position is **sinusoidal** (for PM/HSM) or follows the **derivative of the inductance profile** (for VRSM), given by:
> $$
> T_H(\theta) = N \, i_{rated} \, \phi_m \, \sin(\theta) \quad \text{(PM/HSM)}
> $$
> $$
> T_H(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} i_{rated}^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} \quad \text{(VRSM)}
> $$
> The peak holding torque occurs at the rotor angle where `dL/dθ` (VRSM) or `sin(θ)` (PM) is maximum, i.e., at `θ = 45°` (VRSM) or `θ = 90°` (PM) electrical. **(3 marks: 1 defn + 1 equation + 1 position explanation)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between "Reluctance Torque" and "Mutual Torque" in a hybrid stepper motor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> | Aspect | Reluctance Torque | Mutual Torque |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Origin** | Variation of self-inductance `L(θ)` | Variation of mutual coupling `M(θ)` between PM and stator |
> | **Equation** | $T_{rel} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | $T_{mut} = N \, i \, \phi_m \frac{dM(\theta)}{d\theta}$ |
> | **Dependence on `i`** | Quadratic ($i^2$) | Linear ($i$) |
> | **Exists when** | `L` is a function of `θ` (salient rotor) | Permanent magnet is present |
>
> The reluctance torque is **bidirectional** with current polarity (depends on $i^2$), while the **mutual torque reverses with current direction** (depends on `i`). **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Set A]`
**Q: Derive the torque equation of a Hybrid Stepper Motor starting from the magnetic co-energy principle. Clearly identify all three components of the developed torque and explain the role of the permanent magnet in each term. (14 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Co-energy Formulation and Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define the magnetic co-energy for an HSM.**

A hybrid stepper motor has an axially-magnetised rotor PM and two end-cap stacks with salient poles. The total magnetic co-energy in the air-gap, considering stator phase current `i`, PM flux `φm`, and their mutual coupling, is:

$$
W'_{co} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 + N \, i \, \phi_m \cos(\theta) + \frac{1}{2} \mathcal{R}_{pm}(\theta) \phi_m^2
$$

where:
- `L(θ)` = position-dependent self-inductance of the stator phase
- `N` = number of turns per phase
- `M(θ) = cos(θ)` = normalised mutual inductance function
- `ℛ_pm(θ)` = reluctance of the PM flux path (varies with rotor position due to saliency)

**Step 2: Apply the co-energy torque principle.**

For a current-excited system:

$$
T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta} \right|_{i = \text{const}, \, \phi_m = \text{const}}
$$

**Step 3: Differentiate term-by-term.**

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} + N \, i \, \phi_m \frac{d[\cos\theta]}{d\theta} + \frac{1}{2} \phi_m^2 \frac{d\mathcal{R}_{pm}(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the derivatives.**

$$
\frac{d[\cos\theta]}{d\theta} = -\sin\theta
$$

For the standard KTU sign convention (positive torque in the direction of motion), the second term is written as `+ N i φm sin(θ)` by absorbing the negative sign:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} + N \, i \, \phi_m \sin(\theta) + \frac{1}{2} \phi_m^2 \frac{d\mathcal{R}_{pm}(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

**Final HSM Torque Equation:**

$$
\boxed{T_e^{HSM} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}}_{\text{Reluctance}} + \underbrace{N \, i \, \phi_m \sin\theta}_{\text{Mutual}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \phi_m^2 \frac{d\mathcal{R}_{pm}}{d\theta}}_{\text{Detent}}}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 for co-energy setup, 2 for differentiation, 2 for final equation, 1 for clarity of derivation)**

#### Part (b) — Role of the Permanent Magnet in Each Term (7 Marks)

**1. Reluctance Torque Term:** $T_{rel} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$

- The PM plays **no direct role** in generating reluctance torque. This torque arises purely from the variation of stator-phase self-inductance with rotor angle.
- However, the PM **indirectly affects** `L(θ)` by saturating the iron path — at high `φm`, the differential inductance slope `dL/dθ` is reduced, slightly lowering reluctance torque.
- In a **pure VRSM** (no PM), this is the **only** torque term. **(2 marks)**

**2. Mutual Torque Term:** $T_{mut} = N \, i \, \phi_m \sin(\theta)$

- The PM is the **sole source of magnetic field** coupling with the stator. Without `φm`, this term vanishes.
- The torque is **proportional to `φm`** (linearly) — doubling the PM flux doubles the mutual torque.
- The torque is also **proportional to `i`** (linearly) and reverses sign with current direction (bi-polar control).
- This is the **dominant torque term** in PM steppers and contributes the major share in HSM. **(3 marks)**

**3. Detent Torque Term:** $T_D = \frac{1}{2} \phi_m^2 \frac{d\mathcal{R}_{pm}(\theta)}{d\theta}$

- The PM is the **sole originator** of detent torque. It is independent of stator current `i`.
- It exists because the rotor PM sees a **varying reluctance path** as it rotates (due to stator/rotor tooth saliency).
- Detent torque is **always present** — it acts as a small restoring torque even when the stator is un-energised (fail-safe detent position).
- In a HSM, detent torque is **comparable in magnitude** to one phase's worth of holding torque, providing smooth micro-stepping and stable rest positions. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B (Internal Choice for Question A) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Set B]`
**Q: (a) Derive the torque equation of a Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor from first principles. (7 Marks)**
**(b) A 2-stack VRSM has the following inductance profile per phase: $L(\theta) = 10 + 4\cos(4\theta)$ mH. If the phase current is 3 A, compute the peak reluctance torque developed. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of VRSM Torque Equation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: System Definition.**
A VRSM has a salient-pole rotor with no permanent magnet. The stator phase winding, when energised, produces a flux that links the rotor through a **position-dependent air-gap reluctance** `ℛ(θ)`, and hence a position-dependent inductance:

$$
L(\theta) = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}(\theta)}
$$

**Step 2: Flux Linkage and Stored Energy.**

The flux linkage is:

$$
\lambda = L(\theta) \, i
$$

The stored magnetic energy:

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2
$$

**Step 3: Co-energy Conversion.**
For a current-excited system:

$$
W'_{co} = i \lambda - W = i^2 L(\theta) - \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2
$$

**Step 4: Torque from Co-energy.**

$$
T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta} \right|_{i}
$$

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \left[ \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 \right]
$$

Since `i` is held constant:

$$
\boxed{T_e^{VRSM} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}}
$$

**Key Observation:** Torque is **proportional to `i²`** (quadratic) and **independent of current direction** — a unique property of VRSM that means uni-polar drives can still produce torque in both rotation directions via commutation.

**(7 marks: 2 setup, 2 co-energy, 2 differentiation, 1 final form)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation of Peak Reluctance Torque (7 Marks)

**Given:**

$$
L(\theta) = 10 + 4 \cos(4\theta) \text{ mH}, \quad i = 3 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 1: Find $dL/d\theta$.**

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -4 \times 4 \sin(4\theta) \times 10^{-3} = -16 \sin(4\theta) \text{ mH/rad}
$$

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -16 \times 10^{-3} \sin(4\theta) \text{ H/rad}
$$

**Step 2: Find the angle where $|dL/d\theta|$ is maximum.**

$|\sin(4\theta)| = 1$ when $4\theta = 90°$, i.e., $\theta = 22.5°$ electrical.

**Step 3: Evaluate the maximum slope.**

$$
\left\vert \frac{dL}{d\theta} \right\vert_{\max} = 16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H/rad}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the VRSM torque equation at peak slope.**

$$
T_{e,\text{peak}} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \left\vert \frac{dL}{d\theta} \right\vert_{\max}
$$

$$
T_{e,\text{peak}} = \frac{1}{2} \times (3)^2 \times 16 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
T_{e,\text{peak}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 0.016
$$

$$
T_{e,\text{peak}} = 0.072 \text{ N·m} = 72 \text{ mN·m}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{e,\text{peak}} = 72 \text{ mN·m at } \theta = 22.5° \text{ electrical}}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 for derivative, 1 for max angle, 2 for substitution, 1 for unit conversion, 1 for final boxed answer)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Torque Equation Problems:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (–2 to –3 marks):** Many students write $T_e = -N i \phi_m \sin(\theta)$ without absorbing the sign into the KTU convention. Always confirm with the textbook's sign convention.
> 2. **Forgetting the ½ factor in co-energy (–1 mark):** The co-energy is $\frac{1}{2} L i^2$, not $L i^2$. The ½ is the single most-skipped step.
> 3. **Treating `L(θ)` as constant in HSM/PM derivations (–2 marks):** Even in PM motors, `L(θ)` has a small second-harmonic variation; examiners expect a note on this.
> 4. **Confusing `θ_e` and `θ_m` (–1 to –2 marks):** Use $\theta_e = (P/2) \theta_m$ consistently. Many numerical answers are off by a factor of 2 because of this.
> 5. **Omitting units in the final numerical answer (–1 mark):** Always state `N·m` or `mN·m` explicitly.
> 6. **Mixing VRSM and PM equations in HSM derivation (–3 marks):** In a HSM, you **must** show all three terms with their physical origin. Vague statements like "torque has reluctance and PM components" earn partial credit only.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The **fundamental torque equation** is $T_e = \partial W'_{co} / \partial \theta \big\vert_i$ (co-energy principle).
- ✅ **Three torque components** exist in stepper motors: **Reluctance** (∝ $i^2$), **Mutual** (∝ $i$), and **Detent** (independent of `i`).
- ✅ **VRSM**: Only reluctance torque → $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$; torque direction is **independent of current polarity**.
- ✅ **PM Stepper**: Only mutual torque dominates → $T_e = N i \phi_m \sin(\theta)$; torque direction **reverses with current polarity**.
- ✅ **HSM**: All three terms → $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} + N i \phi_m \sin(\theta) + T_D$.
- ✅ **Electrical–Mechanical Angle**: $\theta_e = (P/2) \theta_m$; never confuse the two in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Holding Torque** is measured at the **peak of the static torque-angle curve** at standstill with rated current.
- ✅ **Detent Torque** is present **even with the stator un-energised** — it is the rotor PM seeking minimum reluctance.
- ✅ **Mutual torque** is **linear in `i`**, while **reluctance torque** is **quadratic in `i`** — this dictates drive strategy (chopper vs. voltage source).
- ✅ **Co-energy** is used (not stored energy) because stepper drives approximate **current source** behaviour.
- ✅ **Sign convention**: Positive torque is in the direction of increasing `θ` (rotor moving toward alignment position).
- ✅ The **½ factor** in co-energy is a **mandatory step** — losing it loses a mark.
- ✅ Units: `L` in **H**, `θ` in **rad**, `i` in **A**, `φm` in **Wb** → Torque in **N·m**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications; Switched Reluctance Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Applications; Switched Reluctance Motors # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM):** A doubly-salient, singly-excited electrical machine in which torque is produced by the tendency of the magnetically salient rotor to move to a position of **minimum reluctance** with respect to the excited stator phase winding. The stator carries concentrated phase windings, while the rotor is a simple, robust, laminated steel structure with **no windings or permanent magnets**. The name itself is a complete descriptor of the operating principle: - **Switched** → Stator phase windings are energized (switched ON/OFF) sequentially using a power electronic converter based on rotor position feedback. - **Reluctance** → Torque arises solely from variation of magnetic reluctance in the magnetic circuit (no slip-ring, no rotor excitation, no permanent magnet). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Dog & Bone" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition Check:** Imagine a mechanical spring-loaded dog tied to a leash, and a bone placed at different positions on the floor. Whenever you point the dog (the energized stator pole) toward a bone (the rotor pole), the dog "snaps" toward the bone to minimise the tension in the leash (reluctance). In an SRM, this snapping force is **reluctance torque**, and by switching the bone position electronically (sequential phase excitation), the dog is forced to chase a rotating magnetic field. A simpler picture: a metallic pin on a rotating disc always slides toward a stationary electromagnet when the electromagnet is energised because the magnetic circuit prefers minimum reluctance (maximum inductance). Replace the pin with a salient rotor pole pair, energise the right stator phase at the right instant, and you have continuous rotation. ## 1.3 Distinguishing Salient Features | Feature | SRM Characteristic | Comparison | |---|---|---| | **Stator** | Salient poles with concentrated coils | Like a BLDC stator but without overlap winding | | **Rotor** | Plain laminated salient steel, no copper | Cheapest, most rugged rotor of any motor | | **Excitation** | **Singly-excited** (only stator) | Unlike induction (induced) or PMSM (PM) | | **Torque origin** | $\frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Pure reluctance torque | | **Operation** | Sequential switching (depends on rotor position) | Requires rotor position sensor | | **Starting current** | Low (typically 30 % of full-load) | Excellent for direct-on-line starts | ## 1.4 Primary Applications Landscape > [!IMPORTANT] > SRMs dominate **high-speed, harsh-environment, and variable-speed** applications because their rotor is just stamped steel — it cannot burn out, cannot demagnetise, and survives centrifugal forces at extreme speeds. - 🚗 **Electric & Hybrid Vehicles** (Tesla Roadster early prototype, Renault), where SRM competes with induction and PMSM. - ✈️ **Aerospace Actuators & Fuel Pumps** (e.g., Sundstrand SRM drives in Boeing & Airbus subsystems) — high reliability, fault tolerance. - ⛏️ **Mining & Oil Drilling** — explosion-proof operation, ability to operate in dirty/hot environments. - 🏠 **Domestic Appliances** — vacuum cleaners, washing machines, fans (e.g., Dyson digital motor, Vax SRM drives). - 🏭 **Industrial Variable-Speed Drives** — conveyors, mixers, textile spindles, pumps. - 🖥️ **Servo & Robotics** — high torque-to-inertia ratio, wide speed range. - 🚆 **Rail Traction Auxiliaries** — door operators, HVAC blowers. # ⚡ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principle of Torque Production — The "Reluctance Snap" When a stator phase is energised, it produces a magnetic field along the path of **minimum reluctance**. If the rotor salient pole is *not* aligned with the excited stator pole, the rotor experiences a tangential force that tries to align the two — producing torque. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Torque exists **only when the inductance L(θ) is varying with rotor position θ**.
