At the aligned position (L = L_max) and unaligned position (L = L_min), the slope $\frac{dL}{d\theta} = 0$, so torque is momentarily zero — these are natural commutation points. ## 2.2 Operating Modes & Quadrant Operation SRMs naturally operate in **all four quadrants** (motoring + generating, forward + reverse) by simply changing the firing angles (turn-on $\theta_{on}$ and turn-off $\theta_{off}$): - **Motoring mode** → Phase excited during the **rising** inductance region ($\frac{dL}{d\theta} > 0$). - **Generating mode** → Phase excited during the **falling** inductance region ($\frac{dL}{d\theta} < 0$). - **Freewheeling** → Phase current circulates through a diode to allow decay. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Below are **all exam-critical formulas** for SRM Module 2. Memorise the units and the sign convention. $$ \begin{aligned} T_e &= \frac{1}{2} \, i^{2} \, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} \quad \text{(Instantaneous torque, N·m)}\\[6pt] W_{f} &= \int_{0}^{I} \lambda(i,\theta)\, di \quad \text{(Co-energy, J)}\\[6pt] T_e &= \left.\frac{\partial W_f(i,\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i=\text{const}}\\[6pt] L(\theta) &= L_{min} + (L_{max} - L_{min}) \cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{N_r\theta}{2}\right) \quad \text{(Idealised inductance profile)}\\[6pt] \beta_s &= \frac{2\pi}{N_s}, \quad \beta_r = \frac{2\pi}{N_r} \quad \text{(Stator / rotor pole arcs, electrical rad)}\\[6pt] \theta_{step} &= \frac{2\pi}{q \, N_r} \quad \text{(Step angle, mechanical rad)}\\[6pt] q &= \text{number of stator phases},\quad N_r = \text{rotor pole count}\\[6pt] \omega_s &= \frac{2\pi f}{N_r} \quad \text{(Synchronous speed, rad/s)}\\[6pt] T_{avg} &= \frac{q \, N_r}{4\pi} \, \psi_{max}^{2} \, \frac{(L_{max} - L_{min})}{L_{max}\, L_{min}} \quad \text{(Approx. average torque, ideal SRM)}\\[6pt] \eta_{SRM} &\approx 0.85 \text{–} 0.95 \quad \text{(Typical efficiency range)}\\[6pt] L/D \text{ ratio} &\approx 0.5 \text{–} 0.7 \quad \text{(Shorter than induction motors → good for high speed)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 2.3.1 Formula Cheat-Table (Markdown) | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Instantaneous reluctance torque | N·m | | 2 | $W_f = \int_0^I \lambda(i,\theta)\, di$ | Field co-energy | J (or Wb·A) | | 3 | $T_e = \frac{\partial W_f}{\partial \theta}\big\vert_i$ | Co-energy torque | N·m | | 4 | $L(\theta) = L_{min} + \Delta L \cos^2(\frac{N_r\theta}{2})$ | Ideal inductance profile | H | | 5 | $\theta_{step} = \frac{360^\circ}{q \, N_r}$ | Mechanical step angle | deg | | 6 | $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi f}{N_r}$ | Synchronous mechanical speed | rad/s | | 7 | $N_s = q \, N_r \pm k$ | Stator–rotor pole combination rule | integer | | 8 | $\Delta L = L_{max} - L_{min}$ | Inductance saliency ratio | H | > [!WARNING] > **Symbol trap:** $\theta$ in SRM formulas is **mechanical rotor position**, NOT electrical. The electrical angle is $\theta_e = \frac{N_r \theta}{2}$ (for a 2-pole-pair rotor pairing). Examiners often test whether students confuse the two. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fault tolerance:** A failed phase in a 4- or 5-phase SRM does not prevent starting/operation — a huge reliability advantage over induction motors in aerospace. - **Bidirectional operation:** No reversal of armature polarity needed; simply advance/retard the firing sequence → cost-saving on contactors. - **Constant power region:** Achieved naturally by advancing $\theta_{on}$ (advancing the turn-on angle) at higher speeds — the same trick used in field-weakening of PM machines. - **Battery EV traction:** SRMs can deliver **constant power over a 3:1 speed range** without a gearbox, simplifying drivetrains. # ⚡ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Instantaneous Reluctance Torque from Co-energy **Starting point:** The magnetic field co-energy stored in a phase winding is a function of both current $i$ and rotor position $\theta$: $$ W_f = W_f(i, \theta) $$ **Step 1 — Co-energy expression for a linear magnetic system:** For a linear magnetic circuit with constant permeability, flux linkage $\lambda$ is proportional to current: $$ \lambda(i,\theta) = L(\theta)\, i $$ **Step 2 — Substituting into the co-energy definition:** $$ W_f = \int_{0}^{i} \lambda(i',\theta)\, di' = \int_{0}^{i} L(\theta)\, i' \, di' $$ **Step 3 — Performing the integration** (since $L(\theta)$ is constant w.r.t. $i'$): $$ W_f = L(\theta) \left[\frac{(i')^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{i} = \frac{1}{2}\, L(\theta)\, i^{2} $$ **Step 4 — Applying the co-energy torque principle:** At constant current, electromagnetic torque is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to mechanical rotor position: $$ T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W_f(i,\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i=\text{const}} $$ **Step 5 — Differentiating step 3 with respect to $\theta$** (only $L$ depends on $\theta$): $$ T_e = \frac{1}{2}\, i^{2}\, \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2}\, i^{2}\, \frac{dL}{d\theta} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** This is the **fundamental SRM torque equation**. Because $i^2$ is always positive, the sign of the torque depends entirely on the sign of $\frac{dL}{d\theta}$. This is why SRMs can produce **unidirectional torque from AC current** — a unique property not shared by induction or PMSM. ## 3.2 Derivation — Ideal Inductance Profile Assume the air-gap permeance is sinusoidally distributed around the air-gap circumference. The permeance $P(\theta)$ (reciprocal of reluctance) is: $$ P(\theta) = P_0 + P_1 \cos(N_r \theta) $$ **Step 1 — Inductance is permeance times turns squared** ($L = N^2 P$): $$ L(\theta) = N^2 \bigl[P_0 + P_1 \cos(N_r \theta)\bigr] $$ **Step 2 — Identify the minimum and maximum**: - At $\theta = \frac{\pi}{N_r}$ (aligned): $\cos = -1$, so $L_{min} = N^2(P_0 - P_1)$. - At $\theta = 0$ (unaligned): $\cos = +1$, so $L_{max} = N^2(P_0 + P_1)$. **Step 3 — Substitute $P_0 = \frac{L_{max}+L_{min}}{2N^2}$ and $P_1 = \frac{L_{max}-L_{min}}{2N^2}$**: $$ L(\theta) = \frac{L_{max} + L_{min}}{2} + \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{2}\cos(N_r\theta) $$ **Step 4 — Apply the half-angle identity** $\cos(N_r\theta) = 2\cos^2(\frac{N_r\theta}{2}) - 1$: $$ L(\theta) = L_{min} + (L_{max} - L_{min})\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{N_r \theta}{2}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **standard textbook inductance profile** used in all KTU derivations. The cos² form guarantees $L(\theta) \geq L_{min}$ always — physically meaningful because inductance cannot be negative. ## 3.3 Derivation — Step Angle & Rotor Pole Selection Rule **Step 1 — Mechanical angle between two consecutive rotor poles:** $$ \theta_r = \frac{2\pi}{N_r} \quad \text{(mechanical rad)} $$ **Step 2 — A full electrical cycle (rise → fall of $L$) corresponds to ONE rotor pole pitch:** Hence the electrical angle = mechanical angle × $\frac{N_r}{2}$ for a 2-pole-pair rotor. **Step 3 — Each phase fires ONCE per electrical cycle.** With $q$ phases firing sequentially, each phase advances by $\frac{360^\circ}{q}$ (electrical) per rotor step. **Step 4 — Mechanical step per phase advance**: $$ \theta_{step} = \frac{360^\circ}{q \, N_r} $$ **Step 5 — Selection rule for valid $N_s$/$N_r$ combinations:** For continuous torque (no dead zones) and forward rotation, both stator pole arc and rotor pole arc must satisfy: $$ \min(\beta_s, \beta_r) > \frac{\theta_{step}}{2} \quad \text{AND} \quad \beta_s + \beta_r \leq \theta_{step} $$ The pole combination rule is: $$ N_s = q \, N_r \pm k \quad \text{where } k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots $$ **Worked example:** For a 3-phase SRM with $N_r = 4$, the rule gives $N_s = 12 \pm k$. Common choice: $N_s = 12$ (k = 0) or $N_s = 8$ (k = 4, but 8 ≠ 3·4±k for k = 4 only if structure permits). For $N_s = 12$, $N_r = 4$: $$ \theta_{step} = \frac{360^\circ}{3 \times 4} = 30^\circ $$ ## 3.4 Derivation — Average Torque (Idealised Square-Wave Current) **Assumption:** Current is a flat-topped pulse of magnitude $I$ during the rising-inductance interval (motoring). **Step 1 — Instantaneous torque during excitation**: $$ T_e = \frac{1}{2} I^{2} \frac{dL}{d\theta} $$ **Step 2 — Integrate over one rising-inductance interval** of width $\Delta\theta = \theta_{step}$: $$ T_{avg} = \frac{1}{\Delta\theta} \int_{\theta_{on}}^{\theta_{on}+\Delta\theta} \frac{1}{2} I^{2} \frac{dL}{d\theta}\, d\theta $$ **Step 3 — The integral of $\frac{dL}{d\theta}$ over the rising interval is $(L_{max} - L_{min})$:** $$ T_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} I^{2} \frac{(L_{max} - L_{min})}{\Delta\theta} = \frac{I^{2} (L_{max} - L_{min})}{2 \, \theta_{step}} $$ **Step 4 — Substituting $\theta_{step} = \frac{2\pi}{q N_r}$**: $$ T_{avg} = \frac{q \, N_r \, I^{2}}{4\pi}\,(L_{max} - L_{min}) $$ **Step 5 — Include flux linkage form** $\psi = L \, i$, hence $\psi_{max} = L_{max} I$, and substituting: $$ T_{avg} = \frac{q \, N_r \, \psi_{max}^{2}}{4\pi}\cdot\frac{(L_{max} - L_{min})}{L_{max}\, L_{min}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering takeaway:** To **maximise torque density**, designers aim for: > 1. **High saliency ratio** $L_{max}/L_{min}$ (typically 6–10 for SRMs). > 2. **Multiple phases** ($q$ = 3, 4, or 5) — reduces torque ripple. > 3. **High pole counts** $N_r$ — increases $\theta_{step}$ frequency → smoother torque. ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Why SRM Where | Application Driver | SRM Winning Attribute | Limitation Compensated By | |---|---|---| | **Electric Vehicle traction** | Constant power over wide speed, no PM (no rare-earth cost) | Acoustic noise → sophisticated control | | **Aerospace actuator** | Fault tolerance, works at 50,000 rpm, hot environment | Position sensor → sensorless control research | | **Domestic appliance** | Low cost, BLDC-like efficiency at lower cost, robust | Higher torque ripple → pole shaping | | **Mining / oil drilling** | Operates at extreme temperatures, intrinsically safe | Limited manufacturer base | | **Servo / robotics** | High torque/inertia, fast acceleration | Needs closed-loop position control | # ⚡ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SRM Cross-Sectional Topology (Functional Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[DC Bus Supply
V_dc] --> B[Asymmetric
Half-Bridge Converter
6 Switches for 3-phase] B --> C[Stator Phase Winding A] B --> D[Stator Phase Winding B] B --> E[Stator Phase Winding C] C -.magnetic coupling.-> F[Salient Rotor
Plain Laminated Steel
No Windings, No PM] D -.magnetic coupling.-> F E -.magnetic coupling.-> F F --> G[Mechanical Output
Shaft + Load] H[Rotor Position
Sensor
Optical / Hall] -->|theta feedback| I[Controller
DSP / Microcontroller] I -->|gate pulses S1-S6| B I -->|speed reference| J[Operator Input
Pedal / Potentiometer] ``` ## 4.2 SRM Cross-Section Schematic (8/6 Configuration) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STATOR[STATOR - 8 Salient Poles with Concentrated Coils] S1[Pole 1] S2[Pole 2] S3[Pole 3] S4[Pole 4] S5[Pole 5] S6[Pole 6] S7[Pole 7] S8[Pole 8] end subgraph ROTOR[ROTOR - 6 Salient Poles, No Windings] R1[Rotor Pole 1] R2[Rotor Pole 2] R3[Rotor Pole 3] R4[Rotor Pole 4] R5[Rotor Pole 5] R6[Rotor Pole 6] end S1 -.phase A coil.-> S5 S2 -.phase B coil.-> S6 S3 -.phase C coil.-> S7 S4 -.phase D coil.-> S8 R1 -.air gap.-> S1 R3 -.air gap.-> S3 R5 -.air gap.-> S5 ``` ## 4.3 Inductance vs Rotor Position Profile ```mermaid flowchart LR A[theta = 0
UNALIGNED
L = L_min] -->|Increasing overlap| B[theta = theta_step/2
Rotor Pole Edge meets Stator Pole Edge
dL/dtheta MAXIMUM] B -->|Full overlap| C[theta = theta_step
ALIGNED
L = L_max
dL/dtheta = 0] C -->|Rotor leaves alignment| D[theta = 1.5 theta_step
dL/dtheta MAX NEGATIVE] D -->|Complete separation| E[theta = 2 theta_step
UNALIGNED AGAIN
L = L_min] ``` ## 4.4 Commutation & Torque Production Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Rotor at theta_0] --> B{Read Rotor Position theta} B -->|theta in rising L region| C[Turn ON Phase X
theta_on fired] C --> D[Current builds up i t
Torque T = 0.5 i^2 dL/dtheta] D -->|theta reaches theta_off| E[Turn OFF Phase X] E --> F[Current freewheels
through diode and decays] F -->|Next phase aligned| G[Turn ON Phase Y] G --> H[Continuous rotation] H --> B ``` ## 4.5 Typical SRM Drive System Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Reference
Speed omega_ref] --> B[Speed
Controller PI] B --> C[Current / Torque
Reference] C --> D[Hysteresis
or PWM
Current Controller] D --> E[Converter
Gate Pulses] E --> F[SRM
Power Stage] F --> G[Mechanical
Load] F --> H[Rotor
Position
Sensor] H --> I[Position
Decoder] I -->|theta, omega| B I -->|theta| D ``` # ⚡ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Why is a Switched Reluctance Motor called a "doubly salient, singly excited" machine? Mention any two advantages of this construction. **Model Answer:** A Switched Reluctance Motor is called **doubly salient** because both the stator and rotor have projecting poles (saliencies). It is called **singly excited** because only the stator carries windings — the rotor has neither windings nor permanent magnets. Two key advantages: 1. The rotor is just laminated steel — extremely robust, no rotor copper losses, no PM de-magnetisation risk, suitable for very high speeds (≥ 50,000 rpm). 2. Fault tolerance — even if one stator phase fails, the remaining phases can still produce torque, making SRMs ideal for safety-critical applications (aerospace, EVs). ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State the torque equation of a Switched Reluctance Motor and explain why the torque is always in the direction of increasing inductance. **Model Answer:** The instantaneous reluctance torque is: $$ T_e = \frac{1}{2}\, i^{2}\, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} $$ Because $i^2 \geq 0$ always, the **sign of the torque equals the sign of $\frac{dL}{d\theta}$**. When $\frac{dL}{d\theta} > 0$ (rotor moving towards alignment with the excited stator pole), the system produces positive torque that further increases $L$ — this is the **minimum reluctance principle** (magnetic circuits always try to minimise stored energy by reducing reluctance, hence maximising inductance). So torque always acts to **increase** inductance, driving the rotor toward the aligned position. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a Switched Reluctance Motor. Derive the expression for instantaneous torque in terms of phase current and inductance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A three-phase, 6/4 pole SRM has $L_{min} = 10$ mH and $L_{max} = 110$ mH. The stator phase winding carries a constant current of $I = 8$ A during the rising-inductance region spanning a mechanical angle of $45^\circ$. Compute: **(i)** the average reluctance torque, **(ii)** the step angle, and **(iii)** the synchronous speed at a supply frequency of 50 Hz. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Construction (3 marks):** - Stator: salient poles (typically 6 or 8) with **concentrated phase windings** wound around diametrically opposite stator poles to form one phase. - Rotor: simple salient pole laminated steel structure (typically 4 or 6 poles), **no windings, no permanent magnets**. - Air-gap: short and uniform on the pole faces, large on the inter-polar sides — gives the desired $L_{max}/L_{min}$ saliency ratio. - Rotor position sensor (Hall / optical encoder) mounted on the shaft. **Working principle (2 marks):** - A stator phase is energised when the rotor pole is *approaching* the excited stator pole (in the rising $L$ region). The rotor experiences a tangential force that pulls it to the aligned position. - As the rotor aligns, the phase is switched off, and the next phase is excited — producing continuous rotation in either direction by simply advancing/retarding the switching sequence. **Derivation of torque (2 marks):** Co-energy stored in a linear magnetic circuit: $$ W_f = \int_0^i \lambda(i',\theta)\, di' = \int_0^i L(\theta)\, i' \, di' = \frac{1}{2}\, L(\theta)\, i^{2} $$ Electromagnetic torque at constant current: $$ T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W_f}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i = \text{const}} = \frac{1}{2}\, i^{2}\, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} \quad \text{N·m} $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - Phases $q = 3$ - $N_s = 6$, $N_r = 4$ - $L_{min} = 10$ mH = $0.010$ H - $L_{max} = 110$ mH = $0.110$ H - $I = 8$ A - Rising-inductance interval $\Delta\theta = 45^\circ = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ rad **(i) Average Torque** (using $T_{avg} = \frac{I^{2}(L_{max}-L_{min})}{2 \, \Delta\theta}$): $$ T_{avg} = \frac{(8)^2 \times (0.110 - 0.010)}{2 \times (\pi/4)} $$ $$ T_{avg} = \frac{64 \times 0.100}{1.5708} = \frac{6.400}{1.5708} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{avg} \approx 4.07 \text{ N·m}} $$ **(ii) Step Angle:** $$ \theta_{step} = \frac{360^\circ}{q \, N_r} = \frac{360^\circ}{3 \times 4} = \boxed{30^\circ} $$ **(iii) Synchronous Speed:** $$ \omega_s = \frac{2\pi f}{N_r} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{4} = 25\pi \approx \boxed{78.54 \text{ rad/s}} $$ In rpm: $$ n_s = \frac{60 f}{N_r} = \frac{60 \times 50}{4} = \boxed{750 \text{ rpm}} $$ --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]** **(a)** Discuss the various applications of Switched Reluctance Motors. Explain why SRMs are preferred in electric vehicle traction and aerospace actuators. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of a closed-loop SRM drive system. List any four advantages and two disadvantages of SRMs compared to induction motors. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Applications of SRMs (3 marks):** 1. **Electric & Hybrid Vehicle traction** — Tesla Roadster (initial prototype), Renault EVs. 2. **Aerospace actuators and fuel pumps** — Sundstrand, Aerospatiale systems in Boeing/Airbus. 3. **Domestic appliances** — Dyson digital motor (vacuum cleaners), washing machines, fans. 4. **Industrial drives** — conveyors, textile spindles, pumps, mixers. 5. **Mining and oil-drilling** — intrinsically safe, high-temp operation. 6. **Servo and robotics** — high torque-to-inertia, wide speed range. **Why preferred in EV traction (2 marks):** - No permanent magnet → **no rare-earth dependency** (cost & supply-chain advantage). - Wide **constant-power speed range** (≥ 3:1) achievable by advancing $\theta_{on}$. - **Regenerative braking** is natural (operates as generator in falling-$L$ region). - **High efficiency** at high speeds (> 90 %), suitable for highway driving. - Robust rotor can withstand harsh road vibration. **Why preferred in aerospace (2 marks):** - **Fault tolerance** — a phase failure does not prevent operation. - Operates at extreme temperatures (no PM de-magnetisation issue, no rotor cooling needed). - **High-speed capability** (50,000+ rpm) suits fuel pumps and turbo-machinery. - Long operating life with **minimal maintenance**. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Closed-Loop SRM Drive Block Diagram (3 marks):** [Refer to diagram in SECTION 4.5] - Speed reference $\omega_{ref}$ compared with measured speed $\omega$ (from position sensor). - Error fed to a PI speed controller → produces current/torque reference. - Hysteresis (or PWM) current controller generates gate pulses for the asymmetric half-bridge converter. - Converter excites the stator phase windings based on rotor position $\theta$ from the position sensor/decoder. **Four Advantages over IM (2 marks):** 1. Higher efficiency (especially at high speeds) — no rotor copper loss. 2. Robust rotor — no rotor windings to burn out, cheaper to manufacture. 3. Constant power over a wider speed range without a gearbox. 4. Fault tolerance and inherent bidirectional operation. **Two Disadvantages (2 marks):** 1. **Torque ripple** is high (typically 10–25 %) compared to IM (1–3 %) — needs pole shaping and sophisticated control. 2. **Acoustic noise and vibration** due to radial magnetic forces — requires careful lamination design. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > - **Don't forget the factor ½** in the torque equation. Many students write $T = i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ and lose 1 mark. > - **Mechanical vs electrical angle confusion.** KTU examiners frequently test this in 3-mark Part A questions. > - **Concentrated winding vs overlap winding.** Always mention that SRM stator has **concentrated** (non-overlapping) windings — this is a favourite "memory check" question. > - **In step-angle problems**, students often forget that $q$ (phases) multiplies $N_r$ in the denominator. > - **Don't call SRM a "reluctance motor" without "switched"** — the switching action is the entire essence of the machine. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > 🎯 **High-Yield Bullet Points for Last-Minute Revision** - ⚡ SRM = **D**oubly **S**alient + **S**ingly **E**xcited. Only stator carries windings. Rotor is plain laminated steel. - ⚡ Fundamental torque equation: $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ — sign of torque = sign of slope of $L$-vs-$\theta$ curve. - ⚡ $i^2$ is always **positive**, so torque always drives rotor toward the **aligned (minimum reluctance)** position. - ⚡ Pole-combination rule: $N_s = q N_r \pm k$, with $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ - ⚡ Step angle: $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{q \, N_r}$. - ⚡ Synchronous speed: $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{N_r}$ rad/s, or $n_s = \dfrac{60 f}{N_r}$ rpm. - ⚡ **Motoring** → phase excited in **rising** $L$ region. **Generating** → phase excited in **falling** $L$ region. - ⚡ Inductance profile: $L(\theta) = L_{min} + (L_{max} - L_{min}) \cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{N_r \theta}{2}\right)$. - ⚡ Torque ripple is the **biggest disadvantage** of SRMs (10–25 %). - ⚡ Acoustic noise is the second biggest disadvantage — caused by radial force variation. - ⚡ Key applications: **EV traction, aerospace, domestic appliances, mining, servo drives**. - ⚡ **No permanent magnet** → no rare-earth cost, no demagnetisation risk, robust at high temperature. - ⚡ **Fault tolerant** — phase failure does not stop the motor (key for aerospace). - ⚡ **Bidirectional rotation** by simply advancing/retarding the switching sequence (no contactor needed). - ⚡ Saliency ratio $\dfrac{L_{max}}{L_{min}}$ typically **6 to 10** for well-designed SRMs. - ⚡ Converter of choice: **Asymmetric half-bridge** — one per phase, allows independent current control and regenerative braking. - ⚡ Rotor **position sensor is mandatory** — most SRMs use an optical encoder or Hall sensors. - ⚡ **Constant power region** is achieved by **advancing turn-on angle** $\theta_{on}$ at higher speeds. - ⚡ Typical efficiency: **85 % – 95 %**, comparable to PMSM, often better than induction motor at high speeds. - ⚡ Compared to PMSM: SRM is **cheaper but noisier and less efficient** at low-to-mid speeds. - ⚡ Compared to BLDC: SRM is **more robust but has higher torque ripple**. - ⚡ Compare to induction motor: SRM is **more efficient at high speed but noisier**. - ⚡ KTU favourite 3-mark question: *"Why is SRM called doubly salient and singly excited?"* — memorise the answer verbatim. - ⚡ KTU favourite 14-mark question: *"Derive the torque equation of an SRM and explain the role of rotor position sensor."* - ⚡ Always draw the **$L$-vs-$\theta$** trapezoidal profile in long answers — examiners award 1 mark for the diagram alone. - ⚡ In numerical problems, **watch the units**: $L$ in **Henries**, $\theta$ in **radians**, current in **Amperes** for SI-consistent torque in N·m. --- > 📘 **End of Module 2 — Applications; Switched Reluctance Motors** > *Stay tuned for Module 3 topics as per KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus.*

topics.title: Constructional Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constructional Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Topic Context within KTU 2024 Scheme

The topic **"Constructional Details"** in **Module 2** of *PEEET743 – Special Electrical Machines* deals with the physical, electromagnetic, and mechanical architecture of **Stepper Motors** — a class of electromechanical energy conversion devices that convert discrete electrical pulses into precise, incremental angular (or linear) mechanical displacements. This is the foundational topic upon which the *operating principles, torque production, and drive electronics* of the subsequent modules are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor:** As per the 2024 Scheme syllabus, Module 2 of Special Electrical Machines exclusively focuses on **Stepper Motors**. "Constructional Details" specifically covers the *Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM)*, the *Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM)*, and the *Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)*. Examiner questions on this topic typically demand labeled diagrams, pole/teeth counting, and material identification.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Stepper Motor

A **Stepper Motor** is a *brushless, synchronous electric machine* that divides a full mechanical rotation ($360^{\circ}$ mechanical) into a discrete number of equal angular steps. Each electrical pulse applied to its stator winding causes the rotor to advance by one precise increment, known as the **step angle** ($\theta_s$), without requiring any feedback sensor for position control. The motion is therefore *inherently open-loop and digitally commanded*.

Mathematically, the step angle is expressed as:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$$

where $N_r$ is the number of rotor teeth (or salient poles) and $m$ is the number of stator phase-excitation sequences per electrical cycle. The number of steps per revolution is:

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s} = N_r \cdot m$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Digital Clock Hand**
> Imagine the second hand of a **digital wall clock**. It does *not* sweep smoothly like an analog watch; instead, it jerks forward in tiny, identical, audible "ticks" — *one tick, one step*. A stepper motor behaves identically: each incoming electrical pulse (the "tick") advances the rotor shaft by a fixed, factory-defined angular increment. The faster the pulses arrive, the faster the shaft rotates — but the size of each "tick" never changes. This **open-loop digital-to-mechanical translation** is why stepper motors dominate printers, CNC machines, robotics, and telescope mounts.

## 1.4 Why Constructional Details Matter in KTU Examinations

The construction directly dictates three performance-defining parameters:

1. **Step Resolution** — determined by the *number of rotor teeth* and the *stator phase configuration*.
2. **Torque Capability** — determined by the *magnetic material* (ferrite vs. rare-earth NdFeB vs. Samarium Cobalt) and *air-gap geometry*.
3. **Detent/Holding Torque** — determined by the *salient pole structure* and *residual magnetism* in the rotor.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Reference Metrics (memorize these):**
> - Common step angles: **$1.8^{\circ}$** (200 steps/rev — Hybrid), **$7.5^{\circ}$** (48 steps/rev — VRSM), **$15^{\circ}$** (24 steps/rev — PMSM).
> - Standard operating DC bus voltage: **$5\text{ V}$ to $48\text{ V}$**; phase current: **$0.5\text{ A}$ to $5\text{ A}$**.
> - Air gap length in stepper motors: typically **$0.05\text{ mm}$ to $0.2\text{ mm}$** (much smaller than conventional machines for sharp torque pulses).

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stepper Motors — A Structural Hierarchy

Before diving into the construction of each type, the student must internalize the three principal structural families. KTU examiners frequently ask for **classification with one-line constructional distinctions**.

| Family | Rotor Construction | Stator Construction | Key Identifying Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM)** | Soft-iron salient-pole rotor (no magnets, no windings) | Multi-phase concentrated windings on salient stator poles | Rotor aligns to *minimum reluctance* path |
| **Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM)** | Permanently magnetized radial-pole rotor (2 or 4 poles typical) | Two-phase or four-phase concentrated stator windings | Rotor has *fixed magnetic polarity*; detent torque at zero excitation |
| **Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)** | Two axially-stacked soft-iron caps with *multiple fine teeth*, separated by a permanent magnet | Two-phase, multi-toothed stator with concentrated windings | Combines *high step resolution* of VRSM with *holding torque* of PMSM |

## 2.2 Constructional Architecture of the Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM)

The VRSM is the structurally simplest member of the family. Its name is derived from the operating principle — *reluctance varies with rotor position* because the rotor is a passive salient-iron piece.

### 2.2.1 Stator Construction of VRSM

- The **stator** is a **laminated silicon-steel stack** (thickness per lamination ≈ $0.35\text{ mm}$ to $0.5\text{ mm}$) designed to confine eddy-current losses during high-frequency pulsed excitation.
- It carries **$N_s$ salient poles** (commonly $N_s = 8$, giving 4 phase pairs; or $N_s = 6$ giving 3 phase pairs).
- Each stator pole has a **concentrated coil** wound around it. Diametrically opposite poles form **one phase winding** and are connected in series such that their MMFs add along the same axis.
- The stator pole faces may be either *uniform* (for low-resolution applications) or *toothed/fine-pitched* (for high-resolution versions).
- A standard three-stack, *multi-toothed* VRSM stator can yield step angles as small as **$1.8^{\circ}$**.

### 2.2.2 Rotor Construction of VRSM

- The **rotor** is a **plain cylindrical (or salient) piece of high-permeability soft iron**, *lacking both permanent magnets and conducting windings*.
- It has $N_r$ salient teeth/poles. For a 4-phase, 8-pole stator VRSM, the rotor typically has $N_r = 6$ teeth.
- The rotor is mounted on a non-magnetic stainless-steel shaft to prevent axial flux leakage.
- The **air gap** between stator and rotor teeth is the *most critical dimensional tolerance*; a typical gap is **$0.1\text{ mm}$ to $0.2\text{ mm}$**.

### 2.2.3 Operating Step in VRSM

When Phase-A is energized, the rotor rotates to align its salient teeth with the energized stator poles (path of *minimum magnetic reluctance*). When Phase-A is de-energized and Phase-B is energized, the rotor advances to the next stable aligned position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Fact:**
> A VRSM has **no detent torque** when unenergized (rotor is free to rotate by hand). This is the *single most distinguishing feature* examiners use in objective questions.

## 2.3 Constructional Architecture of the Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM)

### 2.3.1 Stator Construction of PMSM

- The **stator** is again a **laminated silicon-steel core**, but its poles are *smooth* (not finely toothed like a high-resolution VRSM).
- It carries **two phase windings** (Phase A and Phase B) typically arranged as **two pairs of poles** ($N_s = 4$ or $N_s = 8$).
- The windings are **bifilar-wound** in many commercial designs to allow unipolar drive, or they are wound in a single layer for bipolar drive.
- A typical $15^{\circ}$ PMSM has a **4-pole stator**.

### 2.3.2 Rotor Construction of PMSM

- The **rotor** is a **cylindrical permanent magnet**, axially magnetized (in older designs) or *radially magnetized* (in modern designs) with alternating N–S poles around its circumference.
- For a $15^{\circ}$ step-angle PMSM: $N_r = 24$ (since $\theta_s = 360^{\circ}/(2 \cdot 24) = 7.5^{\circ}$ full-step with $7.5^{\circ}$ half-step modes possible) — *correction*: a $15^{\circ}$ step PMSM with 4 stator phases and 24 rotor poles yields $\theta_s = 360^{\circ}/(2 \cdot 24) = 7.5^{\circ}$. The most common commercial PMSM has $N_r = 2$ or $N_r = 4$ poles giving $\theta_s = 90^{\circ}$ or $45^{\circ}$, which are reduced to $15^{\circ}$ via a *multistage axial stack*.
- The magnet material is typically **barium ferrite** (low cost) or **rare-earth samarium-cobalt / NdFeB** (high torque density).

> [!NOTE]
> **Detent Torque Signature of PMSM:**
> A PMSM has a *substantial detent (cogging) torque* even with no phase excitation — this is the resisting torque you feel when trying to rotate the shaft by hand. This detent torque is a direct consequence of the permanent-magnet rotor's interaction with the slotted stator.

## 2.4 Constructional Architecture of the Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)

The Hybrid Stepper Motor is the **industry workhorse** and the **most commonly tested in KTU** because of its sophisticated construction.

### 2.4.1 The Two-Cap Rotor (Heart of the HSM)

The rotor consists of **two soft-iron end-caps** (each with $N_r$ fine teeth), mounted on a non-magnetic shaft and **axially separated by a permanent magnet** (a cylindrical disc, axially magnetized).

- The **N-pole end-cap** has its teeth displaced by *half a tooth-pitch* relative to the **S-pole end-cap**.
- This axial magnetisation forces the **N-cap teeth to become North poles** and the **S-cap teeth to become South poles** simultaneously, creating a unidirectional axial flux that crosses the air gap at the active end.

### 2.4.2 The Multi-Toothed Stator

- The stator is a **laminated silicon-steel stack** with **two phase windings** (A and B).
- The stator has $2m$ salient poles, each carrying a *concentrated multi-tooth structure* (typically 5 teeth per pole for a $1.8^{\circ}$ motor).
- The stator pole teeth are also *offset by half a tooth pitch* between Phase A and Phase B poles.
- A standard $1.8^{\circ}$ HSM: $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth, $N_s = 8$ stator poles, $m = 2$ phases.

### 2.4.3 Material Stack-Up

| Component | Material | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Stator/Rotor laminations | **M-19 or M-27 grain-oriented silicon steel**, $0.35\text{ mm}$ | Reduces eddy-current loss at high pulse rates |
| Rotor permanent magnet | **NdFeB (Neodymium-Iron-Boron)** grade N35–N52 | Highest residual flux density ($\geq 1.2\text{ T}$) |
| Shaft | **Non-magnetic stainless steel (SS-304)** | Prevents axial flux short-circuit through shaft |
| Bearing | **Sealed deep-groove ball bearing** | Maintains tight air-gap tolerance |
| Encapsulation | **Polymer bobbin over coils** | Provides insulation and mechanical rigidity |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 100% of numerical problems asked in KTU 2024 on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$ | $\theta_s$: step angle, $N_r$: rotor teeth, $m$: phase sequences per cycle | Determines mechanical step resolution |
| 2 | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s}$ | Total steps per revolution | Used to compute pulse train length |
| 3 | $\theta_{elec} = N_r \cdot \theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{m}$ | Electrical angle per step (always $= 90^{\circ}$ for $m=4$) | Relates mechanical to electrical angle |
| 4 | $N_{ph} \cdot N_s = 2m$ for bipolar drive | $N_{ph}$: number of phases, $N_s$: stator poles | Stator design check |
| 5 | $f_{step} = \dfrac{\omega \cdot N_{steps}}{60}$ | $f_{step}$: pulse rate (Hz), $\omega$: shaft speed (rpm) | Translates RPM to drive frequency |
| 6 | $\omega_{max} = \dfrac{f_{max}}{N_{steps}/60}$ | Maximum safe speed (no resonance) | Drive design constraint |
| 7 | $T \propto \dfrac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \cdot I^2$ | Static torque expression for VRSM | Static torque prediction |
| 8 | $\theta_s(\text{HSM}) = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{2 \cdot N_r}$ | Hybrid stepper with 2-phase excitation | Direct HSM calculation |
| 9 | $\theta_s(\text{VRSM}) = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_s \cdot N_r} \cdot 2$ | VRSM, 2-phase-on sequence | Direct VRSM calculation |
| 10 | $T_{detent} \approx 5\%-10\%$ of $T_{holding}$ | Rule of thumb (PMSM/HSM) | Holding-torque estimation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** A VRSM rotor has *no magnet*. Many students incorrectly draw a magnet inside the VRSM rotor in diagrams — this costs **2 marks** instantly in KTU valuation.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stepper motors with the construction described above are deployed wherever **precision open-loop positioning** is required:

- **3D printers** ($1.8^{\circ}$ HSM, microstepped to $0.45^{\circ}$).
- **CNC milling machines** (NEMA-23 / NEMA-34 HSM with optical encoders as a secondary check).
- **Telescopes and observatory mounts** (VRSM for low-cost tracking).
- **Automotive instrument clusters** (PMSM for gauge needles).
- **Robotic joints and prosthetic limbs** (miniature HSM with planetary gearheads).
- **Hard-disk drive head actuators** (VRSM in legacy designs; voice-coil in modern).
- **Watches and clocks** (miniature PMSM, $7.5^{\circ}/15^{\circ}$ step).

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Angle of a Hybrid Stepper Motor

**Problem Statement:** A hybrid stepper motor has $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth and is driven by a *two-phase* winding with full-step excitation ($m = 2$ switching states per electrical cycle per phase-pair, $m_{eff} = 4$ for a 2-phase system). Determine (a) the step angle, (b) the steps per revolution, (c) the pulse frequency required to achieve $150\text{ rpm}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the General Step-Angle Formula.**

For any stepper motor, the step angle is given by the previously established relation:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Variables.**

The rotor has $N_r = 50$ fine teeth. The number of stator phase excitation sequences per mechanical revolution is $m$. For a *2-phase* motor in **full-step, single-phase-on** mode, $m = 2$ (we move through 2 states: A-ON, B-ON → repeat). For **full-step, two-phase-on** mode, $m = 4$ (states: AB, $\bar{A}B$, $A\bar{B}$, $\bar{A}\bar{B}$). We assume the standard 2-phase full-step mode with $m = 4$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and Compute $\theta_s$.**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{50 \times 4} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{200} = 1.8^{\circ}$$

> This matches the industry-standard $1.8^{\circ}$ HSM.

**Step 4 — Compute Steps per Revolution.**

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{1.8^{\circ}} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Required Pulse Frequency for $150\text{ rpm}$.**

Shaft speed in revolutions per second:

$$f_{rev} = \frac{150}{60} = 2.5 \text{ rev/s}$$

Each revolution requires 200 pulses, so:

$$f_{step} = N_{steps} \cdot f_{rev} = 200 \times 2.5 = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta_s = 1.8^{\circ}$, $N_{steps} = 200$, $f_{step} = 500\text{ Hz}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relating Electrical Angle to Mechanical Angle

**Statement:** Show that in a stepper motor, the *electrical angle per step* is independent of the number of rotor teeth.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Definition of Electrical Angle.**

The electrical angle $\theta_e$ is defined as the angle through which the stator MMF vector rotates in *electrical space* per excitation step. It is related to the mechanical angle $\theta_m$ by the *pole-pair factor* $p$ (number of pole pairs):

$$\theta_e = p \cdot \theta_m$$

**Step 2 — Apply to a Stepper Motor.**

In a stepper motor, the effective number of pole pairs is *synthesized* by the rotor tooth count $N_r$ — i.e., $p = N_r/2$ (or $N_r$ for salient-pole machines). Therefore, the electrical angle per step is:

$$\theta_{e,\text{step}} = p \cdot \theta_s = \frac{N_r}{2} \cdot \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{2m}$$

**Step 3 — Observation.**

The rotor teeth $N_r$ cancel out:

$$\theta_{e,\text{step}} = \frac{180^{\circ}}{m}$$

For $m = 4$ (two-phase full-step): $\theta_{e,\text{step}} = 180^{\circ}/4 = 45^{\circ}$. Q.E.D.

> **Insight:** This is why *microstepping* drives can subdivide a single mechanical step into arbitrarily small electrical sub-steps — they are not limited by $N_r$ once the electrical phase-angle is finely resolved.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Air-Gap Energy and Static Torque in a VRSM

**Statement:** Derive the static (detent-free) holding torque of a VRSM from energy principles.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express Co-energy in the Air Gap.**

The co-energy stored in the air gap of a salient-pole magnetic structure with phase current $I$ and rotor position $\theta$ is:

$$W'_{f}(\theta, I) = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) I^2$$

where $L(\theta)$ is the position-dependent phase inductance.

**Step 2 — Define the Torque as Partial Derivative of Co-energy.**

By the principle of virtual work, the electromagnetic torque is:

$$T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'_f}{\partial \theta} \right|_{I = \text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Model $L(\theta)$ as a Trigonometric Series.**

For a salient-pole VRSM, the inductance varies sinusoidally with rotor position:

$$L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$$

where $L_0$ is the *average* inductance and $L_1$ is the *saliency-modulation amplitude*. The derivative is:

$$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = -N_r L_1 \sin(N_r \theta)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify.**

$$T_e = -\frac{1}{2} N_r L_1 I^2 \sin(N_r \theta)$$

The peak (holding) torque occurs at $\sin(N_r \theta) = \pm 1$:

$$T_{peak} = \frac{1}{2} N_r L_1 I^2$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation.**

The torque is **independent of rotor magnet material** (no magnet in VRSM), scales as the **square of current**, and is **proportional to rotor tooth count** $N_r$. This explains why high-resolution VRSMs have *many teeth* and *high current*.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Constructional Table

| Parameter | VRSM | PMSM | HSM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotor material | Soft iron (no magnet) | Hard ferrite / NdFeB magnet | Soft iron + NdFeB disc |
| Rotor teeth/poles | 6–100 salient teeth | 2–24 radial poles | 50–200 fine teeth per cap |
| Stator phases | 3 or 4 | 2 | 2 (with offset teeth) |
| Step angle (typical) | $7.5^{\circ}$–$15^{\circ}$ | $45^{\circ}$–$90^{\circ}$ (gear-reduced) | $0.9^{\circ}$–$1.8^{\circ}$ |
| Detent torque at unenergized | **Zero** | Significant | Moderate (magnet + tooth) |
| Holding torque per frame size | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Inertia | Low | Higher | Medium |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Hybrid Stepper Motor

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional block-level architecture** of a complete HSM system, illustrating how constructional components interact with the drive electronics (which is the most common diagram type asked in KTU 2024 for this topic).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ctrlA[Microcontroller Pulse Generator] --> drvA[Half H-Bridge Driver Phase A]
    ctrlB[Microcontroller Pulse Generator] --> drvB[Half H-Bridge Driver Phase B]
    drvA --> coilA[Stator Phase A Concentrated Winding]
    drvB --> coilB[Stator Phase B Concentrated Winding]
    coilA --> statorCore[Stator Laminated Silicon Steel Stack]
    coilB --> statorCore
    statorCore --> airgap[Air Gap 0.05mm to 0.2mm]
    airgap --> ncap[Rotor N Cap Soft Iron 50 Teeth]
    airgap --> scap[Rotor S Cap Soft Iron 50 Teeth Offset by Half Pitch]
    ncap --> pmagnet[Axial Permanent Magnet NdFeB Disc]
    pmagnet --> scap
    scap --> shaft[Non Magnetic Stainless Steel Shaft SS 304]
    ncap --> shaft
    shaft --> bearing[Sealed Deep Groove Ball Bearings]
    bearing --> housing[Aluminium Die Cast End Bells]
    housing --> outputMech[Mechanical Output 1.8 Degree Step]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Constructional Comparison of VRSM, PMSM, HSM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vRSM[Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor]
    vRSM --> vStator[Stator 6 to 8 Salient Poles with Concentrated Coils]
    vRSM --> vRotor[Rotor Plain Salient Soft Iron No Magnet]

    pMSM[Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor]
    pMSM --> pStator[Stator 4 Poles 2 Phase Concentrated Coils]
    pMSM --> pRotor[Rotor Radial N S Poles Hard Ferrite or NdFeB]

    hSM[Hybrid Stepper Motor]
    hSM --> hStator[Stator 8 Poles 2 Phase with Fine Teeth 5 Teeth per Pole]
    hSM --> hRotorCap1[Upper Soft Iron Cap 50 Teeth]
    hSM --> hRotorMag[Axial Permanent Magnet Disc NdFeB]
    hSM --> hRotorCap2[Lower Soft Iron Cap 50 Teeth Offset Half Pitch]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Stator Winding Connection Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    batt[DC Bus 5V to 48V] --> bulkCap[Bulk Capacitor 1000uF 50V]
    batt --> reg[Logic Level Regulator 5V]
    reg --> logic[Index Logic Circuit]
    logic --> phaseASw[Phase A Switch Leg]
    logic --> phaseBSw[Phase B Switch Leg]
    phaseASw --> coilA1[Stator Pole 1N Coil]
    phaseASw --> coilA2[Stator Pole 1S Coil Diametrically Opposite]
    phaseBSw --> coilB1[Stator Pole 2N Coil]
    phaseBSw --> coilB2[Stator Pole 2S Coil Diametrically Opposite]
    coilA1 --> rotor1[Rotor]
    coilA2 --> rotor1
    coilB1 --> rotor1
    coilB2 --> rotor1
    rotor1 --> shaft1[Output Shaft]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Constructional Cross-Section Schematic (Logical)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    outer1[Outer Stator Yoke Laminated Steel] --> pole1[Stator Pole 1 with Concentrated Coil]
    outer1 --> pole2[Stator Pole 2 with Concentrated Coil]
    outer1 --> pole3[Stator Pole 3 with Concentrated Coil]
    outer1 --> pole4[Stator Pole 4 with Concentrated Coil]
    pole1 --> gap1[Air Gap]
    pole2 --> gap1
    pole3 --> gap1
    pole4 --> gap1
    gap1 --> rotorA[Rotor N Pole Tooth]
    gap1 --> rotorB[Rotor S Pole Tooth]
    gap1 --> rotorC[Next Rotor Tooth]
    gap1 --> rotorD[Next Rotor Tooth]
    rotorA --> shaft2[Non Magnetic Shaft]
    rotorB --> shaft2
    rotorC --> shaft2
    rotorD --> shaft2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note on Diagrams:** When drawing constructional diagrams in the exam, *always* show (1) the stator pole-faces with their teeth/pole-shoes, (2) the rotor teeth/poles, (3) the phase windings on at least 2 stator poles (with arrows indicating current direction), (4) the air gap as a *distinct double-arrow* dimension, and (5) the shaft. A diagram missing the air-gap dimension is marked down by 1 mark.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Short-Answer (Conceptual)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the constructional features of a Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM). Why is the rotor made of soft iron and not a permanent magnet?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A VRSM consists of a **stator** and a **rotor** with no permanent magnet and no rotor winding. The **stator** is a **laminated silicon-steel core** with **$N_s$ salient poles** (typically 6 or 8). Each stator pole carries a **concentrated phase winding**, and diametrically opposite poles are connected in series to form one phase. The **rotor** is a **cylindrical piece of high-permeability soft iron** with **$N_r$ salient teeth** (commonly 6 for a 3-phase, 6/4-pole VRSM). The rotor is mounted on a non-magnetic shaft.

The rotor is made of **soft iron (not a permanent magnet)** because the operating principle depends entirely on *variation of magnetic reluctance* with rotor position. When a stator phase is energized, the rotor rotates to align its teeth with the energized stator poles to provide a *path of minimum reluctance* ($\mathcal{R} = l/\mu A$). A permanent magnet would *short-circuit* this reluctance variation and lock the rotor in a fixed orientation, destroying the stepping action.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Drawing a magnet inside the VRSM rotor in your sketch = **–2 marks** (Examiner deducts 2 marks from full 3).

---

### Question A2: Short-Answer (Definition + Comparison)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q: List the three main types of stepper motors. Differentiate their rotor construction in one sentence each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three main types of stepper motors are:

1. **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM):** Rotor is a *plain salient soft-iron piece* with no magnet and no winding — torque arises from *variation of reluctance*.
2. **Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM):** Rotor is a *cylindrical permanent magnet* with radial N–S poles — torque arises from *interaction between stator field and rotor magnet*.
3. **Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM):** Rotor consists of *two soft-iron end-caps with fine teeth* separated by an *axially-magnetized permanent magnet disc* — torque arises from *combined reluctance and magnet alignment* effects, giving the highest step resolution and holding torque.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — VRSM & PMSM Constructional Focus

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the constructional details of a **3-stack Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor**. Derive the step-angle formula and compute the step angle for a motor with $N_r = 12$ rotor teeth per stack and a 3-phase stator.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compare the constructional features of a **Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM)** and a **Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)** with neat sketches. Highlight the role of the axial permanent magnet in the HSM rotor.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

**Part (a) Model Solution:**

**Constructional Details of a 3-Stack VRSM (7 marks breakdown: 3 marks diagram, 2 marks construction text, 2 marks derivation + calculation):**

A 3-stack VRSM consists of **three identical stator sections stacked axially along the shaft**, each section being a laminated silicon-steel ring with **$N_s$ salient poles** (typically $N_s = 12$) and **3-phase windings** (Phase A, B, C) wound on diametrically opposite pole pairs.

**Rotor:** The rotor is a single shaft carrying **three soft-iron sections**, each with **$N_r = 12$ teeth**. The middle rotor section is *angularly offset* by **$1/3$ of a tooth pitch** from the outer two sections, which are aligned with each other. This mechanical offset provides the *phase displacement* needed for sequential stepping.

**Step-Angle Derivation:**

Each tooth pitch corresponds to a mechanical angle of:

$$\theta_{tp} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r}$$

When Phase A is excited, the rotor aligns with Section 1. When Phase B is excited, the rotor shifts by $1/3$ tooth pitch to align with Section 2 (since the rotor teeth in Section 2 are offset by $1/3$ pitch). Similarly, Phase C aligns with Section 3.

Therefore, the step angle is:

$$\theta_s = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \theta_{tp} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r} = \frac{120^{\circ}}{N_r}$$

Substituting $N_r = 12$:

$$\theta_s = \frac{120^{\circ}}{12} = 10^{\circ}$$

**Number of steps per revolution:**

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{10^{\circ}} = 36 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Part (b) Model Solution:**

**Comparison of PMSM and HSM Construction (7 marks: 3 marks PMSM, 3 marks HSM, 1 mark tabulated comparison):**

**PMSM Construction:**

The PMSM has a **stator** that is a **laminated silicon-steel core** with **4 or 8 smooth salient poles** carrying a **2-phase concentrated winding** (Phase A and Phase B). The windings may be *bifilar* (for unipolar drive) or *single-layer* (for bipolar drive). The **rotor** is a *cylindrical permanent magnet* magnetized with **2 or 4 radial poles** of alternating N–S polarity. Materials used are typically **barium ferrite** (cost-effective) or **NdFeB** (high performance). The rotor is mounted on a standard shaft supported by two sealed ball bearings housed in aluminum end-bells. The PMSM has substantial **detent torque** at zero excitation (typically 5%–10% of holding torque) because the magnet always tries to align with the stator slot openings.

**HSM Construction:**

The HSM has a **multi-toothed stator** — typically 8 salient poles, each carrying 5 fine teeth, with two phases (A and B) whose pole teeth are **offset by half a tooth pitch** to create the quadrature structure. The **rotor** is a *two-cap assembly* on a non-magnetic shaft:

- **Upper soft-iron cap** with 50 fine teeth.
- **Lower soft-iron cap** with 50 fine teeth, **offset by half a tooth pitch** from the upper cap.
- A **permanent magnet disc** (NdFeB, axially magnetized) sandwiched between the two caps.

**Role of the Axial Permanent Magnet:**

The axial magnet serves **two critical functions** in the HSM:

1. It **polarizes** the two rotor caps into opposite magnetic polarities (one cap becomes uniformly N, the other S) so that the *teeth of the upper cap are North poles* and the *teeth of the lower cap are South poles* in the air gap simultaneously.
2. It **provides the static holding (detent) torque** even when no phase is energized, by trying to align its flux with the stator teeth.

| Feature | PMSM | HSM |
|---|---|---|
| Stator teeth | Smooth poles | Fine teeth (5/pole) |
| Rotor poles | 2 or 4 | 50–100 fine teeth per cap |
| Magnet placement | Radial, in rotor body | Axial disc between caps |
| Step angle | $15^{\circ}$–$90^{\circ}$ | $0.9^{\circ}$–$1.8^{\circ}$ |
| Detent torque | High | Moderate |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — HSM & Material Science Focus (Alternative Choice)

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat diagram, describe the **constructional details of a Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM)**. A 2-phase HSM has 50 rotor teeth on each cap. Compute (i) the step angle, (ii) steps per revolution, and (iii) the electrical angle per step.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the **role of lamination, air gap, and material selection** in the construction of a stepper motor. Why is the air gap kept as small as mechanically feasible?

---

### Question B — Model Solution

**Part (a) Model Solution:**

**HSM Constructional Details (4 marks diagram + 3 marks numerical):**

The HSM consists of:
- **Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel stack (M-19 grade, $0.35\text{ mm}$ lamination thickness) with **8 salient poles**. Each pole carries **5 fine teeth**, giving a total of 40 stator teeth. The 8 poles are wound as **2 phases (A and B)** in a concentrated configuration. The Phase B pole teeth are *offset by half a tooth pitch* relative to Phase A.
- **Rotor:** Two soft-iron end-caps (50 fine teeth each) mounted on a **non-magnetic SS-304 stainless steel shaft**, with the two caps **offset by half a tooth pitch** from each other and separated by a **cylindrical NdFeB permanent magnet disc** that is axially magnetized.
- **End bells:** Aluminum die-cast, supporting two **sealed deep-groove ball bearings**.
- **Encapsulation:** Polymer bobbins insulate the windings from the laminations.

**Numerical Computations:**

Given: $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth per cap, $m = 4$ (2-phase full-step excitation, two-phase-on mode).

**(i) Step Angle:**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{50 \times 4} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{200} = 1.8^{\circ}$$

**(ii) Steps per Revolution:**

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{1.8^{\circ}} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**(iii) Electrical Angle per Step:**

$$\theta_{e,\text{step}} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{m} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{4} = 90^{\circ}$$

> **Interpretation:** The stator MMF vector rotates by $90^{\circ}$ in electrical space for each $1.8^{\circ}$ mechanical step.

**Part (b) Model Solution:**

**Role of Lamination (2 marks):** The stator and rotor cores of a stepper motor are made of *thin laminations* of grain-oriented silicon steel (typically **M-19** or **M-27 grade, $0.35\text{ mm}$ thickness**), insulated from one another by a thin oxide or varnish layer. The purpose is to **suppress eddy-current losses** that would otherwise be catastrophic during *high-frequency pulsed excitation* (stepper motors are routinely driven at $1\text{ kHz}$ to $20\text{ kHz}$ pulse rates). Without lamination, the iron core would heat up within seconds and saturate, destroying the torque-producing capability.

**Role of Material Selection (2 marks):**
- **Rotor permanent magnet:** Modern stepper motors use **NdFeB (Neodymium-Iron-Boron)** magnets (grade N35 to N52) because of their very high residual flux density ($B_r \geq 1.2\text{ T}$) and high coercivity ($H_c \geq 800\text{ kA/m}$). This maximizes the *torque-per-unit-volume*.
- **Shaft material:** A *non-magnetic stainless steel* (SS-304) is used to prevent the axial flux from the rotor magnet from *short-circuiting through the shaft* back to the opposite cap, which would bypass the air gap and reduce torque.
- **Bearings:** Sealed *deep-groove ball bearings* maintain the tight air-gap tolerance under axial and radial load.

**Why Air Gap is Kept Small (3 marks):** The air gap is the *primary energy-conversion region* of the machine. Three reasons mandate a small gap:

1. **Torque proportionality:** The electromagnetic torque is *inversely proportional* to the square of the effective air-gap length. Halving the air gap **quadruples** the torque for the same MMF.
2. **Flux density:** Flux density in the air gap is $B_g = \text{MMF}/\mathcal{R}_g$, where reluctance $\mathcal{R}_g = g/(\mu_0 A)$. A larger $g$ directly reduces $B_g$, weakening the rotor's magnetic loading.
3. **Step accuracy and detent stability:** A small, uniform air gap ensures *predictable detent positions* and *minimum position error* between commanded and actual rotor angles, which is critical for open-loop positioning accuracy (typically $\pm 3\%$ of one step angle, non-cumulative).

However, the gap cannot be *zero* — mechanical tolerances, thermal expansion of the rotor, and bearing runout impose a practical lower limit of approximately **$0.05\text{ mm}$** in commercial motors.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on "Constructional Details" Questions:**
> 1. **Drawing a magnet inside a VRSM rotor** — *the rotor of a VRSM is unmagnetized soft iron*. (−2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the air-gap dimension in the diagram** — *always show $g$ with a clear dimension line*. (−1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing "phases" with "poles"** — *a 2-phase motor has 2 electrical phases, not 2 poles*. (−1 mark)
> 4. **Omitting the half-tooth-pitch offset** in the HSM stator/rotor description — *this is the single defining constructional feature of the HSM*. (−2 marks)
> 5. **Stating step-angle formula without stating the excitation mode** — *a 2-phase motor has TWO step angles: $1.8^{\circ}$ (two-phase-on) and $0.9^{\circ}$ (half-step)*. Always specify. (−1 mark)

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Constructional Details of Stepper Motors"**

- [ ] **Three families of stepper motors:** VRSM, PMSM, HSM.
- [ ] **VRSM rotor:** Plain soft-iron salient pole, *no magnet, no winding*. Stator has 3 or 4 phases with concentrated windings.
- [ ] **PMSM rotor:** Cylindrical permanent magnet (2 or 4 radial poles). Stator has 2 phases, smooth poles.
- [ ] **HSM rotor:** *Two soft-iron caps with fine teeth* + *axial NdFeB disc magnet*. Stator has 2 phases with *fine teeth offset by half a tooth pitch*.
- [ ] **Step-angle formula (universal):** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_r \cdot m}$.
- [ ] **HSM step angle (2-phase full-step):** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{2 \cdot N_r}$.
- [ ] **VRSM step angle:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{120^{\circ}}{N_r}$ for 3-stack; $\theta_s = \dfrac{90^{\circ}}{N_r}$ for 4-stack.
- [ ] **Detent torque signature:** VRSM = **zero**; PMSM = **highest**; HSM = **moderate**.
- [ ] **Air gap range:** $0.05\text{ mm}$–$0.2\text{ mm}$.
- [ ] **Lamination:** M-19 / M-27 silicon steel, $0.35\text{ mm}$ thickness, to suppress eddy currents.
- [ ] **Shaft material:** SS-304 non-magnetic stainless steel (prevents axial flux short-circuit).
- [ ] **Most common HSM:** $1.8^{\circ}$, 200 steps/rev, $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth.
- [ ] **Pulse frequency for 1 rev/s in 1.8° HSM:** $200\text{ Hz}$; for 60 rpm: $200\text{ Hz}$.
- [ ] **Electrical angle per step** is *always* $360^{\circ}/m$, independent of $N_r$.
- [ ] **Static torque formula (VRSM):** $T_{peak} = \dfrac{1}{2} N_r L_1 I^2$.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 most-tested diagram:** HSM cross-section with offset rotor caps and axial magnet.
- [ ] **Real-world deployment:** 3D printers, CNC machines, telescopes, robotics, automotive gauges.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Operation - Torque Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principle of Operation - Torque Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stepper Motor (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **stepper motor** is a *brushless, synchronous electromechanical energy conversion device* that converts a *train of discrete digital input pulses* into a *precise, discrete angular displacement* (a "step") of the rotor shaft. The magnitude of each step (the **step angle**, $\beta$) is determined solely by the *number of stator and rotor poles*, not by the magnitude of the applied voltage or current.

In the **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor**, the rotor is a *salient-pole, soft-iron, un-magnetised* cylinder. It carries **no permanent magnet** and **no field winding**. The rotor physically moves to *minimise the magnetic reluctance* of the flux path produced by an excited stator phase, generating a torque known as the **reluctance torque** or **alignment torque**.

The analytical heart of this module is the **torque equation**, which is mathematically derived from the principle of *co-energy variation with respect to angular displacement* at constant excitation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Compass" Intuition

Imagine you have **eight soft-iron needles** mounted on a single rotating disc, each needle perfectly straight and free to pivot. Around the disc sits a ring of **twelve electromagnets**.

> When you switch ON one of the electromagnets, the iron needles "snap" toward it, as if the magnetic field is a giant invisible rubber band pulling them into alignment. Once aligned, the needles lock in place — this is the **holding torque**.

> Now, switch OFF the first electromagnet and switch ON the *next* one in sequence. The needles pivot again, moving a *fixed angular distance* — this is **one step**.

> Repeat this process rapidly in a circular sequence, and the disc rotates *one step at a time* with surgical precision. The faster you cycle the electromagnets, the faster the disc spins — but it ALWAYS moves in identical, repeatable increments.

This is *exactly* how a **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor** works. The "rubber band" effect that snaps the rotor into alignment is the *physical manifestation of the torque equation* we are about to derive.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Quantities Highlighted:**
> - **Step Angle** ($\beta$): The discrete angular rotation per electrical pulse. **Typical values:** **0.72°**, **1.8°**, **7.5°**, **15°**, **30°**.
> - **Resolution (Steps per Revolution)**: $N = 360° / \beta$. **Typical values:** **200, 250, 400, 500 steps/rev**.
> - **Stator Phase Inductance $L(\theta)$**: A *position-dependent* quantity, sinusoidal in $\theta$ by design. Measured in **Henry (H)**.
> - **Phase Current $I$**: The DC excitation current in a phase winding. Measured in **Ampere (A)**.
> - **Static Electromagnetic Torque $T_e$**: The instantaneous torque produced at a fixed rotor position. Measured in **Newton-Metre (N·m)**.
> - **Air-gap Permeability $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \; \text{H/m}$**.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Classification of Stepper Motors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three principal topologies are examinable in KTU 2024:**
> 1. **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** — Rotor is un-magnetised soft iron; torque is purely reluctance-based. *Focus of this module.*
> 2. **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor** — Rotor carries a radially-magnetised permanent magnet; torque is magnet-alignment based (not covered in derivation here).
> 3. **Hybrid Stepper Motor** — Combines a *axially-magnetised* PM rotor with *salerited rotor caps*; produces both alignment and reluctance torque.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation — Structured Logic

The operational behaviour of a **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor** can be decomposed into the following six logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Excitation of a Stator Phase:**
  A DC voltage is applied to one stator phase winding (say Phase A). This establishes a *stationary magnetic field* across the air gap between the excited stator pole and the rotor.

- **Step 2 — Flux Path Establishment:**
  The magnetic flux $\phi$ takes the *path of minimum reluctance* $\mathcal{R}(\theta)$. Because the rotor is a salient soft-iron piece, the *air-gap reluctance is a function of rotor angular position* $\theta$.

- **Step 3 — Production of Reluctance Torque:**
  The rotor experiences a tangential force that drives it *toward the position of minimum air-gap reluctance* (i.e., perfect pole alignment). This mechanical torque is called the **electromagnetic torque** $T_e$ and is fundamentally *positive feedback for alignment* — the system is *unstable* at anti-alignment and *stable* at alignment.

- **Step 4 — Rotor Locking (Holding Torque):**
  When perfect alignment is achieved, the rotor halts and the **holding torque** equals the applied load torque. The system is in *magnetic equilibrium*.

- **Step 5 — Phase Commutation:**
  Phase A is de-energised and the *next* phase (Phase B, located at a known mechanical offset) is energised. The rotor moves by one *step angle* $\beta$ to align with the new excited pole pair.

- **Step 6 — Continuous Rotation:**
  Cycling Phase A $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ A produces continuous, discrete, unidirectional rotation. *Reversing the sequence* produces reverse rotation.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE — Must Memorise All.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \times N_r}$ | Step angle (VR stepper); $N_s$ = stator teeth count, $N_r$ = rotor teeth count | degrees |
| 2 | $T_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | **Static electromagnetic torque** (co-energy form) | N·m |
| 3 | $T_e = -\dfrac{1}{2} \, \phi^2 \, \dfrac{d\mathcal{R}(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Torque expressed in terms of flux and reluctance | N·m |
| 4 | $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \, \theta)$ | Position-dependent phase inductance model | H |
| 5 | $T_e = -\dfrac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, N_r \, L_1 \sin(N_r \, \theta)$ | Expanded sinusoidal torque | N·m |
| 6 | $T_{\text{hold}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, I^2 \, N_r \, L_1$ | **Holding torque** (peak) at $\theta = 90°/N_r$ | N·m |
| 7 | $\mathcal{R}(\theta) = \mathcal{R}_0 + \mathcal{R}_1 \cos(N_r \, \theta)$ | Position-dependent air-gap reluctance | A·turns/Wb |
| 8 | $N = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$ | Total steps per mechanical revolution | dimensionless |
| 9 | $f_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{\omega_{\text{r}}}{6 \, \beta}$ | Step pulse frequency for rotor speed $\omega_r$ (4-stack, 3-phase) | Hz |
| 10 | $W'_{\text{co}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, L(\theta) \, i^2$ | **Magnetic co-energy** stored in the air gap | Joules (J) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Notation Note:** Vertical bars $\vert$ and $\mid$ are *reserved LaTeX delimiters*. In this table, all absolute-value-like notations have been replaced with explicit fraction bars and parentheses to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **3D Printers & CNC Machines:** Stepper motors provide *open-loop* positional control without the cost of encoders.
- **Robotics:** Precise joint actuation for low-speed, high-torque applications.
- **Disk Drives & Printers:** The original commercial application — head positioning.
- **Medical Equipment (Syringe Pumps, Infusion Devices):** Infusion accuracy down to *microlitres per hour*.
- **Satellite Antenna Tracking:** The *open-loop* holding capability aligns antennas to arc-second accuracy without feedback sensors.
- **Textile & Packaging Industries:** Synchronisation of conveyor belts and cutting blades.

The **torque equation** is the *master equation* that determines whether a stepper motor can drive a given mechanical load, and the *KTU ESE routinely tests the derivation* of this equation as a 7 or 14-mark problem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Static Torque Equation (Co-Energy Method)

This is the *flagship derivation* of Module 2 and the most frequently asked 7-mark question in KTU 2024 ESE.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Co-Energy Function

For a *singly-excited* electromagnetic system (one stator phase carrying current $i$ coupled magnetically to a rotor at angular position $\theta$), the **magnetic co-energy** is defined as:

$$
W'_{\text{co}} = \int_{0}^{i} \lambda(\theta, i) \, di
$$

where $\lambda$ is the flux linkage. For a *linear magnetic system* (no saturation), $\lambda = L(\theta) \, i$, and therefore:

$$
W'_{\text{co}} = \int_{0}^{i} L(\theta) \, i \, di
$$

Integrating with respect to current (treating $\theta$ as constant during integration because the rotor has not yet moved):

$$
W'_{\text{co}} = L(\theta) \cdot \frac{i^2}{2}
$$

Hence:

$$
W'_{\text{co}} = \frac{1}{2} \, L(\theta) \, i^2
$$

> **Logic Note:** Co-energy is preferred over stored energy for torque derivation because *co-energy* depends explicitly on current $i$ (the *through variable*), and torque is the *partial derivative* of co-energy with respect to displacement (the *across variable*).

### 3.1.2 Applying the Principle of Virtual Work

The **generalised electromagnetic torque** in a rotating system is obtained by the *partial derivative of co-energy with respect to angular displacement* at **constant current** (i.e., $i = $ const during the virtual motion):

$$
T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'_{\text{co}}}{\partial \theta} \right|_{i = \text{const}}
$$

Differentiating the expression $W'_{\text{co}} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$
T_e = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \left( \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 \right)
$$

Since $i$ is treated as a constant during differentiation:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta}
$$

**Result — The Fundamental Static Torque Equation of a VR Stepper Motor:**

$$
\boxed{\, T_e = \frac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} \,}
$$

> **Sign Convention:** A *positive* $T_e$ drives the rotor toward *increasing* inductance (i.e., toward minimum reluctance, perfect alignment).

### 3.1.3 Equivalent Form in Terms of Flux Linkage and Reluctance

An *equivalent* formulation is often required by KTU when the problem specifies $\phi$ or $\mathcal{R}$ instead of $L$. Starting from $\lambda = L(\theta) \, i = N\phi$ and $L = N^2 / \mathcal{R}(\theta)$:

$$
W'_{\text{co}} = \frac{1}{2} \, L(\theta) \, i^2 = \frac{1}{2} \, \frac{N^2 \, i^2}{\mathcal{R}(\theta)} = \frac{1}{2} \, \frac{(N i)^2}{\mathcal{R}(\theta)} = \frac{1}{2} \, \frac{\lambda^2}{L(\theta)}
$$

Substituting $L(\theta) = N^2 / \mathcal{R}(\theta)$:

$$
W'_{\text{co}} = \frac{1}{2} \, \phi^2 \, \mathcal{R}(\theta)
$$

Differentiating with respect to $\theta$ at *constant flux* (because $\phi$ is the natural variable for a *flux-controlled* system):

$$
T_e = -\frac{1}{2} \, \phi^2 \, \frac{d\mathcal{R}(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

**Result — The Flux-Based Torque Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\, T_e = -\frac{1}{2} \, \phi^2 \, \frac{d\mathcal{R}(\theta)}{d\theta} \,}
$$

> **Sign Convention:** The negative sign indicates that the *system seeks to minimise reluctance* ($d\mathcal{R}/d\theta < 0 \Rightarrow T_e > 0$ drives rotor in the *increasing-$\theta$* direction toward minimum $\mathcal{R}$).

---

## 3.2 Sinusoidal Inductance Profile — Expanded Torque Equation

In a *practical VR stepper motor* with $N_r$ rotor salient poles, the phase inductance varies *sinusoidally* with rotor angle:

$$
L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \, \theta)
$$

where:
- $L_0$ = the *average* (DC) component of inductance,
- $L_1$ = the *peak amplitude* of the AC variation in inductance,
- $N_r$ = the *number of rotor poles* (which sets the electrical spatial frequency).

Differentiating with respect to $\theta$:

$$
\frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} = -N_r \, L_1 \sin(N_r \, \theta)
$$

Substituting into the master torque equation $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$:

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \left[ -N_r L_1 \sin(N_r \theta) \right]
$$

**Result — Expanded Sinusoidal Torque Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\, T_e = -\frac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, N_r \, L_1 \, \sin(N_r \, \theta) \,}
$$

### 3.2.1 Peak Torque (Holding Torque) Derivation

The torque $T_e$ is maximum in magnitude when $\sin(N_r \theta) = \pm 1$, i.e., when $N_r \theta = 90°$ or $N_r \theta = 270°$ (i.e., $\theta = 90°/N_r$ or $\theta = 270°/N_r$).

At $N_r \theta = 90°$:

$$
T_{\text{hold}} = \frac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, N_r \, L_1
$$

$$
\boxed{\, T_{\text{hold}} = \frac{1}{2} \, I^2 \, N_r \, L_1 \,}
$$

This is the **maximum static torque** the stepper can hold at *standstill* without slipping — a critical *nameplate specification*.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **[Problem]** A 3-stack variable reluctance stepper motor has **12 stator teeth** and **8 rotor teeth** per stack. The phase inductance follows the model $L(\theta) = 0.5 + 0.3 \cos(8\theta) \text{ H}$. The phase carries a steady current of $I = 3 \text{ A}$. Calculate the **static electromagnetic torque** at $\theta = 15°$ (mechanical). Also determine the **holding torque**.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Compute Step Angle:**

$$
\beta = \frac{360°}{N_s \times N_r} = \frac{360°}{12 \times 8} = \frac{360°}{96} = 3.75°
$$

**Step B — Identify Model Parameters:**

Here, $L_0 = 0.5 \text{ H}$, $L_1 = 0.3 \text{ H}$, $N_r = 8$, $I = 3 \text{ A}$.

**Step C — Differentiate $L(\theta)$:**

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{d}{d\theta} \left( 0.5 + 0.3 \cos(8\theta) \right) = -0.3 \times 8 \times \sin(8\theta) = -2.4 \sin(8\theta)
$$

**Step D — Evaluate at $\theta = 15°$:**

Convert: $8\theta = 8 \times 15° = 120°$, and $\sin(120°) = \sin(60°) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$.

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta}\bigg|_{\theta=15°} = -2.4 \times 0.8660 = -2.0785 \text{ H/rad}
$$

> **Unit Check:** Inductance must be expressed in Henry and angle in *radians* for $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ to yield H/rad. Convert $15°$ to radians: $15° = \pi/12 = 0.2618$ rad. Then $8\theta = 8 \times 0.2618 = 2.0944$ rad $= 120°$. The sine remains the same: $\sin(2.0944) = 0.8660$.

**Step E — Apply the Master Torque Equation:**

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{2} (3)^2 \times (-2.0785)
$$

$$
T_e = \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times (-2.0785) = 4.5 \times (-2.0785) = -9.353 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Magnitude:** $\vert T_e \vert = 9.353 \text{ N·m}$. The *negative sign* indicates the torque acts to *decrease* $\theta$ (drive rotor backward toward the unaligned position to *minimise reluctance* from the *positive* direction).

> **Physical Interpretation:** The rotor will accelerate in the $-\theta$ direction with torque $9.35$ N·m. To find the *direction* explicitly, check if $dL/d\theta < 0$: yes. Hence the system is on the "wrong side" of the inductance peak and the rotor will retreat toward smaller $\theta$ where inductance is *higher* (closer to maximum alignment).

**Step F — Compute the Holding Torque:**

The holding torque occurs at the position of *peak rate of change* of inductance, i.e., when $N_r \theta = 90°$ (or $270°$), so $\sin(N_r \theta) = \pm 1$:

$$
T_{\text{hold}} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 N_r L_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 8 \times 0.3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 21.6 = 10.8 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
T_e(\theta = 15°) = -9.35 \text{ N·m}, \quad T_{\text{hold}} = 10.8 \text{ N·m}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Two Torque Forms

> [!NOTE]
> The following comparison matrix is critical for answering the *frequently asked* "compare current-source vs flux-source excitation" questions in KTU.

| Parameter | Current-Controlled Form | Flux-Controlled Form |
|-----------|-------------------------|-----------------------|
| Equation | $T_e = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | $T_e = -\dfrac{1}{2} \phi^2 \dfrac{d\mathcal{R}}{d\theta}$ |
| Independent Variable | Current $i$ held constant | Flux $\phi$ held constant |
| Differentiated Quantity | Inductance $L(\theta)$ | Reluctance $\mathcal{R}(\theta)$ |
| Sign of Result | Positive when $dL/d\theta > 0$ | Positive when $d\mathcal{R}/d\theta < 0$ |
| Practical Use | Phase-fed by current-source inverter (chopper drive) | Theoretical analysis with PMs or saturated systems |
| KTU Mark Allocation | Standard 7-mark derivation | Standard 7-mark derivation (alternate form) |
