T_{avg} = \frac{N_r}{2\pi} \int_{\theta_{on}}^{\theta_{off}} \frac{1}{2}\, i^2(\theta)\, \frac{dL}{d\theta}\, d\theta
$$

The factor $N_r/(2\pi)$ arises because $\theta_{stroke} = 2\pi/N_r$, hence $1/\theta_{stroke} = N_r/(2\pi)$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Linearized Inductance Profile

For analytical tractability, the actual non-linear inductance profile of an SRM is often linearized as a trapezoidal function of rotor angle. Define $\theta_1$ (start of rising inductance), $\theta_2$ (end of rising inductance = aligned position), and $\theta_3$ (start of falling inductance). The linearized profile is:

$$
L(\theta) = \begin{cases}
L_{min}, & 0 \le \theta < \theta_1 \\[4pt]
L_{min} + K \cdot (\theta - \theta_1), & \theta_1 \le \theta < \theta_2 \\[4pt]
L_{max}, & \theta_2 \le \theta < \theta_3 \\[4pt]
L_{max} - K \cdot (\theta - \theta_3), & \theta_3 \le \theta < \theta_4 \\[4pt]
L_{min}, & \theta_4 \le \theta < 2\pi/N_r
\end{cases}
$$

where the slope $K = (L_{max} - L_{min})/(\theta_2 - \theta_1)$.

The corresponding $dL/d\theta$ is piecewise constant:

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta} = \begin{cases}
0, & \text{outside rising or falling regions} \\
+K, & \text{rising inductance region} \\
-K, & \text{falling inductance region}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** In the *rising* inductance region, $dL/d\theta > 0$ ⇒ **positive torque (motoring)**. In the *falling* inductance region, $dL/d\theta < 0$ ⇒ **negative torque (generating/braking)**. The controller must therefore turn OFF the phase *before* it enters the falling region to avoid braking torque.

### 3.4 Derivation: Phase Voltage Equation (Magnetization Stage)

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to a single phase winding during the magnetization stage, with both switches of the asymmetric half-bridge closed:

$$
V_{dc} = R \cdot i + \frac{d\lambda}{dt}
$$

Using the chain rule for total time derivative of flux linkage, since $\lambda = L(\theta)\cdot i$:

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial i} \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial \theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = L(\theta) \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + i \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot \omega
$$

Substituting back into the KVL equation:

$$
V_{dc} = R \cdot i + L(\theta) \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + i \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot \omega
$$

The three terms on the right represent:
1. $R \cdot i$ — Ohmic (copper) drop.
2. $L(\theta) \cdot (di/dt)$ — *Transformer EMF* (rate of change of current).
3. $i \cdot (dL/d\theta) \cdot \omega$ — *Motional EMF* or *back-EMF* (rotational coupling).

> **Conversion Logic:** At low speeds, the motional EMF is small, so current rises rapidly and the controller must *chop* to limit it. At high speeds, the motional EMF approaches $V_{dc}$, leaving little voltage headroom for current rise — this is the **fundamental reason why SRMs require angle control at high speeds**.

### 3.5 Derivation: Current Behaviour During Demagnetization

When both switches open at $\theta_{off}$, the phase winding is connected through the two freewheeling diodes to the negative DC rail. The applied voltage becomes $-V_{dc}$:

$$
-V_{dc} = R \cdot i + L(\theta) \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + i \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot \omega
$$

Solving for $di/dt$:

$$
\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{-V_{dc} - R \cdot i - i \cdot (dL/d\theta) \cdot \omega}{L(\theta)}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Since all three terms in the numerator are typically negative during the demagnetization in the motoring region, $di/dt$ is strongly negative — current falls rapidly. The magnetic energy stored in the phase inductance is returned to the DC link through the diodes, which is why SRM converters are inherently *regenerative without additional hardware*.

### 3.6 Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A 6/4 SRM has $L_{min} = \mathbf{10}$ mH, $L_{max} = \mathbf{60}$ mH, and operates with constant phase current $i = \mathbf{10}$ A in the rising-inductance region of stroke angle $\theta_{stroke} = \mathbf{90°}$. Compute the instantaneous torque. Take $\theta_1 = \mathbf{30°}$, $\theta_2 = \mathbf{60°}$.

**Solution:**

The slope $K$ of the inductance profile in the rising region:

$$
K = \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{\theta_2 - \theta_1} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-3} - 10 \times 10^{-3}}{(60° - 30°)(\pi/180)}
$$

$$
K = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{30° \cdot \pi/180°} = \frac{0.05}{0.5236} \approx 0.0955 \, \text{H/rad}
$$

Instantaneous torque:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (10)^2 \cdot 0.0955
$$

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 \cdot 0.0955 \approx 4.775 \, \text{N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Note that doubling the current *quadruples* the torque (quadratic dependence) — a unique characteristic of SRMs that makes them excellent for high-torque applications but also makes torque ripple a critical design concern.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop SRM Drive System

The complete SRM drive system is a cascade of three control loops: an outer *speed loop*, an intermediate *torque/current reference* loop, and an inner *phase current* loop, all gated by rotor-position information.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SpeedRef[Speed Reference omega ref] --> SpeedCtrl[Speed Controller PI]
    LoadTorque[Load Torque Disturbance] --> SpeedCtrl
    SpeedSensor[Tachometer Encoder] --> SpeedFeedback[Actual Speed omega]
    SpeedFeedback --> SpeedCtrl
    SpeedCtrl --> TorqueRef[Torque Reference T ref]
    TorqueRef --> TorqueAllocator[Torque to Current Allocator]
    PositionSensor[Rotary Position Sensor] --> AngleCalc[Rotor Angle Calculator]
    AngleCalc --> LUT[Angle Optimisation LUT for theta on and theta off]
    LUT --> CommutationLogic[Commutation Logic Switch Sequencing]
    TorqueAllocator --> CurrentRef[Phase Current Reference I ref]
    CurrentRef --> HysteresisComparator[Hysteresis Comparator]
    AngleCalc --> CommutationLogic
    CommutationLogic --> HysteresisComparator
    ActualCurrent[Phase Current Feedback from CT] --> HysteresisComparator
    HysteresisComparator --> GateDriver[Gate Driver Circuit]
    GateDriver --> AsymBridge[Asymmetric Half Bridge Converter]
    AsymBridge --> SRMPhase[SRM Phase Winding A B C D]
    SRMPhase --> BackEMF[Back EMF and Reluctance Variation]
    BackEMF --> SRMPhase
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Asymmetric Half-Bridge Power Converter (Per-Phase)

This is the *workhorse topology* of all commercial SRM drives. Each phase has **two IGBTs/MOSFETs** and **two freewheeling diodes**, providing complete independence between phases.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCPos[DC Link Positive Vdc] --> S1[Switch S1 IGBT]
    DCPos --> S2[Switch S2 IGBT]
    S1 --> PhaseTermA[Phase Terminal A]
    S2 --> PhaseTermA
    PhaseTermA --> PhaseWinding[Phase Winding L theta i]
    PhaseWinding --> PhaseTermB[Phase Terminal B]
    PhaseTermB --> D1[Freewheel Diode D1]
    PhaseTermB --> D2[Freewheel Diode D2]
    D1 --> DCPos
    D2 --> DCNeg[DC Link Negative]
    DCNeg --> S1
    DCNeg --> S2
    GateCtrl[Gate Signals from Controller] --> S1
    GateCtrl --> S2
```

> **Operating Modes within the Converter:**
>
> | Mode | S1 | S2 | Phase Voltage |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Magnetization | ON | ON | $+V_{dc}$ |
> | Freewheeling (soft chop) | ON | OFF | $0$ V |
> | Demagnetization | OFF | OFF | $-V_{dc}$ |
> | Idle | OFF | OFF | Disconnected |

### 4.3 Block Diagram — SRM Control State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Idle[Idle State Current i 0] -->|theta reaches theta on| Magnetize[Magnetization Apply Vdc]
    Magnetize -->|theta between theta on and theta off| Chop[Chopping Mode Hysteresis or PWM]
    Chop -->|i exceeds upper band| FW[Freewheel Soft Chop]
    FW -->|i drops below lower band| Chop
    Chop -->|theta reaches theta off| Demag[Demagnetization Apply Vdc]
    Demag -->|current decays to zero| Idle
    Magnetize -->|theta reaches theta off| Demag
```

### 4.4 R-Dump and C-Dump Converter Alternatives (Block-Level Functional Map)

When component cost is a concern, two alternative single-switch-per-phase topologies are used. Their functional flow is mapped below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RDump[R Dump Topology]
        RD1[Phase Winding] --> RD2[Phase Switch]
        RD2 --> RD3[DC Link Return]
        RD2 -->|at turn OFF| RD4[R Dump Diode]
        RD4 --> RD5[Dump Resistor R]
        RD5 --> RD3
    end
    subgraph CDump[C Dump Topology]
        CD1[Phase Winding] --> CD2[Phase Switch]
        CD2 --> CD3[DC Link Return]
        CD2 -->|at turn OFF| CD4[C Dump Diode]
        CD4 --> CD5[Dump Capacitor C]
        CD5 --> CD6[C Dump Chopper]
        CD6 --> RD3
    end
```

> **Conversion Logic:** In *R-dump*, energy is dissipated as heat — inefficient. In *C-dump*, energy is partially recovered into the DC link via a chopper-controlled discharge loop — better efficiency but more components. The *asymmetric half-bridge* (Section 4.2) remains the gold standard for high-performance SRM drives.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions test the **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers must be concise (3–4 sentences) and definition-driven.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "turn-on angle" and "turn-off angle" as used in the control of a Switched Reluctance Motor. State their typical relationship to the rotor stroke angle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **turn-on angle ($\theta_{on}$)** is the rotor position at which a particular stator phase is energized (switches of the converter are closed), initiating current rise in the phase winding. The **turn-off angle ($\theta_{off}$)** is the rotor position at which the same phase is de-energized (switches are opened), initiating current decay.

Both angles are referenced against the **unaligned rotor position**, and their difference $(\theta_{off} - \theta_{on})$ defines the **conduction window**. In a properly tuned drive, $\theta_{on}$ is set slightly before the *minimum-inductance* position and $\theta_{off}$ is set before the *maximum-inductance* position, ensuring that current flows only in the rising-inductance region where motoring torque is produced. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare "hard chopping" and "soft chopping" in the context of SRM current control. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In **hard chopping**, both switches of the asymmetric half-bridge for a given phase are turned ON and OFF together, applying either $+V_{dc}$ or $-V_{dc}$ across the phase. This produces a large voltage swing and rapid current decay but causes high acoustic noise and high switching losses.

In **soft chopping**, only one switch is modulated while the other remains continuously ON, applying either $+V_{dc}$ or $0$ V across the phase. **Advantage:** Soft chopping reduces switching losses by half and lowers acoustic noise; **Hard chopping advantage:** It provides faster current decay during demagnetization. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These questions test the **Apply**, **Analyse**, and **Evaluate** levels. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are each 7 marks. Every sub-question requires a complete step-by-step solution.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Asymmetric Bridge and Current Control**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) Draw the power circuit diagram of the asymmetric half-bridge converter for one phase of a 4-phase SRM. Explain its four modes of operation with the help of the equivalent circuit in each mode. (7 marks)**

**(b) A 3-phase, 6/4 SRM operates with constant current $i = 8$ A in the rising-inductance region. The inductance varies linearly from $L_{min} = 12$ mH to $L_{max} = 72$ mH over an electrical angle of $\theta_2 - \theta_1 = 45°$. Compute (i) the slope $dL/d\theta$ in the rising region, (ii) the instantaneous electromagnetic torque, and (iii) the average torque if the conduction window $\theta_{off} - \theta_{on} = 30°$ lies entirely within the rising-inductance region. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Asymmetric Half-Bridge Power Circuit (7 marks)**

The asymmetric half-bridge provides complete phase independence and inherent regenerative capability. Each phase has two controlled switches ($S_1$, $S_2$) and two freewheeling diodes ($D_1$, $D_2$).

```
              +Vdc
               |
        +------+------+
        |             |
        S1            S2       (IGBT/MOSFET)
        |             |
        +----+--------+----+
             |             |
             A             B     (Phase terminals)
             |             |
             +---L(θ)------+
             |    i        |
             +-------------+
        +----+--------+----+
        |             |
        D1            D2       (Freewheel diodes)
        |             |
        +------+------+
               |
              -Vdc
```

**Four Modes of Operation:**

| Mode | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $V_{phase}$ | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mode 1 — Magnetization** | ON | ON | $+V_{dc}$ | Current rises rapidly; energy stored in $L(\theta)$ |
| **Mode 2 — Freewheeling** | ON | OFF | $0$ V | Current circulates through $S_1$ and $D_2$; decays slowly |
| **Mode 3 — Demagnetization** | OFF | OFF | $-V_{dc}$ | Current decays rapidly through $D_1$ and $D_2$; energy returned to DC link |
| **Mode 4 — Idle** | OFF | OFF | — | Phase disconnected; rotor coasting |

> **Key Point (1 mark):** During Mode 2, the application of $0$ V (rather than $-V_{dc}$) reduces the rate of current change, which is essential for *hysteresis current control* in the chopping region.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $i = 8$ A (constant)
- $L_{min} = 12$ mH $= 0.012$ H
- $L_{max} = 72$ mH $= 0.072$ H
- $\theta_2 - \theta_1 = 45° = \pi/4$ rad $= 0.7854$ rad
- Conduction window $\theta_{off} - \theta_{on} = 30° = \pi/6$ rad $= 0.5236$ rad
- $N_r = 4$ (since 6/4 SRM)

**(i) Slope $dL/d\theta$ (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{\theta_2 - \theta_1} = \frac{0.072 - 0.012}{0.7854} = \frac{0.060}{0.7854}
$$

$$
\frac{dL}{d\theta} \approx 0.0764 \, \text{H/rad}
$$

**(ii) Instantaneous Torque (2 marks):**

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (8)^2 \cdot 0.0764
$$

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 64 \cdot 0.0764 \approx 2.445 \, \text{N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

**(iii) Average Torque (3 marks):**

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{N_r}{2\pi} \int_{\theta_{on}}^{\theta_{off}} \frac{1}{2}\, i^2\, \frac{dL}{d\theta}\, d\theta
$$

Since $i$ and $dL/d\theta$ are constant within the conduction window:

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{N_r}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot (\theta_{off} - \theta_{on})
$$

Substituting values:

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{4}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot (8)^2 \cdot 0.0764 \cdot 0.5236
$$

$$
T_{avg} = 0.6366 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 64 \cdot 0.0764 \cdot 0.5236
$$

$$
T_{avg} \approx 0.814 \, \text{N}\cdot\text{m}
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The average torque is *less* than the instantaneous torque because the conduction window does not cover the entire rising-inductance region. **(7 marks total)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Angle Control and Closed-Loop Strategy**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) Explain with suitable diagrams the three principal control strategies of an SRM drive: voltage PWM control, hysteresis current control, and angle control. State the speed range over which each strategy is typically employed. (7 marks)**

**(b) Derive the phase voltage equation of an SRM during the magnetization stage. Using this equation, explain why angle control becomes mandatory at high speeds. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Control Strategies (7 marks)**

**(1) Voltage PWM Control (2 marks):**

In voltage PWM (or *hard chopping*), both switches of the asymmetric bridge are operated at a fixed high switching frequency ($f_{sw} = 5$ to $20$ kHz) with a variable duty cycle $D$. The effective voltage applied to the phase is:

$$
V_{eff} = D \cdot V_{dc}
$$

The current ripple frequency equals the switching frequency. As speed increases, the back-EMF rises and current naturally falls, so increasing $D$ maintains torque. **Speed range:** Used primarily in the **constant-torque region** at low and medium speeds.

**(2) Hysteresis Current Control (2 marks):**

The actual phase current $i(t)$ is sensed via a current transducer (CT) and compared against $I_{ref} \pm \Delta I/2$. A hysteresis comparator generates the gate signals such that:

- If $i > I_{ref} + \Delta I/2$ → switches OFF (current decays).
- If $i < I_{ref} - \Delta I/2$ → switches ON (current rises).

The chopping frequency is *variable* and load-dependent. **Speed range:** Used at **low speeds** where the current must be tightly regulated to limit torque ripple.

**Sub-variant — Soft Chopping:** Only one switch toggles; the other remains ON, applying $0$ V during the OFF interval (instead of $-V_{dc}$). This halves the voltage swing and reduces acoustic noise.

**(3) Angle Control (2 marks):**

At high speeds, the back-EMF $i \cdot (dL/d\theta)\cdot \omega$ approaches $V_{dc}$, leaving insufficient voltage to chop the current. The controller abandons current regulation and applies the full $V_{dc}$ during a precisely timed conduction window $(\theta_{on}, \theta_{off})$. By *advancing* $\theta_{on}$ and *retarding* $\theta_{off}$, the controller compensates for the slower current rise. **Speed range:** Used in the **constant-power region** at high speeds.

**Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Strategy | Speed Range | Controlled Variable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Voltage PWM | Low–Medium | $D$ (duty cycle) |
| Hysteresis | Low | $I_{ref}$, $\Delta I$ |
| Angle | Medium–High | $\theta_{on}$, $\theta_{off}$ |

---

**(b) Derivation of Phase Voltage Equation and Need for Angle Control (7 marks)**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Apply KVL to a phase during magnetization (both switches closed):

$$
V_{dc} = R \cdot i + \frac{d\lambda(i,\theta)}{dt}
$$

The flux linkage $\lambda(i,\theta) = L(\theta) \cdot i$ in the linear magnetic regime. Using the chain rule for total time derivative:

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial i} \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial \theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = L(\theta) \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + i \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

Since $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$ (rotor angular speed):

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = L(\theta) \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + i \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot \omega
$$

Substituting back:

$$
V_{dc} = R \cdot i + L(\theta) \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + i \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot \omega
$$

This is the **fundamental phase voltage equation**. The three right-side terms are:

| Term | Name | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R \cdot i$ | Ohmic drop | Copper loss |
| $L(\theta) \cdot (di/dt)$ | Transformer EMF | Due to current change |
| $i \cdot (dL/d\theta)\cdot \omega$ | Motional EMF / Back-EMF | Due to rotor motion |

**Why Angle Control at High Speed (2 marks):**

Rearranging for $di/dt$:

$$
\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{V_{dc} - R\cdot i - i\cdot (dL/d\theta)\cdot \omega}{L(\theta)}
$$

At high speeds, $\omega$ is large, making the motional EMF $i \cdot (dL/d\theta) \cdot \omega$ comparable to $V_{dc}$. This leaves very little "voltage headroom" for $L \cdot (di/dt)$, so the current cannot be chopped — it follows a *quasi-trapezoidal* shape determined entirely by the rotor angle. The controller therefore manipulates $\theta_{on}$ (advancing it earlier) so that current has sufficient time to rise to its peak before the back-EMF saturates, and $\theta_{off}$ is also optimized to maximize the area under the $i^2 \cdot (dL/d\theta)$ curve (i.e., torque production). **(7 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the chain rule** — Many students write $\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ and *omit* the motional EMF term. This loses **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Sign confusion in demagnetization** — During demagnetization the applied voltage is $-V_{dc}$, *not* $0$. Mixing signs causes wrong sign of $di/dt$.
> 3. **Mixing up $\theta_{stroke}$ and $\theta_{pole-pitch}$** — In a 6/4 SRM, $\theta_{stroke} = 90°$ (mechanical), but the *pole pitch* of the rotor is $360°/4 = 90°$ — they coincide only in this specific configuration.
> 4. **Not converting degrees to radians** in numerical problems. $dL/d\theta$ in H/rad requires angles in *radians*, not degrees. Many students lose marks here.
> 5. **Skipping the stroke angle $N_r/(2\pi)$ factor** in the average torque formula. Forgetting the $1/\theta_{stroke}$ normalization costs **2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Pin to Your Wall!**

- **SRM is doubly salient, singly excited** — torque from *reluctance variation*, not from magnets or rotor winding.
- **Torque equation:** $\boxed{T = \dfrac{1}{2}\, i^2\, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}}$ — note the *quadratic* current dependence and the requirement $dL/d\theta > 0$.
- **Phase voltage equation:** $\boxed{V_{dc} = R i + L\dfrac{di}{dt} + i\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}\omega}$ — three terms: copper, transformer EMF, motional EMF.
- **Three control strategies:** Voltage PWM (low/medium speed), Hysteresis Current (low speed), Angle Control (high speed).
- **Three control variables:** $\theta_{on}$, $\theta_{off}$, $I_{ref}$ (or $V_{ref}$).
- **Converter topology:** Asymmetric half-bridge is the *gold standard* — independent phase control, inherent regeneration.
- **Hard chopping:** both switches toggle ($+V_{dc}$ ↔ $-V_{dc}$) — fast decay, high noise.
- **Soft chopping:** one switch toggles, other stays ON ($+V_{dc}$ ↔ $0$) — slow decay, low noise.
- **Stroke angle:** $\theta_{stroke} = 2\pi/N_r$ — fundamental unit of commutation.
- **Conduction window:** $\theta_{off} - \theta_{on}$ must lie *entirely* in the rising-inductance region to avoid braking torque.
- **Regenerative braking:** Freewheeling diodes return magnetic energy to DC link during demagnetization — *no extra hardware needed*.
- **Position sensor is mandatory** for proper commutation; sensorless control uses back-EMF or flux-linkage estimation methods (advanced topic).
- **Torque ripple** is high in SRMs due to discrete phase commutation — a *current KTU viva topic*.
- **Average torque formula:** $T_{avg} = \dfrac{N_r}{2\pi} \int_{\theta_{on}}^{\theta_{off}} \dfrac{1}{2}\, i^2(\theta)\, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}\, d\theta$.
- **Standard inductance ratio:** $\xi = L_{max}/L_{min} \approx \mathbf{6}$ to $\mathbf{10}$ for well-designed SRMs.
- **Common KTU mistakes:** wrong unit conversion (deg ↔ rad), forgetting the chain rule, omitting the stroke normalization factor.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotor Position Sensors - Torque Pulsations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rotor Position Sensors - Torque Pulsations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Rotor Position Sensors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rotor Position Sensor (RPS):** An electromechanical/electronic transducer that continuously monitors the instantaneous angular displacement of a machine's rotor and converts it into a train of electrical signals (digital pulses or analog waveforms) used by the inverter/controller to determine the precise instants of phase commutation.

In **Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM)** and **Brushless DC (BLDC) motors**, the rotor position information is **mandatory** because these machines are inherently self-commutating, electronic-commutated machines. Without accurate rotor position feedback, the controller cannot know *when* to energize or de-energize a given stator phase. The performance indices directly affected by RPS accuracy are:

- **Torque per ampere (Nm/A)**
- **Torque ripple (%)**
- **Speed ripple (%)**
- **Acoustic noise (dB)**
- **Efficiency (%)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metrics associated with RPS:**
> - **Resolution:** 0.1° to 0.5° electrical (typical)
> - **Bandwidth:** 1 kHz to 100 kHz
> - **Operating temperature:** −40 °C to +150 °C
> - **Supply voltage:** **3.3 V / 5 V / 15 V** (DC) — standard industrial grades

### 1.2 Torque Pulsations — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque Pulsation (Torque Ripple):** The periodic, undesirable deviation of the instantaneous electromagnetic torque developed by a machine from its average (mean) value over one electrical cycle.

Mathematically,

$$T_{ripple}(\theta) = T_{e}(\theta) - T_{avg}$$

where the **Torque Ripple Factor (TRF)** is defined as:

$$TRF = \frac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{T_{avg}} \times 100\%$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

**Rotor Position Sensor — "The Conductor's Baton":**
Imagine a Western orchestral symphony where 50 musicians must play in perfect synchrony. Without a conductor's baton, the violins might lag behind the trumpets, ruining the harmony. The **rotor position sensor is the conductor's baton** for the machine — at every precise angular instant, it cues the correct "instrument" (stator phase) to play its note (carry current). A faulty sensor is like a drunken conductor — the orchestra collapses into noise (high torque ripple, vibration, poor efficiency).

**Torque Pulsation — "The Bicycle Pedal's Dead Spot":**
Consider a 4-stroke bicycle with a single pedal: when the pedal is at the top-dead-center, you cannot apply forward force — that angular region is a "dead spot" producing zero torque. This is exactly what happens in a **SRM** between phase commutation zones. Torque pulsation is the engineering equivalent of these "dead spots" occurring cyclically — the cyclist's leg must compensate each revolution, producing vibration and effort loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Rotor Position Sensors Are Essential

| Machine Type | Sensor Necessity | Consequence of Removal |
|---|---|---|
| **SRM** | **Mandatory** — non-linear L(θ) requires precise turn-on/turn-off | Loss of starting torque, reverse rotation, possible demagnetization |
| **BLDC** | **Mandatory** — trapezoidal EMF needs 6-step commutation | Stalled rotor, locked-rotor condition, MOSFET burn-out |
| **PMSM** (trapezoidal) | **Mandatory** | Same as BLDC |
| **PMSM** (sinusoidal) | Used for **vector control** dq-frame transformation | Field-weakening failure, poor dynamic response |

### 2.2 Classification of Rotor Position Sensors

**Logical hierarchy of the major sensor families used in special machines:**

**A. Hall Effect Sensors (HES)**

- **Operating principle:** When a current-carrying semiconductor is exposed to a perpendicular magnetic field, a transverse voltage (Hall voltage) is generated.
- **Hall voltage equation:**

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I_B \cdot B}{t}$$

where $R_H$ = Hall coefficient, $I_B$ = bias current, $B$ = magnetic flux density, $t$ = thickness of the semiconductor.

- **Output:** Digital square wave (logic 0 or 1).
- **Placement:** Three HES placed 120° apart on the stator for BLDC trapezoidal control.
- **Resolution:** Coarse — only 60° electrical per commutation step.
- **Advantage:** **Low cost, compact, TTL-compatible**.
- **Disadvantage:** Sensitive to temperature drift; low resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> For SRM with 8/6 (8-pole stator, 6-pole rotor) configuration, **2 Hall sensors offset by 75° mechanical** are sufficient.

**B. Optical Encoders (Incremental)**

- **Operating principle:** A slotted disc rotates between a **light source (LED)** and a **photodetector**. The light pulses generate two quadrature outputs (A and B) plus an index pulse Z.
- **Resolution equation:**

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{N_{PPR}}$$

where $N_{PPR}$ = pulses per revolution. Standard industrial grades: **100, 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096 PPR**.

- **Quadrature decoding** multiplies effective resolution by **×4**.
- **Advantage:** **High resolution (down to 0.044° at 8192 PPR)**.
- **Disadvantage:** Sensitive to dust, vibration, oil contamination — requires sealed housing.

**C. Resolvers (Brushless Encoders)**

- **Operating principle:** A wound rotor (fed through a rotary transformer) couples two stator windings 90° apart. The induced voltages are:

$$V_{sin} = V_0 \cdot \sin(\omega_c t) \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

$$V_{cos} = V_0 \cdot \sin(\omega_c t) \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

- **RDC (Resolver-to-Digital Converter)** demodulates these to extract $\theta$.
- **Resolution:** Up to **16-bit (≈ 0.005°)**.
- **Advantage:** **Rugged, high-temperature tolerant (up to 200 °C)**, immune to dust, oil, vibration.
- **Disadvantage:** **Expensive**, requires RDC chip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Aerospace and military applications almost exclusively use **resolvers** over optical encoders because of their tolerance to harsh environments.

**D. Inductive / RF Sensors**

- **Operating principle:** An excited coil induces eddy currents in a metallic rotor; the reflected impedance is modulated by rotor position.
- Used in **low-cost sensorless systems** as part of startup detection schemes.

**E. Capacitive Encoders**

- **Operating principle:** A high-frequency AC bridge detects changes in capacitance as a modulated electrode pattern on the rotor passes stator plates.
- **Advantage:** High accuracy, sub-arc-second resolution.
- **Disadvantage:** Sensitive to humidity, contamination.

**F. Sensorless Position Estimation (Supplementary)**

- **Back-EMF detection** (BLDC) — only works above minimum speed ≈ 10% of rated.
- **High-frequency signal injection** (PMSM) — works at zero speed.
- **Flux-linkage/current slope method** (SRM) — uses $\frac{di}{dt}$ to estimate $\theta$.

### 2.3 Torque Pulsations — Classification and Origin

| Type | Origin | Dominant In |
|---|---|---|
| **Cogging Torque** | Slotting — interaction of stator slots with rotor magnets | PMSM, BLDC |
| **Commutation Torque Ripple** | Transition from one phase to next | BLDC, SRM |
| **Reluctance Torque Pulsation** | Doubly-salient structure, non-sinusoidal $L(\theta)$ | SRM |
| **Mutual Coupling Torque Ripple** | Phase-to-phase mutual inductance | Multi-phase SRM |

**Torque production in SRM (basic form):**

$$T_e(\theta,i) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

For non-linear magnetic saturation, the co-energy approach gives:

$$T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W'_{co}(\theta,i)}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i = const}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | SRM instantaneous torque (linear region) | Nm |
| 2 | $W_{co} = \tfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$ | Magnetic co-energy | J |
| 3 | $TRF = \tfrac{T_{max}-T_{min}}{T_{avg}} \cdot 100$ | Torque ripple factor | % |
| 4 | $V_H = \tfrac{R_H I_B B}{t}$ | Hall voltage | V |
| 5 | $\theta_{res} = \tfrac{360°}{N_{PPR}}$ | Encoder resolution | °/pulse |
| 6 | $V_{sin} = V_0 \sin(\omega_c t) \sin(\theta)$ | Resolver sine output | V |
| 7 | $V_{cos} = V_0 \sin(\omega_c t) \cos(\theta)$ | Resolver cosine output | V |
| 8 | $f_{sw} = \tfrac{N_r \cdot N}{60}$ | SRM commutation frequency (no-load) | Hz |
| 9 | $T_{ripple,rms} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{\tau}\int_0^{\tau} [T_e(\theta)-T_{avg}]^2 d\theta}$ | RMS torque ripple | Nm |
| 10 | $f_{cogging} = \tfrac{LCM(S,P)}{S} \cdot f_{rot}$ | Cogging frequency | Hz |

where $L(\theta)$ = phase inductance, $N_r$ = rotor poles, $N$ = speed (rpm), $S$ = stator slots, $P$ = pole pairs.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electric Vehicle (EV) traction motors** (e.g., Tesla Model 3 rear motor — SRM variant) use **resolver + Hall hybrid** sensors for safety redundancy.
- **Aerospace fuel pumps** use **resolvers** in IP68 + 200 °C rating.
- **Industrial robotics** use **absolute optical encoders (EnDat, Hiperface, BISS protocols)** for sub-arc-second positional feedback.
- **SRM in white-goods (vacuum cleaners, washers)** uses **sensorless control** to reduce cost by **$2–$5 per unit**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — SRM Instantaneous Torque Equation

We start from the **principle of virtual work**. The mechanical work done by the rotor in an infinitesimal angular displacement $d\theta$ at constant current $i$ equals the change in magnetic co-energy:

$$dW_{mech} = T_e \, d\theta = dW'_{co} \Big|_{i = const}$$

The magnetic co-energy stored in one phase is given by the integral of flux-linkage $\lambda$ with respect to current:

$$W'_{co}(\theta, i) = \int_{0}^{i} \lambda(\theta, i') \, di'$$

In the **linear magnetic region** (i.e., no saturation, $\lambda = L(\theta) \cdot i'$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W'_{co}(\theta, i) &= \int_{0}^{i} L(\theta) \, i' \, di' \\
&= L(\theta) \int_{0}^{i} i' \, di' \\
&= L(\theta) \cdot \left[\frac{i'^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{i} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the co-energy stored in the magnetic field of one phase.

Differentiating $W'_{co}$ with respect to $\theta$ at constant current:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e(\theta, i) &= \left.\frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i = const} \\
&= \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left[\frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,T_e(\theta, i) = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}\,}$$

> **Key Insight:** Notice that torque is **independent of the direction of current**. The square of $i$ means the SRM can be driven by unipolar current — a major advantage in inverter design. The torque is produced **only where** $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta} \ne 0$, which is the *rising* and *falling* inductance region. This non-constancy of $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ across the electrical cycle is the **fundamental cause of torque pulsations** in SRM.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Ripple Factor (TRF) from Instantaneous Torque Profile

Consider an SRM with three phases. The total instantaneous torque is:

$$T_{total}(\theta) = T_1(\theta) + T_2(\theta) + T_3(\theta)$$

where each $T_k$ is non-zero only over a window of $\tfrac{360°}{q \cdot N_r}$ electrical (for $q$ phases, $N_r$ rotor poles).

The **average torque** over one complete electrical cycle $\tau = \tfrac{2\pi}{N_r}$:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{1}{\tau} \int_{0}^{\tau} T_{total}(\theta) \, d\theta = \frac{N_r}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi/N_r} T_{total}(\theta) \, d\theta$$

By symmetry across three phases, the integral splits into three identical phase contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= \frac{3 \cdot N_r}{2\pi} \int_{\theta_{on}}^{\theta_{off}} \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} \, d\theta \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot N_r \cdot i^2}{4\pi} \left[L(\theta_{off}) - L(\theta_{on})\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The **ripple factor** is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TRF &= \frac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{T_{avg}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{\max[T_{total}(\theta)] - \min[T_{total}(\theta)]}{T_{avg}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

For a well-designed SRM with current profiling, the realistic target is $TRF \le 10\%$, and for high-performance servo SRM, $TRF \le 5\%$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hall Sensor Output Signal Frequency

A Hall sensor detects **N_pole pairs** per mechanical revolution. The frequency of the square wave output is:

$$f_{Hall} = \frac{P \cdot N}{60}$$

where $P$ = number of pole pairs and $N$ = mechanical speed in **rpm**.

For a BLDC with $P = 4$ pole pairs running at $N = 3000$ rpm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{Hall} &= \frac{4 \times 3000}{60} \\
&= 200 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

This frequency must be **at least 10× lower** than the Hall sensor's maximum bandwidth (typically 10–100 kHz), which is comfortably satisfied.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Resolver Demodulation

The resolver outputs are multiplied by the carrier $\sin(\omega_c t)$ inside the RDC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sin} \cdot \sin(\omega_c t) &= V_0 \sin^2(\omega_c t) \sin(\theta) \\
&= \frac{V_0 \sin(\theta)}{2} \left[1 - \cos(2\omega_c t)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

After low-pass filtering (cutoff $\ll 2\omega_c$):

$$V_{sin,DC} = \frac{V_0}{2} \sin(\theta)$$

Similarly, $V_{cos,DC} = \tfrac{V_0}{2} \cos(\theta)$. The RDC computes $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\tfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}\right)$ using a **Type-II tracking loop** to give absolute angular position with sub-degree accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Rotor Position Sensor Classification (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROTOR POSITION SENSORS] --> B[CONTACT BASED]
    A --> C[NON CONTACT BASED]
    A --> D[SENSORLESS METHODS]
    B --> B1[Slip Rings and Brushes - obsolete]
    C --> E[Magnetic Type]
    C --> F[Optical Type]
    C --> G[Electromagnetic Type]
    C --> H[Capacitive Type]
    E --> E1[Hall Effect Sensor - low cost digital]
    E --> E2[Magnetoresistive MR Sensor - high precision]
    E --> E3[Tunneling MR TMR - sub arcsecond]
    F --> F1[Incremental Optical Encoder - quadrature ABZ]
    F --> F2[Absolute Optical Encoder - Gray code output]
    G --> G1[Resolver - rugged for aerospace]
    G --> G2[Inductive Encoder - Hiperface protocol]
    H --> H1[Capacitive Encoder - high humidity sensitive]
    D --> D1[Back EMF Detection BLDC above 10 percent speed]
    D --> D2[High Frequency Injection PMSM at zero speed]
    D --> D3[Flux Linkage Slope Method SRM at standstill]
```

### 4.2 SRM Closed-Loop Drive Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC[DC Bus Supply] --> INV[Asymmetric H Bridge Inverter]
    RPS[Rotor Position Sensor - Hall or Resolver] --> DSP[Controller DSP or Microcontroller]
    REF[Reference Speed and Torque] --> DSP
    DSP --> GATE[Gate Driver Circuit]
    GATE --> INV
    INV --> PH[SRM Stator Phases A B C]
    PH --> ROT[Rotor with Salient Poles]
    ROT -.shaft feedback.-> RPS
    PH -.current feedback.-> CS[Current Sensor]
    CS --> DSP
```

### 4.3 Torque Pulsation Sources and Mitigation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    TP[TORQUE PULSATION SOURCES] --> SRC1[Doubly Salient Geometry SRM]
    TP --> SRC2[Trapezoidal Back EMF BLDC]
    TP --> SRC3[Stator Slotting PMSM Cogging]
    TP --> SRC4[Magnetic Saturation]
    TP --> SRC5[Phase Commutation Overlap]
    MIT[Mitigation Techniques] --> M1[Skewing of rotor or stator poles]
    MIT --> M2[Fractional Slot Concentrated Windings]
    MIT --> M3[Current Profiling or Shaping]
    MIT --> M4[Phase Advance Angle Optimization]
    MIT --> M5[Direct Instantaneous Torque Control DITC]
    MIT --> M6[Iterative Learning Control ILC]
    MIT --> M7[Multi level Inverter for SRM]
    SRC1 --> MIT
    SRC2 --> MIT
    SRC3 --> MIT
    SRC4 --> MIT
    SRC5 --> MIT
```

### 4.4 Torque vs Rotor Angle — SRM With and Without Current Profiling

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S1[WITHOUT CURRENT PROFILING]
        A1[Theta 0 deg - Torque 0 Nm] --> A2[Theta 10 deg - Torque 5 Nm]
        A2 --> A3[Theta 20 deg - Torque 9 Nm]
        A3 --> A4[Theta 30 deg - Torque 4 Nm valley]
        A4 --> A5[Theta 40 deg - Torque 0 Nm]
    end
    subgraph S2[WITH PROFILED CURRENT]
        B1[Theta 0 deg - Torque 8 Nm] --> B2[Theta 10 deg - Torque 9 Nm]
        B2 --> B3[Theta 20 deg - Torque 9.5 Nm]
        B3 --> B4[Theta 30 deg - Torque 8.5 Nm]
        B4 --> B5[Theta 40 deg - Torque 8 Nm]
    end
```

The right subgraph shows the dramatic smoothing of torque profile achieved through current profiling — the mathematical heart of low-torque-ripple SRM drives.

### 4.5 Functional Architecture — Resolver-to-Digital Conversion

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Rotor Excitation Coil fed through Rotary Transformer] --> S1[Stator Sine Winding]
    R --> S2[Stator Cosine Winding]
    S1 --> ADC1[High Resolution ADC]
    S2 --> ADC2[High Resolution ADC]
    ADC1 --> DSP2[Resolver to Digital Converter RDC]
    ADC2 --> DSP2
    DSP2 --> DEMOD[Demodulation and Arctan Computation]
    DEMOD --> POS[Digital Position Word 12 to 16 bit]
    POS --> CTRL[Motor Controller]
```
