## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*List any three types of rotor position sensors used in special electrical machines. Compare them on the basis of resolution, ruggedness, and cost.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sensor Type | Resolution | Ruggedness | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hall Effect** | Low (60° elec/step) | Moderate | **Low** |
| **Optical Encoder** | High (down to 0.044°) | Low (dust-sensitive) | Medium |
| **Resolver** | High (16-bit ≈ 0.005°) | **High (200 °C, IP68)** | **High** |

(Each comparison row: 1 mark × 3 = 3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Define torque ripple factor. Mention two methods to reduce torque pulsations in a Switched Reluctance Motor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Torque Ripple Factor is the ratio of the difference between maximum and minimum instantaneous torque to the average torque, expressed as a percentage:

$$TRF = \frac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{T_{avg}} \times 100\%$$

**Mitigation methods (2 marks):**
1. **Current profiling / shaping** — varying the reference current profile within the commutation zone to compensate for $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ variation.
2. **Phase advance angle optimization** — selecting the optimal turn-on and turn-off angles $\theta_{on}$ and $\theta_{off}$ for the lowest TRF.
3. (Any other valid method — e.g., iterative learning control, multi-level inverter, skewed rotor poles — carries 1 additional mark for credit.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the working principle of a **Resolver** as a rotor position sensor. Derive the expression for the induced voltages in the sine and cosine windings. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the working of a **Hall Effect sensor** for rotor position sensing in a BLDC motor. State the position of three Hall sensors for 6-step trapezoidal commutation and sketch the Hall sensor output waveforms. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Resolver Working Principle (7 marks):**

A resolver is essentially a rotary transformer with a **single rotor winding** and **two stator windings** mechanically displaced by **90° electrical**.

- The rotor winding is excited through a **rotary transformer (brushless)** by a high-frequency carrier signal $V_0 \sin(\omega_c t)$, typically at **10 kHz**.
- The coupling between the rotor and the two stator windings is **modulated by the angular position** $\theta$ of the shaft.
- The two stator outputs are therefore:

$$V_{sin} = K \cdot V_0 \sin(\omega_c t) \sin(\theta)$$

$$V_{cos} = K \cdot V_0 \sin(\omega_c t) \cos(\theta)$$

**Derivation (3 marks):**
The flux linking the stator varies sinusoidally with rotor position. The mutual inductance between rotor coil and stator sine coil is $M_{sin} = M_0 \sin(\theta)$, and with cosine coil is $M_{cos} = M_0 \cos(\theta)$. By Faraday's law:

$$V_{sin} = -\frac{d}{dt}[M_{sin} \cdot i_{rotor}] = -M_0 \sin(\theta) \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{V_0 \sin(\omega_c t)}{R_{rotor}}\right]$$

After differentiation and amplification (gain $K$):

$$V_{sin} = K \cdot V_0 \omega_c \cos(\omega_c t) \sin(\theta) \cdot \frac{M_0}{R_{rotor}}$$

Absorbing constants into $K$:

$$V_{sin} = K \cdot V_0 \sin(\omega_c t) \sin(\theta) \quad \text{(similarly for cos)}$$

**Demodulation (2 marks):** Inside a Resolver-to-Digital Converter (RDC), the two outputs are multiplied by $\sin(\omega_c t)$, low-pass filtered, and the arctangent is computed:

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{sin,DC}}{V_{cos,DC}}\right)$$

**Advantages (1 mark):** Rugged, high-temperature operation (200 °C), immune to dust/oil/vibration — preferred in aerospace.

**(b) Hall Effect Sensor in BLDC (7 marks):**

**Working principle (2 marks):**
A Hall element is a thin semiconductor (GaAs, InSb) carrying a bias current $I_B$ along the x-axis. When a magnetic field $B$ is applied along the y-axis, the Lorentz force deflects charge carriers to the z-edge, producing a Hall voltage:

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I_B \cdot B}{t}$$

The Hall element is integrated with a Schmitt-trigger comparator and an open-collector output stage to give a **digital 0/1 logic** output. Internal circuitry (chopper-stabilized op-amp) eliminates temperature drift and offset.

**Placement for 6-step commutation (2 marks):**
For a 3-phase BLDC motor, **three Hall sensors are placed 120° electrical apart** on the non-driving end of the stator. They detect the rotor magnet polarity and produce six unique 3-bit codes (101, 100, 110, 010, 011, 001) — each code corresponding to one of the six 60° electrical commutation steps.

**Waveforms (2 marks):**
The Hall sensor outputs are three **square waves** $H_a, H_b, H_c$, each displaced by 120° electrical. At every transition of any Hall signal, the controller commutates to the next phase pair (e.g., AB → AC → BC → BA → CA → CB → AB). The output frequency of each Hall signal is:

$$f_H = \frac{P \cdot N}{60}$$

where $P$ = pole pairs, $N$ = speed in rpm.

**Commutation table (1 mark):** The six code-to-phase mappings are tabulated in standard BLDC drive datasheets (e.g., TI DRV8313).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the instantaneous electromagnetic torque expression for a Switched Reluctance Motor in the linear magnetic region. Explain why this machine inherently produces **torque pulsations**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a clear block diagram, explain the principle of **Direct Instantaneous Torque Control (DITC)** of an SRM. Show how it reduces torque ripple. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of SRM Torque (7 marks):**

**Co-energy approach (3 marks):**
For a single phase of the SRM, the magnetic co-energy in the linear region is:

$$W'_{co}(\theta, i) = \int_0^i \lambda(\theta, i') \, di' = \int_0^i L(\theta) \cdot i' \, di' = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$$

**Application of virtual work (2 marks):**
Mechanical work in a virtual displacement $d\theta$ at constant $i$:

$$dW_{mech} = T_e \, d\theta = dW'_{co} \Big|_{i = const} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} d\theta$$

Therefore:

$$T_e(\theta, i) = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Why torque pulsations are inherent (2 marks):**
- The function $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ is **non-uniform** over the electrical cycle — it has rising and falling regions separated by the aligned and unaligned positions where $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta} = 0$.
- During phase commutation, two phases share the torque production duty — but if their $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ profiles do not perfectly overlap, the **sum is non-constant**, producing dips and peaks.
- Magnetic saturation introduces additional harmonics in $L(\theta)$ that further ripple the torque.
- SRMs are **doubly salient** — rotor poles and stator poles are both discrete, so torque is intrinsically discrete rather than smooth.

**(b) DITC of SRM (7 marks):**

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
The DITC scheme consists of:
1. **Torque reference generator** — produces $T_{ref}$ from speed error via PI controller.
2. **Torque estimator** — computes instantaneous torque $T_{est} = \sum_k \tfrac{1}{2} i_k^2 \tfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial \theta}$ using measured phase currents and rotor position.
3. **Hysteresis comparator** — compares $T_{est}$ with $T_{ref}$ with a band $\Delta T$. Output is a switching command.
4. **Phase selection logic** — based on $\theta$, determines which phase(s) should be energized to produce torque.
5. **Inverter** — applies positive, negative, or zero voltage to each phase based on comparator output.

**Working principle (2 marks):**
- When $T_{est} < T_{ref} - \Delta T$: energize the rising-inductance phase → torque increases.
- When $T_{est} > T_{ref} + \Delta T$: apply freewheeling or demagnetize → torque decreases.
- The switching is done **independently for each phase** within a sampling period of typically **10–50 µs**.

**Torque ripple reduction (2 marks):**
- Conventional **current chopping control** maintains constant current but allows torque to vary with $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$. DITC directly regulates **torque**, not current.
- The hysteresis band $\Delta T$ is the design parameter — smaller $\Delta T$ → lower ripple, higher switching frequency.
- Achievable **TRF reduction**: from 30–50% (open-loop) to **5–10%** (DITC).
- A further improvement is **DITC + Iterative Learning Control (ILC)** which can bring TRF below **3%**.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes costing marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Writing the SRM torque formula with $i$ instead of $i^2$.** The square is crucial — it makes torque independent of current direction. Examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Confusing Hall sensor and optical encoder applications.** Hall sensors are placed on the **stator**; optical encoders are mounted on the **rotor shaft**.
> 3. **Drawing the Hall sensor output as a sine wave instead of a square wave.** Hall sensors are switching devices — they output **digital** levels.
> 4. **Omitting the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in the co-energy derivation.** Examiners award 1 mark specifically for the correct $\tfrac{1}{2}$.
> 5. **Writing "torque ripple" when the question asks for "torque pulsation."** These terms are interchangeable in KTU papers — but the formula symbol is $T_{ripple}$, not $T_{pul}$. Use the term requested.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the position requirement of Hall sensors** (120° electrical for 3-phase BLDC) — a frequent 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Rotor Position Sensors & Torque Pulsations**

- **Rotor Position Sensor (RPS):** mandatory for SRM, BLDC, trapezoidal PMSM; optional (vector control) for sinusoidal PMSM.
- **Hall sensor:** low cost, low resolution, 60° electrical per step; 3 sensors 120° apart for BLDC.
- **Optical encoder:** high resolution (4096+ PPR); outputs A, B (quadrature), Z (index).
- **Resolver:** rugged, 200 °C capable; outputs $V_{sin}$ and $V_{cos}$ with carrier modulation; demodulated by RDC chip.
- **Sensorless methods:** back-EMF (BLDC), HF injection (PMSM), $\tfrac{di}{dt}$ (SRM) — work only above certain speeds (except HF injection).
- **SRM torque formula (linear region):** $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \tfrac{dL}{d\theta}$.
- **SRM torque is independent of current polarity** — unipolar drive is sufficient.
- **Torque Ripple Factor (TRF):** $TRF = \tfrac{T_{max}-T_{min}}{T_{avg}} \times 100\%$.
- **Sources of torque pulsation:** doubly-salient structure (SRM), trapezoidal EMF (BLDC), slotting (PMSM), magnetic saturation, commutation overlap.
- **Mitigation techniques:** current profiling, phase angle optimization, skewing, fractional slot windings, DITC, ILC, multi-level inverter.
- **Hall output frequency:** $f_H = \tfrac{P \cdot N}{60}$.
- **Encoder resolution:** $\theta_{res} = \tfrac{360°}{N_{PPR}}$; ×4 with quadrature decoding.
- **Cogging frequency:** $f_{cogging} = \tfrac{LCM(S,P)}{S} f_{rot}$ Hz.
- **KTU 14-mark hot topics:** resolver working, Hall sensor placement, DITC block diagram, SRM torque derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sources of Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Special Electrical Machines** (Stepper motors, BLDC, PMSM, SRM, Linear motors, etc.), **Noise** refers to any unwanted acoustic, electromagnetic, or mechanical vibration energy radiated by the machine or its drive system into the surrounding environment, typically quantified by the **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** measured in decibels (dB) referenced to **20 µPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** *Noise in a special electrical machine is the aggregate of airborne pressure fluctuations generated by electromagnetic force harmonics, mechanical unbalance, bearing defects, aerodynamic turbulence, and power-electronic switching actions, which can degrade machine life, operator comfort, and precision in servo applications.*

Mathematically, the Sound Pressure Level is expressed as:

$$
L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) \;\; \text{dB}
$$

where $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ is the threshold of human hearing.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Washing Machine!**
> Imagine your washing machine during the **spin cycle**. Even though it has *no speaker*, you still *hear* it. Why?
> - The **unbalanced clothes** create a *mechanical vibration* (mechanical noise).
> - The **motor's torque ripple** causes the drum to *jerk* rhythmically (electromagnetic noise).
> - **Air being pushed** by the rotating drum makes a *whoosh* (aerodynamic noise).
> - The **VFD/inverter** controlling the motor emits a high-pitched *singing* tone (electronic/switching noise).
>
> A **Special Electrical Machine** (e.g., a Switched Reluctance Motor or a Stepper Motor) behaves identically. Even though it has *no audio transducer*, the internal physics — magnetic pulls, slotting, commutation, bearings — all conspire to "sing" at specific frequencies. Identifying *who* is singing and *at what frequency* is the essence of studying **Sources of Noise**.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Noise Sources (KTU High-Yield)

The **five canonical sources** of noise in special electrical machines (as per KTU 2024 Module-2 syllabus) are:

| S.No | Source Category | Physical Origin | Dominant In |
|:----:|:----------------|:----------------|:------------|
| 1 | **Electromagnetic (EM)** | Radial/tangential MMF harmonics, slotting, saturation | All rotating machines |
| 2 | **Mechanical** | Rotor unbalance, bearing defects, shaft misalignment, resonance | High-speed PMSM, BLDC |
| 3 | **Aerodynamic** | Air turbulence due to fan, rotor-end air, ventilation | Totally enclosed machines |
| 4 | **Electronic / Switching** | PWM carrier frequency, commutation transients | SRM, BLDC drives |
| 5 | **Magnetostrictive** | Core lamination strain under alternating flux | Transformer-coupled machines |

Standard benchmark SPL for industrial machines: **75–85 dB(A)**. For medical/precision: **<55 dB(A)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Noise Source

#### 2.1.1 Electromagnetic (EM) Noise — *The Dominant Source*

EM noise arises from **time-varying radial and tangential forces** between stator and rotor. The Maxwell stress tensor gives the radial force per unit area as:

$$
\sigma_r = \frac{B_r^2 - B_t^2}{2\mu_0}
$$

where $B_r$ and $B_t$ are the radial and tangential flux density components and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this cause noise?** The stator is essentially a *cylindrical shell*. When radial force waves of certain *spatial mode numbers* (m = 0, 2, 4, ...) match the structural mode shapes of the stator, **resonance** occurs and the stator "rings" like a bell — this is the principal cause of the *siren-like whine* heard in SRM and PMSM drives.

**Key EM Noise Generators:**

- **Slot Harmonics:** Permeance variations due to stator/rotor slots produce force harmonics of order:
  $$
  f_{\text{slot}} = 2 \cdot f_1 \cdot m \pm n \cdot N_s \cdot f_r
  $$
  where $f_1$ = supply frequency, $N_s$ = number of slots, $f_r$ = rotor frequency, $n$ = harmonic order, $m$ = spatial mode.

- **Cogging Torque (in PMSM/BLDC):** Interaction of rotor magnets with stator slot openings produces torque pulsation:
  $$
  T_{\text{cog}}(\theta) = -\frac{\partial W_{\text{airgap}}(\theta)}{\partial \theta}
  $$

- **Torque Ripple (in SRM):** Non-sinusoidal phase inductance causes pulsed radial force at every commutation instant.

- **Magnetic Saturation:** Distorts the air-gap flux density, generating **low-order even harmonics (2nd, 4th, 6th)** which excite strong acoustic modes.

---

#### 2.1.2 Mechanical Noise

Originating from:

- **Rotor unbalance:** $F_{\text{cent}} = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$ → produces a tonal at $1\times$ rotational frequency.
- **Bearing defects** (inner race, outer race, rolling element): characteristic frequencies given by:
  $$
  f_{\text{IR}} = \frac{N_b}{2} f_r \!\left(1 + \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right), \quad
  f_{\text{OR}} = \frac{N_b}{2} f_r \!\left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)
  $$
  where $N_b$ = number of balls, $d$ = ball diameter, $D$ = pitch diameter, $\alpha$ = contact angle.
- **Shaft misalignment / coupling faults.**
- **Rotor dynamics:** Critical speeds $\omega_{\text{crit}} = \sqrt{k/m}$ excite lateral vibrations.

---

#### 2.1.3 Aerodynamic Noise

Caused by:
- **Cooling fan blades** passing near obstructions → blade-pass frequency $f_{\text{bp}} = N_{\text{blades}} \cdot f_r$.
- **Air turbulence** in the air-gap and end-winding region.
- Aerodynamic noise scales roughly as $\propto v^5$ to $v^8$ (Lighthill's acoustic analogy); this becomes critical for **high-speed PMSM (>10,000 rpm)**.

---

#### 2.1.4 Electronic / Switching Noise (PWM-Induced)

- Inverter **PWM carrier frequency** $f_{\text{car}}$ (typically 2–20 kHz) excites the stator/lamination stack at that frequency and its sidebands.
- **dv/dt transients** during commutation cause common-mode shaft currents, leading to **bearing currents** and premature bearing damage (a noise source in disguise).
- The **d-q axis current ripple** from finite switching frequency also modulates the air-gap flux, contributing to EM noise.

---

#### 2.1.5 Magnetostrictive Noise

Ferromagnetic laminations *physically expand and contract* under alternating flux. The strain $\lambda_s$ per cycle causes the core to vibrate at $2f_1$ (twice supply frequency). This is dominant in **transformers** and **induction motor stators** but also contributes in **SRM and claw-pole machines**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — All Key Formulas for "Sources of Noise"**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:--|:--------|:--------|:--------------|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}} / 20\,\mu\text{Pa})$ | dB (SPL) |
| 2 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W / W_{\text{ref}})$, $W_{\text{ref}}=10^{-12}$ W | dB (SWL) |
| 3 | Relation $L_W - L_p$ | $L_W = L_p + 10 \log_{10}(4\pi r^2)$ at distance $r$ | dB |
| 4 | Radial force density (Maxwell) | $\sigma_r = (B_r^2 - B_t^2)/(2\mu_0)$ | N/m² |
| 5 | Total radial force | $F_r = \int_0^{2\pi} \sigma_r \, r \, L \, d\theta$ | N |
| 6 | Centrifugal force (unbalance) | $F_c = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$ | N |
| 7 | Bearing inner-race freq. | $f_{\text{IR}} = (N_b/2)\,f_r\,(1 + (d/D)\cos\alpha)$ | Hz |
| 8 | Bearing outer-race freq. | $f_{\text{OR}} = (N_b/2)\,f_r\,(1 - (d/D)\cos\alpha)$ | Hz |
| 9 | Slot harmonic force freq. | $f = 2 f_1 m \pm n N_s f_r$ | Hz |
| 10 | Blade-pass frequency | $f_{\text{bp}} = N_{\text{blades}} \cdot f_r$ | Hz |
| 11 | Magnetostriction frequency | $2 f_1$ (twice electrical frequency) | Hz |
| 12 | Lighthill's 8th-power rule | $L_p \propto \log_{10}(v^8)$ — high-speed aero | dB |
| 13 | PWM-induced vibration | $f_{\text{car}}, 2f_{\text{car}}, f_{\text{car}} \pm 2f_1$ | Hz |
| 14 | Total noise (incoherent sum) | $L_{\text{tot}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum_i 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | dB |
| 15 | Coherent sum (in-phase) | $L_{\text{tot}} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}(N)$ for $N$ equal sources | dB |

> **Pitfall:** Use `\vert` for absolute value (e.g., $\vert p_{\text{rms}}\vert$) to avoid LaTeX/markdown clashes.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Traction Motors (PMSM):** Acoustic comfort is a selling point → manufacturers skew rotors and use fractional-slot concentrated windings to push noise outside the 2–5 kHz sensitive band.
- **SRM Drives:** Inherently noisy due to saliency; modern designs use **random PWM**, **active vibration cancellation**, and **two-phase excitation** to mitigate.
- **Medical Pumps & Robotics:** Noise must be <50 dB(A) — drives source suppression through **sinusoidal BLDC** instead of trapezoidal, and **sleeve bearings** instead of ball bearings.
- **Aerospace Actuators:** Strict MIL-STD-810 vibration/noise compliance drives selection of **fractional-slot PMSM** and **slotless designs**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> For a descriptive topic like "Sources of Noise," KTU expects (a) rigorous derivations of the *fundamental noise-quantification relations* and (b) *analytical decomposition* of EM-force harmonics. Below are the two most important derivations a 2024-scheme student must master.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sound Pressure Level (SPL) from a Point Source

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of acoustic intensity.**
The instantaneous acoustic intensity (power per unit area) radiated by a sound wave is:

$$
I(t) = p(t) \cdot v(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the far-field plane-wave relation.**
For a plane wave in air, the particle velocity $v(t)$ is related to the acoustic pressure by the specific acoustic impedance of air $Z_0 = \rho_0 c \approx 413$ **Pa·s/m** (at 20°C):

$$
v(t) = \frac{p(t)}{Z_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute to obtain intensity in terms of pressure.**

$$
I(t) = \frac{p^2(t)}{Z_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Time-average over one period to get the RMS intensity.**

$$
\langle I \rangle = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{Z_0}
$$

**Step 5 — Define Sound Power Level referenced to $W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ W.**

$$
L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\langle I \rangle \cdot S}{W_{\text{ref}}}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — For a hemispherical radiator of radius $r$ (machine in a half-space, e.g., on a floor), $S = 2\pi r^2$.**

$$
L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2} \cdot 2\pi r^2}{Z_0 \cdot W_{\text{ref}}}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify numerically using $Z_0 = 413$ and $W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ to get the *direct* SPL formula at distance $r$:**

$$
L_p(r) = L_W - 10 \log_{10}(2\pi r^2)
$$

**Step 8 — Most direct form — reference $p_{\text{rms}}$ to $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$:**

$$
\boxed{\,L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{20\,\mu\text{Pa}}\right)\; \text{dB}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** A doubling of acoustic pressure corresponds to a **+6 dB** increase in SPL; a **10×** increase in intensity gives **+10 dB**. A 3 dB change is just *barely* perceptible to the human ear.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Radial Magnetic Force and the Origin of EM Noise

**Step 1 — Begin with the Maxwell Stress Tensor in the air-gap.**
The radial force per unit area acting on the stator surface is:

$$
\sigma_r(\theta,t) = \frac{1}{2\mu_0}\left[B_r^2(\theta,t) - B_t^2(\theta,t)\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Express the air-gap flux density as a Fourier series.**
For a machine with stator and rotor MMF distributions $F_s(\theta,t)$ and $F_r(\theta,t)$ acting on a permeance function $\Lambda(\theta,t)$:

$$
B(\theta,t) = F_s(\theta,t)\Lambda(\theta,t) + F_r(\theta,t)\Lambda(\theta,t)
$$

Substituting in spatial harmonics $n$ and time harmonics $k$:

$$
B_r(\theta,t) = \sum_n \sum_k \hat{B}_{n,k} \cos\!\left(n\theta - k\omega t - \phi_{n,k}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the square $B_r^2$ (dominant term, since $B_t \ll B_r$ in the air-gap).**
The product of two Fourier series of orders $n_1$ and $n_2$ produces **sum and difference** frequencies:

$$
B_r^2(\theta,t) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Force waves of mode } m = \vert n_1 \pm n_2 \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the dominant low-order force modes.**
The most dangerous (radiate most efficiently) are **m = 0** (uniform pressure — no noise, just mean stress) and **m = 2, 4, 6, ...** (elliptical, triangular, ... deformation modes of the stator).

The dominant radial force wave is:

$$
F_r(\theta,t) = \frac{rL}{2\mu_0}\sum_m \sum_k \hat{F}_{m,k}\cos\!\left(m\theta - k\omega t\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Resonant excitation criterion.**
If the **spatial mode** $m$ of a force wave equals a **stator natural frequency mode** AND the **temporal frequency** $k\omega$ matches that mode's natural frequency $\omega_{m,n}$:

$$
k\omega = \omega_{m,n}
$$

then **structural resonance** occurs and the machine "rings" loudly. This is the *physical root cause* of the characteristic whine in SRMs and PMSMs.

**Step 6 — Order of the dominant EM force harmonics:**

$$
f_{\text{force}} = 2 f_1 \pm n_r f_r
$$

where $f_1$ = fundamental electrical frequency, $n_r$ = order of rotor MMF harmonic, $f_r$ = mechanical rotor frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Skewing the rotor by **one stator slot pitch** cancels the $m = \pm N_s$ force wave — this is why most induction motors are skewed. In **PMSM**, fractional-slot/pole combinations (e.g., 10/12, 8/6) are chosen to push the lowest acoustic mode above the audible range.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Frequency Mapping of Noise Sources

This matrix helps *diagnose* a noisy machine by mapping the symptom (audible tone) to its physical cause.

| Symptomatic Frequency | Likely Source | Diagnostic Test | Mitigation |
|:----------------------|:--------------|:----------------|:-----------|
| $1\times$ rotor speed ($f_r$) | Mechanical unbalance | Vibration test, phase-angle plot | Re-balance rotor |
| $2\times f_1$ | Magnetostriction, mains hum | Power-off test | Use grain-oriented steel, clamp laminations |
| $N_s \cdot f_r$ | Slot harmonics | FFT of current; run at no-load | Skew rotor, fractional slot winding |
| $f_{\text{car}}$, $2f_{\text{car}}$ | PWM switching | Modulate carrier, listen | Use random/space-vector PWM, raise $f_{\text{car}}$ above 20 kHz |
| $N_{\text{blades}} \cdot f_r$ | Aerodynamic | Visual + run without fan | Optimize fan blade profile, use quieter blower |
| Bearing characteristic freqs. | Bearing wear | Shock-pulse, envelope analysis | Replace bearing, switch to ceramic hybrid |
| Commutation freq. (PMSM/SRM) | Torque ripple | Torsional accelerometer | Use advanced commutation, current shaping |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Because the topic "Sources of Noise" is fundamentally a **physics-of-vibration** topic, physical drawings (stator deformation modes, flux maps) are best represented here as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** that traces noise from its physical origin to the radiated acoustic field.

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Noise Generation in a Special Electrical Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Electrical Energy V I f] --> B[Power Electronic Converter PWM Inverter]
    B --> C[Machine Stator Winding Currents]
    C --> D[Air Gap MMF and Flux Distribution]
    D --> E[Electromagnetic Force Waves Radial and Tangential]
    E --> F{Stator Structural Mode Match?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Structural Resonance Vibration of Stator Yoke]
    F -- No  --> H[Damped Oscillation Low Amplitude]
    G --> I[Acoustic Pressure Fluctuation in Air]
    H --> I

    J[Rotor Unbalance Mass Eccentricity] --> K[Mechanical Vibration Bearing Housing]
    L[Bearing Defects Inner Outer Race] --> K
    M[Shaft Misalignment Coupling] --> K
    K --> I

    N[Cooling Fan Rotor End Region] --> O[Aerodynamic Turbulence]
    O --> I

    P[Core Lamination Magnetostriction] --> Q[2f1 Vibration of Core Stack]
    Q --> I

    I --> R[Microphone SPL Meter dB A Weighted]
    R --> S[Operator Perception and KTU Compliance Check]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Tree Diagram — Classification of Noise Sources

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Sources of Noise in Special Electrical Machines]
    R --> R1[Electromagnetic]
    R --> R2[Mechanical]
    R --> R3[Aerodynamic]
    R --> R4[Electronic Switching]
    R --> R5[Magnetostrictive]

    R1 --> R1A[Slot Harmonics]
    R1 --> R1B[Saturation Harmonics]
    R1 --> R1C[Saliency Torque Ripple SRM]
    R1 --> R1D[Cogging Torque PMSM BLDC]

    R2 --> R2A[Rotor Unbalance]
    R2 --> R2B[Bearing Defects]
    R2 --> R2C[Resonance at Critical Speed]

    R3 --> R3A[Fan Blade Pass Frequency]
    R3 --> R3B[End Winding Air Turbulence]

    R4 --> R4A[PWM Carrier Tone]
    R4 --> R4B[Common Mode Shaft Current]
    R4 --> R4C[Commutation Transient]

    R5 --> R5A[Core Lamination Strain at 2f1]
```

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Stator Deformation Modes (Vibration Pattern Visualization)

```
       m = 0  (Circular)         m = 2  (Elliptical)         m = 3  (Triangular)
       ┌───────────┐             ┌───────────┐               ┌───────────┐
      /             \           ╱             ╲             ╱╲           ╱╲
     |   (uniform)  |         ╱   ┌───────┐    ╲           │   ╲       ╱   │
      \             /         ╲   │       │    ╱           │    ╲     ╱    │
       └───────────┘           ╲  └───────┘   ╱             │     ╲   ╱     │
        No acoustic radiation  Acoustically active!        Strongly active!
```

> **Note:** Mode $m=0$ is non-radiating (breathing); $m=2, 3, 4$ are the principal *acoustic* modes whose frequencies must be avoided when designing the switching frequency of SRM/BLDC drives.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing SPL with SWL:** SPL ($L_p$) uses $20\log$, SWL ($L_W$) uses $10\log$. Marks deducted for mixing them.
> 2. **Forgetting the 20 µPa reference** in the SPL formula — 2-mark penalty in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Slot harmonic order vs. frequency:** Many students write $n \cdot N_s \cdot f_1$ when the correct one is $n \cdot N_s \cdot f_r$ (rotor frequency, *not* electrical).
> 4. **Acoustic mode m=0 is non-radiating** — students often wrongly cite it as the loudest mode.
> 5. **Unit mistakes:** `Hz` for force waves, `dB(A)` for SPL (note the A-weighting suffix for human-ear-weighted SPL).
> 6. **Bearing frequencies:** Don't confuse ball-pass-frequency-inner (BPFI) with the simpler $N_b f_r$.

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
Define *Sound Pressure Level* and state its formula. What is the standard reference pressure?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is a logarithmic measure of the RMS acoustic pressure of a sound wave, referenced to the threshold of human hearing.
> **Formula:** $\;L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(p_{\text{rms}} / p_{\text{ref}}\right)\;$ dB, where $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$.
> **Reference:** $20\,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa (threshold of hearing at 1 kHz). *(1 mark each for definition, formula, reference — total 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List any **four** sources of noise in a Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM).

> **Model Answer (3 marks — ¾ mark each, any 4):**
> 1. **Radial magnetic force waves** due to high saliency and abrupt commutation.
> 2. **Torque ripple** (pulsating single-polarity torque).
> 3. **PWM switching frequency** of the asymmetric H-bridge drive.
> 4. **Stator-yoke resonance** at specific spatial modes (m = 2, 4, 6).
> 5. **Cooling fan aerodynamic noise.**
> 6. **Bearing noise** at high speeds.
> *(Full 3 marks if any 4 are correctly identified and named.)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]

> **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **five major sources of noise** in special electrical machines. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Derive the expression for **Sound Pressure Level** in dB and explain the difference between **coherent and incoherent** addition of multiple noise sources. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five major sources of noise (7 marks):**

1. **Electromagnetic (2 marks):** Radial force waves of mode $m = 2, 4, 6, \ldots$ generated by interaction of stator/rotor MMF harmonics with slot permeance variations; excite stator resonance → the characteristic *whine* of SRM/PMSM.
2. **Mechanical (1.5 marks):** Rotor unbalance, bearing defects (BPFI/BPFO tones), shaft misalignment, structural resonance at critical speeds.
3. **Aerodynamic (1 mark):** Fan blade-pass frequency $f_{\text{bp}} = N_{\text{blades}} f_r$; air-gap/end-winding turbulence — scales as $v^8$ (Lighthill).
4. **Electronic / Switching (1.5 marks):** PWM carrier and sidebands $f_{\text{car}} \pm 2f_1$; commutation transients; bearing currents.
5. **Magnetostrictive (1 mark):** Lamination strain at $2f_1$ due to magnetostriction of grain-oriented steel.

*(Marks awarded for clarity, completeness, and a labelled block diagram.)*

**(b) SPL derivation + coherent vs. incoherent addition (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in §3.1 of these notes —

$$
I = p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}/Z_0, \quad L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}} / 20\,\mu\text{Pa})\;\text{dB}
$$

Show all steps 1–8 as in §3.1.

**Coherent vs Incoherent Addition (3 marks):**

- **Incoherent** (uncorrelated sources, the usual case in machines):
  $$
  L_{\text{tot}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10} + \cdots\right)
  $$
  Two equal **incoherent** 80 dB sources → $L_{\text{tot}} = 83$ dB (only +3 dB).

- **Coherent** (perfectly in-phase, same frequency, e.g., 2 fans in lockstep):
  $$
  L_{\text{tot}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/20} + 10^{L_2/20}\right) = L_1 + 10\log_{10}(N) = 80 + 3 = 83\;\text{dB}
  $$

  Wait — for $N=2$ *equal* coherent sources: $L_{\text{tot}} = L_1 + 20\log_{10}(2) = 80 + 6 = 86$ dB (a **+6 dB** doubling). Highlight this distinction: coherent summing gives **+6 dB** per doubling; incoherent gives **+3 dB**. This is a frequently-asked KTU follow-up.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set C]

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **electromagnetic sources of noise** in a PMSM/BLDC motor. Discuss *cogging torque* and *slot harmonics* with relevant mathematical expressions. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** What are the **bearing defect frequencies** (BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF)? A 4-pole, 50 Hz induction machine has 9 balls in its bearing, ball diameter 12 mm, pitch diameter 60 mm, and runs at 1450 rpm. Compute BPFI. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EM noise in PMSM/BLDC (7 marks):**

- **Cogging Torque (3 marks):** Interaction of rotor magnets with stator slot openings produces a torque that *varies with rotor position*, even at zero current:
  $$
  T_{\text{cog}}(\theta) = -\frac{\partial W(\theta)}{\partial\theta}, \quad W(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\!\int F^2(\theta)\,d\Lambda(\theta)
  $$
  Mitigation: skewing, fractional-slot windings, auxiliary slots.

- **Slot Harmonics (3 marks):** Stator slotting creates a permeance variation:
  $$
  \Lambda(\theta) = \Lambda_0 + \sum_n \Lambda_n \cos(n N_s \theta)
  $$
  Multiplying with rotor MMF yields sideband force harmonics at:
  $$
  f = f_1 \pm n N_s f_r
  $$
  Mitigation: magnetic slot wedges, semi-closed slots, rotor skew.

- **Radial Force Resonance (1 mark):** Force waves of mode $m = N_s$ or $2N_s$ often match the stator's 2nd or 3rd structural mode.

**(b) Bearing defect frequencies (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **BPFO** (Ball Pass Frequency Outer) = $f_{\text{OR}}$
- **BPFI** (Ball Pass Frequency Inner) = $f_{\text{IR}}$
- **BSF** (Ball Spin Frequency) = $\dfrac{D}{2d}\,f_r\!\left(1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)^{\!2}\right)$
- **FTF** (Fundamental Train Frequency) = $\dfrac{1}{2}\,f_r\!\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)$

**BPFI calculation (5 marks):**

Given: $N_b = 9$, $d = 12$ mm, $D = 60$ mm, $f_r = 1450/60 = 24.167$ Hz, assume $\alpha = 0$ (radial contact).

$$
\text{BPFI} = \frac{N_b}{2}\,f_r\!\left(1 + \frac{d}{D}\right) = \frac{9}{2}\times 24.167 \times \left(1 + \frac{12}{60}\right)
$$

$$
= 4.5 \times 24.167 \times 1.20 = 4.5 \times 29.0 = \mathbf{130.5\;\text{Hz}}
$$

*(Full 5 marks for the substitution, intermediate step, and final answer in Hz.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS**

- ✅ Noise in special machines is a **multi-physics** problem: EM + mechanical + aero + electronic + magnetostrictive.
- ✅ **SPL** uses **20 log** of pressure ratio; **SWL** uses **10 log** of power ratio.
- ✅ Standard reference: $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$; $W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}$ W.
- ✅ **+3 dB** = doubling of *acoustic power*; **+6 dB** = doubling of *acoustic pressure* (or 4× power).
- ✅ **+10 dB** is perceived as *twice as loud* by a human listener.
- ✅ **EM noise** = radial force waves of mode $m = 2, 4, 6, \ldots$ — the $m = 0$ mode does *not* radiate.
- ✅ **Slot harmonic force** frequency: $f = 2f_1 m \pm n N_s f_r$.
- ✅ **Blade-pass frequency:** $f_{\text{bp}} = N_{\text{blades}} \cdot f_r$.
- ✅ **BPFO** = $(N_b/2)\,f_r\,(1 - (d/D)\cos\alpha)$; **BPFI** uses $(+)$ sign.
- ✅ **Aerodynamic noise** scales as $v^5$ to $v^8$ — critical for high-speed PMSM.
- ✅ **Magnetostriction** is at $2f_1$ — twice the supply frequency.
- ✅ **PWM carrier** and its sidebands ($f_{\text{car}} \pm 2f_1$) cause inverter-driven acoustic noise.
- ✅ **Cogging torque** is independent of current; **torque ripple** is current-dependent.
- ✅ Skewing by one slot pitch cancels the lowest slot harmonic.
- ✅ **Fractional-slot concentrated windings** in PMSM push noise to non-audible mode orders.
- ✅ **SRM is the noisiest** special machine due to saliency and abrupt commutation.
- ✅ **Total SPL** (incoherent): $L_{\text{tot}} = 10\log\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$.
- ✅ **Total SPL** (coherent, $N$ equal sources): $L_{\text{tot}} = L_1 + 20\log_{10}(N)$.
- ✅ **Mitigation toolkit:** rotor skew, fractional slot/pole, random PWM, magnetic slot wedges, sleeved bearings, optimized fan blade profile, active vibration cancellation, COM (common-mode) chokes, ceramic-hybrid bearings.
- ✅ **Standard compliance:** ISO 3744 (industrial), MIL-STD-810 (defense), IEC 60034-9 (rotating machines).
- ✅ **A-weighting (dB(A))** reflects the human ear's frequency response — always use dB(A) for *perceived* noise, not raw dB.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Mitigation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Noise Mitigation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Noise Mitigation Techniques** in the context of Special Electrical Machines (SRM, BLDC, PMSM, Stepper, Linear, Axial Flux machines) refer to the comprehensive set of **electromagnetic, mechanical, structural, and control-oriented design strategies** employed to suppress, attenuate, or eliminate unwanted acoustic emissions generated during the operation of the machine.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PEEET743 - Special Electrical Machines (Module 2)*, the acoustic noise in these machines is primarily classified into three principal categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 Focus)**
> *Noise in special machines arises from three principal sources:*
> 1. **Magnetic Noise** — caused by time-varying radial and tangential electromagnetic forces acting on the stator core, producing vibration of the lamination stack and the surrounding air, which is radiated as airborne sound.
> 2. **Mechanical Noise** — originating from bearings, rotor unbalance, brush friction (in universal/AC series motors), and commutator/segment interactions.
> 3. **Aerodynamic Noise** — produced by the rotating cooling fan, air turbulence through ventilation ducts, and slot openings.

The **A-weighted Sound Pressure Level (SPL)**, measured in **dB(A)**, is the standard KTU-board-recommended metric for quantifying acoustic emission, expressed as:

$$\text{SPL}_{dB(A)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{0}}\right)$$

where $p_{rms}$ is the RMS sound pressure and $p_{0} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ is the **threshold of human hearing** (ISO 389-7 reference).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Drumming Bucket**
> Imagine tapping an empty metal bucket with a spoon. The bucket vibrates and produces a loud **drumming sound**. Now, perform three actions:
> 1. **Stuff a wet towel inside the bucket** (analogous to *mechanical damping* and slot wedges).
> 2. **Wrap the outside with a thick rubber sheet** (analogous to *acoustic enclosure and lamination clamping*).
> 3. **Tap the bucket softly and irregularly instead of rhythmically** (analogous to *PWM random modulation and current profiling*).
>
> The sound reduces dramatically. **Noise mitigation in special machines works on the exact same three principles** — damp the source, isolate the path, and smoothen the excitation.

## 1.3 Sources of Noise — Quick Reference

| Noise Source | Frequency Range | Dominant In |
|---|---|---|
| Radial force harmonics ($2f_s, 4f_s, \ldots$) | 100 Hz – 5 kHz | SRM, PMSM, BLDC |
| Torque ripple frequency | $f_{ripple} = m \cdot f_s$ | SRM, BLDC |
| PWM switching noise | 2 – 20 kHz | All inverter-fed machines |
| Slot ripple / cogging | $f_{slot} = Z \cdot f_{rot}$ | PMSM, BLDC |
| Bearing vibration | 200 Hz – 2 kHz | Universal, high-speed machines |
| Aerodynamic (fan) | 500 Hz – 4 kHz | All machines with fans |
| Magnetostriction | 100 – 1000 Hz | All iron-core machines |

where $f_s$ = supply/stator frequency, $m$ = order of harmonic, $Z$ = number of slots, $f_{rot}$ = mechanical rotational frequency.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick-Recall:** In KTU valuation, if a 3-mark question asks "List the sources of noise in special machines," always present the answer in the **Magnetic / Mechanical / Aerodynamic** triad format — this is the board-preferred classification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of Magnetic Noise Generation

The fundamental physics underlying magnetic noise in special machines follows this **causal chain**:

1. **Time-varying currents** in the stator winding produce a **time-varying air-gap flux density** $B(\theta, t)$.
2. The interaction of this flux with the ferromagnetic core produces a **Maxwell Stress Tensor** distribution on the stator bore surface.
3. The **radial component of this stress** (dominant) and the **tangential component** (produces torque ripple) create **distributed dynamic forces**.
4. These forces excite **structural modes of vibration** in the stator yoke, lamination stack, and end-windings.
5. The vibrating surfaces radiate **acoustic pressure waves** into the surrounding air.

The **radial force per unit area** (Maxwell Stress) at the air-gap surface is given by:

$$p_r(\theta, t) = \frac{B_r^2(\theta, t) - B_t^2(\theta, t)}{2\mu_0} \approx \frac{B^2(\theta, t)}{2\mu_0}$$

where:
- $B_r$ = radial flux density component
- $B_t$ = tangential flux density component
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$ = **permeability of free space**

For small air-gap machines (where $B_t \ll B_r$), the approximation $p_r \approx B^2 / 2\mu_0$ holds, and the **dominant forcing frequency** is $2f$ (twice the electrical excitation frequency) because the squaring operation doubles the frequency.

## 2.2 Structural Mechanics of Stator Vibration

The stator yoke behaves as a **cylindrical shell** with natural modes characterized by mode numbers $(m, n)$ where:
- $m$ = circumferential mode number (number of nodal diameters)
- $n$ = axial mode number (number of nodal circles)

The **natural frequency of mode $(m,n)$** of a cylindrical shell is approximated by:

$$f_{m,n} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{K_{m,n}}{M_{m,n}}}$$

where $K_{m,n}$ is the modal stiffness and $M_{m,n}$ is the modal mass of the stator yoke assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resonance Phenomenon:** Maximum acoustic noise occurs when the **electromagnetic forcing frequency** coincides with a **stator natural frequency**. This is why KTU board questions often ask: *"Why is cogging torque reduction important for noise control?"* — because cogging harmonics can excite structural resonances.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $\text{SPL} = 20\log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$ | **dB(A)** | Acoustic noise quantification |
| 2 | Reference pressure | $p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ | **Pa** | ISO 389-7 threshold |
| 3 | Radial force density | $p_r = B^2/2\mu_0$ | **N/m²** | Maxwell stress analysis |
| 4 | Dominant forcing frequency | $f_{force} = 2 f_s$ | **Hz** | Magnetic noise fundamental |
| 5 | Slot ripple frequency | $f_{slot} = Z \cdot f_{rot}$ | **Hz** | Cogging & slot harmonics |
| 6 | Torque ripple frequency | $f_{T} = m \cdot f_s$ | **Hz** | SRM, BLDC torque pulsation |
| 7 | A-weighted correction | $A(f)$ per IEC 61672 | **dB** | Human-ear response weighting |
| 8 | Vibration velocity | $v = \omega \cdot X$ | **m/s** | Surface vibration level |
| 9 | Vibration displacement | $X = F / (m\omega_n^2) \cdot (1 - r^2)^{-1}$ | **m** | Resonance response |
| 10 | Power spectral density | $S_{pp}(f) = \vert P(f) \vert^2 / \Delta f$ | **Pa²/Hz** | Frequency-domain noise analysis |
| 11 | Insertion loss (enclosure) | $IL = 10\log_{10}(W_{in}/W_{out})$ | **dB** | Acoustic enclosure effectiveness |
| 12 | Stator natural frequency (mode $m$) | $f_{m,n} \propto \frac{m(m^2-1)}{\sqrt{m^2+1}} \cdot \frac{t}{D^2}$ | **Hz** | Lamination design |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Per the KTU V10 protocol, vertical bar notation uses `\vert` (e.g., $S_{pp}(f) = \vert P(f) \vert^2 / \Delta f$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Noise mitigation is **not a luxury** in modern special-machine applications:

- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** BLDC/PMSM traction motors are mounted close to passengers; NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) targets demand **< 60 dB(A)** cabin noise.
- **HVAC & White Goods:** SRM and BLDC compressors in refrigerators/air-conditioners must comply with **IEC 60704** standards.
- **Medical Equipment:** Stepper/servo motors in MRI, surgical robots demand **< 40 dB(A)**.
- **Drones & Robotics:** High-speed BLDC motors require PWM silent-drive techniques to meet aviation/consumer standards.
- **Industrial Servo Drives:** ISO 10816 vibration standards mandate strict limits on machine tool spindle motors.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Radial Force Wave from Air-Gap Field

### Given:
A simplified 2-pole SRM/BLDC with air-gap flux density:
$$B(\theta, t) = B_0 \cos(\omega t - p\theta)$$

where $p$ = number of pole pairs, $\omega = 2\pi f_s$ = electrical angular frequency.

### To Derive:
The radial pressure distribution $p_r(\theta, t)$ on the stator bore.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Write the Maxwell stress expression at the air-gap surface. The radial pressure is:

$$p_r(\theta, t) = \frac{B^2(\theta, t)}{2\mu_0}$$

*Conversion logic:* The Maxwell Stress Tensor reduces to $B^2/2\mu_0$ when the tangential field is negligible — valid for small air gaps in machines.

**Step 2:** Substitute the flux expression:

$$p_r(\theta, t) = \frac{1}{2\mu_0} \left[B_0 \cos(\omega t - p\theta)\right]^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos^2(x) = \frac{1 + \cos(2x)}{2}$:

$$p_r(\theta, t) = \frac{B_0^2}{4\mu_0} \left[1 + \cos(2\omega t - 2p\theta)\right]$$

**Step 4:** Separate the **steady (DC) component** and the **time-varying (oscillatory) component**:

$$p_r(\theta, t) = \underbrace{\frac{B_0^2}{4\mu_0}}_{\text{Static preload}} + \underbrace{\frac{B_0^2}{4\mu_0}\cos(2\omega t - 2p\theta)}_{\text{Forcing wave}}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the **forcing frequency** and **spatial mode number**:

- Forcing frequency: $f_{force} = 2f_s$ (twice the supply frequency)
- Spatial mode number: $m = 2p$ (twice the pole-pairs)
- Peak amplitude: $p_{r,\max} = B_0^2 / (4\mu_0)$

### Result:

$$\boxed{p_r(\theta, t) = \frac{B_0^2}{4\mu_0}\left[1 + \cos(2\omega t - 2p\theta)\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The **static term** $B_0^2/4\mu_0$ produces a uniform pre-stress on the stator (silent). The **oscillatory term** $\cos(2\omega t - 2p\theta)$ is the source of vibration and noise. Its amplitude is $B_0^2/4\mu_0$, so **halving the flux density** $B_0$ reduces the noise pressure by **6 dB** (because $20\log_{10}(1/2) \approx -6$ dB).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sound Pressure Level for a Piston Source

### Given:
A flat stator end surface vibrating with RMS velocity $v_{rms}$ and area $A$.

### To Derive:
The radiated sound power and SPL at distance $r$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** The acoustic power radiated by a vibrating piston (Kirchhoff-Helmholtz integral) for a baffled piston with $ka \ll 1$ (low-frequency approximation, $k = 2\pi/\lambda$, $a$ = piston radius) is:

$$W_{acoustic} = \rho_0 c_0 S \langle v^2 \rangle_{rms}$$

where $\rho_0 \approx 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (air density), $c_0 \approx 343 \, \text{m/s}$ (speed of sound), $S$ = piston area.

**Step 2:** Substitute $W = \rho_0 c_0 S v_{rms}^2$:

**Step 3:** The intensity at distance $r$ from a point source (free field) is:

$$I = \frac{W_{acoustic}}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{\rho_0 c_0 S v_{rms}^2}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 4:** The RMS sound pressure relates to intensity by $I = p_{rms}^2 / (\rho_0 c_0)$:

$$p_{rms}^2 = \rho_0 c_0 I = \frac{(\rho_0 c_0)^2 S v_{rms}^2}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 5:** Take square root and form the SPL in dB:

$$p_{rms} = \frac{\rho_0 c_0 \sqrt{S} \cdot v_{rms}}{2\sqrt{\pi} \cdot r}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the Sound Pressure Level:

$$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\rho_0 c_0 \sqrt{S} \cdot v_{rms}}{2\sqrt{\pi} \cdot r \cdot p_0}\right)$$

### Numerical Illustration:

For a typical BLDC motor: $S = 0.02 \, \text{m}^2$, $v_{rms} = 1 \, \text{mm/s} = 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s}$, $r = 1 \, \text{m}$:

$$p_{rms} = \frac{(1.2)(343)\sqrt{0.02}(10^{-3})}{2\sqrt{\pi}(1)(20 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 1.30 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1.30}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \approx 96.3 \, \text{dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** A common student error is to use the peak pressure instead of RMS. Always use $p_{rms}$ for SPL calculation, otherwise the answer is wrong by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ (≈ 3 dB).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Noise Sources vs. Mitigation Techniques

| Noise Source | Physical Origin | Mitigation Technique | Effectiveness | KTU Board Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Radial force harmonics** | $B^2/2\mu_0$ interaction | Skewing, fractional slot/pole | ★★★★★ | **High** |
| **Cogging torque** | Slot-pole MMF interaction | Magnet skewing, slotless design | ★★★★ | **High** |
| **Torque ripple (SRM)** | Discrete phase excitation | Two-stage commutation, current shaping | ★★★★★ | **High** |
| **PWM switching noise** | Inverter hard switching | Random PWM, SVPWM, soft switching | ★★★★ | **Medium** |
| **Bearing noise** | Mechanical raceway defects | Ceramic hybrid bearings, preload tuning | ★★★ | **Low** |
| **Aerodynamic noise** | Fan blade tip vortex | Skewed/bladed fan, no-fan design | ★★★ | **Low** |
| **Magnetostriction** | Iron core dimensional change | High-Si steel, amorphous cores | ★★ | **Low** |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Design Procedure for Skew Angle (Anti-Cogging)

### Given:
PMSM with $Z = 12$ slots, $2p = 8$ poles (i.e., $p = 4$).

### To Find:
The rotor magnet skew angle $\theta_{skew}$ (in mechanical degrees) that minimizes cogging torque.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Identify the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of slots and poles:
$$\text{LCM}(Z, 2p) = \text{LCM}(12, 8) = 24$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **cogging period** in mechanical degrees (one full cycle of cogging):
$$\theta_{cog} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\text{LCM}(Z, 2p)} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{24} = 15^{\circ}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **cogging frequency** (per mechanical revolution):
$$N_{cog} = \frac{\text{LCM}(Z, 2p)}{2p} = \frac{24}{8} = 3 \text{ cogging cycles/rev}$$

**Step 4:** The optimal skew to **completely cancel the fundamental cogging component** is:
$$\theta_{skew,opt} = \frac{\theta_{cog}}{2} = \frac{15^{\circ}}{2} = 7.5^{\circ} \text{ (mechanical)}$$

**Step 5:** For a multi-pole rotor with $2p$ poles, the **electrical skew** is:
$$\theta_{skew,elec} = p \cdot \theta_{skew,opt} = 4 \times 7.5^{\circ} = 30^{\circ} \text{ (electrical)}$$

### Result:

$$\boxed{\theta_{skew,opt} = \frac{180^{\circ}}{p \cdot \text{LCM}(Z, 2p)} = \frac{180^{\circ}}{4 \times 24} = 1.875^{\circ} \text{ (electrical, alternative form)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** Always express skew in **mechanical degrees for the rotor** and clearly state the LCM calculation — this is the most frequently asked 7-mark derivation question in KTU Module 2.
