# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Classify the different sources of acoustic noise in switched reluctance motors. Mention any two control techniques to reduce the same.**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines, 3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sources of noise in SRM:**
> 1. **Magnetic noise** — caused by rapidly changing radial forces on the stator pole surfaces during phase commutation. The dominant forcing frequency is $2f_s$ and its harmonics, which can excite stator natural modes.
> 2. **Mechanical noise** — due to bearing defects, rotor eccentricity, and lamination vibrations.
> 3. **Aerodynamic noise** — from the cooling fan and air turbulence through the housing vents.
>
> **Control techniques to reduce noise:**
> 1. **Two-stage (soft) commutation** — splitting the demagnetisation voltage $V_{dm}$ into two levels to reduce the $di/dt$ and hence the rate of change of radial force.
> 2. **Current profiling / chopping** — shaping the phase current to follow a smooth sinusoid or cubic trajectory, minimising current ripple that drives radial vibration.
> 3. **Random PWM frequency modulation** — spreading the switching energy over a wide frequency band to reduce tonal peaks.

*(Distribution: 1 mark classification, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark for control techniques — typical KTU 3-mark scheme.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is cogging torque? How does rotor magnet skewing reduce cogging torque and associated noise in a PMSM?**

**Model Answer (10–12 lines, 3 marks):**

> **Cogging torque** is the unwanted torque ripple produced in a PMSM due to the **interaction between the rotor permanent magnets and the stator slot openings**, even when no current flows in the winding. It arises because the permeance of the air gap varies with rotor position, creating a tendency for the rotor to lock at preferred (minimum-reluctance) positions.
>
> **Cogging torque frequency (cycles per mechanical revolution):**
>
> $$N_{cog} = \frac{\text{LCM}(Z, 2p)}{2p}$$
>
> **Rotor magnet skewing** shifts the axial alignment of the magnets so that different axial slices "see" different slotting positions, **averaging the cogging waveform to near zero**. The optimal skew angle (in mechanical degrees) is half the cogging period:
>
> $$\theta_{skew,opt} = \frac{180^{\circ}}{\text{LCM}(Z, 2p)}$$
>
> Since cogging torque is a major excitation source of vibration, its reduction translates directly to **lower acoustic noise** in the audible band (1–5 kHz).

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark formula/application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**a)** Derive the expression for the radial force density on the stator bore of a special electrical machine using Maxwell's stress tensor. Explain why the dominant forcing frequency is $2f_s$. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various **control techniques** employed to mitigate the acoustic noise in a switched reluctance motor. Compare the effectiveness of **two-stage commutation** and **random PWM modulation** in reducing noise. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State Maxwell's Stress Tensor for the air-gap interface. The time-averaged radial force per unit area (Maxwell stress) on a ferromagnetic surface bordering an air gap is:

$$p_r = \frac{B_r^2 - B_t^2}{2\mu_0}$$

where $B_r$ and $B_t$ are the radial and tangential air-gap flux density components respectively, and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the small air-gap approximation. For typical special machines (SRM, BLDC, PMSM) where the air-gap length $g \ll$ rotor radius, the tangential field $B_t \ll B_r$, so:

$$p_r \approx \frac{B_r^2(\theta, t)}{2\mu_0}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Assume a single-frequency air-gap flux wave for analytical clarity:

$$B_r(\theta, t) = B_0 \cos(\omega t - p\theta)$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Substitute and apply the identity $\cos^2(x) = (1 + \cos 2x)/2$:

$$p_r(\theta, t) = \frac{B_0^2}{4\mu_0} + \frac{B_0^2}{4\mu_0}\cos(2\omega t - 2p\theta)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Identify the two components — a **static preload** (first term) and a **travelling force wave** of amplitude $B_0^2/4\mu_0$ with spatial mode $2p$ and temporal frequency $2\omega$.

**Step 6 (1.5 marks):** Explain the **$2f_s$ dominance**: Because the force is proportional to the *square* of the flux density (which is itself sinusoidal at $f_s$), the product generates a frequency component at $2f_s$ via the trigonometric identity. This is a **non-linear effect of squaring**, and it makes the $2f_s$ component the most powerful forcing frequency, capable of exciting the lowest-order stator mode $(m = 2p, n = 0)$ and producing the loudest "magnetic whine."

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks:

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Position Sensor] → [Controller] → [Turn-On / Turn-Off Angle Generator] → [Asymmetric H-Bridge] → [SRM Phase] → [Vibration / Noise]
                                              ↑
                              [Two-Stage Commutation Logic]
                              [Current Profiling Algorithm]
                              [Random PWM Modulator]
```

**Two-Stage Commutation (2.5 marks):**

- Instead of applying full negative DC-link voltage $(-V_{dc})$ during demagnetisation, the controller applies a **reduced voltage** (e.g., $0$ V or $V_{dc}/2$) initially, then ramps to $-V_{dc}$.
- This **reduces $di/dt$** at turn-off, lowering the **rate of change of radial force** $\partial p_r / \partial t$.
- The result: the **impulsive vibration** at commutation is "softened," reducing the high-frequency tonal component in the noise spectrum.
- Typical reduction: **3–8 dB(A)** in the 2–8 kHz band.

**Random PWM Modulation (1.5 marks):**

- The switching frequency $f_{sw}$ is **randomly varied** within a band (e.g., $f_{sw} \in [15 \text{ kHz}, 25 \text{ kHz}]$) using a pseudo-random number generator.
- This **spreads the switching-tone energy** over a wide frequency range, converting a sharp tonal peak into broadband "white-ish" noise.
- Typical reduction: **5–10 dB(A)** at the original switching frequency.

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Two-Stage Commutation | Random PWM |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Mechanism | Soften current transient | Spread spectral energy |
| Frequency Targeted | Low (commutation events) | High (switching band) |
| Hardware Complexity | Medium (extra voltage level) | Low (algorithm change) |
| Noise Reduction | 3–8 dB(A) | 5–10 dB(A) |
| Side Effects | Slight torque-speed loss | Slight current ripple increase |

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**a)** What are the **three principal sources of noise** in special electrical machines? Discuss the **magnetic noise mechanism** in detail, with reference to stator structural modes. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Describe **mechanical and structural techniques** to mitigate noise. With a suitable derivation, show how an **acoustic enclosure** reduces the Sound Pressure Level at the measurement point. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks:

**Three Sources (2 marks):**
1. **Magnetic noise** — electromagnetic origin (radial/tangential forces).
2. **Mechanical noise** — bearings, rotor unbalance, brush friction.
3. **Aerodynamic noise** — cooling fan, air turbulence.

**Magnetic Noise Mechanism (5 marks):**

The air-gap flux density $B(\theta, t)$ in any AC machine is a **spatial-temporal function** that, when squared (Maxwell stress), produces radial force waves of mode number $m$ and frequency $f$.

**Stator structural modes** are characterised by mode pairs $(m, n)$ where $m$ is the number of nodal diameters around the circumference and $n$ is the number of nodal circles along the axis. The natural frequency $f_{m,n}$ of mode $(m, n)$ is given by:

$$f_{m,n} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{K_{m,n}}{M_{m,n}}}$$

For a typical induction/PMSM stator, the dominant mode is the **elliptical mode $(m=2, n=0)$** with frequency 200–2000 Hz.

**Resonance criterion for maximum noise:**

$$\text{Resonance} \iff f_{force}(m) = f_{m,n}$$

When the **electromagnetic forcing frequency at mode $m$** matches the **stator natural frequency at the same mode $m$**, large-amplitude vibration occurs. This produces the **"magnetic whine"** characteristic of PWM-driven SRM/BLDC machines.

A typical PMSM with $2p = 4$ poles on 50 Hz supply has $f_{force} = 100$ Hz at mode $m = 4$, which can excite the $(m=4, n=0)$ stator mode if the latter lies near 100 Hz.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks:

**Mechanical and Structural Techniques (3 marks):**

1. **Stator yoke stiffening** — increasing lamination thickness raises the modal stiffness $K_{m,n}$ and thus shifts $f_{m,n}$ upward, away from forcing frequencies.
2. **Bearing selection** — using **ceramic hybrid bearings** or **sealed-for-life greased bearings** reduces bearing-induced vibration by 2–5 dB(A).
3. **Resin impregnation** — filling the air gaps in the slot with epoxy resin acts as a **damping layer**, increasing the structural damping factor $\zeta$ and reducing vibration amplitude by $1/(2\zeta)$.
4. **Tightening bolt preload optimisation** — properly torqued frame bolts prevent lamination flutter, a common source of high-frequency buzz.
5. **Rotor balancing to ISO 1940 G2.5 grade** — eliminates unbalance forces at $1\times$ and $2\times$ rotational frequency.

**Acoustic Enclosure Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Let the **sound power emitted by the bare machine** be $W_0$ (in watts). The sound power **transmitted through the enclosure wall** is $W_t$, given by:

$$W_t = W_0 \cdot \tau$$

where $\tau$ is the **transmission coefficient** of the wall (typically $\tau \ll 1$ for a double-wall insulated enclosure).

**Step 2:** The **Insertion Loss (IL)** of the enclosure is defined as:

$$IL = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W_0}{W_t}\right) = -10 \log_{10}(\tau)$$

**Step 3:** For a **double-wall partition** with mass per unit area $m_1$ and $m_2$ separated by an air gap of thickness $d$ at frequency $f$, the mass law gives:

$$\tau \approx \left(\frac{1}{2\pi f}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_0 c_0^2}{m_1 m_2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (2\pi f d / c_0)^2}$$

**Step 4:** For typical enclosure parameters: $m_1 = 5 \, \text{kg/m}^2$, $m_2 = 5 \, \text{kg/m}^2$, $d = 0.05 \, \text{m}$, $f = 1000 \, \text{Hz}$:

$$\tau \approx \frac{(1.2)(343)^2}{(2\pi \cdot 1000)^2 (5)(5)[1 + (2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.05/343)^2]}$$

$$\tau \approx \frac{1.413 \times 10^5}{9.87 \times 10^7 \times 0.84} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5:** The Insertion Loss:

$$IL = -10 \log_{10}(1.7 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 27.7 \, \text{dB}$$

**Conclusion:** A properly designed double-wall acoustic enclosure can reduce noise by **~25–30 dB(A)**, which is a significant reduction — a 10 dB drop corresponds to a perceived halving of loudness.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Noise Mitigation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in SPL calculation** — Students write the formula $20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$ but forget that $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$. Result: numerical answer off by orders of magnitude. **Deduct 1 mark.**
> 2. **Conflating "cogging" with "torque ripple"** — Cogging is position-dependent (no current), torque ripple is current-dependent. Examiners specifically test this distinction. **Deduct 1 mark** if confused.
> 3. **Missing the "$\mathbf{2f_s}$" frequency doubling argument** — A 7-mark question on Maxwell stress almost always requires the squaring step and identification of $2f_s$ as the dominant forcing frequency. **Deduct 2 marks** if absent.
> 4. **Skewing in wrong units** — Always specify skew as **mechanical degrees** for the rotor and provide both $\theta_{skew,opt}$ and the LCM-based formula. **Deduct 0.5 mark** for unit ambiguity.
> 5. **PWM question = control-level only** — Don't describe mechanical fixes (bearing, enclosure) in a PWM question — answer in the correct mitigation tier. **Deduct 1 mark** for category mismatch.
> 6. **Missing the resonance condition** — Any discussion of "why the noise is loud" must mention that the forcing frequency matches the stator natural mode. **Deduct 1 mark** otherwise.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Noise Mitigation Techniques (Module 2)**

- **Three Sources of Noise:** Magnetic, Mechanical, Aerodynamic. (Always start with this triad.)
- **SPL Formula:** $\text{SPL} = 20\log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$ dB(A); $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.
- **Maxwell Radial Stress:** $p_r = B^2/2\mu_0$. The squaring produces the $2f_s$ forcing.
- **Slot Ripple / Cogging Frequency:** $f_{slot} = Z \cdot f_{rot}$; cogging cycles/rev $= \text{LCM}(Z, 2p)/(2p)$.
- **Skew Angle:** $\theta_{skew,opt} = 180^{\circ}/\text{LCM}(Z, 2p)$ mechanical degrees.
- **Torque Ripple Frequency (SRM):** $f_T = m \cdot f_s$, where $m$ = harmonic order.
- **Mitigation Tiers (4 P):** **P**ath (mechanical/structural), **P**ower-electronic (PWM strategies), **P**hysical design (slot/pole/skew), **P**assive (enclosure).
- **SRM-Specific Control:** Two-stage commutation, current profiling, soft chopping, random turn-on modulation.
- **PMSM-Specific Design:** Fractional slot concentrated windings (FSCW), magnet skewing, slotless design, magnet pole-arc optimisation.
- **Resonance Criterion:** Maximum noise occurs when $f_{force}(m) = f_{m,n}$ (stator natural mode).
- **Insertion Loss of Enclosure:** $IL = 10\log_{10}(W_{in}/W_{out}) = -10\log_{10}(\tau)$.
- **KTU 2024 Marks Distribution (Module 2):** Typically 1×3-mark (short answer) + 1×7-mark (part of 14-mark question). Mastery of Maxwell stress derivation + LCM skewing is essential.
- **Human-Ear Sensitivity:** The A-weighting curve peaks near 2–5 kHz — this is exactly the band where PWM switching and cogging harmonics live, making **audible whine** a critical NVH concern in EVs and appliances.
- **Key Standards:** ISO 10816 (vibration), IEC 60704 (household appliance noise), ISO 1940 (rotor balancing), ISO 389-7 (sound reference).
- **One-line Summary for the Board:** *"Noise in special machines arises from electromagnetic forces ($2f_s$ dominant), mechanical resonances, and aerodynamic flow; mitigation is achieved by attacking the source (skew, profile shaping), the path (damping, stiffening), or the radiation (enclosure, isolation)."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Special Electrical Machines (SEM)**, the term **"Applications"** refers to the systematic study of the specific industrial, commercial, aerospace, biomedical, and domestic deployment scenarios where non-conventional electrical machines (such as Stepper Motors, Permanent Magnet Brushless DC Motors, Switched Reluctance Motors, Servo Motors, and Linear Induction Motors) outperform conventional DC and AC induction machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Applications of special electrical machines encompass the matched pairing of a machine's intrinsic electromagnetic characteristics (high torque density, precise positioning, fast dynamic response, or linear motion) to specific load and operational requirements in modern mechatronic and energy-efficient systems.

### 1.2 Special Machines Covered in Module 2

For the **KTU 2024 PEEET743** syllabus, **Module 2** predominantly focuses on the **Stepper Motor** and the **Permanent Magnet Brushless DC (PMBLDC) Motor**. The "Applications" topic integrates the theoretical construction and working principles of these machines into their real-world deployment.

| Machine | Core Capability | Primary Application Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Stepper Motor | Discrete angular positioning | CNC, 3D Printers, Robotics |
| PMBLDC Motor | High-speed, high-efficiency operation | EVs, Drones, HVAC, Appliances |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Compass and the Drone Propeller**
>
> Imagine a **maritime compass** (Stepper Motor analogy). It does not spin freely like a fan; instead, it locks precisely into one of 32 cardinal directions, "clicking" into position. This is exactly how a stepper motor operates: it moves in discrete, exact angular steps (e.g., 1.8° per step) without needing any position feedback sensor. It is the "go-to" choice whenever a system needs to *know exactly where it is* without measuring it.
>
> Now imagine a **drone propeller** (PMBLDC Motor analogy). It must spin smoothly, silently, and efficiently at thousands of RPM, responding instantly to throttle commands. A brushed DC motor would overheat and wear out its brushes within weeks; a PMBLDC motor uses electronic commutation instead of mechanical brushes, making it the perfect choice for high-speed, long-life, energy-efficient rotation.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Step Angle** ($\theta_s$): The angular displacement per pulse, typically **1.8°** (200 steps/rev) or **0.9°** (400 steps/rev) for hybrid steppers.
- **Holding Torque** ($T_h$): The torque the motor can hold while energized but stationary, measured in **N·m**.
- **Detent Torque** ($T_d$): Residual magnetic torque when unenergized, typically **5–10%** of holding torque.
- **Back EMF Constant** ($K_e$): Voltage induced per unit speed, in **V/(rad/s)**.
- **Torque Constant** ($K_t$): Torque produced per unit current, in **N·m/A**, where $K_t = K_e$ in SI units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Applications are a Critical Study Area

The applications of Special Electrical Machines are not arbitrary; they emerge from the unique **operational advantages** these machines provide over conventional machines:

**A. Why Stepper Motors? — The Logic of Open-Loop Positioning**
1. **Discrete digital-to-mechanical conversion:** Each electrical pulse produces one precise mechanical step. No encoder required.
2. **Inherent holding torque:** Can lock the shaft at a position even without continuous rotation.
3. **Direct compatibility with microcontrollers:** Perfect for digital systems (Arduino, PLC, FPGA).
4. **Low-cost positioning:** Cheaper than servo systems for low-to-medium speed applications.

**B. Why PMBLDC Motors? — The Logic of High-Efficiency Rotation**
1. **No mechanical brushes → no wear → long life.**
2. **High power density:** More torque per kg than induction or brushed DC motors.
3. **Excellent heat dissipation:** Heat is in the stator, where it can be easily cooled.
4. **High-speed capability:** Can operate efficiently at >10,000 RPM.
5. **Low electromagnetic interference (EMI) and low acoustic noise.**

### 2.2 Detailed Application Matrix

#### 2.2.1 Applications of Stepper Motors

**A. Computer Numerical Control (CNC) Machines**
- Used for driving the X, Y, Z axes of milling machines, lathes, routers, and plasma cutters.
- **Why:** Open-loop precision positioning, high holding torque to resist cutting forces, and direct pulse-train control from G-code interpreters.

**B. 3D Printers**
- **Why:** Stepper motors drive the extruder and the X/Y/Z positioning with micrometer-level accuracy. The 1.8° step angle translates to fine linear resolution via lead screws (typically 1.25 µm per microstep).

**C. Robotics**
- Used in robotic arms, grippers, and joint actuators.
- **Why:** Repeatable positioning, ability to hold loads against gravity without drift, and simple interfacing.

**D. Textile and Printing Machinery**
- Drives plotters, printers, scanners, and fabric-feeding mechanisms.
- **Why:** Precise linear positioning synchronized with print heads.

**E. Watches, Clocks, and Camera Lenses**
- Miniature permanent magnet stepper motors drive quartz analog clocks, autofocus lenses, and aperture diaphragms.
- **Why:** Extremely low power consumption and precise incremental motion.

**F. Medical Equipment**
- Infusion pumps, blood analyzers, CT scanner gantries, and surgical robots.
- **Why:** Reliability, precise dosing, and the absence of brush-induced particles (critical in clean environments).

**G. Automotive Applications**
- Dashboard gauges, headlight beam adjustment, HVAC flaps, and electric mirror positioning.
- **Why:** Compact size, low cost, and direct digital bus compatibility.

#### 2.2.2 Applications of PMBLDC Motors

**A. Electric and Hybrid Vehicles (EV/HEV)**
- **Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV, Hyundai Kona:** Traction motors.
- **Why:** High torque density, regenerative braking capability, and >95% efficiency.

**B. Drones and Quadcopters**
- **DJI, Parrot:** All modern commercial drones use outrunner BLDC motors.
- **Why:** Extreme power-to-weight ratio (kW/kg), high RPM (8,000–15,000), and high reliability.

**C. Computer Hard Disk Drives (HDDs)**
- Spindle motors to spin the magnetic platters at 5,400 or 7,200 RPM.
- **Why:** Silent operation, low heat, and long life.

**D. HVAC and Refrigeration**
- Variable-speed compressors in air conditioners and refrigerators.
- **Why:** 20–30% energy savings over fixed-speed induction motors; soft starting.

**E. Home Appliances**
- Washing machines (direct-drive), ceiling fans with BLDC technology (e.g., **Atomberg, Crompton Energion**), vacuum cleaners (Dyson), mixers, and blenders.
- **Why:** Energy Star ratings, silent operation, and long life (no brush replacement).

**F. Industrial Automation**
- Conveyor belts, pumps, fans, and servo positioning systems.
- **Why:** Variable speed control, integrated electronics, and Industry 4.0 compatibility.

**G. Aerospace and Defense**
- Actuators in flight control surfaces, fuel pumps, and missile fin control.
- **Why:** High reliability, no brush sparking (explosion-proof), and wide operating temperature range.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Step Angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r}$ where $N_r$ = no. of rotor teeth | degrees | Stepper motor positioning |
| Shaft Resolution | $\Delta x = \theta_s \times \dfrac{p}{360°}$ | m | Lead-screw linear displacement |
| Holding Torque (Hybrid) | $T_h \propto N_r \times \phi_m \times I$ | N·m | Stepper motor torque |
| Back EMF (PMBLDC) | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | V | PMBLDC speed control |
| Torque (PMBLDC) | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | N·m | PMBLDC electromechanical conversion |
| Speed (PMBLDC) | $\omega = \dfrac{V_s - I_a R_a}{K_e}$ | rad/s | PMBLDC voltage equation |
| Power | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | W | All rotating machines |
| Synchronous Speed (SRM/PMBLDC) | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM | Variable frequency drives |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** In SI units, $K_e = K_t$. If $K_t$ is in N·m/A, then $K_e$ is automatically in V·s/rad. If using practical units, $K_e$ (V/kRPM) and $K_t$ (oz·in/A) differ by a conversion factor of **1352.0**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The study of applications trains the engineer to perform **machine selection** based on:
- **Load profile:** Constant torque vs. variable torque.
- **Speed range:** Sub-RPM (steppers) vs. high-speed (BLDC).
- **Positioning accuracy:** Open-loop (stepper) vs. closed-loop (BLDC with encoder).
- **Environment:** Cleanroom (BLDC), explosive atmospheres (BLDC—no sparks), cost-sensitive (stepper).
- **Energy efficiency targets:** IE4/IE5 motors in modern appliances are almost exclusively PMBLDC or SRM.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Displacement per Step in a Stepper-Driven Lead-Screw System

**Given:**
- Step angle of stepper motor: $\theta_s = 1.8°$
- Lead of the lead screw (pitch): $p = 5 \text{ mm/rev}$
- Microstepping factor: $m = 16$

**Find:** Effective linear displacement per pulse $\Delta x$.

**Step 1: Identify the relationship between step angle and linear motion.**

When a stepper motor rotates by one full step ($\theta_s$), the lead screw advances axially by a fraction of its pitch proportional to the rotation.

$$
\text{Linear displacement per full step} = \theta_s \times \dfrac{p}{360°}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\Delta x_{\text{full step}} = 1.8° \times \dfrac{5 \text{ mm}}{360°}
$$

$$
\Delta x_{\text{full step}} = \dfrac{9}{360} \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ mm} = 25 \text{ μm}
$$

**Step 3: Account for microstepping.**

Microstepping divides each full step into $m$ equal sub-steps. The effective angular resolution becomes:

$$
\theta_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{m} = \dfrac{1.8°}{16} = 0.1125°
$$

**Step 4: Compute the effective linear resolution.**

$$
\Delta x_{\text{effective}} = 0.1125° \times \dfrac{5 \text{ mm}}{360°}
$$

$$
\Delta x_{\text{effective}} = \dfrac{0.5625}{360} \text{ mm} = 0.0015625 \text{ mm} = 1.5625 \text{ μm}
$$

**Conclusion:** With a 5 mm pitch lead screw and 16× microstepping, the 3D printer can resolve linear positions to within **1.56 µm** — sufficient for high-resolution 3D printing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Stepper Motor Holding Torque for a CNC Mill

**Given:**
- Mass of the X-axis carriage: $M = 10 \text{ kg}$
- Coefficient of friction on linear rails: $\mu = 0.1$
- Lead screw pitch: $p = 5 \text{ mm/rev}$
- Lead screw efficiency: $\eta = 0.9$
- Safety factor: $SF = 1.5$

**Find:** Minimum required holding torque $T_h$.

**Step 1: Calculate the friction force on the linear rails.**

$$
F_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot M \cdot g = 0.1 \times 10 \times 9.81
$$

$$
F_{\text{friction}} = 9.81 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Convert the linear friction force to a rotational torque on the lead screw.**

The lead screw converts torque $T$ into linear force $F$ via:

$$
F = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot \eta \cdot T}{p}
$$

Rearranging for $T$:

$$
T = \dfrac{F \cdot p}{2\pi \cdot \eta}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
T = \dfrac{9.81 \text{ N} \times 0.005 \text{ m}}{2\pi \times 0.9}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{0.04905}{5.6549}
$$

$$
T = 0.00867 \text{ N·m} = 8.67 \text{ mN·m}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the safety factor.**

$$
T_{\text{required}} = SF \times T = 1.5 \times 8.67 \text{ mN·m}
$$

$$
T_{\text{required}} \approx 13.0 \text{ mN·m}
$$

**Conclusion:** The CNC machine designer should select a stepper motor with a **holding torque of at least 0.013 N·m** (plus an additional margin for cutting forces during machining).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: PMBLDC Motor Speed Under Load

**Given:**
- Supply voltage: $V_s = 48 \text{ V}$
- Armature resistance: $R_a = 0.5 \text{ }\Omega$
- Back EMF constant: $K_e = 0.05 \text{ V·s/rad}$
- Load torque: $T_L = 2 \text{ N·m}$
- Torque constant: $K_t = 0.05 \text{ N·m/A}$ (equal to $K_e$ in SI)

**Find:** Steady-state no-load and full-load speeds.

**Step 1: Establish the voltage equation.**

$$
V_s = I_a R_a + E_b = I_a R_a + K_e \omega
$$

**Step 2: Relate armature current to load torque.**

$$
T_L = K_t \cdot I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \dfrac{T_L}{K_t}
$$

$$
I_a = \dfrac{2}{0.05} = 40 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the speed at full load.**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{V_s - I_a R_a}{K_e}
$$

$$
\omega = \dfrac{48 - (40)(0.5)}{0.05}
$$

$$
\omega = \dfrac{48 - 20}{0.05} = \dfrac{28}{0.05} = 560 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to RPM.**

$$
N = \omega \times \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = 560 \times 9.5493
$$

$$
N \approx 5348 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate no-load speed (when $I_a \approx 0$).**

$$
\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_s}{K_e} = \dfrac{48}{0.05} = 960 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
N_0 \approx 9169 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Conclusion:** This PMBLDC motor operates between approximately **5,348 RPM (full load)** and **9,169 RPM (no load)** — a wide speed range ideal for **fan, pump, and EV traction applications**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Selection Matrix for Applications

| Application | Recommended Machine | Speed Range | Position Accuracy | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3D Printer (X/Y/Z) | Stepper (Hybrid) | Low (<1000 RPM) | High (±5 µm) | Low |
| CNC Mill | Servo + Stepper | Medium | Very High | High |
| Drone Propeller | PMBLDC (Outrunner) | Very High (>10,000 RPM) | Not Required | Medium |
| EV Traction | PMBLDC / PMSM | High | Not Required | Very High |
| HDD Spindle | PMBLDC | Medium (5,400–7,200 RPM) | Not Required | Low |
| Robotic Arm Joint | Servo / Stepper | Variable | High | Medium-High |
| Washing Machine | PMBLDC (Direct Drive) | Variable | Not Required | Medium |
| Quartz Clock | PM Stepper | Ultra-Low | Continuous | Very Low |
| HVAC Compressor | PMBLDC / SRM | Variable | Not Required | Medium-High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Application Mapping — Stepper Motor Deployments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Special Electrical Machines] --> B[Stepper Motor]

    B2 --> B2b[Extruder Motor]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — PMBLDC Motor in an Electric Vehicle Powertrain

```mermaid

    GEAR -->|Torque| WHEEL[Drive Wheels]
    WHEEL -->|Vehicle Motion| VEHICLE[Electric Vehicle]

```

### 4.4 Application Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Choose Special Machine] --> Q1{Positioning Required?}

    Q1 -->|No| Q3{High Speed Required?}

    Q2 -->|No, Need Encoder Feedback| SERVO[Select Servo Motor]

    Q3 -->|Yes, High Speed and Efficiency| BLDC[Select PMBLDC Motor]
    Q3 -->|No, Low to Medium Speed| Q4{Robust and Variable Reluctance?}

    Q4 -->|Yes| SRM[Select Switched Reluctance Motor]
    Q4 -->|No, Need Linear Motion| LIM[Select Linear Induction Motor]

    STEPPER --> APP1[Application: 3D Printer, CNC, Robotics]
    SERVO --> APP2[Application: Industrial Robotics, CNC Servo]

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> **List any six applications of stepper motors in modern industry.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** machines for X, Y, Z axis positioning.
2. **3D printers** for extruder and bed movement with micro-step precision.
3. **Robotics** for arm joints, grippers, and end-effectors.
4. **Medical equipment** such as infusion pumps, CT scanners, and surgical robots.
5. **Automotive dashboard** instruments and headlight levelling systems.
6. **Textile and printing** machinery for plotters, scanners, and fabric-feed rollers.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> **Why are PMBLDC motors preferred over brushed DC motors in modern electric vehicles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
PMBLDC motors are preferred because:
1. **No brushes → no mechanical wear → long life** (typically >20,000 hours vs. ~2,000 hours for brushed DC). *(1 mark)*
2. **Higher efficiency (>90%)** due to absence of brush friction and lower rotor $I^2R$ losses (rotor is just permanent magnets). *(1 mark)*
3. **Better heat dissipation** as heat is generated in the stator, which can be actively water-cooled. Higher torque density enables compact, lightweight traction motors. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a Hybrid Stepper Motor. List any **four industrial applications** of the same. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A 5-phase hybrid stepper motor has **50 rotor teeth**. Determine:
> (i) Step angle,
> (ii) Number of steps per revolution,
> (iii) Shaft speed in RPM when pulsed at 1,000 pulses per second. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working of Hybrid Stepper Motor (7 marks)**

A **hybrid stepper motor** combines the best features of variable reluctance and permanent magnet stepper motors. *(1 mark)*

**Construction:**
- **Rotor:** Two rotor cups, each with $N_r$ teeth, mounted on the same shaft and axially displaced by half a tooth pitch. A rare-earth permanent magnet (e.g., NdFeB) sits between the two cups, magnetizing one cup as N-pole and the other as S-pole. *(2 marks)*
- **Stator:** Has multiple pole pairs with teeth matching the rotor tooth pitch. Two-phase windings are wound on stator poles such that diametrically opposite poles belong to the same phase. *(1 mark)*

**Working:**
- When phase A is energized, the N-pole rotor teeth align with phase A stator teeth. The S-pole rotor teeth align one half-pitch offset. *(1 mark)*
- When phase A is switched off and phase B is energized (energized after half-pitch displacement), the rotor rotates by one full step angle $\theta_s = 360°/(2 \times N_r)$. *(1 mark)*

**Four Industrial Applications:** *(1 mark)*
1. CNC machine tool axes.
2. 3D printer stepper drivers.
3. Robotics and pick-and-place machines.
4. Quartz analog clocks and camera autofocus lenses.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** $m = 5$ phases, $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth.

**(i) Step Angle:**

For a hybrid stepper, the step angle formula is:

$$
\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \times N_r}
$$

$$
\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{5 \times 50} = \dfrac{360°}{250}
$$

$$
\theta_s = 1.44°/\text{step} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Number of steps per revolution:**

$$
N_{\text{steps}} = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s} = \dfrac{360}{1.44} = 250 \text{ steps/rev} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Shaft speed for pulse rate of 1,000 pps:**

$$
N_{\text{RPM}} = f_p \times \dfrac{60}{N_{\text{steps}}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{RPM}} = 1000 \times \dfrac{60}{250} = 240 \text{ RPM} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain with a block diagram the closed-loop speed control of a **PMBLDC motor** used in an electric vehicle. List **three key advantages** of using PMBLDC over induction motors in EV applications. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A PMBLDC motor has the following specifications:
> $V_s = 48 \text{ V}$, $R_a = 0.4 \text{ }\Omega$, $K_e = 0.04 \text{ V·s/rad}$, $K_t = 0.04 \text{ N·m/A}$.
> Calculate:
> (i) The no-load speed in RPM,
> (ii) The full-load speed when armature current is 30 A,
> (iii) The full-load torque. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Closed-Loop PMBLDC Speed Control (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram Explanation:**
- The **throttle pedal** sends a reference speed $N_{\text{ref}}$ to the **ECU** (Electronic Control Unit). *(1 mark)*
- The ECU commands a **three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** using PWM based on rotor position feedback from **Hall sensors** (or encoder). *(2 marks)*
- The inverter energizes the three stator phase windings in the correct sequence, generating a rotating magnetic field. *(1 mark)*
- The **Hall sensor signals** are fed back to the ECU for precise electronic commutation, ensuring torque is always produced in the correct direction. *(1 mark)*
- A **PI controller** compares $N_{\text{ref}}$ with the actual speed from the encoder and adjusts the PWM duty cycle to maintain constant speed. *(1 mark)*
- **Three Advantages over Induction Motors in EVs:** *(1 mark)*
  1. Higher efficiency (>95% vs. ~88% for IM) → longer range.
  2. Higher power and torque density → lighter vehicle.
  3. Better heat dissipation (heat in stator, not rotor).

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** $V_s = 48 \text{ V}$, $R_a = 0.4 \text{ }\Omega$, $K_e = 0.04 \text{ V·s/rad}$, $K_t = 0.04 \text{ N·m/A}$, $I_a = 30 \text{ A}$.

**(i) No-load speed:**

$$
\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_s}{K_e} = \dfrac{48}{0.04} = 1200 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
N_0 = \omega_0 \times \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = 1200 \times 9.5493 \approx 11{,}459 \text{ RPM} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

**(ii) Full-load speed:**

$$
\omega_{FL} = \dfrac{V_s - I_a R_a}{K_e} = \dfrac{48 - (30)(0.4)}{0.04}
$$

$$
\omega_{FL} = \dfrac{48 - 12}{0.04} = \dfrac{36}{0.04} = 900 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
N_{FL} = 900 \times 9.5493 \approx 8{,}594 \text{ RPM} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

**(iii) Full-load torque:**

$$
T_{FL} = K_t \cdot I_a = 0.04 \times 30 = 1.2 \text{ N·m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $K_e$ and $K_t$ units:** In KTU problems, $K_e$ is *always* in **V·s/rad** (SI). If the question gives $K_e$ in V/kRPM, you **must** convert using $K_e (\text{V·s/rad}) = K_e(\text{V/kRPM}) \times \dfrac{1000}{60 \times 2\pi}$. Failure to convert is the **#1 source of numerical errors**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rad/s to RPM:** The final answer MUST be in RPM (multiply by 60/2π ≈ 9.5493). Writing "ω = 900 rad/s" when the question asks for RPM costs **1 full mark**.
>
> 3. **In Stepper Motor Numericals:** Confusing the step angle formula. For **Variable Reluctance (VR) steppers**, $\theta_s = 360°/(N_s \times N_r)$. For **Hybrid steppers**, $\theta_s = 360°/(2 \times N_r)$ for two-phase, and $\theta_s = 360°/(m \times N_r)$ for multi-phase. Writing the wrong formula costs **2–3 marks instantly**.
>
> 4. **In CNC Application Questions:** Students often forget to mention **microstepping** when explaining 3D printer stepper motor applications. Always state "1.8° step angle with 16× or 32× microstepping provides 1.56 µm or 0.78 µm effective resolution."
>
> 5. **No diagrams = lost marks:** Every KTU answer for "Explain with diagram" type questions **must include a labelled figure**. Without a diagram, expect a **1.5 to 2-mark penalty** on a 7-mark question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Applications of Special Electrical Machines**

**Stepper Motors — Applications:**
- ✅ CNC machines (X, Y, Z axes); high holding torque resists cutting forces.
- ✅ 3D printers; microstepping provides micrometer precision.
- ✅ Robotics; open-loop positioning reduces cost.
- ✅ Medical pumps and CT scanners; no brush particles.
- ✅ Quartz clocks, automotive gauges, camera autofocus; low power, precise incremental motion.
- ✅ Step angle formula: $\theta_s = 360° / (m \cdot N_r)$ for hybrid stepper.
- ✅ Holding torque provides locked-rotor holding force even when un-energized (with permanent magnets).

**PMBLDC Motors — Applications:**
- ✅ Electric vehicles (traction); high efficiency (>95%) and power density.
- ✅ Drones and quadcopters; extreme power-to-weight ratio.
- ✅ Hard disk drives; silent, low-heat spindle operation.
- ✅ HVAC compressors; variable speed for energy saving.
- ✅ Home appliances (fans, washers, vacuum cleaners); IE4/IE5 efficiency.
- ✅ Aerospace actuators; no sparking (explosion-proof), wide temp range.
- ✅ Voltage equation: $V_s = I_a R_a + K_e \omega$.
- ✅ Torque equation: $T = K_t I_a$.

**Selection Heuristics for Examiners:**
- 🔹 **Need precise open-loop positioning?** → **Stepper Motor**.
- 🔹 **Need high-speed, high-efficiency, long-life rotation?** → **PMBLDC**.
- 🔹 **Need variable speed for pumps/fans?** → **PMBLDC**.
- 🔹 **Need linear motion?** → **Linear Induction Motor**.
- 🔹 **Need high robustness in harsh environments?** → **SRM**.

**Quick-Recall Equations:**
$$
\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r} \text{ (VR); } \theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{2 N_r} \text{ (2-phase Hybrid)}
$$

$$
N_{\text{RPM}} = f_p \cdot \dfrac{60}{N_{\text{steps}}}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{120 f}{P} \text{ (Synchronous speed of PMBLDC/SRM)}
$$

$$
\omega = \dfrac{V_s - I_a R_a}{K_e}; \quad T = K_t I_a
$$

**Key Distinctions to Avoid Confusion in Exams:**
- 🔸 **Stepper motor = open loop**, PMBLDC = **closed loop** (with sensors).
- 🔸 **Stepper motor = low speed** (<2000 RPM), PMBLDC = **high speed** (>10,000 RPM).
- 🔸 **Stepper motor = full torque at standstill**, PMBLDC = **torque ∝ current** (zero current → zero torque at standstill).
- 🔸 **Stepper = discrete steps**, PMBLDC = **smooth continuous rotation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PM Brushless DC Motor - Constructional Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PM Brushless DC Motor - Constructional Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Permanent Magnet Brushless DC (PMBLDC) Motor** is a synchronous electric machine in which the field excitation is provided by **high-energy permanent magnets** mounted on the rotor, while the armature windings are located on the stator. The commutation function — traditionally performed by brushes and a mechanical commutator in a conventional DC motor — is achieved electronically using a **three-phase voltage source inverter (VSI)** gated by rotor-position feedback obtained from **Hall-effect sensors** (or sensorless estimators).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEET743, Module 3)**, the PMBLDC motor belongs to the family of *self-controlled synchronous machines* and is formally classified as a **trapezoidal-back-EMF, square-wave-current** type machine (as opposed to a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor, PMSM, which uses sinusoidal excitation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** In board examinations, students MUST clearly distinguish between a BLDC motor (trapezoidal EMF, square-wave current) and a PMSM (sinusoidal EMF, sinusoidal current). Both share the same physical construction, but differ in the **shape of the back-EMF waveform** and the **commutation strategy** used in the inverter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Waterwheel"

Imagine a traditional ceiling fan with blades that act like **sails catching wind**. Now imagine replacing the wind with three precise water-jets (representing the three-phase stator currents) placed at 120° around a circular wall. To keep the sails turning smoothly in one direction, you must switch each water-jet ON and OFF at exactly the right moment — **without ever touching the sails themselves**.

In a PMBLDC motor:
- The **sails** = Permanent magnets on the rotor.
- The **water-jets** = Stator phase windings energized sequentially.
- The **switching rule** = Electronic controller using Hall-sensor feedback.
- The fact that nothing "touches" the rotor = **brushless** operation.

This analogy captures the essence of **electronic commutation** — the motor's "brain" (controller) replaces the physical brushes by reading rotor position and energizing the appropriate stator phase at the right instant.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| **Number of phases** | **3 (three-phase)** |
| **Air-gap flux density (B)** | **0.6 – 1.0 T** (typical for NdFeB rotors) |
| **Supply DC link voltage** | **24 V, 48 V, 310 V DC** (application dependent) |
| **Number of pole pairs (P/2)** | **1 to 6** (multi-pole designs common for low-speed high-torque) |
| **Energy product of NdFeB magnets** | **200 – 400 kJ/m³** |
| **Curie temperature of NdFeB** | **310 – 340 °C** |
| **Slot-pole combinations** | **6-slot/4-pole, 12-slot/8-pole, 36-slot/30-pole**, etc. |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol **P** in KTU textbooks denotes the total **number of poles** (an even integer), while **P/2** denotes the number of **pole pairs**. In torque/EMF equations, careful attention must be paid to whether the formula uses *P* or *P/2*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Breakdown of Constructional Components

The PMBLDC motor's construction is conceptually an "inside-out" version of a conventional DC motor — the armature is on the **outside (stator)** and the field is on the **inside (rotor)**. This inversion is the key insight that makes the machine brushless.

### A. Stator (Stationary Armature) Assembly

1. **Stator Core (Laminated Stack)**
   - Built from thin, **insulated silicon-steel laminations** (typically **0.35 mm or 0.5 mm** thickness).
   - Function: to provide a low-loss magnetic flux path and house the windings in slots.
   - Slotted construction is most common; slotless (toothless) designs are used in low-power, low-ripple applications.

2. **Stator Windings**
   - **Three-phase, distributed (or concentrated)** windings placed in the stator slots.
   - Two winding configurations are encountered in KTU problems:
     - **Concentrated (tooth) winding** — high fill factor, short end-turns, preferred in low-power BLDCs.
     - **Distributed (overlapping) winding** — produces a more trapezoidal EMF waveform, preferred in high-power designs.
   - Phases are spatially displaced by **120 electrical degrees**.

3. **Stator Frame and Bearings Housing**
   - Provides mechanical support, heat dissipation, and bearing seats.
   - End-shields accommodate the rotor shaft and sensor wiring.

### B. Rotor (Field System) Assembly

The rotor carries the **permanent magnets** and is magnetically designed to produce either a **trapezoidal** or **sinusoidal** air-gap flux density distribution.

1. **Permanent Magnet Materials (KTU-high-yield)**

| Magnet Type | Energy Product (BH)max | Remanence Br | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Alnico** | 50–80 kJ/m³ | 0.7–1.3 T | Low cost, high temperature |
| **Ferrite (Ceramic)** | 25–40 kJ/m³ | 0.3–0.45 T | Low cost, low performance |
| **SmCo (Samarium-Cobalt)** | 150–240 kJ/m³ | 0.8–1.1 T | High-temperature aerospace |
| **NdFeB (Neodymium)** | 200–400 kJ/m³ | 1.0–1.4 T | **Most widely used in BLDCs** |

2. **Rotor Topologies (MOST IMPORTANT FOR KTU)**

- **Surface-Mounted PM (SPM) Rotor** — Magnets glued on the rotor surface. Produces **trapezoidal EMF**. Most common in BLDCs. Mechanically fragile at high speeds.
- **Interior (Buried) PM (IPM) Rotor** — Magnets embedded inside the rotor iron. Produces **sinusoidal EMF** (used in PMSM). Mechanically robust, suitable for high-speed operation and field-weakening.
- **Inset PM Rotor** — Magnets inserted into slots cut on the rotor surface — hybrid of SPM and IPM.
- **Sleeve / Canned Rotor** — Magnets encased in a non-magnetic sleeve (e.g., carbon-fiber or Inconel) for high-speed applications (used in turbomolecular pumps, machine-tool spindles).

3. **Shaft**
   - Non-magnetic stainless steel in contact with magnets; magnetic steel used elsewhere.

### C. Rotor Position Sensors

- **Hall-effect sensors** (most common) — three sensors displaced by 120° mechanical / 120° electrical, providing 6 discrete position signals per electrical cycle.
- **Optical encoders, resolvers** — used in high-precision applications.
- **Sensorless schemes** — back-EMF zero-crossing detection; popular in cost-sensitive and harsh-environment applications.

### D. Power Electronic Commutator (External to Motor)

- **Three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** with **6 MOSFETs / IGBTs** in a bridge configuration.
- **DC-link capacitor** for ripple filtering.
- **Gate-driver circuits** and **microcontroller / DSP** for commutation logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"Why is the inverter considered part of the BLDC motor system?"*
> **Model Answer:** Because the BLDC motor cannot operate without electronic commutation. The inverter, controller, and sensors form an **integral part** of the motor system — they are not optional accessories. This is a key KTU viva favourite.

## 2.2 Why These Constructional Choices? — The 'Why' Behind the Design

- **Why magnets on rotor and not on stator?** Placing magnets on the rotor eliminates sliding contacts, reduces rotor copper losses (zero rotor I²R losses), and improves heat dissipation since stator losses are easier to cool.
- **Why trapezoidal EMF?** It allows the use of a simple 6-step (square-wave) current commutation, requiring only 6 discrete switching events per electrical cycle — drastically simplifying the inverter control compared to a PMSM.
- **Why Hall sensors at 120° spacing?** Three sensors with 60° logical combinations generate **6 unique 3-bit codes** (101, 100, 110, 010, 011, 001) — exactly the number of commutation steps per electrical revolution. This 1:1 mapping between Hall code and inverter switching state is the foundation of BLDC control.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$E_{ph} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}$$ | EMF per phase (wave winding, A=2) | V; P=poles, Φ=Wb, Z=conductors, N=rpm |
| 2 | $$E_{ph} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{120 \cdot A}$$ | EMF per phase (lap winding, A=P) | V; A = number of parallel paths |
| 3 | $$E = k_e \cdot \omega$$ | Back-EMF constant relation | $k_e$ in V·s/rad |
| 4 | $$T = k_t \cdot I$$ | Torque–current relation | $k_t$ in N·m/A |
| 5 | $$k_e = k_t \quad \text{(in SI units)}$$ | Back-EMF and torque constants are numerically equal | SI convention |
| 6 | $$f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120}$$ | Supply frequency of induced EMF (for reference) | Hz |
| 7 | $$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \cdot N}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot P \cdot N}{60}$$ | Synchronous angular speed | rad/s |
| 8 | $$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \frac{E \cdot I_a}{\omega_s}$$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 9 | $$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T e(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$$ | Mechanical power developed (RMS form) | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In exam scripts, students often write $E = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$ without specifying whether A=2 (wave) or A=P (lap). Always state the winding type first. Also, BLDC motors typically use a **simplex wave-wound** armature with A=2.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

PMBLDC motors are the workhorses of:
- **Electric vehicles (EVs)** — traction motors, e-bikes, hoverboards.
- **Aerospace** — fuel pumps, flight-control actuators, drone propulsion.
- **Home appliances** — washing machines (LG direct-drive), refrigerator compressors, ceiling fans.
- **Industrial automation** — CNC machine-tool spindles, robotic joints, conveyor drives.
- **Medical and computing** — hard-disk spindle drives, surgical tools, ventilators.
- **Renewable energy** — small wind turbines (acting as generators).

The combination of **high efficiency (typically 85–95%)**, **high power density**, **long life (no brush wear)**, and **excellent speed-torque characteristics** makes the BLDC motor the preferred choice where DC-motor-like performance is needed without the maintenance burden of brushes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — EMF Equation of a BLDC Motor

**Given:**
- Total number of conductors on the armature = **Z**
- Total number of poles = **P**
- Number of parallel paths = **A** (= 2 for wave, = P for lap)
- Flux per pole = **Φ Weber**
- Rotational speed = **N rpm**

**Step 1: Express flux cut by one conductor per revolution**

In one complete mechanical revolution, a conductor on the rotor (in a *conventional* DC motor) cuts flux from **P poles**. But in a BLDC, the conductor is on the stator and the magnetic field rotates with the rotor. From the stationary conductor's reference frame, the magnetic field completes **P/2** cycles per mechanical revolution. The flux cut by **one conductor in one revolution** is:

$$\Phi_{\text{per conductor per rev}} = P \cdot \Phi$$

**Step 2: Number of revolutions per second**

The rotor makes $N/60$ revolutions per second.

**Step 3: Average EMF induced in one conductor**

According to Faraday's law, the average EMF per conductor is flux-linkage change per second:

$$e_{\text{avg, 1 cond}} = \frac{\text{Flux cut per second}}{\text{per conductor}} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N}{60}$$

**Step 4: Total conductors per parallel path**

Since there are A parallel paths in the armature, the number of conductors connected **in series per path** is:

$$Z_{\text{series}} = \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 5: Total EMF per parallel path (i.e., per phase in a wave-wound BLDC)**

$$E_{ph} = e_{\text{avg, 1 cond}} \times Z_{\text{series}}$$

Substituting:

$$E_{ph} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{ph} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}\,}$$

**Step 6: Special case for wave winding (A = 2)**

$$E_{ph} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{120}$$

**Step 7: Special case for lap winding (A = P)**

$$E_{ph} = \frac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The lap-wound result is independent of **P**, which is a classic KTU derivation question. Always include the units check: $[P \text{ (poles)}] \times [\Phi \text{ (Wb)}] \times [Z \text{ (conductors)}] \times [N \text{ (rev/s)}] = \text{Wb/s} = \text{V}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electromagnetic Torque Developed

**Starting point:** Mechanical power developed equals electrical power converted.

$$P_m = E_{ph} \cdot I_a$$

For a 3-phase BLDC, the total converted power considering all three phases (each carrying current for 2/3 of a cycle in 120° conduction) is:

$$P_m = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \left( e_a i_a + e_b i_b + e_c i_c \right) dt$$

For a **trapezoidal EMF** of peak value $E_p$ and **square-wave phase current** of magnitude $I_a$:

$$P_m = 2 \cdot E_p \cdot I_a$$

(only two phases conduct at any instant, each contributing $E_p I_a$).

**Relating $E_p$ to flux and speed:**

The peak EMF per phase of a trapezoidal BLDC can be expressed in terms of electrical angular speed $\omega_e$:

$$E_p = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega_e \cdot k_w$$

where $k_w$ is the winding factor ($k_w = k_d \cdot k_p$).

**Angular speed conversion:**

$$\omega_e = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \omega_m = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\pi P N}{60}$$

**Mechanical power:**

$$P_m = 2 \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega_e \cdot k_w \cdot I_a = P \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w \cdot I_a \cdot \omega_e$$

**Torque** (using $P_m = T \cdot \omega_m$ and $\omega_m = \frac{2}{P} \omega_e$):

$$T \cdot \omega_m = P \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w \cdot I_a \cdot \omega_e$$

$$T = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w \cdot I_a \cdot \omega_e}{\omega_m} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w \cdot I_a \cdot \omega_e}{\frac{2}{P} \omega_e}$$

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{P^2}{2} \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w \cdot I_a\,}$$

**Alternative compact form:**

Defining torque constant $k_t$:

$$T = k_t \cdot I_a, \quad \text{where} \quad k_t = \frac{P^2}{2} \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Some textbooks quote $T = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ assuming $\omega_e$ and $\omega_m$ are used interchangeably. Always state which **angular speed** (mechanical or electrical) and how the pole count is being interpreted (P = total poles vs. P = pole pairs). The KTU 2024 board examiner's key accepts both conventions **only if the convention is explicitly declared** at the start of the solution.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between Back-EMF and Torque Constants

**Energy conservation argument:**

In a lossless conversion from electrical to mechanical energy:

$$P_{\text{elec}} = P_{\text{mech}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e \cdot i = T \cdot \omega$$

Now, $e = k_e \omega$ and $T = k_t i$, so:

$$k_e \omega \cdot i = k_t i \cdot \omega$$

Dividing both sides by $i \omega$ (non-zero):

$$\boxed{\,k_e = k_t\,}$$

**In SI units**, $k_e$ (in V·s/rad) is **numerically equal** to $k_t$ (in N·m/A).

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant result is frequently tested in KTU 3-mark questions. The dimensional equivalence $[V \cdot s / rad] = [N \cdot m / A]$ comes from $V = J/C$, $s$, $rad$ (dimensionless), $N \cdot m = J$, and $A = C/s$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — BLDC Motor System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    DCSupply["DC LINK SUPPLY"] --> DCBus["DC BUS CAPACITOR"]
    DCBus --> Inverter["THREE PHASE VOLTAGE SOURCE INVERTER VSI"]
    Inverter -->|Phase A| SA["STATOR PHASE A WINDING"]
    Inverter -->|Phase B| SB["STATOR PHASE B WINDING"]
    Inverter -->|Phase C| SC["STATOR PHASE C WINDING"]
    SA --> Airgap["ROTATING AIR GAP FLUX"]
    SB --> Airgap
    SC --> Airgap
    Airgap --> RotorPM["ROTOR PERMANENT MAGNETS N S"]
    RotorPM --> Shaft["OUTPUT SHAFT MECHANICAL LOAD"]
    HallA["HALL SENSOR A 120 DEG"] --> Controller["MICROCONTROLLER DSP GATE DRIVER"]
    HallB["HALL SENSOR B 120 DEG"] --> Controller
    HallC["HALL SENSOR C 120 DEG"] --> Controller
    RotorPM -->|Magnetic Feedback| HallA
    RotorPM -->|Magnetic Feedback| HallB
    RotorPM -->|Magnetic Feedback| HallC
    Controller -->|PWM Gating Signals| Inverter
    Controller -->|Speed Torque Reference| UserInput["USER REFERENCE COMMAND"]
    UserInput --> Controller
```

```mermaid

    subgraph Stator_Assembly
        SCore["STATOR LAMINATED CORE SILICON STEEL"]
        SSlots["STATOR SLOTS 6 / 12 / 36"]
```

## 4.3 Component-by-Component Schematic Summary

The following table summarises the **functional flow** shown in the diagrams:

| # | Block | Material / Type | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DC Supply** | Battery / Rectifier | Provides DC link voltage |
| 2 | **DC Bus Capacitor** | Electrolytic / Film | Filters DC link ripple |
| 3 | **VSI Inverter** | 6 IGBT / MOSFET bridge | Converts DC to 3-phase AC |
| 4 | **Stator Windings** | Copper, 3-phase | Create rotating magnetic field |
| 5 | **Air Gap** | Air, 0.5–2 mm | Couples stator and rotor fields |
| 6 | **Rotor PMs** | NdFeB / SmCo | Provides constant field flux |
| 7 | **Hall Sensors** | 3 sensors at 120° | Detect rotor position |
| 8 | **Controller** | DSP / Microcontroller | Commutation logic + PWM |
| 9 | **Shaft** | Stainless steel | Transmits mechanical output |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Define a PMBLDC motor. List its main constructional parts.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Permanent Magnet Brushless DC (PMBLDC) motor** is a synchronous machine in which the field is produced by permanent magnets on the rotor, and the armature windings are placed on the stator. Commutation is achieved electronically using a three-phase voltage source inverter gated by rotor-position feedback.

**Main constructional parts (1 mark each component group):**
1. **Stator** — laminated silicon-steel core with three-phase distributed windings placed in slots; housed in a frame.
2. **Rotor** — permanent magnets (NdFeB / SmCo / Ferrite) mounted on or inside a back-iron core, fixed to a non-magnetic shaft.
3. **Position Sensors** — three Hall-effect sensors spaced 120° electrical apart, providing rotor-position feedback.
4. **Power Electronic Commutator** — three-phase VSI with 6 switches, gate drivers, and a microcontroller/DSP-based controller.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — Why is the rotor of a BLDC motor placed inside and the stator outside? Mention two advantages.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a BLDC motor, the **armature (winding) is on the stator** and the **field (magnets) is on the rotor**. This is the reverse of a conventional DC motor. **Reasons (1.5 marks for explanation + 1.5 marks for advantages):**

- The rotor carries only magnets, so it has **no copper losses (zero I²R loss)** in the rotor, increasing efficiency.
- Heat generated in the stator windings is easier to **dissipate** because the stator is in direct contact with the outer frame (larger surface area).
- Mechanical commutation hardware (brushes, commutator) is eliminated since the armature does not rotate — this gives **high reliability and long life**.
- The **rotor inertia is reduced** (no windings or commutator on the rotor), giving **better dynamic response**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with KTU Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong denominator (60 vs 120):** Many students forget to divide by 2 for wave winding. Always state A=2 explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing P and P/2:** If the question says *"4-pole BLDC"*, use P=4 in the EMF equation, NOT P/2.
> 3. **Ignoring winding factor $k_w$:** For concentrated windings $k_w \approx 1$, but you must state this assumption.
> 4. **Confusing BLDC and PMSM:** The torque equation $T = k_t I$ with square-wave current is BLDC-specific. Don't use the PMSM d-q form unless explicitly asked.
> 5. **Unit errors:** $E_b$ in volts, $\Phi$ in Weber, $N$ in **rpm** (not rad/s) when using the $E = P\Phi ZN/(60A)$ formula.

---

### Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **constructional details of a PMBLDC motor**. Highlight the function of each part. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **EMF equation** of a PMBLDC motor. A 4-pole, 3-phase BLDC motor has 36 slots, single-layer wave winding with 10 conductors per coil, flux per pole of 0.015 Wb, and runs at 1500 rpm. Calculate the EMF per phase. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Constructional Details (7 marks):**

Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes. The motor consists of:

1. **Stator** (2 marks): Laminated silicon-steel core with 36 slots. The three-phase windings (Phases A, B, C) are distributed, each occupying 12 slots, displaced by 120° electrical. Each coil has 10 turns (i.e., 20 conductors per coil-side) for a total of $Z = 36 \times 10 = 360$ conductors. End-shields house the bearings and provide the mounting structure.

2. **Rotor** (2 marks): Surface-mounted NdFeB permanent magnets on a mild-steel back-iron core. With P=4 poles, there are 2 N-poles and 2 S-poles arranged alternately. The non-magnetic shaft connects to the mechanical load.

3. **Position Sensors** (1.5 marks): Three Hall-effect sensors mounted on the stator end-shield at 120° mechanical spacing, providing 3-bit position codes (H1, H2, H3).

4. **Power Electronic Commutator** (1.5 marks): Three-phase VSI with 6 IGBTs; gate-driver circuit; microcontroller running a commutation lookup table. The DC link is supplied from a battery or rectified mains.

**Functional flow:** Controller reads Hall codes → applies commutation table → drives VSI → produces rotating field → rotor PMs lock on → mechanical torque on shaft.

**(b) EMF Equation and Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1 above:

$$E_{ph} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}$$

**Substitution (3 marks):** Given: P = 4, Φ = 0.015 Wb, N = 1500 rpm.

Calculate Z: 36 slots × 10 conductors/coil = 360 conductors total. For 3-phase wave winding, A = 2.

$$E_{ph} = \frac{4 \times 0.015 \times 360 \times 1500}{60 \times 2}$$

$$E_{ph} = \frac{4 \times 0.015 \times 360 \times 1500}{120}$$

$$E_{ph} = \frac{32400}{120} = 270 \text{ V}$$

**Result statement (1 mark):** The EMF induced per phase is **270 V**. If line-to-line EMF is required, $E_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot E_{ph} = \sqrt{3} \times 270 \approx 467.6$ V for a star-connected machine.

---

### Q3. Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern]

**(a)** Compare the **surface-mounted PM (SPM)** and **interior PM (IPM)** rotor constructions of a BLDC motor. List two advantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-pole, 3-phase BLDC motor runs at 3000 rpm. Each phase has 240 conductors, wave-wound. The flux per pole is 8 mWb. Calculate: (i) the EMF per phase, (ii) the mechanical power developed if the phase current is 20 A, and (iii) the electromagnetic torque. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) SPM vs IPM Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | SPM Rotor | IPM Rotor |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnet location** | Mounted on rotor surface | Buried inside rotor iron |
| **Air-gap flux** | Trapezoidal | Sinusoidal (with reluctance variation) |
| **Mechanical strength** | Low (need sleeve for high speeds) | High (magnets protected by iron) |
| **EMF waveform** | Trapezoidal (BLDC mode) | Sinusoidal (PMSM mode) |
| **Reluctance torque** | Zero | Significant (additional torque component) |
| **Speed range** | Limited by sleeve strength | Wide (field-weeding possible) |
| **Rotor losses** | Higher (eddy currents in magnets) | Lower (magnets shielded by iron) |
| **Manufacturing cost** | Lower | Higher (complex geometry) |
| **Applications** | Computer fans, EVs, appliances | EV traction, high-speed spindles |

**SPM Advantages (1 mark each):** (i) Simple construction and low manufacturing cost. (ii) Concentrated air-gap flux gives high starting torque.

**IPM Advantages (1 mark each):** (i) Robust mechanically, suitable for high-speed operation. (ii) Combined PM and reluctance torque gives higher power density and wider constant-power speed range.

*(2 marks for tabular comparison + 2 marks for advantages of each = 4 marks; balance 3 marks for diagram/sketch description and conclusion.)*

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** P = 6, N = 3000 rpm, Z = 240, wave-wound (A = 2), Φ = 8 mWb = 0.008 Wb, $I_a$ = 20 A.

**(i) EMF per phase (2 marks):**

$$E_{ph} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A} = \frac{6 \times 0.008 \times 240 \times 3000}{60 \times 2}$$

$$E_{ph} = \frac{34560}{120} = 288 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Mechanical power developed (2.5 marks):**

For a 3-phase BLDC with trapezoidal EMF and 120° conduction, two phases conduct at any instant. Total mechanical power:

$$P_m = 2 \cdot E_{ph} \cdot I_a = 2 \times 288 \times 20 = 11520 \text{ W} = 11.52 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Electromagnetic torque (2.5 marks):**

Angular speed:

$$\omega_m = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi = 314.159 \text{ rad/s}$$

Torque:

$$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_m} = \frac{11520}{314.159} = 36.67 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final answer box:** $E_{ph} = 288$ V, $P_m = 11.52$ kW, $T = 36.67$ N·m.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and pin it on your study wall — these are the high-frequency KTU items.**

**Core Definition:**
- PMBLDC = Permanent Magnet on Rotor + Stator Armature + Electronic Commutation (inverter + Hall sensors).

**Constructional Pillars (must memorise):**
1. **Stator** = laminated Si-steel core + 3-phase windings (wave or distributed) in slots.
2. **Rotor** = PMs (NdFeB preferred) on back-iron + non-magnetic shaft.
3. **Sensors** = 3 Hall sensors at 120° electrical spacing.
4. **Inverter** = 3-phase VSI, 6 switches, with PWM control.
5. **Controller** = DSP/MCU running 6-step commutation logic.

**Rotor Types to Distinguish:**
- SPM → Trapezoidal EMF → BLDC mode → Low rotor mechanical strength.
- IPM → Sinusoidal EMF → PMSM mode → Includes reluctance torque.

**EMF Formula (memorise verbatim):**

$$E_{ph} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}$$

- Wave: A = 2
- Lap: A = P

**Torque Formula (memorise):**

$$T = k_t \cdot I_a \quad ; \quad k_e = k_t \text{ (SI units)}$$

**Frequency Relation (for exam derivations):**

$$f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120} \text{ Hz}$$

**Common KTU Pitfalls (REPEAT these before the exam):**
- ❌ Don't write **P/2** in the EMF equation — write **P** (total poles).
- ❌ Don't confuse BLDC (trapezoidal, square-wave) with PMSM (sinusoidal).
- ❌ Don't forget to state A = 2 for wave winding.
- ❌ Don't drop units (V, Wb, A, rad/s) in numerical answers.
- ❌ Don't omit the inverter + sensors when describing constructional parts.

**Key Advantages of BLDC (frequently asked in 2-mark questions):**
- High efficiency (no rotor copper loss, no brush friction).
- High power density.
- Long life and low maintenance.
- Better speed-torque characteristics.
- No sparking — safe in explosive environments.

**Key Disadvantages (asked as 1-mark contrast):**
- Higher initial cost (magnets + electronics).
- Need for rotor-position sensors or sensorless estimator.
- Demagnetisation risk at high temperature (especially NdFeB).
- Difficult to control at very low speeds (sensorless schemes struggle).

**Trick to Remember Pole–Phase Combination:**
> 6-step commutation = **3 phases × 2 switching states per phase = 6 states per electrical cycle**, driven by **3 Hall sensors giving 2³ = 8 codes (one is "invalid" 000/111)**. The 6 valid codes map 1:1 onto the 6 inverter switching states.

This is THE foundational concept of BLDC control — make sure you can sketch the **commutation table** (Hall codes H1H2H3 → Switches T1, T2, …, T6) from memory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
