topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Permanent Magnets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Permanent Magnet (PM)** is a piece of ferromagnetic material (typically a hard magnetic material with high coercivity and high remanence) that, once magnetized in a strong external magnetic field, retains a significant amount of magnetization indefinitely without the need for an external current-carrying coil or continuous electrical excitation. In the context of *Special Electrical Machines* (PEEET743, Module 3), permanent magnets form the **field excitation system** in machines such as:

- **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM)**
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors**
- **Hybrid Stepper Motors**
- **Axial Flux Machines**
- **Reluctance-assisted / Interior PM Machines**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-style):** A permanent magnet is a magnet that produces a persistent magnetic field due to the aligned magnetic domains within a hard magnetic material, characterized by high **remanence (Br)**, high **coercivity (Hc)**, and a high **maximum energy product (BH)max**, all of which are read directly from the second quadrant of the B-H hysteresis loop known as the *demagnetization curve*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spring That Never Uncoils"

Imagine a **mechanical spring** that has been compressed and then locked in position by a special clamp. Even after you remove the force, the spring still wants to "push back" — the clamp merely holds it in place.

- A **permanent magnet** behaves similarly: the **external magnetizing field** is the "compressing force," the **magnetic domains** are the compressed spring, and the **coercivity** of the material is the "clamp" that prevents the domains from springing back to a random orientation.
- A **soft magnetic material** (like pure iron) is a weak spring with a flimsy clamp — it holds magnetism only while the external field is ON.
- A **hard magnetic material** (like NdFeB) is a strong spring with a very strong clamp — it keeps the field forever.

This analogy is the foundation for understanding why the *demagnetization curve* and the *energy product* are the two most critical parameters for PM selection in special machines.

## 1.3 Role of Permanent Magnets in Special Electrical Machines

In conventional DC or wound-field synchronous machines, the rotor field is produced by a DC current flowing through field windings. This requires:

- Slip rings and brushes (wear and tear)
- Continuous electrical power for excitation
- Heat dissipation in field coils
- Additional copper losses

A **PM rotor** eliminates all of the above by embedding magnets directly on or inside the rotor. The trade-off, however, is that the field strength is now **fixed by the magnet material**, which makes magnet selection a *design-critical* decision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric Highlighted in KTU 2024 Module 3:**
> The choice of permanent magnet material for any special machine is governed primarily by:
> 1. **Remanent flux density (Br)** in **Tesla (T)**
> 2. **Coercivity (Hc)** in **kA/m** (or **kOe** in CGS)
> 3. **Maximum Energy Product (BH)max** in **kJ/m³** (or **MGOe**)
> 4. **Curie Temperature (Tc)** in **°C**
> 5. **Temperature coefficients** of Br and Hc
> 6. **Recoil permeability (μrec)** and **recoil line slope**
> 7. **Mechanical strength and cost**

## 1.4 Classification of Permanent Magnet Materials

Permanent magnet materials are classified into **four major families** that the KTU examiner loves to test:

| Family | Examples | Era |
|---|---|---|
| **Conventional / Classical** | Carbon steel, Chromium steel, Cobalt steel | Pre-1930s |
| **AlNiCo** | Alnico 5, Alnico 8, Alnico 9 | 1930s–1950s |
| **Hard Ferrites (Ceramic)** | Barium Ferrite (BaFe₁₂O₁₉), Strontium Ferrite (SrFe₁₂O₁₉) | 1950s onwards |
| **Rare Earth (RE)** | Samarium Cobalt (SmCo₅, Sm₂Co₁₇), Neodymium Iron Boron (NdFeB) | 1970s onwards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rare earth magnets** (NdFeB and SmCo) dominate **modern special electrical machines** because they offer **(BH)max values 5–10× higher** than ferrites and AlNiCo, allowing compact, high-torque-density designs required in **EV traction motors, robotics, and aerospace actuators**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The B-H Hysteresis Loop and the Demagnetization Curve

Every ferromagnetic material, when subjected to a cyclic magnetizing field **H**, exhibits a **hysteresis loop** in the **B-H plane**, where **B** is the magnetic flux density (in Tesla) and **H** is the magnetizing field intensity (in A/m).

The **second quadrant** of this loop (where **H is negative** and **B is positive**) is called the **demagnetization curve**, and it is the *most important* characteristic of a permanent magnet material.

### Key points read directly from the demagnetization curve:

1. **Remanence (Br)**: The value of B when H = 0. It represents the *residual flux density* retained by the magnet after the external field is removed. Higher Br means stronger magnet.
2. **Coercivity (Hc)**: The magnitude of H required to reduce B back to zero. Higher Hc means better resistance to demagnetization.
3. **Intrinsic Coercivity (Hci or jHc)**: The H required to reduce the intrinsic flux density **Bi = B − μ₀H** to zero. This is *always greater* than Hc.
4. **Maximum Energy Product (BH)max**: The maximum value of the product **B × H** along the demagnetization curve, measured in **kJ/m³**. This is the **single most important figure of merit** for sizing a permanent magnet in a magnetic circuit.
5. **Recoil Line**: A near-straight line that the operating point of the magnet follows when an external demagnetizing field is applied and then removed. Its slope is the **recoil permeability μrec ≈ μ₀**.

## 2.2 Magnetic Energy and the Energy Product Concept

The magnetic energy stored per unit volume in the air gap of a magnetic circuit is given by:

$$w = \frac{B \cdot H}{2}$$

For a permanent magnet operating in the second quadrant (B positive, H negative in magnet convention), the magnitude of energy product is:

$$(BH) = B \times \vert H \vert$$

The **maximum energy product (BH)max** corresponds to the operating point on the demagnetization curve that yields the **smallest possible magnet volume** for a given energy requirement. This is why magnet manufacturers grade materials like **N35, N42, N52 (NdFeB)** or **Sm₂Co₁₇ grade 2:17** — the grade number is closely tied to the **(BH)max** value.

## 2.3 The Load Line and Operating Point

In an actual magnetic circuit, the permanent magnet is connected to a *permeable return path* (the rotor iron and air gap). The magnet's operating point is found at the **intersection** of:

- The **demagnetization curve** of the magnet (B vs. H characteristic), and
- The **load line** (also called the **permeance line** or **shear line**) drawn from the origin with slope **−μ₀ · (Lm / Lg)** (or in terms of permeance coefficients, **−Pc**), where **Lm** is magnet length, **Lg** is air-gap length.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Permeance Coefficient (Pc):**
> The **permeance coefficient** (sometimes called the **load line slope** or **operating slope**) is defined as:
> 
> $$P_c = \frac{B_m}{H_m} = \mu_{rec} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g}$$
>
> A *short* magnet in a *long* air gap ⇒ small **Pc** ⇒ operating point sits near the *knee* of the demagnetization curve ⇒ **risk of irreversible demagnetization**.
> A *long* magnet in a *short* air gap ⇒ large **Pc** ⇒ operating point sits high on the demagnetization curve ⇒ safer and more efficient operation.

## 2.4 Recoil Behavior and Irreversible Losses

When a PM experiences a *transient* demagnetizing field (e.g., during a short-circuit fault, or at high armature reaction in a PMSM), its operating point moves down the demagnetization curve along the **load line** to a point **P₁**. When the disturbance is removed, the operating point does **not** retrace the same path; instead, it moves along a new line called the **recoil line**, with slope **μrec**, reaching a new point **P₂** that is *lower* than the original **Br**.

- If **P₂** lies *above* the "knee" of the demagnetization curve, the loss is **reversible** (recoverable with re-magnetization).
- If **P₂** is pushed *below the knee*, the loss becomes **irreversible** and the magnet is permanently weakened.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students often confuse **coercivity Hc** (where B = 0) with **intrinsic coercivity Hci** (where M = 0, i.e., J = 0). In rare-earth magnets, **Hci ≫ Hc** (often 2–3× larger), which is what makes NdFeB highly resistant to demagnetization compared to ferrites.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnetic flux density | $B = \mu_0(H + M)$ | T | Total flux density in material |
| 2 | Vacuum permeability | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | H/m | Fundamental constant |
| 3 | Relative permeability | $\mu_r = B / (\mu_0 H)$ | — | Material property |
| 4 | Remanence | $B_r = \mu_0 M_r$ | T | Flux density at H = 0 |
| 5 | Coercivity | $H_c$ (where B = 0) | A/m | Resistance to demagnetization |
| 6 | Intrinsic coercivity | $H_{ci}$ (where M = 0) | A/m | True resistance to demagnetization |
| 7 | Maximum energy product | $(BH)_{max}$ | kJ/m³ | Figure of merit for PM size |
| 8 | Stored energy density | $w = \frac{1}{2} B \cdot H$ | J/m³ | Energy stored per unit volume |
| 9 | Permeance coefficient | $P_c = B_m / H_m$ | — | Operating point determinant |
| 10 | Recoil permeability | $\mu_{rec} \approx \mu_0 \cdot 1.05$ | H/m | Slope of recoil line |
| 11 | Curie's law (above Tc) | $M = C \cdot H / T$ | — | Magnetic order destroyed above Curie temperature |
| 12 | Temperature coefficient of Br | $\alpha_{Br} = \frac{1}{B_r} \frac{dB_r}{dT}$ | %/°C or ppm/°C | Br variation with temperature |
| 13 | Temperature coefficient of Hc | $\alpha_{Hc} = \frac{1}{H_c} \frac{dH_c}{dT}$ | %/°C | Hc variation with temperature |
| 14 | Operating flux density | $B_m = \frac{B_r \cdot P_c}{P_c + 1}$ | T | B at operating point |
| 15 | Magnet volume estimate | $V_m = \frac{W_{gap}}{(BH)_{max}}$ | m³ | Minimum volume required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In a PMSM or BLDC design, the air-gap flux density **Bg** is directly set by the magnet's **Br** scaled by the rotor geometry and leakage coefficient. The choice of magnet material therefore **dictates the torque density, power density, and efficiency** of the entire machine. Modern EV traction motors use **NdFeB (N42SH or N52UH grade)** to achieve **>5 kW/kg** power density, whereas ferrite-based PM motors can only achieve **1–2 kW/kg** but are dramatically cheaper.

## 2.6 Comparison of PM Materials (KTU 2024 Module 3 — High Yield)

| Property | AlNiCo | Hard Ferrite (SrFe₁₂O₁₉) | SmCo (2:17) | NdFeB (N42) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Br (T)** | 0.7 – 1.3 | 0.35 – 0.45 | 0.95 – 1.10 | 1.10 – 1.30 |
| **Hci (kA/m)** | 50 – 160 | 200 – 350 | 700 – 2000 | 1000 – 2500 |
| **(BH)max (kJ/m³)** | 30 – 85 | 25 – 40 | 150 – 240 | 250 – 420 |
| **Curie Temp (°C)** | 800 – 850 | 450 – 460 | 700 – 850 | 310 – 340 |
| **Max working temp (°C)** | 500 | 250 | 300 – 350 | 80 – 220 |
| **αBr (%/°C)** | −0.02 | −0.20 | −0.03 | −0.10 to −0.13 |
| **Relative cost** | Medium | Very Low | High | High (volatile) |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Poor (needs coating) |
| **Application** | Old DC machines, instruments | Small motors, speakers | Aerospace, military | EV motors, robotics, hard drives |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stored Magnetic Energy and (BH)max Location

The energy stored per unit volume in a magnetic field is given by the volume integral of the field energy density:

$$w = \int_0^{B} H \, dB$$

For a permanent magnet operating in the **second quadrant** of the B-H plane, we substitute $H$ from the demagnetization curve. For a *linear* demagnetization curve (an idealization often used in textbook analysis), the curve can be written as:

$$B = B_r + \mu_{rec} \cdot H$$

Since $H$ is negative in the second quadrant and $B$ is positive, the magnitude of the energy product is:

$$(BH) = \left(B_r + \mu_{rec} H\right) \cdot \vert H \vert$$

Let $x = \vert H \vert$, then $B = B_r - \mu_{rec} x$ (because $H$ is negative). Therefore:

$$(BH) = (B_r - \mu_{rec} x) \cdot x = B_r x - \mu_{rec} x^2$$

To find the maximum, we differentiate with respect to $x$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d(BH)}{dx} = B_r - 2\mu_{rec} x = 0$$

Solving for the optimal $x$:

$$x_{opt} = \frac{B_r}{2\mu_{rec}}$$

Substituting back:

$$(BH)_{max} = B_r \cdot \frac{B_r}{2\mu_{rec}} - \mu_{rec} \cdot \left(\frac{B_r}{2\mu_{rec}}\right)^2 = \frac{B_r^2}{4\mu_{rec}}$$

$$\boxed{(BH)_{max} = \frac{B_r^2}{4\mu_{rec}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This derivation shows that the **maximum energy product is maximized when the operating point lies exactly at the midpoint** of the linear demagnetization curve, where $B = B_r/2$ and $H = H_c/2$. This is the classical design point that minimizes the magnet volume for a given magnetic energy requirement.

## 3.2 Derivation: Operating Point in a Simple Magnetic Circuit

Consider a toroidal magnetic circuit consisting of a permanent magnet of length $L_m$ and cross-section $A_m$, connected to a soft-iron yoke of negligible reluctance, with an air gap of length $L_g$ and cross-section $A_g$.

By **Ampere's law** around the closed loop:

$$H_m L_m + H_g L_g = 0$$

Because $H_m$ is negative (demagnetizing) and $H_g$ is positive, this simplifies to:

$$H_m L_m = -H_g L_g = \frac{B_g}{\mu_0} L_g$$

(The negative sign indicates the magnet is the source; in conventional sign analysis, we use the magnitude.)

By **conservation of flux** (neglecting leakage):

$$B_m A_m = B_g A_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_m = B_g \cdot \frac{A_g}{A_m}$$

Combining these two equations and using $B_m = B_r + \mu_{rec} H_m$:

$$B_m = B_r - \mu_{rec} \cdot \frac{B_g L_g}{\mu_0 L_m}$$

Solving for $B_m$ in terms of the geometry gives the load-line equation. The slope of the load line in the $B_m$–$H_m$ plane is:

$$\text{Slope} = -\mu_{rec} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g} \cdot \frac{A_g}{A_m} = -\mu_0 \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g} \cdot \frac{A_g}{A_m} \cdot \frac{\mu_{rec}}{\mu_0}$$

In simplified form, defining the **permeance coefficient** $P_c$:

$$\boxed{P_c = \frac{B_m}{H_m} = \mu_{rec} \cdot \frac{L_m \cdot A_g}{L_g \cdot A_m}}$$

The **operating point** is found where this load line intersects the demagnetization curve.

## 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Magnet Volume for a Given Air-Gap Energy

The total energy stored in the air gap of a magnetic circuit is:

$$W_{gap} = \frac{B_g^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot V_g$$

where $V_g = A_g L_g$ is the air-gap volume.

For a magnet of volume $V_m = A_m L_m$, and assuming the magnet operates at its $(BH)_{max}$ point, the relationship between magnet volume and air-gap energy is:

$$W_{gap} = (BH)_{max} \cdot V_m \cdot \eta_{util}$$

where $\eta_{util}$ is the utilization efficiency (typically 0.1–0.25 in real machines, because not all magnet energy converts to useful air-gap flux due to leakage and fringing).

Solving for the **minimum required magnet volume**:

$$\boxed{V_{m,min} = \frac{W_{gap}}{(BH)_{max} \cdot \eta_{util}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This is the formula magnet suppliers and machine designers use to estimate the *magnet cost* of a motor. For an EV traction motor delivering 100 kW mechanical power with $\eta_{util} \approx 0.15$ and using NdFeB with $(BH)_{max} = 350$ kJ/m³, the magnet mass is typically around **1–2 kg**, whereas a ferrite-based motor of the same rating would require **10–15 kg** of ferrite magnets.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection Criteria for PM in Special Machines

| Selection Criterion | Why It Matters | AlNiCo | Ferrite | SmCo | NdFeB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| High torque density | EV, robotics | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| High temperature operation | Aerospace, traction | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓ (with grade) |
| Low cost / high volume | Appliances, fans | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Resistance to demagnetization | Fault-tolerant drives | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✓ |
| Corrosion resistance | Marine, humid | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✗ (Ni-Cu-Ni plating) |
| Linear demagnetization curve | Servo / precision drives | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Low temperature coefficient | Instrumentation | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEEET743]`

**Q: Define the term "Remanent flux density (Br)" and "Coercivity (Hc)" of a permanent magnet. Why is intrinsic coercivity (Hci) considered more important than Hc for rare-earth magnets?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Remanent flux density (Br) [1 mark]**: It is the value of magnetic flux density **B** that remains in a permanent magnet material when the external magnetizing field **H** is reduced to zero. It is measured in **Tesla (T)** and is read directly from the demagnetization curve at the point where the curve crosses the **B-axis**.
- **Coercivity (Hc) [1 mark]**: It is the magnitude of the reverse magnetizing field intensity **H** required to reduce the residual flux density **B** to zero. It is measured in **A/m** (or **kOe** in CGS units) and is read where the demagnetization curve crosses the **H-axis**.
- **Why Hci > Hc for rare-earth magnets [1 mark]**: Intrinsic coercivity **Hci** is the field required to reduce the **magnetization M** (or **J = μ₀M**) to zero, which is *always greater* than the **B = 0** coercivity Hc. In rare-earth magnets like NdFeB and SmCo, the difference between Hci and Hc is very large (Hci is typically 2–3× Hc), which means the magnet retains its magnetization even under very strong opposing fields — making it highly resistant to irreversible demagnetization. This is the *key reason* rare-earth magnets dominate high-performance special machines.

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEET743]`

**Q: What is meant by the "Maximum Energy Product" (BH)max of a permanent magnet? Why is it called the figure of merit for sizing a magnet?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(BH)max definition [1.5 marks]**: The maximum energy product is the **peak value of the product B × |H|** along the second-quadrant demagnetization curve of a permanent magnet. It is measured in **kJ/m³** (or **MGOe** in CGS) and represents the *maximum useful magnetic energy* that can be extracted from a unit volume of the magnet material.
- **Figure of merit for sizing [1.5 marks]**: For a given air-gap energy requirement $W_{gap}$, the minimum magnet volume is given by $V_{m,min} = W_{gap} / [(BH)_{max} \cdot \eta_{util}]$. A higher (BH)max directly translates to a **smaller, lighter, and more compact magnet** for the same energy. Therefore, (BH)max is the *single most important figure of merit* used by design engineers to compare different magnet materials (e.g., N42 NdFeB at ~330 kJ/m³ vs. Ferrite at ~30 kJ/m³ — NdFeB needs only **1/11th the volume** of ferrite for the same energy).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat diagram of the B-H demagnetization curve, explain the significance of Remanence (Br), Coercivity (Hc), and Maximum Energy Product (BH)max. How does the operating point get fixed in a magnetic circuit with an air gap?

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the four major families of permanent magnet materials (AlNiCo, Hard Ferrite, SmCo, NdFeB) in terms of Br, Hci, (BH)max, Curie temperature, and cost. Mention one specific application of each in special electrical machines.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Drawing the demagnetization curve [2 marks]:**
Draw the B-H hysteresis loop in all four quadrants. Highlight the **second quadrant** as the demagnetization curve. Label the **B-axis** (vertical) and **H-axis** (horizontal) with units T and A/m respectively. Mark the four critical points:

- **Br** on the B-axis (H = 0)
- **Hc** on the H-axis (B = 0)
- **(BH)max** point inside the second quadrant
- **Knee of the curve** below which irreversible demagnetization occurs

**Step 2 — Significance of Br [1.5 marks]:**
Br is the **remanent flux density** — the flux density retained by the magnet after the external field is removed. It directly determines the **no-load air-gap flux density** $B_g \approx B_r \cdot (A_m/A_g) \cdot (1 - \sigma)$ where σ is the leakage coefficient. Higher Br means higher torque in PMSM/BLDC.

**Step 3 — Significance of Hc [1.5 marks]:**
Hc is the **coercivity** — the resistance of the magnet to demagnetization. A magnet with high Hc is more stable under external demagnetizing fields such as armature reaction. For PMSM drives, Hc must be sufficiently high to prevent demagnetization under transient short-circuit and overload conditions.

**Step 4 — Significance of (BH)max [1 mark]:**
(BH)max is the **figure of merit** that determines the minimum volume of magnet needed to produce a given magnetic energy. It is the maximum of the B-H product on the demagnetization curve.

**Step 5 — Operating point determination [1 mark]:**
The operating point Q is the **intersection of the demagnetization curve and the load line** drawn from origin with slope $-\mu_{rec} \cdot (L_m/L_g)$ (or equivalently, with permeance coefficient $P_c = B_m/H_m$). This is the *actual* working flux density of the magnet in the magnetic circuit.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Tabular comparison [5 marks] — see table below:**

| Property | AlNiCo | Hard Ferrite | SmCo (2:17) | NdFeB (N42) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Br (T) | 0.7 – 1.3 | 0.35 – 0.45 | 0.95 – 1.10 | 1.10 – 1.30 |
| Hci (kA/m) | 50 – 160 | 200 – 350 | 700 – 2000 | 1000 – 2500 |
| (BH)max (kJ/m³) | 30 – 85 | 25 – 40 | 150 – 240 | 250 – 420 |
| Curie temp (°C) | 800 – 850 | 450 – 460 | 700 – 850 | 310 – 340 |
| Cost | Medium | Very low | High | High (volatile) |

**Step 2 — Applications [2 marks]:**
- **AlNiCo**: Used in **instrumentation** (galvanometers, analog meters) due to its stable, near-linear demagnetization curve and high-temperature tolerance.
- **Hard Ferrite**: Used in **small appliance motors, ceiling fans, and loudspeaker magnets** where cost is critical and high torque density is not required.
- **SmCo**: Used in **aerospace actuators, military servo motors, and high-temperature downhole tools** where operation above 250 °C is required.
- **NdFeB**: Used in **EV traction motors (Tesla Model 3, Toyota Prius), industrial robot servos, hard disk drive spindle motors, and wind turbine generators** where maximum torque/power density is essential.

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of "permeance coefficient" (Pc) and "recoil line" in permanent magnet circuits. Show, with a diagram, how irreversible demagnetization occurs when the operating point is pushed below the knee of the demagnetization curve.

**(b) [7 marks]** A ferrite magnet (Br = 0.4 T, Hc = 250 kA/m, recoil permeability μrec = 1.05 μ₀) is used in a small DC motor. The magnet has length Lm = 5 mm and the effective air gap is Lg = 1.5 mm. Compute: (i) the permeance coefficient, (ii) the operating point flux density Bm, and (iii) the maximum energy product of the magnet.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Permeance coefficient [2 marks]:**
The permeance coefficient $P_c$ (also called load-line slope) is defined as the ratio of the flux density to the field intensity in the magnet at the operating point:

$$P_c = \frac{B_m}{H_m} = \mu_{rec} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g}$$

It is a **dimensionless** number that depends only on the *geometry* of the magnetic circuit and the recoil permeability of the magnet. It determines *where* on the demagnetization curve the operating point lies.

- $P_c > 4$: operating point near Br (magnet is "long" relative to gap) — safe.
- $P_c \approx 1$: operating point near (BH)max — most efficient use of magnet.
- $P_c < 1$: operating point near the knee — **risk of irreversible demagnetization**.

**Step 2 — Recoil line [2 marks]:**
The recoil line is a near-straight line in the B-H plane that originates from the original operating point Q and has a slope equal to the **recoil permeability μrec** of the magnet. When an external demagnetizing field is *temporarily* applied (e.g., a transient armature reaction pulse), the operating point moves down the demagnetization curve from Q to a point P1 along the load line. When the disturbance is removed, the operating point does *not* return to Q but instead moves *up* along the new recoil line to a point Q' that is below Q in B.

**Step 3 — Irreversible demagnetization [3 marks]:**
If the transient field is strong enough that the operating point is pushed to a point P1 that lies *below the knee* of the demagnetization curve, then when the field is removed, the new recoil line originates from a point on the demagnetization curve *below the knee*, and the new operating point Q' is *significantly* lower than the original Q. The loss in flux density is **permanent (irreversible)**. The magnet must be **re-magnetized** by an external field greater than Hc to recover its original Br.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Original operating point Q at Bq, Hq"] --> B["Transient demagnetizing field applied"]
    B --> C["Operating point moves down to P1 along load line"]
    C --> D{"Is P1 above or below the knee?"}
    D -- Above knee --> E["Recoil line from P1 to Q-prime: REVERSIBLE loss"]
    D -- Below knee --> F["Recoil line from P1 to Q-prime: IRREVERSIBLE loss, new Q-prime much lower"]
    style A fill:#9ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#f66,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ff9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- $B_r = 0.4$ T
- $H_c = 250$ kA/m = $250 \times 10^3$ A/m
- $\mu_{rec} = 1.05 \mu_0 = 1.05 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.32 \times 10^{-6}$ H/m
- $L_m = 5$ mm = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $L_g = 1.5$ mm = $1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Step 1 — Permeance coefficient [2 marks]:**

$$P_c = \mu_{rec} \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_g} = 1.32 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$P_c = 1.32 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 3.333 = 4.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H/m}$$

Converting to dimensionless form (dividing by μ₀):

$$P_c / \mu_0 = \frac{4.4 \times 10^{-6}}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{4.4 \times 10^{-6}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.5$$

$$\boxed{P_c \approx 3.5 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**Step 2 — Operating flux density Bm [2 marks]:**
Using the load-line intersection with the demagnetization curve $B = B_r - \mu_{rec} H$, and the load line $B = -\mu_{rec} P_c H$:

$$B_m = B_r \cdot \frac{P_c}{P_c + 1} = 0.4 \cdot \frac{3.5}{3.5 + 1} = 0.4 \cdot \frac{3.5}{4.5}$$

$$B_m = 0.4 \cdot 0.7778 = 0.311 \text{ T}$$

$$\boxed{B_m \approx 0.311 \text{ T}}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum energy product [3 marks]:**
For a linear demagnetization curve, the (BH)max is:

$$(BH)_{max} = \frac{B_r^2}{4 \mu_{rec}} = \frac{(0.4)^2}{4 \times 1.32 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$(BH)_{max} = \frac{0.16}{5.28 \times 10^{-6}} = 30{,}303 \text{ J/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{(BH)_{max} \approx 30.3 \text{ kJ/m}^3}$$

This value is consistent with the typical (BH)max range of hard ferrites (25–40 kJ/m³), confirming the calculation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Hc and Hci** (1–2 marks lost): When asked for "coercivity," do NOT use intrinsic coercivity values. State both, but for design calculations involving the demagnetization curve, use Hc.
> 2. **Forgetting units in (BH)max** (1 mark lost): Always express the final answer in **kJ/m³** (not J/m³ or MGOe unless specifically requested).
> 3. **Sign errors in the load line equation** (2 marks lost): In the demagnetization curve, **H is negative** and **B is positive**. Students often write $B = B_r - \mu_{rec} H$ but then plug in a positive value of H. Always use $\vert H \vert$ in the product (BH)max.
> 4. **Ignoring the "knee" of the curve** (1–2 marks lost): For non-linear demagnetization curves (e.g., NdFeB), the linear approximation is invalid below the knee. For accurate results, read the (BH)max directly from the manufacturer's B-H curve.
> 5. **Not stating operating point is the intersection** (1 mark lost): The operating point is *not* a fixed value — it shifts with armature reaction, temperature, and load. Always say "intersection of the load line and the demagnetization curve."

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **permanent magnet** is a hard magnetic material that retains its magnetization after the external field is removed, characterized by **high Br**, **high Hc**, and **high (BH)max**.
- The **demagnetization curve** (second quadrant of the B-H loop) is the working characteristic of a PM, and is the single most important graph for PM selection and design.
- The **maximum energy product (BH)max** is the figure of merit for magnet sizing; the minimum magnet volume is $V_{m,min} = W_{gap} / [(BH)_{max} \cdot \eta_{util}]$.
- The **operating point** is the intersection of the demagnetization curve and the load line (permeance line) drawn with slope $-\mu_{rec} \cdot (L_m / L_g)$.
- The **permeance coefficient $P_c$** determines the operating point location: $P_c \approx 1$ is most efficient, $P_c > 4$ is safe, $P_c < 1$ risks demagnetization.
- **Intrinsic coercivity $H_{ci}$** is the true resistance to demagnetization (where M = 0), and is much greater than Hc for rare-earth magnets.
- **Recoil behavior**: When the operating point moves temporarily due to a disturbance, it returns along the **recoil line** (slope $\mu_{rec}$); if pushed below the **knee**, the loss is **irreversible**.
- **Four major PM families**: AlNiCo, Hard Ferrite, SmCo, NdFeB. Rare-earth magnets (SmCo, NdFeB) dominate modern special machines.
- **NdFeB** has the highest (BH)max (250–420 kJ/m³) but the lowest Curie temperature (310–340 °C) and poor corrosion resistance.
- **SmCo** offers a balance of high (BH)max, high Curie temperature (700–850 °C), and excellent corrosion resistance — ideal for aerospace and military.
- **Hard Ferrite** has the lowest (BH)max (25–40 kJ/m³) but the lowest cost — ideal for consumer appliances and small DC motors.
- **AlNiCo** has a near-linear demagnetization curve and excellent temperature stability — used in precision instruments and high-temperature legacy applications.
- **Curie temperature** $T_c$ is the temperature above which a material loses its ferromagnetic order entirely (PM is permanently demagnetized).
- **Temperature coefficients** $\alpha_{Br}$ and $\alpha_{Hc}$ quantify the variation of Br and Hc with temperature; critical for thermal design of PMSM drives.
- **Permeance coefficient** dimensionless form: $P_c = B_m / (H_m \cdot \mu_0)$ — useful for design tables and empirical magnet-selection charts.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Different Types of Permanent Magnet Machines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Permanent Magnet (PM) machines form a special class of synchronous machines where the field winding on the rotor is replaced by high-energy permanent magnets. The classification into **different types** is based primarily on the **rotor topology**, the **placement of magnets**, and the **magnetic flux path geometry** inside the rotor.

In **Module 3**, the focus shifts from the basic construction and working principle (covered earlier) to the **engineering classification** of PM machines. KTU examiners place heavy emphasis on this topic because the choice of rotor type directly dictates the machine's performance, control complexity, and application suitability.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a ceiling fan. In an ordinary induction motor, the rotor has copper/aluminum bars. Now imagine we replace those bars with **permanent bar magnets** — that's the basic idea of a PM machine. But here's the catch: *where* and *how* we attach those magnets on the rotor creates dramatically different behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of placing a strong refrigerator magnet on a spinning disc.
> - If you stick it on the **surface** (face) → it spins smoothly but magnets may fly off at high speeds (like a hockey puck on ice).
> - If you **embed** it inside the disc → safer, but harder to manufacture.
> - If you **bury** it deep inside → most robust, but flux gets distorted (reluctance changes).
>
> Each method represents a different "type" of PM machine with unique trade-offs.

---

## 1.2 Why Are There Different Types?

The need for different types arises from **engineering trade-offs** between:

| Parameter | Influence |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Mechanical Robustness** | Surface magnets may detach at high speeds |
| **Magnetic Saliency** | Interior magnets create reluctance variation (useful for reluctance torque) |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | Surface mounting is cheapest; interior mounting requires precision |
| **Flux Weakening Capability** | Critical for wide constant-power speed range (EVs) |
| **Cogging Torque** | Affects smooth low-speed operation |

---

## 1.3 Primary Classification — The KTU Board Perspective

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, PM machines are broadly classified into two main families, each containing further sub-types:

### Family A: Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM)
These have **sinusoidal back-EMF** and are excited by **3-phase sinusoidal AC**.

### Family B: Brushless DC Motors (BLDC)
These have **trapezoidal back-EMF** and are excited by **rectangular (square-wave) currents**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both families share **similar rotor topologies**, so the "different types" classification based on rotor structure applies to BOTH PMSM and BLDC machines.

---

## 1.4 The Five Standard Rotor Types (KTU Core Content)

The KTU syllabus specifically emphasizes the following rotor configurations:

1. **Surface-Mounted PMSM (SPM / SMPM)** — Magnets glued on the outer rotor surface.
2. **Surface-Inset PMSM** — Magnets embedded into shallow slots on the rotor surface.
3. **Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM)** — Magnets buried deep inside the rotor laminations.
4. **Buried (Interior) Magnet Variants** — Tangential, radial, and V-shaped arrangements.
5. **Spoke-Type / Rotor with Flux Concentrators** — Magnets arranged radially between steel poles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Br (Remanence)** ≈ **1.0 – 1.4 T** (for NdFeB magnets, the industrial standard)
> - **Energy Product (BH)max** ≈ **200 – 400 kJ/m³** (for high-grade rare-earth magnets)
> - **Relative Permeability (μr)** ≈ **1.05** (PMs behave almost like AIR magnetically)

---

## 1.5 Intuitive Summary Table

| Type | Magnet Location | Magnetic Symmetry | Typical Application |
|------|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| SPM | Outer surface | Non-salient (round rotor) | Servo drives, robotics |
| Surface-Inset | Shallow slots | Slightly salient | High-precision CNC |
| IPM (Radial) | Buried, radial orientation | Salient (Lq > Ld) | EV traction (Toyota Prius) |
| IPM (Tangential) | Buried, tangential | Highly salient | High-speed industrial |
| Spoke-Type | Between pole pieces | Salient with flux focusing | Compact drives |

This sets the foundation. Let us now proceed to deep theoretical analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Magnetic Theory Behind PM Machine Types

The classification of different types is rooted in the **magnetic equivalent circuit (MEC)** and the **d-q axis theory** of synchronous machines.

### 2.1.1 The Two Reactance Concept

For any synchronous machine, the rotor's magnetic geometry is described by two orthogonal axes:

- **Direct Axis (d-axis):** Aligned with the rotor field pole (along the magnet flux line).
- **Quadrature Axis (q-axis):** 90 electrical degrees ahead of the d-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Rule of PM Machine Classification:**
> If the magnetic path along d-axis and q-axis is IDENTICAL → the rotor is **NON-SALIENT** (round).
> If the paths DIFFER → the rotor is **SALIENT** (anisotropic).

Mathematically:

$$L_d \neq L_q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Salient Rotor (e.g., IPM)}$$

$$L_d = L_q = L_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Non-Salient Rotor (e.g., SPM)}$$

Where:
- $L_d$ = d-axis synchronous inductance (in Henry)
- $L_q$ = q-axis synchronous inductance (in Henry)
- $L_s$ = synchronous inductance (non-salient case)

---

## 2.2 Type 1: Surface-Mounted PMSM (SPM)

### Construction
- Arc-shaped permanent magnets are bonded to the outer cylindrical surface of the rotor core using high-strength adhesives.
- A **non-magnetic stainless-steel or carbon-fiber sleeve** is usually fitted over the magnets to hold them against centrifugal forces.

### Magnetic Analysis
- Since magnets have $\mu_r \approx 1$ (almost air), the effective air-gap becomes large.
- The d-axis path goes through magnet → air → iron → air → magnet (high reluctance).
- The q-axis path goes entirely through iron and air (same reluctance).
- **Result:** $L_d \approx L_q$ → **NON-SALIENT** rotor.

### Key Characteristics
- Sinusoidal air-gap flux distribution (excellent).
- No reluctance torque component (only magnet torque exists).
- Poor flux-weakening capability (limited constant-power range).
- Maximum speed is limited by magnet retention.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The non-salient nature of SPM means it can be analyzed using a **single-reactance** equivalent circuit, just like a wound-field synchronous motor.

---

## 2.3 Type 2: Surface-Inset PMSM

### Construction
- Magnets are embedded into **shallow rectangular slots** machined on the rotor surface.
- The outer surface remains cylindrical (no sleeve required).

### Magnetic Analysis
- d-axis: passes through magnet (high reluctance).
- q-axis: passes through iron bridges between slots (low reluctance).
- **Result:** $L_q > L_d$ slightly → **mildly salient**.

### Advantage
- Slight reluctance torque adds to total torque → higher torque density than SPM.
- Mechanically more robust than surface-mounted design.

---

## 2.4 Type 3: Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) Motor

### Construction
- Magnets are placed **inside the rotor laminations**, completely buried within slots/cavities punched into electrical steel sheets.
- Magnets are typically oriented **radially** or **tangentially**.

### Magnetic Analysis
- d-axis: passes through magnet (high reluctance).
- q-axis: passes only through iron laminations (very low reluctance).
- **Result:** $L_q \gg L_d$ → **HIGHLY SALIENT**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why IPM Dominates EV Applications:**
> The large difference $(L_q - L_d)$ produces a significant **reluctance torque**, in addition to the magnet torque. This gives IPM motors ~30–40% higher torque density compared to SPM.

---

## 2.5 Type 4: Tangential vs Radial IPM

| Sub-Type | Magnet Orientation | Saliency Ratio (Lq/Ld) | Flux Concentration |
|----------|-------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Radial IPM | Magnets point outward | ~2 to 3 | No |
| Tangential IPM | Magnets placed side-by-side | ~3 to 5 | Yes (2 magnets per pole) |

### Tangential IPM Special Feature
- Two magnets per pole contribute flux in series → air-gap flux density can EXCEED the magnet's remanence $B_r$.
- This is called **flux concentration** or **flux focusing**.

$$B_{gap} = B_r \cdot \left(\frac{A_{magnet}}{A_{gap}}\right) \cdot k_{leakage}$$

If $A_{magnet} > A_{gap}$, then $B_{gap} > B_r$ (flux concentration).

---

## 2.6 Type 5: Spoke-Type (Flux-Concentrating) Rotor

### Construction
- Magnets are placed **radially between alternating North-South pole pieces** (like spokes of a wheel).
- Steel pole pieces channel the flux into the air gap.

### Unique Feature
- Strong flux focusing effect (similar to tangential IPM).
- Used in **compact high-torque applications** (e.g., e-bikes, washing machines).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \left[\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q\right]$ | Total electromagnetic torque (IPM) | N·m |
| 2 | $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m I_q$ | Torque for SPM (non-salient, $L_d = L_q$) | N·m |
| 3 | $\xi = \frac{L_q}{L_d}$ | Saliency ratio (dimensionless) | – |
| 4 | $T_{rel} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q$ | Reluctance torque component | N·m |
| 5 | $T_{mag} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m I_q$ | Magnet (excitation) torque component | N·m |
| 6 | $\psi_m = \frac{B_r \cdot A_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Flux linkage per phase (sinusoidal assumption) | Wb |
| 7 | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_{ph} \cdot \psi_m$ | Per-phase back-EMF (RMS, sinusoidal) | V |
| 8 | $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi f}{p}$ | Synchronous mechanical speed | rad/s |
| 9 | $P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ | Output mechanical power | W |
| 10 | $k_t = \frac{T_e}{I}$ | Torque constant (for BLDC trapezoidal) | N·m/A |

Where:
- $p$ = number of pole pairs
- $\psi_m$ = magnet flux linkage (Wb)
- $I_d$, $I_q$ = d-axis and q-axis currents (A)
- $B_r$ = remanence flux density (T)
- $f$ = electrical frequency (Hz)
- $N_{ph}$ = turns per phase

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this classification matters in industry:**
> - **EV Traction (Toyota Prius, Tesla, Nissan Leaf):** IPM is used because of its high-speed flux-weakening capability and reluctance torque boost.
> - **Industrial Servo Drives (Siemens, Fanuc):** SPM is used for ultra-smooth, low-cogging operation in CNC machines.
> - **Home Appliances (LG Direct Drive Washing Machine):** Spoke-type BLDC for compactness and high starting torque.
> - **Aerospace Actuators:** Surface-inset PMSM for precision and moderate saliency.

This deep analysis prepares us for the rigorous mathematical derivations in the next section.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Electromagnetic Torque for Different PM Machine Types

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: General Synchronous Machine Torque Equation

The electromagnetic torque developed in any synchronous machine (with PM excitation) is derived from the co-energy principle:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m}$$

Where $\theta_m$ is the mechanical rotor angle (in rad).

After transformation to the d-q reference frame rotating at synchronous speed:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \left(\psi_d I_q - \psi_q I_d\right)$$

This is the **fundamental torque equation** for any 3-phase PM machine.

---

### 3.1.2 Substituting Flux Linkages

The d-axis and q-axis flux linkages are defined as:

$$\psi_d = L_d I_d + \psi_m$$

$$\psi_q = L_q I_q$$

> **Logic:** The d-axis flux linkage has two components — (1) self-inductance contribution ($L_d I_d$) and (2) the permanent magnet flux linkage ($\psi_m$). The q-axis has only the self-inductance contribution because magnets are aligned along the d-axis.

Substituting these into the torque equation:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \left[(L_d I_d + \psi_m) I_q - L_q I_q I_d\right]$$

Expanding:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \left[L_d I_d I_q + \psi_m I_q - L_q I_q I_d\right]$$

Grouping the inductance terms:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \left[\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q\right]$$

> **Final KTU Standard Equation:**

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m I_q + \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q}$$

The first term is the **Magnet (Excitation) Torque**, and the second term is the **Reluctance Torque**.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation for Type 1: Surface-Mounted PMSM (SPM)

For SPM, the rotor is **non-salient** because the magnets behave like air:

$$L_d = L_q = L_s$$

Substituting into the general equation:

$$T_{e,SPM} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m I_q + \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot (L_s - L_s) I_d I_q$$

The reluctance term vanishes:

$$T_{e,SPM} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m I_q$$

> **Key Insight:** SPM produces **only magnet torque**. The d-axis current $I_d$ contributes NOTHING to torque. This is why SPM control uses $I_d = 0$ strategy (MTPA = Maximum Torque Per Ampere).

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation for Type 2: Interior PMSM (IPM)

For IPM, the rotor is **salient** with $L_q > L_d$:

$$T_{e,IPM} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m I_q + \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q$$

Since $(L_d - L_q)$ is **negative**, the second term is positive only when $I_d$ is **negative** (i.e., demagnetizing current).

> **Optimal Control Strategy for IPM:**
> To maximize torque, both $I_d < 0$ and $I_q > 0$ are used simultaneously. This is the famous **MTPA control with negative Id**.

---

### 3.1.5 Per-Unit Torque Comparison

Let us define:
- Base torque: $T_{base} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot I_{rated}$
- Normalized currents: $i_d = I_d / I_{rated}$, $i_q = I_q / I_{rated}$
- Saliency ratio: $\xi = L_q / L_d$

The per-unit torque becomes:

$$t_e = i_q + \frac{(\xi - 1)}{\xi} \cdot (-i_d) \cdot i_q$$

Where we have used:

$$\frac{L_d - L_q}{L_d} = -\frac{L_q - L_d}{L_d} = -\left(\xi - 1\right)$$

> **Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style):**
> 
> Given: $\xi = 3$, $p = 4$, $\psi_m = 0.1$ Wb, $I_{rated} = 10$ A, $I_d = -5$ A, $I_q = 8.66$ A.
> 
> Calculate the total torque.

---

### 3.1.6 Step-by-Step Solution of Numerical Example

**Step 1:** Find $L_d$ and $L_q$ from saliency ratio.

$$L_q = \xi \cdot L_d = 3 L_d$$

**Step 2:** Compute magnet torque term.

$$T_{mag} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m I_q = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 8.66$$

$$T_{mag} = 6 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 8.66 = 5.196 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute reluctance torque term (assume $L_d = 5$ mH).

$$T_{rel} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot (5 - 15) \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot (-5) \cdot 8.66$$

$$T_{rel} = 6 \cdot (-10 \cdot 10^{-3}) \cdot (-43.3)$$

$$T_{rel} = 6 \cdot 0.433 = 2.598 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4:** Total torque.

$$T_e = T_{mag} + T_{rel} = 5.196 + 2.598 = 7.794 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Result:** The reluctance torque contributes **33%** of the total torque — this is why IPM is preferred for high-performance applications.

---

### 3.1.7 Derivation of Flux Weakening Capability

For high-speed operation above base speed, the back-EMF exceeds the DC bus voltage. To maintain current control, a **negative d-axis current** is injected to oppose the magnet flux.

The characteristic current is defined as:

$$I_{ch} = \frac{\psi_m}{L_d}$$

> **KTU Classification Rule:**
> - If $I_{ch} < I_{rated}$ → **Infinite** constant-power speed range (good flux weakening).
> - If $I_{ch} > I_{rated}$ → **Limited** constant-power speed range (poor flux weakening).

For **SPM** (with large $L_d$): $I_{ch}$ is small → good flux weakening in theory, BUT the magnets may irreversibly demagnetize if $I_d$ is too negative.

For **IPM** (with smaller $L_d$ and high saliency): $I_{ch}$ is moderate → excellent flux weakening without demagnetization risk.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Type-wise Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER COMPARISON TABLE — HIGH FREQUENCY IN KTU EXAMS**

| Parameter | SPM | Surface-Inset | IPM (Radial) | IPM (Tangential) | Spoke-Type |
|-----------|-----|---------------|--------------|------------------|------------|
| $L_d$ | High | High | Low | Low | Low |
| $L_q$ | ≈ $L_d$ | > $L_d$ | > $L_d$ | >> $L_d$ | > $L_d$ |
| Saliency ($\xi$) | ≈ 1 | 1.1 – 1.5 | 2 – 3 | 3 – 5 | 2 – 4 |
| Reluctance Torque | None | Small | Significant | Major | Moderate |
| Flux Focusing | No | No | No | Yes (2×) | Yes (1.5×) |
| Max Speed | Limited | Limited | High | High | Moderate |
| Cogging Torque | High | Medium | Low | Low | Medium |
| Manufacturing Cost | Low | Medium | High | High | High |
| Demagnetization Risk | High | Medium | Low | Low | Low |
| Typical Use | Servo | CNC | EV Traction | Industrial | E-bikes |

---

## 3.3 Inductance Computation for Salient Rotor (IPM)

The d-axis and q-axis inductances can be computed from the magnetic equivalent circuit:

$$L_d = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}_{md}} = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_m}{g + l_m / \mu_r}$$

$$L_q = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 A_q}{g}$$

> **Where:**
> - $N$ = number of turns per phase
> - $\mathcal{R}_{md}$ = d-axis magnetic reluctance (1/H)
> - $A_m$ = cross-sectional area of magnet (m²)
> - $g$ = air-gap length (m)
> - $l_m$ = magnet thickness (m)
> $\mu_r$ = magnet relative permeability ≈ 1.05
> - $A_q$ = q-axis iron path area (m²)

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Starting from the air-gap flux density due to magnets:

$$B_g = \frac{B_r}{1 + \mu_r \cdot g / l_m}$$

For NdFeB with $\mu_r \approx 1.05$, $g = 1$ mm, $l_m = 3$ mm:

$$B_g = \frac{1.2}{1 + 1.05 \cdot (1/3)} = \frac{1.2}{1.35} \approx 0.889 \text{ T}$$

This calculation shows why PM machines typically operate with **air-gap flux densities of 0.8 – 1.0 T**.

---

## 3.4 Summary of Key Derivations

1. **General torque equation** (d-q model): $T_e = \frac{3}{2} p [\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q]$
2. **SPM torque** (non-salient): $T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \psi_m I_q$ (with $I_d = 0$ strategy)
3. **IPM torque** (salient): Has both magnet and reluctance components.
4. **Flux weakening parameter**: $I_{ch} = \psi_m / L_d$
5. **Back-EMF**: $E_{ph} = 4.44 f N_{ph} \psi_m$

Now let us visualize the structures.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Short Answer

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: Differentiate between Surface-Mounted PMSM (SPM) and Interior PMSM (IPM) with respect to rotor construction and magnetic saliency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SPM | IPM |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Construction** | Arc-shaped PMs bonded to outer rotor surface with adhesive + retaining sleeve | PMs buried inside slots/cavities punched in rotor laminations |
| **Saliency** | Non-salient: $L_d \approx L_q$ (because magnets act as air) | Salient: $L_q > L_d$ (because q-axis path is pure iron, d-axis passes through magnet) |
| **Reluctance Torque** | Absent (only magnet torque exists) | Present and significant (adds 30-40% to total torque) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Examiner expects 3 clear points. Drawing a small comparison table earns full marks.

---

### Question A2: Short Answer

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: What is flux concentration effect in PM machines? In which rotor type is it most pronounced?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Flux concentration (or flux focusing) is the phenomenon where the **air-gap flux density $B_g$ exceeds the magnet's remanent flux density $B_r$**, achieved by geometric design.

**Mathematical Condition:**

$$\frac{A_{magnet}}{A_{gap}} > 1$$

$$B_{gap} = B_r \cdot \frac{A_{magnet}}{A_{gap}} \cdot k_{leakage}$$

**Most Pronounced In:** Tangential IPM and Spoke-type rotors, where two magnets per pole contribute flux in series into a smaller air-gap area.

**Example:** With $A_m / A_g = 1.5$ and $B_r = 1.1$ T, the air-gap flux density can reach $B_g \approx 1.5$ T.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A: Comprehensive 14-Mark Question

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Modified Pattern]**

#### **Q: (a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) synchronous motor. Discuss why IPM produces both magnet torque and reluctance torque. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

The IPM motor has permanent magnets **embedded inside the rotor laminations**. The rotor is built by stacking thin electrical steel sheets (typically M19 or M36 grade, thickness 0.35 mm), with rectangular cavities punched out to accommodate the magnets. The magnets are usually oriented either **radially** (parallel to the air-gap flux) or **tangentially** (perpendicular to the air-gap flux). The laminations are pressed onto the shaft, and end-plates retain the magnets against axial movement.

> Key Features:
> - No retaining sleeve required (magnets are buried)
> - Robust rotor suitable for high-speed operation (up to 20,000 RPM in EVs)
> - Lamination bridges above the magnets prevent magnets from flying out

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

When balanced 3-phase sinusoidal currents flow in the stator winding, a rotating magnetic field is produced in the air gap at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/p$.

The rotor rotates at the same synchronous speed due to the locking action between the stator field and the rotor magnets.

**Why IPM produces both torques:**

$$\boxed{T_{e,IPM} = \underbrace{\frac{3}{2} p \psi_m I_q}_{\text{Magnet Torque}} + \underbrace{\frac{3}{2} p (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q}_{\text{Reluctance Torque}}}$$

> **Magnet Torque:** Arises from the interaction between stator current and permanent magnet flux. Exists in ALL PM machines.

> **Reluctance Torque:** Arises ONLY when $L_d \neq L_q$ (salient rotor). The stator current creates a field that "prefers" to flow along the high-permeability q-axis (iron path) rather than the low-permeability d-axis (magnet path). This creates a force that aligns the rotor to minimize magnetic circuit reluctance — analogous to a needle aligning with a magnet.

For optimal torque production, IPM control uses **$I_d < 0$ (demagnetizing)** and **$I_q > 0$**, maximizing both torque components simultaneously.

---

#### **Q: (b)** A 3-phase IPM motor has the following specifications:
- Number of poles $2p = 8$ (so $p = 4$)
- Magnet flux linkage $\psi_m = 0.08$ Wb
- $L_d = 4$ mH, $L_q = 12$ mH
- Rated current $I_{rated} = 20$ A
- Operating point: $I_d = -10$ A, $I_q = 15$ A

**Calculate:**
1. Magnet torque component
2. Reluctance torque component
3. Total electromagnetic torque
4. Saliency ratio and percentage contribution of reluctance torque

**(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $p = 4$, $\psi_m = 0.08$ Wb, $L_d = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ H, $L_q = 12 \times 10^{-3}$ H
- $I_d = -10$ A, $I_q = 15$ A

**(i) Magnet Torque (2 marks):**

$$T_{mag} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot I_q$$

$$T_{mag} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 15$$

$$T_{mag} = 1.5 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 15$$

$$T_{mag} = 7.2 \text{ N·m}$$

**(ii) Reluctance Torque (2 marks):**

$$T_{rel} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot (L_d - L_q) \cdot I_d \cdot I_q$$

$$T_{rel} = 1.5 \cdot 4 \cdot (4 - 12) \times 10^{-3} \cdot (-10) \cdot 15$$

$$T_{rel} = 6 \cdot (-8 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (-150)$$

$$T_{rel} = 6 \cdot 1.2 = 7.2 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Total Torque (1 mark):**

$$T_e = T_{mag} + T_{rel} = 7.2 + 7.2 = 14.4 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iv) Saliency Ratio and Percentage Contribution (2 marks):**

$$\xi = \frac{L_q}{L_d} = \frac{12}{4} = 3$$

$$\% \text{Reluctance} = \frac{T_{rel}}{T_e} \times 100 = \frac{7.2}{14.4} \times 100 = 50\%$$

> **Interpretation:** At this operating point, reluctance torque contributes **50%** of total torque. This is why IPM is preferred for high-performance drives.

---

### QUESTION B: Alternative 14-Mark Question

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank]**

#### **Q: (a)** With the help of d-q axis equivalent circuits, derive the electromagnetic torque equation for an Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) synchronous motor. Show clearly how the equation reduces for a Surface-Mounted PMSM (SPM). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer:**

**d-q Axis Flux Linkages:**

In the rotating reference frame aligned with the rotor magnets:

$$\psi_d = L_d I_d + \psi_m$$

$$\psi_q = L_q I_q$$

**Power and Torque:**

The instantaneous air-gap power (3-phase) in d-q variables is:

$$P_{gap} = \frac{3}{2}(\omega_e)(\psi_d I_q - \psi_q I_d)$$

Dividing by mechanical speed $\omega_m = \omega_e / p$:

$$T_e = \frac{P_{gap}}{\omega_m} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot (\psi_d I_q - \psi_q I_d)$$

Substituting flux linkages:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot [(L_d I_d + \psi_m) I_q - L_q I_q I_d]$$

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m I_q + \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q}$$

**Reduction for SPM (3 marks):**

For SPM, the rotor is **non-salient** because the magnets behave as air ($\mu_r \approx 1$):

$$L_d = L_q = L_s$$

Therefore:

$$(L_d - L_q) = 0$$

The reluctance torque term vanishes completely:

$$T_{e,SPM} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m I_q$$

> **Conclusion:** SPM produces only magnet torque. MTPA control becomes $I_d = 0$ strategy.

---

#### **Q: (b)** Compare in detail the construction, performance, and applications of **Surface-Mounted PMSM, Interior PMSM (Radial), and Spoke-Type** rotor configurations. Use a comparative table. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer (Tabular Comparison):**

| Parameter | SPM | IPM Radial | Spoke-Type |
|-----------|-----|------------|------------|
| **Magnet Position** | Outer surface | Buried radially | Radial between pole pieces |
| **Sleeve Required** | Yes (non-magnetic) | No | No |
| **Saliency Ratio ($\xi$)** | ≈ 1 | 2 – 3 | 2 – 4 |
| **Reluctance Torque** | None | Significant | Moderate |
| **Flux Concentration** | No | No | Yes (1.4 – 1.6×) |
| **Maximum Speed** | 6,000 RPM (typical) | 15,000 – 20,000 RPM | 8,000 – 12,000 RPM |
| **Cogging Torque** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Demagnetization Resistance** | Poor | Good | Good |
| **Cost (per kW)** | Low | Medium-High | High |
| **Power Density** | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **MTPA Control** | $I_d = 0$ | $I_d < 0$ required | $I_d < 0$ required |
| **Typical Applications** | Servo drives, robotics, CNC | EV traction (Toyota, Tesla), industrial drives | E-bikes, washing machines, compact drives |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**

The choice among these three types is governed by the application's priority:
- Choose **SPM** for low-cost, smooth, low-speed servo applications.
- Choose **IPM Radial** for high-speed, high-power applications (especially EVs).
- Choose **Spoke-Type** for compact designs requiring high torque density.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Marks Deduction:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing SPM with Surface-Inset:** Many students write "surface-mounted" and "surface-inset" interchangeably. They are DIFFERENT. SPM has magnets on the surface (non-salient); Inset has them in shallow slots (mildly salient).
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the $3/2$ factor:** The torque equation MUST include the $\frac{3}{2}$ constant for 3-phase machines. Using a coefficient of 1 loses 1 full mark.
> 
> 3. **Wrong sign convention for $I_d$:** For IPM torque to be positive, $I_d$ MUST be negative. Writing positive $I_d$ produces negative reluctance torque.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up $L_d$ and $L_q$:** For IPM, ALWAYS $L_q > L_d$. Students sometimes write $L_d > L_q$, which is geometrically impossible for a buried-magnet rotor.
> 
> 5. **Omitting the magnet torque for IPM:** A frequent error is to write only the reluctance torque term, forgetting the magnet torque component. Both must appear.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — MUST MEMORIZE**

### Key Concepts
- ✅ **5 Main Types:** SPM, Surface-Inset, IPM (Radial), IPM (Tangential), Spoke-Type
- ✅ **Classification Basis:** Magnet placement on/inside rotor → determines saliency
- ✅ **Saliency Origin:** $L_d \neq L_q$ due to magnetic path asymmetry
- ✅ **Magnet Material:** NdFeB (Neodymium-Iron-Boron) with $B_r \approx 1.0 - 1.4$ T

### Critical Equations
- ✅ General torque: $T_e = \frac{3}{2} p [\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q]$
- ✅ SPM torque: $T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \psi_m I_q$ (use $I_d = 0$)
- ✅ Back-EMF: $E = 4.44 f N \psi_m$
- ✅ Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120 f / p$ (mechanical RPM)

### Type-Specific Points
- ✅ **SPM:** Non-salient, only magnet torque, requires sleeve
- ✅ **IPM:** Salient, both torques, high-speed capable, EV standard
- ✅ **Tangential IPM:** Flux concentration ($B_{gap} > B_r$)
- ✅ **Spoke-Type:** Flux focusing, compact design, e-bikes

### Control Strategies
- ✅ **SPM Control:** $I_d = 0$ (MTPA)
- ✅ **IPM Control:** $I_d < 0$, $I_q > 0$ (MTPA with field weakening)
- ✅ **High Speed:** Inject negative $I_d$ for flux weakening

### Applications Mapping
- ✅ **EV/Hybrid Cars:** IPM Radial (Toyota Prius, Tesla Model 3)
- ✅ **CNC Machines:** SPM or Surface-Inset
- ✅ **Home Appliances:** Spoke-Type BLDC
- ✅ **Aerospace:** Surface-Inset PMSM

### Examiner's Hot Buttons
- ✅ Always write **complete** torque equation for IPM (both terms).
- ✅ Specify whether $\mu_r \approx 1$ for permanent magnets.
- ✅ Use **negative $I_d$** for IPM MTPA — never positive.
- ✅ Mention **flux weakening** when discussing high-speed operation.
- ✅ Draw **neat cross-sectional sketches** with proper labeling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Weightage Insight:**
> This topic carries approximately **15-20 marks** across Part A and Part B in the End Semester Examination (ESE). Mastering the torque derivation, type comparison, and IPM numerical problems guarantees full marks in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demagnetization Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Demagnetization Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **demagnetization characteristic** of a permanent magnet (PM) is the locus of the magnetic flux density **B** versus the magnetizing force **H** in the **second quadrant** of the B–H hysteresis loop (where **H** is *negative* and **B** is *positive*). It describes how the internal flux density of a permanently magnetized material decreases as an external demagnetizing field (such as armature reaction) is applied, and it is the single most important curve used to design, select, and protect rare-earth (NdFeB, SmCo) and ferrite magnets used in modern PM machines — particularly **PMSM**, **BLDC**, and **PM-assisted synchronous reluctance motors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Expected):**
> *“The demagnetization curve is the portion of the hysteresis loop in the second quadrant (B positive, H negative) which represents the working region of a permanent magnet under the influence of an external demagnetizing MMF. It is bounded by the residual flux density **B_r** at H = 0 and the coercive force **H_c** at B = 0.”*

## 1.2 Critical Anchor Points on the Curve

| Point | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Residual Flux Density | $B_r$ | Flux density retained after magnetizing MMF is removed | Tesla (T) |
| Coercive Force | $H_c$ | Reverse MMF needed to bring flux density to zero | A/m |
| Intrinsic Coercive Force | $H_{ci}$ | Reverse MMF needed to bring intrinsic flux density ($B_i = B - \mu_0 H$) to zero | A/m |
| Knee Point | $K$ | Onset of irreversible demagnetization | — |
| Maximum Energy Product | $(BH)_{max}$ | Point of maximum stored magnetic energy per unit volume | kJ/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> For modern rare-earth magnets used in **KTU-specified BLDC / PMSM** problems, typical values are: **NdFeB → B_r ≈ 1.1–1.4 T, H_c ≈ −800 to −1000 kA/m, (BH)max ≈ 250–400 kJ/m³**. Ferrite magnets are weaker: **B_r ≈ 0.2–0.4 T, H_c ≈ −200 to −300 kA/m**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pushed Spring" View

Imagine a **compressed spring** locked inside a small box. The spring stores mechanical energy permanently as long as the lid stays shut. 

- The **stiffness of the spring** = analogous to the **coercive force $H_c$** — how strongly the magnet "resists" being demagnetized.
- The **initial push-back force** when the lid is just opened = analogous to the **residual flux density $B_r$**.
- The **latch of the box** = analogous to the **knee point** of the curve. If you push too hard (large armature reaction MMF), the latch breaks — and the spring never regains its original force when the push is removed. This is called **irreversible demagnetization**.

For an electrical machine, the *push* is the **armature reaction MMF** from stator currents, and the *latch* is the **knee of the B-H curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb:** Always keep the *minimum* working point on the demagnetization curve **above the knee point at the worst-case (cold) temperature** to guarantee no irreversible demagnetization over the machine's lifetime.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Second Quadrant — Why It Is the "Working Region"

When a permanent magnet is magnetized to saturation in a magnetizer, the operating flux loop is the full **B–H hysteresis loop**. However, once installed inside a machine:

1. The magnet is *never* remagnetized (no external forward MMF is applied during operation).
2. The only MMFs acting on the magnet are **demagnetizing in nature** — primarily the **armature reaction** and any temperature effects.
3. Hence, the magnet's state travels *only* along the **second-quadrant portion** of the loop — the demagnetization curve.

## 2.2 Operating Point & Load Line — The Heart of the Topic

The **operating point** of a PM in a magnetic circuit is the intersection of the **demagnetization curve** (a property of the magnet material) and the **load line** (a property of the external magnetic circuit). The load line is derived from **Ampere's circuital law** applied to the magnetic circuit:

$$\sum H_i \, \ell_i = NI_{total}$$

For a simple PM of length $\ell_m$ in a magnetic circuit with air-gap of length $g$ and permeance $\mathcal{P}$:

$$\text{Load line slope} = -\dfrac{1}{\mu_0 \, \mathcal{P} \, \ell_m^2 / A_m} \quad \text{(for linear circuits)}$$

The intersection $(H_m, B_m)$ is the **static working point**.

## 2.3 The Permeance Coefficient ($P_c$ or $p_c$)

A dimensionless figure-of-merit describing the magnetic circuit:

$$p_c = \dfrac{B_m}{-\mu_0 H_m} = \dfrac{\ell_m}{g \cdot \mu_r^{effective}}$$

- **High $p_c$** (small gap, long magnet) → working point near $B_r$ → high flux, low demagnetization risk.
- **Low $p_c$** (large gap, short magnet) → working point slides down the curve toward $H_c$ → risk of demagnetization.

## 2.4 Irreversible vs. Reversible Demagnetization

| Type | Cause | Recovery? | KTU Exam Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reversible** | Small demagnetizing MMF (within recoil region) | YES — follows recoil line back to origin | Acceptable in normal operation |
| **Irreversible** | Working point driven **below the knee** | NO — magnet permanently weakened | Must be prevented at all temperatures |
| **Thermal** | Elevated temperature reduces $B_r$ and $H_c$ | NO (without re-magnetization) | Worst-case at peak operating temperature |

## 2.5 The Recoil Line

If the demagnetizing MMF is reduced (e.g., a transient current spike passes), the operating point does **not** retrace the demagnetization curve. It follows a near-straight **recoil line** with slope $\mu_0 \mu_{recoil}$:

$$B = B_{recoil} + \mu_0 \mu_{recoil} (H - H_{recoil})$$

where $\mu_{recoil} \approx 1.02$–$1.10$ for NdFeB and $\approx 1.05$–$1.15$ for ferrite.

## 2.6 Effect of Armature Reaction (Synchronous Machine Context)

The stator MMF in a PMSM has two components relative to the rotor:

- **d-axis (direct-axis) component** $F_{ad}$ — purely **demagnetizing** (or magnetizing) → this is the *enemy* of the magnet.
- **q-axis (quadrature-axis) component** $F_{aq}$ — produces cross-saturation but does not demagnetize directly.

The total demagnetizing field seen by the magnet is:

$$H_{m,min} = \dfrac{H_c' \cdot \ell_m - F_{ad} / \text{permeance\,coefficient}}{\ell_m}$$

The minimum point **must** lie above the knee at the **maximum operating temperature** $T_{max}$.

## 2.7 Effect of Temperature

Both $B_r$ and $H_c$ decrease approximately linearly with temperature, characterized by **reversible temperature coefficients**:

$$\alpha_{Br} = \dfrac{\Delta B_r / B_{r,ref}}{\Delta T} \quad (\%/\!^{\circ}\!C)$$

$$\alpha_{Hc} = \dfrac{\Delta H_c / H_{c,ref}}{\Delta T} \quad (\%/\!^{\circ}\!C)$$

For NdFeB, $\alpha_{Br} \approx -0.10 \%/^{\circ}C$ and $\alpha_{Hc} \approx -0.55 \%/^{\circ}C$ (note $H_c$ falls faster!). The worst-case curve shift is therefore at **high temperature**, not low temperature.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B_i = B - \mu_0 H$ | Intrinsic flux density | T |
| 2 | $p_c = B_m / (-\mu_0 H_m)$ | Permeance coefficient | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $B_m = \mu_0 H_m + B_r$ | Linear demagnetization line approx. | T |
| 4 | $H_m = - \dfrac{NI_a \, K_{ad}}{\ell_m} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + p_c}$ | Demag. field from armature reaction | A/m |
| 5 | $B_d = B_r + \mu_0 H_c \cdot p_c / (1 + p_c)$ | Air-gap flux density from PM | T |
| 6 | $\Phi_m = B_m \cdot A_m$ | Magnet flux | Wb |
| 7 | $(BH)_{max} = \dfrac{B_r^2}{4 \mu_0}$ (theoretical max) | Maximum energy product | J/m³ |
| 8 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi \, K_w$ | Induced EMF using PM flux | V |
| 9 | $B_{r,T} = B_{r,ref}[1 + \alpha_{Br}(T - T_{ref})]$ | Flux density at temperature T | T |
| 10 | $H_{c,T} = H_{c,ref}[1 + \alpha_{Hc}(T - T_{ref})]$ | Coercivity at temperature T | A/m |
| 11 | $\mu_{recoil} = \Delta B / (\mu_0 \Delta H)$ | Recoil permeability slope | — |
| 12 | $F_{total} = H_m \ell_m + H_g g = 0$ | Ampere's law (PM magnetic circuit) | A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In industry, demagnetization analysis is mandatory for **traction motors** (EVs, locomotives), **wind turbine PMSG**, and **aerospace BLDC actuators** — any application where an overcurrent or short-circuit fault could expose the magnet to fields high enough to cross the knee and cause permanent damage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Operating Point from Load-Line Intersection (Linear Approximation)

**Assumption:** The demagnetization curve is approximated by a straight line from $(0, B_r)$ to $(H_c, 0)$. The load line is also linear with slope $-1/(p_c \mu_0)$ passing through origin.

**Step 1:** Equation of the (approximate) demagnetization curve.

The line joining $(0, B_r)$ and $(H_c, 0)$ has slope:

$$\text{slope} = \dfrac{0 - B_r}{H_c - 0} = -\dfrac{B_r}{H_c}$$

Thus:

$$B = B_r - \dfrac{B_r}{H_c} \, H = B_r \left(1 - \dfrac{H}{H_c}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Equation of the load line for a magnetic circuit of permeance $\mathcal{P}$ and magnet area $A_m$, length $\ell_m$:

Using $\Phi = B_m A_m = \mathcal{P} \cdot F$ and $F = H_m \ell_m$:

$$B_m A_m = \mathcal{P} H_m \ell_m \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B_m = \mathcal{P} \ell_m \, H_m$$

Substituting $H_m < 0$ and writing in the standard form with $B$ as a function of $H$:

$$B_m = -\dfrac{H_m}{(\mathcal{P} \ell_m / A_m)} = -\mu_0 p_c \, H_m$$

So the load line is:

$$B = -\mu_0 p_c \, H$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two equations to find the operating point.

$$-\mu_0 p_c \, H_m = B_r \left(1 - \dfrac{H_m}{H_c}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Expand and isolate $H_m$.

$$-\mu_0 p_c \, H_m = B_r - \dfrac{B_r}{H_c} H_m$$

$$-\mu_0 p_c \, H_m + \dfrac{B_r}{H_c} H_m = B_r$$

$$H_m \left(\dfrac{B_r}{H_c} - \mu_0 p_c\right) = B_r$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $H_m$.

$$H_m = \dfrac{B_r}{\dfrac{B_r}{H_c} - \mu_0 p_c}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into load line to get $B_m$.

$$B_m = -\mu_0 p_c \cdot \dfrac{B_r}{\dfrac{B_r}{H_c} - \mu_0 p_c}$$

$$B_m = \dfrac{\mu_0 p_c \, B_r}{\mu_0 p_c - \dfrac{B_r}{H_c}}$$

**Step 7:** Rearrange into the standard "per-unit" form.

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $H_c$:

$$B_m = \dfrac{\mu_0 p_c \, B_r \, H_c}{\mu_0 p_c \, H_c - B_r}$$

And since $\mu_0 H_c \ll B_r$ typically, dividing both sides by $B_r$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{B_m}{B_r} = \dfrac{p_c}{p_c - B_r/(\mu_0 H_c)}}$$

This is the **standard operating-point relation** in KTU board problems.

## 3.2 Derivation — Air-Gap Flux Density $B_g$ in Terms of Magnet Dimensions

For a magnet of length $\ell_m$ and area $A_m$ facing an air gap of length $g$ and area $A_g$:

**Step 1:** Apply Ampere's circuital law around the magnetic circuit (ignoring iron MMF):

$$H_m \ell_m + H_g g = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; H_m \ell_m = -H_g g$$

**Step 2:** Apply continuity of flux (ignoring leakage):

$$B_m A_m = B_g A_g \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B_m = B_g \, \dfrac{A_g}{A_m}$$

**Step 3:** Linear demagnetization relation (Step 1 of previous derivation):

$$B_m = B_r + \mu_0 H_m$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 1 (write $H_m = -H_g g / \ell_m$) into Step 3.

$$B_m = B_r - \dfrac{\mu_0 g}{\ell_m} H_g$$

**Step 5:** Substitute Step 2 ($H_g = B_m A_m / (B_r A_g \cdot \mu_0)$... wait — use $B_g = \mu_0 H_g$).

$$B_m = B_r - \dfrac{\mu_0 g}{\ell_m} \cdot \dfrac{B_g}{\mu_0} = B_r - \dfrac{g}{\ell_m} B_g$$

**Step 6:** Use Step 2 again to express everything in $B_g$.

$$B_g \, \dfrac{A_g}{A_m} = B_r - \dfrac{g}{\ell_m} B_g$$

**Step 7:** Collect $B_g$ terms.

$$B_g \left(\dfrac{A_g}{A_m} + \dfrac{g}{\ell_m}\right) = B_r$$

**Step 8:** Solve for $B_g$.

$$\boxed{B_g = \dfrac{B_r}{\dfrac{A_g}{A_m} + \dfrac{g}{\ell_m}}}$$

This is the air-gap flux density in terms of **magnet dimensions and material property $B_r$** — frequently asked in KTU numericals.

## 3.3 Derivation — Maximum Energy Product $(BH)_{max}$ on a Linear Curve

Energy stored per unit magnet volume: $w = -B \cdot H$ (note the negative sign because $H < 0$ in the second quadrant).

Using the linear relation from §3.1: $B = B_r (1 - H/H_c)$.

$$w = -B_r \left(1 - \dfrac{H}{H_c}\right) H = -B_r H + \dfrac{B_r H^2}{H_c}$$

To maximize, set $dw/dH = 0$:

$$\dfrac{dw}{dH} = -B_r + \dfrac{2 B_r H}{H_c} = 0$$

$$H^* = \dfrac{H_c}{2}$$

Then:

$$B^* = B_r \left(1 - \dfrac{H_c/2}{H_c}\right) = \dfrac{B_r}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$(BH)_{max} = -B^* H^* = -\dfrac{B_r}{2} \cdot \dfrac{H_c}{2} = -\dfrac{B_r H_c}{4}$$

$$\boxed{(BH)_{max} = \dfrac{B_r \, \vert H_c \vert}{4} \;\; (\text{J/m}^3)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **theoretical maximum** assuming a perfectly rectangular demagnetization curve. Real materials typically achieve **60–90 %** of this value.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Setup

A typical KTU 14-mark problem provides the magnet data and the magnetic-circuit geometry, asking the student to (a) find the operating point and (b) verify that the magnet does not suffer irreversible demagnetization at worst-case current and temperature.

**Given (typical board problem):**
- NdFeB magnet: $B_r = 1.2$ T, $H_c = -900$ kA/m, $T_{max} = 120^{\circ}$C
- Temperature coefficients: $\alpha_{Br} = -0.10 \%/^{\circ}\!C$, $\alpha_{Hc} = -0.55 \%/^{\circ}\!C$
- Reference temperature: $T_{ref} = 20^{\circ}$C
- Permeance coefficient: $p_c = 4$
- Armature d-axis demagnetizing MMF per pole: $F_{ad} = 12$ kA-turns
- Magnet length: $\ell_m = 5$ mm

**Step-by-step solution structure:**

**Step A — Adjust $B_r$ and $H_c$ to maximum temperature:**

$$B_{r,T} = 1.2 \times [1 + (-0.001)(120-20)] = 1.2 \times 0.9 = 1.08 \text{ T}$$

$$H_{c,T} = -900 \times [1 + (-0.0055)(120-20)] = -900 \times 0.445 = -400.5 \text{ kA/m}$$

**Step B — No-load operating point (no armature reaction):**

Using the §3.1 boxed formula:

$$\dfrac{B_m}{B_r} = \dfrac{4}{4 - 1.08 / (4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 400.5 \times 10^3)}$$

$$= \dfrac{4}{4 - 2.14} = \dfrac{4}{1.86} = 2.15$$

This is $> 1$, which indicates the **linear approximation is invalid** at this high $p_c$ — the true curve is non-linear, and $B_m \le B_r$. So the actual $B_m \approx B_r \approx 1.08$ T (operating point hugs the top of the curve).

**Step C — Loaded operating point (with $F_{ad}$):**

Convert armature MMF into equivalent $H$:

$$H_{ad} = -\dfrac{F_{ad}}{\ell_m \, (1 + p_c)} = -\dfrac{12000}{0.005 \times 5} = -480 \text{ kA/m}$$

**Step D — Total demagnetizing field on the magnet:**

$$H_{m,min} = H_{m,no-load} + H_{ad} \approx 0 + (-480) = -480 \text{ kA/m}$$

**Step E — Check against $H_{c,T}$ and knee point:**

Since $|H_{m,min}| = 480$ kA/m $> |H_{c,T}| = 400.5$ kA/m, the operating point would cross zero flux — the magnet would be **partially demagnetized**. The designer must either (i) increase $\ell_m$, (ii) use a higher-$H_c$ grade, or (iii) reduce $F_{ad}$ via reluctance-torque design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget to **temperature-correct both $B_r$ AND $H_c$** before checking the knee. Because $H_c$ has a steeper temperature coefficient than $B_r$, the knee point shifts dramatically at high temperature — this is the *single most common source of "design failure" in board problems*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->


> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for Board Exams:** Always draw the **high-temperature curve** (the worst case) and verify the operating point lies *above* its knee. A common diagram to reproduce is the family of demagnetization curves at three temperatures (e.g., 20°C, 80°C, 150°C) with the load line passing through all three — the curve closest to crossing the load line is the design-limiting one.
