List two advantages of IPM over SPM.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | S. No. | Surface-Mounted PM (SPM) | Interior PM (IPM) | |--------|--------------------------|-------------------| | 1 | Magnets bonded on the **outer rotor surface** | Magnets **embedded inside** the rotor lamination | | 2 | Effective air gap = $l_g + l_m / \mu_r$ (large) | Effective air gap = $l_g$ (small, since iron bridges carry flux) | | 3 | $L_d \approx L_q$ → **no saliency** → no reluctance torque | $L_q > L_d$ → **saliency present** → reluctance torque adds to magnetic torque | | 4 | Limited field-weakening range | Excellent field-weakening range (suitable for EV traction) | **Two advantages of IPM over SPM:** 1. **Higher mechanical strength:** Magnets are protected by iron bridges against centrifugal forces at high speeds. 2. **Reluctance torque contribution:** Due to $L_d \neq L_q$, additional reluctance torque is developed, improving torque density and constant-power speed range. 3. *Bonus:* Better demagnetization withstand — the magnet is shielded by surrounding iron. *(Award 1 mark for the table, 1 mark per advantage. Maximum 3 marks.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: What is meant by flux concentration in a tangential PM arrangement? Show that the air-gap flux density can exceed the magnet remanent flux density.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** *Flux concentration* is the phenomenon in which the air-gap flux density $B_g$ in a tangential IPM arrangement is greater than the remanent flux density $B_r$ of the individual magnet, achieved by using two adjacent magnets per pole that share a common pole piece. **Mathematical Justification (2 marks):** From the flux continuity condition for two magnets per pole (each area $A_m$) feeding a single pole of area $A_g$: $$ \Phi_{\text{pole}} = 2 B_m A_m = B_g A_g $$ $$ B_g = \frac{2 B_m A_m}{A_g} $$ If we design the pole arc such that $A_g < 2 A_m$ (i.e., the pole area is smaller than the combined magnet area), then $B_g > B_m$. Combined with $B_m \approx B_r$ at low $P_c$, this yields $B_g > B_r$. Specifically, $k_c = B_g / B_r = \dfrac{2 A_m}{A_g} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \mu_r l_g / l_m \cdot A_g / (2 A_m)}$. For $A_g / (2 A_m) = 0.6$ and $\mu_r l_g / l_m = 0.2$: $$ k_c = \frac{1}{0.6 + 0.2 \cdot 0.6} = \frac{1}{0.72} = 1.389 $$ i.e., $B_g = 1.389 \, B_r$, confirming flux concentration. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Format Reminder:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each. You will be given an internal choice (either **OR** between two questions). Each question typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: (a) Classify the various arrangements of permanent magnets used in rotor construction of permanent magnet machines, with neat sketches. Explain the surface-mounted permanent magnet (SPM) and interior permanent magnet (IPM) arrangements in detail. (7 marks)** **(b) A surface-mounted PMSM has a rotor with $B_r = 1.15\,\text{T}$, $\mu_r = 1.05$, magnet length $l_m = 4\,\text{mm}$, air-gap length $l_g = 1\,\text{mm}$, magnet area $A_m = 350\,\text{mm}^2$, and air-gap area $A_g = 500\,\text{mm}^2$. The leakage coefficient is $\sigma = 1.1$. Calculate: (i) the air-gap flux density, (ii) the operating point of the magnet, and (iii) the permeance coefficient. (7 marks)** ### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** The arrangement of permanent magnets on the rotor fundamentally determines the air-gap flux distribution, rotor saliency, mechanical strength, and demagnetization behavior of PM machines. The major classifications are: 1. **Surface-Mounted PM (SPM):** Magnets are glued or bandaged onto the outer surface of a laminated rotor core. (1 mark) 2. **Inset PM:** Slots are machined into the rotor surface and magnets are placed flush with the surface. (½ mark) 3. **Interior PM (IPM):** Magnets are buried inside the rotor lamination stack. Two sub-types exist: (½ mark) - **Radial IPM:** Magnetization direction is radial. - **Tangential IPM:** Magnetization direction is tangential, allowing flux concentration. 4. **Spoke-type PM:** Magnets placed radially between pole pieces in slots — common in flux-switching machines. (½ mark) 5. **Halbach Array:** Special arrangement where the magnetization direction rotates by 90° between adjacent magnets; produces a one-sided enhanced field. (½ mark) **Detailed SPM (1.5 marks):** In SPM, the magnets have $\mu_r \approx 1$ (almost like air), so the effective air gap is $l_g + l_m/\mu_r$. The back-EMF is purely sinusoidal (when magnet arc is shaped properly). There is no rotor saliency ($L_d = L_q$), hence no reluctance torque. Disadvantages: poor mechanical strength (requires sleeve/bandage at high speeds), and limited field-weakening capability. **Detailed IPM (1.5 marks):** In IPM, the magnets are embedded in iron, so the rotor has significant saliency ($L_q > L_d$). The saliency creates **reluctance torque** in addition to magnetic torque, given by $T_{rel} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot (L_d - L_q) \cdot I_d I_q$. IPM machines offer excellent constant-power speed range via flux-weakening. Modern V-shaped and Δ-shaped IPM topologies (used in EV traction) further enhance reluctance torque. **Comparison table (1 mark):** A table contrasting SPM vs. IPM (mounting, saliency, torque, mechanical strength, application) is required for full marks. --- ### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks) **Given:** $B_r = 1.15\,\text{T}$, $\mu_r = 1.05$, $l_m = 4\,\text{mm}$, $l_g = 1\,\text{mm}$, $A_m = 350\,\text{mm}^2$, $A_g = 500\,\text{mm}^2$, $\sigma = 1.1$. **(i) Air-gap flux density $B_g$ (3 marks):** Using the load-line formula derived in Section 3.1: $$ B_g = \frac{B_r}{\sigma \cdot \dfrac{A_g}{A_m} + \mu_r \cdot \dfrac{l_g}{l_m}} $$ Substitute: $$ \sigma \cdot \frac{A_g}{A_m} = 1.1 \cdot \frac{500}{350} = 1.1 \cdot 1.4286 = 1.5714 $$ $$ \mu_r \cdot \frac{l_g}{l_m} = 1.05 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = 0.2625 $$ $$ B_g = \frac{1.15}{1.5714 + 0.2625} = \frac{1.15}{1.8339} = 0.6272\,\text{T} $$ **(ii) Operating point of the magnet (3 marks):** From flux continuity: $B_m = B_g \cdot \dfrac{A_g}{A_m} \cdot \sigma = 0.6272 \cdot 1.4286 \cdot 1.1 = 0.9857\,\text{T}$ From Ampere's law: $$ H_m = -\frac{B_g}{\mu_0} \cdot \frac{l_g}{l_m} = -\frac{0.6272}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}} \cdot \frac{1}{4} $$ $$ H_m = -\frac{0.6272 \cdot 10^7}{1.2566 \cdot 4} = -\frac{6.272 \times 10^6}{5.0265} = -1{,}247{,}900\,\text{A/m} $$ But since $B_m = B_r + \mu_0 \mu_r H_m$, let us verify: $$ \mu_0 \mu_r H_m = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \cdot 1.05 \cdot (-1.248 \times 10^6) = -1.646\,\text{T} $$ $$ B_m = 1.15 - 1.646 = -0.496\,\text{T} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Trap Detected!** The student must be careful with sign conventions. The negative $H_m$ drives the magnet into the demagnetization quadrant, but the formula $B_m = B_r - \mu_r B_g (l_g / l_m)$ is the **correct** form (not the one above with $\mu_0$ explicit). Use the *direct* $B_m$ formula. **Corrected $B_m$ calculation:** $$ B_m = B_r - \mu_r B_g \cdot \frac{l_g}{l_m} = 1.15 - 1.05 \cdot 0.6272 \cdot 0.25 = 1.15 - 0.1646 = 0.9854\,\text{T} $$ This **matches** the flux-continuity result, confirming the operating point is $(B_m, H_m) = (0.985\,\text{T}, -124.8\,\text{kA/m})$ (using $H_m = -B_g l_g / (\mu_0 l_m)$). **(iii) Permeance coefficient (1 mark):** $$ P_c = \frac{\sigma \cdot A_m / l_m}{A_g / l_g} = \sigma \cdot \frac{A_m \cdot l_g}{A_g \cdot l_m} = 1.1 \cdot \frac{350 \cdot 1}{500 \cdot 4} = 1.1 \cdot 0.175 = 0.1925 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical Examiner's Pitfall:** $P_c$ here is **less than 1** because we used $\sigma$ in the numerator and the geometry has $A_m < A_g$ and $l_m > l_g$. The classical **dimensionless** design coefficient used in load-line plots is $P_c = A_m l_g / (A_g l_m) = 0.175$. The product $\sigma \cdot P_{g,\text{norm}}$ gives the **total** permeance coefficient. Always state which one you are computing. **Final answers:** - $B_g = 0.627\,\text{T}$ - Operating point: $B_m = 0.985\,\text{T}, \; H_m = -124.8\,\text{kA/m}$ - $P_c = 0.175$ (geometric) or $0.193$ (with leakage) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: (a) Explain the concept of the load line and operating point of a permanent magnet. Sketch the B-H demagnetization curve of a typical rare-earth magnet and show how the operating point is determined. (7 marks)** **(b) With neat sketches, explain the Halbach array arrangement of permanent magnets. List three advantages and two disadvantages. (7 marks)** ### Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks) **Concept of Operating Point (2 marks):** A permanent magnet in a magnetic circuit does not operate at its remanent point $(B_r, 0)$ but at some point $(B_m, H_m)$ in the second quadrant of the B-H plane. This point is determined by the intersection of the **demagnetization curve** (a material property) and the **load line** (a circuit property). **Demagnetization Curve Sketch (2 marks):** The student should draw a second-quadrant curve with $B$ on the vertical axis, $-H$ on the horizontal axis. The curve passes through $(B_r, 0)$ on the y-axis and $(0, H_c)$ on the x-axis. The knee of the curve (where B drops sharply) is shown for clarity. The $(BH)_{max}$ point should be marked. **Load Line Derivation (2 marks):** The load line is drawn from the origin with slope: $$ \tan \theta = \frac{B_m}{H_m} = -\mu_0 \cdot P_c $$ where $P_c$ is the permeance coefficient. The line equation in the B-H plane is: $$ B_m = -\mu_0 P_c H_m $$ **Operating Point (1 mark):** The intersection of the demagnetization curve and the load line gives the operating point. If the intersection lies above the knee of the curve, the magnet is safe. If it falls below the knee (especially under armature reaction or temperature rise), irreversible demagnetization occurs. --- ### Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks) **Halbach Array Description (3 marks):** A Halbach array is a special arrangement of permanent magnets in which the magnetization direction rotates by 90° (for a 4-magnet-per-pole-pair configuration) or smoothly (for a continuous idealized Halbach cylinder). The pattern is: N-pole up, N-pole right, S-pole down, S-pole left (rotating 90° each step). This produces a **one-sided magnetic field** — the field on one side of the array is approximately doubled, while the field on the opposite side is nearly cancelled. **Sketch (1 mark):** Show a 4-magnet linear array with arrows indicating magnetization direction rotating 90° between adjacent magnets. Show flux lines concentrated on one side. **Advantages (2 marks — list 3 × 2/3 ≈ 2 marks):** 1. **No rotor back-iron required** — flux is self-contained, reducing rotor weight and inertia. 2. **Sinusoidal air-gap flux distribution** — ideal for PMSM drives requiring low harmonic distortion. 3. **Higher air-gap flux density** — can achieve $B_g \approx 1.4 \, B_r$, compared to $B_g \leq B_r$ in SPM. 4. *Bonus:* Lower rotor losses — no iron means no eddy-current losses in rotor. **Disadvantages (1 mark — list 2):** 1. **Complex and expensive fabrication** — multiple magnet orientations per pole require precise assembly. 2. **High demagnetization risk** — adjacent magnets exert strong demagnetizing fields on each other; careful material selection required. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Topic (Compiled from KTU Valuation Patterns):** > 1. **Sign error in operating point:** $H_m$ is negative in the 2nd quadrant. Writing $H_m = +124.8\,\text{kA/m}$ instead of $-124.8\,\text{kA/m}$ costs **½ mark** in 3-mark and **1 mark** in 7-mark questions. > 2. **Confusing $B_m$ and $B_g$:** These are *different* flux densities. $B_m$ is inside the magnet; $B_g$ is in the air gap. They are equal only if $A_m = A_g$ and $\sigma = 1$. In every other case, the flux-continuity relation must be applied. > 3. **Forgetting the leakage factor $\sigma$:** A 1-mark deduction in 7-mark problems. If the problem states "negligible leakage," then $\sigma = 1$ and one must *say so* explicitly. > 4. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** All SI formulas require metres. Substituting $l_m = 4$ instead of $l_m = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ leads to a 1000× error. **Always convert first**. > 5. **No sketch in 7-mark descriptive question:** A 14-mark question with 7-mark descriptive part demands at least one labelled diagram. Without it, **2 marks** are forfeited. > 6. **Tabulation missing in comparison questions:** KTU examiners explicitly reward tabular comparison over paragraph form. > 7. **Missing the concept of flux concentration:** When asked why tangential IPM is preferred for large machines, students often fail to mention the $B_g > B_r$ advantage. ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print & Pin to Wall!):** - ✅ **Six main arrangements:** SPM, Inset, IPM (radial), IPM (tangential), Spoke, Halbach. - ✅ **Mounting topology vs. magnetization direction are independent design choices.** - ✅ **Load line equation:** $B_m = -\mu_0 P_c H_m$. Intersection with B-H curve gives operating point. - ✅ **Air-gap flux density formula (SPM):** $B_g = \dfrac{B_r}{\sigma \dfrac{A_g}{A_m} + \mu_r \dfrac{l_g}{l_m}}$. - ✅ **Tangential IPM has flux concentration:** $B_g$ can exceed $B_r$ when $A_g < 2 A_m$. - ✅ **IPM gives reluctance torque** (because $L_d \neq L_q$); SPM does not. - ✅ **SPM has $L_d = L_q$** (no saliency, $\mu_r \approx 1$ for the magnet). - ✅ **Halbach array: self-shielding**, no rotor back-iron needed, sinusoidal $B_g$. - ✅ **Spoke-type: flux-switching, high $B_g$, trapezoidal back-EMF.** - ✅ **Permeance coefficient design target:** $P_c = 5$–$8$ for "optimum" magnet utilization (near $(BH)_{max}$). - ✅ **Demagnetization withstand:** IPM > Inset > SPM (in general). Tangential IPM is most robust. - ✅ **EV traction motors (e.g., Toyota Prius, Tesla):** IPM with V-shape or Δ-shape. - ✅ **Industrial servos:** SPM (fractional-slot concentrated winding) for low cogging. - ✅ **Common magnet materials and $B_r$:** NdFeB (1.0–1.4 T), SmCo (0.8–1.1 T), Ferrite (0.2–0.4 T), AlNiCo (0.5–1.3 T). - ✅ **Always convert mm → m** before substituting in SI formulas. - ✅ **A diagram earns 2 marks** in a 7-mark descriptive part — never skip. - ✅ **Tabulated comparison earns full marks** in "differentiate" questions. ---

topics.title: Magnetization of Permanent Magnets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magnetization of Permanent Magnets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Magnetization of Permanent Magnets (PMs)** is the process by which a hard ferromagnetic material is subjected to a strong external magnetizing force (H-field) to align its internal magnetic domains, thereby producing a persistent, residual magnetic flux density (**B_r**) that remains in the material after the external field is removed. This residual magnetism is the source of excitation in permanent magnet machines such as PMSM, BLDC, and PM-synchronous generators.

In a permanent magnet, the operating point lies on the **demagnetization curve** (the second quadrant of the hysteresis loop) where both **B** (flux density) and **H** (magnetizing field strength) carry the same sign convention used in KTU textbook notation: **B** is positive while **H** is negative in the demagnetization quadrant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Permanent magnets form the field system of most modern special machines. The student must clearly understand the **B-H characteristics**, **demagnetization curve**, **energy product (BH)max**, and the concept of **operating point / load line** for design and analysis of PM machines.

## 1.2 Fundamental Magnetic Quantities

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Description |
|--------|----------|---------|-------------|
| B | Magnetic Flux Density | Tesla (T) | Lines of flux per unit area |
| H | Magnetizing Field Strength | A/m | External field applied |
| M | Magnetization | A/m | Internal dipole alignment |
| μ₀ | Permeability of free space | 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m | **≈ 1.2566 × 10⁻⁶ H/m** |
| μ_r | Relative Permeability | Dimensionless | μ/μ₀ |

The constitutive relation for a magnetic material is:

$$B = \mu_0 (H + M)$$

For a permanent magnet in free space, this becomes the key KTU equation:

$$B = \mu_0 H + \mu_0 M$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge & Bucket" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:**
> Imagine a **sponge** representing a ferromagnetic material and a **bucket of water** representing the applied magnetizing field (H).
> 1. When you press the sponge into water (apply H), it soaks up water (magnetic domains align).
> 2. When you remove the sponge from the bucket (remove H), some water drips out — this is like the **demagnetization curve dropping from saturation to B_r**.
> 3. The water that stays locked inside the sponge represents the **remanence (B_r)** — the permanent magnetism.
> 4. The harder the sponge (high **coercivity H_c**), the more water it retains — this is why **hard magnetic materials** make good permanent magnets.

## 1.4 Types of Magnetic Materials

Permanent magnet behaviour is exhibited only by **ferromagnetic** materials, classified as either **soft** or **hard** magnetic materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Soft Magnetic Materials** (e.g., silicon steel, soft iron): Low coercivity, narrow hysteresis loop — used as **core material** in machines, not as magnets.
> - **Hard Magnetic Materials** (e.g., AlNiCo, Ferrite, SmCo, NdFeB): High coercivity, wide hysteresis loop — used to **manufacture permanent magnets**.

## 1.5 Common Permanent Magnet Materials Used in Special Machines

| Material | B_r (T) | H_c (kA/m) | (BH)max (kJ/m³) | Application |
|----------|---------|------------|-----------------|-------------|
| AlNiCo 5 | 1.25 | –51 | 44 | Tachogenerators, small motors |
| Barium Ferrite | 0.40 | –250 | 36 | DC motors, loudspeakers |
| Samarium Cobalt (SmCo₅) | 0.90 | –700 | 160 | Servo motors, aerospace |
| Neodymium-Iron-Boron (NdFeB) | 1.20 | –900 | 280 | PMSM, BLDC, EV traction |

> [!NOTE]
> **NdFeB** has the highest energy product commercially available and dominates modern PMSM/BLDC designs. However, it has a **low Curie temperature (~310 °C)** and is prone to **irreversible demagnetization** at high temperatures — a common KTU exam point.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hysteresis Loop — Step-by-Step Logic

A complete magnetization cycle of a hard magnetic material produces the **B-H hysteresis loop**, which is divided into four quadrants. The KTU board typically focuses on **Quadrant II (Demagnetization curve)**.

**Step 1 — Initial Magnetization (virgin curve):**
Starting from a demagnetized state (B = 0, H = 0), as H is increased from 0, the flux density B rises along the **virgin magnetization curve**. The material enters the **knee region** where domains rapidly align.

**Step 2 — Saturation:**
Beyond a certain H, B no longer increases significantly. The material reaches **magnetic saturation (B_sat)**. All magnetic domains are now fully aligned.

**Step 3 — Remanence (B_r):**
When H is reduced back to zero, B does **not** return to zero. It settles at a value called **remanence / retentivity (B_r)**. This is the **residual flux density** retained by the magnet — the source of energy in a PM machine.

**Step 4 — Coercivity (H_c):**
To reduce B back to zero, a **negative** magnetizing field must be applied. The magnitude of H required is called the **coercivity (H_c)** or **coercive field strength**. **High H_c ⇒ hard magnet**.

**Step 5 — Demagnetization curve (Quadrant II):**
The portion of the hysteresis loop in the second quadrant (positive B, negative H) is called the **demagnetization curve** — this is the **operating region** of a permanent magnet in any machine.

## 2.2 Why the Demagnetization Curve Matters in PM Machines

In a permanent magnet machine:
- The magnet is **always** operated in **Quadrant II** of the B-H loop.
- The **operating point** is determined by the intersection of the **demagnetization curve** with the **load line** (also called the **permeance line**) of the external magnetic circuit.
- Any external demagnetizing field (e.g., armature reaction in a motor) shifts the operating point **down the demagnetization curve**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:**
> If the operating point drops below the **"knee"** of the demagnetization curve, the magnet suffers **irreversible demagnetization** and loses a portion of its magnetism permanently. This is a **2-mark favorite** in KTU exams.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $B = \mu_0(H + M)$ | Fundamental B-H-M relation | T |
| 2 | $B = \mu H$ | In linear media | T |
| 3 | $B = \mu_0 H + \mu_0 M$ | For a permanent magnet in free space | T |
| 4 | $w = \tfrac{1}{2} B \cdot H$ | Magnetic energy density (in air gap) | J/m³ |
| 5 | $(BH)_{\max}$ | Maximum energy product (figure of merit) | kJ/m³ |
| 6 | $\mu_{\text{rec}} = \dfrac{\Delta B}{\Delta H}$ | Recoil permeability (slope of minor loop) | H/m |
| 7 | $p_c = \dfrac{B_m}{H_m} = \dfrac{\mu_0 l_m}{A_g k_{\text{lk}}}$ | Permeance coefficient (load line slope) | dimensionless × 10⁻⁶ |
| 8 | $B_m = \dfrac{B_r}{1 + \mu_{\text{rec}}\, p_c}$ | Operating flux density of magnet | T |
| 9 | $H_m = -\,p_c \cdot B_m$ | Operating field strength of magnet | A/m |
| 10 | $L_m = \dfrac{\mu_{\text{rec}}\, A_m}{l_m}$ | Magnet internal inductance | H |

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy product (BH)max** is the single most important parameter for ranking permanent magnets. A magnet with higher (BH)max stores more magnetic energy per unit volume — hence PMSMs use NdFeB for higher power density.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor):** Field excitation comes entirely from rotor-mounted NdFeB magnets. Knowing the operating point is essential to avoid demagnetization under fault currents.
- **BLDC Motors:** Similar to PMSM; demagnetization limit decides safe peak current.
- **PM Generators (Wind Turbines):** Permanent magnets in the rotor — high (BH)max reduces magnet mass.
- **Magnetic Levitation (Maglev):** NdFeB arrays in the track require precise B-H analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Load Line (Permeance Line) for a PM Circuit

Consider a simple magnetic circuit consisting of a permanent magnet of length **l_m** and cross-sectional area **A_m**, supplying flux through an air gap of length **l_g** and area **A_g**.

### Step 1: Apply Ampere's Law Around the Magnetic Circuit

The line integral of H around the closed magnetic path equals the net MMF:

$$\oint H \cdot dl = NI = 0 \text{ (no external winding)}$$

For a PM-only circuit, the contributions come from the magnet and the air gap:

$$H_m l_m + H_g l_g = 0$$

This says: the **magnet MMF** balances the **air-gap MMF drop**.

### Step 2: Express the Air-Gap H-field in Terms of B

In the air gap, the flux is continuous (ignoring leakage):

$$B_m A_m = B_g A_g \quad\Rightarrow\quad B_g = B_m \dfrac{A_m}{A_g}$$

And in air, $B_g = \mu_0 H_g$, so:

$$H_g = \dfrac{B_m}{\mu_0} \cdot \dfrac{A_m}{A_g}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Back Into Ampere's Law

$$H_m l_m + \left(\dfrac{B_m}{\mu_0} \cdot \dfrac{A_m}{A_g}\right) l_g = 0$$

Solving for H_m:

$$H_m = -\,\dfrac{B_m \, l_g \, A_m}{\mu_0 \, l_m \, A_g}$$

### Step 4: Define the Permeance Coefficient p_c

$$p_c \equiv -\,\dfrac{H_m}{B_m} = \dfrac{l_m \, A_g}{l_g \, A_m} \cdot \mu_0$$

But conventionally, the permeance coefficient is expressed as:

$$p_c = \dfrac{B_m}{H_m} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \, l_m \, A_g}{l_g \, A_m} \quad \text{(load line slope, dimensionless with }\mu_0\text{ factor)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some KTU textbooks omit the μ₀ in the definition. The unitless **p_c = l_m A_g / (l_g A_m)** is more common. Always check the convention used in your reference.

### Step 5: Final Load-Line Equation (Operating Line)

$$\boxed{\;B_m = -\,\mu_0 \, p_c \, H_m\;}$$

The **load line** is a straight line through the origin of the B-H plane (Quadrant II) with slope **−μ₀ p_c**. Its intersection with the demagnetization curve gives the **operating point (B_m, H_m)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Energy Product (BH)max

The **magnetic energy density** stored in a magnet at any operating point is given by:

$$w = -\,\tfrac{1}{2}\, B \cdot H$$

(The negative sign appears because both B > 0 and H < 0 in Quadrant II, and energy must be positive.)

### Step 1: Express w in Terms of B Only Along the Demagnetization Curve

Using the load-line relation $H = -B/p_c$:

$$w(B) = -\,\tfrac{1}{2}\, B \cdot \left(-\,\dfrac{B}{p_c}\right) = \dfrac{B^2}{2 p_c}$$

Alternatively, from the **rectangular (BH) energy product**:

$$(BH) = B \cdot \vert H \vert = B \cdot \dfrac{B}{p_c} = \dfrac{B^2}{p_c}$$

### Step 2: Maximize (BH) with Respect to B

$$\dfrac{d(BH)}{dB} = \dfrac{d}{dB}\!\left(\dfrac{B^2}{p_c}\right) = \dfrac{2B}{p_c}$$

Wait — this grows monotonically. The correct maximization is over the **demagnetization curve**, not the load line. Treat the demagnetization curve as a straight line approximation:

$$B = B_r + \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}} H$$

For hard magnets the recoil permeability is nearly constant. The energy product becomes:

$$(BH) = (B_r + \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}} H) \cdot \vert H \vert$$

$$(BH) = -B_r H - \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}} H^2$$

### Step 3: Differentiate with Respect to H and Set to Zero

$$\dfrac{d(BH)}{dH} = -B_r - 2 \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}} H = 0$$

$$H_{\text{opt}} = -\,\dfrac{B_r}{2 \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}}}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Back to Find B_opt and (BH)max

$$B_{\text{opt}} = B_r + \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}} H_{\text{opt}} = B_r - \dfrac{B_r}{2} = \dfrac{B_r}{2}$$

$$(BH)_{\max} = B_{\text{opt}} \cdot \vert H_{\text{opt}} \vert = \dfrac{B_r}{2} \cdot \dfrac{B_r}{2 \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}}}$$

$$\boxed{\;(BH)_{\max} = \dfrac{B_r^{\,2}}{4 \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}}}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For an ideal rectangular demagnetization curve** (where H = −H_c throughout), the result simplifies to $(BH)_{\max} = B_r H_c / 4$. This is the **theoretical upper limit** used in KTU comparisons.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Recoil Line (Minor Hysteresis Loop)

When a magnet's operating point is **temporarily** shifted (e.g., during a short-circuit transient) and returns, it follows a **minor loop** called the **recoil line**.

**Step 1:** Suppose the operating point moves from (B₁, H₁) on the demagnetization curve to (B₂, H₂) under a transient demagnetizing field.

**Step 2:** When the transient field is removed, the operating point does **not** return along the original curve. Instead, it traces a straight line back to the demagnetization curve, given by:

$$B - B_1 = \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}} (H - H_1)$$

The slope of this line is **μ₀ μ_rec**, where μ_rec is the **relative recoil permeability** — typically between 1.0 and 1.15 for hard magnets.

**Step 3:** The new operating point (B', H') lies on this recoil line, intersecting the demagnetization curve at a **lower B_r** (irreversible loss) if the excursion went past the knee.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:**
> Many students incorrectly assume that B_r is constant. The **effective remanence** after a transient demagnetization is the intercept of the recoil line with the B-axis — which is **less than the original B_r**. This is a classic 14-mark derivation trap.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Recoil vs. Original Operating Point

| Condition | Operating Point Behavior | Result |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------|
| Mild demagnetization (above knee) | Moves along recoil line back to demag curve | Small **reversible** flux loss |
| Severe demagnetization (below knee) | Recoil line intercepts demag curve lower | **Irreversible** loss — magnet must be **re-magnetized** |
| Recoil permeability | $\mu_{\text{rec}} = \Delta B / \mu_0 \Delta H$ | Slope of minor loop |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Magnetization Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Demagnetized Material] --> B[Apply External H-field]
    B --> C[Domains Begin to Align]
    C --> D[Material Reaches Saturation B_sat]
    D --> E[Remove External H-field]
    E --> F[Residual Flux Density B_r Retained]
    F --> G{Operating Region}
    G --> H[Quadrant II: Demagnetization Curve]
    H --> I[Determine Permeance Coefficient p_c]
    I --> J[Plot Load Line from Origin]
    J --> K[Intersection = Operating Point B_m, H_m]
    K --> L{Operating Point vs Knee?}
    L -->|Above Knee| M[Stable Operation]
    L -->|Below Knee| N[Irreversible Demagnetization Warning]
    N --> O[Re-magnetization Required]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: PM Magnetic Circuit with Load Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MagCircuit[Permanent Magnet Magnetic Circuit]
        PM[Permanent Magnet NdFeB] -->|B_m, H_m| GAP[Air Gap l_g, A_g]
        GAP -->|B_g, H_g| CORE[Soft Iron Core]
        CORE -->|Return Path| PM
    end
    LP[Load Line Slope p_c] -.->|Intersects Demag Curve| OP[Operating Point B_m, H_m]
    DC[Demagnetization Curve Quadrant II] -.->|Provides B vs H| OP
    OP --> EM[Stored Energy Density 0.5 B_m times H_m]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart: Recoil Line Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP0[Original Operating Point P0 on Demag Curve] --> AP[Apply Transient Demag Field H_applied]
    AP --> OP1[New Point P1 Below P0]
    OP1 --> RM[Remove Transient Field]
    RM --> RP[Recoil Back Along Minor Loop]
    RP --> Q1{Within Knee Region?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Returns to Original B_r: Reversible]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Returns to Lower B_r: Irreversible Loss]
    Q3 --> RE[Re-magnetize or Replace Magnet]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Magnet Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MM[Magnetic Materials] --> FM[Ferromagnetic]
    MM --> PM1[Paramagnetic]
    MM --> DM[Diamagnetic]
    FM --> SOFT[Soft Magnetic: Low H_c, High mu_r]
    FM --> HARD[Hard Magnetic: High H_c, Forms PMs]
    SOFT --> SI[Silicon Steel]
    SOFT --> FE[Soft Iron Composite]
    HARD --> ALN[AlNiCo]
    HARD --> FER[Ferrite Ba Sr]
    HARD --> SMC[SmCo]
    HARD --> NDF[NdFeB]
    NDF --> N35[N35 N42 N52 Grades]
    NDF --> EV[Used in PMSM BLDC EV Traction]
    SMC --> AE[Used in Aerospace Servo Motors]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define the term "remanence" with reference to a permanent magnet. Why is it important in special electrical machines?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
**Remanence (B_r)** is the residual magnetic flux density that remains in a permanent magnet material after the external magnetizing field H is reduced to zero. It represents the **magnitude of B on the demagnetization curve at H = 0**.
- **Units:** Tesla (T)
- **Importance:** In special machines like PMSM and BLDC, B_r directly determines the **air-gap flux density**, which dictates the **back-EMF, torque, and power output** of the machine. A higher B_r yields higher torque density for the same frame size.

---

### Question 2
**Q: What is meant by the "energy product" of a permanent magnet? Why is (BH)max the figure of merit?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The **energy product (BH)** of a permanent magnet is the product of flux density B and demagnetizing field strength H at a given operating point on the demagnetization curve. It has units of **J/m³**.
The **maximum energy product (BH)max** is the peak value of BH along the demagnetization curve. It is the **figure of merit** because:
1. It indicates the **maximum magnetic energy per unit volume** the magnet can deliver to the external circuit.
2. A higher (BH)max means **smaller, lighter magnets** can produce the same flux, which is critical in **EV traction, aerospace, and robotics** applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the B-H hysteresis loop of a hard magnetic material. Mark on it the quantities B_r, H_c, (BH)max, and the operating region. Discuss why the second quadrant of the loop is called the demagnetization curve.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A NdFeB magnet has the following specifications:
- Remanence B_r = 1.2 T
- Coercivity H_c = −900 kA/m
- Recoil relative permeability μ_rec = 1.05

If the magnet is used in a circuit with a permeance coefficient **p_c = 2.0 (in μ₀ units)**, find:
1. The operating point (B_m, H_m).
2. The energy product at the operating point.
3. Comment on whether the magnet is being used efficiently.

### Model Solution for (a) [7 Marks]

**B-H Hysteresis Loop of a Hard Magnetic Material:**

The hysteresis loop is a closed curve in the B-H plane obtained by cycling the magnetizing field H from +H_max to −H_max and back. For a hard magnetic material (e.g., NdFeB), the loop is **wide and tall**, indicating high B_r and high H_c.

**Salient features of the loop:**

| Quadrant | Name | Physical Meaning |
|----------|------|------------------|
| I | Magnetization curve | Fresh magnetization from zero |
| II | **Demagnetization curve** | Operating region of PM |
| III | Reverse magnetization | Domains flip direction |
| IV | Return path | Re-magnetization cycle |

**Key quantities marked on the loop:**

```
B (Flux Density, T)
↑
|        Saturated loop
|       /‾‾‾‾‾\          /‾‾‾\
|      /       \        /      \
|     /         \      /        \
|    /           \    /          \   ← B_r (y-intercept of Q-II)
|---/-------------\--/------------\----→ H (Field, A/m)
| 0/    (BH)max  \\//              \-H_c (x-intercept of Q-II)
| /     Rectangle  \\\\              \
|/                  \\\\              \
              Quadrant II  ← "DEMAG CURVE"
```

- **B_r (Remanence):** Value of B on the y-axis when H = 0 (in Q-II).
- **H_c (Coercivity):** Magnitude of H on the x-axis when B = 0 (in Q-II).
- **(BH)max:** Largest rectangle that can be inscribed under the demagnetization curve in Q-II.
- **Operating region:** Quadrant II is called the demagnetization curve because for any **applied demagnetizing field** (negative H), the magnet continues to provide a positive flux B. The PM is **always operated in Q-II** in machines.

**Why Q-II is the demagnetization curve:** The material is magnetized once at manufacture. In service, the only fields experienced are **armature-reaction demagnetizing fields** (negative H) and the magnet's response is along Q-II where it provides the **residual B** needed to drive flux through the air gap.

### Model Solution for (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- B_r = 1.2 T
- H_c = −900 × 10³ A/m = −9 × 10⁵ A/m
- μ_rec = 1.05
- p_c = 2.0 (dimensionless, with implicit μ₀)

**Step 1 — Find the operating point (B_m, H_m):**

The demagnetization curve (assumed linear in Q-II) is:

$$B = B_r + \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}} H$$

The load line is:

$$B = -p_c \mu_0 H \quad \text{or} \quad H = -\,B/(p_c \mu_0)$$

Substituting into the demagnetization equation:

$$B = B_r - \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}} \cdot \dfrac{B}{p_c \mu_0} = B_r - \dfrac{\mu_{\text{rec}}\, B}{p_c}$$

$$B\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu_{\text{rec}}}{p_c}\right) = B_r$$

$$B_m = \dfrac{B_r}{1 + \mu_{\text{rec}}/p_c} = \dfrac{1.2}{1 + 1.05/2.0} = \dfrac{1.2}{1.525}$$

$$B_m \approx 0.787 \text{ T}$$

For H_m:

$$H_m = -\,\dfrac{B_m}{p_c \mu_0} = -\,\dfrac{0.787}{2.0 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$H_m \approx -\,3.13 \times 10^{5} \text{ A/m} = -313 \text{ kA/m}$$

**Step 2 — Energy product at the operating point:**

$$(BH)_m = B_m \cdot \vert H_m \vert = 0.787 \times 3.13 \times 10^{5}$$

$$(BH)_m \approx 2.46 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^{3} = 246 \text{ kJ/m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with (BH)max:**

The theoretical (BH)max (rectangular approximation):

$$(BH)_{\max} = \dfrac{B_r H_c}{4} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 9 \times 10^{5}}{4} = 270 \text{ kJ/m}^{3}$$

**Efficiency ratio:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{(BH)_m}{(BH)_{\max}} = \dfrac{246}{270} \approx 0.91 = 91\%$$

**Comment:** The magnet is being used at **91% efficiency**, which is **excellent** — close to the optimum operating point. A p_c of 2.0 places the operating point near the maximum energy product, making the design near-ideal for maximum energy extraction.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with diagrams the construction of the demagnetization curve of a permanent magnet. Derive the expression for the **permeance coefficient** of a simple magnetic circuit containing a PM and an air gap.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A SmCo magnet has B_r = 0.85 T and recoil permeability μ_rec = 1.10. The magnet has dimensions l_m = 8 mm and A_m = 600 mm², and supplies flux to an air gap of l_g = 2 mm and A_g = 500 mm². Determine:
1. The permeance coefficient p_c.
2. The operating flux density B_m.
3. The total flux supplied to the air gap.
4. The force equivalent (using Maxwell's stress) at the air gap.

### Model Solution for (a) [7 Marks]

**Construction of the Demagnetization Curve:**

The demagnetization curve is the portion of the hysteresis loop in **Quadrant II** (B > 0, H < 0). It is obtained experimentally as follows:

1. **Step 1:** A virgin magnet sample is placed in a strong electromagnet and saturated by applying H > H_sat.
2. **Step 2:** H is gradually reduced to zero. The residual flux density **B_r** is recorded at H = 0.
3. **Step 3:** H is then increased in the **negative direction** (demagnetizing). At several values of H, the corresponding B is measured.
4. **Step 4:** The locus of these (B, H) points forms the **demagnetization curve** in Q-II, terminating at B = 0, H = −H_c.

**Derivation of Permeance Coefficient:**

Apply Ampere's Law to the closed magnetic path:

$$H_m l_m + H_g l_g = 0$$

Conservation of flux:

$$B_m A_m = B_g A_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_g = B_m \dfrac{A_m}{A_g}$$

In the air gap, $B_g = \mu_0 H_g$:

$$H_g = \dfrac{B_m A_m}{\mu_0 A_g}$$

Substituting:

$$H_m l_m + \dfrac{B_m A_m l_g}{\mu_0 A_g} = 0$$

$$H_m = -\,\dfrac{B_m A_m l_g}{\mu_0 A_g l_m}$$

The permeance coefficient is defined as:

$$\boxed{\;p_c = \dfrac{B_m}{H_m} = -\,\dfrac{\mu_0 l_m A_g}{l_g A_m}\;}$$

### Model Solution for (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- B_r = 0.85 T
- μ_rec = 1.10
- l_m = 8 mm = 0.008 m
- A_m = 600 mm² = 600 × 10⁻⁶ m²
- l_g = 2 mm = 0.002 m
- A_g = 500 mm² = 500 × 10⁻⁶ m²
- μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m

**Step 1 — Permeance coefficient p_c:**

$$p_c = \dfrac{\mu_0 l_m A_g}{l_g A_m}$$

$$p_c = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 0.008 \times 500 \times 10^{-6}}{0.002 \times 600 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Numerator: $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 0.008 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-12}$

Denominator: $0.002 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$

$$p_c = \dfrac{5.027 \times 10^{-12}}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.19 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H/m}$$

Often the dimensionless form is used:

$$p_c^{\text{(dimless)}} = \dfrac{l_m A_g}{l_g A_m} = \dfrac{0.008 \times 500 \times 10^{-6}}{0.002 \times 600 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-9}}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.33 \times 10^{-3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The form **p_c = l_m A_g / (l_g A_m)** (dimensionless) is more common in KTU textbooks and exam problems. Use whichever the question statement implies.

**Step 2 — Operating flux density B_m:**

Using $B_m = B_r / (1 + \mu_{\text{rec}}/p_c)$ with p_c = 3.33 × 10⁻³:

$$B_m = \dfrac{0.85}{1 + 1.10/3.33\times 10^{-3}}$$

The denominator is dominated by the second term:

$$1 + \dfrac{1.10}{3.33 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1 + 330.3 = 331.3$$

$$B_m \approx \dfrac{0.85}{331.3} \approx 2.57 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T} = 2.57 \text{ mT}$$

**Step 3 — Total air-gap flux:**

$$\Phi_g = B_m A_m = 2.57 \times 10^{-3} \times 600 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\Phi_g = 1.54 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Wb} = 1.54 \text{ μWb}$$

**Step 4 — Maxwell stress force at the air gap:**

Maxwell's stress equation gives force per unit area as:

$$\dfrac{F}{A_g} = \dfrac{B_g^{\,2}}{2 \mu_0}$$

With $B_g = B_m A_m / A_g = 2.57 \times 10^{-3} \times 600/500 = 3.08 \times 10^{-3}$ T:

$$\dfrac{F}{A_g} = \dfrac{(3.08 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{9.49 \times 10^{-6}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\dfrac{F}{A_g} \approx 3.78 \text{ N/m}^{2}$$

$$F = 3.78 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.89 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N} = 1.89 \text{ mN}$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Students often forget that in Quadrant II, B > 0 but H < 0. Writing $B_m = B_r + \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}} H$ with H as a positive number is **WRONG** — H is negative. Always substitute H = −|H|.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch in p_c:** Mixing the dimensional form (H/m) and dimensionless form leads to B_m values that are 10⁶ times off. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting μ₀ in Energy Product:** The formula $(BH)_{\max} = B_r^2 / (4 \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}})$ requires μ₀ in the denominator — the unit is J/m³, not T·A/m.
> 4. **Confusing B_r with Operating B:** B_r is the flux density at H = 0. The **operating B_m** is **always less** than B_r (since p_c > 0). A common mistake is to use B_r in the torque equation instead of B_m.
> 5. **Recoil vs. Original Demag Curve:** If asked about stability after a transient, the answer is the **recoil line**, not the original demagnetization curve.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Magnetization of Permanent Magnets**

- [x] **B-H Hysteresis Loop** divides into 4 quadrants; **Q-II** is the demagnetization curve.
- [x] **B_r (Remanence):** Residual flux density at H = 0 (positive, in Tesla).
- [x] **H_c (Coercivity):** Negative H required to bring B to 0 (in A/m, magnitude matters).
- [x] **Hard vs. Soft Magnets:** Hard → high H_c, wide loop (AlNiCo, NdFeB, SmCo, Ferrite).
- [x] **Demagnetization Curve** is the **operating region** of all PM machines.
- [x] **Load Line (Permeance Line):** Straight line through origin of Q-II with slope $p_c = \mu_0 l_m A_g / (l_g A_m)$.
- [x] **Operating Point:** Intersection of demagnetization curve and load line.
- [x] **Energy Product (BH)max:** Figure of merit for a PM; rectangular approx: $(BH)_{\max} = B_r H_c / 4$.
- [x] **Recoil Permeability μ_rec:** Slope of minor loop; 1.0–1.15 for hard magnets.
- [x] **Irreversible Demagnetization:** Occurs when operating point drops **below the knee** of the demag curve.
- [x] **Recoil Line Equation:** $B - B_1 = \mu_0 \mu_{\text{rec}}(H - H_1)$.
- [x] **Material Hierarchy (Energy Product):** NdFeB > SmCo > AlNiCo > Ferrite.
- [x] **Magnetization Process:** Apply H → align domains → saturate → remove H → retain B_r.
- [x] **Operating B_m Formula:** $B_m = B_r / (1 + \mu_{\text{rec}} / p_c)$.
- [x] **Operating H_m Formula:** $H_m = -B_m / (p_c \mu_0)$.
- [x] **PMSM / BLDC Implication:** Operating point must stay above the knee under all load/short-circuit conditions.
- [x] **Curie Temperature Concern:** NdFeB has low Curie temp (~310 °C) — risk of thermal demagnetization in EV traction motors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axial and Parallel Magnetizations - Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Axial and Parallel Magnetizations - Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Permanent Magnet (PM) machines — specifically **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM)**, **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors**, and **Axial Flux Machines (AFM)** — the term "magnetization" describes the directional orientation of the magnetic dipole moments (M) inside a permanent magnet, which dictates how the magnetic flux lines exit and enter the rotor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — Magnetization Vector (M):**
> Magnetization is defined as the magnetic dipole moment per unit volume of the material. For a permanent magnet of volume $V_m$ and remanent flux density $B_r$, the magnetization vector has magnitude $\vert M \vert = B_r / \mu_0$ and points from the South pole to the North pole inside the magnet.

Based on this vector orientation, two fundamental magnetization topologies are defined in KTU Module 3:

**1. Parallel (Radial) Magnetization:**
The magnetization vector $\vec{M}$ is oriented **parallel to the main flux path**, which in a cylindrical radial-flux machine means the vector points **radially outward** (or inward) from the rotor surface. The N-pole and S-pole lie on the curved outer surface of the rotor, and flux crosses the air gap in a **radial direction**.

**2. Axial Magnetization:**
The magnetization vector $\vec{M}$ is oriented **along the axis of rotation** (i.e., parallel to the machine shaft). The N-pole and S-pole are located on the flat end-faces of a disc-shaped rotor. Flux crosses the air gap in an **axial direction**, making this the foundational principle of Axial Flux Permanent Magnet (AFPM) machines.

> [!NOTE]
> The classification "Parallel vs. Axial" is decided by the angle between $\vec{M}$ and the machine's rotational axis:
> - $\vec{M} \perp$ shaft → **Parallel / Radial Magnetization** (used in conventional cylindrical PMSM/BLDC)
> - $\vec{M} \parallel$ shaft → **Axial Magnetization** (used in disc-type AFPM machines)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**The "Pencil on a Table" Analogy:**

Imagine you have a strong bar magnet shaped like a pencil, and you place it near a compass needle.

| Scenario | Magnet Orientation | Compass Reaction | Machine Equivalent |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **A** | Pencil standing upright, tip pointing **up** | Needle moves sideways (perpendicular to pencil axis) | **Axial Magnetization** — flux exits through the flat end-face along the shaft direction |
| **B** | Pencil lying flat, N-pole on the **left** end | Needle aligns along the pencil length | **Parallel Magnetization** — flux exits radially across the cylindrical surface |

**Real-world Analogy — The Ceiling Fan vs. The Table Fan:**

- A **conventional ceiling fan motor** uses **parallel (radial) magnetization** — the rotor is a vertical cylinder with curved PMs on its outer skin; flux travels horizontally (radially) from rotor to stator.
- A **table fan / pancake motor** uses **axial magnetization** — the rotor is a flat disc, and flux travels vertically (axially) across a thin disc-shaped air gap.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "**P**arallel magnetization is for cylindrical rotors, **A**xial magnetization is for disc rotors." The first letter of the magnetization type matches the first letter of the rotor shape.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following KTU-board-standard values and metrics are used throughout this topic:

- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$
- **Remanent flux density of modern NdFeB magnets:** $B_r = 1.0 \text{ to } 1.4 \ \text{T}$
- **Remanent flux density of Ferrite magnets:** $B_r = 0.35 \text{ to } 0.45 \ \text{T}$
- **Relative recoil permeability (NdFeB):** $\mu_r \approx 1.04 \text{ to } 1.10$ (treated as $\approx 1$ in most KTU problems)
- **Air gap flux density (typical):** $B_g = 0.6 \text{ to } 0.9 \ \text{T}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Operating Principles

#### 2.1.1 Parallel (Radial) Magnetization — Operating Principle

**Step 1 — Rotor Construction**
The rotor is a solid or laminated steel cylinder. Curved arc-shaped permanent magnets (typically NdFeB or SmCo) are **surface-mounted** on the rotor periphery. Each magnet occupies one pole-pitch, and the polarity alternates: N-S-N-S around the circumference.

**Step 2 — Magnetization Vector Direction**
For a $P$-pole machine, the magnetization vector of the $k^{th}$ pole points radially outward (for N) or inward (for S). Mathematically, in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta)$:

$$
\vec{M}_k = M_0 \, \hat{r} \cdot (-1)^k
$$

where $M_0 = B_r / \mu_0$ is the magnitude of magnetization, $\hat{r}$ is the unit radial vector, and $k$ indexes the pole.

**Step 3 — Why "Parallel" the Name?**
The magnetization vector $\vec{M}$ is **parallel** to the radial unit vector $\hat{r}$, which is the same direction as the air-gap flux density $\vec{B}_g$ in a non-salient rotor. Hence the name "parallel magnetization."

**Step 4 — Air Gap Field Production**
The flux produced by the magnet crosses the air gap **radially** and links with the stator winding. The effective air gap length is the **sum** of the mechanical clearance and the magnet thickness:

$$
g_{eff} = g_m + l_m
$$

where $g_m$ is the mechanical air gap and $l_m$ is the magnet thickness in the radial direction. Because $\mu_r \approx 1$ for NdFeB, the magnet acts almost like air magnetically, so the field is **weakened** by the magnet's reluctance.

**Step 5 — Saliency & Inductance**
Surface-mounted parallel-magnetized rotors are **non-salient** because the magnet permeability equals air permeability. Therefore:

$$
L_d = L_q = L_s
$$

This simplifies the torque equation significantly (no reluctance torque component).

**Step 6 — EMF Generation**
As the rotor spins, the rotating magnetic field from the magnets cuts the stationary stator conductors, inducing a sinusoidal (PMSM) or trapezoidal (BLDC) back-EMF.

---

#### 2.1.2 Axial Magnetization — Operating Principle

**Step 1 — Rotor Construction**
The rotor is a **flat disc** (or a pair of discs for double-sided AFPM) mounted on the shaft. Rectangular or trapezoidal PMs are surface-mounted on the flat faces of the disc, with N and S poles alternating circumferentially.

**Step 2 — Magnetization Vector Direction**
In this case, the magnetization vector points **along the shaft axis** (z-axis). For the top face of a single-rotor single-stator AFPM:

$$
\vec{M}_k = M_0 \, \hat{z} \cdot (-1)^k
$$

where $\hat{z}$ is the unit vector along the shaft.

**Step 3 — Why "Axial" the Name?**
The magnetization vector $\vec{M}$ is **axial**, i.e., parallel to the axis of rotation. The flux exits the N-pole face, crosses a small axial air gap, enters the stator teeth, flows circumferentially through the stator yoke, crosses back through the air gap at the S-pole region, and returns through the rotor yoke.

**Step 4 — Air Gap Field & Effective Length**
The axial air gap is the **only** mechanical clearance between the disc rotor and the disc stator. The PM thickness is **not** added to the mechanical air gap in the magnetic sense if the disc is sandwiched; the flux path is purely axial in the air gap region.

**Step 5 — High Flux Density Achievable**
Because there is no magnet material in the air gap itself (the magnet is on the back-iron of the disc), the air gap flux density can be made very high — typically $B_g = 0.8$ to $1.2 \ \text{T}$ in well-designed AFPMs.

**Step 6 — EMF & Torque**
The flux per pole depends on the **radial pole area** $A_{pole}$ (the face area of the magnet):

$$
\Phi = B_g \cdot A_{pole}
$$

The torque in an AFPM machine is given by:

$$
T = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot B_g \cdot A_{linear}
$$

where $D_o$ and $D_i$ are the outer and inner disc diameters, and $A_{linear}$ is the linear current density (A/m).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use Axial magnetization?** Axial flux machines offer a much **shorter axial length** for the same torque output, making them ideal for direct-drive applications like **electric vehicle in-wheel motors, wind turbine generators, and aerospace actuators**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for KTU 2024 ESE problems on this topic. Master every entry.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Magnetization magnitude | $M_0 = B_r / \mu_0$ | Magnitude of M, in A/m |
| 2 | B-H relation in PM | $B = \mu_0(H + M)$ | Inside a permanent magnet |
| 3 | Air gap flux density (parallel) | $B_g = B_r \cdot \dfrac{l_m}{l_m + \mu_r g_m}$ | For surface-mounted radial-flux PMSM |
| 4 | Air gap flux density (axial) | $B_g = B_r \cdot \dfrac{l_m}{l_m + \mu_r g_a}$ | For disc-type AFPM |
| 5 | Flux per pole | $\Phi = B_g \cdot A_{pole}$ | $A_{pole}$ in m², $\Phi$ in Wb |
| 6 | Effective pole area (radial) | $A_{pole} = \pi D L / P$ | D = rotor OD, L = stack length, P = poles |
| 7 | Effective pole area (axial) | $A_{pole} = \dfrac{\pi}{4P}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$ | Disc machine, $D_o, D_i$ outer/inner diameter |
| 8 | Induced EMF per phase (RMS) | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi \, K_w$ | Sinusoidal PMSM |
| 9 | Induced EMF (BLDC, line) | $E = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega}{60}$ | Trapezoidal BLDC |
| 10 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120 \, f / P$ | rpm |
| 11 | Electromagnetic torque (PMSM) | $T = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot (\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q)$ | General vector torque |
| 12 | Torque (non-salient PMSM) | $T = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \psi_m \cdot I_q$ | When $L_d = L_q$ (parallel mag. case) |
| 13 | Torque (axial flux, single-sided) | $T = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot B_g \cdot A$ | A = electrical loading (A/m) |
| 14 | Power output | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega_m$ | $\omega_m$ in rad/s |
| 15 | Reluctance of air gap | $\mathcal{R}_g = g / (\mu_0 A)$ | For parallel-flux path |
| 16 | MMF of PM | $\mathcal{F}_m = H_c \cdot l_m$ | $H_c$ = coercivity |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Preferred Magnetization | Reason |
|--------------------|------------------------|--------|
| **Industrial servo drives (CNC)** | Parallel (radial) | High dynamic response, established manufacturing |
| **Electric Vehicle traction motors** | Parallel (interior) | High speed capability, field-weakening range |
| **In-wheel EV motors (porsche, protean)** | **Axial** | Pancake form factor, fits inside wheel hub |
| **Wind turbine direct-drive generators** | **Axial** | Very high torque at low RPM, compact diameter |
| **Hard disk drive spindles** | Parallel (radial) | Ultra-high precision, low torque ripple |
| **Aircraft actuators & drones** | **Axial** | Lightweight, high power density |
| **HVAC compressors** | Parallel (radial) BLDC | Cost-effective, long life |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Air Gap Flux Density in Parallel (Radial) Magnetization

**Problem Statement:** A surface-mounted PMSM has a rotor outer diameter $D = 100$ mm, a magnet thickness $l_m = 4$ mm (radial), and a mechanical air gap $g_m = 1.5$ mm. The magnet is NdFeB with $B_r = 1.2$ T and $\mu_r = 1.05$. Determine the air gap flux density $B_g$ at the magnet center.

**Step 1 — Set up the magnetic equivalent circuit**

The PM is modelled as a flux source $\mathcal{F}_m$ in series with its own reluctance $\mathcal{R}_m$, and this combination is in series with the air gap reluctance $\mathcal{R}_g$.

$$
\mathcal{F}_m = H_c \cdot l_m
$$

$$
\mathcal{R}_m = \frac{l_m}{\mu_0 \mu_r A_m}
$$

$$
\mathcal{R}_g = \frac{g_m}{\mu_0 A_g}
$$

Assuming $A_m \approx A_g = A$ (no fringing correction), the total circuit reluctance is:

$$
\mathcal{R}_{total} = \mathcal{R}_m + \mathcal{R}_g = \frac{l_m}{\mu_0 \mu_r A} + \frac{g_m}{\mu_0 A}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the flux equation**

Applying Ampere's law around the loop:

$$
\Phi = \frac{\mathcal{F}_m}{\mathcal{R}_{total}} = \frac{H_c \, l_m}{\dfrac{l_m}{\mu_0 \mu_r A} + \dfrac{g_m}{\mu_0 A}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify to find air gap flux density**

The air gap flux density is $B_g = \Phi / A$, so:

$$
B_g = \frac{\mu_0 H_c \, l_m / A}{\dfrac{l_m}{\mu_0 \mu_r A} + \dfrac{g_m}{\mu_0 A}} = \frac{H_c \, l_m}{\dfrac{l_m}{\mu_r} + g_m}
$$

Using the identity $B_r = \mu_0 \mu_r H_c$ (remanence in the magnet's B-H second quadrant), we get $H_c = B_r / (\mu_0 \mu_r)$. Substituting:

$$
B_g = \frac{\dfrac{B_r}{\mu_0 \mu_r} \cdot l_m}{\dfrac{l_m}{\mu_r} + g_m} = \frac{B_r \cdot l_m}{l_m + \mu_r \, g_m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU-board Result (Parallel Magnetization):**
>
> $$\boxed{B_g = B_r \cdot \frac{l_m}{l_m + \mu_r \, g_m}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation**

Plug in $B_r = 1.2$ T, $l_m = 4$ mm, $g_m = 1.5$ mm, $\mu_r = 1.05$:

$$
B_g = 1.2 \times \frac{4}{4 + 1.05 \times 1.5}
$$

$$
B_g = 1.2 \times \frac{4}{4 + 1.575}
$$

$$
B_g = 1.2 \times \frac{4}{5.575}
$$

$$
B_g = 1.2 \times 0.7175 = 0.861 \ \text{T}
$$

**Conclusion:** The air gap flux density is approximately **0.861 T**, which is a realistic value for a high-performance PMSM.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Air Gap Flux Density in Axial Magnetization

**Problem Statement:** An axial flux PM machine has a single air gap $g_a = 1.0$ mm, a magnet thickness $l_m = 5$ mm (axial), and a magnet of $B_r = 1.1$ T, $\mu_r = 1.05$. Find the air gap flux density.

**Step 1 — Magnetic equivalent circuit**

The flux path is purely axial through the magnet, then axial through the air gap. The same series MMF–reluctance model applies, but the magnet is in the **flux path itself** (not on a cylindrical surface):

$$
\mathcal{F}_m = H_c \cdot l_m = \frac{B_r}{\mu_0 \mu_r} \cdot l_m
$$

$$
\mathcal{R}_{total} = \frac{l_m}{\mu_0 \mu_r A} + \frac{g_a}{\mu_0 A}
$$

**Step 2 — Air gap field expression**

Following the same derivation as in 3.1:

$$
B_g = \frac{\Phi}{A} = \frac{\mathcal{F}_m}{A \cdot \mathcal{R}_{total}}
$$

$$
B_g = \frac{H_c \, l_m}{\dfrac{l_m}{\mu_r} + g_a} = B_r \cdot \frac{l_m}{l_m + \mu_r \, g_a}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU-board Result (Axial Magnetization):**
>
> $$\boxed{B_g = B_r \cdot \frac{l_m}{l_m + \mu_r \, g_a}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation**

$$
B_g = 1.1 \times \frac{5}{5 + 1.05 \times 1.0}
$$

$$
B_g = 1.1 \times \frac{5}{5 + 1.05}
$$

$$
B_g = 1.1 \times \frac{5}{6.05}
$$

$$
B_g = 1.1 \times 0.8264 = 0.909 \ \text{T}
$$

**Conclusion:** The axial machine produces a slightly **higher** air gap flux density (0.909 T) than the radial machine (0.861 T) for comparable dimensions, demonstrating one engineering advantage of axial flux topology.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: EMF Equation for Surface-Mounted PMSM (Parallel Magnetization)

**Step 1 — Flux per pole**

$$
\Phi = B_g \cdot A_{pole} = B_g \cdot \frac{\pi D L}{P}
$$

where $D$ is the rotor outer diameter, $L$ is the stack length, and $P$ is the number of poles.

**Step 2 — Frequency of induced EMF**

For a $P$-pole machine spinning at $N$ rpm:

$$
f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120} \ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-phase RMS EMF**

$$
E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi \, K_w
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

Given: $P = 4$, $N = 1500$ rpm, $D = 100$ mm, $L = 80$ mm, $B_g = 0.861$ T, $N_{ph} = 200$ turns, $K_w = 0.96$.

Compute frequency:

$$
f = \frac{4 \times 1500}{120} = 50 \ \text{Hz}
$$

Compute flux per pole:

$$
\Phi = 0.861 \times \frac{\pi \times 0.1 \times 0.08}{4} = 0.861 \times 0.006283 = 5.41 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Wb}
$$

Compute EMF:

$$
E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 200 \times 5.41 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.96
$$

$$
E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 200 \times 5.41 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.96 \approx 230.5 \ \text{V}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Parallel (Radial) Magnetization | Axial Magnetization |
|-----------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Rotor shape** | Cylindrical | Disc / pancake |
| **Magnet orientation** | $\vec{M} \perp$ shaft (radially) | $\vec{M} \parallel$ shaft |
| **Flux direction in air gap** | Radial | Axial |
| **$B_g$ formula** | $B_r \cdot \dfrac{l_m}{l_m + \mu_r g_m}$ | $B_r \cdot \dfrac{l_m}{l_m + \mu_r g_a}$ |
| **Typical $B_g$** | 0.6 – 0.9 T | 0.8 – 1.2 T |
| **Power density** | Medium (3 – 5 kW/kg) | **High** (5 – 8 kW/kg) |
| **Aspect ratio** | Long axial length | Short axial, large diameter |
| **Saliency** | Non-salient ($L_d = L_q$) | Depends on topology |
| **Cooling** | Easier (radial fan path) | Harder (closed disc) |
| **Cost of PM** | Lower (less material) | Higher (more magnet area) |
| **Application** | Servos, EV traction, spindles | Wind, EV in-wheel, aerospace |
