topics.title: Self-Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Self-Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — SELF-CONTROL (Brushless DC & PMSM Drives)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Self-Control (Self-Controlled Mode of Operation)**
> A mode of operation of a synchronous / brushless DC motor drive in which the **frequency of the stator supply is not independent**, but is directly **slaved (locked) to the instantaneous rotor speed** through rotor-position feedback. The inverter feeding the machine is commutated **electronically** using the rotor-position signal, so that the stator rotating field always travels **in synchronism with the permanent-magnet rotor**.

Mathematically, the relationship that defines a self-controlled drive is:

$$f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120} \;\; \text{Hz}$$

where $P$ is the number of poles and $N$ is the mechanical speed in **rpm**. The above relation must hold **at every instant** of operation; the drive cannot be stable outside this condition.

> [!NOTE] **Why the name "Self-Control"?**
> The machine controls its own supply frequency through rotor-position feedback — hence the supply is "self-controlled" by the rotor. There is no independent frequency reference; the rotor dictates the electrical frequency.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **street performer walking and singing at the same time**:
- If the singer sings to a *fixed* metronome, but the walker's pace changes, the singer will fall out of rhythm (this is **separate / open-loop control** — prone to instability and pull-out).
- If the singer *listens* to their own footsteps and adjusts the singing tempo to match, they always remain in rhythm, no matter how fast or slow they walk (this is **self-control**).

The BLDC / PMSM rotor in self-control acts like the "walker's footsteps," and the inverter acts like the "singer" — it constantly listens to the rotor's actual position and re-times its switching pulses to follow it.

A second analogy: think of a **child on a swing** being pushed by a parent.
- The push (stator excitation) is delivered only when the swing reaches a certain angle.
- The push frequency automatically equals the swing frequency — the parent does not push at a "preset" clock rate, but at the *natural* rhythm of the swing.
- If the swing slows down, the pushes slow down too. This is exactly what a self-controlled drive does — the inverter "pushes" (commutates) only when the rotor reaches the appropriate angular position.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Supply frequency $f$** is **NOT** a free variable — it is computed from rotor position in real time.
- **Pole pair number $p = P/2$** is a fixed machine constant.
- **Electrical angular velocity** $\omega_e = p \cdot \omega_m$ rad/s, where $\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ is the mechanical angular velocity in **rad/s**.
- **Slip** $s = 0$ in the self-controlled synchronous mode (no asynchronous operation).

> [!NOTE] **KTU High-Yield Note**
> In KTU 2024 scheme valuation, examiners specifically look for the statement: *"The stator frequency tracks the rotor speed, eliminating the stability (pull-out) problem of open-loop synchronous motors."* Always write this in your answer.

---

### 1.4 Where Self-Control is Used (Real-World Context)

Self-control is the **industry-standard** mode of operation for:
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) motors** in drone propellers, computer fans, EV auxiliary drives, hard-disk spindle motors.
- **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM)** in servo drives, robotics, CNC spindles, electric-vehicle traction (Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV drivetrain).
- **Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM)** motors in hybrid vehicles (Toyota THS, Honda i-MMD).

> [!TIP] **Why is self-control so dominant in industry?**
> Because it completely **eliminates the pull-out / hunting problem** of conventional synchronous motors — the machine *cannot* fall out of synchronism by definition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation — Structured Logic

The self-control scheme is built around a **closed-loop rotor-position feedback** architecture. The operating sequence is:

1. **Rotor position sensing** — A position sensor (Hall-effect sensor, optical encoder, or resolver) detects the angular position of the permanent-magnet rotor in real time.
2. **Signal decoding** — The position information is converted into six discrete switching states (one for every 60° electrical) by a decoder / logic circuit.
3. **Gate pulse generation** — The decoded signals are passed through a gate-driver stage which produces the firing pulses for the **six IGBTs / MOSFETs** of the 3-phase inverter.
4. **Inverter switching** — The 3-phase voltage-source inverter (VSI) feeds the BLDC / PMSM stator windings with the appropriate phase sequence.
5. **Electromagnetic torque** — The interaction of the stator field with the rotor magnets produces a unidirectional torque because the field is *always* correctly oriented with respect to the rotor.
6. **Continuous tracking** — As the rotor moves, the sensor output changes, the decoder updates, and the inverter re-switches — completing the **electronic commutation cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Insight**
> The "commutation" in a BLDC / PMSM drive is **electronic, not mechanical**. There are no brushes and no commutator — the inverter + rotor-position sensor together act as an "electronic commutator."

---

## 2.2 Difference Between Separate Excitation and Self-Control

| Feature | Separate Excitation (Open-loop V/f) | Self-Control (Closed-loop) |
|---|---|---|
| Supply frequency source | Independent oscillator | Derived from rotor position |
| Stability | Limited — pull-out can occur at high loads | Inherently stable — no pull-out |
| Speed accuracy | Poor under load changes | Excellent — speed determined by supply voltage/frequency |
| Hunting problem | Present | Absent |
| Sensor requirement | Not mandatory | Mandatory (Hall/Encoder/Resolver) |
| Dynamic response | Slow | Fast |
| Use case | Simple fan/pump drives | Servo, robotics, EV traction |
| Complexity | Low | Higher (sensor + decoder) |

> [!WARNING] **Common KTU Mistake**
> Students often write *"self-control uses V/f control"* — this is **WRONG**. V/f is an open-loop scheme. Self-control is **not** a V/f drive; the frequency is dictated by the rotor, not by a V/f law.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Relation | Unit | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f$ | Stator electrical frequency | $f = \dfrac{P \cdot N}{120}$ | Hz | Must equal rotor electrical frequency |
| $N$ | Mechanical rotor speed | $N = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | rpm | Determined by inverter switching rate |
| $P$ | Number of poles | Machine constant | — | Even integer (PMSM / BLDC) |
| $p$ | Number of pole pairs | $p = P/2$ | — | — |
| $\omega_m$ | Mechanical angular velocity | $\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | — |
| $\omega_e$ | Electrical angular velocity | $\omega_e = p \cdot \omega_m = 2\pi f$ | rad/s | Synchronous condition |
| $s$ | Slip | $s = 0$ | — | Synchronous mode |
| $E_b$ | Back-EMF (per phase) | $E_b = k_e \cdot \omega_m$ | V | $k_e$ = back-EMF constant |
| $T_e$ | Electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \dfrac{P_{em}}{\omega_m} = \dfrac{3}{2} p \left( \lambda_m i_q \right)$ | N·m | $i_q$ = q-axis stator current |
| $V_s$ | Stator phase voltage | $V_s \approx E_b + I_a R_a$ (steady state) | V | At low speeds, $I_a R_a$ dominates |
| $K_t$ | Torque constant | $K_t = \dfrac{3}{2} p \lambda_m$ | N·m/A | $\lambda_m$ = rotor flux linkage |

> [!NOTE] **CRITICAL: LaTeX Pipe-Symbol Warning**
> In the table above, the absolute-value / "such that" notation has been written using descriptive words (e.g., "where", "given by") rather than the vertical pipe `|` character, to avoid breaking the markdown table renderer. In your answer sheet, you may freely use $\vert \cdot \vert$.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The self-control principle is the **technological foundation** of:
- **Electric vehicle traction drives** (zero hunting → safe high-speed cruising).
- **Aerospace actuators and CNC machines** (precise speed tracking under load transients).
- **Hard-disk spindle motors** (jitter-free rotation at 5400 / 7200 / 10000 rpm).
- **Medical ventilators and robotic surgery arms** (bi-directional, fast dynamic response).
- **Renewable-energy wind-turbine pitch and yaw systems** (reliable variable-speed operation).

> [!TIP] **Why Self-Control Wins in Modern Drives**
> Because there is no inherent pull-out torque limit — the only torque limit is set by the inverter current rating and the machine's thermal limit. This is why modern EV motors (PMSM) are always operated in self-control mode.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Frequency–Speed Relationship $f = P N / 120$

We derive the fundamental synchronous relationship that **must** hold in any self-controlled drive.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of mechanical angular velocity.**

The mechanical angular velocity of the rotor, expressed in revolutions per second, is

$$\frac{N}{60} \;\; \text{rev/s}$$

where $N$ is the rotor speed in **rpm** (revolutions per minute).

**Step 2 — Convert revolutions per second to electrical cycles per second.**

In a $P$-pole machine, one mechanical revolution corresponds to $P/2$ electrical cycles. Therefore the number of electrical cycles per second (i.e., the stator supply frequency $f$) is

$$f = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the constant.**

Multiplying the numerical constants:

$$f = \frac{P \cdot N}{2 \cdot 60} = \frac{P \cdot N}{120}$$

This is the **canonical synchronous relationship**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Result**
> $$\boxed{\,f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120}\,} \quad \text{Hz, with } P = \text{total poles}, \; N = \text{rpm}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expression of Torque in Self-Controlled PMSM

We derive the electromagnetic torque of a PMSM operated in self-control, using the standard $d$–$q$ axis model.

**Step 1 — Write the $q$-axis stator voltage equation** (steady state, neglecting resistance for clarity):

$$V_q = R_s \, i_q + \omega_e L_q \, i_d + \omega_e \lambda_m$$

where $\lambda_m$ is the permanent-magnet flux linkage, $L_q$ is the $q$-axis inductance, and $R_s$ is the stator resistance.

**Step 2 — Write the $d$-axis stator voltage equation:**

$$V_d = R_s \, i_d - \omega_e L_q \, i_q$$

**Step 3 — Compute the input electrical power** (3-phase, balanced sinusoidal):

$$P_{in} = \frac{3}{2} \left( V_d \, i_d + V_q \, i_q \right)$$

**Step 4 — Subtract copper loss** to obtain air-gap power:

$$P_{ag} = P_{in} - \frac{3}{2} R_s \left( i_d^2 + i_q^2 \right)$$

After substitution and simplification (cancelling the $R_s$ terms in $V_q$ and $V_d$):

$$P_{ag} = \frac{3}{2} \omega_e \left[ \lambda_m \, i_q + (L_d - L_q) \, i_d \, i_q \right]$$

**Step 5 — Convert air-gap power to electromagnetic torque** by dividing by the mechanical angular velocity $\omega_m = \omega_e / p$:

$$T_e = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_m} = \frac{3}{2} \, p \left[ \lambda_m \, i_q + (L_d - L_q) \, i_d \, i_q \right]$$

**Step 6 — Identify the two torque components** (extremely important for KTU valuation):

$$T_e = \underbrace{\frac{3}{2} p \lambda_m i_q}_{\text{Magnet / Excitation torque}} + \underbrace{\frac{3}{2} p (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q}_{\text{Reluctance torque}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Special Case — Surface-mounted PMSM (SPM)**
> For surface-mounted magnets, $L_d = L_q$, so the reluctance term vanishes, and
> $$T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \lambda_m i_q = K_t \, i_q$$
> This is the famous *"torque is directly proportional to $q$-axis current"* result, identical in form to a separately-excited DC motor — which is the entire reason PMSM/BLDC drives in self-control behave like "ideal" DC motors.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relation Between $\omega_e$ and $\omega_m$

Starting from $f = P N / 120$:

**Step 1 — Multiply both sides by $2\pi$** to convert Hz → rad/s:

$$2\pi f = \frac{2\pi P N}{120}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the left side as $\omega_e$ (electrical rad/s)**:

$$\omega_e = \frac{2\pi P N}{120}$$

**Step 3 — Recall that $\omega_m = 2\pi N / 60$** (mechanical rad/s):

$$\omega_m = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Divide the two equations** to obtain the synchronous identity:

$$\frac{\omega_e}{\omega_m} = \frac{2\pi P N / 120}{2\pi N / 60} = \frac{P}{2} = p$$

$$\boxed{\, \omega_e = p \, \omega_m \,}$$

> [!TIP] **Examination Pearl**
> This single identity $\omega_e = p \cdot \omega_m$ tells you everything about why pole count matters: doubling the poles halves the mechanical speed for the same electrical frequency. This is the fundamental reason EV traction motors are designed with **high pole counts** (often 8 or 10 poles) to reduce mechanical RPM for the same inverter frequency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Self-Controlled PMSM / BLDC Drive

Below is a complete functional architecture flow of a self-controlled scheme, showing the closed-loop path from rotor position to inverter firing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DC["DC Link / Battery V_dc"]:::pwr
    INV["Three Phase Voltage Source Inverter (6 IGBTs)"]:::pwr
    MTR["BLDC or PMSM Stator Windings"]:::mch
    ROT["Permanent Magnet Rotor"]:::mch
    POS["Rotor Position Sensor - Hall Effect or Optical Encoder"]:::sns
    DEC["Position Decoder and Logic Circuit"]:::ctrl
    GD["Gate Driver and Isolation Stage"]:::ctrl
    SP["Speed Reference and PWM Controller"]:::ctrl

    SP -- "PWM pulses" --> GD
    POS -- "Three phase H1 H2 H3 signals" --> DEC
    DEC -- "Six gate timing signals S1 to S6" --> GD
    GD -- "Isolated gate pulses to IGBTs" --> INV
    DC -- "DC bus voltage" --> INV
    INV -- "Phase voltages Va Vb Vc" --> MTR
    MTR -- "Mechanical rotation" --> ROT
    ROT -- "Mechanical coupling" --> POS

    classDef pwr fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#ff6b35,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mch fill:#0e3b2e,stroke:#3ddc84,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sns fill:#3a1d4d,stroke:#bb86fc,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#4a3a0a,stroke:#ffd60a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **red loop** is the *power* path: DC bus → Inverter → Stator.
- The **green loop** is the *mechanical* path: Stator → Rotor (shaft).
- The **purple loop** is the *position-sensing* path: Rotor → Sensor → Decoder → Gate driver → Inverter.
- The **yellow loop** is the *command* path: External speed reference → PWM controller → Gate driver.
- The closed feedback from **Rotor → Position Sensor** is what makes the drive *self-controlled*.

---

## 4.2 Commutation State Diagram — Six-Step Electronic Commutation

For a 3-phase BLDC motor, every 60° electrical, the active phase pair changes. The table below is the canonical "6-step commutation sequence" — memorize it for KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: 0 to 60 deg - Phase A High, Phase B Low"]:::stp
    S2["Step 2: 60 to 120 deg - Phase A High, Phase C Low"]:::stp
    S3["Step 3: 120 to 180 deg - Phase B High, Phase C Low"]:::stp
    S4["Step 4: 180 to 240 deg - Phase B High, Phase A Low"]:::stp
    S5["Step 5: 240 to 300 deg - Phase C High, Phase A Low"]:::stp
    S6["Step 6: 300 to 360 deg - Phase C High, Phase B Low"]:::stp
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S1

    classDef stp fill:#0b3954,stroke:#087e8b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE] **KTU Examination Tip**
> In the exam, if you draw the block diagram and label **any 4 of the 6 commutation states** in a small table, you will easily fetch full marks for the "explain the working" sub-question.

---

## 4.3 Sensorless Self-Control — Architecture Block Diagram

For high-reliability applications (e.g., aerospace, sealed pumps), the rotor position can be *estimated* from the back-EMF rather than measured.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M["PMSM or BLDC Motor"]:::mch
    V["Terminal Voltage Sensing - Va Vb Vc"]:::sns
    I["Phase Current Sensing - Ia Ib"]:::sns
    EMF["Back EMF Estimator - Zero Crossing Detector"]:::ctrl
    PLL["Phase Locked Loop for Rotor Angle Estimation"]:::ctrl
    GD["Gate Driver Unit"]:::ctrl
    INV["Three Phase Inverter"]:::pwr
    DC["DC Source"]:::pwr

    DC --> INV
    INV --> M
    M -- "Phase voltages" --> V
    M -- "Phase currents" --> I
    V --> EMF
    I --> EMF
    EMF --> PLL
    PLL --> GD
    GD --> INV

    classDef pwr fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#ff6b35,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mch fill:#0e3b2e,stroke:#3ddc84,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sns fill:#3a1d4d,stroke:#bb86fc,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#4a3a0a,stroke:#ffd60a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> [!WARNING] **Common Confusing Point**
> In sensorless mode, the drive cannot start from standstill using back-EMF (because EMF is zero at zero speed). It requires an *open-loop ramp start* and then transitions to closed-loop back-EMF-based self-control above a minimum speed (typically > 5–10% of rated speed).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define self-control as applied to synchronous / brushless DC motor drives. Why is the rotor-position feedback essential?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Self-control is a closed-loop mode of operating a synchronous or BLDC motor in which the **frequency of the inverter-fed stator supply is automatically and continuously made equal to the rotor electrical frequency** using rotor-position feedback. The mathematical condition is $f = P N / 120$, which must hold at every instant.

**Why feedback is essential (1 mark):** Without rotor-position feedback, the stator field cannot be correctly oriented with respect to the rotor magnets. This causes loss of synchronism (pull-out) at high loads, and the motor will stall. The feedback ensures that commutation occurs exactly when the rotor magnets align with the appropriate stator phase axis, producing **unidirectional torque** at all instants.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Compare separate-excited and self-controlled modes of synchronous motor drives in terms of stability and dynamic response.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Separate-Excited (Open-loop V/f) | Self-Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| Stability | Limited — pull-out torque is a hard limit | Inherently stable — no pull-out |
| Hunting | Present, may need damper windings | Absent |
| Dynamic response | Slow, sluggish | Fast and precise |
| Sensor needed | No | Yes (Hall / Encoder) |

In one concluding line: *Self-control completely eliminates the pull-out and hunting problems of conventional synchronous motors at the cost of added sensor complexity.* (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> In a 14-mark self-control question, the most common mark-losing mistakes are:
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the closed feedback loop** from rotor to position sensor — examiners specifically look for this arrow. -2 marks.
> 2. **Writing the V/f equation instead of the synchronous condition** $f = PN/120$. -1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing BLDC and PMSM terminology** — they have *similar* self-control schemes but differ in back-EMF waveform (trapezoidal vs sinusoidal). -1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting unit symbols** (Hz, rpm, N·m). -0.5 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the pull-out / hunting elimination** explicitly. -1 mark.

---

### **Question A — (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a self-controlled BLDC motor drive. Identify the function of each block. (7 marks)**

**(b) Derive the electromagnetic torque expression of a PMSM operating in self-control mode using the d–q model. Hence show that for a surface-mounted PMSM, $T_e \propto i_q$. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the diagram exactly as in Section 4.1. Label the following six blocks clearly:
1. **DC Source / Battery** — provides constant DC link voltage $V_{dc}$.
2. **3-Phase Voltage Source Inverter** — six IGBTs $S_1$ to $S_6$, converts DC to variable-frequency 3-phase AC.
3. **BLDC / PMSM Stator** — receives phase voltages $V_a, V_b, V_c$.
4. **Permanent-Magnet Rotor** — rotates at mechanical speed $N$ rpm.
5. **Rotor Position Sensor** — Hall sensor / optical encoder; outputs 3 digital signals.
6. **Decoder + Gate Driver** — converts sensor signals to 6 firing pulses for the IGBTs.

**Working (3 marks):** Write the working in 4–5 sentences:
- The rotor position sensor detects which two stator phases should be energized to produce torque in the **current rotor position**.
- The decoder logic activates the corresponding two IGBTs, applying DC voltage across the two active phases.
- Current flows through the active windings, producing a stator MMF that interacts with the rotor magnet field, generating unidirectional torque.
- As the rotor moves by 60° electrical, the sensor signals change, the next pair of IGBTs is fired, and the cycle repeats.
- Thus, electronic commutation replaces the mechanical commutator–brush assembly of a conventional DC motor.

**Key Result (1 mark):** The supply frequency automatically tracks the rotor: $f = PN/120$, ensuring perfect synchronism at all times.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Setup (1 mark):** Use the standard d–q reference frame rotating at synchronous electrical speed $\omega_e$ with $d$-axis aligned to the rotor flux $\lambda_m$.

**Voltage equations (1 mark):**

$$V_d = R_s i_d - \omega_e L_q i_q$$
$$V_q = R_s i_q + \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \lambda_m$$

**Input power (1 mark):**

$$P_{in} = \frac{3}{2}\left( V_d i_d + V_q i_q \right)$$

**Air-gap power (1 mark):** After subtracting copper losses $\frac{3}{2} R_s (i_d^2 + i_q^2)$ and simplifying:

$$P_{ag} = \frac{3}{2} \omega_e \left[ \lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$$

**Torque (2 marks):** Dividing by $\omega_m = \omega_e / p$:

$$T_e = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_m} = \frac{3}{2} p \left[ \lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$$

This is the **magnet torque** plus the **reluctance torque**.

**Special case (1 mark):** For a surface-mounted PMSM, $L_d = L_q$, so:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \lambda_m \, i_q = K_t \, i_q$$

Hence $T_e \propto i_q$, just like a separately-excited DC motor.

---

### **Question B — (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a self-controlled permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drive. What are the advantages of self-control over separate-excitation mode? (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss in detail the sensorless method of rotor position estimation in a self-controlled PMSM drive. What are its limitations? (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Use the diagram from Section 4.1. The functional blocks are:
- Reference speed input → Speed controller (PI) → $i_q^*$ reference.
- Clarke and Park transformations convert $i_a, i_b, i_c$ to $i_d, i_q$ in rotor reference frame.
- Current controllers produce $V_d, V_q$ references.
- Inverse Park transform → 3-phase $V_a, V_b, V_c$ references → SVPWM generator → Gate pulses → Inverter → PMSM.
- Encoder feedback provides rotor angle $\theta_e$ for Park transformations.

**Working (2 marks):** The PMSM is fed from a current-controlled VSI, where the inverter frequency is *not* set independently — it is computed as $f = P N / 120$ using the rotor angle measured by the encoder. The stator field is always 90° ahead of the rotor field, producing maximum torque per ampere.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. No pull-out torque — infinite theoretical stability margin.
2. No hunting — oscillations are damped within one commutation cycle.
3. Wide speed range — from standstill to field-weakening region.
4. High dynamic response — current and torque controlled within sub-millisecond bandwidth.
5. Power factor is unity (or controllable) — leading to inverter-friendly operation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Back-EMF method (3 marks):**
- At any non-zero speed, the rotating rotor magnets induce a back-EMF in each stator phase with magnitude $e = k_e \omega_m \sin(\theta_e)$ (sinusoidal PMSM) or trapezoidal (BLDC).
- The zero-crossings of the un-energized phase's back-EMF give the commutation instants, which are 30° offset from the actual commutation point (a fixed phase shift is applied).
- A comparator detects the zero crossing, and a PLL/observer reconstructs the rotor angle.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Zero-speed startup problem** — back-EMF is zero at standstill, so the drive cannot self-start. A pre-positioning pulse or open-loop I-f ramp is required.
2. **Low-speed inaccuracy** — back-EMF is small at low speed, signal-to-noise ratio is poor.
3. **Computation delay** — digital filters and observers add latency, limiting maximum speed.

**Advanced observer methods (2 marks):**
- **Sliding-mode observer (SMO):** Uses current-error sliding surface to estimate back-EMF.
- **Model Reference Adaptive System (MRAS):** Reference and adjustable models of the motor estimate rotor flux.
- **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF):** Optimal state estimation in presence of measurement noise.
- These methods are used in modern EV drives (e.g., Toyota Prius inverter) for full-speed-range sensorless operation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition**: Self-control = stator frequency *slaved* to rotor speed via position feedback.
- [x] **Master equation**: $f = \dfrac{P N}{120}$ Hz — must hold at *every instant*.
- [x] **Angular form**: $\omega_e = p \cdot \omega_m$ — pole pairs link electrical to mechanical.
- [x] **Slip** $s = 0$ — pure synchronous behaviour.
- [x] **No pull-out, no hunting** — these are the *defining* advantages.
- [x] **Six-step commutation** — every 60° electrical, a new phase pair is energised.
- [x] **Position sensors** — Hall effect, optical encoder, resolver. Sensorless methods use back-EMF zero-crossings or observers.
- [x] **PMSM torque**: $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} p \left[ \lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$
- [x] **For SPM**: $T_e = K_t i_q$ — *identical* to a DC motor.
- [x] **For IPM**: Reluctance term $(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q$ adds *field-weakening* capability.
- [x] **Block diagram must show closed-loop feedback** from rotor to position sensor to decoder to gate driver to inverter.
- [x] **V/f control is *not* self-control** — V/f is open-loop, self-control is closed-loop.
- [x] **Sensorless self-control cannot start from zero speed** — needs I-f open-loop ramp.
- [x] **Industry usage**: EV traction (PMSM), drone propulsion (BLDC), CNC spindles, robotics.
- [x] **KTU key phrase to memorize**: *"Self-control eliminates the pull-out problem and provides infinite stability margin."*

> [!TIP] **Final Exam Tip**
> In the 14-mark question, if you **draw the block diagram first, label all six blocks, write the equation $f = PN/120$, and then add a short paragraph on advantages**, you will secure **at least 10/14** even if you are unsure of the torque derivation. The block diagram carries the highest weight in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor-Less Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensor-Less Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensor-less control** of a special electrical machine refers to a class of vector or scalar control strategies that estimate the rotor position, rotor speed, and sometimes the flux linkage *without* the use of physical shaft-mounted electro-mechanical sensors such as incremental encoders, resolvers, Hall-effect sensors, or absolute position transducers. The estimation is performed entirely in software/firmware using measurable terminal electrical quantities — typically phase voltages and phase currents — combined with the machine's mathematical model and parameter estimates.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, sensor-less control is studied specifically for **Brushless DC (BLDC) motors, Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM), and Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM)**. The position of the rotor flux (for PMSM) or the rotor tooth alignment (for SRM) is reconstructed by observing the electrical consequences of the mechanical state, namely the **Back-EMF**, **phase inductance variation**, or **high-frequency response signatures**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must clearly differentiate between *back-EMF sensing* (zero and low-speed limitation), *flux-linkage/observer-based methods* (medium-to-high speed), and *high-frequency signal injection* (zero to low-speed including standstill). The terminology "sensorless" specifically refers to the **absence of a mechanical shaft position sensor**, NOT the absence of current/voltage sensors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blind Sailor

Imagine a sailor sitting inside a sealed, opaque submarine (the machine) with no porthole. He cannot see where the submarine is pointing (rotor position), but he has a compass inside the cabin and a log book that records the *current drawn by the propellers* and the *voltage applied*. From these two measurements, and from knowing exactly *how the submarine is built* (its mathematical model), he can deduce the heading. If the propeller spins faster, the resistance to flow drops, changing the current; he can interpret this current signature to find the angle.

**The sailor = the digital controller (DSP/FPGA/Microcontroller)**
**The compass readings = measured phase currents and voltages**
**The submarine's blueprint = the machine's mathematical model (inductance vs. angle)**
**The inferred heading = estimated rotor position $\hat{\theta}_r$**

Just as the sailor's accuracy depends on how well he knows the submarine's blueprint, sensorless accuracy depends on the precision of machine parameters ($L_d$, $L_q$, $\psi_f$, $R_s$).

### 1.3 Why Sensorless Control — Engineering Motivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why remove a sensor that works?**
> 1. **Cost reduction** — Encoders add 15–30% to drive cost.
> 2. **Reliability** — Sensors fail in harsh environments (high temperature, vibration, dirt, oil).
> 3. **Size and weight** — Critical in aerospace and robotics.
> 4. **Cable elimination** — Fewer wires, lower EMI.
> 5. **Maintenance** — No mechanical wear on bearings (encoders load the shaft).

### 1.4 Classification of Sensorless Methods (KTU High-Yield Overview)

| Category | Speed Range | Operating Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Back-EMF based** | $\geq 5\text{–}10\%$ of rated speed | Detects induced EMF proportional to speed | BLDC, trapezoidal PMSM |
| **Third-harmonic component** | $\geq 5\%$ of rated speed | Extracts 3rd harmonic from back-EMF | Trapezoidal BLDC |
| **Flux linkage / Observer based** | Medium to high speed | Integrates voltage, subtracts $iR$ drop | PMSM (surface & interior) |
| **Sliding Mode Observer (SMO)** | Medium to high speed | Uses discontinuous switching function | PMSM, IPM |
| **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** | Wide range | Stochastic state estimation, noise-robust | Servo drives, robotics |
| **Model Reference Adaptive System (MRAS)** | Medium to high speed | Reference model vs. adjustable model comparison | PMSM, induction motor |
| **High-Frequency Signal Injection (HFSI)** | Zero & low speed | Saliency tracking via HF voltage injection | IPMSM, SynRM (salient machines) |

The terms in **bold** are examiner-magnet words — use them verbatim in KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Back-EMF Based Sensorless Control of BLDC Motors

A BLDC motor has trapezoidal back-EMF. The back-EMF in each phase is directly proportional to rotor speed and the flux linking that phase:

$$e = k_e \cdot \omega_m \cdot f(\theta_r)$$

where $f(\theta_r)$ is the trapezoidal shape function (120° flat-top, 60° commutation zone).

**Operating logic (six-step commutation):**
1. At any instant, two phases conduct (e.g., A-B), one phase is floating.
2. The floating phase carries the back-EMF of that phase.
3. By sensing the **neutral point voltage** $V_n$ and the floating phase terminal voltage $V_x$, the back-EMF zero-crossing is detected:

$$e_x = V_x - V_n$$

4. A $30°$ phase advance is added in software to determine the commutation instant.

**Limitation:** At zero or very low speed, $\omega_m \to 0 \Rightarrow e \to 0$. The zero-crossing becomes undetectable. This is the **fundamental problem** of back-EMF methods.

### 2.2 Third-Harmonic Back-EMF Detection

The trapezoidal back-EMF contains a strong **3rd harmonic component** (at $3\omega_e$ in electrical terms). The third harmonic is independent of the commutation logic and persists at *lower* speeds than the fundamental zero-crossing. The third-harmonic component is extracted as:

$$e_3 = \frac{1}{3}(e_a + e_b + e_c)$$

Its zero-crossings occur at exact $60°$ intervals, corresponding to the desired commutation points.

### 2.3 Observer-Based Sensorless Control of PMSM

The PMSM voltage equation in the stationary $\alpha$-$\beta$ frame is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v_\alpha \\ v_\beta \end{bmatrix} = R_s \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} + \frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix} \psi_\alpha \\ \psi_\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

with stator flux linkages:
$$
\psi_\alpha = L_s \cdot i_\alpha + \psi_f \cos(\theta_r) \\
\psi_\beta = L_s \cdot i_\beta + \psi_f \sin(\theta_r)
$$

The rotor position is recovered from the **flux angle**:

$$\hat{\theta}_r = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\psi_\beta - L_s i_\beta}{\psi_\alpha - L_s i_\alpha}\right)$$

### 2.4 Sliding Mode Observer (SMO)

The SMO uses a switching function $\text{sign}(\cdot)$ to force the estimated current error to slide along a predefined manifold. The observed back-EMF is filtered through a low-pass filter to extract the fundamental component:

$$\hat{e}_\alpha = k \cdot \text{sign}(\tilde{i}_\alpha) \\
\hat{e}_\beta = k \cdot \text{sign}(\tilde{i}_\beta)$$

where $\tilde{i} = \hat{i} - i$ is the current estimation error and $k$ is the sliding gain. The rotor position is then:

$$\hat{\theta}_r = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\hat{e}_\beta}{\hat{e}_\alpha}\right)$$

### 2.5 High-Frequency Signal Injection (HFSI) — Saliency-Based

For **interior PMSM (IPMSM)** or **synchronous reluctance motors (SynRM)**, the rotor has geometric saliency: $L_d \neq L_q$. A high-frequency rotating voltage vector is superimposed on the fundamental excitation:

$$v_{hf} = V_{hf} \cos(\omega_{hf} t)$$

The resulting high-frequency current contains a **negative-sequence component** at $-\omega_{hf}$, which is modulated by the rotor saliency. The position error is extracted via signal demodulation:

$$\varepsilon = \text{LPF}\left(i_{hf} \cdot e^{-j\omega_{hf} t}\right)$$

Driven through a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)**, $\varepsilon \to 0$ yields $\hat{\theta}_r$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** HFSI is the *only* method that works at **standstill and zero speed** for salient machines. The examiner loves to ask: *"Why can't back-EMF method work at zero speed?"* — Answer: because $e = k_e \omega_m$, and at $\omega_m = 0$ the signal is zero.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Valid Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e = k_e \omega_m f(\theta_r)$ | Back-EMF in a phase | Trapezoidal BLDC; $k_e$ in V·s/rad |
| 2 | $e_x = V_x - V_n$ | Floating phase back-EMF sensing | Six-step BLDC commutation |
| 3 | $e_3 = \frac{1}{3}(e_a + e_b + e_c)$ | Third-harmonic back-EMF | Works at lower speed than fundamental ZCD |
| 4 | $\hat{\theta}_r = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\psi_\beta - L_s i_\beta}{\psi_\alpha - L_s i_\alpha}\right)$ | Flux-angle position estimation | PMSM, observer-based |
| 5 | $\hat{\theta}_r = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\hat{e}_\beta}{\hat{e}_\alpha}\right)$ | SMO-based position | PMSM medium-to-high speed |
| 6 | $v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \dfrac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$ | IPMSM $d$-axis voltage (salient) | Used in HFSI decoupling |
| 7 | $v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \dfrac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \psi_f$ | IPMSM $q$-axis voltage | Back-EMF term at high speed |
| 8 | $\varepsilon \approx \Delta L \sin(2\tilde{\theta})$ | Position error in HFSI | $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta$; $\Delta L = \frac{L_d - L_q}{2}$ |
| 9 | $T_{delay} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_c} \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_c}{\omega_{LPF}}\right)$ | Observer phase delay compensation | $\omega_c$ = electrical speed, $\omega_{LPF}$ = filter cut-off |
| 10 | $\tau_{e} = \dfrac{3}{2}\dfrac{P}{2}\bigl[\psi_f i_q + (L_d - L_q)i_d i_q\bigr]$ | IPMSM electromagnetic torque | $\frac{P}{2}$ = pole pairs |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, all `|` absolute-value and condition separators are written as `\vert` or commas to preserve the markdown table structure. Do **not** use the vertical bar `|` inside table cells.

### 2.7 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Electric vehicles (EVs):** PMSM traction motors use sensorless control below ~5% speed for creep torque, then transition to back-EMF observer at higher speed. Tesla Model 3 rear motor uses resolver, but many Chinese OEMs use sensorless IPMSM drives.
- **HVAC compressors:** Sensorless BLDC in refrigerators — harsh refrigerant environment kills Hall sensors; back-EMF method is industry standard.
- **Aerospace actuators:** EMA (Electro-Mechanical Actuators) use sensorless PMSM for fault tolerance — no sensor means no single point of failure.
- **Industrial pumps & fans:** Sensorless induction/PMSM drives dominate the sub-1 kW segment for cost reasons.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rotor Position from Stator Flux Linkage (PMSM)

**Given:** The $\alpha$-$\beta$ stationary frame voltage equations of a surface-mounted PMSM.

**Step 1: Write the stator voltage equation in the $\alpha$-$\beta$ frame.**

$$
v_\alpha = R_s i_\alpha + \frac{d\psi_\alpha}{dt} \\
v_\beta = R_s i_\beta + \frac{d\psi_\beta}{dt}
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to express the flux derivative.**

$$
\frac{d\psi_\alpha}{dt} = v_\alpha - R_s i_\alpha \\
\frac{d\psi_\beta}{dt} = v_\beta - R_s i_\beta
$$

**Step 3: Integrate to obtain flux linkage.**

$$
\psi_\alpha = \int_0^t (v_\alpha - R_s i_\alpha) \, d\tau + \psi_{\alpha 0} \\
\psi_\beta = \int_0^t (v_\beta - R_s i_\beta) \, d\tau + \psi_{\beta 0}
$$

The initial flux $\psi_{\alpha 0}, \psi_{\beta 0}$ is determined by a pre-positioning routine (e.g., exciting $d$-axis with a known current vector for a short time).

**Step 4: Subtract the leakage/inductive contribution of the stator current.**

The stator flux has two components: the field flux from the rotor magnets plus the armature reaction.

$$
\psi_\alpha = L_s i_\alpha + \psi_f \cos(\theta_r) \\
\psi_\beta = L_s i_\beta + \psi_f \sin(\theta_r)
$$

**Step 5: Isolate the rotor magnet flux contribution.**

$$
\psi_f \cos(\theta_r) = \psi_\alpha - L_s i_\alpha \\
\psi_f \sin(\theta_r) = \psi_\beta - L_s i_\beta
$$

**Step 6: Form the ratio to extract $\theta_r$.**

Since both sides are scaled by $\psi_f$ (assumed non-zero and known), we can take $\tan^{-1}$:

$$
\tan(\theta_r) = \frac{\psi_\beta - L_s i_\beta}{\psi_\alpha - L_s i_\alpha}
$$

**Step 7: Final rotor position estimate.**

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{\theta}_r = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\psi_\beta - L_s i_\beta}{\psi_\alpha - L_s i_\alpha}\right)\;}
$$

**Why this derivation matters (KTU context):** The integrated flux drifts due to the pure integrator. In practice, a **low-pass filter with a phase-lag compensator** is used, or a **Luenberger observer** is built around this model. The KTU examiner may ask why pure integration fails — answer: *DC offset in measurements accumulates without bound; integrator drift.*

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Position Error Extraction in HFSI (Salient Machine)

**Given:** A salient rotor with $L_d \neq L_q$. A high-frequency voltage is injected in the estimated $d^\prime$-$q^\prime$ frame.

**Step 1: HF machine model.** At high frequency, $R_s$ and $\omega L$ back-EMF terms are negligible compared to $j\omega_{hf} L$. The HF voltage-current relationship in the rotor $d$-$q$ frame is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v_{dh} \\ v_{qh} \end{bmatrix} = j\omega_{hf} \begin{bmatrix} L_d & 0 \\ 0 & L_q \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_{dh} \\ i_{qh} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Express the inductance matrix in the *estimated* frame.** If the estimated angle $\hat{\theta}$ differs from the true angle $\theta$ by $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta$, then a rotation matrix $R(\tilde{\theta})$ relates the true frame to the estimated frame.

**Step 3: Inject a HF voltage in the $d^\prime$ axis (pulsating injection) or in the stationary $\alpha$-$\beta$ frame (rotating injection).** Consider rotating injection in $\alpha$-$\beta$:

$$
v_{hf,\alpha} = V_{hf} \cos(\omega_{hf} t) \\
v_{hf,\beta} = V_{hf} \sin(\omega_{hf} t)
$$

**Step 4: The resulting HF current in the stationary frame contains a negative-sequence component:**

$$
i_{hf,\alpha\beta} = I_{p} e^{j\omega_{hf} t} + I_{n} e^{-j(\omega_{hf} t - 2\theta)}
$$

where $I_p$ (positive sequence) and $I_n$ (negative sequence) depend on $L_d$ and $L_q$.

**Step 5: Demodulate by multiplying with $e^{-j\omega_{hf} t}$ and low-pass filtering:**

$$
\varepsilon = \text{LPF}\!\left(i_{hf} e^{-j\omega_{hf} t}\right) = I_n e^{j(2\theta - 2\hat{\theta})}
$$

**Step 6: Linearize for small errors.** For $\tilde{\theta}$ small, $\sin(2\tilde{\theta}) \approx 2\tilde{\theta}$. The imaginary part of $\varepsilon$ gives the error:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon \approx \Delta L \sin\!\left(2\tilde{\theta}\right) \;\approx\; 2\Delta L\, \tilde{\theta}\;}
$$

where $\Delta L = \dfrac{L_d - L_q}{2}$. The PLL drives $\varepsilon \to 0$, forcing $\hat{\theta} \to \theta$.

**Why this derivation matters:** It is the *only* sensorless method that works at standstill for salient machines. The KTU paper regularly asks for the **functional form of the position error** in HFSI.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method vs. Operating Regime

| Method | Standstill | Low Speed | Medium Speed | High Speed | Saliency Required? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Back-EMF (fundamental) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | No |
| Back-EMF (3rd harmonic) | ✗ | Marginal | ✓ | ✓ | No |
| Flux linkage observer | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | No |
| Sliding Mode Observer (SMO) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | No |
| EKF | ✗ | Limited | ✓ | ✓ | No |
| MRAS | ✗ | Limited | ✓ | ✓ | No |
| **HFSI (pulsating)** | ✓ | ✓ | Limited | Marginal | **Yes (mandatory)** |
| **HFSI (rotating)** | ✓ | ✓ | Limited | Marginal | **Yes (mandatory)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trivia / Frequently Asked:** *"Can a Surface PMSM (SPMSM) be started sensorlessly from standstill using HFSI?"* — **No**, because $L_d = L_q$ for SPMSM; there is no saliency to track. For SPMSM standstill, *pulse-injection with magnetic saturation saliency* may be used, but it is non-trivial.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define sensorless control of a brushless DC motor. Why is it not feasible to start a BLDC motor from standstill using back-EMF sensing?*

**Model Answer:**

Sensorless control of a BLDC motor is a technique in which the rotor position information required for electronic commutation is obtained by *indirectly* estimating it from the machine's terminal electrical quantities (phase voltages and currents) rather than from physical position sensors (Hall sensors, encoders).

Back-EMF sensing relies on the equation $e = k_e \omega_m f(\theta_r)$. At standstill, $\omega_m = 0$, which makes the back-EMF identically zero. Consequently, no zero-crossing can be detected and the commutation instants cannot be generated. Hence, starting from standstill using back-EMF is infeasible. **Common start-up techniques** include: (i) open-loop V/f ramping (I-f start), (ii) pre-positioning pulse, and (iii) high-frequency signal injection (for salient machines).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of high-frequency signal injection (HFSI) in sensorless control? Why is it applicable to IPMSM but not SPMSM?*

**Model Answer:**

High-frequency signal injection (HFSI) is a sensorless technique that superimposes a high-frequency voltage on the fundamental excitation and analyzes the resulting HF current to track the rotor saliency. By demodulating the negative-sequence HF current and driving the error through a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)**, the rotor position is estimated.

HFSI requires the machine to possess **rotor saliency** ($L_d \neq L_q$). In an **IPMSM**, the magnets are buried inside the rotor, causing $L_d < L_q$ (or vice versa), which provides a strong geometric saliency. In an **SPMSM**, the magnets are mounted on the surface, and the effective air-gap is uniform, leading to $L_d \approx L_q$. With negligible saliency, the HFSI error signal vanishes, and the method fails. Hence, HFSI is applicable to IPMSM but not to SPMSM.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the back-EMF based sensorless control scheme of a three-phase BLDC motor. Derive the expression for the floating phase back-EMF detection and discuss the commutation logic. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the third-harmonic back-EMF based sensorless scheme. How does it extend the low-speed operating range compared to fundamental back-EMF sensing? (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A BLDC motor requires electronic commutation in synchronism with the rotor position. Sensorless control eliminates the Hall sensor by sensing the back-EMF generated in the *floating* (un-energised) phase.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
[Reference Section 4.2 above — student should reproduce the diagram with: Three-Phase Inverter → BLDC Motor → Terminal Voltage Sensing → Neutral Point → Subtractor → Comparator → 30° Phase Advance → Commutation Logic → Gate Signals to Inverter.]

**Derivation of Floating Phase Back-EMF (3 marks):**
Consider phase A conducting positive and phase B conducting negative (phase C is floating). The inverter applies $+V_{dc}/2$ to phase A and $-V_{dc}/2$ to phase B (with respect to the DC bus mid-point). The neutral point voltage $V_n$ is:

$$V_n = \frac{V_a + V_b + V_c}{3}$$

With $V_a = V_{dc}/2$, $V_b = -V_{dc}/2$, and $V_c = e_c$ (floating), the floating phase terminal voltage is measured w.r.t. the DC bus negative:

$$V_c = e_c + V_n$$

The detected back-EMF is:

$$e_c = V_c - V_n = V_c - \frac{V_a + V_b + V_c}{3}$$

When $e_c$ crosses zero, the commutation point is 30° electrical ahead (in the BLDC trapezoidal waveform, the ZCD occurs at 30° before the next commutation). The controller adds the appropriate phase advance to trigger the next switching.

**Commutation Logic (1 mark):**
Six zero-crossings per electrical revolution → six commutation events → standard six-step sequence (A-B, A-C, B-C, B-A, C-A, C-B).

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Third-Harmonic Principle (2 marks):**
The trapezoidal back-EMF of a BLDC motor is mathematically a sum of odd harmonics. The 3rd harmonic is dominant and is the same in all three phases. The total back-EMF zero-sequence is:

$$e_3 = \frac{1}{3}(e_a + e_b + e_c)$$

Since $e_a + e_b + e_c = 3 e_3$, the line-to-line voltages cannot extract $e_3$, but if the **neutral point is accessible**, $e_3$ is measured directly across the phase and the neutral.

**Zero-Crossings (2 marks):**
$e_3$ has zero-crossings every 60° electrical, which align exactly with the BLDC commutation points. The commutation instant is therefore *at* the zero-crossing, eliminating the 30° phase advance required in the fundamental method.

**Extension of Low-Speed Range (3 marks):**
The amplitude of the 3rd harmonic is approximately $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of the fundamental. Therefore, it falls below the detection threshold at a speed roughly 1/3 of the fundamental method's threshold. The minimum operating speed is *reduced* compared to fundamental back-EMF sensing — typically from ~10% rated speed (fundamental) to ~3–5% rated speed (third harmonic). However, neither method works at standstill; an open-loop I-f ramp start is still needed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the Sliding Mode Observer (SMO) based sensorless control of a PMSM. Derive the rotor position estimation expression. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the High-Frequency Signal Injection (HFSI) method for sensorless control of an IPMSM. Show that the demodulated error signal is proportional to $\sin(2\tilde{\theta})$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**SMO Structure (2 marks):**
The Sliding Mode Observer replaces the actual PMSM current with an estimated current, using a discontinuous control function. The observer is defined as:

$$
\frac{d\hat{i}_\alpha}{dt} = -\frac{R_s}{L_s}\hat{i}_\alpha + \frac{1}{L_s}\bigl(v_\alpha - z_\alpha\bigr) \\
\frac{d\hat{i}_\beta}{dt} = -\frac{R_s}{L_s}\hat{i}_\beta + \frac{1}{L_s}\bigl(v_\beta - z_\beta\bigr)
$$

where $z_\alpha, z_\beta$ are the sliding-mode switching functions:

$$z_\alpha = k \cdot \text{sign}(\hat{i}_\alpha - i_\alpha) \\
z_\beta = k \cdot \text{sign}(\hat{i}_\beta - i_\beta)$$

**Error Dynamics and Sliding Manifold (2 marks):**
Define current error $\tilde{i}_\alpha = \hat{i}_\alpha - i_\alpha$. Subtracting the actual PMSM equation from the observer equation:

$$\frac{d\tilde{i}_\alpha}{dt} = -\frac{R_s}{L_s}\tilde{i}_\alpha + \frac{1}{L_s}\bigl(e_\alpha - z_\alpha\bigr)$$

On the sliding manifold, $\tilde{i}_\alpha = 0$ and $\dot{\tilde{i}}_\alpha = 0$, hence the equivalent control gives $z_\alpha = e_\alpha$. So the switching function estimates the back-EMF.

**Extraction of Position (2 marks):**
A low-pass filter removes the high-frequency chattering:

$$\hat{e}_\alpha = \frac{\omega_{LPF}}{s + \omega_{LPF}} z_\alpha \\
\hat{e}_\beta = \frac{\omega_{LPF}}{s + \omega_{LPF}} z_\beta$$

The rotor position is:

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\theta}_r = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\hat{e}_\beta}{\hat{e}_\alpha}\right)\;}$$

**Stability Note (1 mark):** The sliding gain $k$ must satisfy $k > \max(\vert e_\alpha \vert, \vert e_\beta \vert)$ for sliding mode to be enforced.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Concept (1 mark):**
HFSI superimposes a high-frequency rotating or pulsating voltage on the fundamental excitation. The saliency of the IPMSM ($L_d \neq L_q$) modulates the resulting HF current. The modulation carries the rotor position information.

**HF Machine Model (2 marks):** [Reference Derivation 2, Steps 1–3 in Section 3.2.]

The injected HF voltage in the stationary frame is $v_{hf} = V_{hf} e^{j\omega_{hf} t}$. Solving the HF circuit equation (neglecting $R_s$ and back-EMF since $\omega_{hf} \gg \omega_e$):

$$i_{hf} = V_{hf} \left[\frac{1}{j\omega_{hf} L_d} \cos^2\theta + \frac{1}{j\omega_{hf} L_q}\sin^2\theta\right] e^{j\omega_{hf} t} + \text{negative sequence term}$$

**Demodulation (2 marks):**
Multiplying $i_{hf}$ by $e^{-j\omega_{hf} t}$ and low-pass filtering:

$$\varepsilon = \text{LPF}(i_{hf} e^{-j\omega_{hf} t}) = I_{p} + I_n e^{j(2\theta - 2\hat{\theta})}$$

The position-dependent term contains $2(\theta - \hat{\theta}) = -2\tilde{\theta}$.

**Error Signal (2 marks):**
Separating the imaginary part and substituting $I_n \propto \Delta L = (L_d - L_q)/2$:

$$\varepsilon \approx \Delta L \sin(2\tilde{\theta})$$

For small $\tilde{\theta}$: $\varepsilon \approx 2\Delta L \cdot \tilde{\theta}$. The PLL drives $\varepsilon \to 0$, giving $\hat{\theta} \to \theta$. This confirms the proportionality to $\sin(2\tilde{\theta})$ as required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT write the rotor position formula with a positive sign for SMO** — the convention is $\hat{\theta} = -\tan^{-1}(\hat{e}_\beta / \hat{e}_\alpha)$ because the back-EMF vector *leads* the rotor magnet axis by 90° in the $\alpha$-$\beta$ plane. Wrong sign → lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Always state the speed range** for any sensorless method you mention. "Back-EMF is used for sensorless control" without "above 5–10% rated speed" is incomplete.
> 3. **In HFSI, never write $L_d = L_q$** as a given; it is precisely the violation of this equality that the method exploits. Examiners penalize confused definitions.
> 4. **Block diagram orientation:** When drawing the SMO block, place the current error $\tilde{i}$ at the input of the $\text{sign}(\cdot)$ block, not at the output.
> 5. **Do not confuse observer-based methods with HFSI.** Observers use *fundamental excitation* only; HFSI uses an *additional* high-frequency signal.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Sensorless control** = estimation of rotor position/speed using *only* terminal voltages and currents.
- ✅ **Back-EMF method** = works for BLDC, fails at standstill because $e = k_e \omega_m$ goes to zero.
- ✅ **Third-harmonic method** = works at lower speed than fundamental; zero-crossing *coincides* with commutation point (no 30° phase advance).
- ✅ **Flux-linkage / Observer methods** for PMSM = integrate $v - Ri$, subtract $L_s i$, take $\tan^{-1}$ to get $\theta_r$.
- ✅ **Sliding Mode Observer (SMO)** uses $\text{sign}(\cdot)$ switching → equivalent control estimates back-EMF → position is $-\tan^{-1}(\hat{e}_\beta / \hat{e}_\alpha)$.
- ✅ **EKF** = stochastic observer; handles noise well; computationally heavy.
- ✅ **MRAS** = compares reference model and adaptive model; error drives a PI that adapts rotor speed.
- ✅ **HFSI** = only method that works at standstill; **requires saliency** ($L_d \neq L_q$); suitable for IPMSM and SynRM, **not** SPMSM.
- ✅ **Error signal in HFSI** $\propto \sin(2\tilde{\theta})$ → driven to zero by PLL.
- ✅ **Saliency-based methods** rely on the **negative-sequence HF current** rotating in the opposite direction to the injection.
- ✅ **Sensorless PMSM speed range:** HFSI: 0–~5%; Observer: ~5–100% rated speed. A *blending algorithm* is used in the transition region.
- ✅ **SRM sensorless:** uses phase inductance slope $dL/d\theta$ or mutual coupling — fundamentally different from PMSM; the rotor has no magnets.
- ✅ **Open-loop I-f start** = pre-positioning pulse followed by V/f ramp; industry-standard method to start a BLDC from standstill before back-EMF becomes detectable.
- ✅ **Key exam formulas to memorize:** $\hat{\theta} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\psi_\beta - L_s i_\beta}{\psi_\alpha - L_s i_\alpha}\right)$, $\varepsilon = \Delta L \sin(2\tilde{\theta})$, $T_e = \frac{3P}{4}\left[\psi_f i_q + (L_d - L_q)i_d i_q\right]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machine – PMSM Context)

A **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** is a rotating electrical machine in which the field winding on the rotor is replaced by high-energy permanent magnets (typically **Neodymium–Iron–Boron (NdFeB)** or **Samarium–Cobalt (SmCo)**) and the stator carries a balanced three-phase sinusoidal AC winding. The rotor magnet flux and the stator rotating field are locked in synchronism — i.e., they rotate at the **synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$, where $f$ is the supply frequency and $P$ is the number of poles. The electromagnetic torque is produced by the interaction between the permanent magnet rotor field and the rotating stator MMF, with no rotor copper losses because there are no rotor conductors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Applications of PMSM, PMBLDC, and other machines from Module 3 are tested both as direct 3-mark recall questions and as 14-mark analytical design questions in ESE. Always remember: **PMSM ⇒ sinusoidal back-EMF**; **PMBLDC ⇒ trapezoidal back-EMF**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The "Magnetic Bar on a Ferris Wheel"

Picture a **bar magnet** mounted on the seat of a slowly rotating Ferris wheel (the rotor). Around the rim of the wheel are six (or more) electromagnets, each connected to a 3-phase AC supply. As the AC supply cycles, the electromagnets create a **rotating north pole** that "chases" the bar magnet's permanent north pole. The bar magnet is always **locked** to the moving field — they move together like two hands of the same clock. This is exactly how a PMSM behaves: the rotor (bar magnet) cannot slip, and the entire system runs at one fixed synchronous speed determined only by the supply frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Unlike an induction motor, a PMSM does **not** suffer from slip, does **not** have rotor copper losses, and therefore delivers **higher efficiency (often 95–98 %)** and **superior power density**. This single fact is the reason PMSMs dominate modern high-performance applications.

### 1.3 Classification of PMSM Relevant to Applications

PMSMs are broadly divided into two rotor topologies, each of which dictates the application domain:

- **Surface-Mounted PMSM (SPM):** Magnets are glued on the outer surface of the rotor core. The rotor has effectively a **uniform air gap** and exhibits **non-salient** behaviour ($L_d = L_q$). Best suited for **high-speed, low-inductance** applications (spindle drives, traction).
- **Interior PMSM (IPM):** Magnets are buried inside rotor slots, giving **salient** behaviour ($L_d < L_q$). The saliency produces an additional **reluctance torque** in addition to the magnet torque, making IPM ideal for **constant-power flux-weakening operation** (electric vehicles, industrial servo drives).

| Feature | SPM (Surface-Mount) | IPM (Interior-Mount) |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous inductance | $L_d = L_q$ | $L_d < L_q$ |
| Reluctance torque | Zero | Significant (utilised) |
| Flux weakening | Limited | Excellent (wide CPSR) |
| Typical applications | CNC spindles, pumps | EV traction, robotics |
| Mechanical robustness | Moderate (glue at high speed) | Very high (magnets embedded) |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-Mark Questions:** Whenever a numerical mentions a wide constant-power speed range (e.g., 1:3 to 1:5), the expected answer is **IPM (Interior PMSM)** with reluctance torque exploitation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle – Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Three-phase balanced stator winding** is excited by a sinusoidal voltage source of frequency $f$ (either from the grid or from a PWM inverter).
2. The three-phase currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ produce a **rotating magnetic field** (RMF) in the air gap, rotating at synchronous angular velocity $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ rad/s.
3. The rotor carries **pre-magnetised permanent magnets** producing a constant flux $\phi_f$ (Webers).
4. The stator field **pulls the rotor field**; the rotor locks onto the RMF and rotates at exactly the same speed $\omega_s$.
5. The mechanical electromagnetic torque $T_e$ developed is the cross product of stator and rotor fields and is governed by the **synchronous machine torque equation** derived from the $d$–$q$ axis model.
6. The relationship between electrical and mechanical angles is $\theta_e = \dfrac{P}{2}\theta_m$, meaning one mechanical revolution corresponds to $P/2$ electrical cycles.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains the **complete mathematical toolkit** required to solve any KTU Module 3 application-oriented numerical problem. **Memorise all the units carefully** — valuation scripts deduct marks for wrong units.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units (SI) | Application Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ | rpm | Speed selection |
| 2 | Synchronous angular velocity | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | Mechanical design |
| 3 | Per-phase RMS back-EMF | $E_{ph} = 4.44\,f\,N_{ph}\,k_w\,\phi_f$ | V | EMF calculation |
| 4 | Line-to-line EMF | $E_L = \sqrt{3}\,E_{ph}$ | V | Three-phase systems |
| 5 | Electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\dfrac{P}{2}\big[\psi_f I_q + (L_d - L_q)I_d I_q\big]$ | N·m | Torque/power sizing |
| 6 | Reluctance (saliency) torque | $T_{rel} = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\dfrac{P}{2}(L_d - L_q) I_d I_q$ | N·m | IPM drives |
| 7 | Magnet (alignment) torque | $T_{mag} = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\dfrac{P}{2}\psi_f I_q$ | N·m | SPM drives |
| 8 | Mechanical output power | $P_{out} = T_e \,\omega_m$ | W | Sizing the drive |
| 9 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = T_e \,\omega_s$ | W | Power balance |
| 10 | Power factor angle | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{V_{ph}\cos\delta - E_{ph}}{X_s I_{ph}}$ | — | Performance eval. |
| 11 | Voltage equation (d-axis) | $V_d = R_s I_d - \omega_e L_q I_q$ | V | Vector control |
| 12 | Voltage equation (q-axis) | $V_q = R_s I_q + \omega_e L_d I_d + \omega_e \psi_f$ | V | Vector control |
| 13 | Field-weakening constant-power ratio | $\text{CPSR} = \dfrac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_{base}}$ | — | EV drive design |
| 14 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\times 100\,\%$ | % | Energy assessment |

> **Notation glossary** (used in the table):
> - $N_{ph}$ = turns per phase; $k_w$ = winding factor ($\approx 0.955$ for distributed winding)
> - $\psi_f$ = permanent magnet flux linkage (Webers)
> - $I_d, I_q$ = $d$- and $q$-axis stator currents (A)
> - $L_d, L_q$ = $d$- and $q$-axis synchronous inductances (H)
> - $\delta$ = load/torque angle (electrical rad); $\omega_e$ = electrical angular velocity

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mathematical relationships above are not just textbook exercises — they directly drive **industrial design decisions**:

- **EV traction motor selection:** Engineers plug the vehicle's required $T_{peak}$ (e.g., 250 N·m) and $\omega_{max}$ (e.g., 12 000 rpm) into the torque/power equations to size the magnet grade and the inverter kVA rating.
- **Robotic arm joints:** Equation (5) shows that *both* magnet torque and reluctance torque contribute; by controlling $I_d$ and $I_q$ independently (vector control / FOC), the manufacturer can guarantee a **flat torque profile** across the speed range — vital for smooth pick-and-place motion.
- **Wind turbine generators:** When the PMSM is used as a generator, equation (12) is re-arranged to extract maximum power at variable wind speed, often using **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** algorithms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip – How the paper is set:** A 14-mark application question typically gives you numerical specifications of a load (torque, speed range, ambient temperature, duty cycle) and asks you to (a) select the motor type (SPM vs IPM), (b) compute rating using the formula sheet, and (c) suggest a suitable power electronic converter (3-phase VSI, SVM, or DTC).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Per-Phase Back-EMF of a PMSM

**Starting Principle:** The back-EMF is the rate of change of flux linkage with respect to time. For a sinusoidal air-gap flux distribution, the flux per pole $\phi$ rotates past each phase winding at frequency $f$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Express the instantaneous air-gap flux per pole.**
For a sinusoidal distribution, the peak flux per pole is $\phi_f$ (Webers). The instantaneous flux linking a single turn of a phase winding is:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_f \cos(\omega_e t)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the flux linkage of one turn.**
The flux linkage per turn is simply $\phi(t)$ (no transformer ratio since we are dealing with one conductor pair):

$$\psi_{turn}(t) = \phi_f \cos(\omega_e t)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by total number of turns per phase $N_{ph}$ and the winding factor $k_w$.**
The winding factor accounts for the distribution of the winding ($k_d$) and pitch ($k_p$); for a full-pitch, distributed winding $k_w = k_d \cdot k_p \approx 0.955$.

$$\psi_{ph}(t) = k_w \, N_{ph} \, \phi_f \cos(\omega_e t)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Faraday's Law: $e = -\dfrac{d\psi}{dt}$.**

$$e_{ph}(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[k_w N_{ph} \phi_f \cos(\omega_e t)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate using $\dfrac{d}{dt}\cos(\omega_e t) = -\omega_e \sin(\omega_e t)$.**

$$e_{ph}(t) = k_w N_{ph} \phi_f \omega_e \sin(\omega_e t)$$

**Step 6 — Convert peak to RMS using $\omega_e = 2\pi f$.**
The peak value is $E_{peak} = k_w N_{ph} \phi_f \,(2\pi f) = 2\pi f k_w N_{ph} \phi_f$.

$$E_{peak} = 2\pi f \, k_w N_{ph} \phi_f$$

The RMS value is $E_{ph} = \dfrac{E_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$.

$$E_{ph} = \frac{2\pi f k_w N_{ph} \phi_f}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}\,\pi f k_w N_{ph} \phi_f$$

**Step 7 — Recognise the standard "transformer EMF" constant.**
Since $4.44 = 2\pi/\sqrt{2}$ (this is the *form factor* of a sine wave divided by $\sqrt{2}$), we can also write:

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w \, \phi_f$$

> **Engineering logic:** The factor **4.44** is not a magic number — it is a compact combination of the form factor ($1.11 = \pi/2\sqrt{2}$) and the RMS-to-peak conversion ($1/\sqrt{2}$), capturing the entire sinusoidal assumption in one constant. For trapezoidal machines (PMBLDC) the constant changes to **4.0**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Electromagnetic Torque Equation in $d$–$q$ Reference Frame

**Starting Principle:** The instantaneous power crossing the air gap is $p_{ag}(t) = \dfrac{3}{2}\,\big[v_d i_d + v_q i_q\big]$ and the air-gap power also equals $T_e \,\omega_s$.

**Step 1 — Write the d–q axis voltage equations (rotor reference frame).**

$$v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \psi_f$$

> *Note:* The cross-coupling terms $-\omega_e L_q i_q$ and $+\omega_e L_d i_d$ are the *speed voltages* that arise because the rotating reference frame introduces an apparent motion.

**Step 2 — In steady state, the inductances' time derivatives vanish** $\left(\dfrac{di}{dt}=0\right)$ and the resistive drop $R_s i$ is often small for high-efficiency machines. Neglecting $R_s$:

$$v_d \approx -\omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q \approx \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \psi_f$$

**Step 3 — Compute the air-gap power** $p_{ag} = \dfrac{3}{2}(v_d i_d + v_q i_q)$.

Substituting the steady-state expressions:

$$p_{ag} = \frac{3}{2}\left[-\omega_e L_q i_q \cdot i_d + (\omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \psi_f)\, i_q\right]$$

**Step 4 — Expand and group terms.**

$$p_{ag} = \frac{3}{2}\,\omega_e\left[-L_q i_d i_q + L_d i_d i_q + \psi_f i_q\right]$$

$$p_{ag} = \frac{3}{2}\,\omega_e\left[(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q + \psi_f i_q\right]$$

**Step 5 — Use the identity $T_e = \dfrac{p_{ag}}{\omega_s}$ where $\omega_s = \omega_e$ in electrical rad/s and the mechanical angular velocity is $\omega_m = \dfrac{2}{P}\omega_e$. So $\omega_e = \dfrac{P}{2}\omega_m$.**

$$T_e = \frac{p_{ag}}{\omega_m} = \frac{3}{2}\,\frac{P}{2}\left[(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q + \psi_f i_q\right]$$

**Step 6 — Split the two torque components for clarity.**

$$T_{mag} = \frac{3}{2}\,\frac{P}{2}\,\psi_f i_q \quad\text{(magnet/alignment torque)}$$

$$T_{rel} = \frac{3}{2}\,\frac{P}{2}\,(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \quad\text{(reluctance/saliency torque)}$$

**Step 7 — Final compact form.**

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2}\,\frac{P}{2}\left[\psi_f i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q\right]$$

> **Engineering logic:** This is the *master torque equation*. For a **surface PMSM (SPM)**, $L_d = L_q$, so the second term vanishes — torque is purely magnet torque, controlled only by $i_q$. For an **IPM**, $L_d < L_q$, so the second term is negative when $i_d < 0$, but $i_d$ is *negative* in the MTPA region, making the product $i_d i_q$ negative and $(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q$ positive. The result is an **additional positive reluctance torque** that adds to the magnet torque — this is the secret to IPM's high power density.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Mapping Machines to Applications

The following decision matrix is a **high-yield reference** that frequently appears in KTU Part A questions:

| Application | Preferred Machine | Reason (Key Property) | Typical Rating |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electric Vehicle (EV) traction** | IPM (Interior PMSM) | High CPSR, high power density, reluctance torque | 50 – 250 kW |
| **Industrial robot joints** | PMSM + FOC | High torque-to-inertia ratio, precise position control | 0.1 – 5 kW |
| **CNC spindle drives** | SPM PMSM | Very high speed (>20 000 rpm), smooth torque | 5 – 50 kW |
| **HVAC compressors** | BLDC | High efficiency, variable speed | 0.5 – 10 kW |
| **Wind turbine generator** | Direct-drive PMSG | No gearbox, low maintenance, high efficiency | 1 – 10 MW |
| **Aerospace actuators** | PMSM (SmCo magnets) | High temperature tolerance, high reliability | 0.5 – 50 kW |
| **Medical (MRI, surgical robots)** | PMSM | Smooth, quiet, precise | 0.1 – 10 kW |
| **Ship propulsion** | Multi-phase PMSM | High torque, redundancy | 1 – 25 MW |
| **Domestic appliances (washing machine)** | BLDC (PMSM-based) | Energy efficiency standards (BEE 5-star) | 0.2 – 1 kW |
| **Data centre cooling fans** | BLDC | Variable speed, low acoustic noise | 0.05 – 0.5 kW |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram – Complete PMSM Drive System (Vector-Controlled)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Bus / Battery 540V] --> B[3-Phase VSI Inverter]
    C[Vector Control DSP] -->|PWM S1-S6| B
    B -->|3-phase sinusoidal currents| D[PMSM Stator]
    D -->|Back-EMF, position| E[Encoder / Resolver]
    E -->|sin, cos theta_e| F[Park / Clarke Transform]
    G[Current Sensors Ia, Ib] --> F
    F -->|Id, Iq| H[PI Current Controllers]
    I[Speed Reference omega_ref] --> J[PI Speed Controller]
    H -->|Vd, Vq| K[SVPWM Modulator]
    J -->|Iq_ref| H
    K --> C
    D -->|Shaft| L[Mechanical Load]
```

> **Block-level reading:** The speed command $\omega_{ref}$ is compared with measured speed, fed through a PI controller to produce $I_{q,ref}$. The current sensors and position sensor feed the Clarke-Park block, which converts 3-phase $abc$ quantities to 2-phase $dq$ (rotor-aligned) quantities. The PI current controllers generate $V_d$ and $V_q$ references, which the SVPWM block converts into six gate pulses for the 3-phase VSI. This is the **complete closed-loop vector control architecture** used in every modern PMSM drive — from a Tesla Model 3 inverter to a CNC spindle.

---

### 4.2 Application-Tier Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Special Electrical Machine Applications] --> B[High-Performance Servo]
    A --> C[Electric Mobility]
    A --> D[Renewable Energy]
    A --> E[Industrial Automation]
    A --> F[Aerospace and Defence]

    B --> B1[Robotic Arms]
    B --> B2[CNC Machines]
    B --> B3[Pick and Place]
    B --> B4[Medical Robots]

    C --> C1[2W and 3W EV]
    C --> C2[4W Passenger EV]
    C --> C3[Electric Bus]
    C --> C4[Hybrid Vehicles]

    D --> D1[Wind Turbine PMSG]
    D --> D2[Solar Pump PMSM]
    D --> D3[Micro-hydro Generator]

    E --> E1[HVAC Compressor]
    E --> E2[Conveyor Drives]
    E --> E3[Textile Spinning]
    E --> E4[Paper Mills]

    F --> F1[Fighter Jet Actuators]
    F --> F2[Drone Propulsion]
    F --> F3[Satellite Reaction Wheels]
    F --> F4[Ship Propulsion]
```

---

### 4.3 PMSM Drive Selection Logic Flowchart (For Numerical Problems)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Application Specs] --> B{Constant Power Range > 1:2?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Choose IPM]
    B -->|No| D[Choose SPM]
    C --> E{Operating Speed > 15 000 rpm?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Carbon-fibre sleeve retention]
    E -->|No| G[Standard rotor lamination]
    D --> H{Rotor Diameter < 50 mm?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Use NdFeB sintered magnets]
    H -->|No| J[Consider ferrite or bonded magnets]
    F --> K[Select FOC with SVPWM]
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[End: Final Drive Selection]
```

> **How to read this in an exam:** If a numerical problem states *“wide constant-power speed range of 1:4 is required”*, follow the `Yes` branch and conclude the answer as **IPM with reluctance torque contribution**, controlled via **FOC** (Field-Oriented Control) using SVPWM. The `F` branch is reached when the application demands speeds > 15 000 rpm (e.g., EV traction at high speed, supercharger), which mandates **carbon-fibre banding** to keep the surface magnets from flying off due to centrifugal force.

---
