The **Equivalent Circuit of a LIM** is the modified per-phase representation of the motor that incorporates correction factors — the **Transverse Edge Effect Coefficient ($C_e$)** and the **End Effect Factor ($K_p$) / Goodness Factor ($G$)** — to accurately model the asymmetric, non-uniform, and finite geometry of the machine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are dragging a wide iron sheet (the secondary) through a narrow magnetic tunnel (the primary) of a LIM.

- **Transverse Edge Effect Analogy:** Think of a long, wide sheet of metal moving through a doorway. The middle of the sheet passes through easily, but the edges of the sheet "bulge" outward because the induced eddy currents at the edges have no return path inside the sheet and must loop through the surrounding air. This creates extra losses and weakens the magnetic coupling at the edges — just like how a wide carpet going through a narrow door has its edges curl up.

- **End Effect Analogy:** Consider a long train entering a short tunnel. As the front of the train enters, the air pressure builds up (entry transient), and as the tail exits, a suction is created (exit transient). Similarly, in a LIM, the secondary enters and exits the primary, creating temporary disturbances. The entry end "pushes" the secondary forward (propulsion) but the exit end creates a small *braking* force because the trailing part of the field is weaker than the leading part.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Goodness Factor (G)** is a key dimensionless number that quantifies how well a LIM is designed. A high G means end effects are negligible (RIM-like behaviour), while a low G indicates strong end-effect losses.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

The following parameters govern the magnitude of these effects:

- **Goodness Factor, $G = \dfrac{\omega \mu_0 \sigma L^2}{\pi^2}$** — dimensionless
- **Slip, $s = \dfrac{v_s - v_r}{v_s}$** — dimensionless
- **Synchronous velocity, $v_s = 2 \tau f$** in **m/s**
- **Transverse Edge Effect Coefficient, $C_e$** — typically between **0.6 and 0.9**
- **Secondary conductivity, $\sigma$** in **S/m** (or m/Ω·mm²)
- **Air-gap permeance correction factor, $K_p$** — depends on slot/pole combination

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transverse Edge Effect — Structured Logic

**Why it occurs:**
The secondary reaction plate of a LIM has a finite width ($2a$), while the primary also has a finite width ($2c$). The induced EMF in the secondary drives currents that must close their paths. Currents in the central region can flow directly across the plate (low resistance path), but currents near the edges have to detour, increasing effective resistance and reducing magnetising effect.

**How it manifests in the equivalent circuit:**

1. The transverse edge effect is modelled by introducing a **coefficient $C_e$** that modifies the *magnetising branch* of the equivalent circuit.
2. The effective magnetising reactance is multiplied by a factor $\dfrac{1}{C_e}$, while the effective secondary resistance is also scaled.
3. The corrected goodness factor becomes $G_e = C_e \cdot G$.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

For a single-sided LIM, the transverse edge effect coefficient is given by:

$$C_e = 1 - \dfrac{\tanh(\beta a)}{\beta a \left[1 + \tanh^2(\beta a)\right]}$$

where $\beta$ is the propagation constant along the transverse direction and $a$ is the half-width of the secondary plate.

## 2.2 Longitudinal End Effect — Structured Logic

**Why it occurs:**
In a RIM, the magnetic field is established continuously around a closed circular air-gap. In a LIM, the field is "open" — it has a definite beginning (entry end) and end (exit end). As the secondary plate slides into the primary:

- The **entry end** has a building-up magnetic field (transient).
- The **exit end** has a collapsing magnetic field (transient).
- These transients induce additional eddy currents in the secondary, which oppose the change in flux (Lenz's law).

**Effect on performance:**

- The exit-end transient produces a **braking force** (negative thrust).
- The entry-end transient produces a slight **boost** in thrust but with increased losses.
- Net effect: reduced efficiency and a force imbalance that depends on slip and speed.

## 2.3 Goodness Factor and End-Effect Factor

The Goodness Factor is the single most important parameter that determines the severity of end effects:

$$G = \dfrac{\omega \mu_0 \sigma L^2 s}{\pi^2}$$

where $L$ is the length of the primary stack. The corresponding end-effect factor is often expressed as:

$$K_p = 1 - \dfrac{1 - e^{-G}}{G}$$

For **high G (G > 10):** End effects are negligible, motor behaves like a RIM.
For **low G (G < 1):** End effects dominate, efficiency drops sharply.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Synchronous Velocity | $v_s = 2 \tau f$ | m/s |
| 2 | Linear Slip | $s = (v_s - v_r) / v_s$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 3 | Goodness Factor | $G = \omega \mu_0 \sigma L^2 s / \pi^2$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Transverse Edge Coefficient | $C_e = 1 - \tanh(\beta a) / \beta a[1 + \tanh^2(\beta a)]$ | 0.6 to 0.9 |
| 5 | Effective Goodness | $G_e = C_e \cdot G$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | End-Effect Force Factor | $K_e = 1 - (1 - e^{-G})/G$ | 0 to 1 |
| 7 | Thrust Force (corrected) | $F_x = \dfrac{m \cdot I_2^2 \cdot R_2'}{v_s \cdot s} \cdot K_e$ | Newtons |
| 8 | Normal Force | $F_n \approx \dfrac{B_g^2 \cdot A}{2 \mu_0} \cdot K_n$ | Newtons |
| 9 | Magnetising Reactance (modified) | $X_m' = X_m / C_e$ | Ohms |
| 10 | Secondary Resistance (modified) | $R_2' = R_2 / s \cdot K_r$ | Ohms |
| 11 | Air-gap Power | $P_g = 3 I_2^2 \cdot R_2'/s$ | Watts |
| 12 | Mechanical Power Output | $P_m = P_g \cdot K_e \cdot (1-s)$ | Watts |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table, "m" denotes the number of phases, $\tau$ is the pole pitch, and $B_g$ is the peak air-gap flux density.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of This Analysis

- **High-speed Maglev trains** (e.g., Shanghai Transrapid) use LIMs where end effects significantly limit efficiency at low speeds.
- **Automated people movers** in airports rely on accurate LIM equivalent circuits for thrust and levitation force calculations.
- **Conveyor belt drives** in mining and steel plants use double-sided LIMs (DLIMs) where transverse edge effects dominate.
- **Crane drives** in shipyards and warehouses: low-speed LIMs where end-effect compensation is critical.
- **Electromagnetic launchers (EMALS)** on aircraft carriers: short-primary, long-secondary LIMs with extreme end effects.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Transverse Edge Effect Coefficient ($C_e$)

**Starting Point — Maxwell's Equations in the Transverse Direction:**

For a 2D model of the LIM in the transverse (x-z) plane, the magnetic vector potential $\vec{A}$ satisfies:

$$\dfrac{\partial^2 A_z}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 A_z}{\partial z^2} = j \omega \mu_0 \sigma A_z - \mu_0 J_{sz}$$

Let us assume the primary MMF is sinusoidally distributed in the z-direction, so we can write:

$$A_z(x,z) = \hat{A}(x) \cos(\pi z / \tau)$$

**Substitution into the Helmholtz Equation:**

Plugging this form into the governing PDE and assuming no primary current density in the air region (only in the secondary region), we obtain for the secondary:

$$\dfrac{d^2 \hat{A}}{dx^2} - \beta^2 \hat{A} = 0$$

where the **transverse propagation constant** is:

$$\beta^2 = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{\tau}\right)^2 + j \omega \mu_0 \sigma$$

**Solving the ODE:**

The general solution is:

$$\hat{A}(x) = A_1 \cosh(\beta x) + A_2 \sinh(\beta x)$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

By symmetry about the centre ($x=0$), the derivative must vanish:

$$\left.\dfrac{d\hat{A}}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_2 = 0$$

At the transverse edge ($x = a$), the magnetic field must be continuous with the external field, leading to a Neumann-type condition. The transverse current density must vanish outside the plate, so:

$$\left.\dfrac{\partial \hat{A}}{\partial x}\right|_{x=a} = -\mu_0 K_x(a)$$

where $K_x$ is the surface current density at the edge. Combining the solution with the integral form of the transverse current, the result after extensive algebra yields:

$$C_e = 1 - \dfrac{\tanh(\beta a)}{\beta a \left[1 + \tanh^2(\beta a)\right]}$$

**Limiting Case Verification:**

For a very wide plate ($a \to \infty$), $\tanh(\beta a) \to 1$, giving:

$$C_e \to 1 - \dfrac{1}{2 \beta a} \to 1$$

This confirms that **no transverse edge effect** exists for an infinitely wide plate, as expected.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Equivalent Circuit of a LIM (Including TEE and LEE)

**Step 1: Standard RIM Equivalent Circuit**

The classical per-phase equivalent circuit of a rotary induction motor is:

$$Z_{RIM} = R_1 + j X_1 + \left( j X_m \parallel \dfrac{R_2'}{s} \right)$$

**Step 2: Incorporating Transverse Edge Effect**

The transverse edge effect modifies the magnetising branch. The corrected secondary impedance referred to the primary is:

$$Z_2' = \dfrac{R_2'}{s \cdot C_e} + j X_2' \cdot C_e$$

and the magnetising reactance becomes:

$$X_m^{LIM} = X_m^{RIM} \cdot \dfrac{1}{C_e}$$

The corrected goodness factor is:

$$G_e = G \cdot C_e$$

**Step 3: Incorporating Longitudinal End Effect**

End effects are modelled by a **complex correction factor** $K = K_r + j K_i$ applied to the air-gap power transfer. Using the work of Laithwaite and Nasar, the corrected air-gap power is:

$$P_g^{LIM} = 3 \cdot I_2^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s} \cdot K_e$$

where:

$$K_e = 1 - \dfrac{1 - e^{-G}}{G}$$

This factor accounts for the **asymmetric entry and exit** of the secondary, effectively reducing the propulsive force.

**Step 4: The Final Modified Equivalent Circuit**

The complete LIM equivalent circuit per phase:

$$Z_{LIM} = R_1 + j X_1 + \left( j X_m^{LIM} \parallel \left( \dfrac{R_2'}{s} + j X_2' \right) \right)$$

with **thrust** and **normal force** calculated using:

$$F_x = \dfrac{P_g^{LIM} \cdot K_e}{v_s}$$

$$F_n = \dfrac{3 \cdot I_m^2 \cdot X_m^{LIM}}{2 \mu_0 \omega \cdot v_s} \cdot \text{(geometric factor)}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Goodness Factor

| Goodness Factor $G$ | End-Effect Severity | Efficiency Impact | Typical Application |
|----------------------|----------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| $G < 0.5$ | Severe | Efficiency < 30% | Short-stroke launchers |
| $0.5 < G < 2$ | Significant | Efficiency 30%–55% | Low-speed conveyors |
| $2 < G < 5$ | Moderate | Efficiency 55%–75% | Urban transit LIMs |
| $G > 10$ | Negligible | Efficiency > 80% | High-speed maglev |
| $G \to \infty$ | None | Efficiency $\to$ RIM | (Theoretical limit) |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A single-sided LIM has the following specifications: pole pitch $\tau = 0.10$ m, frequency $f = 50$ Hz, secondary conductivity $\sigma = 3.0 \times 10^7$ S/m, primary stack length $L = 1.0$ m, slip $s = 0.2$. Determine the goodness factor and the end-effect force factor.

**Step 1 — Synchronous velocity:**

$$v_s = 2 \tau f = 2 \times 0.10 \times 50 = 10 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Angular frequency:**

$$\omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \pi \times 50 = 314.159 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Goodness factor:**

$$G = \dfrac{\omega \mu_0 \sigma L^2 s}{\pi^2} = \dfrac{314.159 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3.0 \times 10^7 \times 1.0^2 \times 0.2}{\pi^2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**

$$\mu_0 \sigma = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3.0 \times 10^7 = 12 \pi$$

Therefore:

$$G = \dfrac{314.159 \times 12\pi \times 0.2}{\pi^2} = \dfrac{314.159 \times 2.4}{\pi} = \dfrac{753.98}{3.1416} \approx 240$$

**Step 5 — End-effect force factor:**

$$K_e = 1 - \dfrac{1 - e^{-240}}{240} \approx 1 - \dfrac{1 - 0}{240} = 1 - 0.00417 \approx 0.9958$$

**Conclusion:** With $G = 240$, the end-effect force factor is nearly 1, meaning the end effect is negligible — the LIM behaves almost like a RIM. This is a high-goodness-factor machine.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LIM Modified Equivalent Circuit (Block + Mermaid)

Since a true LIM equivalent circuit has parallel/series R-L branches, a Mermaid block diagram is more appropriate than a complex schematic drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Voltage Source V1] --> B[Stator Resistance R1]
    B --> C[Stator Leakage Reactance jX1]
    C --> D{Magnetising Branch}
    D --> E[Magnetising Reactance jXmLIM]
    D --> F[Corrected Secondary Impedance Z2 LIM]
    F --> G[R2 over s with Ce factor]
    F --> H[jX2 with Ce factor]
    I[Goodness Factor G] --> J[Transverse Edge Effect Ce]
    J --> E
    J --> F
    K[End Effect Factor Ke] --> L[Thrust Force Fx Output]
    E --> L
    F --> L
    M[Slip s and Speed vr] --> K
```

**Description of the Mermaid Block Diagram:**

- The **primary side** (R1, jX1) represents the stator of the LIM with the linearised travelling-wave excitation.
- The **magnetising branch** (jXmLIM) is *corrected* by the transverse edge effect coefficient $C_e$.
- The **secondary branch** ($R_2/s$, $jX_2$) is similarly scaled by $C_e$.
- The **Goodness Factor $G$** feeds into the calculation of $C_e$ and into the end-effect factor $K_e$.
- The **Thrust Force Output $F_x$** is the final propulsive force after all correction factors are applied.

## 4.2 Force-Velocity Characteristic with End-Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Slip s increases] --> Q{Region of Operation}
    Q --> R[Low slip region s 0 to 0.1]
    Q --> S[Mid slip region s 0.1 to 0.5]
    Q --> T[High slip region s 0.5 to 1.0]
    R --> U[End effects dominant, Fx rises sharply]
    S --> V[End effects moderate, Fx peaks near s 0.3]
    T --> W[End effects small, Fx falls gradually]
    U --> X[Final Thrust Speed Curve]
    V --> X
    W --> X
```

## 4.3 Transverse Flux Distribution (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Centre of Plate x 0] --> B[Maximum Flux Density B max]
    A --> C[Strongest Induced EMF]
    C --> D[Largest Eddy Current]
    E[Edge of Plate x a] --> F[Reduced Flux Density due to fringing]
    E --> G[Weaker Induced EMF]
    G --> H[Smaller Eddy Current loops through air]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:** The flux density profile is parabolic (or hyperbolic) across the transverse direction, peaking at the centre and falling at the edges. This non-uniformity is the root cause of **transverse edge effects**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEET743]`
**Define the Goodness Factor of a Linear Induction Motor and explain its significance in determining the severity of end effects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Goodness Factor (G)** of a Linear Induction Motor is a dimensionless quantity defined as:

$$G = \dfrac{\omega \mu_0 \sigma L^2 s}{\pi^2}$$

where $\omega$ is the angular supply frequency, $\mu_0$ is the permeability of free space, $\sigma$ is the secondary plate conductivity, $L$ is the length of the primary stack, and $s$ is the slip.

**Significance:**
- For **G > 10**, end effects are negligible, and the LIM behaves like a conventional rotary induction motor. **(1 mark)**
- For **G < 1**, end effects dominate, causing severe reduction in thrust and efficiency. **(1 mark)**
- It serves as a design guideline: increasing $L$ or $\sigma$ raises $G$ and suppresses end effects. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEET743]`
**What is meant by the transverse edge effect in a Linear Induction Motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **transverse edge effect** is the phenomenon by which the induced eddy currents in the secondary reaction plate of a LIM, near the transverse edges, are forced to close their paths through the air rather than within the conducting plate, due to the **finite width** of the secondary. **(1.5 marks)**

**Consequences:**
- Non-uniform flux density distribution across the width (maximum at centre, minimum at edges). **(0.75 marks)**
- Increased effective secondary resistance and reduced effective magnetising reactance, requiring correction via the coefficient $C_e$ in the equivalent circuit. **(0.75 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEET743, Module 4, Choice A]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **transverse edge effect** and **longitudinal end effect** in a Linear Induction Motor. Discuss how these effects influence the equivalent circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **Goodness Factor** of a LIM and explain its role in evaluating the end-effect force factor. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Transverse Edge Effect (TEE):** (1 mark)

The TEE arises due to the **finite width of the secondary reaction plate**. The induced EMF in the secondary drives transverse currents, which at the edges must close their paths through the surrounding air rather than within the plate. This causes the air-gap flux density to be **non-uniform** across the width — maximum at the centre, minimum at the edges.

**Definition of Longitudinal End Effect (LEE):** (1 mark)

The LEE is caused by the **finite length of the primary**. As the secondary slides under the primary, the magnetic field at the **entry end** is building up, while at the **exit end** it is collapsing. These transients induce additional eddy currents in the secondary that oppose the flux change (Lenz's law), causing:
- A **braking force component** at the exit end.
- A modified **thrust profile** along the length.

**Mathematical Treatment:** (2 marks)

TEE is represented by the coefficient:

$$C_e = 1 - \dfrac{\tanh(\beta a)}{\beta a [1 + \tanh^2(\beta a)]}$$

LEE is represented by the end-effect factor:

$$K_e = 1 - \dfrac{1 - e^{-G}}{G}$$

**Influence on Equivalent Circuit:** (2 marks)

- The magnetising reactance is modified: $X_m^{LIM} = X_m^{RIM} / C_e$.
- The secondary resistance is scaled: $R_2^{LIM} = R_2^{RIM} \cdot C_e / s$.
- The air-gap power is multiplied by $K_e$, reducing the effective thrust.
- The equivalent circuit transforms from a simple RIM-type to a **LIM-specific** representation.

**Diagram:** (1 mark)

[Mention any one block diagram or conceptual sketch of the modified equivalent circuit.]

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of Goodness Factor:** (4 marks)

Starting from Maxwell's equations in the secondary plate, the induced eddy current per unit width is:

$$J_y = \sigma E_y = -j \omega \sigma A_z$$

The total eddy-current loss per unit area is:

$$P_{eddy} = \dfrac{J_y^2}{\sigma} = \sigma \omega^2 A_z^2$$

Balancing the input air-gap power with the eddy-current loss, and integrating over the length $L$ and slip $s$, the **Goodness Factor** emerges as the ratio of the magnetic Reynolds number to the slip-dependent terms:

$$G = \dfrac{\omega \mu_0 \sigma L^2 s}{\pi^2}$$

**Step-by-step simplifications:** (1 mark)

Note that $\mu_0 \sigma$ has units of $(\text{H/m})(\text{S/m}) = \text{s/m}^2$. Thus $\omega \mu_0 \sigma L^2$ is dimensionless, and dividing by $\pi^2$ keeps it dimensionless. Confirmed.

**Role in End-Effect Force Factor:** (2 marks)

The end-effect force factor is:

$$K_e = 1 - \dfrac{1 - e^{-G}}{G}$$

- For **large G**: $e^{-G} \to 0$, so $K_e \to 1 - 1/G \to 1$. End effects vanish. **(1 mark)**
- For **small G**: $K_e \to 1 - (1 - 1 + G)/G = 0$. Thrust is severely reduced. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEET743, Module 4, Choice B]` (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Draw the **modified equivalent circuit** of a Linear Induction Motor and explain each component, including the correction factors for transverse and longitudinal effects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-sided LIM has $\tau = 0.08$ m, $f = 60$ Hz, $L = 0.8$ m, $\sigma = 5.0 \times 10^7$ S/m, and $s = 0.15$. Calculate the synchronous velocity, Goodness Factor, and the end-effect force factor $K_e$. Comment on the severity of end effects. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Modified Equivalent Circuit:** (3 marks)

The per-phase modified equivalent circuit contains:
- $R_1, X_1$ — primary resistance and leakage reactance (similar to RIM).
- $X_m^{LIM} = X_m / C_e$ — corrected magnetising reactance.
- $R_2'/s \cdot C_e$ — corrected secondary resistance term.
- $X_2' \cdot C_e$ — corrected secondary leakage reactance.
- An additional **braking component** representing the longitudinal end effect, often shown as a parallel branch with resistance $R_{end}$ proportional to $(1 - K_e)$.

**Explanation of Correction Factors:** (3 marks)

- **$C_e$ (Transverse Edge Effect Coefficient):** $0.6 \le C_e \le 0.9$. It scales the secondary impedance and inverse-scales the magnetising reactance. Physically represents the increased effective resistance and decreased effective magnetising due to edge fringing.
- **$K_e$ (End-Effect Force Factor):** $0 < K_e \le 1$. Multiplied with the air-gap power to obtain the corrected thrust. Physically represents the loss of propulsive force due to entry/exit transients.

**Importance of Modified Circuit:** (1 mark)

Without these corrections, the conventional RIM equivalent circuit **overestimates** the thrust, **underestimates** the losses, and gives incorrect slip characteristics for a LIM.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $\tau = 0.08$ m, $f = 60$ Hz, $L = 0.8$ m, $\sigma = 5.0 \times 10^7$ S/m, $s = 0.15$.

**Step 1: Synchronous Velocity** (2 marks)

$$v_s = 2 \tau f = 2 \times 0.08 \times 60 = 9.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Angular Frequency** (1 mark)

$$\omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \pi \times 60 = 376.99 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Goodness Factor** (2 marks)

$$G = \dfrac{\omega \mu_0 \sigma L^2 s}{\pi^2} = \dfrac{376.99 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0 \times 10^7 \times 0.8^2 \times 0.15}{\pi^2}$$

Simplification:

$$\mu_0 \sigma = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0 \times 10^7 = 20\pi$$

Therefore:

$$G = \dfrac{376.99 \times 20\pi \times 0.64 \times 0.15}{\pi^2} = \dfrac{376.99 \times 1.92}{\pi} = \dfrac{723.82}{3.1416} \approx 230.4$$

**Step 4: End-Effect Force Factor** (1 mark)

$$K_e = 1 - \dfrac{1 - e^{-230.4}}{230.4} \approx 1 - \dfrac{1}{230.4} \approx 0.9957$$

**Step 5: Comment on Severity** (1 mark)

Since $G \approx 230.4 \gg 10$ and $K_e \approx 0.9957$, the end effects are **negligible**. The LIM behaves essentially like an "unrolled" rotary induction motor, and the conventional equivalent circuit can be used with high accuracy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mixing Trap:** Many students confuse $\mu_0$ (in H/m) with $\mu_r$ (dimensionless) when calculating $G$. Always use $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
> 2. **Slip Definition:** Linear slip $s = (v_s - v_r)/v_s$ is dimensionless and **must not** be confused with rotor slip in RIMs where frequency is involved.
> 3. **Forgetting $C_e$:** Students often present the RIM equivalent circuit for a LIM. Examiner deducts up to **3 marks** for omitting $C_e$ and $K_e$ corrections.
> 4. **Direction of Correction:** $X_m^{LIM} = X_m^{RIM} / C_e$ (divided, not multiplied). Confusing the direction is a common error.
> 5. **Range of $G$:** A common mistake is to assume $G$ is always small. Real high-speed LIMs can have $G > 100$ where end effects are negligible.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Transverse Edge Effect (TEE)** = finite width issue. Model with **$C_e$** (0.6–0.9).
- [ ] **Longitudinal End Effect (LEE)** = finite length issue. Model with **$K_e$** (0–1) and **Goodness Factor $G$**.
- [ ] **Goodness Factor** $G = \omega \mu_0 \sigma L^2 s / \pi^2$ is the **key design parameter**.
- [ ] **$G > 10$** → end effects negligible; **$G < 1$** → end effects dominate.
- [ ] **Modified Equivalent Circuit:** $X_m^{LIM} = X_m / C_e$, $R_2^{LIM} = R_2 \cdot C_e / s$.
- [ ] **Thrust force** $F_x = P_g \cdot K_e / v_s$ (already corrected).
- [ ] **Normal force** acts to **attract** the secondary toward the primary in single-sided LIMs.
- [ ] **Double-sided LIMs (DLIMs)** cancel transverse edge effects by symmetry.
- [ ] **Short-primary LIMs** have low $G$ and severe end effects; **short-secondary LIMs** are preferred for high-speed applications.
- [ ] **Real-world applications:** Maglev, conveyor drives, crane drives, electromagnetic aircraft launchers.
- [ ] **Always** include $C_e$ and $K_e$ when drawing a LIM equivalent circuit in exams.
- [ ] **Plotting tip:** The thrust-slip curve of a LIM has a **gentler peak** and **lower maximum** than a RIM due to end effects.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thrust-Speed Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thrust-Speed Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Thrust-Speed Characteristics of Linear Induction Motor (LIM)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Special Electrical Machines (PEEET743)**, the **Thrust-Speed Characteristic** is the fundamental performance curve of a **Linear Induction Motor (LIM)** that plots the developed electromagnetic axial thrust force (in Newtons) as a function of the linear synchronous speed or vehicle velocity (in m/s). It is the linear-motion analog of the well-known **Torque-Speed characteristic** of a rotary induction motor.

Mathematically, it describes the relationship:
$$F_{th} = f(v_s - v_r) = f(s \cdot v_s)$$

where $F_{th}$ is the developed thrust, $v_s$ is the synchronous linear velocity, $v_r$ is the actual vehicle/secondary velocity, and $s$ is the **linear slip** defined as:

$$s = \frac{v_s - v_r}{v_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is critical under Module 4 (Linear Electric Machines). The student is expected to derive the thrust equation, identify regions of operation (motoring, generating, plugging), and understand the degrading effect of **end effects / edge effects** on the ideal characteristic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy trolley along a railway track. The harder the trolley pushes back on you (friction + load), the more force (thrust) you must apply to keep it moving.

- In a rotary motor, this force creates a **twisting moment (Torque, T)** at a certain **rotational speed (N)**.
- In a linear motor, the same electromagnetic action creates a **pushing force (Thrust, F)** along a straight line at a certain **linear speed (v)**.

So, just like an induction motor has a torque-slip curve, a LIM has a **thrust-slip curve**. The shape of this curve tells the engineer:
1. **Starting thrust** — how much push the motor can generate from standstill.
2. **Maximum (pull-out) thrust** — the peak push the motor can sustain without stalling.
3. **Normal operating region** — the linear, stable zone where the motor should ideally operate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the thrust drop at high speeds?**
> Just as in a rotary motor, at synchronous speed ($v_r = v_s$), the relative motion between the magnetic field and the secondary conductor becomes zero, so no flux is cut, no current is induced, and therefore no thrust is produced. Conversely, at standstill ($v_r = 0$), slip is maximum, and the motor produces a finite **starting thrust**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard quantities are used universally in KTU board examinations for LIM analysis:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range (KTU Problems) |
|----------|--------|---------------|------------------------------|
| Synchronous linear velocity | $v_s$ | m/s | 3 – 100 m/s |
| Linear slip | $s$ | dimensionless | 0 – 1 |
| Developed thrust | $F_{th}$ | Newtons (N) | 100 N – 50 kN |
| Pole pitch | $\tau$ | meters (m) | 0.05 – 0.5 m |
| Supply frequency | $f$ | Hertz (Hz) | 50 Hz (India Standard) |
| Power factor | $\cos\phi_2$ | dimensionless | 0.4 – 0.8 |
| Air-gap flux density | $B_g$ | Tesla (T) | 0.4 – 0.8 T |

> [!TIP]
> **Universal Constant Used:** $\pi \approx 3.1416$ and $f = 50$ Hz for Indian KTU numerical problems unless otherwise stated.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Origin of Thrust in a LIM

A Linear Induction Motor is essentially a rotary induction motor that has been "cut open" and unrolled flat. The stator (called the **primary** or **reaction plate**) is fed a balanced 3-phase AC supply and produces a **travelling magnetic field** that moves along the length of the motor at the synchronous linear velocity:

$$v_s = 2 f \tau$$

where $\tau$ is the pole pitch in meters, and $f$ is the supply frequency in Hz.

This travelling flux cuts the **secondary** (a conducting sheet, usually aluminum or copper, backed by a ferromagnetic iron core). By Faraday's law, an EMF is induced, currents flow, and by Lenz's law, these currents interact with the travelling field to produce a **Lorentz force** directed along the axis of motion. This axial force is the **electromagnetic thrust**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the analysis NOT identical to a rotary motor?**
> Because the LIM primary is *finite* in length and *open-ended*, the magnetic field at the entry and exit edges is distorted. This phenomenon, called the **end effect / edge effect**, causes the actual thrust to be lower than the *ideal* thrust predicted by rotary-motor analogy. This is the #1 KTU board favorite concept on this topic.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation Logic

The thrust equation is derived using the per-phase equivalent circuit (the same as a rotary induction motor, but with linear quantities).

**Step 1 — The per-phase secondary induced EMF at slip $s$:**

$$E_2^s = s \cdot E_2$$

**Step 2 — The per-phase secondary current:**

$$I_2 = \frac{s \cdot E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2}}$$

**Step 3 — The per-phase air-gap power transferred to the secondary:**

$$P_{gap} = m \cdot I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

where $m$ is the number of phases.

**Step 4 — Convert rotary mechanical power to linear thrust:**

The mechanical power developed in linear form is the thrust multiplied by the relative velocity $(v_s - v_r) = s \cdot v_s$:

$$P_{mech} = F_{th} \cdot v_s \cdot (1 - s)$$

**Step 5 — Equate developed power to thrust × slip velocity:**

$$F_{th} \cdot v_s = \frac{P_{gap} \cdot s}{1}$$

After rigorous algebraic simplification (shown in detail in Section 3), the **ideal thrust-slip equation** emerges as:

$$F_{th} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2 / s}{v_s \cdot \left[ \left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^2 + X_2^2 \right]}$$

## 2.3 Regions of the Thrust-Speed Curve

The curve can be divided into four quadrants, exactly like a rotary motor's torque-speed curve:

| Region | Slip Range | Speed Range | Mode of Operation | Application |
|--------|------------|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| I | $s > 1$ | $v_r < 0$ | **Plugging / Braking** | Rapid deceleration of maglev train |
| II | $0 < s < 1$ | $0 < v_r < v_s$ | **Forward Motoring** | Normal propulsion |
| III | $s < 0$ | $v_r > v_s$ | **Regenerative Braking** | Energy recovery during coasting |
| IV | $s = 1$ | $v_r = 0$ | **Starting** | Initial launch from rest |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Mistake:** Students often confuse **"starting thrust"** (at $s = 1$) with **"maximum thrust"** (at slip $s = s_m$). These are *not* the same point on the curve. Maximum thrust occurs at the **pull-out slip**, NOT at standstill.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation a student is expected to memorize for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments on this topic. Pay special attention to the units column.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $v_s = 2 f \tau$ | Synchronous linear velocity | m/s |
| 2 | $s = (v_s - v_r) / v_s$ | Linear slip | dimensionless |
| 3 | $F_{th} = \dfrac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot (R_2 / s)}{v_s \cdot [(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2]}$ | Ideal developed thrust | N |
| 4 | $F_{st} = \dfrac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{v_s \cdot (R_2^2 + X_2^2)}$ | Starting thrust (at $s = 1$) | N |
| 5 | $s_m = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | Slip at maximum thrust | dimensionless |
| 6 | $F_{max} = \dfrac{m \cdot E_2^2}{2 \cdot v_s \cdot X_2}$ | Maximum (pull-out) thrust | N |
| 7 | $P_{gap} = F_{th} \cdot v_s$ | Air-gap power | Watts |
| 8 | $P_{out} = F_{th} \cdot v_r$ | Output mechanical power | Watts |
| 9 | $\eta_{linear} = v_r / v_s = (1 - s)$ | Linear efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $v_r = 2 f \tau (1 - s)$ | Actual vehicle velocity | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA ALERT — KTU Boards:** The **maximum thrust equation ($F_{max}$)** and the **slip at maximum thrust ($s_m$)** are the two most-tested formulas. Memorize them; one of them will appear in almost every KTU exam on this topic.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The thrust-speed characteristic is the single most important design curve for engineers working on:

1. **Maglev High-Speed Trains (Shanghai Transrapid, JR-Maglev):** Engineers must size the LIM such that the maximum thrust exceeds the sum of aerodynamic drag + rolling resistance at cruise speed.
2. **Linear Metro Systems (Bombardier Innovia, Kawasaki):** For urban transit, the starting thrust must be high enough to accelerate a fully-loaded train.
3. **Industrial Conveyor Belt Drives:** LIMs replace rotary motor + belt systems, allowing direct linear propulsion with no mechanical transmission.
4. **Electromagnetic Aircraft Launch Systems (EMALS):** The US Navy uses linear motors to launch aircraft from carriers — the thrust profile must be precisely controlled via the thrust-speed curve.
5. **Stirling Engine Free-Piston Generators & Reciprocating Compressors:** Linear alternators are used in space and defense applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **complete, board-exam-quality, step-by-step**. Do not skip any step. KTU examiners award partial marks for each correct transition.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal Thrust-Slip Equation of a LIM

### Starting Point
We begin from the per-phase equivalent circuit of the secondary of a Linear Induction Motor, which is identical to the standard induction motor rotor circuit referred to the stator.

### Step 1: Per-Phase Secondary Induced EMF at Slip s

When the secondary conductor (reaction plate) moves at velocity $v_r$ relative to the travelling field moving at $v_s$, the **relative velocity** is:

$$v_{rel} = v_s - v_r$$

The slip is defined as the ratio of this relative velocity to the synchronous velocity:

$$s = \frac{v_s - v_r}{v_s}$$

The frequency of the EMF induced in the secondary is therefore:

$$f_2 = s \cdot f$$

The per-phase induced EMF in the secondary is proportional to this slip frequency:

$$E_2^s = s \cdot E_2$$

### Step 2: Per-Phase Secondary Current

The secondary winding has resistance $R_2$ and leakage reactance $X_2$ (referred to the primary). At slip frequency $f_2 = s \cdot f$, the actual reactance is $2 \pi f_2 L_2 = s \cdot X_2$.

By Ohm's law for the secondary loop:

$$I_2 = \frac{s \cdot E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2}}$$

### Step 3: Air-Gap Power per Phase

The power dissipated in the secondary resistance (which equals the air-gap power transferred to the secondary per phase) is:

$$P_{gap, \phi} = I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

### Step 4: Total Air-Gap Power (All $m$ Phases)

For a $m$-phase LIM (typically 3-phase):

$$P_{gap} = m \cdot I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

Substituting the expression for $I_2$:

$$P_{gap} = m \cdot \frac{(s \cdot E_2)^2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2} \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

Simplifying $(s \cdot E_2)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{s} = s \cdot E_2^2$:

$$P_{gap} = \frac{m \cdot s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2}$$

### Step 5: Developed Thrust from Power Balance

The mechanical power developed (which is the air-gap power minus the rotor copper loss) is:

$$P_{mech} = P_{gap} \cdot (1 - s)$$

In linear motion, mechanical power is thrust times velocity:

$$P_{mech} = F_{th} \cdot v_r = F_{th} \cdot v_s \cdot (1 - s)$$

Equating the two expressions for $P_{mech}$:

$$F_{th} \cdot v_s \cdot (1 - s) = \frac{m \cdot s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2} \cdot (1 - s)$$

Canceling $(1 - s)$ from both sides (valid for $s \neq 1$):

$$F_{th} \cdot v_s = \frac{m \cdot s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s \cdot X_2)^2}$$

### Step 6: Final Ideal Thrust-Slip Equation

$$\boxed{F_{th} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot (R_2 / s)}{v_s \cdot \left[\left(\frac{R_2}{s}\right)^2 + X_2^2\right]}}$$

This is the **fundamental thrust equation of an ideal LIM**, derived from the rotary induction motor analogy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Slip at Maximum Thrust ($s_m$)

### Starting Point
Differentiate the thrust expression with respect to slip $s$ and set the derivative to zero.

$$F_{th} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2 \cdot s}{v_s \cdot (R_2^2 + s^2 \cdot X_2^2)}$$

### Step 1: Define a Constant
Let $K = \dfrac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{v_s}$. Then:

$$F_{th} = \frac{K \cdot s}{R_2^2 + s^2 \cdot X_2^2}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Quotient Rule

$$\frac{dF_{th}}{ds} = \frac{K \cdot (R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - K \cdot s \cdot (2 s X_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2}$$

### Step 3: Set Numerator to Zero

$$K \cdot (R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - 2 K \cdot s^2 \cdot X_2^2 = 0$$

$$R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$

$$R_2^2 - s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for $s$

$$\boxed{s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The slip at which maximum thrust occurs is independent of voltage and depends only on the ratio of rotor resistance to rotor reactance. This is *identical* to the rotary induction motor result.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Thrust (Pull-Out Thrust)

### Starting Point
Substitute $s_m = R_2 / X_2$ back into the thrust equation.

### Step 1: Evaluate the Denominator

$$\left(\frac{R_2}{s_m}\right)^2 + X_2^2 = \left(\frac{R_2}{R_2/X_2}\right)^2 + X_2^2 = X_2^2 + X_2^2 = 2 X_2^2$$

### Step 2: Evaluate the Numerator

$$\frac{R_2}{s_m} = \frac{R_2}{R_2/X_2} = X_2$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$F_{max} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot X_2}{v_s \cdot 2 X_2^2} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2}{2 \cdot v_s \cdot X_2}$$

### Step 4: Final Result

$$\boxed{F_{max} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2}{2 v_s X_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** $F_{max}$ depends only on the **square of the supply voltage** and is **inversely proportional to $X_2$**. It is *independent* of $R_2$. This is why high-reactance secondary conductors hurt the peak thrust capability of a LIM.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Starting Thrust (at $s = 1$)

### Starting Point
Substitute $s = 1$ into the ideal thrust equation.

### Step 1: Simplify Denominator

$$\left(\frac{R_2}{1}\right)^2 + X_2^2 = R_2^2 + X_2^2$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$F_{st} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{v_s \cdot (R_2^2 + X_2^2)}$$

### Step 3: Final Result

$$\boxed{F_{st} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{v_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Some students write $F_{st} = F_{max}$. This is **FALSE**. Starting thrust and maximum thrust are equal *only* in the special case where $R_2 = X_2$ (i.e., $s_m = 1$). For $R_2 \neq X_2$, they are different points on the curve.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — The Effect of End Effects (Board Favorite)

The ideal equations above assume the primary is infinitely long, which is physically impossible. The **end effect** arises because the entry and exit edges of the finite primary cause:

1. **Asymmetric current distribution** in the secondary.
2. **Distortion of the air-gap flux density** (no longer a pure travelling wave).
3. **A longitudinal end-effect force component** that opposes motion.

The end effect is captured by an **equivalent correction factor**:

$$F_{th,actual} = F_{th,ideal} \cdot K_{end}$$

where $K_{end} < 1$ is the **end-effect factor**, which is a function of the **Goodness Factor** $G$:

$$G = \frac{2 \mu_0 f \tau^2 \cdot \sigma \cdot L}{\pi^2 \cdot g}$$

where $\sigma$ is the secondary surface conductivity, $L$ is the primary stack length, and $g$ is the air-gap length.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams:** You are not required to derive the end-effect factor formula, but you **must** be able to *qualitatively explain* why end effects reduce thrust and how $G$ influences the curve.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 3-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase LIM has a synchronous velocity of $12$ m/s and a pole pitch of $0.08$ m. The secondary parameters are $R_2 = 0.5 \ \Omega$ and $X_2 = 2 \ \Omega$. The induced EMF per phase is $E_2 = 100$ V. Calculate:
1. The slip at maximum thrust.
2. The maximum thrust.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** From the formula derived above:

$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25$$

**Part (b):**

$$F_{max} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2}{2 v_s X_2} = \frac{3 \times (100)^2}{2 \times 12 \times 2} = \frac{30000}{48} = 625 \ \text{N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $s_m = 0.25$ (dimensionless), $F_{max} = 625$ N.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Functional Schematics

## 4.1 LIM System Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

Since a true physical drawing of a LIM is complex, the following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the propulsion system, clearly showing how the thrust-speed characteristic is measured and utilized in real systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PSU[Three-Phase AC Supply] --> VFD[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
    VFD --> CTRL[Motion Controller ECU]
    CTRL --> LIM[Linear Induction Motor Primary]
    LIM -->|Travelling Flux| GAP[Air Gap]
    GAP -->|Induced EMF| SEC[Secondary Reaction Plate]
    SEC -->|Lorentz Force| THRUST[Developed Thrust F_th]
    THRUST -->|Acts On| VEH[Vehicle / Load]
    VEH -->|Velocity v_r| SENSOR[Linear Speed Sensor Encoder]
    SENSOR --> FB[Feedback Signal v_r]
    FB --> CTRL
    THRUST --> DSP[Thrust Transducer Load Cell]
    DSP --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System]
    DAQ -->|Plots F_th vs v_r| PLOT[Thrust Speed Characteristic Curve]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The forward path (top to bottom) shows how electrical energy is converted to mechanical thrust. The feedback loop (right side) shows how the controller regulates the VFD frequency to keep the LIM operating at the desired point on the thrust-speed curve.

## 4.2 Thrust-Speed Curve — Conceptual Map

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **logical map of the characteristic curve**, identifying key operating points:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[v_r = 0  Standstill s = 1] -->|Accelerating| B[F_st Starting Thrust]
    B -->|Slip Decreases| C[F_max Pull Out Thrust at s = s_m]
    C -->|Stable Region| D[Normal Operating Point 0 to 0.2 slip]
    D -->|Light Load| E[No Load Point s = s_nl]
    E -->|Synchronous| F[v_r = v_s  s = 0  F_th = 0]
    F -->|Overdriven| G[Generating Mode s less than 0]
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Reference (Block Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Supply V1 per phase] --> R1[R1 Primary Resistance]
    R1 --> X1[X1 Primary Leakage Reactance]
    X1 --> XM[XM Magnetising Reactance]
    XM --> R2S[R2 prime / s Equivalent Secondary Resistance]
    R2S --> X2P[X2 prime Equivalent Secondary Reactance]
    X2P --> N[Neutral]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Equivalent Circuit:** The secondary branch contains the **slip-dependent resistance** $R_2' / s$, which is the key element that varies with vehicle speed. As $s \to 0$ (near synchronous), $R_2'/s \to \infty$, so the secondary current goes to zero — hence zero thrust at synchronous speed. As $s = 1$ (standstill), $R_2'/s = R_2'$, and the current is finite, producing the starting thrust.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part A question carries **3 marks**, and the expected answer length is approximately 1 page. Students should answer in **3-4 crisp points** with a supporting diagram or formula.

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define the term "thrust" as applied to a Linear Induction Motor. How does it differ from the "torque" of a rotary induction motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Thrust is the linear electromagnetic force developed by a Linear Induction Motor along the direction of motion of the travelling magnetic field. It is measured in **Newtons (N)**.

2. **Torque analogy (1 mark):** Torque is the rotary equivalent of thrust and is measured in **Newton-meters (N·m)**. The thrust $F_{th}$ and torque $T$ are related by the pole pitch $\tau$ as $T = F_{th} \cdot \tau$.

3. **Key difference (1 mark):** Thrust produces **linear translation** of the secondary, whereas torque produces **rotational motion** of the rotor. The thrust-slip curve of a LIM is the direct linear analog of the torque-slip curve of a rotary induction motor.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the formula for the maximum thrust of a Linear Induction Motor. Under what condition does the slip at maximum thrust occur?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$F_{max} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2}{2 v_s X_2}$$

where $m$ is the number of phases, $E_2$ is the secondary induced EMF per phase, $v_s$ is the synchronous velocity, and $X_2$ is the secondary leakage reactance.

2. **Condition (1.5 marks):** The maximum thrust occurs at the **pull-out slip**:

$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$

3. **Insight:** $F_{max}$ is **independent of the secondary resistance $R_2$** but varies with the **square of the supply voltage**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is for **14 marks**, typically split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students must answer ONE of the two internal choices provided.

---

### 📌 Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**Q. A (a)** Derive the expression for the **ideal thrust-slip characteristic** of a Linear Induction Motor starting from the per-phase equivalent circuit. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the per-phase equivalent circuit (similar to a rotary induction motor), the secondary induced EMF at slip $s$ is $E_2^s = s \cdot E_2$, and the secondary current is:

$$I_2 = \frac{s \cdot E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$$

The air-gap power per phase is $I_2^2 \cdot (R_2/s)$. For $m$ phases:

$$P_{gap} = \frac{m \cdot s^2 \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2 / s}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2} = \frac{m \cdot s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}$$

Equating mechanical power to $F_{th} \cdot v_s \cdot (1 - s)$ and canceling $(1-s)$:

$$\boxed{F_{th} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot (R_2 / s)}{v_s \cdot [(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2]}}$$

This is the **ideal thrust-slip equation**, plotted as a curve with $F_{th}$ on the y-axis and $v_r$ (or $s$) on the x-axis.

---

**Q. A (b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz LIM has a pole pitch of $\tau = 0.25$ m. The secondary parameters are $R_2 = 1.2 \ \Omega$ and $X_2 = 3.6 \ \Omega$ per phase. The per-phase induced EMF is $E_2 = 220$ V. Calculate: (i) Synchronous velocity, (ii) Slip at maximum thrust, (iii) Maximum thrust, (iv) Starting thrust. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Synchronous velocity (2 marks):**

$$v_s = 2 f \tau = 2 \times 50 \times 0.25 = 25 \ \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Slip at maximum thrust (1 mark):**

$$s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{1.2}{3.6} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

**(iii) Maximum thrust (2 marks):**

$$F_{max} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2}{2 v_s X_2} = \frac{3 \times (220)^2}{2 \times 25 \times 3.6} = \frac{145200}{180} = 806.67 \ \text{N}$$

**(iv) Starting thrust (2 marks):**

$$F_{st} = \frac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2}{v_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)} = \frac{3 \times 48400 \times 1.2}{25 \times (1.44 + 12.96)} = \frac{174240}{25 \times 14.4} = \frac{174240}{360} = 484 \ \text{N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** $v_s = 25$ m/s, $s_m = 0.333$, $F_{max} = 806.67$ N, $F_{st} = 484$ N. Notice that $F_{st} < F_{max}$, confirming that starting thrust is NOT the maximum thrust for $R_2 \neq X_2$.

---

### 📌 Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]**

**Q. B (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **thrust-speed characteristic** of a Linear Induction Motor. Mark the regions of motoring, generating, and plugging. Discuss the effect of **end effects** on the characteristic. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

The thrust-speed characteristic of a LIM is a plot of developed electromagnetic thrust $F_{th}$ (y-axis) versus the actual vehicle velocity $v_r$ (x-axis), with the synchronous velocity $v_s$ marked as a vertical asymptote where $F_{th} = 0$.

**Key features of the curve (3 marks):**
- The curve starts at the **starting thrust** $F_{st}$ at $v_r = 0$.
- It rises to a **maximum thrust** $F_{max}$ at velocity $v_m = v_s (1 - s_m)$.
- It then falls smoothly to **zero** at $v_r = v_s$.
- For $v_r > v_s$ (slip becomes negative), the curve dips into the **generating mode**.
- For $v_r < 0$ (slip $> 1$), the curve continues into the **plugging / braking mode**.

**Regions marked (2 marks):**
- **Motoring:** $0 < v_r < v_s$ (slip between 1 and 0)
- **Generating:** $v_r > v_s$ (slip negative)
- **Plugging:** $v_r < 0$ (slip greater than 1)

**Effect of end effects (2 marks):**
The finite length of the primary causes the magnetic field at the entry and exit edges to be distorted, which results in:
1. Reduction in the average air-gap flux density.
2. Asymmetric secondary current distribution.
3. A net reduction in developed thrust — the actual curve lies **below the ideal curve**, especially in the **high-slip (low-speed) region**.
4. The starting thrust is most severely affected, often reduced by 20–40% in practical LIMs.

---

**Q. B (b)** A single-sided LIM is used to propel a conveyor at $v_r = 8$ m/s. The synchronous velocity is $v_s = 10$ m/s. The motor develops a thrust of $F_{th} = 500$ N. Calculate: (i) The linear slip, (ii) The air-gap power, (iii) The output mechanical power, (iv) The linear efficiency. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Linear slip (2 marks):**

$$s = \frac{v_s - v_r}{v_s} = \frac{10 - 8}{10} = 0.2$$

**(ii) Air-gap power (2 marks):**

$$P_{gap} = F_{th} \cdot v_s = 500 \times 10 = 5000 \ \text{W} = 5 \ \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Output mechanical power (2 marks):**

$$P_{out} = F_{th} \cdot v_r = 500 \times 8 = 4000 \ \text{W} = 4 \ \text{kW}$$

**(iv) Linear efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\eta_{linear} = \frac{v_r}{v_s} = (1 - s) = 0.8 = 80\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** $s = 0.2$, $P_{gap} = 5$ kW, $P_{out} = 4$ kW, $\eta_{linear} = 80\%$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS ON THIS TOPIC:**
> 1. **Confusing starting thrust with maximum thrust.** They are equal ONLY when $R_2 = X_2$. For all other cases, $F_{st} \neq F_{max}$. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if you state that $F_{st} = F_{max}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rotary parameters to linear.** When a problem gives rotor resistance, it must be **referred to the stator** before using the equivalent circuit. Check whether the problem states "referred to stator" or "referred to rotor."
> 3. **Using the wrong formula for $v_s$.** A common error is $v_s = f \cdot \tau$ (missing the factor of 2). The correct formula is $v_s = 2 f \tau$. This mistake alone costs **2 marks**.
> 4. **Ignoring end effects in numerical problems.** If the problem mentions "actual" thrust or "with end effects," you must apply a correction factor $K_{end} < 1$ or explicitly state that the actual thrust is lower than the ideal.
> 5. **Not labeling units.** Forgetting to write N (Newtons), m/s, or Hz will cost **0.5 to 1 mark** per quantity, as KTU follows strict unit-marking policy.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- 🔹 **Thrust** is the linear force developed by a LIM, analogous to torque in a rotary motor.
- 🔹 **Synchronous velocity:** $v_s = 2 f \tau$ — memorize this with the factor of 2.
- 🔹 **Linear slip:** $s = (v_s - v_r) / v_s$ — ranges from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.
- 🔹 **Ideal thrust equation:** $F_{th} = \dfrac{m \cdot E_2^2 \cdot (R_2 / s)}{v_s \cdot [(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2]}$
- 🔹 **Slip at max thrust:** $s_m = R_2 / X_2$ — independent of voltage.
- 🔹 **Maximum thrust:** $F_{max} = m \cdot E_2^2 / (2 v_s X_2)$ — depends on $V^2$, not on $R_2$.
- 🔹 **Starting thrust:** $F_{st} = m \cdot E_2^2 R_2 / [v_s (R_2^2 + X_2^2)]$ — occurs at $s = 1$.
- 🔹 **Air-gap power:** $P_{gap} = F_{th} \cdot v_s$ — analogous to $T \cdot \omega_s$ in rotary motors.
- 🔹 **Output power:** $P_{out} = F_{th} \cdot v_r$ — analogous to $T \cdot \omega_r$.
- 🔹 **Linear efficiency:** $\eta = v_r / v_s = 1 - s$ — the "ideal" mechanical efficiency.
- 🔹 **End effects** reduce the actual thrust below the ideal curve, especially at high slips (low speeds).
- 🔹 **Four regions:** Motoring, Generating, Plugging, and Starting — all are present on the full thrust-speed curve.
- 🔹 **Goodness Factor $G$** quantifies the influence of end effects: high $G$ means more end-effect loss.
- 🔹 **Applications:** Maglev trains, linear metros, EMALS, conveyor drives, reciprocating machines.
- 🔹 **KTU board-tested formulas:** $v_s$, $s_m$, $F_{max}$, $F_{st}$ — at least one will appear in every exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications; Single Phase Special Electrical Machines - AC Series Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications; Single Phase Special Electrical Machines - AC Series Motor # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **AC Series Motor**, popularly known as the **Universal Motor**, is a single-phase commutator-type special electrical machine whose construction is essentially identical to a **DC series motor**, but which is designed and optimized to operate efficiently on **single-phase AC supply** as well as on DC supply. The armature and field windings are connected in **series**, and the entire combination is fed through a **commutator-brush arrangement**. Because the field and armature currents reverse simultaneously on AC, the torque produced is unidirectional and pulsating, making it a versatile high-speed drive for domestic and portable applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEET743 / Module 4):** > The AC Series Motor is classified under "Single Phase Special Electrical Machines" because, unlike standard induction motors, it requires commutation and is therefore capable of operating at speeds well above synchronous speed (often **8,000 – 25,000 RPM**), a feat not possible with single-phase induction machines. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Universal Kitchen Mixer** > Imagine your home mixer-grinder. When you press the start button, the blades spin at extremely high speed (much faster than a regular fan motor). When you load it with wet dough or hard spices, the speed drops but the torque increases — exactly the speed-torque behavior of a DC series motor. This is because the same machine is running on your **230 V, 50 Hz single-phase AC mains** and yet behaving like a high-speed series DC motor. That machine is a **Universal Motor**. ## 1.3 Why It Is Called "Universal" The motor is called *Universal* because of the following key property: - It can operate on **both AC and DC** supplies without any modification in construction. - The **series connection** ensures that armature current ($I_a$) and field current ($I_f$) are the **same** at every instant. - Since $T \propto \Phi \cdot I_a$, and both $\Phi$ and $I_a$ reverse together, the **torque is always positive** (unidirectional). ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Supply Voltage | **230 V, 50 Hz** (India) / 110–120 V, 60 Hz (USA) | | Operating Speed | **8,000 – 25,000 RPM** | | Power Rating | **50 W – 2 kW** (fractional kW range) | | Efficiency | **40 % – 75 %** | | Starting Torque | **3 to 5 times rated torque** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction (Identical to DC Series Motor) The AC series motor has the following components: - **Stator (Field System):** Laminated silicon-steel core with **salient poles**. Each pole carries a **field winding** wound with relatively few turns of thick wire (to carry full armature current). - **Rotor (Armature):** Same as DC machine — slotted laminated core with wave/lap winding connected to a **commutator**. - **Brushes & Commutator:** Carbon brushes riding on a copper commutator ensure proper commutation under AC excitation. - **Compensating Winding (optional but standard in good designs):** Embedded in the pole faces, connected in series with the armature, and arranged so that its MMF opposes the **armature reaction MMF**, thereby improving commutation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lamination is critical on AC:** Because the motor operates on AC, the entire magnetic circuit (stator and rotor) must be **laminated** to minimize eddy-current losses. This is the main structural difference from a DC series motor (whose field yoke is often solid). ## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Supply Applied:** Single-phase AC voltage $V$ is applied across the series combination of field winding and armature. 2. **Common Current:** Since the circuit is purely series, the same current $i(t)$ flows through both. 3. **Flux Production:** The field winding produces an alternating flux $\phi(t)$ in the air gap, in phase with the current. 4. **Torque Production:** The armature current $i(t)$ interacts with this flux. Instantaneous torque is $T_{inst} = k \cdot \phi(t) \cdot i(t)$. 5. **Bidirectional Cancellation:** On AC, both $\phi(t)$ and $i(t)$ reverse simultaneously (every half cycle), so their **product is always positive**. 6. **Unidirectional Rotation:** The rotor therefore experiences torque in the same direction throughout — producing continuous rotation in one direction. ## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step - **Why series connection?** So that the field flux and armature current reverse **together** — guaranteeing unidirectional torque. - **Why lamination?** AC produces a time-varying flux; a solid iron core would heat up severely due to eddy currents. - **Why compensating winding?** Armature reaction on AC is severe and shifts the neutral plane continuously, causing **poor commutation and sparking** at the brushes. The compensating winding neutralizes this. ## 2.4 Performance Characteristics | Feature | Behavior | |---|---| | Speed regulation | Poor (speed drops sharply with load) | | No-load speed | Dangerously high — motor may self-destruct if belt breaks | | Starting torque | Very high (≈ 3–5 × full load) | | Speed range | Very high (suitable for > 3000 RPM) | | Reversal | By reversing either field OR armature connection (not both) | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T = k \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Instantaneous electromagnetic torque (same as DC series) | N·m | | 2 | $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_f) + j I_a(X_a + X_f)$ | AC voltage equation (phasor form) | V | | 3 | $E_b = k \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF equation (rotational) | V | | 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a(R_a + R_f)}{k\Phi}$ | Speed equation from voltage balance | rad/s | | 5 | $N \propto \dfrac{V}{k\Phi}$ | Speed is inversely proportional to flux | RPM | | 6 | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} k \Phi_m I_m$ | Average torque (for sinusoidal AC, $\Phi_m$ = peak flux, $I_m$ = peak current) | N·m | | 7 | $P_{conv} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power developed | W | | 8 | $Z_s = \sqrt{(R_a+R_f)^2 + (X_a+X_f)^2}$ | Synchronous impedance per phase | $\Omega$ | | 9 | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{R_a + R_f}{Z_s}$ | Power factor of the motor | dimensionless | | 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency (typically 40 – 75 %) | % | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often use $T = k \Phi I$ (DC form) and forget the factor of **½** when dealing with sinusoidal AC quantities. The correct AC average torque is $T_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2} k \Phi_m I_m$ where $\Phi_m$ and $I_m$ are **peak** values. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Universal motors dominate the **fractional-kW, high-speed** market. They are used where: - Light weight is critical (handheld tools). - High speed-torque ratio is needed. - Cost must be low. - Compactness is essential. In industry, similar high-speed commutator machines are still used in **traction auxiliaries**, **sewing machine drives**, and **portable woodworking tools**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Torque in a Universal Motor on AC **Given:** - Flux: $\phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$ - Current: $i_a(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ **Step 1:** Write the instantaneous torque equation (same form as a DC motor). $$ T_{inst}(t) = k \cdot \phi(t) \cdot i_a(t) $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the time-varying flux and current. $$ T_{inst}(t) = k \cdot \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t) $$ **Step 3:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\sin^2(\omega t) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$. $$ T_{inst}(t) = k \cdot \Phi_m I_m \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} $$ **Step 4:** Separate the constant (average) part and the pulsating part. $$ T_{inst}(t) = \underbrace{\frac{k \Phi_m I_m}{2}}_{\text{Average torque}} - \underbrace{\frac{k \Phi_m I_m}{2} \cos(2\omega t)}_{\text{Pulsating component}} $$ **Step 5:** Identify the average torque. $$ T_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} k \Phi_m I_m $$ **Conclusion:** The torque has an average value of $\tfrac{1}{2} k \Phi_m I_m$ plus a pulsating component of **twice the supply frequency**. This pulsation is one reason the motor is noisy and produces vibration. > [!NOTE] > The torque expression shows that even though the supply is sinusoidal, the **mechanical output is partly unidirectional (DC-like) and partly at $2f$**. This is a unique feature of commutator-type AC motors. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage Equation and Power Factor **Given:** Series combination of field winding ($R_f, X_f$) and armature ($R_a, X_a$). **Step 1:** Apply KVL around the loop. $$ V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_f) + j I_a(X_a + X_f) $$ **Step 2:** Recognize that the back-EMF $E_b$ lags the current by nearly 90° (because $\Phi$ is in phase with $I$ and $E_b \propto j\omega\Phi$). **Step 3:** Group the resistive and reactive drops. $$ V = E_b + I_a R_{total} + j I_a X_{total} $$ where $R_{total} = R_a + R_f$ and $X_{total} = X_a + X_f$. **Step 4:** Magnitude form. $$ \vert V \vert = \sqrt{(E_b + I_a R_{total})^2 + (I_a X_{total})^2} $$ **Step 5:** Speed expression (ignoring the small effect of $X_{total}$ for simplicity). $$ \omega = \frac{\sqrt{V^2 - (I_a X_{total})^2} - I_a R_{total}}{k \Phi} $$ **Step 6:** Power factor expression. $$ \cos\phi = \frac{I_a R_{total}}{V} \quad \text{(idealised, ignoring } E_b \text{ contribution)} $$ > [!TIP] > The power factor of a universal motor is typically **0.7 – 0.9 lagging** at full load and worsens to **0.3 – 0.5** at no-load because the back-EMF becomes very small relative to leakage reactance drops. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DC vs AC Operation of the Same Motor | Parameter | On DC Supply | On AC Supply | |---|---|---| | Flux waveform | Constant | Sinusoidal (with $2f$ ripple) | | Torque waveform | Constant | $T_{avg}$ + $2f$ pulsation | | Commutation | Satisfactory | Difficult (sparking) | | Iron loss | Lower | Higher (lamination required) | | Power factor | Unity (theoretically) | Lagging (0.5 – 0.9) | | Speed | Slightly lower (no $X$ drop) | Slightly lower (due to $X$ drop) | | Efficiency | Slightly higher | Slightly lower | ## 3.4 Speed Calculation — Worked Numerical **Problem:** A 230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole universal motor has $R_a = 4\ \Omega$, $R_f = 6\ \Omega$, $X_{total} = 20\ \Omega$. At a load current of $I_a = 5$ A, the back-EMF constant is such that $k\Phi = 0.5$ V·s/rad. Find the speed. **Step 1:** Total series resistance. $$ R_{total} = R_a + R_f = 4 + 6 = 10\ \Omega $$ **Step 2:** Resistive drop. $$ I_a R_{total} = 5 \times 10 = 50\ \text{V} $$ **Step 3:** Reactive drop. $$ I_a X_{total} = 5 \times 20 = 100\ \text{V} $$ **Step 4:** Magnitude of back-EMF (using KVL magnitude). $$ E_b = \sqrt{V^2 - (I_a X_{total})^2} - I_a R_{total} $$ $$ E_b = \sqrt{230^2 - 100^2} - 50 $$ $$ E_b = \sqrt{52900 - 10000} - 50 = \sqrt{42900} - 50 $$ $$ E_b \approx 207.12 - 50 = 157.12\ \text{V} $$ **Step 5:** Compute speed. $$ \omega = \frac{E_b}{k\Phi} = \frac{157.12}{0.5} = 314.24\ \text{rad/s} $$ **Step 6:** Convert to RPM. $$ N = \frac{60 \cdot \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 314.24}{2\pi} \approx 3000\ \text{RPM} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Universal Motor Drive ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Mains Supply
230 V 50 Hz] --> B[Switch and Fuse
Protection] B --> C[Series Field Winding
Stator Pole Rf Xf] C --> D[Armature Winding
on Rotor Ra Xa] D --> E[Commutator and
Brush Assembly] E --> F[Mechanical Output
Shaft Load] G[Compensating Winding
in Pole Face] -.->|Opposes armature reaction| D H[Mechanical Load
Tool or Appliance] --> F F --> I[Torque and Speed
Feedback Sensor] I --> J[Controller
Triac or Diac] J -.->|Phase angle control| B ``` ## 4.2 Circuit Topology — Series Connection Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD V[Voltage Source
AC 230 V] --> Fld{Field Winding
Rf and Xf} Fld --> Comp[Compensating Winding
Rc in Series] Comp --> Arm{Armature
Ra and Xa} Arm --> Br[Brush 1] Br --> Comm[Commutator] Comm --> Br2[Brush 2] Br2 --> V note1[Field and armature currents
are identical]:::note Fld -.- note1 Arm -.- note1 classDef note fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Phasor Diagram (Textual Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR Ia[Current Ia
Reference Phasor] --> FLUX[Flux Phi
in phase with Ia] Ia --> Eb[Back EMF Eb
Lags Ia by 90 deg] Ia --> IR[I x R drop
in phase with Ia] Ia --> IX[I x X drop
leads Ia by 90 deg] V[Terminal Voltage V
Phasor Sum] --> IR V --> IX V --> Eb ``` ## 4.4 Torque Waveform on AC (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sinusoidal Current
50 Hz] --> B[Sinusoidal Flux
50 Hz in Phase] A --> C[Instantaneous Torque
k Phi i at 100 Hz] B --> C C --> D[Average T avg
Unidirectional] C --> E[Pulsating Component
at 2 times supply frequency] ``` ## 4.5 Application Tree — Where Universal Motors Are Used ```mermaid flowchart TD UM[Universal Motor] --> DOM[Domestic Appliances] UM --> TOOL[Portable Power Tools] UM --> OTH[Other Applications] DOM --> M1[Mixers and Blenders] DOM --> M2[Vacuum Cleaners] DOM --> M3[Washing Machines
small] DOM --> M4[Hair Dryers] DOM --> M5[Sewing Machines] TOOL --> T1[Hand Drills] TOOL --> T2[Angle Grinders] TOOL --> T3[Circular Saws] TOOL --> T4[Jigsaws] TOOL --> T5[Sanders] OTH --> O1[Electric Shavers] OTH --> O2[Photocopiers] OTH --> O3[Toys and Hobby Tools] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question] **Why is an AC series motor called a "universal motor"? Mention any two reasons.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) The AC series motor is called a **universal motor** because it can operate on **both AC and DC** supplies with satisfactory performance. - (1 mark) This is because the **field and armature are connected in series**, so the flux and armature current reverse simultaneously on AC, producing unidirectional torque. - (1 mark) Hence the same machine can be used for applications supplied by either AC mains or DC batteries without any structural change. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Question] **List any three applications of universal motors.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) Domestic applications: **mixers, blenders, vacuum cleaners, hair dryers**. - (1 mark) Portable power tools: **hand drills, angle grinders, circular saws**. - (1 mark) Other applications: **sewing machines, electric shavers, photocopying machines**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question] **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle** of a single-phase AC series motor (universal motor). Derive the **expression for average torque** when operated on AC supply. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **230 V, 50 Hz universal motor** has the following parameters: $R_a = 2\ \Omega$, $R_f = 3\ \Omega$, $X_{total} = 12\ \Omega$. The motor draws a current of $6$ A at a certain load. The back-EMF constant is $k\Phi = 0.45$ V·s/rad. Calculate: **(i)** the back-EMF, **(ii)** the speed in RPM, and **(iii)** the power factor. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Question A #### Solution to Part (a) — Construction, Working and Average Torque **Construction (3 marks):** - The universal motor is essentially a **DC series motor** built with **completely laminated magnetic circuit** (stator and rotor) to reduce eddy-current losses on AC. - The **stator** has salient poles carrying the **field winding** ($R_f$, $X_f$) connected in series with the armature. - The **rotor** is a DC-type armature with a commutator and carbon brushes. - A **compensating winding** is embedded in the pole faces to neutralise armature reaction and improve commutation. **Working Principle (2 marks):** - When the motor is connected to AC supply, the **same current $i(t)$ flows** through both field and armature windings. - The flux $\phi(t)$ produced by the field is in phase with the current. - Torque at any instant is $T_{inst} = k \phi(t) i(t)$. - On AC, both $\phi$ and $i$ reverse simultaneously, so $T_{inst}$ is always positive — producing **unidirectional rotation**. **Derivation of Average Torque (2 marks):** $$ \phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t), \quad i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t) $$ $$ T_{inst} = k \Phi_m I_m \sin^2(\omega t) = \frac{k \Phi_m I_m}{2}\left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right] $$ $$ \boxed{T_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} k \Phi_m I_m} $$ #### Solution to Part (b) — Numerical **Given:** $V = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_a = 2\ \Omega$, $R_f = 3\ \Omega$, $X_{total} = 12\ \Omega$, $I_a = 6$ A, $k\Phi = 0.45$ V·s/rad. **(i) Back-EMF (3 marks):** Step 1 — Total series resistance. $$ R_{total} = R_a + R_f = 2 + 3 = 5\ \Omega $$ Step 2 — Reactive drop. $$ I_a X_{total} = 6 \times 12 = 72\ \text{V} $$ Step 3 — Using KVL magnitude (assuming $E_b$ aligned with $V$ in real axis for simplicity): $$ E_b = \sqrt{V^2 - (I_a X_{total})^2} - I_a R_{total} $$ $$ E_b = \sqrt{230^2 - 72^2} - 6 \times 5 $$ $$ E_b = \sqrt{52900 - 5184} - 30 $$ $$ E_b = \sqrt{47716} - 30 \approx 218.44 - 30 $$ $$ \boxed{E_b \approx 188.44\ \text{V}} $$ **(ii) Speed in RPM (2 marks):** $$ \omega = \frac{E_b}{k\Phi} = \frac{188.44}{0.45} \approx 418.76\ \text{rad/s} $$ $$ N = \frac{60 \times \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 418.76}{2\pi} \approx \boxed{4000\ \text{RPM}} $$ **(iii) Power Factor (2 marks):** $$ Z_s = \sqrt{R_{total}^2 + X_{total}^2} = \sqrt{5^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = 13\ \Omega $$ $$ \cos\phi = \frac{R_{total}}{Z_s} = \frac{5}{13} \approx \boxed{0.385\ \text{lagging}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Writing the speed formula as $\omega = (V - I_a R_{total}) / k\Phi$ — this is the **DC approximation** and ignores the reactive drop. KTU expects you to use the **phasor form** since this is an AC series motor. > - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert rad/s to RPM in the final answer. Always write $N = 60\omega / 2\pi$. > - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Writing the torque formula $T = k\Phi I$ for AC operation without the factor of **½**. On AC, the average torque is $T_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2} k \Phi_m I_m$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Question] **(a)** Explain the **role of the compensating winding** in an AC series motor. Why is **lamination of the entire magnetic circuit** essential in this motor? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **performance of an AC series motor on AC and DC supplies** with respect to: **(i)** commutation, **(ii)** torque pulsation, **(iii)** iron losses, **(iv)** power factor, and **(v)** speed. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Question B #### Solution to Part (a) — Compensating Winding and Lamination **Compensating Winding (4 marks):** - In a single-phase AC series motor, the **armature reaction MMF is alternating** (since the armature current is AC). This continuously shifts the **magnetic neutral axis (MNA)** in the commutating zone. - This shifting MNA causes **severe sparking at the brushes** and poor commutation. - A **compensating winding**, embedded in the pole faces and connected in series with the armature, produces an MMF that is **equal and opposite** to the armature reaction MMF at every instant. - This **neutralises the armature reaction**, makes the air-gap flux distribution uniform, and ensures **sparkless commutation** on AC. **Lamination of Magnetic Circuit (3 marks):** - On AC, the main flux $\phi(t)$ is time-varying, inducing eddy currents in any solid iron part. - Eddy-current losses are proportional to $(B \cdot f \cdot t)^2$, so they become severe on AC (especially 50/60 Hz). - Therefore, **both stator and rotor cores are built from thin laminations** (0.35 – 0.5 mm) insulated from one another. - This drastically reduces eddy-current losses, prevents overheating, and improves efficiency. #### Solution to Part (b) — Comparison Table | S.No. | Parameter | On DC Supply | On AC Supply | |---|---|---|---| | (i) | Commutation | Satisfactory | Difficult — sparking tendency; requires compensating winding | | (ii) | Torque pulsation | Smooth, constant | Pulsating at $2f$ superimposed on average torque | | (iii) | Iron losses | Low (no $f$ component) | Higher due to AC flux; lamination is mandatory | | (iv) | Power factor | Unity (ideal) | Lagging (0.5 – 0.9) due to $X_{total}$ | | (v) | Speed | Slightly higher (no $IX$ drop) | Slightly lower due to $IX$ drop across leakage reactance | (Each correct comparison = 1.4 marks; 5 rows × 1.4 ≈ 7 marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:** > - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Stating that commutation is "the same" on AC and DC. The MNA shift is **AC-specific** and is a heavily tested KTU point. > - **Common Mistake 2 (–1.5 marks):** Saying lamination is needed for "the same reason as in a DC motor." Wrong — DC motors may have solid yokes; AC series motors require **complete lamination** including the yoke, poles, and armature. > - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Confusing the pulsation frequency as $f$ instead of $2f$. Since $T \propto \sin^2(\omega t)$, the ripple is at **twice** the supply frequency. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Universal motor = DC series motor built with complete lamination, capable of running on both AC and DC. - **Constructional musts:** Laminated stator and rotor; compensating winding for sparkless commutation; series connection of field and armature. - **Working formula:** $T_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2} k \Phi_m I_m$ on sinusoidal AC; $T = k \Phi I$ on DC. - **Torque nature:** Unidirectional but pulsating at $2f$ on AC; constant on DC. - **Back-EMF equation:** $E_b = k \Phi \omega$ (true for both AC and DC). - **Voltage equation on AC:** $V = E_b + I_a R_{total} + j I_a X_{total}$ — note the reactive drop. - **Power factor:** Always lagging on AC (0.5 – 0.9); unity on DC. - **Speed:** Inversely proportional to flux — high at light load, dangerous if belt/load breaks. - **Compensating winding:** Neutralises alternating armature reaction; essential for AC operation. - **Lamination:** Mandatory across the **entire magnetic circuit** (stator yoke, poles, and armature). - **Applications:** Mixers, blenders, vacuum cleaners, hair dryers, sewing machines, hand drills, angle grinders, saws, sanders, shavers, photocopying machines. - **Reversal:** Reverse either the field or the armature connection — never both. - **High-yield KTU keywords:** "Universal motor", "compensating winding", "lamination", "$T_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2} k\Phi_m I_m$", "$2f$ pulsation", "armature reaction on AC", "lagging power factor". - **Examiner traps to avoid:** Forgetting the **½ factor** in AC torque; ignoring **reactive drop** when calculating speed; stating lamination is only for armature (it is for the **entire** core); confusing $f$ vs $2f$ in torque pulsation.

topics.title: Repulsion Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Repulsion Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Repulsion Motor** is a specially constructed single-phase AC commutator motor in which the armature winding is **not electrically connected** to the supply mains. Instead, the armature receives power **inductively** through a transformer action from the stator (field) winding. The brushes mounted on the commutator are **short-circuited** through a low-resistance path, and the torque is produced purely by the **mutual repulsion** between the stator field poles and the induced magnetic poles on the rotor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** *"A repulsion motor is a single-phase AC series motor in which the armature is supplied inductively (not conductively) and the brushes are short-circuited. The name 'repulsion' derives from the fact that the rotor is repelled (pushed away) by the stator field, rather than being physically attracted, due to the induced like-pole formation on the rotor under the short-circuited brushes."*

The repulsion motor is essentially a **mechanically identical twin** of the DC series motor, but operates entirely on **AC supply** without any conductive connection between the rotor and the external circuit. This is the single most important distinguishing feature of this machine for KTU 2024 scheme questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnet Push-Pull" Intuition

Imagine you are holding two bar magnets in your hands. One magnet is fixed (this is the **stator field**), and the other is on a rotating shaft (this is the **rotor/armature**). Now, if you bring the **fixed magnet's North pole** near the rotating magnet, and somehow arrange the rotating magnet so that its **North pole also faces the fixed North pole**, the rotating magnet will be violently **pushed away (repelled)** — this is exactly what happens in a repulsion motor.

The trick is: how do we make sure the rotor always shows a **"like pole"** to the stator? This is achieved by:
