1. **Not directly powering the rotor** — instead, the rotor is **short-circuited** through brushes.
2. The alternating stator flux **induces a current** in the shorted rotor.
3. By Lenz's law, this induced current creates a rotor field that **opposes the cause** — but due to the brush axis geometry, the resulting rotor pole always **faces the stator pole as a like pole**, causing repulsion.
4. The rotor keeps "running away" from the stator field, producing continuous rotation in one direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of it as a game of "tag" where the stator field is "it" — the rotor always moves to escape the field's grasp. The shorted brushes force the rotor to always have a like pole facing the stator, ensuring perpetual repulsion (and thus, rotation).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard parameters are essential for KTU 2024 numerical questions on the repulsion motor:

- **Supply Frequency:** Operates on standard single-phase AC, typically **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz**.
- **Number of Poles:** Usually a **2-pole** or **4-pole** configuration.
- **Starting Torque:** **3 to 4 times** the full-load torque (one of the highest among single-phase AC motors).
- **Starting Current:** **Low** (about 2 to 3 times full-load current), unlike AC series motors.
- **Power Factor:** Poor at light loads; improves at full load (typically **0.7 to 0.85 lagging**).
- **Speed Range:** Can be **infinitely varied** (like a DC series motor) by brush shifting.
- **Brush Position Angle (α):** Critical parameter; measured from the axis of the stator field (magnetic neutral axis) to the brush axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — The "α = 0" and "α = 90°" Boundary Conditions:** When the brush axis is aligned with the stator field axis (α = 0°), the motor behaves like a **transformer with shorted secondary** (no rotation). When α = 90°, the brushes are on the magnetic neutral axis, and again **no rotation** occurs. Maximum torque occurs at **α = 45°**.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Classification Snapshot

The repulsion motor family has three principal variants, all examinable under Module 4:

| Variant | Key Feature | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple (Plain) Repulsion Motor** | Two brushes only; α is fixed by mechanical design | Most commonly asked |
| **Compensated Repulsion Motor** | Additional compensating winding to improve commutation and P.F. | High-weightage topic |
| **Repulsion-Start Induction Motor (RSIM)** | Lifts brushes after starting; runs as induction motor | Frequently asked in Part B |
| **Repulsion Induction Motor (RIM)** | Continuous brush contact; double-cage rotor | Less frequent, but in syllabus |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 Module 4 questions, **75% of marks** in this topic come from the **Simple Repulsion Motor** (principle, torque, phasor diagram), while the remaining marks focus on the **Compensated** and **Repulsion-Start Induction Motor** types.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Repulsion Motor — Component-by-Component Breakdown

A repulsion motor consists of the following major parts. For KTU descriptive questions, you must be able to draw and label each:

### A. Stator (Field System)
- Laminated **silicon steel core** with **salient poles** (similar to a single-phase induction motor stator).
- Each pole carries a **field winding** connected in series with a single-phase AC supply.
- The two field windings are connected in **series-aiding** so that adjacent poles have **opposite polarity** (N–S–N–S).
- Produces a **pulsating (alternating) magnetic flux** along the direct axis (D-axis) of the stator.

### B. Rotor (Armature)
- **Laminated drum-type armature** carrying a **distributed DC-type winding** (like a DC machine armature).
- Wound in a closed loop (like a Gramme ring or drum winding) and connected to a **multi-segment commutator**.
- The rotor is **identical** to that of a DC machine.
- Carries a commutator, and is mounted on the same shaft as the rotor core.

### C. Brush Gear (The Heart of the Repulsion Motor)
- Two **carbon brushes** are mounted on a movable brush holder (rocker arm).
- The brushes are **connected by a thick, low-resistance copper strap** (i.e., they are **short-circuited**).
- The brush axis can be **rotated** around the commutator by a **brush-shifting mechanism** (hand-wheel + gear).
- The angle between the brush axis and the stator field axis is denoted as **α** (alpha).

> [!NOTE]
> **The brush-shifting lever is the single most important control element** of the repulsion motor. It is the KTU examiner's favorite component to ask about — be sure to draw it clearly.

---

## 2.2 Principle of Operation — The "Repulsion" Logic

The repulsion motor operates on the **transformer action** plus **Lenz's law of repulsion**:

### Step 1: Stator Field Establishment
- Single-phase AC supply is given to the stator field winding.
- A **pulsating alternating flux** Φ (varying sinusoidally with time) is set up along the direct axis (D-axis) of the stator.

### Step 2: Transformer Action in the Rotor
- This alternating flux links with the rotor winding.
- An EMF is induced in the rotor coils, exactly as in the secondary of a transformer.
- Since the brushes are **short-circuited**, a **large short-circuit current** flows in the rotor.

### Step 3: Rotor Flux Creation
- The rotor current produces its **own magnetic flux** (rotor flux, Φᵣ) at a position **perpendicular to the brush axis**.

### Step 4: Repulsion Force
- The stator flux (Φₛ) and rotor flux (Φᵣ) interact.
- At α = 0° (brushes on D-axis), the two fluxes are **in space quadrature** (90° apart), producing no net torque.
- At α = 45°, the stator and rotor fluxes are **optimally oriented**, producing **maximum torque**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU's "Why it Rotates" Answer:** Rotation occurs because the short-circuited brush axis forces the rotor to develop induced poles that are **always of the same polarity as the adjacent stator poles** (e.g., N–N and S–S facing each other), causing continuous **mutual repulsion** of like poles.

---

## 2.3 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values and parallel symbols use `\vert` or `\parallel` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T \propto \Phi \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | Fundamental torque equation | N·m | α = brush shift angle from MNA |
| 2 | $T_{max}$ at $\alpha = 45°$ | Maximum torque condition | N·m | $\sin(45°) = 1/\sqrt{2}$ |
| 3 | $T = 0$ at $\alpha = 0°$ or $90°$ | Zero-torque conditions | N·m | Brush on D-axis or Q-axis |
| 4 | $N \propto V / \Phi$ | Speed proportional to V/Φ | rpm | Like DC series motor |
| 5 | $E_b = k \cdot \Phi \cdot N$ | Back-EMF equation | Volts | $k$ = machine constant |
| 6 | $V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$ | Voltage equation (after transformation) | Volts | Rotor side referred |
| 7 | $\Phi_{rotor} = \Phi \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | Rotor flux component (perpendicular to stator field) | Wb | Max at α = 90° |
| 8 | $I_{rotor} \propto \Phi \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | Rotor current component | A | Max at α = 0° |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

The repulsion motor, despite being a "special machine," has niche but important applications:

1. **Household Refrigerators and Compressors (Historical):** High starting torque with low starting current made it ideal for compressor loads.
2. **Printing Presses and Lathes:** Where high starting torque and infinitely variable speed (via brush shifting) were required.
3. **Hoists, Cranes, and Elevators:** The high starting torque (3-4× FL) and series-motor-like characteristics (high torque at low speed) were exploited.
4. **Fans and Pumps (small ratings):** Speed regulation via brush shifting.
5. **Washing Machines (Older Designs):** Repulsion-start induction motors were used for the starting winding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Context:** With the advent of **VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives)** and **PMSM drives**, the repulsion motor has become largely obsolete. However, KTU 2024 syllabus retains it because it forms the **conceptual foundation** for understanding AC commutator machines in general.

---

## 2.5 Compensated Repulsion Motor — Brief Overview (For KTU)

The **simple repulsion motor** suffers from:
- **Poor commutation** (sparking at brushes, especially at high speeds).
- **Low power factor** at light loads.
- **Distorted armature reaction** (the rotor flux distorts the main field).

To overcome these, two **compensating windings** are added in the stator pole faces, in series with the field winding. The two main types are:

### Type 1: **Deri Repulsion Motor (Conductive Compensation)**
- A compensating winding is placed in the stator poles and **connected conductively** in series with the armature (via an extra pair of brushes called **conductive brushes**).
- The compensating winding neutralizes armature reaction.

### Type 2: **Latour Repulsion Motor (Inductive Compensation)**
- The compensating winding is **shorted on itself** (inductive compensation), forming a closed loop on the stator.
- The induced EMF in the compensating winding opposes the rotor MMF, providing compensation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The **conductive compensation (Deri motor)** is more effective but more complex. The **inductive compensation (Latour motor)** is simpler and is the more commonly asked variant in KTU 2024.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Torque Equation Using Double Revolving Field Theory

The most elegant KTU-board-approved method to derive the torque equation of a repulsion motor is the **double revolving field theory**, which treats the pulsating stator flux as equivalent to two counter-rotating fluxes of half amplitude.

### Assumptions (Standard for KTU Examinations)
1. The stator field is purely **sinusoidal** and **pulsating** along the D-axis.
2. The magnetic circuit is **linear** (no saturation).
3. Brush resistance is **negligible** compared to rotor winding resistance.
4. The rotor winding is **symmetrically distributed**.
5. The brush shift angle **α** is constant during steady-state analysis.

### Derivation — Step by Step

Let the stator (field) flux be:

$$\Phi_s(t) = \Phi_{max} \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

This pulsating flux is resolved into two counter-rotating components of magnitude $\Phi_{max}/2$ each:

$$\Phi_f = \frac{\Phi_{max}}{2} \cdot e^{j\omega t} \quad \text{(forward rotating component)}$$

$$\Phi_b = \frac{\Phi_{max}}{2} \cdot e^{-j\omega t} \quad \text{(backward rotating component)}$$

---

#### Step 1: Resolve the Rotor Current into Two Components

The rotor (armature) current $I_2$ is induced in the short-circuited brush circuit. We resolve $I_2$ into two perpendicular components:

- **Component along stator field axis (D-axis):** $I_2 \cos(\alpha)$ — this is the **magnetizing component** which produces flux.
- **Component perpendicular to stator field axis (Q-axis):** $I_2 \sin(\alpha)$ — this is the **torque-producing component**.

#### Step 2: Calculate the Rotor Flux

The rotor flux is produced by the component $I_2 \cos(\alpha)$ acting on the rotor magnetic circuit:

$$\Phi_r = k_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

where $k_1$ is a constant depending on rotor MMF per ampere.

#### Step 3: Express Torque Using the Cross-Product Principle

The torque is proportional to the **vector cross product** of the stator flux and the rotor MMF:

$$T = k_2 \cdot \Phi_s \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

#### Step 4: Substitute the Sinusoidal Time Variation

Including the time-domain pulsation:

$$T(t) = k_2 \cdot \Phi_{max} \cdot \cos(\omega t) \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

#### Step 5: Apply the Double Revolving Field Decomposition

Substituting $\cos(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}\right]$:

$$T(t) = k_2 \cdot \frac{\Phi_{max}}{2} \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha) \cdot \left[e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}\right]$$

#### Step 6: Decompose into Forward and Backward Torques

The two terms correspond to:

$$T_f = k_2 \cdot \frac{\Phi_{max}}{2} \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha) \cdot e^{j\omega t} \quad \text{(forward torque)}$$

$$T_b = k_2 \cdot \frac{\Phi_{max}}{2} \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha) \cdot e^{-j\omega t} \quad \text{(backward torque)}$$

#### Step 7: Compute the Average Torque

Since the forward and backward torques are equal in magnitude, the **average steady-state torque** is:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\left[T_f + T_b\right] = k_2 \cdot \frac{\Phi_{max}}{2} \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

$$\boxed{T_{avg} = K \cdot \Phi \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)}$$

where $K$ is the motor constant combining all proportionality factors.

---

### 3.1.1 Special Cases from the Torque Equation

| Case | Brush Position | $\sin(\alpha)$ | Resulting Torque | KTU Board Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha = 0°$ (brushes on D-axis) | $\sin(0) = 0$ | $T = 0$ | No rotation; motor acts as a transformer |
| 2 | $\alpha = 45°$ (mid-position) | $\sin(45°) = 1/\sqrt{2}$ | $T = T_{max}$ | **Maximum starting torque** — KTU favorite |
| 3 | $\alpha = 90°$ (brushes on Q-axis) | $\sin(90°) = 1$ | But $I_2 = 0$ → $T = 0$ | No rotation; rotor flux is maximum but no current |
| 4 | $\alpha = 30°$ | $\sin(30°) = 0.5$ | $T = 0.5 \cdot T_{max}$ | Half of maximum torque |
| 5 | $\alpha = 60°$ | $\sin(60°) = \sqrt{3}/2$ | $T \approx 0.866 \cdot T_{max}$ | Near-maximum torque |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write that maximum torque occurs at $\alpha = 90°$. **This is WRONG** — at $\alpha = 90°$, the rotor current is **zero** (no EMF induced in the shorted coils because they are in the magnetic neutral axis), so torque is zero. Maximum torque occurs at the **trade-off point** of $\alpha = 45°$.

---

## 3.2 Phasor Diagram of a Repulsion Motor

The phasor diagram is essential for KTU 2024 Board questions. Here is the standard construction:

### Construction Steps (Memorize This Sequence)
1. Take the **stator flux Φ** as the reference phasor, along the horizontal axis.
2. The **induced EMF in the rotor ($E_2$)** lags Φ by 90° (Lenz's law), so $E_2$ is drawn vertically downward.
3. The **rotor current $I_2$** lags $E_2$ by an angle $\theta_2$ (rotor impedance angle), where $\tan(\theta_2) = X_2 / R_2$.
4. The **stator current $I_s$** has two components: the magnetizing current $I_m$ (lagging Φ by 90°) and the load component.
5. The **applied voltage V** leads Φ by the no-load power factor angle.

### Phasor Relationships (KTU 2024 Standard)

$$E_2 = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_2 \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

The $\cos(\alpha)$ factor appears because only the flux component **linking the shorted coils** is effective in inducing the rotor EMF.

$$I_2 = \frac{E_2}{Z_2} = \frac{E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_2^2}}$$

$$\text{Power Input} = V \cdot I_s \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

$$\text{Power Converted} = E_2 \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos(\theta_2)$$

---

## 3.3 Speed Equation of a Repulsion Motor

From the DC series motor analogy, the speed is:

$$N = \frac{V - I_2 \cdot R_2}{k \cdot \Phi_{effective}}$$

Since $\Phi_{effective} \propto \Phi \cdot \sin(\alpha)$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_2 \cdot R_2}{k \cdot \Phi \cdot \sin(\alpha)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** As the load torque increases, $I_2$ increases, causing the speed to **drop sharply** (series motor characteristic). The brush shift angle α acts as the **speed control parameter** — increasing α reduces speed and vice versa.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Why α = 45° Gives Maximum Torque

This is a frequent KTU 2024 Part A question. Here is the rigorous justification:

Starting from:

$$T = K \cdot \Phi \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

The rotor current $I_2$ depends on the EMF, which in turn depends on the **flux component cutting the shorted coils**, which is $\Phi \cdot \cos(\alpha)$:

$$E_2 \propto \Phi \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

$$I_2 = \frac{E_2}{Z_2} \propto \frac{\Phi \cdot \cos(\alpha)}{Z_2}$$

Substituting back into the torque equation (and assuming $Z_2$ is approximately constant):

$$T \propto \Phi \cdot \left[\frac{\Phi \cdot \cos(\alpha)}{Z_2}\right] \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

$$T \propto \frac{\Phi^2}{Z_2} \cdot \sin(\alpha) \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

Using the trigonometric identity:

$$\sin(\alpha) \cdot \cos(\alpha) = \frac{1}{2}\sin(2\alpha)$$

$$T \propto \frac{\Phi^2}{2 Z_2} \cdot \sin(2\alpha)$$

**Maximum of $\sin(2\alpha)$ occurs at $2\alpha = 90°$, i.e., $\alpha = 45°$.**

$$\boxed{\alpha_{max} = 45° \implies T_{max} = \frac{\Phi^2}{2 Z_2} = \frac{\Phi^2}{2\sqrt{R_2^2 + X_2^2}}}$$

## 4.2 Functional State Machine — Operation Modes

This state machine depicts the **operating modes** of a repulsion motor as a function of brush shift angle α.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PowerOn
    PowerOn --> FluxBuild: Stator energized Phi_s established
    FluxBuild --> InduceEMF: Transformer action on rotor coils
    InduceEMF --> ShortCircuitCurrent: I_2 flows through brush path
    ShortCircuitCurrent --> PhiRBuildup: Rotor flux Phi_r established

    state CheckAlpha <<choice>>
    PhiRBuildup --> CheckAlpha: Evaluate brush position

    CheckAlpha --> ZeroTorque: alpha = 0 deg or 90 deg
    CheckAlpha --> MaxTorque: alpha = 45 deg
    CheckAlpha --> PartialTorque: alpha between 0 and 90 deg

    ZeroTorque --> NoRotation: T = 0 motor acts as transformer
    MaxTorque --> FullSpeedOperation: T_max = Phi^2 over 2 Z_2
    PartialTorque --> ReducedSpeed: Speed depends on sin alpha

    FullSpeedOperation --> LoadApplied
    ReducedSpeed --> LoadApplied
    LoadApplied --> BrushShift: Operator adjusts alpha
    BrushShift --> CheckAlpha
    NoRotation --> [*]
```

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Repulsion Motor. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A repulsion motor is a **single-phase AC commutator motor** in which:
1. The **armature is not electrically connected** to the supply — it receives power **inductively** through transformer action.
2. The **brushes are short-circuited** through a low-resistance copper strap.
3. The torque is produced by the **mutual repulsion** between the stator field and the induced rotor field.

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, mention all three points clearly. Many students lose 1 mark by omitting the "inductive (not conductive) supply" detail.

---

### Question 2: Why does maximum torque occur at α = 45° in a repulsion motor? [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The torque is given by $T = K \cdot \Phi \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)$. The rotor current $I_2$ is induced by the flux component $\Phi \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ linking the shorted coils. Substituting:
$T \propto \sin(\alpha) \cdot \cos(\alpha) = \frac{1}{2}\sin(2\alpha)$.
This expression is maximum when $2\alpha = 90°$, i.e., **$\alpha = 45°$**, giving $T_{max} = \Phi^2 / (2 Z_2)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: Part (a) [7 Marks] + Part (b) [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

#### Part (a): With a neat diagram, explain the construction and principle of operation of a simple repulsion motor. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A simple repulsion motor consists of:
- **Stator:** Laminated salient poles carrying single-phase AC field winding, connected to AC mains.
- **Rotor:** DC-type drum armature with multi-segment commutator, identical to a DC machine.
- **Brush Gear:** Two carbon brushes mounted on a movable brush holder and **short-circuited** by a thick copper braid.
- **Brush Shifting Mechanism:** Hand-wheel and gear arrangement to vary the angle **α** between the brush axis and the stator field axis.

**Principle of Operation (4 marks):**
1. Single-phase AC is applied to the stator field winding, producing a **pulsating alternating flux Φ** along the D-axis.
2. This flux links with the rotor winding and induces an **EMF $E_2$** in the rotor coils (transformer action).
3. The brushes being short-circuited allow a **large rotor current $I_2$** to flow.
4. The rotor current produces a flux $\Phi_r$ **perpendicular to the brush axis**.
5. The interaction of $\Phi_s$ and $\Phi_r$ produces a **torque $T = K \Phi I_2 \sin(\alpha)$** which rotates the rotor.
6. The direction of rotation is **opposite to the direction of brush shift** from the magnetic neutral axis.
7. **At α = 45°**, the torque is maximum; **at α = 0° or 90°**, the torque is zero.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Point 6:** Many students write that the direction of rotation is **the same as the brush shift direction**. This is **WRONG** and costs 1-2 marks. Always state the rule: *"Rotation is opposite to the direction of brush shift from the magnetic neutral axis."*

---

#### Part (b): Derive the torque equation of a repulsion motor and show that the maximum torque occurs at a brush shift angle of 45°. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

Refer to the complete derivation in SECTION 3.1 of these notes. Key steps to write:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Express stator flux as $\Phi_s = \Phi_{max} \cos(\omega t)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Use double revolving field theory to resolve into forward and backward components of amplitude $\Phi_{max}/2$.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Resolve rotor current $I_2$ into two perpendicular components:
- $I_2 \cos(\alpha)$ along stator field axis (magnetizing)
- $I_2 \sin(\alpha)$ perpendicular to stator field axis (torque-producing)

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Apply cross-product principle: $T \propto \Phi_s \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)$, substitute $\cos(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2}[e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}]$, and average over a cycle.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Arrive at $T_{avg} = K \Phi I_2 \sin(\alpha)$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Show that substituting $I_2 \propto \Phi \cos(\alpha) / Z_2$ gives $T \propto \sin(2\alpha)$, maximized at $\alpha = 45°$ with $T_{max} = \Phi^2 / (2 Z_2)$.

---

### Question B: Part (a) [7 Marks] + Part (b) [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

#### Part (a): Explain the compensated repulsion motor with diagrams. Compare the conductively compensated (Deri) and inductively compensated (Latour) types. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The simple repulsion motor suffers from poor commutation, low power factor, and armature reaction. **Compensated repulsion motors** add an auxiliary **compensating winding** in the stator pole faces to overcome these defects.

**Deri Repulsion Motor — Conductive Compensation (3 marks):**
- A compensating winding $W_c$ is placed in the stator pole faces.
- It is connected in **series** with the **armature** via two additional **conductive brushes** mounted on the commutator.
- The compensating winding MMF **opposes and neutralizes** the rotor armature MMF, eliminating armature reaction.
- The compensation is **complete and effective**, but the system is complex due to extra brushes.

**Latour Repulsion Motor — Inductive Compensation (3 marks):**
- The compensating winding is wound in the stator pole faces but is **shorted on itself** (no external connection).
- The flux from the rotor induces an EMF in the shorted compensating winding, which causes a current to flow that **opposes the rotor MMF** (Lenz's law).
- This provides **automatic inductive compensation** without extra brushes.
- Simpler construction but compensation is **partial** (depends on transformer ratio).

| Feature | Deri (Conductive) | Latour (Inductive) |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Series with armature | Shorted on itself |
| Extra brushes | Required | Not required |
| Effectiveness | Complete | Partial |
| Complexity | Higher | Lower |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Latour description:** A common error is to say the Latour compensating winding is "connected to the supply." It is **NOT**. It is **shorted on itself** to form a closed loop. State this explicitly for full marks.

---

#### Part (b): A 230 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole repulsion motor has the following parameters: rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.5 \, \Omega$ per phase referred, rotor reactance $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$ per phase referred. If the brush shift angle is 30° and the stator flux is 0.05 Wb, calculate: (i) rotor induced EMF, (ii) rotor current, (iii) average torque developed. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ poles
- $R_2 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$
- $\alpha = 30°$, $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb
- Assume $N_2 k_w = 100$ (typical machine constant for this problem)

**Step 1: Calculate Rotor Induced EMF (2 marks)**
The induced EMF in the rotor, considering the brush position, is:
$$E_2 = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_2 \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$
$$E_2 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 100 \times 0.05 \times \cos(30°)$$
$$E_2 = 4.44 \times 50 \times 100 \times 0.05 \times 0.866$$
$$E_2 = 961.41 \text{ V} \approx 961 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Rotor Current (2 marks)**
$$Z_2 = \sqrt{R_2^2 + X_2^2} = \sqrt{0.5^2 + 1.0^2} = \sqrt{1.25} = 1.118 \, \Omega$$
$$I_2 = \frac{E_2}{Z_2} = \frac{961.41}{1.118} = 859.9 \text{ A} \approx 860 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Average Torque (3 marks)**
Using $T = K \Phi I_2 \sin(\alpha)$ where $K = 1/(2\pi) \cdot (P/2)$ for a 4-pole machine in SI units (assuming $K = 30$ as the standard constant for this problem):
$$T = K \cdot \Phi \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$
$$T = 30 \times 0.05 \times 860 \times \sin(30°)$$
$$T = 30 \times 0.05 \times 860 \times 0.5$$
$$T = 645 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — EMF Equation Units:** Many students forget the $\cos(\alpha)$ factor in the EMF equation. KTU strictly penalizes this 1-mark deduction. Also, the value of $K$ in the torque equation depends on whether you express $T$ in **N·m** (SI units, $K \approx 30$ for $P=4$) or in some normalized form. Always check the units expected in the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Repulsion Motor**

- ✅ **Definition:** Single-phase AC commutator motor with **short-circuited brushes** and **inductively powered** armature.
- ✅ **Principle:** Mutual **repulsion** of like poles (stator vs. rotor), NOT attraction.
- ✅ **Construction:** Stator (laminated, salient poles, AC winding) + Rotor (DC-type armature + commutator) + Brush gear (movable, shorted) + Brush-shifting mechanism.
- ✅ **The Brush Shift Angle α:** Measured from the **magnetic neutral axis (MNA)** to the **brush axis**.
- ✅ **Zero Torque Conditions:** $\alpha = 0°$ (transformer action only) and $\alpha = 90°$ (no induced current).
- ✅ **Maximum Torque Condition:** $\alpha = 45°$, with $T_{max} = \Phi^2 / (2 Z_2)$.
- ✅ **Torque Equation:** $T = K \cdot \Phi \cdot I_2 \cdot \sin(\alpha)$.
- ✅ **Direction of Rotation:** **Opposite** to the direction of brush shift from MNA.
- ✅ **Starting Torque:** **3 to 4 times** full-load torque (very high).
- ✅ **Starting Current:** **Low** (2-3 times FL) — major advantage over AC series motor.
- ✅ **Speed Control:** **Brush shifting** is the primary method; also via series resistance.
- ✅ **Speed-Torque Characteristic:** **Series motor type** (high N at no-load, drops sharply with load).
- ✅ **Compensated Types:** **Deri (conductive)** — extra brushes, complete compensation. **Latour (inductive)** — shorted winding, partial compensation.
- ✅ **Repulsion-Start Induction Motor (RSIM):** Starts as repulsion motor, then a centrifugal device **short-circuits the commutator** and **lifts the brushes**; runs as induction motor.
- ✅ **Repulsion Induction Motor (RIM):** Has a **double-cage rotor** + commutator; brushes remain in contact; combines high starting torque with good running performance.
- ✅ **Real-World Equivalence:** Conceptually equivalent to a **DC series motor** but with **AC supply** and **inductive (not conductive) armature supply**.
- ✅ **KTU Trick Question Alert:** If asked "Why not a simple AC series motor?" — Answer: AC series motor has **conductive** armature supply (spark-prone commutator at AC frequencies), while repulsion motor has **inductive** supply (no such problem).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hysteresis Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hysteresis Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Hysteresis Motor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Hysteresis Motor** is a self-starting synchronous motor that operates on the principle of **hysteresis loss** occurring in a smooth, high-retentivity ferromagnetic rotor. The rotor, typically made of hardened chrome steel or cobalt steel, develops a constant **hysteresis torque** from standstill to synchronous speed due to the lag (hysteresis lag angle $\delta$) between rotor magnetization and the rotating stator field.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Hysteresis Motor is classified as a *self-starting synchronous motor* along with reluctance motors. It is unique because it has **no rotor windings, no rotor teeth, and no rotor slots** — it is a plain smooth cylinder of special magnetic material.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine a heavy flywheel (the smooth rotor) made of a "magnet-sticky" rubber. Imagine a rotating bar magnet (the stator field) spinning around it. Due to the rubber's "stickiness" (hysteresis), the magnetization of the flywheel always lags behind the magnet. This persistent lag creates a continuous "pulling" force, just like a tug-of-war where one side is always a fraction of a second behind the other — that behind-ness is what produces torque.

In simpler words — the **rotor cannot instantly magnetize and demagnetize** because the rotor material has high **coercivity** ($H_c$). This reluctance to change magnetization creates a *drag torque* that synchronizes the rotor to the rotating field.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Material Standards
| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Hysteresis constant ($\eta$) for Chrome Steel** | **0.025 to 0.07** |
| **Hysteresis constant ($\eta$) for Cobalt Steel (Alnico)** | **0.06 to 0.09** |
| **Residual Flux Density ($B_r$)** | **0.8 to 1.4 Tesla** |
| **Coercivity ($H_c$)** | **High (50 to 200 kA/m)** |
| **Steinmetz exponent ($n$)** | **1.6** (standard for most magnetic materials) |

> [!NOTE]
> The Steinmetz exponent of **1.6** appears in the empirical formula $P_h = \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V$, which is fundamental to the hysteresis torque derivation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Features

### 2.1.1 Stator
- Single-phase or three-phase laminated stator core with distributed winding (same as induction motor).
- For fractional-horsepower applications, **shaded-pole construction** is also used.
- The stator produces a **uniform rotating magnetic field** with constant magnitude.

### 2.1.2 Rotor (Most Critical Element)
- A **smooth cylindrical ring** of high-retentivity hardened steel (chrome steel or cobalt steel).
- **NO slots, NO teeth, NO windings, NO commutator** — purely a solid magnetic cylinder.
- Rotor is mounted on a non-magnetic hub (brass or aluminum) to prevent eddy current path formation in the shaft.
- Sometimes the rotor is **slitted axially** (slots cut longitudinally) to reduce eddy current losses.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Stator Energization**
When the stator is connected to a single-phase AC supply (through an auxiliary winding or capacitor), a **rotating magnetic field** of constant magnitude $B_s$ is produced, rotating at synchronous speed $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$.

**Step 2: Rotor Magnetization**
As the rotating field sweeps across the rotor, the rotor material is subjected to cyclic magnetization. Each elemental volume of the rotor undergoes one complete cycle (from $+B_m$ to $-B_m$ and back) per electrical cycle.

**Step 3: Hysteresis Lag**
Due to the **high coercivity** of rotor material, the rotor flux density $B_r$ lags behind the stator field $B_s$ by an angle $\delta$, called the **hysteresis lag angle**.

**Step 4: Torque Production**
The interaction between the lagging rotor field and the stator field produces a tangential force, generating **hysteresis torque** $T_h$.

**Step 5: Acceleration**
The rotor accelerates due to this constant torque from standstill. Importantly, the hysteresis torque is **independent of speed** — it remains constant from $N = 0$ to $N = N_s$.

**Step 6: Synchronous Operation**
When the rotor reaches synchronous speed, the magnetization pattern "freezes" and rotor poles lock with the stator field. The motor now runs at synchronous speed with **no slip** and **no hysteresis loss** in operation (only during acceleration).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | RPM |
| 2 | $T_h = \frac{P \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot B_m^{1.6}}{2\pi}$ | Hysteresis torque from energy balance | N·m |
| 3 | $P_h = \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V$ | Hysteresis power loss | Watts |
| 4 | $T_h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot \phi \cdot F \cdot \sin\delta$ | Hysteresis torque from field interaction | N·m |
| 5 | $T_h = K \cdot V \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot f$ | Compact form (K depends on P and $\eta$) | N·m |
| 6 | $\delta = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \cdot \eta \cdot B_m^{0.6}}{k_1}\right)$ | Hysteresis lag angle approximation | radians |
| 7 | $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi f}{P}$ | Synchronous angular speed | rad/s |
| 8 | $B_m^{1.6} \propto B_m^2$ (approx) | At high saturation | Tesla |
| 9 | $\eta = $ Steinmetz hysteresis constant | Material-dependent | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Worthy Insight:** In hysteresis motor derivations, replace $\vert B_m \vert$ expressions with $B_m$ (assume positive). NEVER use the pipe symbol — use $\vert B_m \vert$ only in plain text, NOT in markdown tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Applications
- **Phonograph turntables** (constant torque for smooth playback).
- **Electric clocks and timing devices** (precise synchronous speed).
- **Tape recorders and capstan drives** (vibration-free operation).
- **Gyroscopes and instrument servos** (smooth low-speed operation).
- **Aerospace and defence** equipment (high reliability, low maintenance).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hysteresis Torque from Energy Balance

### Statement
Prove that the hysteresis torque developed by a hysteresis motor is constant from standstill to synchronous speed and is given by $T_h = \frac{P \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot B_m^{1.6}}{2\pi}$.

### Derivation

Let us denote:
- $V$ = Volume of the rotor core (m³)
- $\eta$ = Steinmetz hysteresis constant (dimensionless)
- $B_m$ = Maximum flux density in rotor (Tesla)
- $f$ = Supply frequency (Hz)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $n_s$ = Synchronous speed (RPS) = $f/P$

**Step 1: Hysteresis Loss per Cycle per Unit Volume**

By the Steinmetz empirical formula, the hysteresis loss per unit volume per cycle is:

$$W_h = \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \quad \text{(J/m³/cycle)}$$

This represents the energy dissipated as heat per cubic meter of rotor material per electrical cycle.

**Step 2: Total Hysteresis Loss per Second (Power Loss)**

When the rotor is running at a speed of $n$ revolutions per second, the rotor magnetic field completes $P \cdot n$ reversals per second. Therefore, the electrical cycling frequency experienced by each particle is:

$$f_{eff} = P \cdot n$$

The total hysteresis power loss (which equals the mechanical power developed) is:

$$P_h = V \cdot W_h \cdot f_{eff} = V \cdot \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot P \cdot n$$

**Step 3: Equate to Mechanical Power Output**

The mechanical power developed equals the hysteresis torque times the angular velocity:

$$P_m = T_h \cdot \omega_r$$

where $\omega_r = 2\pi n$ (mechanical angular velocity).

Since all the hysteresis input power is converted to mechanical output (negligible other losses during the analysis):

$$T_h \cdot (2\pi n) = V \cdot \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot P \cdot n$$

**Step 4: Simplification (Cancellation of $n$)**

Dividing both sides by $n$:

$$T_h \cdot 2\pi = V \cdot \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot P$$

**Step 5: Final Expression for Hysteresis Torque**

$$\boxed{T_h = \frac{P \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot B_m^{1.6}}{2\pi}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** The speed $n$ cancels out completely! This proves that the hysteresis torque is **independent of rotor speed** — it is the same at standstill, at half synchronous speed, and at synchronous speed. This is the **hallmark characteristic** of hysteresis motors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hysteresis Torque from Hysteresis Lag Angle

### Statement
Derive the expression for hysteresis torque using the angle $\delta$ between rotor and stator MMF axes.

### Derivation

Consider a 2-pole model for clarity. The stator MMF $F$ produces a flux $\phi$. The rotor, due to hysteresis, retains magnetization at an angle $\delta$ behind the stator axis.

**Step 1: Energy Stored in Magnetic Field**

The energy stored in the magnetic field per electrical cycle per unit volume, considering hysteresis loop area, is:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot F \cdot \phi \cdot \sin\delta$$

This represents the area enclosed in the $B$-$H$ loop (hysteresis loop) for the rotor material.

**Step 2: For Multi-Pole Configuration**

For a motor with $P$ poles, the energy expression per cycle per pole is multiplied by $P/2$:

$$W_{total} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot F \cdot \phi \cdot \sin\delta = \frac{P}{4} \cdot F \cdot \phi \cdot \sin\delta$$

**Step 3: Power Dissipated Per Cycle**

At any rotor speed $n$ rev/sec, the power developed is:

$$P_m = W_{total} \cdot P \cdot n = \frac{P^2}{4} \cdot F \cdot \phi \cdot \sin\delta \cdot n$$

**Step 4: Torque from Power Equation**

Using $P_m = T_h \cdot 2\pi n$:

$$T_h \cdot 2\pi n = \frac{P^2}{4} \cdot F \cdot \phi \cdot \sin\delta \cdot n$$

**Step 5: Final Form**

$$\boxed{T_h = \frac{P^2 \cdot F \cdot \phi \cdot \sin\delta}{8\pi} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot \phi \cdot F \cdot \sin\delta \cdot \left(\frac{P}{4\pi}\right)}$$

The simplified form (taking a generalized coefficient) is:

$$\boxed{T_h = K \cdot P \cdot \phi \cdot F \cdot \sin\delta}$$

where $K$ is a constant depending on geometry.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Hysteresis Motor | Reluctance Motor | Induction Motor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Starting torque** | Constant at all speeds | High at low slip, drops at sync | Moderate, drops at slip = 0 |
| **Synchronous operation?** | Yes | Yes | No (always has slip) |
| **Rotor type** | Smooth cylinder (no slots) | Salient poles (slots) | Squirrel cage / wound |
| **Self-starting?** | Yes | Yes | Yes (but asynchronous) |
| **Torque at standstill** | $T_h$ (constant) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Construction complexity** | Simple rotor | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Noise level** | Very low | Low | Moderate (slot harmonics) |
| **Efficiency** | Lower (hysteresis loss) | Higher | Higher |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Q:** Define a Hysteresis Motor. Mention any two of its salient features.

**Model Answer:**
A Hysteresis Motor is a self-starting synchronous AC motor that operates on the principle of hysteresis loss in its rotor. The rotor is a smooth cylinder made of high-retentivity material like hardened chrome steel.

**Salient features:**
1. It has a smooth cylindrical rotor with no teeth, slots, or windings.
2. It produces a constant torque from standstill to synchronous speed.
3. It runs at synchronous speed without slip and is virtually noiseless.

*(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Q:** Why is the rotor of a hysteresis motor made of high retentivity steel? What is the role of the hysteresis lag angle $\delta$?

**Model Answer:**
The rotor is made of high retentivity steel (chrome steel or cobalt steel) to ensure that the magnetic flux established in the rotor does not collapse quickly. This **delays the demagnetization** when the stator field rotates away.

**Role of $\delta$:** The hysteresis lag angle $\delta$ is the angle by which the rotor flux lags behind the stator MMF axis. This lag creates a tangential force component $F \sin\delta$, which is responsible for the hysteresis torque. Without $\delta$, no torque would be developed.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q:** With a neat constructional sketch, explain the working principle of a single-phase Hysteresis Motor. Derive the expression for the hysteresis torque developed by the motor. **(14 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Construction and Working Principle (7 Marks)

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** It consists of a laminated silicon steel core with a single-phase main winding and an auxiliary winding (with a capacitor in series) to produce a rotating magnetic field. For very small motors, a shaded-pole construction is used.
- **Rotor:** A smooth cylindrical ring of hardened chrome steel or cobalt steel is mounted on a shaft via a non-magnetic hub (brass). The rotor has **no slots, no teeth, and no windings**.

**Working Principle:**
1. When the stator is energized, a rotating magnetic field of constant magnitude is set up, rotating at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$.
2. This rotating field magnetizes the rotor. Since the rotor material has high retentivity, the magnetic axes in the rotor are delayed with respect to the stator field.
3. This delay is the **hysteresis lag angle $\delta$**, which is fixed (typically 30° to 40°) and depends on the rotor material.
4. The interaction between the stator MMF $F$ and rotor flux $\phi_r$ produces a tangential force proportional to $\sin\delta$, resulting in a torque $T_h$.
5. The rotor accelerates uniformly because $T_h$ is independent of speed.
6. At synchronous speed, the rotor poles lock with the stator field, and the motor continues to run at synchronous speed without slip.

#### (b) Derivation of Hysteresis Torque (7 Marks)

The Steinmetz hysteresis loss per cycle per unit volume of rotor material is:

$$W_h = \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \quad \text{(J/m³/cycle)}$$

For a rotor of volume $V$ running at $n$ rev/sec, the number of magnetic reversals per second is $P \cdot n$. Thus, total hysteresis power loss is:

$$P_h = V \cdot \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot P \cdot n$$

Equating this to mechanical power developed:

$$T_h \cdot \omega_r = V \cdot \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot P \cdot n$$

Since $\omega_r = 2\pi n$:

$$T_h \cdot 2\pi n = V \cdot \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot P \cdot n$$

Cancelling $n$ from both sides:

$$T_h \cdot 2\pi = V \cdot \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot P$$

$$\boxed{T_h = \frac{P \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot B_m^{1.6}}{2\pi}}$$

**Key Inference:** Since $n$ cancels out, $T_h$ is independent of speed, confirming constant torque from standstill to synchronous speed.

*(14 marks)*

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2022]**

**Q:** Compare the Hysteresis Motor with the Reluctance Motor. Derive the expression for hysteresis power loss and explain why the hysteresis motor develops constant torque. **(14 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| Feature | Hysteresis Motor | Reluctance Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor material | Hardened steel (chrome/cobalt) | Salient-pole ferromagnetic steel |
| Rotor construction | Smooth cylindrical ring | Salient poles with slots/teeth |
| Starting torque | Constant at all speeds | Variable, depends on reluctance path |
| Operating speed | Synchronous | Synchronous |
| Hysteresis loss | High (used for torque) | Low (undesirable) |
| Noise level | Very low | Slightly higher due to saliency |
| Applications | Phonographs, clocks, tape recorders | Pumps, fans, compressors |

#### (b) Hysteresis Power Loss and Constant Torque (7 Marks)

**Derivation of Hysteresis Power Loss:**

The Steinmetz formula for hysteresis loss per cycle per unit volume is:

$$P_{h,vol} = \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \quad \text{(J/m³/cycle)}$$

For a rotor of volume $V$ magnetized at frequency $f$:

$$\boxed{P_h = \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V \quad \text{(Watts)}}$$

**Why Constant Torque?**

Mechanical power developed = Hysteresis input power

$$T_h \cdot \omega_s = P_h = \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V$$

Since $f = \frac{P \cdot \omega_s}{2\pi}$:

$$T_h = \frac{\eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot V \cdot P}{2\pi}$$

The expression contains **no speed variable** ($n$ or $\omega$), proving $T_h$ is constant. This is because:
- The energy dissipated per cycle is fixed by the area of the B-H loop.
- Each revolution contributes a fixed amount of energy from the rotating field.
- The torque required to absorb this energy is therefore constant regardless of how fast the rotor is spinning.

*(14 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Points (where KTU examiners slash marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to cancel $n$ in the derivation (–2 marks):** Students often leave $n$ in the final expression of $T_h$. Always show the cancellation step explicitly.
>
> 2. **Confusing hysteresis constant $\eta$ with efficiency:** The symbol $\eta$ in Steinmetz formula is NOT efficiency. It is the **Steinmetz hysteresis constant** (dimensionless, 0.025 to 0.09).
>
> 3. **Wrong Steinmetz exponent:** Always use **1.6** for the exponent of $B_m$, not 2.0 (that is for eddy current).
>
> 4. **Forgetting non-magnetic hub:** Examiners often ask "why is the rotor mounted on a non-magnetic hub?" — answer: **to prevent eddy current circulation through the shaft**, which would otherwise cause additional losses and weaken rotor magnetization.
>
> 5. **Missing rotor slots/saliency mention:** If asked "why does the rotor have no slots?", answer: **smooth rotor ensures uniform air gap flux, reducing cogging torque and noise.**
>
> 6. **Unit mistakes:** Steinmetz $\eta$ is dimensionless, $B_m$ is in Tesla, $V$ in m³, $f$ in Hz.

---

## 5.4 Numerical Practice Problem

**Problem:** A 4-pole, 50 Hz hysteresis motor has a rotor volume of $3 \times 10^{-4}$ m³. The maximum flux density is 1.2 T, and the Steinmetz hysteresis constant is 0.05. Calculate:
1. The synchronous speed
2. The hysteresis torque developed

**Solution:**

**Given:** $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz, $V = 3 \times 10^{-4}$ m³, $B_m = 1.2$ T, $\eta = 0.05$

**(1) Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

**(2) Hysteresis Torque:**

$$T_h = \frac{P \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot B_m^{1.6}}{2\pi}$$

First, compute $B_m^{1.6} = (1.2)^{1.6}$

$$\ln(1.2)^{1.6} = 1.6 \times \ln(1.2) = 1.6 \times 0.1823 = 0.2917$$

$$B_m^{1.6} = e^{0.2917} = 1.3387 \text{ T}^{1.6}$$

Now substitute:

$$T_h = \frac{4 \times 0.05 \times (3 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1.3387}{2\pi}$$

$$T_h = \frac{8.032 \times 10^{-5}}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{T_h \approx 1.278 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N·m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The hysteresis torque is very small in this calculation because the rotor volume is small. In real applications, the torque is higher due to larger rotor volumes and better magnetic materials.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** Self-starting synchronous motor based on hysteresis loss in smooth rotor.
- **Rotor material:** Hardened chrome steel (preferred) or cobalt steel (alnico).
- **Rotor features:** NO slots, NO teeth, NO windings, NO commutator.
- **Non-magnetic hub:** Brass or aluminum between rotor and shaft to prevent eddy currents.
- **Hysteresis constant $\eta$:**
  - Chrome steel: **0.025 to 0.07**
  - Cobalt steel: **0.06 to 0.09**
- **Steinmetz exponent:** **1.6** (always).
- **Hysteresis loss formula:** $P_h = \eta \cdot B_m^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V$ (Watts).
- **Hysteresis torque formula:** $T_h = \dfrac{P \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot B_m^{1.6}}{2\pi}$ (N·m).
- **Speed independence:** $T_h$ is constant from standstill to synchronous speed (key characteristic).
- **Hysteresis lag angle $\delta$:** Angle between rotor flux and stator MMF (typically 30° to 40°).
- **Hysteresis torque (alternate form):** $T_h = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot \phi \cdot F \cdot \sin\delta$.
- **At synchronous speed:** Rotor poles lock with stator field; no slip; no further hysteresis loss.
- **Advantages:** Smooth operation, constant torque, low noise, self-starting, high reliability.
- **Disadvantages:** Low efficiency (due to continuous hysteresis), low starting power factor, expensive rotor material.
- **Applications:** Phonographs, electric clocks, tape recorders, capstan drives, gyroscopes, precision instruments.
- **Synchronous speed formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ RPM.
- **Comparison with Reluctance Motor:** Hysteresis has smooth rotor and constant torque; Reluctance has salient poles and variable torque.

### Quick Comparison Mnemonics

- **"H = Hard steel + High retentivity + lag = torque"** — for Hysteresis Motor.
- **"Poles produce Pull"** — $T_h \propto P$.
- **"Volume very Vital"** — $T_h \propto V$.
- **"Frequency feeds the loss"** — $P_h \propto f$.
- **"1.6 is the magic number"** — Steinmetz exponent.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** If you remember only one formula for the exam, remember $T_h = \dfrac{P \cdot \eta \cdot V \cdot B_m^{1.6}}{2\pi}$. This single equation, with proper cancellation of speed $n$, answers 80% of all hysteresis motor numerical and derivation questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Universal Motor - Construction - Principle of Operation - Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET743 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Universal Motor - Construction - Principle of Operation - Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Universal Motor** is a special class of commutator-type series-wound DC machine that is specially designed and compensated to operate satisfactorily on **both single-phase AC supply and DC supply** with approximately the same speed and output characteristics. It belongs to the family of *single-phase AC commutator motors* and is essentially a **fractional-kilowatt (sub-1 kW)** machine used extensively in domestic appliances and portable tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A single-phase series motor, when constructed with a fully laminated magnetic circuit and provided with a compensating winding, is called a *Universal Motor*. It can run on AC or DC with similar performance characteristics.

Mathematically, a universal motor can be classified by its governing torque equation:

$$T_{avg} = k \cdot I_a^2 \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

where $\phi$ is the angle between the armature current and the resultant air-gap flux.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Chameleon Motor":**
> Imagine a DC series motor — that is, a motor where the field winding is connected in *series* with the armature so that the same current flows through both. Now, ordinary DC series motors fail on AC because the alternating flux induces huge eddy currents in the solid iron core (causing overheating) and creates severe commutation problems. To make this "chameleon" work on AC too, engineers make **two key surgical changes**:
> 1. They **slice the iron core into thin laminations** (like a stack of paper-thin sheets) to block the eddy-current loops.
> 2. They add a **compensating winding** (a hidden auxiliary coil embedded in the pole faces) to cancel the bad reactance voltage caused by transformer action between field and armature.
>
> Once these two changes are made, the same motor happily runs on either DC or AC — hence the name **Universal** (works everywhere).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard specifications are characteristic of a typical universal motor:

- **Rated Power:** **50 W to 1.5 kW** (fractional kW class)
- **Rated Speed:** **3,000 – 15,000 rpm** (very high speed)
- **Supply Frequency:** **DC or 50 Hz single-phase AC** (also works on 60 Hz / 400 Hz in aircraft applications)
- **Number of Poles:** Usually **2-pole** construction
- **Typical Efficiency:** **30 % – 70 %** (lower than induction motors due to commutator/brush losses)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** Remember the **"L-C-F"** triad for universal motor design modifications — **L**aminations, **C**ompensating winding, and **F**ewer turns in series field (to reduce reactance).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of Universal Motor — Component-Level Breakdown

The universal motor is structurally a **commutator motor** with two sub-assemblies: a *stator (field system)* and a *rotor (armature)*.

### 2.1.1 Stator (Field System)
- **Laminated Pole Cores:** Made of **high-grade silicon steel laminations** (0.35 mm to 0.5 mm thickness) stacked and riveted together. This is *crucial* to reduce eddy-current losses under AC operation.
- **Field Windings (Series Field):** Wound around the poles and connected in **series** with the armature. Usually only **2 poles** for fractional kW ratings.
- **Compensating Winding:** Embedded in the pole shoes (slots cut into the pole faces) and connected in series with the armature. It is wound such that its **MMF opposes the armature reaction MMF**, thereby improving commutation under AC.
- **Yoke (Frame):** Made of cast iron or pressed steel, providing the return magnetic path.

### 2.1.2 Rotor (Armature)
- **Armature Core:** Also **laminated** (unlike a conventional DC motor's solid core) to suppress eddy currents during AC operation.
- **Armature Winding:** A conventional **lap or wave wound** DC armature winding placed in slots.
- **Commutator:** A cylindrical assembly of hard-drawn copper segments insulated by mica. The brushes ride on this.
- **Carbon Brushes:** Usually 2 brushes, mounted in brush holders with constant-pressure springs. Positioned at the **magnetic neutral axis (MNA)**, which is often *shifted backward* under load to improve commutation.

### 2.1.3 Two Broad Construction Variants
1. **Concentrated-Pole (Non-Compensated) Type:** No compensating winding; cheap; used in low-power (<100 W) appliances like table fans and sewing machines.
2. **Distributed-Field (Compensated) Type:** Has compensating winding; used for higher-power applications like portable drills, mixers, polishers, etc.

## 2.2 Principle of Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Operation on DC Supply
1. When DC is applied, current flows through the field winding and armature in series.
2. The field winding produces a **stationary magnetic flux** ($\phi$) in the air gap.
3. The armature, carrying this current in the magnetic field, experiences a **force** $F = BIL$ on each conductor.
4. By **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**, the armature rotates. Torque $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a$.

### 2.2.2 Operation on AC Supply
1. AC current reverses direction **every half-cycle**. Since both the field and armature windings are in **series**, the current reverses in *both* simultaneously.
2. Because torque depends on the *product* of flux and current ($T \propto \phi I_a$), and both reverse together, the torque direction remains **unidirectional**.
3. Hence, the motor continues to rotate in the same direction on AC — the **principle of the series motor on AC** is identical to its DC behavior.
4. The laminations and compensating winding handle the AC-specific issues (eddy currents and armature reaction reactance).

### 2.2.3 The Three Critical AC-Specific Issues
> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Eddy-Current Losses in Iron:** AC flux induces circulating currents in solid cores → solved by **laminating** the entire magnetic circuit (both stator and rotor).
> 2. **Excessive Series-Field Reactance:** AC field winding has high $X_L = 2\pi f L$ which limits current and reduces torque/power factor → solved by **reducing the number of field-winding turns** and using a low-reluctance magnetic path.
> 3. **Poor Commutation Due to Transformer EMF:** AC flux linking the commutating coils induces a high transformer voltage that arcs at the brushes → solved by **compensating winding** and **interpoles** in some designs.

## 2.3 Speed Control Methods

Universal motors are designed for **wide-range speed control**, which is one of their biggest commercial advantages.

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Resistance (Rheostatic) Control** | Series resistance reduces voltage across motor | Cheap fans, toys |
| **Tapped Field Winding** | Tapping the field winding alters field ampere-turns | Small universal fans |
| **Autotransformer / Variac** | Variable AC voltage applied to motor | Heavy-duty tools, mixers |
| **Centrifugal Governor (Mechanical)** | Disconnects/shorts an auxiliary winding at high speed | Older mixer-grinders |
| **Electronic Phase-Angle Control (TRIAC/DIAC)** | Varies the firing angle of a TRIAC to chop AC waveform | Modern blender, hand mixer, drill machines |
| **PWM Control (Modern)** | High-frequency switching with MOSFET/IGBT | High-end power tools, vacuum cleaners |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations for any KTU 2024 university examination on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Back EMF (same as DC) | $E_b = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60\,A}$ | $Z$=total conductors, $N$=speed in rpm, $P$=poles, $A$=parallel paths |
| 2 | Armature current (DC) | $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a + R_{se}}$ | $R_{se}$ = series field resistance |
| 3 | Torque (DC, series) | $T = k\, \phi I_a$ | Linear in flux and current |
| 4 | Average Torque (AC, series) | $T_{avg} = k\, \phi I_a \cos(\phi)$ | $\phi$ = phase angle between $\Phi$ and $I_a$ |
| 5 | Power Developed (Mechanical) | $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Air-gap power in watts |
| 6 | AC Input Power | $P_{in} = V \cdot I_a \cos(\phi)$ | Single-phase apparent × power factor |
| 7 | Speed Equation (DC) | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \phi}$ | Useful for speed-torque analysis |
| 8 | Induced Transformer EMF in Coil | $E_t = 4.44\, f\, N_{coil}\, \phi_{max}$ | Important for commutation design |
| 9 | Reactance of Series Field | $X_{se} = 2\pi f L_{se}$ | Must be kept low to maintain power factor |
| 10 | Speed Ratio (AC to DC at same load) | $\dfrac{N_{AC}}{N_{DC}} \approx \dfrac{\cos(\phi)}{1}$ | AC speed is slightly lower due to reactance drop |

**Important boundary conditions to remember:**
- At **no-load**, $\phi \to 0$ ⇒ $N \to \infty$ (dangerously high). Universal motors are **never started without load** — they will self-destruct.
- For stable operation, **load torque must increase with speed** (e.g., fan-blade load $T \propto N^2$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Torque of a Universal Motor on AC Supply

**Objective:** To derive an expression for the average torque developed by a universal motor when fed from a single-phase AC source.

### Step 1 — Define the Instantaneous Supply Voltage
Let the AC supply be expressed as:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

where $V_m$ is the peak voltage and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency.

### Step 2 — Express the Instantaneous Armature Current
Since the field and armature are in series, the same current flows through both. Neglecting saturation and assuming a low-reluctance magnetic circuit, the instantaneous current lags the voltage by a small phase angle $\phi$ (the motor's internal power-factor angle):

$$i_a(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

### Step 3 — Express the Instantaneous Air-Gap Flux
The flux is produced by the series field winding, which carries $i_a(t)$. Assuming a linear magnetic circuit, the flux is proportional to the current and reverses with it:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

where $\phi_m$ is the peak air-gap flux proportional to $I_m$.

### Step 4 — Write the Instantaneous Torque Equation
For a DC series motor, $T(t) \propto \phi(t) \cdot i_a(t)$. The same physical law applies instantaneously in a universal motor:

$$T(t) = k \cdot \phi(t) \cdot i_a(t)$$

where $k$ is the machine constant depending on construction (poles, conductors, etc.).

### Step 5 — Substitute the Time-Varying Quantities
Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 3:

$$T(t) = k \cdot \phi_m \sin(\omega t - \phi) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$T(t) = k \cdot \phi_m I_m \sin^2(\omega t - \phi)$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Trigonometric Identity
Using the identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$T(t) = \dfrac{k \cdot \phi_m I_m}{2}\left[1 - \cos\bigl(2(\omega t - \phi)\bigr)\right]$$

$$T(t) = \dfrac{k \cdot \phi_m I_m}{2} - \dfrac{k \cdot \phi_m I_m}{2}\cos\bigl(2\omega t - 2\phi\bigr)$$

### Step 7 — Average Over One Complete Cycle
The second term is a cosine wave of frequency $2f$ which has a **zero average** over one full cycle. Therefore:

$$T_{avg} = \dfrac{k \cdot \phi_m I_m}{2}$$

### Step 8 — Express in RMS and Phase-Angle Form
Using $I_m = \sqrt{2}\, I_{rms}$ and $\phi_m = \sqrt{2}\, \Phi_{rms}$ (sinusoidal assumption):

$$T_{avg} = \dfrac{k \cdot (\sqrt{2}\,\Phi_{rms})(\sqrt{2}\,I_{rms})}{2} = k\, \Phi_{rms}\, I_{rms}$$

However, since the flux lags the current by the same angle $\phi$ (due to magnetic circuit characteristics), the rigorous form preserving the power-factor angle is:

$$\boxed{\,T_{avg} = k\, \phi_{rms}\, I_{rms} \cos(\phi)\,}$$

This is the **governing average-torque equation** of a universal motor on AC supply, and it is the **single most important equation** for any KTU Part B numerical problem on this topic.

### Step 9 — Comparative Insight: AC vs DC Torque
For **DC operation** at the same RMS current, the average torque would be:

$$T_{avg,\,DC} = k\, \phi_{DC}\, I_{DC}$$

Since $\phi \propto I$ in a series motor, the torque on DC behaves as $T_{DC} \propto I^2$. On AC, because the flux and current oscillate but the product averages out, the **quadratic relationship is preserved** in terms of RMS values, but reduced by the factor $\cos(\phi)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The factor $\cos(\phi)$ — where $\phi$ is typically 20°–35° in a well-designed universal motor — means the AC torque is roughly 82 %–94 % of the DC torque at the same RMS current. This small deficit is precisely why the universal motor is *almost* identical in performance on AC and DC.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: AC vs DC Operation of the Same Universal Motor

The following table contrasts the same physical machine under the two supply modes, which is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question** (often phrased as *"Compare the performance of a universal motor on AC and DC supply"*).

| Performance Parameter | On DC Supply | On AC Supply | Reason for Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Factor** | Unity ($\cos\phi = 1$) | **Lagging, 0.8 – 0.95** | Reactance of series field winding |
| **Speed at same load** | Higher (baseline) | **5 % – 10 % lower** | Reactive drop reduces effective voltage |
| **Torque at same $I_{rms}$** | $T_{DC}$ (reference) | $T_{AC} \approx 0.85\, T_{DC}$ | $\cos(\phi)$ reduction factor |
| **Commutation** | Good (resistance commutation) | **Poor** | Transformer EMF in commutating coils |
| **Efficiency** | Higher | Slightly lower | Additional iron losses (hysteresis + eddy) |
| **Starting Current** | Very high (15–20× rated) | Similarly high | Both have no back EMF at start |
| **Noise** | Quiet | **Slightly noisy** | Magnetostriction + 100 Hz torque ripple |
| **Cooling Requirement** | Same | Slightly more | Higher iron losses in AC mode |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A 230 V, 50 Hz, universal motor takes 5 A (RMS) and develops a torque of 2.5 N·m. The power factor is 0.85 lagging. Calculate (a) the input power, (b) the apparent power, and (c) the power developed mechanically.

**Solution:**

**(a) Input power (real):**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

$$P_{in} = 230 \times 5 \times 0.85 = 977.5 \text{ W}$$

**(b) Apparent power:**

$$S = V \cdot I = 230 \times 5 = 1150 \text{ VA}$$

**(c) Mechanical power developed (using torque and speed relation):**

$$P_m = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$$

We need the speed. From the torque equation: $T = k \phi I \cos\phi$, and using $P_m = E_b \cdot I$, we get:

$$E_b = \dfrac{P_m}{I} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60\, I}$$

This illustrates the iterative nature of speed calculation — KTU typically provides speed directly in numerical questions to avoid this. **Practice deriving speed using $N = \dfrac{V\cos\phi - I(R_a+R_{se})}{k\phi}$ for full-marks credit.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since the universal motor's physical cross-section is too complex to render cleanly in a vector diagram, the following **Mermaid block-level functional architecture** is provided, mapping the *electrical interconnection* and *functional role* of every component. This is the **schematic representation** most examiners expect in KTU answer scripts.

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Universal Motor

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[AC OR DC SINGLE PHASE SUPPLY] --> nodeB[SUPPLY TERMINALS L AND N]
    nodeB --> nodeC[COMPENSATING WINDING CW]
    nodeC --> nodeD[SERIES FIELD WINDING SF]
    nodeD --> nodeE[ARMATURE WINDING AW]
    nodeE --> nodeF[COMMUTATOR C]
    nodeF --> nodeG[CARBON BRUSHES B1 AND B2]
    nodeG --> nodeH[BACK TO SUPPLY NEUTRAL N]
    nodeF --> nodeI[MECHANICAL OUTPUT SHAFT]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[LOAD FAN OR DRILL OR MIXER]
    nodeK[LAMINATED STATOR CORE POLE CORES 2 POLE] -.-> nodeD
    nodeL[LAMINATED ROTOR ARMATURE CORE] -.-> nodeE
    nodeM[COMPENSATING WINDING EMBEDDED IN POLE SHOES] -.-> nodeC
```

## 4.2 Textual Description of the Diagram (For Answer Scripts)

When drawing in an examination, reproduce the following labelled cross-section:

**Components (numbered for easy labelling):**

1. **Stator Yoke (Outer Frame)** — Cast iron cylindrical shell providing the return flux path.
2. **Pole Cores (2 in number, laminated)** — Project inward from the yoke; carry the field winding.
3. **Pole Shoes (Laminated with slots)** — Spread the flux uniformly in the air gap and house the compensating winding.
4. **Series Field Winding SF** — Wound around the pole cores; connected in series with the armature.
5. **Compensating Winding CW** — Placed in the slots of pole shoes, in series with armature, MMF opposing armature reaction.
6. **Armature Core (Laminated drum type)** — Rotates inside the stator bore.
7. **Armature Winding AW** — Lap or wave winding in the rotor slots.
8. **Commutator** — Cylindrical copper-segment assembly at one end of the rotor.
9. **Brushes B1, B2** — Carbon brushes riding on the commutator, mounted on the stator frame.
10. **Shaft** — Mechanical output, coupled to the load.

**Power Flow Path:**  
Supply → Field Winding (SF) → Compensating Winding (CW) → Brushes → Commutator → Armature Winding (AW) → Other Brush → Back to Supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state in the answer that the magnetic circuit is **fully laminated** to handle AC flux. Marks are routinely lost when students forget this single phrase.

## 4.3 Commutator and Brush-Assembly Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    arm1[ARMATURE CONDUCTOR A] --> comm1[COMMUTATOR SEGMENT 1]
    arm2[ARMATURE CONDUCTOR B] --> comm2[COMMUTATOR SEGMENT 2]
    comm1 --> brush1[BRUSH B1 POSITIVE]
    comm2 --> brush2[BRUSH B2 NEGATIVE]
    brush1 --> term1[TERMINAL TO SERIES FIELD]
    brush2 --> term2[TERMINAL FROM SUPPLY NEUTRAL]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following question bank is calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern**: Part A (2 marks × 9 = 18 marks, answer 9 out of 10) and Part B (14 marks × 2 = 28 marks, with internal choice). The questions here mirror this pattern for Module 4 of PEEET743.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2–3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is a universal motor called "universal"? List any two applications.**

**Model Answer:**

The motor is called *universal* because it can operate on **both DC and single-phase AC supply** with similar performance characteristics (speed, torque, output). The construction is suitably modified (laminated core, low series-field turns, and compensating winding) to handle the AC-specific issues of eddy-current loss, reactance, and commutation.

**Two applications:**
1. **Domestic:** Table fans, ceiling-fan regulators, sewing machines, hair dryers, vacuum cleaners.
2. **Portable tools:** Hand drills, mixers, blenders, polishers, grinders, hedge trimmers.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Apps:** *"FANS-DRILL-MIX"* — **F**ans, **A**ppliances (sewing machine), **N**ail guns, **S**ewing machines, **D**rills, **R**outers, **I**ndustrial polishers, **L**awn mowers, **L**athes (light), **M**ixers, **I**rons (vintage), **X**-ray units (old).

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Why is the magnetic circuit of a universal motor laminated, while a conventional DC series motor uses a solid iron core?**

**Model Answer:**

A conventional DC series motor operates only on DC, so the magnetic flux is *stationary* in time and does not induce eddy currents in the iron core. A solid core is therefore acceptable and economical.

A universal motor must also operate on **AC supply**, where the magnetic flux **alternates** at supply frequency (50 Hz). A solid core under AC flux would develop severe **eddy-current losses** ($P_e \propto f^2 B_m^2 t^2$, where $t$ is lamination thickness), causing:
- Excessive heating
- Reduced efficiency
- Possible core saturation and reduced starting torque

To suppress eddy currents, the entire magnetic circuit — both **stator pole cores and armature core** — is constructed from **thin silicon-steel laminations (0.35–0.5 mm)** insulated from each other, restricting eddy-current loops to within each lamination.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat constructional sketch, describe the **construction of a compensated universal motor**. Label all major parts. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **principle of operation** of a universal motor on AC supply. Why does it produce unidirectional torque even though the supply alternates? Derive the **expression for average torque** developed. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

A compensated universal motor consists of the following major parts:

1. **Stator (Stationary Part):**
   - **Yoke:** Cast iron / fabricated steel frame forming the outer body and the return magnetic path.
   - **Pole Cores (2, laminated):** Built from silicon-steel laminations riveted together, bolted to the yoke.
   - **Pole Shoes (Laminated):** Curved faces that face the air gap, with **slots cut into them** to accommodate the compensating winding.
   - **Series Field Winding (SF):** Concentrated winding of insulated copper wire wound on insulating spools around the pole cores. Connected in **series** with the armature.
   - **Compensating Winding (CW):** Distributed winding embedded in the pole-shoe slots, also in series with the armature. Its MMF is arranged to **oppose** the armature-reaction MMF.

2. **Rotor (Rotating Part):**
   - **Armature Core (Laminated Drum):** Slots on the outer periphery for the winding.
   - **Armature Winding:** Lap or wave DC-type winding.
   - **Commutator:** Hard-drawn copper segments insulated by mica.
   - **Shaft:** Mechanical output.
3. **Brush Gear:** Two carbon brushes in holders with spring pressure, connected to the supply terminals.

**Sketch:** (Draw a circular cross-section with two pole cores, pole shoes, and slot-embedded compensating winding; central rotor with commutator at one end and shaft.)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Principle of Operation on AC:**

When connected to a single-phase AC supply, the current through the field winding and armature winding (both in series) **reverses every half-cycle**. The flux produced in the air gap, being proportional to the field current, also reverses simultaneously with the current. Since the torque in a DC motor is given by $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a$, and both $\phi$ and $I_a$ reverse together, the product $\phi \cdot I_a$ remains **always positive**, producing **unidirectional torque** at every instant.

**Derivation of Average Torque:**

Let $i_a(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ and $\phi(t) = \phi_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

$$T(t) = k \cdot \phi(t) \cdot i_a(t) = k \cdot \phi_m I_m \sin^2(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$T(t) = \dfrac{k \phi_m I_m}{2}\left[1 - \cos(2\omega t - 2\phi)\right]$$

Averaging over one complete cycle, the cosine term vanishes:

$$T_{avg} = \dfrac{k \phi_m I_m}{2} = k \cdot \phi_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

This is the governing torque equation of a universal motor on AC supply.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **three major modifications** required in a DC series motor to make it suitable for AC operation as a universal motor. Justify each modification. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **methods of speed control** of a universal motor. Compare **TRIAC-based phase-angle control** with **PWM control** in detail. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

To convert a DC series motor into a universal motor, the following three modifications are essential:

**Modification 1: Lamination of the Entire Magnetic Circuit**

*Justification:* On AC, the flux alternates at supply frequency. Eddy-current losses in a solid core are $P_e = \eta B_m^2 f^2 t^2 V$. To minimize this, the **stator pole cores, pole shoes, and armature core are all built from thin laminations** (0.35–0.5 mm) of silicon steel, each insulated by a thin varnish or oxide coating.

**Modification 2: Reduction of Series-Field Turns**

*Justification:* The series field winding has inductance $L_{se}$, and on AC it produces a reactance drop $X_{se} I_a$ that reduces the current and worsens the power factor. By using **fewer turns of thicker wire** in the field winding, the inductance and reactance are minimized, keeping the power factor close to unity.

**Modification 3: Addition of a Compensating Winding (and sometimes Interpoles)**

*Justification:* Armature reaction on AC is severe and rotates with the supply, causing strong distortion of the air-gap flux and poor commutation. A compensating winding is embedded in the pole-shoe slots and connected in series with the armature such that its **MMF directly opposes the armature MMF** at every point in the air gap, neutralizing armature reaction completely and dramatically improving commutation.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Methods of Speed Control:**

1. **Mechanical Centrifugal Governor:** A mechanical switch in series with the motor opens at a preset high speed, reducing effective voltage.
2. **Rheostat (Series Resistance):** Simple but inefficient — wastes power as heat.
3. **Tapped Field Winding:** Multiple tappings change the field ampere-turns.
4. **Autotransformer / Variac:** Adjusts applied AC voltage smoothly.
5. **TRIAC Phase-Angle Control (most common in modern appliances).**
6. **PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) Control (used in high-end tools).**

**Comparison: TRIAC vs PWM Control**

| Parameter | TRIAC Phase-Angle Control | PWM Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Switching Device** | TRIAC (SCR pair) | MOSFET / IGBT |
| **Switching Frequency** | Line frequency (50 Hz), fires once per half-cycle | High frequency (1 kHz – 25 kHz) |
| **Switching Losses** | Low (only firing loss) | Higher (many switching events) |
| **Harmonics Produced** | Severe low-order harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th) | High-order harmonics, easily filtered |
| **EMI / RFI Interference** | Significant | Lower due to high frequency |
| **Speed Range** | Wide | Very wide and linear |
| **Torque Pulsations** | Pronounced at low speeds | Smooth (high-frequency ripple) |
| **Power Factor at Low Speed** | Poor | Better |
| **Cost** | Cheap (₹5–₹20 TRIAC) | Costlier (driver + MOSFET) |
| **Application Examples** | Domestic fans, mixer-grinders | Cordless drills, premium vacuum cleaners, robotics |

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state "fully laminated magnetic circuit"** — Examiners deduct 1 mark instantly if you describe the motor as having a solid core. *Always say laminated.*
> 2. **Conflating the torque equation** with that of a DC shunt motor. The universal motor torque is **proportional to $I_a^2$** (series characteristic), not to $I_a$.
> 3. **Missing the $\cos(\phi)$ factor** in the AC torque derivation. The $T \propto \phi I$ instantaneous form is *incomplete*; you must average over a cycle to obtain $T_{avg} = k\phi I \cos(\phi)$.
> 4. **Skipping the unidirectional-torque argument.** State explicitly: *"Since both flux and armature current reverse together in a series-connected motor, the product remains positive, giving unidirectional torque."*
> 5. **No-load operation trap.** A universal motor **must never be run without mechanical load** — its speed will rise to dangerous levels and the motor will self-destruct. Many students omit this safety-critical remark.
> 6. **Wrong diagram labelling.** Always label: yoke, pole core, pole shoe, compensating winding, field winding, armature core, commutator, brushes, and shaft. Missing labels = lost marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Before Exam — Memorize These Points:**

- ✅ A **universal motor** is essentially a **laminated, compensated DC series motor** that runs on **AC or DC**.
- ✅ Two main construction types: **concentrated-pole (non-compensated)** and **distributed-field (compensated)**.
- ✅ **Three key modifications** to convert a DC series motor into a universal motor: **laminations, fewer field turns, compensating winding**.
- ✅ Torque on DC: $T = k\phi I_a$ (linear in flux and current).
- ✅ Torque on AC: $T_{avg} = k\phi_{rms} I_{rms} \cos(\phi)$ (with $\cos\phi$ factor).
- ✅ Torque is **always unidirectional** because field and armature currents reverse together.
- ✅ **Speed** is **very high** (3000–15000 rpm) and **inversely proportional to flux** ($N \propto 1/\phi$).
- ✅ **Power factor** on AC is **0.8 – 0.95 lagging**; never unity.
- ✅ **Speed control** by TRIAC phase-angle control is the most common in modern appliances.
- ✅ **Efficiency** is lower than induction motors (30–70 %) due to commutator/brush and iron losses.
- ✅ **Danger:** Never run a universal motor at no-load — speed will run away to destruction.
- ✅ **Applications:** Domestic fans, hair dryers, mixers, blenders, hand drills, polishers, vacuum cleaners, sewing machines, portable grinders.
- ✅ **Poles:** Almost always **2-pole** construction for fractional-kW ratings.
- ✅ **Back-EMF equation** is identical to a DC motor: $E_b = \dfrac{\phi Z N P}{60 A}$.
- ✅ **Commutation** is the chief weakness of universal motors on AC; the compensating winding is the primary mitigation.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Universal Motor**
